posts.summary: Gruber launches Daring Fireball by analyzing Apple's new PowerMac G4 lineup, finding the specs competent but unsurprising. He dismisses the community's fixation on Jaguar and .Mac pricing as shortsighted, arguing the real issues are declining PowerMac sales and slow Mac OS X adoption among professional users. He identifies a chicken-and-egg problem: pro users may be avoiding new hardware because they're unhappy with OS X, or avoiding OS X because the hardware hasn't been compelling enough. The new dual-processor machines are designed for OS X, making this question critical to Apple's future.
key_points: The new PowerMac G4s offer modest speed bumps with meaningful architectural improvements (167MHz bus, DDR SDRAM), but nothing surprising since these debuted with the Xserve months earlier.
The community's obsession with Jaguar and .Mac pricing misses the real issues: declining high-margin PowerMac sales and slow OS X adoption among professional users.
Apple's business depends on high-end, high-margin machines — consumer products like the iMac and iBook aren't enough to sustain the company.
There's a chicken-and-egg problem between PowerMac sales and OS X adoption: pros may be avoiding one because of the other, and the answer determines Apple's trajectory.
Mac OS X avoided catastrophe but hasn't yet become a smash hit, particularly with the creative professional user base Apple depends on.
money_quotes: The modern Mac user is a cheap bastard.

There are two big problems facing Apple. The first is that PowerMac sales are down, and have been down for quite some time. No other problem compares to this one in magnitude, because the fact remains that Apple is in the business of selling computers.
It's simple: low-end machines are low-margin, high-end machines are high-margin.
The fear, before Mac OS X debuted, was that it was going to be an utter flop. Which would have been absolutely catastrophic for Apple, because there was no Plan B.
While that situation has clearly been averted, Mac OS X is by no means a smash hit.
A second processor just doesn't do much for you under Mac OS 9.
But if it's the latter — that is, if pro users are giving the finger to Mac OS X — things could get ugly.
posts.key_insight: Apple's real crisis isn't software pricing but a chicken-and-egg problem where professional users are neither buying new PowerMacs nor adopting Mac OS X, and which problem is driving the other will determine whether things turn around or get ugly.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights a Cory Doctorow article in TidBITS about Hollywood's lobbying efforts to pass legislation that would cripple the PC and consumer electronics industries in the name of copyright protection. He argues the core problem is not controversy but ignorance — the technical issues are too complex for most people to grasp, yet anyone who does understand them opposes the legislation. Gruber praises Doctorow for making a smart argument accessible to a general audience without dumbing it down.
key_points: The entertainment industry is lobbying for legislation that would fundamentally cripple PCs and consumer electronics to protect copyright
The challenge is a communication gap: 99% of people lack the technical background to understand why this legislation is dangerous
This is not a matter of controversy but of ignorance — no informed person supports this legislation
Cory Doctorow's TidBITS article succeeds at making the technical argument accessible without oversimplifying it
money_quotes: legislation which is intended to completely cripple both the PC and consumer electronics industries, all in the name of protecting Hollywood's precious bodily fluids.

For people who don't have the technical chops to comprehend the underlying issues (which is about 99 percent of the U.S. population), the whole thing just sails over their head, and gets filed under "So what?"
It's like something out of Terry Gilliam's Brazil.
This is not an issue of controversy — it's an issue of ignorance.
No one is in favor of this legislation other than a bevy of entertainment industry fat cats in Burbank.
Anyone who understands it is opposed to it; the problem is that almost no one understands.
It's not that he's dumbed the issue down — he's simply done the hard work of making a smart argument in terms that anyone can understand.
posts.key_insight: Hollywood's anti-technology legislation succeeds not because people support it, but because the technical stakes are too complex for most people to grasp.

posts.summary: Gruber presents a detailed technical solution for automatically converting straight quotes into typographically correct 'smart quotes' in Movable Type blog posts. He builds on Mark Pilgrim's earlier work, arguing that proper typography should be automated rather than requiring manual preflight scripts. The solution uses Brad Choate's MTPerlScript and MTMacro plug-ins to educate quotes at the template level, keeping raw dumb quotes in the database. Gruber walks through the Perl regex algorithm, which must carefully avoid converting quotes inside HTML tags. This post is an early predecessor to his SmartyPants plug-in, which later obsoleted the approach described here.
key_points: Mac users have expected automatic smart quotes in word processors for 15+ years, but HTML has lagged behind because curly quotes require ugly entities
The ideal workflow stores plain dumb quotes in the database and converts them to smart quotes only at template render time
The regex algorithm must distinguish between quotes in content text and quotes inside HTML tags, requiring lookaheads and loops rather than simple substitutions
The logic for opening vs. closing quotes is simple: if the preceding character is non-whitespace, it's a closing quote; remaining quotes are opening
This hack eventually evolved into Gruber's standalone SmartyPants plug-in for Movable Type
money_quotes: In HTML markup, however, smart quotes have been a royal pain in the ass.
But the whole point of modern blogging tools is that they make posting articles easy.

Normal, non-obsessive people will only tolerate steps 1 and 3: write, then post. And they're right — educating quotes is not a difficult problem to solve, and your software should be able to do it for you.

The way it ought to work is to write using plain old easy-to-type dumb quotes, store the article in Movable Type's database with the dumb quotes, and have the quote education take place at the template level, when the article gets published.
We're eating our own dog food here at Daring Fireball, so we're using this very script ourselves.
The whole thing gives off a tad whiff of being a hack.
posts.key_insight: Typography in web publishing should be handled automatically by software at render time, not manually by authors — good tools eliminate the trade-off between typographic quality and ease of use.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that HyperCard failed because it violated fundamental Mac UI conventions — stacks looked and felt nothing like real Mac applications, creating an alien GUI universe within the Mac OS. He supports Apple's decision under Steve Jobs to let HyperCard fade away, dismissing periodic demands from devotees to revive it as futile nostalgia. While acknowledging HyperCard's visionary influence on the web's page-based metaphor, he contends it was fundamentally a developer tool that couldn't produce genuine Macintosh software. He notes that only HyperCard developers mourn its loss, not normal users, and points to Cocoa as the real path forward.
key_points: HyperCard stacks felt like their own GUI universe, never achieving the 'Mac-like' feel that Mac users instinctively expect from software
Hiding the menu bar by default was an insult to Mac UI sensibilities and symptomatic of HyperCard's disregard for platform conventions
HyperCard's demise was a deliberate decision by Steve Jobs in 1998, and four years later the debate should be settled
Only HyperCard developers want it back — normal users never liked or used stacks, which undercuts the revival argument
HyperCard was visionary in concept but failed in execution: a Mac developer tool that couldn't generate real Mac software
money_quotes: Mac users have an innate sense of "Mac-like"; most Mac users can determine whether a particular software package is Mac-like within 60 seconds of launching it and poking around.
It always felt like HyperCard was its own little GUI universe running within the Mac OS.
Stacks felt and looked consistent with other stacks, but never felt, looked, or acted like other Macintosh apps.
Hiding the menu bar is fine for games, but for just about anything else, it's an outright insult to Mac UI sensibilities.
Holding out hope that Apple will see the light and bring HyperCard back to life is like planning for the resurrection of 8-Track tapes.
The only people clamoring for the return of HyperCard are HyperCard developers. Normal users don't care, because normal users seldom used or liked HyperCard stacks.
HyperCard was a Macintosh developer tool that couldn't generate real Macintosh software.
posts.key_insight: HyperCard failed because it was a Mac development tool that couldn't produce software that actually felt like Mac software — violating the very platform conventions its users cared most about.

posts.summary: Gruber dismisses a New York Times article by John Markoff that speculates about an Apple-branded mobile phone called 'iPhone.' He argues the article relies entirely on unnamed 'industry analysts' who have a poor track record predicting Apple's moves, while Steve Jobs confirms nothing. Gruber picks apart the article's logic that Jaguar's new features hint at a mobile device, arguing that applications like Mail and Sherlock are desktop software that couldn't simply run on a cell phone. He concludes with a characteristically pedantic aside about the Times's failure to use Apple's correct trademark 'Mac OS.'
key_points: The NYT iPhone speculation is based entirely on unnamed industry analysts who have no credible track record with Apple predictions
The article confuses applications (Mail, Sherlock) with portable technologies — these couldn't run on a phone without being completely rewritten
iSync is legitimately about mobile integration, but that's its explicit purpose as part of the digital hub strategy, not evidence of a secret phone project
The Times's copy editing inconsistently expands 'Mac' to 'Macintosh' while leaving 'OS' unexpanded, violating both Apple's trademark and the paper's own style conventions
money_quotes: Go ahead and read it, but remember that it's all bullshit speculation at this point.
Industry analysts know nothing about Apple, and given their record in the tech industry in the last few years, it's a wonder anyone quotes them at all.
Even the Daring Fireball could have offered better insight than these bozos.
These are applications, not technologies.
The article seems to insinuate that Apple could make Sherlock run on a cell phone; that's impossible, unless the cell phone were actually running Mac OS X, which definitely is impossible.
"OS" (as a noun, not a name) seems entirely out of place in a newspaper that still puts the periods in "F.B.I." and "C.I.A.".
posts.key_insight: Speculation about Apple products from industry analysts and mainstream press is unreliable because they consistently confuse surface-level feature descriptions with actual technical feasibility and strategic intent.

posts.summary: Gruber celebrates the resurrection of the PGP product line under the new PGP Corporation, after Network Associates bungled its stewardship. The key news is that PGP Desktop Security for Mac OS X will ship before end of 2002, with the software already completed but previously shelved. Gruber uses this as a springboard to argue that open source alternatives like GPG, while cryptographically sound, fail at user interface design. He broadens this into a critique of the Unix-world misconception that GUIs are superficial wrappers around 'real' software, citing Sun's StarOffice as another example of confusing visual polish with genuine usability. The core argument is that good UI is deep engineering work, not a cosmetic layer.
key_points: PGP Corporation rescued the PGP product line from Network Associates, who had killed it despite completed Mac OS X and Windows XP versions sitting ready to ship
GPG is cryptographically equivalent to PGP but fails as end-user software because good UI design is genuinely hard engineering work, not a superficial addition
The Unix world harbors a widespread misconception that a GUI is a thin skin over 'real' software, when in fact the difference between good and bad UI runs deep
Sun's StarOffice pitch to Apple reveals the same misunderstanding — thinking Mac users reject software for being ugly rather than for being unintuitive and non-conformant to platform conventions
money_quotes: What's wrong with GPG is what's wrong with so much open source software — the user interface.
Open source software has proven itself to be fantastic for creating developer-level technology, and horrible at creating end-user software.
User interface design is hard work, and implementing a good design takes a long time.

There is a widespread misconception in the Unix world that a GUI is something that sits on top of 'real' software; that all the real engineering work is done at low levels, producing APIs and command-line tools, and that one can reasonably expect to wrap a GUI around the completed low level technologies in short order.
Sure, *that's* the problem: the current StarOffice interface isn't 'beautiful'. It doesn't have anything to do with being unintuitive, unwieldy, and not conforming to any standard Mac UI guidelines.
Sun thinks that the only thing StarOffice needs to achieve popularity on Mac OS X is better graphic design.
Nobody makes a product more beautiful on Apple than Apple.
posts.key_insight: Good user interface design is deep engineering work, not a cosmetic layer, and this fundamental misunderstanding is why open source software and companies like Sun consistently fail to create compelling end-user experiences.

posts.summary: Gruber challenges the popular theory that Quark's failure to release a Mac OS X-native version of QuarkXPress is the primary reason for slow OS X adoption and weak PowerMac sales. He argues that other major apps like Photoshop and Office already shipped native OS X versions without moving the needle, so blaming Quark is scapegoating. Instead, he proposes that the real barrier is the sheer magnitude of change OS X represents for design professionals who are loyal Mac users but not computer enthusiasts. These users work in organizations where migration involves expensive training and IT coordination, not just a $129 upgrade.
key_points: Neither Photoshop nor Office going native on OS X triggered meaningful jumps in adoption or PowerMac sales, undermining the 'blame the developers' narrative
Many Quark users were still on version 3.32, released six years prior — they are not aggressive upgraders, making them unlikely to drive platform migration
A native OS X QuarkXPress would benefit Quark more than Apple, since it's the one feature XTensions can't replicate
The real barrier to OS X adoption among design professionals is the massive workflow disruption and training costs, not any single missing application
Apple's most valuable customers — design professionals buying high-margin hardware — may be the last to switch because they prioritize getting work done over adopting new technology
money_quotes: If only those cocksuckers at Quark would get their act together, everyone would start buying new machines and join hands in singing the OS X theme song.
What exactly can Apple do about Quark? Take the source code for XPress and finish the carbonization for them?
QuarkXPress is just a convenient punching bag.
If any single program was going to drive OS X adoption and new PowerMac sales, it was Photoshop, which is much more performance-hungry than QuarkXPress.
It's not Apple which stands to benefit from QuarkXPress for OS X, but Quark itself.
A core segment of Apple's user base consists of loyal, long-standing Mac users who buy very expensive, high-margin, top-of-the line hardware — but who are design nerds, not computer nerds.

The end result may be that the customers Apple most wants to switch to Mac OS X will be among the last to do so. Not because they're curmudgeons or Luddites or stubborn — but simply because they have work to do.
posts.key_insight: Apple's slow OS X adoption wasn't caused by any single missing app but by the fact that its most valuable customers — design professionals — prioritize uninterrupted productivity over platform migration.

posts.summary: Gruber shares an AppleScript he wrote for BBEdit that replicates the functionality of Dan Crevier's 1996 HTML Grabber plug-in, which fetches web page source code via HTTP and displays it in a text window. The original plug-in only worked in Mac OS 9, so Gruber's script leverages Mac OS X's 'do shell script' command to call the curl utility instead. He walks through the script's implementation details, explaining how AppleScript properties, dialog boxes, and BBEdit's language detection work together. The post serves as both a practical tool and a demonstration of how Mac OS X elegantly bridges GUI scripting with Unix command-line power.
key_points: The AppleScript/curl hybrid replaces a Classic Mac OS plug-in with a native OS X solution that's trivially simple to write
Mac OS X's 'do shell script' command puts the entire Unix toolset at the disposal of AppleScript, combining GUI convenience with command-line power
The script preserves the last-used URL between invocations by using an AppleScript property
BBEdit 6.5's automatic language detection only works when window contents are set as a property during creation, not when set afterward — a subtle implementation detail that matters for usability
money_quotes: This sort of AppleScript/shell hybrid shows Mac OS X at its best.
While a command line by itself does not make for a good user interface, it can be an amazingly easy-to-use programming interface.
The do shell script command puts the whole Unix command-line toolset at your fingertips.
Sort of like the View Source command in a web browser, except much more convenient if you're already working in BBEdit.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's marriage of AppleScript's GUI scripting with Unix command-line tools via 'do shell script' represents the platform at its best — combining user-facing convenience with programmer-facing power in just a few lines of code.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Tim O'Reilly's observation that Mac OS X is attracting droves of Linux and Unix users, not just Windows switchers. He agrees that Unix users find OS X revelatory because basic usability features that Mac OS 9 users take for granted are novel to them. However, he argues that this cuts both ways: Mac OS X is the world's best desktop Unix, but that's a low bar, and only longtime Mac users notice its remaining rough edges. He pushes back on O'Reilly's suggestion that Apple should target Unix/Linux users in its Switch campaign, noting the market math doesn't work and Linux nerds already know about OS X.
key_points: Unix and Linux users are switching to Mac OS X in large numbers because basic usability features feel miraculous compared to desktop Linux
Mac OS 9 users are the only group with legitimate complaints about OS X because they're accustomed to an even more polished experience
Being the world's best desktop Unix isn't saying much, since the competition is so poor at consumer-facing tasks
O'Reilly's claim that capturing Unix desktop users would double Apple's market share is mathematically implausible
Apple doesn't need to advertise Mac OS X to Linux nerds — they already know about it
money_quotes: Unix-heads love Mac OS X, and are switching to it in droves.
For Mac OS 9 users, plugging in a peripheral and having it 'just work' is not an amazing feature, it is a baseline.
Only Mac OS 9 users see anything to complain about with Mac OS X, because they're the only people accustomed to something even more polished.
From a Mac user's perspective, Mac OS X still has rough edges; from a Linux user's, it's the Hope diamond.
Mac OS X is clearly the world's best desktop Unix — unfortunately, that isn't saying much.
Apple doesn't need to advertise to Linux nerds about the wonders of Mac OS X; they already know.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's appeal to Unix users reveals a paradox: it's simultaneously the best desktop Unix ever made and a step down from the classic Mac experience, and only longtime Mac users can see both sides.

posts.summary: Gruber interviews Maarten Hekkelman, creator of the Pepper text editor, about his decision to discontinue the product. Hekkelman details Pepper's journey from BeOS (as Pe) to Mac OS to a cross-platform release on Windows and Linux, explaining how performance issues, piracy, poor reception on VersionTracker, and Jaguar incompatibility led him to quit. The interview explores the challenges of cross-platform development for a solo developer, the economics of shareware pricing, and Hekkelman's surprisingly positive assessment of Windows development tools versus his disappointment with Mac OS X. Notably, Hekkelman sold only three copies on Linux (one fraudulent), illustrating the near-impossibility of selling small commercial software to that audience.
key_points: Pepper's cross-platform port to Windows and Linux consumed enormous effort but failed to generate sufficient revenue, with the Linux version selling only three copies

Hekkelman was deeply disappointed by Mac OS X as both a user and developer, citing slow performance, UI regressions, and Carbon's instability, while praising Windows development tools and documentation
Low pricing strategy backfired — Pepper was priced too low to sustain development, and Hekkelman concludes that selling hundreds of cheap copies produces only 'peanuts'
BBEdit's market dominance meant Pepper could only thrive in the brief window before BBEdit shipped for Mac OS X, after which sales dried up immediately
Hekkelman predicts the software industry will bifurcate into expensive enterprise packages and open source, with no viable middle ground for small commercial apps
money_quotes: A huge amount of work that went into designing the ultimate GUI was thrown away and all we got back was a bag full of candy that was dog slow.
I sold three copies and one of those three was a fraud. I did have thousands of downloads though.
Selling hundreds of peanuts is lots of work and the result is, peanuts.

I am afraid that will be the future of the entire software industry. Eventually it will only be possible to sell huge software packages with lots of support; all the small apps will be open source in the end.
Windows is like heaven to a programmer. There are so many tools to chose from and the documentation is wonderful.
Bare Bones was getting arrogant and I thought I wanted to be the little boy shouting that the emperor didn't have clothes.
I really believe WinXP is the true successor of the old Mac OS. It is fast, stable and if you pick your software well it is also easy to use.
Most people asking to become beta testers are only hoping for free software and never give feedback.
posts.key_insight: The economics of indie software development are brutally unforgiving: low pricing, cross-platform porting costs, and dominant incumbents can destroy years of work, foreshadowing the squeeze that would define the shareware market for decades.

posts.summary: Gruber reacts to the release of Netscape 7.0 with dismissal and sadness. He questions why anyone would use Netscape 7 over Mozilla 1.1, since Netscape removed Mozilla's most popular feature — pop-up ad blocking — and replaced it with ads for AOL services. He sees this as both strategically foolish and a melancholy decline from Netscape's glory days, when new versions genuinely advanced the web.
key_points: Netscape 7.0 is based on Mozilla but strips out the most popular feature: pop-up ad blocking
Netscape not only removed ad-blocking but added its own ads for AOL services into the browser UI
There is no rational reason to choose Netscape 7 over Mozilla 1.1
The decline of Netscape from web pioneer to ad-laden AOL product is both absurd and tragic
money_quotes: Does anyone care?

Not only did they remove the ad-blocking — a feature that is popular because people are annoyed by stupid ads — but they junked up the browser UI by adding a bunch of their own ads for crappy AOL services.
This isn't just preposterously dumb — it's sad.
Especially so if you remember when Netscape was cool, back when a major new version of Netscape meant that the entire web instantly became a better place.
posts.key_insight: Netscape's transformation from web pioneer to an ad-stuffed shell of Mozilla perfectly illustrates how corporate ownership can strip everything good from a product and replace it with everything users hate.

posts.summary: Gruber posts a brief update explaining the lack of recent content due to his move from Massachusetts back to Philadelphia. He thanks readers for feedback on his previous interview with Maarten Hekkelman. He takes a characteristic jab at Massachusetts sandwich-making while recommending John Siracusa's extensive Jaguar (Mac OS X 10.2) review at Ars Technica as reading material in the interim.
key_points: Site updates have been sparse due to Gruber's move from Massachusetts to Philadelphia
Reader feedback on the Maarten Hekkelman interview was appreciated
Gruber recommends John Siracusa's Mac OS X Jaguar review at Ars Technica as essential reading
A lighthearted dig at Massachusetts food culture reveals Gruber's Philadelphia roots and personality
money_quotes: By "midst", I mean "we're in Philly, painting an empty new apartment, but our stuff is boxed up waiting for movers in Massachusetts."
The good news is that I've eaten more decent sandwiches this week than I did in two whole years living in Massachusetts.
If you are not Italian, you should not be baking bread.
That should tide you over, if you're looking for good reading.
posts.key_insight: Even in a brief housekeeping post, Gruber's voice shines through — personal asides about Philly sandwiches and Italian bread paired with a pointed recommendation of Siracusa's Jaguar review as the gold standard of Mac OS X commentary.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's most important announcements weren't iCal but two other moves: ending Mac OS 9 boot support on new Macs in 2003, and the 'Design Freely' promotion offering a free copy of Adobe InDesign with new PowerMac G4 purchases. He reads the InDesign giveaway as a direct attack on Quark, an admission that PowerMac sales are disappointing, and a sign that InDesign itself isn't selling well. He argues the promotion won't convert many Quark users because InDesign isn't dramatically better — just different enough to be frustrating. However, the OS 9 boot deadline may be the real driver pushing designers to buy new Macs now, regardless of the InDesign offer.
key_points: Apple ending OS 9 boot support in 2003 is a bigger story than iCal, forcing users to buy now if they want OS 9 as a fallback
The free InDesign promotion is a direct shot at Quark and an implicit admission that current PowerMac G4s aren't compelling enough on their own merits
InDesign hasn't displaced QuarkXPress because it's not dramatically better — just different enough to slow experienced Quark users down
Pro designers aren't software cheapskates; price isn't what kept them from switching, so giving InDesign away free won't change the calculus
The OS 9 deadline creates more purchase urgency than the InDesign giveaway ever could
money_quotes: If we discount Mac OS X 10.0 (because it mostly stunk), Mac OS X has really only been out for about a year.
That this is a direct shot at Quark is indisputable. There's a reason that the offer is for InDesign, not for Photoshop or Illustrator.
Apple clearly wants the pro design market to buy new PowerMacs now, and these new machines are not appealing enough on their own merits.
The perception is that the current G4's just aren't that much better than the models from two (or more) years ago.
Pro designers aren't software cheapskates — it's not the price tag for InDesign that has kept them from abandoning QuarkXPress.
What has kept InDesign from putting Quark out of business is the simple fact that InDesign is not QuarkXPress.
Time is a precious commodity to most designers, especially those who work in page layout. Designers are almost always on deadline, and they will generally jump at anything that can save them time.
Even if you have no intention of ever switching to InDesign, if you're a Quark user contemplating a new Mac, now looks like the time to buy.
posts.key_insight: Apple's OS 9 boot deadline is a more powerful sales driver than any software giveaway, because it creates genuine urgency that a free app — no matter how good — cannot.

posts.summary: Gruber systematically dismantles Joel Spolsky's argument that there's no business case for developing Mac software. Spolsky claimed that with only ~4% market share, Mac developers need 25x the adoption rate of Windows developers to justify the effort. Gruber counters that raw market share is misleading because most Windows users are corporate workers or computer-phobic home users who don't buy third-party software, while Mac users disproportionately comprise enthusiasts who actively purchase software. He further argues that Spolsky's comparison of Mac development to building Netscape-only websites is fundamentally flawed, and that his dismissal of Mac dominance in graphics is absurd. Gruber concludes that Mac users reward well-designed software, making the platform ideal for developers who value craft over feature count.
key_points: Raw market share is a misleading metric because not all computer users are equal consumers of commercial software — corporate and casual users rarely buy third-party software
Comparing Mac development to building Netscape-only websites is a category error: browsers aren't APIs, and sane developers target standards, not specific browsers
Mac users disproportionately consist of enthusiasts who actively seek out and purchase software, making the effective market share much larger than 4%
The Mac market rewards design quality over feature quantity — well-designed Mac apps win their categories, while Windows categories tend to be won by Microsoft
Software that originates on Mac and ports to Windows often succeeds (Photoshop), while Windows software ported to Mac almost always fails (Corel)
money_quotes: Spolsky only seems to be debating whether professional developers should write for the Mac in addition to Windows; that a serious developer might ignore Windows and write only for the Mac is apparently inconceivable.
Browsers aren't API's. Sane web developers don't target specific browsers, they target standards, and all browsers which support them.
Let's divide computer users into two groups — people who think computers are fun, and people who just happen to use a computer. Which group do you think buys more software?
Amongst computer users who actually buy software, Mac users comprise a significantly larger chunk than 4 percent. Significantly.
Windows software ported to the Mac almost always fails. Mac software ported to Windows very often succeeds.
On the Mac, in any given software category, the best app usually wins. On Windows, in any given software category, Microsoft usually wins.
Given a choice between two otherwise unmarked software packages, one labeled 'more features', the other labeled 'better designed interface', most Mac users will choose the latter.
If the 'elite graphics people in big American cities' aren't an indicator of the industry's OS preference, who is? Non-elite PowerPoint users in rural areas?
posts.key_insight: Market share statistics dramatically undercount the Mac's commercial software opportunity because Mac users are disproportionately the kind of enthusiasts who actually buy software and reward quality design.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Steven Frank of Panic's frustration with VersionTracker user reviews, where impatient and unreasonable users threaten developers with bad reviews instead of reporting bugs directly. He argues that anonymous review forums like VersionTracker's have zero credibility due to anonymity, poor writing, technical inaccuracies, and a fixation on price rather than quality. He notes the irony that such abusive users only harm good developers who actually try to be responsive, while bad developers remain unaffected. Gruber concludes that while VersionTracker is useful as a software listing service, the review feature actively damages its brand and should be removed entirely.
key_points: Users who threaten developers with bad reviews instead of reporting bugs through proper channels only punish responsive developers while leaving bad ones unscathed
There are only two reasonable responses to software bugs: report them to the developer or keep them to yourself — publicly lambasting software without first contacting the developer is unreasonable
Real criticism in any field depends entirely on credibility, which anonymous review forums fundamentally lack
VersionTracker reviews are undermined not just by anonymity but by poor writing, technical inaccuracies, and an obsessive focus on price
VersionTracker's value is narrowly focused on being a comprehensive software listing — the review feature detracts from that brand
money_quotes: They only annoy the good guys: developers who are actually trying to be responsive to their users. The shitty developers who ignore their users aren't bothered, since, well, they ignore their users.
If you're going to take the time to write a report about the bug, why post it to the VersionTracker forums without having first emailed a report to the developer?
Is the goal to see the software actually improve, or just to get off on pissing all over someone else's work?
Everyone knows that marketing is usually bullshit — only the gullible believe unverified marketing information.
Real criticism — whether it be for software, cars, books, or movies — hinges upon but a single thing: credibility.
There is no credibility whatsoever in VersionTracker's anonymous review forums.
That the user reviews are so uniformly bad detracts from the VersionTracker brand.
posts.key_insight: Anonymous user review forums undermine legitimate criticism because real criticism requires credibility, and platforms that host uniformly terrible reviews only damage their own brand while punishing the developers who actually care.

posts.summary: Gruber annotates a photo of his workspace submitted to a 'Workspaces' project, walking through each item on and around his desk. He catalogs his Ikea furniture, aging Macs, peripherals, and bookshelf with characteristic dry humor. The post is a lighthearted personal inventory that reveals his setup as a Mac-centric developer and writer in 2002.
key_points: Gruber's workspace centers on two Macs: an older PowerMac 9600 running OS 9 and a PowerBook G3 running OS X 10.2
The post showcases his identity as a Mac power user, regex enthusiast, and O'Reilly book collector
Self-deprecating humor pervades the descriptions, from the Ikea desk name to the worn Yankees cap
money_quotes: Solidly constructed, highly configurable, but saddled with the unfortunate product name of "Jerker".
192 MB of RAM, an amount which was once considered a handsome sum of memory, but which can now be obtained for free in a box of Frosted Flakes.
Genuine New York Yankees baseball cap, size 7 and one-quarter, circa 1990; it is a bit, um, well-worn.
posts.key_insight: A playful, self-aware tour of a 2002 Mac enthusiast's desk that reveals personality through the details of everyday objects.

posts.summary: Gruber uses a fictional dialogue between Avie Tevanian and a QA tester to satirize Apple's dismissive attitude toward Mac OS 9 compatibility during the Jaguar release. The comedy centers on the Jaguar install disc shipping with a readme PDF that can't be double-clicked to open under OS 9, because Apple didn't bother setting HFS type/creator metadata. Each time the QA tester raises a legitimate concern, Avie dismisses it as cosmetic or deprecated. The irony is that the 'Welcome to Mac OS X' folder's readme file only works if you're already running Mac OS X. Gruber uses the format to highlight Apple's increasingly hostile posture toward its own legacy OS during the transition period.
key_points: The Jaguar install disc shipped with a readme PDF that couldn't be opened by double-clicking under Mac OS 9 due to missing HFS type/creator metadata
Apple dismissed type/creator codes as deprecated even though the install disc needed to boot from OS 9
The circular absurdity: the fix instructions would need to go in the readme, which is the file users can't open
The piece satirizes Apple's philosophy of using broken backward compatibility as implicit encouragement to upgrade to OS X
money_quotes: Set the type and creator. So it'll open in Acrobat.
Type/creator? That junk's deprecated.
It's the readme file, the first file most users will try to open, in a folder named 'Welcome to Mac OS X', but it can only be double-clicked if you're already running Mac OS X.
There's the lesson: 'Everything works better on OS X.'
We'll put it in the readme.
This isn't getting fixed, is it?
posts.key_insight: Apple's eagerness to deprecate Mac OS 9 conventions led to the absurd situation where the Jaguar install disc's own readme file was broken for the very users it was supposed to welcome to OS X.

posts.summary: This link roundup post covers three topics: the misguided Cocoa vs. Carbon debate among Mac OS X users, a positive review of the Danger Hiptop gadget, and a follow-up on RSS feed availability. Gruber highlights Slava Karpenko's essay arguing that users shouldn't care whether apps are built with Cocoa or Carbon. He also praises NetNewsWire's approach to RSS aggregation, distinguishing it from competitors not by what it does but how it does it.
key_points: The Cocoa vs. Carbon debate among Mac users is misguided — users shouldn't care about developer tooling choices
Gruber defers to actual practitioners (Slava Karpenko) over opinionated users who lack technical knowledge
NetNewsWire's appeal isn't just its function as an RSS reader but its execution and design — the 'how' matters as much as the 'what'
Community workarounds like regex scrapers filled the gap when major sites refused to offer RSS feeds
money_quotes: Users shouldn't care about the tools developers use to create software.
A vocal subset of under-informed Mac OS X users relentlessly argue that Cocoa is good, and Carbon is bad.
A programmer who works with both Cocoa and Carbon — and thus actually knows what he's talking about.
It's not just the *what* that makes NetNewsWire appealing; it's also the *how*.
posts.key_insight: What distinguishes great software isn't the technology used to build it or even what it does, but how it does it — execution and design matter more than implementation details.

posts.summary: Gruber updates his Movable Type smart quotes script, fixing a bug caused by using Perl's q() operator with parenthesis delimiters that broke on unbalanced parens (like smileys), and improving edge case handling for quotes around HTML tags. He takes advantage of new integration between Brad Choate's MTPerlScript and MTMacro plugins to create cleaner template syntax. He acknowledges a remaining limitation with leading contractions and abbreviated decades, noting that even major word processors fail at this. He also critiques Matthew Mullenweg's alternative regex-based approach for failing on valid HTML markup and single-character quoted strings.
key_points: Fixed a bug where Perl's q() delimiter broke on unbalanced parentheses, commonly triggered by smileys like :-)
Improved the quote education algorithm to handle edge cases with HTML tags like <em> inside quoted text
New plugin integration allows cleaner pseudo-tag syntax in MT templates instead of inline Perl calls
Leading contractions ('80s, 'Twas) remain unsolved, but major word processors fail at this too
Mullenweg's regex-only approach fails on valid HTML with spaces around attribute equals signs and on single-character quoted strings
money_quotes: If you tried to publish a post containing unbalanced parens — most frequently because of smileys: :-) — MT would fail to publish the article, and you'd see cryptic errors (which probably looked like downright gibberish if you don't speak Perl).
I figure if BBEdit, Word, and QuarkXPress aren't smart enough to get this right, I don't care if my script is either.
I don't believe it's possible to correctly educate quotes in HTML markup only using regular expressions.
I'm uncomfortable failing against valid markup, especially in this case, when it will prevent the tag from working at all.
Mullenweg comes close, but his implementation will do bad things with perfectly valid markup.
posts.key_insight: Properly converting straight quotes to curly quotes in HTML is a deceptively hard problem that requires parsing beyond regular expressions, and the right approach prioritizes never breaking valid markup over handling every edge case.

posts.summary: Gruber challenges the popular narrative from the O'Reilly Mac OS X Conference that Unix developers switching to Mac OS X is greatly improving the Mac software ecosystem. He argues that 'developers who use Mac OS X' and 'Mac developers' are not the same thing, pointing out that most great Mac OS X software is either long-standing Mac apps or new software written specifically for Mac OS X, not Unix ports. He cites OpenOffice and Matlab as examples of terrible Unix ports that fail to meet Mac interface standards. His conclusion is that Unix matters to Mac OS X under the hood, but the application layer must be built to Mac standards to count as real Mac software.
key_points: The conflation of 'developers who use Mac OS X' with 'Mac developers' is a fundamental error — Mac software must meet Mac interface and usability standards, not merely run on the platform.
The best new Mac OS X software (Watson, NetNewsWire, Chimera) was written specifically for the platform, while Unix ports like OpenOffice are terrible user experiences.
Most major Mac OS X apps are long-standing Mac apps that transitioned to the new OS, not newcomers from the Unix world.
Unix's value to Mac OS X is as a foundation layer, not at the application level where users interact with software.
Porting an X-Windows app to Mac OS X does not make it a Mac app — the Mac has specific, high interface standards that must be met.
money_quotes: Where this line of thinking goes wrong is by equating 'developers who use Mac OS X' with 'Mac developers'. They are not the same thing. Mac software is not simply software that runs on Mac OS X.
If I'm wrong, then answer me this: Where is all the great new Mac software ported from Unix?
Getting an X-Windows app to run on Mac OS X and calling it a 'Mac version' is like putting a Mercedes hood ornament on a Chevy Malibu and calling it 'my Benz'.
The Mac is not just another GUI. It's the GUI, the one with high standards for interface design and usability.
Apple's Human Interface Guidelines are not hints. They are rules.
Yes, Unix matters to Mac OS X. But where it matters is under the hood, not at the application level.
Open source vaporware is no better than commercial vaporware.
posts.key_insight: Unix's value to Mac OS X is as an invisible foundation — porting Unix apps to the Mac without adopting Mac interface standards produces software that betrays what makes the Mac the Mac.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights a bug in Mac OS X Jaguar's Script Editor where two save options for compiled scripts—differing only in capitalization—actually produce fundamentally different file formats (resource fork vs. data fork). Apple's Knowledge Base article incorrectly claims the two options are identical, and this misinformation has persisted for weeks. He then pivots to the broader state of AppleScript, citing John C. Welch's observation that third-party developers like Microsoft and Adobe provide better scripting support than Apple itself.
key_points: Jaguar's Script Editor bug offers two compiled script formats that differ only in capitalization but produce incompatible files—one using the traditional resource fork, the other the new data fork format
Apple's Knowledge Base article incorrectly states the two formats are identical, and this wrong information remained published for weeks
Most apps that read compiled scripts only understand the traditional resource fork format, so the data fork option causes errors
Third-party developers like Microsoft, Adobe, and AOL provide better AppleScript support than Apple's own applications
The bug itself is forgivable; publishing incorrect documentation about it is not
money_quotes: Bugs happen. That's life.
Sounds good. Too bad it's completely wrong.
Both choices write the same script data, but the resulting files are very different indeed.
The bug in Script Editor is excusable; the Knowledge Base article, however, is not. It's just plain wrong, and it's been up for over three weeks.
At exactly what point other than the OS does Apple take the lead here? Oh wait…iDVD.
Most software coming out of Apple these days has half-baked scripting support, at best.
posts.key_insight: A software bug is forgivable, but publishing confidently wrong documentation about it reveals a deeper carelessness—and Apple's neglect of AppleScript extends well beyond one bad Knowledge Base article.

posts.summary: Gruber shares a quip from Adam Engst about the O'Reilly Mac OS X Conference, where four or five attendees identified as former NeXT users. The humor lies in Engst's implication that four or five people constitutes 'a large percentage of the NeXT user population,' poking fun at how small NeXT's market share was. It's a brief link post that uses someone else's joke to make a point about NeXT's niche status and its outsized influence on Mac OS X.
key_points: NeXT had a tiny user base despite its technical significance
Mac OS X's Unix underpinnings drew former NeXT users to the platform
The joke highlights the irony that NeXT's technology became the foundation of Apple's future despite negligible market penetration
money_quotes: Four or five people even said they'd been NeXT users — it was nice to see such a large percentage of the NeXT user population represented at the conference.
posts.key_insight: NeXT's minuscule user base belied its enormous technical influence on what became Mac OS X.

posts.summary: Gruber surveys the crowded field of Mac file transfer applications and makes his case for MacSFTP as his preferred choice. Despite well-known competitors like Fetch, Transmit, and Interarchy, he favors MacSFTP for its SFTP support, clean interface, BBEdit integration, and Keychain password storage. He appreciates that it only shows remote files rather than cluttering the window with a local file browser. While noting minor UI gripes about row height and permissions display, he concludes it exemplifies the Unix philosophy of doing one thing well.
key_points: The Mac file transfer app category is extremely crowded, with both legacy Mac and NeXT apps competing alongside newcomers
MacSFTP stands out for its SFTP protocol support, simple interface, and BBEdit integration via the Edit in BBEdit protocol
Gruber prefers apps that show only remote files, using the Finder for local file management rather than a built-in dual-pane view
MacSFTP earns praise for practical details like Keychain integration, cross-platform OS 9/X support, and AppleScript scripting
The app embodies the Unix philosophy of doing one thing well, which Gruber considers the hallmark of a great utility
money_quotes: With the possible exception of crappy do-nothing text editors, the single most-crowded category for Mac utilities might be file transfer apps.
I'm not so security-obsessed that I refuse to use plain old FTP, but since my web host supports SFTP, why not?
A local file list in a file transfer app just gets in my way.
I wish the Set Permissions window showed the numeric value for the current settings (e.g. "755", "644") because that's how my feeble mind recognizes Unix-style permissions.
MacSFTP is my favorite kind of utility — it does one thing and does it well.
posts.key_insight: The best utilities are those that do one thing well, with a clear interface that stays out of the user's way rather than trying to do everything.

posts.summary: Gruber praises Unsanity's Labels X, a third-party hack that restores System 7-era file labeling to Mac OS X's Finder. He marvels at how seamlessly it integrates, adding a Labels column to list views despite no public API existing for that. He contrasts Unsanity's approach favorably with Apple's own handling of Get Info comments, which broke compatibility between OS 9 and OS X. Despite his instincts screaming that such deep system hacks can't possibly be stable, he admits they just work.
key_points: Labels X restores classic Mac OS file labeling to OS X, including a custom Finder column, despite no public API for adding columns
Unsanity's implementation maintains cross-OS compatibility with Mac OS 9 labels, while Apple's own Get Info comments broke compatibility between OS 9 and OS X
Gruber argues users shouldn't have to care about implementation details — what matters is that a small third-party developer achieved seamless integration where Apple failed
Despite using undocumented hooks and 'black magic,' Unsanity's haxies prove remarkably stable in practice
Unsanity charges only $7 per haxie, making their business model as confounding as their technical achievements
money_quotes: Is there a more aptly-named Mac developer than Unsanity?
It's so smoothly integrated into the Finder that it doesn't feel like a third-party add-on, but rather a natural part of the Finder itself.
In fact, it might even be better than if Apple itself had added labeling to the OS X Finder.
But users shouldn't have to worry about implementation details.

What matters is the end result — that software from Unsanity, a small third-party developer, integrates seamlessly with Mac OS 9 file labels; and software from Apple doesn't integrate at all with Mac OS 9 file comments.
With every haxie they release, I think to myself, this can't possibly work. Then I download, install, and prove my instincts wrong.

I know in my heart they're using black magic (a.k.a. undocumented hooks) to perform these tasks. I know that there's a non-zero probability that this could have an adverse effect on system stability. But damn if they don't just work.
More confounding than Unsanity's technical savvy is how they turn a profit charging no more than a measly $7 for these things.
posts.key_insight: A tiny third-party developer using undocumented hacks achieved better OS integration and cross-version compatibility than Apple itself, exposing how Apple neglected basic Finder functionality in the transition to OS X.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects a Microsoft-published testimonial from an alleged Mac-to-Windows switcher, arguing convincingly that the article is fabricated. He notes the lack of a byline, the use of corporate marketing language like 'messaging and collaboration client,' and the absurdity of the switcher praising features (Office, Internet Explorer, multi-user accounts) that already exist on the Mac. He debunks the $450 price difference claim with real pricing data and marvels that Microsoft couldn't articulate a single genuine advantage of switching to Windows. The article was later confirmed as fake when the author's photo was found in a stock image archive.
key_points: The Microsoft 'switcher' testimonial contains corporate marketing language no real person would use, such as calling Outlook a 'messaging and collaboration client'
The alleged switcher praises Office, Internet Explorer, and multi-user accounts as Windows advantages — all of which already exist on the Mac
The claimed $450 laptop price advantage is debunked with real Dell vs. iBook pricing showing only a $50 difference for comparable specs
Despite having real advantages to cite (gaming, browsing speed, software availability), the fabricated article fails to mention any of them
Microsoft later removed the article after the author's photo was found in a stock image archive, confirming it was manufactured
money_quotes: Does anyone outside of Microsoft's tin-eared marketing department describe Outlook as a 'messaging and collaboration client'?
Geez, if only there were a version of Office for the Mac.
Geez, if only there were a version of Internet Explorer for the Mac. This concept of naming and organizing my Favorites sounds very appealing.
What's so odd about the article is that it doesn't contain a single actual advantage to switching to Windows.
Whether or not you like Apple's Switch campaign, at least Apple's switchers sound genuine.
It never ceases to amaze me that a company so spectacularly successful as Microsoft could be so utterly ham-fisted in its marketing. They can't buy credibility.
Can you imagine a single Mac user reading this article and thinking, 'Hey, that sounds great. Maybe I should switch to Windows.'
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's fake switcher testimonial is so poorly crafted that it praises features already available on the Mac, revealing a marketing department so disconnected it can't even articulate genuine advantages of its own platform.

posts.summary: Gruber investigates the Microsoft 'Switcher' fiasco, where Microsoft published a fake testimonial from a supposed Mac-to-Windows convert, complete with a stock photo from Getty Images. The AP tracked down a PR employee named Valerie Mallinson as the likely author, but Gruber digs deeper, finding that the AP's detective work simply involved examining metadata in a Word template linked from the page. He argues Microsoft's real scandal isn't the bad marketing but the systematic fabrication of customer testimonials, noting that even after being caught, Microsoft doubled down by claiming the author was 'an actual customer' — when she was a paid PR contractor. Gruber points to another suspicious Microsoft Insider article, purportedly by a seventh grader, that reads like marketing copy. He concludes that fabricating testimonials appears to be standard operating procedure at Microsoft.
key_points: The AP's investigation was less impressive than reported — they simply found Mallinson's name in Word file metadata from a submission template, not from the article itself
Microsoft's damage control made things worse: they admitted to bad marketing but maintained the fiction that a real customer wrote the article, when the author was a paid PR contractor
The real story isn't that Microsoft responded to Apple's Switch campaign, but that the testimonial was entirely fabricated — 'Bad marketing is one thing. Bald-faced lying is another.'
Gruber identifies another likely fake testimonial on Microsoft's site, supposedly written by a seventh grader but filled with marketing buzzwords
When Gruber called the PR firm Wes Rataushk and Associates to ask about Mallinson, they denied she worked there and hung up on him
money_quotes: Bad marketing is one thing. Bald-faced lying is another.
In what world does a person getting paid to write Microsoft PR qualify as an 'actual customer'?
The real story is that at Microsoft — indisputably one of the most powerful, most successful corporations in the world — it is standard operating procedure to fabricate customer testimonials.
Caught in a lie, Microsoft stupidly chose to lie again. This is the exact sort of situation 'No comment' was made for.

Apple's Switch campaign is a big, slick, multi-million dollar advertising campaign. Microsoft's switcher article was one measly web page, and a poor one at that. What makes this story interesting isn't that article existed at all, but that it wasn't even true.
The Daring Fireball is most definitely not an anti-Microsoft web site. I'm not opposed to Windows — I'm opposed to blatant lying.
Just between you and me, Wes Rataushk and Associates doesn't exactly seem to be the world's best PR firm.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's fake switcher testimonial wasn't just bad marketing — it revealed a pattern of fabricating customer stories, and their cover-up attempt was worse than the original lie.

posts.summary: Gruber provides two brief updates related to Microsoft's controversial 'Switcher' ad campaign. He announces a radio appearance on Your Mac Life to discuss the fiasco, and shares a link revealing that Microsoft's supposed Mac-to-PC switcher, Valerie Mallinson, has connections that undermine her credibility as a genuine convert. The post is a quick follow-up adding evidence to the growing embarrassment around Microsoft's astroturfed switching campaign.
key_points: Gruber was invited to discuss the Microsoft switcher ad controversy on Your Mac Life radio
Evidence surfaced that Valerie Mallinson, presented by Microsoft as a genuine Mac-to-PC switcher, was not who she appeared to be
The post contributes to the broader narrative of Microsoft's botched attempt to counter Apple's 'Switch' campaign with fabricated testimonials
money_quotes: Two tidbits regarding the recent Microsoft unpleasantness.
Just in case you're holding out any hope whatsoever that Valerie Mallinson is actually a recent Mac-to-PC switcher who hasn't yet tried a Pocket PC, read this story and search for her name.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's fake switcher campaign continued to unravel as evidence mounted that their supposed converts were not genuine.

posts.summary: Gruber systematically dismantles an eWeek report claiming Apple would release a journaling file system called 'Elvis' in a minor OS X 10.2.2 update. He argues the article's technical claims are nonsensical — you can't layer journaling on top of HFS+ without creating an entirely new file system, and Apple would never debut something so fundamental in a point release. He further undermines the story by documenting co-author Matthew Rothenberg's pattern of publishing inaccurate rumors and then retroactively claiming vindication when vaguely related announcements occur. Gruber makes specific predictions about what will actually happen, staking his credibility on the line.
key_points: The eWeek claim that journaling can be layered atop HFS+ as a toggleable option is technically nonsensical — journaling is fundamental to a file system's format, not an add-on.
Apple has only introduced three new disk formats since 1984, making a new file system in a minor point release historically implausible.
Co-author Rothenberg has a documented pattern of publishing vague rumors, then crowing about being 'confirmed' when loosely related announcements appear.
The claim that Apple seeded a developer build without the Elvis enhancements contradicts common sense — a new file system would be the feature most needing widespread testing.
Gruber makes four specific, falsifiable predictions and explicitly offers to eat crow if wrong, modeling intellectual honesty in tech commentary.
money_quotes: It's bullshit detection week here at Daring Fireball.
You can't just turn HFS+ into a journaling file system with a magic layer of pixie dust.
To change a disk from one file system to another, you need to reformat it. What eWeek is claiming simply isn't possible.
The whole point of a journaling file system is that the journaling data is a fundamental part of the format itself.
Nor is it plausible that Apple would debut a brand-new file system in a minor OS update.
Rothenberg will claim he was right, but that Apple executives pulled the new feature at the last minute.
My advice is not to believe rumors that don't come with attributed quotes or credible screenshots.
I don't have any sources within Apple. Nor do I have a beta copy of 10.2.2. I've got nothing but my own speculation.
posts.key_insight: Technical literacy and source credibility analysis are the best defenses against rumor-mongering in tech journalism.

posts.summary: Gruber issues a correction to his earlier dismissal of eWeek's report about Apple adding a journaling file system to Mac OS X 10.2.2. He had claimed it was impossible to add journaling without reformatting a disk, but readers pointed out that Linux's ext3 file system does exactly that — it's compatible with ext2 and can store its journal in a single file. Gruber acknowledges the technical error but maintains his broader skepticism that the feature will actually ship in the 10.2.2 update.
key_points: Gruber previously argued that adding journaling to an existing file system without reformatting was technically impossible
Linux's ext3 file system disproves both of his technical claims — it's backward-compatible with ext2 and can write the journal to a single file
Despite conceding the technical error, Gruber stands by his overall skepticism about the eWeek report
money_quotes: So I take back the bit about it being "impossible", but I stand by the rest of my argument.
I don't think this is going to appear in 10.2.2.
What eWeek is claiming simply isn't possible.
The whole point of a journaling file system is that the journaling data is a fundamental part of the format itself.
posts.key_insight: Gruber demonstrates intellectual honesty by publicly correcting his technical error while maintaining his broader editorial judgment about the rumor's plausibility.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes the 2002 dispute between Apple and IDG over Macworld Expo, arguing that Apple has rational reasons for pulling out: the shows are enormously expensive, force product announcements on a rigid schedule rather than when products are ready, and are controlled by IDG rather than Apple. He dismantles the popular narrative that Apple needs the Expos for publicity, arguing instead that Steve Jobs is what makes the keynotes newsworthy, not the event itself. He portrays IDG chief Charlie Greco's threat to bar Apple from future shows as delusional, since Apple could easily create its own announcement venues, particularly through its retail stores.
key_points: Macworld Expos cost Apple over $10 million per show while giving them no control over the events they essentially bankroll
The fixed January/July schedule hurts Apple by suppressing sales beforehand and forcing announcements on an artificial timeline rather than when products are ready
The keynotes are special because of Steve Jobs, not because of the Expo — IDG should be thankful for him, not the reverse
Apple could easily replace Expos with its own events, including simulcasts to Apple Stores, at lower cost and on its own schedule
IDG's threat to bar Apple from San Francisco is self-destructive — a Macworld without Apple is like an airport without airlines
money_quotes: The Macworld keynotes are not special events which Apple should be thankful for. They are special events because of Steve Jobs, and IDG should be thankful for him.
This is like being faced by a gunman, and threatening to shoot yourself first.
Apple is not merely the biggest company in the Mac industry. It is the hub around which the entire industry revolves.
A Macworld Expo without Apple is like an airport without any airlines. The duty-free shop isn't going to keep the lights turned on.
It's absurd to think that Apple should blindly allow IDG to dictate the terms and venue of Macworld Expo.
Suffice it to say, Apple pays a lot of fucking money for these shows.
Remember the Macworld keynote addresses prior to Jobs's return? Neither do I.
posts.key_insight: The Macworld Expo's value proposition is fundamentally asymmetric: Apple provides the content, the star power, and the money, while IDG controls the venue, the schedule, and the terms — making Apple's eventual departure not ego-driven but economically rational.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights a Switch commercial spoof by Pete Green, distinguishing it from other parodies that mock Mac users. He argues this particular spoof works because it speaks directly to Mac users rather than ridiculing them. The brief post endorses the video as genuinely hilarious and resonant because it captures something true about the Mac user experience.
key_points: Most Switch commercial parodies fail because they rely on mocking Mac users
Pete Green's spoof succeeds by speaking directly to Mac users instead
The humor works because it reflects a truth Mac users recognize
money_quotes: This one by Pete Green, which is fucking piss-your-pants hilarious, speaks directly to Mac users.
It's only funny because it's true.
posts.key_insight: The best humor about a community comes from understanding it, not mocking it from the outside.

posts.summary: Gruber describes his quest to make Daring Fireball's RSS 0.91 feed pass the newly released RSS Validator by Mark Pilgrim and Sam Ruby. The validator found three errors in his Movable Type-generated feed, which he took personally since his HTML and CSS already validated. Rather than switching to RSS 2.0 as the validator suggested, he chose to fix the 0.91 template because he values its simplicity. The core issue was a timezone offset format incompatibility, which he solved by writing a small Movable Type plug-in.
key_points: The new RSS Validator by Pilgrim and Ruby found errors in Movable Type's default RSS 0.91 template output
Gruber prefers RSS 0.91 over 1.0 and 2.0 specifically because of its simplicity
The main validation failure was Movable Type's timezone offset format including a colon (-05:00) which violated RFC 822
He wrote a small MT plug-in to output RFC 822-compliant timezone offsets and shared it publicly
money_quotes: My HTML validates, my CSS validates, so damn it, my RSS ought to validate.
All things considered, I like RSS 0.91 because it is really, really simple; RSS 1.0 and 2.0 are not.
I shouldn't have taken this personally, because my RSS feed was automatically generated by Movable Type's default RSS 0.91 template, but take it personally I did.
posts.key_insight: When your tools don't produce valid output, the right response is to fix the tools rather than abandon the standard you prefer for its simplicity.

posts.summary: Gruber reports that Apple's QuickTime 6.0.2 installer for Mac OS X contains a Perl script using deprecated uppercase comparison operators (EQ/NE) that fail on Perl 5.8. While the installer works on stock Jaguar with Perl 5.6, users who upgraded to Perl 5.8 following Apple's own instructions are affected. Gruber argues the bug is inexcusable because using the standard '-w' switch would have flagged the deprecated syntax immediately. He connects this to Apple's pattern of shipping poorly-written installer scripts, referencing the iTunes installer that erased drives the previous year.
key_points: Apple's QuickTime installer uses deprecated Perl operators (EQ/NE) that break on Perl 5.8, which Apple itself tells users how to install
The bug would have been caught trivially by using Perl's standard '-w' warning switch, a basic best practice taught in every beginner tutorial
Apple has a pattern of shipping careless installer scripts, previously causing the iTunes installer to erase user drives
The contradiction of Apple publishing a how-to for upgrading Perl while shipping code incompatible with that upgrade shows internal disconnect
money_quotes: The mistake is inexcusable, for several reasons.
One of the first lessons in every Perl beginners tutorial is that you should always start your scripts with the "-w" switch.
I simply can't emphasize strongly enough just how boneheaded it is not to use "-w" — for *any* script, let alone one which is going to be executed by *hundreds of thousands* of your customers.
The script probably ought to be running under the "use strict" pragma as well, but "-w" is all they needed to flag this mistake.
You'd think after last year's iTunes installer fiasco, which was caused by a poorly-written shell script, Apple would have learned something.
posts.key_insight: Apple's failure to use basic Perl best practices in a widely-distributed installer script reveals a troubling carelessness with code quality, especially given their history of installer bugs.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights Brad Choate's argument that the WINE project succeeding in running Windows apps on Linux would ultimately harm the Linux platform. The logic is simple: if you want to run Windows software, just use Windows. Gruber extends this by dismissing Windows software as largely terrible, and takes a swipe at cross-platform Mac software that compromises on native interface conventions.
key_points: Platform compatibility layers like WINE undermine the native platform by removing incentive to build native software
Windows software is dismissed as overwhelmingly low quality
Cross-platform software that ignores native UI conventions is equally problematic on Mac
money_quotes: For running Windows applications, there is a perfectly good OS for doing that: Windows. I'd rather run Linux software on Linux.
Not to mention that the vast majority of Windows software is craptacular, and the remainder is mediocre, at best.
There's also some Mac software wearing funny widgets.
posts.key_insight: Running another platform's software through a compatibility layer weakens your own platform's ecosystem and imports inferior software in the process.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Matthew Thomas's essay about UI cruft, pushing back on the arguments that Save and Quit commands are anachronisms. He defends explicit saving by noting that auto-save creates problems with file naming, location, and temporary documents. He concedes that automatic backup files for crash recovery would be useful, but rejects removing Save as a concept. On the Quit command, he argues that experienced users benefit from keeping applications running without open windows, and that accidental quitting is a non-problem on the Mac due to good UI design conventions.
key_points: Auto-save forces bad defaults on users who organize files deliberately — there's no universal default folder or naming scheme that works for all documents
Many documents are temporary and never intended to be saved, so auto-saving creates cleanup burden rather than reducing work
A better compromise would be silent automatic backup files for crash recovery, hidden from the user
Keeping applications running without open windows is a deliberate power-user workflow, not a confusing implementation detail
The Mac's UI conventions (menu placement, Cmd-Q consistency, unsaved-changes prompts) already prevent accidental quitting
money_quotes: Personally, I nearly always despise applications that offer "automatic" anything. The effort needed to override the "auto" behavior is usually more of a pain in the ass than the original task the feature is trying to automate.
I don't save my Perl scripts to the same folder as my grocery list.
If you don't choose the names for your own files, how do you identify them when you try to reopen them later on?
It would be annoying if I had to manually clean up a bunch of files that a too-smart-for-its-own-good application created for me automatically.
I can't buy the argument that the Save command, as a general concept, is nothing more than a crufty anachronism.
I'd be pissed if Photoshop quit automatically when I closed the last open document.
The only people confused by this concept are novices; for everyone else, it is often useful to maintain manual control over when an application quits.
If that doesn't happen, you're using a shitty application.
posts.key_insight: Explicit user control over saving and quitting isn't UI cruft — it's a feature that respects how experienced users actually work, and removing it would create more problems than it solves.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights a concerning Orrin Hatch amendment buried in the Homeland Security bill that would make it easier for ISPs to disclose email communications without a warrant. He frames this as a post-election consequence, with Republicans now empowered to further erode digital privacy protections that were already weakened by the Patriot Act. The sardonic reference to Phil Zimmerman — creator of PGP encryption — underscores the widening gap between government surveillance ambitions and privacy advocacy.
key_points: An Orrin Hatch amendment hidden in the Homeland Security bill would let ISPs disclose emails without warrants
This further erodes privacy protections already weakened by the Patriot Act
The Republican election victory enables passage of legislation hostile to digital privacy
The Phil Zimmerman reference highlights the tension between encryption advocacy and government surveillance expansion
money_quotes: I take it the Republicans won't be conferring any awards on Phil Zimmerman any time soon.
posts.key_insight: Election outcomes have immediate, concrete consequences for digital privacy, as victorious politicians waste no time burying surveillance expansions in must-pass legislation.

posts.summary: Gruber revisits his earlier dismissal of an eWeek report claiming Mac OS X 10.2.2 would introduce a journaling file system. He acknowledges that journaling did appear in the Server version of 10.2.2, scoring a point for eWeek. However, he argues the feature is merely a journaling extension atop the existing HFS+ file system rather than a truly new file system, and that eWeek's breathless description of it as a surprise corporate data feature was overblown. He concludes by drawing a distinction between being technically right and being accurate.
key_points: Mac OS X 10.2.2 did include journaling for HFS+, but only exposed in the Server edition's Disk Utility, contradicting Gruber's earlier dismissal
The journaling feature is an extension to existing HFS+, not a new file system in the traditional sense, similar to Linux's ext3 relationship to ext2
Apple's own documentation was inconsistent in spelling and lacked any substantive explanation of what the journaling feature actually does
eWeek's original description of the feature as providing 'a new, historical view of their data' significantly overstated what the journaling extension delivers
money_quotes: So, while I'm forced to admit Rothenberg and dePlume were right, that doesn't mean they were accurate.
So is there a brand-new journaling file system in 10.2.2 or not? The answer depends on what your definition of "file system" is.
In response, I went out on a limb and called eWeek's article bullshit.
The inconsistent spelling and capitalization is Apple's, not mine.
One problem is that there doesn't yet seem to be any documentation detailing exactly what the benefits to HFS+ journaling are.
posts.key_insight: Being technically correct about a fact doesn't make your reporting accurate when the framing and characterization are misleading.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights Apple's announcement of journaling support for HFS+, drawing attention to Apple's careful wording. He notes that Apple deliberately avoids calling it a 'journaling file system,' instead describing it as a journaling feature added to HFS+. He also points out that this capability is limited to Mac OS X Server, not the consumer version of the OS.
key_points: Apple added journaling capabilities to HFS+ but carefully avoided calling it a journaling file system
The distinction in terminology suggests Apple views journaling as a feature layered onto HFS+ rather than a fundamental redesign
The journaling feature was initially limited to Mac OS X Server only
money_quotes: Apple does not refer to it as a 'journaling file system', but rather as 'a robust new journaling feature for the Mac OS Extended (HFS+) file system'.
posts.key_insight: Apple's precise language around HFS+ journaling reveals a deliberate strategy of incrementally improving an existing file system rather than replacing it.

posts.summary: Gruber announces SmartyPants 1.0, a free Movable Type plug-in that automatically converts straight quotes to typographically correct curly quotes. It replaces his earlier, more cumbersome MTPerlScript-based solution for smart quotes. The new plug-in is easier to install, has a smarter algorithm, supports backtick-style quotes and smart ellipses, and properly ignores content inside pre and code tags. He recommends existing users of his old solution switch to SmartyPants for its improved accuracy and control.
key_points: SmartyPants is a free Movable Type plug-in that handles typographic quote transformation automatically
It significantly improves on Gruber's previous MTPerlScript-based smart quotes solution with easier installation and a smarter algorithm
The plug-in adds support for backtick-style quotes, smart ellipses, and properly ignores content in pre/code tags
This is an early example of Gruber's commitment to typographic correctness on the web, a thread that would later lead to Markdown
money_quotes: Remember my instructions for generating smart quotes in Movable Type using Brad Choate's MTPerlScript plug-in? Forget about that. I've got something better now.
It's easier, more accurate, faster, and offers you more control over which transformations occur when you publish articles.
posts.key_insight: SmartyPants represents Gruber's early dedication to bringing proper typography to web publishing, presaging his later creation of Markdown.

posts.summary: Gruber defends BBEdit 7's HTML syntax checker against Dori Smith's complaints that it produces erroneous results compared to the W3C validator. He demonstrates that BBEdit and the W3C validator find different classes of errors — BBEdit parses attribute values while the W3C validator does reference counting — and that both tools have blind spots. Smith's blog actually contains real markup errors (unencoded slashes in URL query strings) that BBEdit correctly flags but the W3C validator misses. Gruber argues the two tools complement each other, and that when either reports an error, the correct response is to fix it rather than blame the tool.
key_points: BBEdit's syntax checker and the W3C HTML validator work fundamentally differently, catching different classes of errors rather than disagreeing with each other
The W3C validator does not parse attribute values, meaning nonsensical values like width='foobar' pass validation without complaint
BBEdit does not perform reference counting, so structural errors like duplicate <body> tags go undetected
Smith's blog contained real URI encoding errors — unencoded slashes in query strings — that BBEdit correctly identified per RFC 2396
Passing a validator does not mean your markup is error-free; it only means it passed that particular validator's checks
money_quotes: Only in the mind of Dori Smith could new support for two additional web programming languages serve as evidence that Bare Bones is not listening to web developers.
She may do a lot of HTML validation, but she doesn't do it well.
Just what does it mean when your web pages pass through an HTML validator without error? Only that they've passed through a validator.
Browsers support all sorts of crappy markup.
But why leave it to chance when encoding the / in the search part will remove the ambiguity?
The shortcomings in both tools are that they both miss certain kinds of errors, not that they report bogus errors.
Rather than competing, they complement each other rather nicely, which is why I use both.
