posts.key_insight: Passing one HTML validator doesn't mean your markup is correct — different validation tools catch different classes of errors, and the proper response to a reported error is to fix it, not blame the tool.

posts.summary: Gruber endorses Nicholas Riley's criticism of the Mac OS X Finder, calling it an embarrassment to Apple and to the legacy of the original Finder. He highlights that after three major releases and years of development, the OS X Finder remains slow, buggy, and painful to use. He also points to a screenshot of a shoddy Finder dialog box with multiple UI problems, adding his own observation that the caution icon violates Apple's own Human Interface Guidelines by being misplaced.
key_points: The OS X Finder is slow, buggy, and painful despite three major releases of development
The OS X Finder dishonors the legacy of the original Finder from classic Mac OS
A single Finder dialog box contains at least seven identifiable UI problems
Apple's own Finder violates Apple's own Human Interface Guidelines for dialog layout
money_quotes: The OS X Finder is an embarrassment, not only to Apple, but to the good name of the original Finder, from the original Macintosh through Mac OS 9.
Why, after three major releases and multiple years of development, is the OS X Finder so slow, buggy and painful to use?
posts.key_insight: The OS X Finder is an embarrassment that fails to live up to the legacy of the original Finder and even violates Apple's own design guidelines.

posts.summary: Gruber endorses Steven Frank's critique of .dmg disk image files as a software distribution format on Mac OS X. Drawing on his own experience answering tech support emails for a Mac developer, he confirms that disk images are conceptually confusing for many non-technical users, even long-time Mac owners. He acknowledges that the trend of including background images with drag-to-install instructions helps, but agrees that compressed archives like StuffIt are more intuitive for most users.
key_points: Disk images (.dmg files) are conceptually confusing for non-technical Mac users, even those with years of experience
The trend of including instructional background pictures in disk image windows helps reduce confusion
Compressed archives like StuffIt are easier for most users to understand than disk images
The problem is especially acute for users who don't install new software frequently
money_quotes: The entire concept of a disk image is mighty confusing to many non-technical users, including many who have been using Macs for years, but don't install new software very often.

I think the recent trend of including a background picture in the disk image window, which instructs the user to copy the application icon to /Applications (or wherever), goes a long way to removing the confusion.
posts.key_insight: Even longtime Mac users find disk images conceptually confusing, making software installation an unnecessarily frustrating experience that better distribution formats could solve.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that the Mac OS X Finder is a fundamentally broken replacement for the classic Mac OS Finder, designed by former NeXT engineers who neither understood nor appreciated the original's spatial orientation paradigm. He traces the problem to Apple's acquisition of NeXT, where engineers with a preference for cosmetic polish over usability took control of UI decisions. The classic Finder's genius was its lack of abstraction — folders mapped one-to-one to windows, creating a direct manipulation experience unmatched by any file browser. The OS X Finder destroyed this spatial consistency, replacing it with a confusing hybrid that behaves differently depending on toolbar visibility. Gruber proposes restoring spatial orientation as the core paradigm while keeping column view as a separate browser window class. He concludes that the current Finder team is the least likely group at Apple to fix the problem.
key_points: The OS X Finder is not a sick version of the classic Finder — it's an entirely different application with a skin-deep resemblance to the original
The classic Finder's spatial orientation paradigm created a one-to-one mapping between folders and windows, eliminating abstraction and enabling direct manipulation
Former NeXT engineers brought a design philosophy of cosmetic appeal over usability, exemplified by System Preferences and iCal
Mac users asked for two things from OS X — stability and performance — and instead got stability, worse performance, and unwanted UI changes
Gruber proposes keeping column view as a separate 'Column Browser' window class while restoring spatial orientation as the Finder's fundamental paradigm
money_quotes: The classic Finder is like using your own hands. The OS X Finder is like using a joystick to control a set of robot hands — clumsy and inherently less intuitive.
But the Finder is not sick; it is dead, replaced by an entirely different application that's been patched to bear a superficial, skin-deep resemblance to the real Finder.
The hallmarks of NeXT's UI design are extravagant attention to cosmetic appeal, and nearly no attention whatsoever to actual usability.

UI design decisions at Apple are now in the hands of people who do not understand good UI design. What makes it sad is not just that Apple's standards used to be so much higher, but that there are still so many talented designers working there.
In the OS X Finder, double-clicking on a folder produces completely different results depending on the visibility state of the current window's toolbar. This is madness.
Why replace the greatest and most beloved application in the history of the Macintosh with an ill-conceived 'file browser'?
Things that look the same should act the same, in proper UI design.
If it looks better, it must be better, right? With that metric in mind, you can start to understand why the NeXTies think so highly of their own UI design skills.
posts.key_insight: The OS X Finder's fundamental failure is that it abandoned the classic Finder's spatial orientation paradigm — where every folder maps to exactly one window — in favor of a browser-style abstraction designed by NeXT engineers who prioritized cosmetic appeal over the direct manipulation that made the Mac great.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier critique of the OS X Finder, correcting his speculation about the engineering team (there's only one Finder team at Apple, responsible for both OS 9 and OS X) and amplifying the argument with supporting voices from Zeldman, Steven Frank, and Michael Tsai. He pushes back against critics who dismiss Finder complaints as mere resistance to change, arguing that the OS X Finder is genuinely slower, less consistent, and less intuitive than its predecessor. Gruber emphasizes that many professional Mac users heavily depend on the Finder as a productivity tool, unlike switchers who rarely manage files. He attributes the design regression to Apple's dissolution of its Human Interface Group, leaving programmers without the design supervision needed for consistency.
key_points: There is only one Finder team at Apple responsible for both OS 9 and OS X, but the real issue is who designed the Finder, not who coded it.
Michael Tsai argues Apple's interface problems stem from Steve Jobs dissolving the Human Interface Group, leaving no one to enforce consistent design principles.
Dismissing Finder criticism as resistance to change ignores that professional Mac users pound on the Finder daily as an essential productivity tool.
The OS 9 Finder succeeded at being simple for novices while offering power-user flexibility — the OS X Finder lost that balance.
Users conditioned by poorly designed file managers on other platforms have learned to stop managing files, so they can't see what Mac users have lost.
money_quotes: In the end, it doesn't matter who coded it. What matters is who designed it.
Programmers need HI experts in the same way that writers need editors.
If your file manager is irritating, you learn to stop managing your files.
That the OS X Finder is good enough for some does not mean it is good enough for all.
When newcomers to the Mac dismiss long-time Mac users' criticism with a bit of 'good enough for me' hand-waving, it belies their ignorance of what the Mac truly stands for.
The OS 9 Finder was appealing to newbies and experts alike. The OS X Finder is appealing to newbies but... well... I still do most of my file manipulation in Terminal.
It's true that many long-time Mac users do complain, loudly, about petty differences in Mac OS X. That doesn't mean, however, that all complaints are petty.
posts.key_insight: Dismissing power users' Finder complaints as mere resistance to change reveals ignorance of how central the Finder is to professional Mac workflows — and how much was lost when Apple dissolved the team responsible for enforcing interface consistency.

posts.summary: Gruber takes a brief break from his ongoing series about the Mac OS X Finder to enjoy a snow day. The entire post is a playful announcement that technical commentary is on hold because he's outside having fun in the snow. He includes several photos documenting the snowy day. It's a rare purely personal, lighthearted post from the early days of Daring Fireball.
key_points: Gruber pauses his Finder commentary series for a personal snow day
The post is almost entirely photos with minimal text, a rare format for Daring Fireball
Shows the informal, personal tone of early blogging culture
money_quotes: Continuing commentary regarding the Mac OS X Finder has been postponed, due to a tremendously fun pile of snow.
posts.key_insight: Even the most dedicated tech commentator knows when to set aside the keyboard and go play in the snow.

posts.summary: Gruber recommends Quinn, a Tetris clone for OS X discovered by Brent Simmons. He praises its faithful gameplay, smooth animation, and configurable controls. He highlights the developer's attention to detail, particularly the decision to remove the Help menu entirely rather than leave a useless placeholder. The post is a brief but enthusiastic endorsement of a well-crafted freeware Mac game.
key_points: Quinn is a faithful Tetris clone for OS X with excellent gameplay, smooth animation, and configurable keystrokes
The game includes an Undo feature that compensates for accidental mis-drops, a practical if technically cheating concession
The developer removed the Help menu entirely rather than leaving a vestigial menu item with a useless dialog — a detail Gruber finds admirable
Good software design means knowing what to leave out, including unnecessary UI elements
money_quotes: Quinn is faithful to the original game — as Brent writes: no dumb "innovations" or anything. Right on.

Mr. Härtel had the common decency to remove the Help menu entirely, rather than leave behind a single vestigial menu item that brings up an idiotic "Help is not implemented for this application" dialog box.
It's just a great little Mac app.
posts.key_insight: Good software craftsmanship shows in the small details, like removing a Help menu rather than leaving behind a useless placeholder.

posts.summary: Gruber takes aim at Leander Kahney, Wired News's Apple beat writer, for his 'Cult of Mac' series that reduces Apple coverage to freak-show stories about obsessive fans rather than substantive product journalism. He dismantles a quoted claim from marketer Marc Gobe that Apple's survival depends entirely on branding and has 'nothing to do with products,' arguing that any competent journalist would have challenged such an absurd assertion. Gruber connects this to a broader media trend of treating all opinions as equally valid and refusing to call out obvious nonsense. He characterizes Kahney's approach as deliberate provocation designed to generate angry links rather than illuminate anything. Rather than sending hate mail, Gruber suggests simply ignoring Kahney entirely.
key_points: Wired News's entire Apple coverage consists of Kahney's 'Cult of Mac' series about eccentric fans, with no serious product or technology journalism
The claim that Apple survives on branding alone and 'it's got nothing to do with products' is trivially disprovable, yet Kahney lets it stand unchallenged
Modern media's false balance — treating all opinions as equally valid — is a failure of journalism, not a virtue
Kahney's provocation strategy is self-reinforcing: the more asinine the articles, the more widely they're linked
The most effective response to deliberately inflammatory coverage is to ignore it rather than generate the outrage it feeds on
money_quotes: Calling bullshit "bullshit" is not bias. It is journalism.

This passage exemplifies not only what is wrong with Kahney's so-called journalism, but what is wrong with so much of the American news media today. It's not journalism at all. It's just button-pushing — stirring up controversy and conflict for the sole purpose of drawing attention.

There is a disconcerting trend in the modern media which holds that all opinions are equally valid, and that it is not appropriate for ostensibly unbiased journalists to declare which opinions are right, and which are wrong.
If the iPod's success is due solely to branding, can you name another portable MP3 player that is just as good as the iPod?
It is obvious that Kahney's goal is not to illuminate, but rather to instigate.
Mr. Kahney's stories are never actually about the Macintosh, or anything important related to it, but instead are about the type of kooks who name their kids "Quadra" and "Centris".
What matters is that he is a muck-raker, a yellow journalist, and yet he is a staff writer for an otherwise respectable news publication.
posts.key_insight: Journalism that refuses to challenge obviously false claims in the name of balance isn't objectivity — it's abdication of the journalist's core responsibility.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to misconceptions about his criticism of the Mac OS X Finder, clarifying that he doesn't think the OS 9 Finder was perfect, only that it was good enough to build upon rather than discard. He argues that the OS X Finder's browser metaphor solves a problem most Mac users didn't have, and that any upgrade to a widely-used application must prioritize keeping existing users happy. He contends that the Finder is far more than a file manager for most Mac users — it essentially is the Mac OS to them — and that the radical redesign has left the majority of long-time users feeling alienated. He concludes that simply redesigning the Finder to appeal to classic Mac users would make the entire OS X experience feel familiar to millions.
key_points: The OS X Finder's browser metaphor was a solution to a problem most Mac users didn't have — the 'too many windows' complaint was not widely shared.
When upgrading a widely-used application, keeping existing users happy must be goal number one, regardless of how well-intentioned the changes are.
For most Mac users, the Finder isn't just a file manager — it is the interface to the computer as a whole, making radical changes to it especially disorienting.
Defenders of the OS X Finder tend to be power users with Windows/Unix experience who underestimate how central the Finder is to traditional Mac users.
Most complaints about Mac OS X's interface are really complaints about the Finder specifically, and fixing it alone would transform the entire OS X experience.
money_quotes: Moving to Mac OS X is a great opportunity for developers to implement new features and new ideas, but it's not an excuse to pull the rug out from under existing users.
The browser metaphor underlying the OS X Finder is a solution to a problem that most Mac users didn't have.
When you're designing an upgrade to a widely-used application, goal number one must be to keep your existing users happy.
An upgrade that doesn't appeal to existing users is never a good idea, no matter how well intentioned the changes.
The beauty of the Macintosh is that you don't need to understand how a computer works in order to maintain control over your computer; you only need to understand how the Mac works.
If you're not a computer nerd, it is neither easy nor fun to learn to use the new Finder. It is discouraging. There is a sense of 'Jeez, what the hell is going on here?'
There is a strong sentiment among many long-time Mac users that Mac OS X is not for them. The excitement is all about how it's the best Unix ever.
posts.key_insight: The OS X Finder's radical departure from spatial orientation was a betrayal of the users who needed it most — the non-technical majority for whom the Finder was not merely a file manager but the entire interface to their computer.

posts.summary: Gruber discloses his prior employment at Bare Bones Software before publishing a series of BBEdit-related articles. He worked there from October 2000 through July 2002 and wants readers to know his opinions are entirely his own. He clarifies the causal direction: his fandom of BBEdit led him to work at Bare Bones, not the other way around.
key_points: Gruber proactively discloses his former employment at Bare Bones Software before writing about their products
He draws a clear distinction between corporate opinion and personal opinion
He argues his appreciation for BBEdit preceded and motivated his employment, not the reverse
money_quotes: Having worked for them is not the reason why I'm such a fan of their software; rather, it's because I like their software that I wanted to work for them.
posts.key_insight: Genuine enthusiasm for a product is best demonstrated by transparency about potential conflicts of interest, and the causal direction of fandom matters.

posts.summary: Gruber admits he planned to review BBEdit 7.0 but procrastinated until Michael Tsai published a comprehensive review for ATPM, rendering his own unnecessary. He praises Tsai's review as a model of what software reviews should be: detailed, researched, accurate, and written by a domain expert. The post is essentially a recommendation to read Tsai's piece instead.
key_points: Gruber defers to Michael Tsai's BBEdit 7.0 review in ATPM as the definitive take
He argues good software reviews require genuine expertise with the application being reviewed
Procrastination is humorously framed as a problem-solving strategy
money_quotes: Procrastination eventually solves all problems.
This is what every software review should be — detailed, researched, accurate, and most importantly, written by someone who has expertise with the application.
posts.key_insight: The best software reviews come from writers with deep expertise in the application, not generalist tech journalists.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to John C. Welch's three-part defense of the Mac OS X Finder, acknowledging Welch's arguments as reasonable but unpersuasive. He rejects Welch's core premise that long-time Mac users are simply resistant to any change, arguing instead that the OS X Finder is unpopular with nearly all veteran users. Gruber sides with Michael Tsai's position that satisfying both switchers and long-time users is achievable. He criticizes Apple for stubbornly sticking with an unpopular design despite two years of widespread criticism, comparing their stance to NeXT's failed strategy of waiting for users to come around.
key_points: Welch's defense of the OS X Finder hinges on dismissing critics as change-averse cranks, which Gruber argues mischaracterizes the breadth of dissatisfaction
The OS X Finder is unpopular with almost all long-time Mac users, not just a vocal minority of habitual complainers
Tsai's insight that there is little actual conflict between user groups and that Apple could satisfy both is presented as the correct framing
Apple's two-year refusal to address widespread Finder criticism suggests a stubborn belief that users will eventually appreciate the design
Gruber draws a pointed parallel between Apple's Finder strategy and NeXT's failed approach of waiting for the market to recognize their brilliance
money_quotes: But much of his counter-argument hinges on the idea that long-time Mac users are complaining about the Finder simply because they're cranks who complain every time something changes in the Mac OS.
The Mac OS X Finder is unpopular with almost *all* long-time Mac users.
Apple's goal should be to make a Finder that pleases both recent switchers and long-time Mac users.

But their response after more than two years of widespread criticism indicates a dogged determination to stick with an unpopular design, as though they believe someday soon millions of Mac users will suddenly snap out of it and recognize the brilliance of the OS X Finder.
Which sounds suspicously similar to the plan NeXT clung to with their operating system.
posts.key_insight: Apple's stubborn refusal to address widespread Finder criticism mirrors NeXT's fatal mistake of assuming users would eventually appreciate a design they consistently rejected.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews three Apple developer tool releases: Project Builder's support for external editors like BBEdit, a rewritten Script Editor 2.0 beta, and the new GUI Scripting capability for AppleScript. He's enthusiastic about the first two but deeply ambivalent about GUI Scripting, which allows AppleScript to control application interfaces by manipulating visual elements rather than through proper scripting dictionaries. While acknowledging GUI Scripting's usefulness for QA testing and automating unscriptable apps, he argues it's a fundamentally fragile approach that depends on exact UI layouts and fears it will give lazy developers an excuse not to implement proper scripting support.
key_points: Project Builder finally supports external editors like BBEdit and emacs, which matters because good dev tools make happier programmers who write better software.
Script Editor 2.0 is a major rewrite that adds basic features the decade-old original lacked, like Find and multiple undo, though it's no replacement for the pro-level Script Debugger.
GUI Scripting lets AppleScript control any application's visual interface, which is a radical departure from AppleScript's traditional model of sending Apple events to internal scripting interfaces.
GUI Scripting is inherently fragile because scripts depend on exact UI layouts and refer to controls by position rather than by name, meaning any interface change breaks the script.
The real danger of GUI Scripting is that it provides cover for developers who don't want to build proper scripting dictionaries into their applications.
money_quotes: You probably don't use developer tools like Project Builder, but if you use a Mac, you're no more than one degree of separation away from someone who does. Good development tools make programmers happy, and happy programmers write better software.
The old Script Editor has been around for at least 10 years, and still doesn't have a Find command. In a programming editor.
The new Script Editor is like iMovie; Script Debugger is like Final Cut Pro. The old Script Editor is like trying to make a flip-book-movie using a pencil and wet paper.
Holy crap is right.
That sucks. It means your script is utterly dependent on the exact layout of the visual interface.
If Apple's going to acknowledge that you might want to change the settings for your mouse via AppleScript, they ought to allow you to do it the right way.
What I fear, however, is that GUI Scripting will provide cover to lazy and ignorant developers who do not wish to provide genuine scripting interfaces to their applications.
posts.key_insight: GUI Scripting is a seductive hack that solves a real problem — unscriptable apps — but risks becoming an excuse for developers to never build proper scripting interfaces in the first place.

posts.summary: Gruber satirizes Jef Raskin's reputation for claiming credit for numerous computing innovations. The post is a mock top-10 list mixing Raskin's actual claims (inventing the GUI, the Macintosh project) with absurd ones (binary arithmetic, Boolean logic, Slurpees). Gruber takes a specific shot at Raskin's LEAP text navigation system, pointing out its counterintuitive design where arrow keys behave inconsistently. The humor works by placing real claims alongside obviously ridiculous ones, implying Raskin's actual claims are equally inflated.
key_points: Satirizes Jef Raskin's tendency to claim credit for foundational computing concepts he didn't invent
Specifically critiques the LEAP navigation system's unintuitive design where arrow keys behave inconsistently
Mixes Raskin's real claims (GUI, mouse, Mac project contributions) with absurd ones to deflate his credibility
The title 'Rascal' is a playful dig built from Raskin's name
money_quotes: The graphical user interface.
More humane spelling of "Jeff".

"LEAP" system of text navigation, which is so intuitive that the left and right arrow keys do something completely different (move the insertion point) than the up and down arrow keys (which scroll the view).
Boolean logic.
posts.key_insight: Gruber uses absurdist humor to puncture Jef Raskin's inflated claims of credit for computing innovations, while landing a substantive critique of his LEAP interface design.

posts.summary: Gruber compares BBEdit 7's new built-in Sort Lines and Process Duplicate Lines plug-ins with Craig Maynard's newly revived LineSort plug-in, which has been released as free and open-source software. He explains how the shift from C plug-ins to Perl filters and AppleScripts reduced the BBEdit plug-in ecosystem, and how LineSort's absence on Mac OS X was keenly felt. He analyzes the UI tradeoffs between Bare Bones's modal dialogs (keyboard-driven) and LineSort's floating palette (mouse-driven), and compares their AppleScript interfaces. He concludes the situation is a win for everyone, since LineSort is now free and works across Mac OS versions.
key_points: The BBEdit plug-in ecosystem declined not from lack of interest but because Perl filters and AppleScripts became easier alternatives to writing C plug-ins.
Modal dialogs have underappreciated advantages over floating palettes, particularly keyboard-driven shortcuts that don't conflict with menu commands.
LineSort's UI is less polished but more straightforward, combining sort and duplicate removal in a single action versus BBEdit's two separate dialogs.
BBEdit's AppleScript syntax for sorting is more verbose but more consistent with the application's overall scripting model.
The competition between free LineSort and BBEdit 7's built-in tools is a genuine win-win, with no revenue conflict since LineSort went freeware.
money_quotes: I don't think the relative dearth of recent plug-ins is because there is any less developer interest in BBEdit — I think it's simply that Perl filters and AppleScripts have become so much easier to write.
Modal dialogs get a bad rap from many users, so you might find it curious that Bare Bones would choose to use them for new plug-ins.

These shortcuts don't conflict with normal menu command shortcuts, because most menu commands are disabled when a modal dialog is open. A floating palette such as LineSort's can only be driven using the mouse.
I definitely wouldn't describe LineSort's interface as pretty, but it seems more straightforward, mainly because it doesn't offer as many checkboxes as do Bare Bones's plug-in dialogs.
There's no reason not to declare the situation a win for everyone.
posts.key_insight: The decline of BBEdit's C plug-in ecosystem wasn't a loss but a natural evolution, as scripting languages made text manipulation tasks easier — and when both built-in tools and free third-party alternatives coexist, users win.

posts.summary: Gruber offers his obligatory Macworld San Francisco 2003 predictions, covering new Macs, iPods, AirPort, and a tablet computer. He argues the most obvious announcement should be new computers, noting Apple is still fundamentally a computer maker despite its peripheral products. He predicts new professional desktops with FireWire 2 and USB2 support, which would naturally justify the OS X-only boot requirement. He expresses cautious optimism about video-capable iPods, expects faster AirPort hardware, and dismisses tablet computer rumors.
key_points: Apple is still a computer manufacturer first — media players and wireless base stations are merely peripherals
New hardware technologies like FireWire 2 and USB2 would make the OS X-only boot requirement a natural progression rather than a painful limitation
The iPod proved $500 gadgets can sell, but the Newton showed $1,000 gadgets don't — pricing will determine the next iPod's success
Tablet computers appeal more to journalists looking for something new to write about than to actual consumers
money_quotes: FCC regulations require Macintosh-oriented web sites to publish predictions of upcoming Apple announcements prior to each Macworld Expo.
You may recall that Apple is still a computer manufacturer. Media players and wireless base stations are merely peripherals.
If you want great new hardware technologies, you want Mac OS X.
The iPod proved that $500 gadgets can sell. The Newton showed that $1,000 gadgets don't.
Journalists like the idea of tablet computers because they're a new concept, and so they're something to write about. But do people actually want them?
Then again, there must be some purpose for Inkwell.
posts.key_insight: Apple's most important announcement at any Expo should be new computers, because despite the growing halo of peripherals, Apple remains fundamentally a computer company whose hardware drives its software story.

posts.summary: Gruber promotes his article for O'Reilly's Mac DevCenter about scripting rectangular selections in BBEdit 7. The piece highlights how BBEdit 7's AppleScript support allows creating 'rectangular' selections that aren't actually rectangular. He uses a screenshot to demonstrate this quirky capability, finding amusement in the naming contradiction.
key_points: Gruber wrote a technical article for O'Reilly's Mac DevCenter on BBEdit 7's rectangular selections via AppleScript
BBEdit 7's AppleScript interface allows creating 'rectangular' selections that are not actually rectangular in shape
The post serves as a brief promotional pointer to the full article published elsewhere
money_quotes: I put quotes around rectangular because, as the following screenshot indicates, you're not limited to actual rectangles when you create rectangular selections via AppleScript.
posts.key_insight: BBEdit 7's AppleScript support for rectangular selections is more flexible than the name implies, allowing non-rectangular selection shapes.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's surprise release of Safari at Macworld Expo 2003, explaining why Apple needed its own browser and why they chose the obscure KHTML engine over the expected Gecko/Mozilla. He argues that Mac OS X lacked a decent native browser — IE was ugly and tied Apple to Microsoft, Mozilla was a cross-platform monstrosity, OmniWeb had a broken rendering engine, and Opera required payment. Apple's choice of KHTML over Gecko came down to performance: KHTML was lean at 140,000 lines of code versus Gecko's 1.5 million, enabling dramatically faster startup and page load times. Even in public beta, Safari was already the fastest browser on the Mac.
key_points: Apple's dependence on IE as the default Mac browser meant dependence on Microsoft — an unacceptable business risk regardless of Microsoft's good faith
The two separate challenges of building a browser — the HTML rendering engine and the browser application — explain why no existing Mac option was satisfactory
Apple chose KHTML over Gecko because KHTML was 140,000 lines versus Gecko's 1.5 million, enabling superior performance and easier integration with Cocoa
Dave Hyatt's prescient 2002 critique of Gecko's architecture — its bloated cross-platform abstractions, poor threading model, and startup penalty — foreshadowed exactly why Apple avoided it
Safari's beta was already the fastest browser on Mac OS X by every measurable metric, addressing a real and publicly embarrassing performance gap
money_quotes: Remember the good old days, when betas were secret? Actually, what's changed is that what used to be called "1.0" is now called "public beta".
It's just good business sense to avoid any situation in which you are dependent upon Microsoft acting in good faith.
Mozilla is a cross-platform monstrosity.
OmniWeb is a good browser application with a lousy HTML engine. Mozilla, on the other hand, is a terrible browser application (at least by Macintosh standards) with a very good HTML engine.
Good HTML rendering engines aren't exactly falling out of the sky.

A browser on OS X done right should be able to dust Gecko in terms of speed and footprint. It should be able to just smoke Gecko in startup time and page load time. The fact that this hasn't been done yet doesn't mean it can't be done.
Apple's stated goal is to make Safari the best browser on the Mac. What's unstated, but clearly their larger goal, is to make it the best browser in the world, period.
posts.key_insight: Apple's choice of the obscure KHTML engine over the popular Gecko was a bold architectural bet that lean, clean code would outperform a bloated but feature-complete alternative — and even the public beta proved them right.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Jason Kottke's proposal to merge Safari and Sherlock into a combined browser-plus-web-services application, arguing forcefully against it. He contends that Mac OS X desperately needs an excellent dedicated web browser first, and that bloating Safari with additional functionality would repeat Netscape's fatal mistake of turning Navigator into Communicator. He critiques Kottke's mock-ups for violating fundamental Mac UI principles by blurring the distinction between applications and windows. However, Gruber acknowledges the underlying problem is real — web services front-ends need improvement — and proposes the correct solution: making Safari's WebCore rendering engine available as a framework for other apps like Sherlock and NetNewsWire to embed, rather than cramming those apps inside the browser.
key_points: Safari fills a critical gap as Mac OS X lacks a single excellent dedicated web browser, making focused execution more important than revolutionary new features
Netscape's decline from Navigator to bloated Communicator serves as a cautionary tale against expanding browser scope beyond web browsing
Kottke's proposal violates Mac UI principles by blurring the distinction between applications and windows, resembling the widely criticized Windows MDI interface
The correct architectural solution is to make WebCore available as an embeddable framework for other apps, not to merge apps into the browser
Smaller, focused applications represent both the Macintosh Way and the Unix Way, and Safari should respect that tradition
money_quotes: There still does not exist a single excellent web browser for Mac OS X — every single browser for OS X has significant flaws.
Do one thing and do it right. There's no reason to move beyond the browser until someone makes a decent one.
Extending the browser to radical new ideas beyond web browsing is exactly what started Netscape down the road to Assville.
Smaller, focused applications are the Macintosh Way (and the Unix Way as well, which is one of many reasons why Unix nerds are feeling so at home in Mac OS X).
On the Macintosh, the visual scope of an application is the entire display. They are not contained within windows; windows belong to applications, not the other way around.
NetNewsWire and Sherlock should not live inside Safari; rather, Safari's WebCore engine should be available inside NetNewsWire and Sherlock.
Kottke's solution is inside-out.
posts.key_insight: The right response to inadequate web services front-ends is not to bloat the browser into a platform, but to make the browser's rendering engine available as a component other focused applications can embed.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's release of WebCore — a framework wrapper around the KHTML rendering engine — may be even more significant than Safari itself. He explains how the LGPL license enabled Apple to use KHTML in a proprietary browser while contributing improvements back, contrasting this with the GPL's more restrictive 'viral' nature. He dismisses Stallman's objections to the LGPL, arguing that quality software requires paid engineers. The core excitement is that WebCore gives independent Mac developers access to a fast, standards-compliant HTML rendering engine they can embed in their own apps — filling a longstanding gap that Windows developers had already solved via the IE rendering API.
key_points: WebCore as a public framework may be more consequential than Safari itself, because it enables an entire ecosystem of Mac apps to embed HTML rendering
The LGPL license was critical — it allowed Apple to build a proprietary browser while requiring them to release improvements to KHTML, a mutually beneficial arrangement the GPL would have prevented
Apple's investment of months of top-tier engineering time into KHTML demonstrates the practical benefits of the LGPL over the GPL for encouraging corporate contributions to open source
The lack of a system-wide HTML rendering engine had been a significant gap for Mac developers compared to Windows, where the IE rendering API was widely used in third-party apps
Developers like Omni Group and Brent Simmons were already exploring WebCore for OmniWeb and NetNewsWire, showing immediate ecosystem impact
money_quotes: Here's something that might be even more exciting than Safari, the web browser: WebCore, Apple's Mac OS X framework wrapper for the KHTML library.

Stallman lives in a bizarro-universe where quality application software magically appears from the fingertips of volunteers. In the real world, quality software comes from talented engineers, and talented engineers want to get paid.
KHTML is benefitting tremendously from this relationship with Apple — but it wouldn't have gotten one bit of this if it had instead been licensed under the GPL.

WebCore isn't just an effort to satisfy the legal obligations of using LGPL software. It is repackaged and encapsulated with an API designed to make it easier for other developers to use it in their own applications.
The lack of a decent system-wide HTML rendering engine has been a gaping hole for Mac developers. WebCore looks like it might just fill it.
posts.key_insight: Apple's release of WebCore as a developer-friendly framework was potentially more important than Safari itself, because it filled a critical gap in the Mac development ecosystem by giving every developer access to a fast, standards-compliant HTML rendering engine.

posts.summary: This is a link-roundup post collecting several items related to Safari and the web. Gruber highlights Apple's Safari update, Mark Pilgrim's exhaustive CSS compatibility testing for Safari across 27 browsers, Tantek Çelik's work on the Tasman rendering engine for MSN on Mac OS X, and Dennis Mahoney's mockup proposing that Kottke and Megnut merge their weblogs. The post is notable primarily for Gruber's admiration of Pilgrim's thoroughness in documenting Safari's CSS quirks.
key_points: Apple released an early Safari update recommended for all users
Mark Pilgrim tested hundreds of CSS import cases across 27 browsers to document Safari's CSS handling quirks
Tantek Çelik revealed his work on the Tasman rendering engine powering MSN for Mac OS X
Gruber considers Pilgrim's Safari coverage the definitive resource for web developers
money_quotes: Mark Pilgrim's Safari coverage is simply amazing — the definitive resource for web developers.
But, Jesus, how does he find the time for such extensive investigations?
posts.key_insight: In Safari's earliest days, independent web developers like Mark Pilgrim were doing the essential, painstaking work of documenting browser compatibility that the entire web development community depended on.

posts.summary: Gruber examines his site's server logs from Safari's first week of public beta to see which browsers his readers actually use. Despite widespread complaints that Safari is worthless without tabbed browsing, it has already become the most popular browser among Daring Fireball readers. The two most popular Mac OS X browsers are platform-exclusive (Safari and Chimera), while the factory-default IE for Mac has fallen to third place. The post uses real data to gently rebuke the conventional wisdom about tabs being a dealbreaker, while offering wry observations about browser market share among his tech-savvy readership.
key_points: Safari became the #1 browser among DF readers in its first week as a public beta, despite lacking tabbed browsing
The two most popular Mac OS X browsers (Safari and Chimera) are platform-exclusive, pushing the factory-default IE Mac to third place
A significant number of readers visit from Windows, prompting Gruber's quip to 'get back to work'
Niche browsers like iCab outperformed Netscape 6/7 on Mac, and Opera on Mac had a negligible four users
Gruber acknowledges his readership is not a representative sample of Mac users but finds the trends telling nonetheless
money_quotes: Give me tabs or give me death.
In its first week of existence, as a public beta with numerous shortcomings, Safari has already become the number one web browser for Daring Fireball readers.
The two most popular browsers for Daring Fireball readers on Mac OS X are unique to OS X, and the factory default browser is in third place.
A lot of you are reading Daring Fireball from Windows boxes. Get back to work.
More of you are using iCab than Netscape 6/7 (on the Mac, at least). Would you have believed this to be possible three years ago?
Only 4 readers using Opera for Macintosh. Ouch.
Mac users agree: Mozilla stinks.
posts.key_insight: Real-world adoption data from Safari's first week showed that users were flocking to it despite its lack of tabbed browsing, undermining the loud consensus that tabs were a prerequisite for a viable browser.

posts.summary: Gruber directs readers to Dan Benjamin's Macworld news summary on Hivelogic, praising it as 'exemplary analysis' while teasing that he'll discuss two minor errors the next day. He also playfully notes Hivelogic's design feature that changes on reload, linking to Benjamin's own post about it.
key_points: Recommends Dan Benjamin's Macworld coverage as exemplary analysis
Teases a forthcoming discussion of two minor errors in Benjamin's summary
Highlights Hivelogic's dynamic design feature that rewards reloading the page
money_quotes: Tomorrow there will be a discussion regarding the two minor errors in his otherwise exemplary analysis.
posts.key_insight: A brief endorsement of Dan Benjamin's Macworld analysis that showcases Gruber's characteristic blend of collegial praise and teasing nitpickery.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to two points from Dan Benjamin's Macworld write-up. First, he pushes back on the conspiracy theory that Apple hired Dave Hyatt to eliminate Chimera as competition, arguing instead that Hyatt was simply the best person to build a Mac browser given his experience. Second, he tempers enthusiasm about Apple's X11 release, arguing that while useful for Unix nerds, X11 apps are fundamentally not Mac software and the release was far from the most important announcement at Macworld. He draws a key distinction between software that runs on the Mac and genuine Mac software.
key_points: Apple hiring Dave Hyatt from Chimera was about acquiring rare browser-building expertise, not eliminating competition — both Safari and Chimera pull toward web standards and away from Microsoft dependence.
X11 on Mac OS X is useful but only for Unix nerds; it has no relevance to regular Mac users.
There is a crucial distinction between 'Mac software' and 'software that runs on the Mac' — X11 apps with windows and menus are still Unix programs lacking Mac-ness.
Apple's X11 wasn't groundbreaking since working X11 implementations for Mac OS X already existed; Apple just did it better.
money_quotes: There's no need to craft conspiracy theories.
Chimera and Safari might be competitors, but they certainly are not enemies. They're both pulling in the same direction — towards web standards, and away from over-dependence on Microsoft.
There is Mac software, and then there is software that runs on the Mac.

That X11 applications consist of standard GUI elements such as windows, menus, and buttons does not change the fact that they are Unix programs: tricky to install and remove, poorly designed, and utterly lacking in Mac-ness.
That X11 is a graphical environment doesn't mean it's any more interesting to most Mac users than the text-based programs accessible via Terminal.
This isn't a bad thing. It's quite good, in fact. But only for the terminally nerdy.
posts.key_insight: The distinction between genuine Mac software and Unix software that merely runs on Mac OS X is fundamental — having a GUI doesn't make an X11 app a Mac app any more than Terminal makes Unix interesting to regular users.

posts.summary: Gruber weighs in on Apple's X11 implementation for Mac OS X by highlighting two contrasting perspectives: Michael Tsai's concern that it will lead to lazy Unix ports that degrade the Mac user experience, and Dan Benjamin's argument that X11 is essential for Apple's enterprise strategy. Gruber finds both points valid but adds a telling observation: Steve Jobs deliberately avoided mentioning X11 during his keynote. This silence signals that X11 is a pragmatic, behind-the-scenes tool for developers and enterprise customers, not something Apple wants to promote to regular Mac users.
key_points: Apple's X11 implementation risks encouraging poorly-ported Unix apps that lack native Mac UI polish
X11 support is strategically important for Apple's enterprise ambitions, giving Unix software companies an easy path to the Mac
Jobs's deliberate omission of X11 from the keynote reveals Apple's positioning: a necessary tool, not a flagship feature
The tension between pragmatic platform compatibility and maintaining Mac user experience standards is a recurring Apple dilemma
money_quotes: There is a reason why Jobs made no mention whatsoever of X11 during the keynote.
Those who need to know, now know.
I worry that Apple's X11 server will encourage more ports of this type, and that regular Mac users will be forced to use them.

If Apple is to succeed in the Enterprise — and it must, especially with its shrinking educational market-share — then it must offer easy inroads for UNIX software companies to bring their applications to the Mac without significant code changes.
posts.key_insight: Apple's silence about X11 at the keynote was the message itself — it's a pragmatic concession to enterprise needs, deliberately kept out of the consumer spotlight to protect the Mac user experience brand.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Jeffrey Zeldman's complaint that modern Mac browsers like Safari and Chimera no longer let users view page source in an external editor like BBEdit. He examines Russell Harlan's AppleScripts that solve this problem and offers improvements, showing that Safari's document object has a native 'source' property that eliminates the need for curl. He walks through a refined AppleScript that fetches source directly from Safari and opens it in BBEdit with proper line break handling and HTML syntax highlighting.
key_points: Modern Mac OS X browsers removed the ability to view source in an external editor, a regression from earlier versions
Safari's AppleScript dictionary includes a 'source' property on documents, making curl unnecessary for fetching page source
Using curl has the advantage of including HTTP headers, but for source code alone, Safari's native property is simpler
Safari returns Unix-style line breaks that need conversion to Mac-style for BBEdit, easily handled with a grep replace
money_quotes: This isn't necessary, however, because Safari's document object has a source property.
If that info is interesting to you, then by all means, use curl. But if you just want the source, you can ask Safari for it.
It seldom hurts to be explicit.
posts.key_insight: Safari's AppleScript support is more capable than early adopters realized, with a native source property that makes viewing page source in BBEdit straightforward without command-line workarounds.

posts.summary: Gruber corrects his earlier criticism of Safari's scriptability, demonstrating that Apple's new browser is actually fairly scriptable through its Safari suite, though it lacks standard web browser Apple event commands like OpenURL. He walks through the differences between scripting Safari and Internet Explorer via AppleScript, noting Cocoa's unintuitive syntax requirements. The post culminates in a practical AppleScript that lists all open Safari URLs in a dialog and inserts the selected one into BBEdit, streamlining his link-insertion workflow.
key_points: Safari lacks standard web browser Apple event commands (OpenURL/GetURL) but is scriptable through its own Safari suite with document objects
Cocoa apps require unintuitive 'at end of documents' syntax for creating new documents via AppleScript, contributing to AppleScript's reputation as difficult
Gruber shares a practical script to pull URLs from open Safari windows into BBEdit for faster hyperlink insertion
The Safari scripting approach using document properties differs significantly from IE's more straightforward OpenURL command
money_quotes: Ends up that criticism was somewhat misguided — Safari actually is fairly scriptable.
Unintuitive superfluous syntax like this, combined with unhelpful error messages, is what gives AppleScript a reputation for being hard to write.
And a rather unhelpful error, at that: "An error of type 6 has occurred."
In what should come as a surprise to no one, I do all of my Fireball writing in BBEdit.
I'm not sure why the "at end of documents" part is necessary, but scripts won't work without it.
posts.key_insight: Safari's scriptability is real but hampered by Cocoa's unintuitive AppleScript conventions and unhelpful error messages, making it functional yet frustrating compared to IE's more straightforward scripting dictionary.

posts.summary: Gruber provides two updates to his earlier article on scripting Safari URLs via AppleScript. The first addresses Safari's lack of standard browser AppleScript commands for opening URLs, with Jim Correia providing the elegant solution of using 'open location' from Standard Additions instead of a clunky workaround. The second update credits Bill Hoffman for simplifying the URL list-building code from a verbose repeat loop to a single-line 'URL of every document' call. Both updates demonstrate the iterative, community-driven nature of finding better solutions.
key_points: Safari lacked standard AppleScript dictionary entries for opening URLs, but the Standard Additions OSAX 'open location' command works as a clean alternative
The original workaround of creating a new document then setting its URL was unnecessarily complex compared to the 'open location' solution
AppleScript's 'URL of every document' is a more direct and idiomatic way to build a list than manually iterating with a repeat loop
Reader contributions from Jim Correia and Bill Hoffman drove both improvements, showing the value of community feedback
money_quotes: Although Safari doesn't have an entry in its dictionary for the standard GetURL Apple event, it is defined in the Standard Additions OSAX as open location.
Those are Apple events, and there's no way those applications are sending complicated Apple events to Safari — they're sending an open URL event.
Much nicer, no?
No reason, except that I didn't think of it. Hoffman's code is shorter and more direct.
posts.key_insight: Safari's undocumented support for the 'open location' Standard Additions command provides a clean, simple way to script URL opening that its own AppleScript dictionary fails to advertise.

posts.summary: Gruber provides AppleScript adaptations of his Safari URL save/restore scripts for Internet Explorer on Mac, written with characteristic nostalgic humor about IE's now-forgotten era. He notes that IE for Mac actually had decent web standards support and scriptability, though its scripting model was clunkier than Safari's. The post walks through the code differences, showing how IE required manual list-building through non-object-model verbs rather than Safari's elegant one-liner. He frames the whole exercise in mock-historical terms, joking about Mac OS 9 and the rainbow Apple logo as ancient relics.
key_points: IE for Mac once offered better web standards support than any other browser of its era and was reasonably scriptable
Safari's AppleScript object model is far cleaner than IE's, which required manual parsing through procedural scripting verbs
The same IE scripts could work on both Mac OS X and Mac OS 9, showing cross-platform AppleScript compatibility
The post frames IE for Mac and Mac OS 9 as comically ancient history, despite being only recently supplanted
money_quotes: That's right, kids — Microsoft used to write Macintosh software.
a relic so old that it used the rainbow-colored Apple logo; ask your grandpa about it
IE forces us to create this list by hand, parsing the results of non-object-model scripting verbs. This is how Abraham Lincoln wrote scripts.
In addition to offering better web standards support than any other browser in that era, IE for Mac was also quite scriptable.
Combining Safari, AppleScript and the nature of the OS X system, a new and more efficient way of browsing can be created that makes the question of tabbed browsing somewhat irrelevant.
posts.key_insight: Safari's cleaner AppleScript object model represented a meaningful improvement over IE for Mac's clunky scripting interface, symbolizing the broader shift from Microsoft's dominance to Apple's own browser ecosystem.

posts.summary: Gruber presents a practical set of AppleScript solutions for saving, restoring, and clearing open Safari URLs — a feature browsers didn't natively offer at the time. He walks through three scripts that store open URLs to a preferences file and reopen them on demand, explaining AppleScript concepts like text item delimiters and file I/O along the way. The post argues that scriptability is the whole point of good application software, because it lets users add features developers didn't anticipate. The tutorial doubles as an advocacy piece for AppleScript's utility and Safari's scriptable architecture.
key_points: Scriptable applications empower users to add features on their own, saving developers from having to anticipate every need
Web browsers at the time lacked session restore, making users reluctant to quit with multiple tabs open
Bookmarking temporary URLs is overkill — the scripts solve the problem of remembering URLs you want to read once and discard
AppleScript's global text item delimiters require careful save-and-restore discipline to avoid side effects
The three-script approach (save, restore, clear) provides a complete workflow with proper error handling and user confirmation
money_quotes: The whole point of scriptable application software is that it allows us, the users, to add and tweak features.
By spending the time up-front to make an application scriptable, developers save time down the line, by allowing users to add new features on their own, using (relatively) simple scripts.
Having all these windows open makes me reluctant to quit my browser.
I don't want to bookmark them — that would be overkill. These are URLs I want to read once, then close.

Because text item delimiters is a global AppleScript property shared by all AppleScripts on your computer, the right thing to do is to save the current value, change text item delimiters to what we want, then restore the previous value when we're finished.
Clearly named buttons help keep things, well, clear.
posts.key_insight: Application scriptability is a force multiplier — it lets users solve their own workflow problems without waiting for developers to ship features.

posts.summary: Gruber announces Dan Benjamin's new venture Hiveware and its first product, Enkoder, a tool to protect email addresses on web pages from spam harvesters. He explains the problem: mailto links expose addresses to spambots crawling the web. While HTML entity encoding offers some protection, it's easily defeated by a few lines of Perl. Enkoder uses JavaScript obfuscation to hide addresses from source code scrapers while keeping them clickable in browsers. Gruber acknowledges it's 'heightened obscurity' rather than impregnable security, but recommends it as a practical step ahead of spammers.
key_points: Putting email addresses in plain text on web pages guarantees spam, as harvesters crawl for mailto links
HTML entity encoding is a rudimentary defense easily defeated by simple Perl scripts
Enkoder uses JavaScript obfuscation to hide addresses from source code while preserving usability in browsers
The tool is available free in three forms: Mac OS X app, web form, and cross-platform command-line tool
money_quotes: You can tell that the new Enkoder is better and more fun, because its name is spelled with a k.
If you put an address on a web page in plain text, soon thereafter you will begin receiving spam at that address.
Many, not all.
The problem with this technique is that it isn't very hard at all to write a spam harvesting spider that can decode HTML entities. A few lines of Perl will do it.
This is not impregnable security. It is merely heightened obscurity.
posts.key_insight: Email address protection on the web is an arms race of obscurity rather than true security, but practical JavaScript obfuscation can stay one step ahead of spam harvesters.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the release of SmartyPants 1.1, a Movable Type plugin for typographically correct punctuation. The update improves dash handling, fixes curly quote bugs, and adds new formatting options. He also teases upcoming integration with Movable Type 2.6's new Text Formatting system, closing with an Indiana Jones reference about 'top men' working on it.
key_points: SmartyPants 1.1 adds flexible en-dash and em-dash shortcuts and fixes the previous overly restrictive space-dependent dash behavior
Bug fixes include corrected curly quote behavior and expanded tag exclusion list for code-like elements
Movable Type 2.6 will introduce a plugin-friendly Text Formatting system that SmartyPants will integrate with
money_quotes: Previously, it would look for " — " (space dash dash space), which was dumb, since many people do not use spaces around their em dashes.
We have top men working on integrating SmartyPants with MT 2.6's Text Formatting system. Who? Top men.
Spread the word, and smarten your quotes.
posts.key_insight: Typographic details like proper curly quotes and em dashes matter, and tools should make correct typography the default rather than requiring fussy workarounds.

posts.summary: Gruber catalogs his interface gripes with Apple's Safari browser, prompted by Dave Hyatt's open call for GUI suggestions. He criticizes several specific design decisions: a non-functional 'Show' label in the View menu that violates Apple's own Human Interface Guidelines, the combined Stop/Reload button that can trigger the opposite action, the lack of external View Source support, the inline Bookmarks window that creates confusion, hardcoded blue button graphics that ignore the user's Graphite appearance setting, and the brushed metal window skin. Throughout, he appeals to Apple's own HIG documentation as the standard Safari should meet.
key_points: The 'Show' label in Safari's View menu violates the HIG principle that menu items must be commands, and is unnecessary since 'View' already serves as the implicit verb
The combined Stop/Reload button is a clever space-saver that creates a dangerous race condition where clicking a fraction too late triggers the exact opposite action
Safari's inline Bookmarks display causes confusion because the window title and address bar still show the hidden page, and the bookmarks window doesn't appear in the Windows list
Safari's hardcoded blue Address Bar button graphics ignore the user's system-wide Graphite appearance preference, violating Apple's own HIG guidance on custom controls
Gruber repeatedly cites Apple's Human Interface Guidelines to hold Safari accountable to Apple's own stated standards
money_quotes: Less than one day later, there are already over 600 comments, most of which are asking for tabbed browsing. Jesus, what a great idea! Why didn't anyone suggest that before?
I don't know where this little turd came from, but it should be removed.
Menu items are commands. No ifs ands or buts. There should never be a menu item that is anything other than a menu item.
Guess what happens if you try to hit Stop, but you're a fraction of a second too late? You wanted to stop, but the exact opposite happens.
I've tried to like this, I've really tried, but I don't.
You know it's open, so you click-and-hold on the Safari icon in your Dock. The contextual menu lists all your open windows, but none of them are called 'Bookmarks'.
posts.key_insight: Apple's own Safari browser violates Apple's own Human Interface Guidelines in multiple ways, and holding software to its platform's design standards is how you get better software.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Opera's complaints about Apple releasing Safari, arguing that every major OS ships a free browser and Opera has no grounds for grievance. He points out that Opera was years late to the Mac platform, shipped Windows versions first, and never produced a Mac-like interface. The piece broadens into a larger argument about Apple's strategic need for Mac-exclusive software that is genuinely better than Windows counterparts. Gruber distinguishes between Apple stepping on cross-platform developers like Opera (justified) versus Mac-only developers like Karelia's Watson (problematic). He argues that the tech press misunderstands the Mac's success by measuring it only on Microsoft's terms of market share and profit.
key_points: Every desktop OS ships a free browser, so Apple releasing Safari is not uniquely harmful to third-party developers
Opera failed on the Mac because it was years late, Windows-first, and used ugly cross-platform UI rather than native Mac design
Apple's competitive advantage depends on Mac-exclusive software that is better than Windows counterparts, which cross-platform apps like Opera cannot provide
The tech press misjudges the Mac by measuring success only in market share and profit rather than quality and user satisfaction
There is a meaningful distinction between Apple competing with cross-platform developers (acceptable) and Mac-only developers like Karelia (problematic)
money_quotes: Ugly skins may be popular on Windows, but on the Mac, most people want their applications to look like normal Mac applications.
Fuck me? No, fuck you, Opera.
The only way to see the Mac as unsuccessful is to compare it to Windows on Microsoft's terms — market share and raw profit.
What they miss is that the Mac's primary purpose is to be better. Windows's primary purpose is to be ubiquitous.
To be better requires Mac-only software that works better than its Windows counterparts.
Opera is exactly what Apple doesn't need: exactly like the Windows version, but six months behind.
Watson is exactly the sort of third-party software Apple needs for the Mac: original, useful, fun, and only for the Mac.
posts.key_insight: Apple's strategic imperative is Mac-exclusive software that is genuinely better than Windows alternatives, which is why competing with cross-platform also-rans like Opera is not just acceptable but necessary.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews Konfabulator, a $25 JavaScript runtime engine for Mac OS X that runs small visual 'widgets' created by Kaleidoscope co-creator Arlo Rose. While he finds the concept neat and praises JavaScript as the scripting language choice, he criticizes two major decisions: mandatory skinning with no option for native Aqua UI elements, and the absence of an IDE for creating widgets. He argues the business model is backwards — the runtime should be free to maximize the audience for widgets, while the IDE should be the paid product. Ultimately, he sees Konfabulator as prioritizing visual flash over developer productivity, targeting pixel tweakers rather than programmers.
key_points: Konfabulator uses JavaScript as its scripting language, which Gruber considers a much better choice than the originally planned Perl, especially for beginning programmers
Mandatory skinning with bitmap images is a significant design flaw — there's no way to use native Aqua UI elements, making even simple widgets require extensive pixel work
The lack of an IDE is Konfabulator's most perplexing omission, forcing developers to hand-edit XML with pixel offsets and manage projects without proper tooling
The business model is inverted: charging $25 for the runtime limits the audience for widget creators, when the runtime should be free and the IDE should be the paid product
Konfabulator is clearly targeted at the Kaleidoscope crowd — visual tweakers — rather than programmers who want to build useful small utilities
money_quotes: JavaScript is a terrific scripting language. Certainly it is most famous for client-side web browser scripting, but it's really a general purpose scripting language.
What I don't like, not one bit, is that "skinning" is not an option — it is mandatory.
It just seems silly not to make it easy to create a non-skinned default UI.
Konfabulator is clearly geared to appeal to the Kaleidoscope crowd: tweakers who prefer to spend their time hacking pixels in Photoshop than hacking their actual source code.
I wouldn't write it for Konfabulator, because I wouldn't want to limit the audience to those who've paid $25 for Konfabulator.
It makes more sense to me to give away the runtime engine, and let everyone run widgets for free. Then charge money for the IDE.
Creating Konfabulator widgets isn't hard in the way that differential equations are hard; it just looks like a pain in the ass.
I would have been happier if their work for version 1.0 had gone toward automating the construction of Konfabulator widgets, at expense of visual effects.
posts.key_insight: Konfabulator's fatal flaw is prioritizing visual appeal over developer experience and getting its business model backwards — the runtime should be free to grow the ecosystem, while the creation tools should be the product worth paying for.

posts.summary: Gruber recounts buying his wife a new iMac for Valentine's Day, along with an AirPort Extreme Base Station for wireless networking. While the hardware setup was impressively easy, the AirPort connection immediately became unreliable, displaying a maddening 'Status Not Available' error. He spent hours troubleshooting instead of letting his wife enjoy the new machine, finding that Dean Allen at Textism had suffered the same problem — with disastrous results for his PowerBook. After a night of defeat, the connection mysteriously fixed itself following a hardware reset and reconfiguration, and the piece ends with his wife happily ripping CDs at blazing speed.
key_points: Apple's hardware design is praised as clever and elegant, from captive screws to the Kubrickian-white aesthetic, making physical setup a breeze
The AirPort Extreme wireless networking experience was frustratingly unreliable, undermining Apple's promise that wireless is easier than wired
Dean Allen's infamous Textism account of the same AirPort bug — ending in a destroyed PowerBook — serves as both cautionary tale and comic relief
The problem resolved itself mysteriously after a hardware reset, with no clear explanation of what went wrong
Despite the networking nightmare, the iMac's raw speed impressed — ripping CDs faster than you can physically load them
money_quotes: The lady in your life does not want flowers or candy or champagne today. She wants an iMac.
It seems downright uncouth to connect one of these pristine Kubrickian-white iMacs via CAT-5 cable.
Easier than setting up a wired network, my ass.
While Mr. Allen's technique does have a certain appeal (in that it solves the problem of having a computer that cannot connect to a base station), it wouldn't do at all in my situation.
You can't just smash up a brand-new computer. Maybe if you're Johnny Depp holed up in a swanky hotel, but not if you're Johnny Gruber holed up in your home office.
So I went to bed, defeated. Unconnected. Shackled by CAT-5 cables.
Then I woke up this morning, and damn if the motherfucker hadn't fixed itself.
posts.key_insight: Apple's hardware design can be exquisitely thoughtful while its software and networking can be maddeningly unreliable, and the gap between those two experiences is where user frustration lives.

posts.summary: Gruber defends the paramount importance of UI consistency in Mac OS X against arguments that Apple should be free to experiment with novel interface widgets. He responds to a thread of criticism from Mac developers about Apple's inconsistent control widgets, and pushes back against Buzz Andersen's suggestion that consistency matters less than it did in 1984. Gruber argues that the Mac's intuitiveness stems directly from its consistency, and that Apple's new widgets aren't innovative—they're just different for difference's sake. He uses text edit fields as a concrete example of how Apple is squandering Mac OS X's opportunity to finally achieve perfect consistency. He closes by noting that as competing platforms become more consistent, the Mac risks losing its distinguishing advantage.
key_points: Apple's inconsistent UI widgets aren't innovative—they perform the same tasks as standard controls but behave differently, which is worse than true innovation
Ease-of-use is a result of intuitiveness and consistency, not the other way around; designing for 'morons' without those foundations produces unsatisfying software
Mac OS X's built-in text system finally makes perfect consistency easier than ever, yet Apple's own app teams are creating slightly inconsistent text fields
As Windows and other platforms become more consistent, the Mac's UI advantage narrows if Apple doesn't maintain its own standards
Apple recently standardized the Preferences shortcut to Cmd-comma in the HIG, but most of its own apps haven't adopted it yet
money_quotes: The hallmark advantages of the Macintosh are consistency and intuitive UI design.
It's not pedantry that inspires Mac afficionados to gripe about Apple's violations of the Macintosh Human Interface Guidelines.
The guidelines outlined in the HIG form a cohesive whole describing a philosophy of design.
Ease-of-use is a result, intuitiveness and consistency are the cause.
The problem is these inconsistent control widgets from Apple aren't innovative. They're just different for difference's sake.

It is easier to create text edit fields that are completely consistent across the entire OS than ever before. But instead of leading the way, Apple's application teams are creating various slightly inconsistent text edit fields.
If the Mac OS interface gets less consistent going forward, it will become less distinguishable from its competitors.
The time to nip a bad trend is at the beginning.
posts.key_insight: Apple's UI experiments aren't genuine innovation but gratuitous inconsistency that undermines the very consistency-driven intuitiveness that has always been the Mac's core advantage.

posts.summary: Gruber shares browser usage statistics from Daring Fireball's access logs for early February 2003, shortly after Apple's Safari browser debuted. Safari leads with 4,013 unique visitors, followed closely by IE Windows at 3,737, with Camino (formerly Chimera) at 1,121. He discusses the inherent inaccuracies of counting unique visitors via IP addresses and notes that minority browsers like OmniWeb and Opera can't be reliably counted due to user-agent spoofing. He acknowledges his readership skews heavily toward Mac enthusiasts and shouldn't be taken as representative of general browser usage.
key_points: Safari quickly became the dominant browser among Daring Fireball's Mac-centric readership within weeks of its January 2003 debut
Accurate unique visitor counting is impossible due to DHCP reassignment and shared IP addresses behind proxies and routers
Minority browsers like OmniWeb, Opera, and iCab can't be reliably tracked because they spoof their user-agent strings to bypass browser-detection scripts
Gruber expresses surprise at the significant number of Windows users reading his Mac-focused site, speculating many read from work
money_quotes: I'm still amazed by the number of Daring Fireball readers who are using Windows. I suppose some of you are here because of SmartyPants, but my guess is that many of you are reading Daring Fireball from work. Good for you.
The content of Daring Fireball is such that the readership surely skews toward enthusiastic Mac nerds.
I still think these numbers are interesting, but in no way do I (nor should you) interpret them as being indicative of browser usage amongst Mac users in general.

Many minority browsers claim to be some version of Internet Explorer or Netscape in order to get past stupid browser-detection scripts that would otherwise prevent these browsers from accessing a site's content.
"Camino", in case you haven't heard, is the new name for the browser formerly known as Chimera. Though others seem to dislike the new name, I like it. It's got style.
posts.key_insight: Safari rapidly dominated among Mac enthusiasts within weeks of launch, but the messy realities of user-agent spoofing and IP-based counting make any browser statistics inherently imprecise.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Cory Doctorow's argument that curly quotes and typographic punctuation should be avoided on the web because they break RSS feeds and cause compatibility problems. As the author of SmartyPants, Gruber argues the solution isn't to retreat to ASCII but to demand smarter software that handles character encoding properly. He defends Robin Williams's typography advocacy and argues that 400 years of typesetting knowledge shouldn't be abandoned because of lazy software engineering. He provides a practical BBEdit AppleScript for converting smart punctuation to ASCII as a stopgap, while insisting the real fix is better tools like Dean Allen's Textile that handle entities automatically.
key_points: The problem with curly quotes on the web isn't the characters themselves but software that fails to handle HTML entities properly
ASCII is a cripplingly deficient character set and retreating to it is a step backward, not a pragmatic solution
Traditional typographic conventions from 400 years of print are a body of knowledge to learn from, not obsolete relics to discard
The web's distinction between HTML source and rendered output is precisely why HTML entities exist — to display proper typography from plain ASCII source
The responsibility lies with software developers to write tools that handle smart punctuation transparently, not with writers to dumb down their text
money_quotes: Doctorow's solution is for everyone to just stick with 7-bit ASCII characters. My solution is to fix or discard any retarded software that still insists on such restrictions.

And exactly whose paradise is ASCII? The ASCII character set is comprised of a measly 128 characters, many of which are non-printing control characters. It's the crummiest character set imaginable, the lowest of lowest common denominators.

The idea that web designers ought to ignore the lessons of traditional typesetting is as silly as recommending photographers ignore the lessons of composition, framing, and color from the art of painting.
That print typesetting is a 400-year-old art form doesn't make it obsolete. Quite the opposite, it means we're fortunate enough to have a tremendous body of knowledge to learn from.
Even French? How generous! Finally, the millions of people whose names contain characters such as 'é' can spell their names correctly without fear of offending Cory Doctorow's sensibilities.
Dealing with HTML entities is a trivial software problem. Trivial. You shouldn't have to deal with HTML entities by hand.
Proper typographic punctuation is 400-year-old news. That it's considered exotic, or even non-standard, on today's web is embarrassing.
The solution isn't for everyone to limit themselves to the same character set used on 1970's-era VT-100 terminals. The solution is for software developers to write smarter software.
posts.key_insight: When software can't handle proper typography, the answer is to fix the software, not to abandon centuries of typographic craft for the lowest common denominator.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Bill Bumgarner's excitement about Mac OS X 10.2.4's installer being able to find applications by bundle identifier rather than hard-coded paths. Gruber points out that classic Mac OS installers had this capability over a decade earlier, making the celebration misplaced. He argues this isn't a new feature worth praising but rather the belated elimination of a deficiency that should never have existed in OS X. He also notes that the problem of resolving multiple apps with the same identifier was already solved in the classic Mac era with a simple dialog.
key_points: Mac OS X 10.2.4's installer gained the ability to find applications by bundle identifier rather than fixed paths
Classic Mac OS had equivalent functionality since the early 1990s, making this a regression that was finally corrected rather than an innovation
The OS X installer should have worked this way from version 10.0 onward
The problem of multiple matching applications was already solved in classic Mac OS with a simple user dialog
money_quotes: I thought this was pretty cool, too. The first time I saw it, over 10 years ago.
Mac users have been accustomed to installers and updaters that can find older versions of applications anywhere, on any local volume, since the early 90's.

That this ability to locate applications using a method other than hard-coded paths is new to the built-in Mac OS X installer isn't cause for celebration; it's just one less idiotic deficiency to complain about.
There's no reason whatsoever it shouldn't have been like this ever since 10.0.

If it's anything like what Mac users have been used to for the past 10 years, the solution is simple: display a dialog listing each matching application, and ask the user which copy they want to update.
posts.key_insight: Features that existed in classic Mac OS and were lost in the transition to OS X shouldn't be celebrated when they're finally restored — they should be expected.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his previous AppleScript for converting curly quotes to ASCII in BBEdit, revealing that a reader pointed out BBEdit's built-in Convert to ASCII plug-in is scriptable, reducing his 14-line script to just three lines. He explains the advantages of the simpler approach: it's faster, doesn't pollute the undo stack, and converts far more characters. He then extends the discussion to BBEdit's Translate command for converting non-ASCII characters to HTML entities, sharing a custom AppleScript he uses regularly when writing HTML content. The post is a practical tutorial on leveraging BBEdit's scripting capabilities for text processing workflows.
