key_points: BBEdit's Convert to ASCII plug-in is scriptable, replacing a manual 14-line search-and-replace script with just three lines
The plug-in approach is superior: faster execution, atomic undo behavior, and broader character conversion coverage
BBEdit's Translate command (Command-Option-T) converts between non-ASCII characters and HTML entities bidirectionally
Since BBEdit's Translate command isn't recordable, you need to write AppleScript manually to automate it with custom settings
Gruber's workflow involves typing real characters (like ©) and then running a script to convert them to HTML entities, avoiding manual entity typing
money_quotes: The answer: because I forgot that the Convert to ASCII plug-in is scriptable.
The Convert to ASCII plug-in works nearly instantaneously.
The Convert to ASCII command is atomic, so you can just hit Undo once.
The Translate command is so useful, and so convenient, that it's easy for an old BBEdit user like yours truly to forget that many people might not know about it.
Instead of dealing with entities manually, I just type real characters, then run my script.
posts.key_insight: The best text-processing workflows come from deeply understanding your tools' scriptable capabilities, often replacing verbose custom solutions with elegant built-in features.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights Matthew Thomas's two-part critique of web-based email interfaces. Thomas walks through a scenario where a customer loses an email draft due to the inherent fragility of webmail, then argues that the fundamental design flaw is trying to build a usable email client inside a web browser. The core problem is that web applications will always be inferior to native equivalents due to browser window constraints, server round-trips, and HTML form limitations. Thomas concedes webmail persists only because no better solution offers the same roaming access.
key_points: Web-based email interfaces are inherently fragile, leading to lost drafts and user confusion
Webmail suffers from fundamental UX problems: scrolling send buttons, slow server round-trips, and platform-independent abstraction limitations
Native email clients are inherently superior to web-based alternatives in usability
Webmail's popularity is driven solely by the need for roaming access, not by quality
money_quotes: The underlying design flaw here is the existence of Webmail, trying to put a usable e-mail interface inside a Web browser window.
Webmail and other 'Web applications' will always be very difficult to use, relative to native equivalents.
Webmail is only as popular as it is because there isn't a less execrable alternative which provides the same roaming ability.
Constant server round-trips to update the UI make them disgustingly slow and flickery.
They rely on a platform-independent interface abstraction (HTML forms).
posts.key_insight: Webmail is a fundamentally compromised design that persists not because it works well, but because no native alternative has solved the roaming access problem.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights a useful but little-known feature in Mac OS X Jaguar's system-wide Script menu: creating an Applications subfolder inside ~/Library/Scripts/ allows application-specific scripts to appear at the top level of the Script menu when that app is frontmost. He credits Erik Barzeski for discovering the trick and shares it with his readers. He also takes a jab at Barzeski for breaking all his site's URLs during a reorganization, linking to Tim Berners-Lee's guidance on permanent URIs. Finally, he agrees with Michael Tsai's criticism of the Script menu's use of disabled menu items as labels.
key_points: Jaguar's Script menu supports application-specific scripts via a ~/Library/Scripts/Applications/<AppName>/ folder structure
Barzeski broke all his site's links during a reorganization, violating the principle that cool URIs don't change
The Script menu's use of disabled menu items as section labels is a UI antipattern seen across several Apple apps
money_quotes: Note to Erik from Tim Berners-Lee: Don't do that.
Unfortunately, Script Menu uses the annoying "disabled menu item as a label" trick that's also seen in the Apple menu, iTunes, Mail, and Safari.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's Script menu has a hidden but powerful feature for organizing application-specific scripts, though its UI implementation leaves something to be desired.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews Bare Bones Software's TextWrangler, a $49 replacement for the long-standing free BBEdit Lite. He critiques the naming decision, arguing the product should have retained the BBEdit brand, and details the features gained and lost compared to both BBEdit Lite and full BBEdit. He notes the curious decision to support only C-family syntax coloring when scripting languages would serve more users. He closes by acknowledging that while the end of a free BBEdit is a loss, professional software costs money to produce, and compares the situation favorably to Adobe's Photoshop pricing.
key_points: TextWrangler is essentially an upgraded BBEdit Lite with a new name and a $49 price tag, ending a decade of free BBEdit availability
The naming decision was a mistake — dropping 'BBEdit' from the name makes TextWrangler seem like an unrelated product rather than part of the BBEdit family
The choice to support only C/C++/Objective-C syntax coloring while omitting Perl and Python is puzzling, since Project Builder already handles C well and for free
Professional software costs real money to produce, and BBEdit no longer needs the free version for brand recognition
The Photoshop comparison puts the pricing in perspective — there has never been a free Photoshop, and Photoshop Elements costs twice as much
money_quotes: TextWrangler is clearly derived from BBEdit, but its name creates the impression that it's something altogether new.

And so today is the first day since BBEdit 2.1 shipped in April 1992 that there is not a free version of BBEdit available. That's a shame, because all things considered, the world was a better place when there was a free version of BBEdit available for anyone to use.
Anyone who claims to have been using BBEdit 'ever since 1.0' is either (a) lying; (b) delusional; or (c) listed somewhere in the BBEdit About box.
What Photoshop is to bitmapped graphic files, BBEdit is to text files.
Professional software costs real money to produce.
TextWrangler's creator code is a nice in-joke: '!Rch'; i.e. 'not Rich'.
posts.key_insight: Ending a free product to charge for a professional-grade replacement is a defensible business decision, but the branding misstep of severing the BBEdit name connection undermines the transition.

posts.summary: Gruber endorses Mark Pilgrim's guide on blocking spambots and unwanted web robots, sharing his own experience with abusive bots hitting Daring Fireball. He highlights NameProtect's bots (over 1,100 hits that month) and a spambot disguised with a Microsoft user-agent string (92 hits in one minute). He frames his newfound support for banning robots through the lens of the Mos Eisley Cantina scene from Star Wars, where droids are refused service — a playful analogy for ejecting unwanted bots from one's web server.
key_points: Mark Pilgrim published a practical guide for blocking spambots and spybots that Gruber considers essential for any web site operator
Daring Fireball was being heavily abused by NameProtect bots (1,100+ hits/month) and a spam harvester using a Microsoft user-agent string (92 hits in a single minute)
Gruber uses the Star Wars cantina scene as a humorous analogy for banning unwanted robots from web servers
The post highlights the early-2000s problem of aggressive web crawlers consuming server resources and harvesting content
money_quotes: Wonderful, powerful information, useful to anyone running a web site.
Since 1977, I have been opposed to the policy of banning robots. But I now have a new perspective on this.
Your droids. They'll have to wait outside. We don't want them here.
The unnamed weaselly spambot harvester whose user-agent string contains 'Microsoft URL Control' which at one point on February 21 hit this site 92 times in a single minute.
posts.key_insight: Aggressive spambots and spybots that abuse web servers deserve to be blocked without hesitation, much like droids unwelcome in the Mos Eisley Cantina.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to widespread criticism of his earlier suggestion that Bare Bones Software should have named TextWrangler 'BBEdit Junior.' He acknowledges that while readers agreed 'BBEdit' should appear in the name, the 'Junior' suffix was universally panned. He shares his wife's particularly cutting assessment that even 'AssWrangler' would have been preferable. Despite the backlash, he humorously doubles down by invoking the Banana Junior 6000 from Bloom County as precedent.
key_points: Gruber's 'BBEdit Junior' naming suggestion was widely rejected by readers
There was consensus that 'BBEdit' should appear in the product name, just not with 'Junior'
His wife's 'AssWrangler' counterproposal served as the post's comedic highlight
Gruber refuses to back down, citing the fictional Banana Junior 6000 as his defense
money_quotes: My suggestion that it ought to have been named "BBEdit Junior" is widely hailed as one of the worst ideas ever.
There is also universal agreement that tacking on "Junior" was not the way to do it.
One reader in particular, who shall remain nameless, but whom I am married to, went so far as to profess that even "AssWrangler" would have been a better name than "BBEdit Jr."
If "Junior" was good enough for the Banana Electronics corporation's legendary Banana Junior 6000, then it's certainly good enough for BBEdit.
posts.key_insight: Gruber playfully refuses to concede a universally mocked naming opinion, turning the criticism into comedy by doubling down with an absurd pop-culture defense.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the release of SmartyPants 1.2, his typographic punctuation tool. The major update expands compatibility beyond Movable Type to also support Blosxom weblogs as a combination plug-in. It additionally works as a BBEdit text filter for educating punctuation in HTML markup. The post is a brief release announcement directing readers to the full change notes.
key_points: SmartyPants 1.2 expands beyond Movable Type to support both MT and Blosxom as a single combination plug-in
The tool now works as a BBEdit text filter for HTML markup not destined for a weblog
The release reflects Gruber's ongoing commitment to typographic correctness on the web
money_quotes: The big news is that it's not just for Movable Type anymore.
It is now a combination plug-in that you can use, unchanged, for both Movable Type and Blosxom weblogs.
It also works as a BBEdit text filter, for educating punctuation in HTML markup that isn't headed into an MT or Blosxom weblog.
posts.key_insight: SmartyPants 1.2 broadens the tool's reach beyond a single blogging platform, reflecting Gruber's belief that proper typography should be accessible regardless of publishing tool.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier report that BBEdit Lite was no longer available, noting that Bare Bones Software has clarified their policy. While BBEdit Lite is discontinued and unsupported, it remains legally available for download. Gruber documents the exact change in wording on Bare Bones' website to show he wasn't wrong in his original reading — the page initially said BBEdit Lite was 'no longer available,' which was later softened. He also notes a new comparison page between TextWrangler and BBEdit Lite.
key_points: Bare Bones Software reversed or clarified their position on BBEdit Lite's availability after initially stating it was 'no longer available'
Gruber documents the exact before-and-after wording change on the BBEdit Lite web page to vindicate his earlier eulogy
BBEdit Lite remains legally downloadable, with versions going back to 3.5 for System 6 still on the FTP site
A new comparison page was created to show differences between TextWrangler and BBEdit Lite
money_quotes: Ends up this comment from my initial look at TextWrangler is no longer true.

Just to prove I wasn't smoking crack when I wrote the above eulogy for BBEdit Lite, a comparison between yesterday's update to the BBEdit Lite web page and the version in my local cache from a few days ago shows the difference.
Version 6.1.2 is still available for download on their FTP site, as are versions 4.6, 4.1, and even version 3.5, for you holdouts still clinging to System 6.
posts.key_insight: Gruber meticulously documents a subtle policy reversal by Bare Bones Software, demonstrating his characteristic attention to exact wording and his insistence on getting the details right.

posts.summary: Gruber weighs in on a debate about whether the Mac OS X Finder should allow replacing a folder with a same-named file (or vice versa), a behavior the classic Finder deliberately prevented. He agrees with Rentzsch that such replacements are almost always mistakes with catastrophic consequences, but also agrees with Bumgarner that treating all same-named replacements consistently makes sense. His proposed solution: instead of irrevocably deleting the replaced item, the Finder should move it to the Trash, preserving the convenience of one-step replacement while making the action reversible. He notes the only historical reason this wasn't done was scarce disk space on early Macs, a constraint that no longer applies.
key_points: The classic Finder deliberately prevented replacing files with folders (or vice versa) as a design decision, not a technical limitation
The OS X Finder's replacement behavior is catastrophically destructive: replaced items are irrevocably deleted, not moved to the Trash, and Undo cannot restore them
Gruber proposes that the Finder should move replaced items to the Trash instead of deleting them, making all replacements non-destructive and fully undoable
The original Trash concept solved accidental deletion but was never applied to file replacement, likely because early Macs lacked sufficient disk space
Alias resolution would need minor updates to handle replaced items sitting in the Trash
money_quotes: No matter how much aggregate time you might save over the course of a lifetime by the current OS X Finder's permission of such folder-for-file-or-vice-versa replacements, it will never add up to the time you will lose if you get screwed by inadvertently allowing it just once.
The Trash was one of the biggest and best ideas in the original Macintosh. The concept of the Trash is simple, powerful, and obvious.

Nearly 20 years later, all computers now ship with a Trash-like interface for file deletion. Why? Because the time lost by having to first move files to the Trash and then invoke a separate 'Empty Trash' command is insignificant compared to how much time you will lose if you delete something important accidentally.
The Macintosh Trash almost completely solved the problem of people accidentally erasing files, a problem which, if you're old enough to remember, was rampant on systems that lacked a Trash metaphor.
But the Finder doesn't use the Trash when you replace a file, and never has. After permitting the replacement in the confirmation dialog, the original item is gone, deleted, nuked.
I think we can use some of this disk space to make file replacement non-destructive.
It could also be argued that it's an indication the OS X Finder engineers didn't thoroughly consider the ramification of allowing a file to be replaced by a folder.
posts.key_insight: The Finder's file replacement behavior is a glaring inconsistency: the Mac pioneered the Trash to prevent accidental deletion, yet replacing a same-named item bypasses it entirely, irrevocably destroying the original.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier suggestion that the Finder should move replaced items to the Trash instead of permanently deleting them. He had anticipated a problem with the Mac OS Alias Manager, which traditionally tracked aliases by file ID first, meaning aliases would follow the old file into the Trash. However, he discovered that Apple changed the Alias Manager in Jaguar (10.2) to resolve aliases by path first, file ID second. This means aliases now automatically point to a new replacement file at the same path rather than following the original to the Trash. Gruber sees this as both an improvement in its own right and as removing a technical obstacle to his proposed Finder behavior change.
key_points: The Mac OS Alias Manager changed in Jaguar to resolve aliases by path first, file ID second — reversing the previous priority order
This behavioral change means aliases now point to replacement files at the same location rather than following the original file to the Trash
The change removes a key technical obstacle to Gruber's proposal that the Finder should move replaced items to the Trash instead of permanently deleting them
Gruber provides a step-by-step reproducible test to verify the new alias resolution behavior
money_quotes: Unlike previous versions of Mac OS, aliases are now resolved by path first, file ID second.
This new behavior is different, but I think it's a decided improvement, in that "the right thing" will happen more often than before when you have aliases pointing to an item that you replace.
It also means that it should be a little bit easier than I thought for Apple to change the Finder to move replaced items to the Trash instead of deleting them.
posts.key_insight: Apple's quiet change to alias resolution priority in Jaguar — path-first instead of file-ID-first — both improves everyday behavior and removes a technical barrier to making the Finder's file replacement safer by moving replaced items to the Trash.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Safari incorrectly applies anti-aliasing to small monospaced fonts like Monaco 9 and 10, which were designed as pixel-perfect bitmap fonts for displaying source code. He explains that while anti-aliasing improves the on-screen appearance of proportional typefaces by capturing more of their high-resolution character, it actively harms readability for monospaced fonts where distinguishing individual characters is critical. He demonstrates through browser comparisons that Camino correctly leaves these fonts un-aliased while Safari smudges them, making punctuation like backticks, dollar signs, and tildes harder to distinguish. He notes this is puzzling since other Cocoa apps like TextEdit already handle this correctly, and hopes it's a bug rather than a deliberate choice.
key_points: Monospaced fonts serve a critical modern purpose: displaying source code where precise character recognition of punctuation is essential, and anti-aliasing undermines this by blurring distinctions between similar characters.
Anti-aliasing works well for prose because reading relies on whole-word recognition, but source code demands individual character recognition, making the tradeoffs fundamentally different.
Monaco 9 is a surviving masterpiece of the original Mac's hand-tuned bitmap fonts, and Monaco 10 is a worthy OS X successor — neither was designed to be anti-aliased.
Safari's behavior is puzzling because other Cocoa applications correctly avoid anti-aliasing small Monaco, suggesting this may be a bug rather than a design decision.
Older Mac bitmap fonts like Geneva 9 also look worse when anti-aliased, appearing thin and wispy rather than crisp, and Camino is clever enough to avoid this.
money_quotes: It's downright silly that an apostrophe should consume the same space as an m.
Monospaced typefaces serve one great purpose in the modern age: they are ideal for displaying markup and programming language source code.
When you read normal prose, your brain doesn't recognize individual characters — it recognizes entire words.
The real Helvetica is what you see on the printed page.
The ability to discern individual characters is more important than creating on-screen characters that more accurately reflect the style of high-resolution output.
9-point Monaco is the last surviving masterpiece of the original Macintosh's painstakingly hand-tuned set of pixel-perfect bitmap fonts, and deservedly so.
The anti-aliased version of Monaco just isn't an attractive typeface; it looks smudged, not smooth.
posts.key_insight: Anti-aliasing is the right default for prose typography but the wrong choice for monospaced code fonts, where precise character discrimination matters more than faithfulness to high-resolution output.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the release of SmartyPants 1.2.1, a minor bug fix update that resolves Perl 5.005 compatibility issues and Blosxom static mode problems. He also notes the coincidental same-day release of BBEdit 7.0.3 from Bare Bones Software, which fixes text encoding bugs affecting Unix filters and scripts. The BBEdit update specifically resolves issues that affected running SmartyPants as a filter in the previous BBEdit version.
key_points: SmartyPants 1.2.1 fixes a Perl compatibility issue where a POSIX-style regex character class required Perl 5.6+
The update also fixes compatibility with Blosxom in static rendering mode
BBEdit 7.0.3 released the same day fixes text encoding bugs with Unix filters that affected SmartyPants integration
money_quotes: This is a minor update, consisting primarily of small bug fixes for compatibility with Perl 5.005.
posts.key_insight: A straightforward release announcement for SmartyPants 1.2.1 and a note on a complementary BBEdit bug fix that resolved SmartyPants integration issues.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the release of SmartyPants 1.2.2, just two days after 1.2.1, to fix a bug that broke compatibility with Perl 5.005. The irony is that version 1.2.1 was partly intended to improve Perl 5.005 support but instead made SmartyPants completely inoperable under that version. He then launches into an elaborate fictional explanation involving Paco, a South American woolly monkey he trained first as a graphic designer and then as a programmer. The joke is that Paco's sloppy coding habits — refusing to use 'use strict,' writing unreadable code, and never testing — caused the embarrassing bug. Gruber uses the conceit to poke fun at himself while making pointed observations about programming discipline and the importance of testing.
key_points: SmartyPants 1.2.1 accidentally broke Perl 5.005 compatibility while trying to fix it, necessitating an immediate 1.2.2 release
Gruber takes a dig at budget web hosting companies for still running Perl 5.005 years after 5.6 was available
The elaborate Paco the monkey fiction satirizes poor programming practices: no strict mode, unreadable code, and zero testing
The underlying serious point is that the bug would have been caught instantly with even minimal testing under the target environment
money_quotes: Despite the fact that Perl 5.6 has been out for several years and provides numerous vast improvements, it is apparently de rigueur when running a second-rate web hosting company to provide your customers with Perl 5.005.
I do not need to be coldcocked by my lower-primate assistant.
What I wanted was a monkey, a loyal friend who, when otherwise unoccupied, could sit on my shoulder and pick crumbs out of my hair.
Paco proved to be an enormous time saver, much more so than AppleScript.
Two years into the effort, and he still refuses to invoke Perl's "use strict" pragma.
Yes, he can write functional programs, but his code is nearly unreadable, and he never performs tests.
Once he gets to the point where he thinks his code looks like it should work, he scampers away.
He has been sternly reprimanded, but it is my opinion that he is unrepentant.
posts.key_insight: A humorous fictional monkey programmer serves as the vehicle for a self-deprecating admission that even simple bugs slip through when you skip testing on the target environment.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up his earlier anti-aliasing critique of Safari with a deeper technical explanation of why the browser renders small monospaced fonts poorly. The core issue isn't anti-aliasing itself but that Safari uses outline font versions instead of bitmap fonts, which other Mac OS X applications correctly prefer for small monospaced sizes like Monaco 9 and 10. He walks through the history of bitmap versus outline fonts on the Mac, from the original Macintosh through PostScript and TrueType, to explain why bitmap versions exist and when they're superior. Safari engineer Dave Hyatt responds that the issue stems from using lower-level Core Graphics APIs for performance, and proposes CSS3 font-smooth as a solution. Gruber argues this misses the point: the fix isn't toggling anti-aliasing but using the correct font version. He frames his critique not as subjective preference but as Safari violating an established Mac OS X convention that other applications follow.
key_points: Safari's poor rendering of small monospaced fonts is caused by preferring outline fonts over bitmap fonts, not by anti-aliasing itself — the anti-aliasing is an effect, not the cause

Bitmap fonts were hand-tuned pixel-by-pixel for specific sizes and look perfect at those sizes without anti-aliasing, while outline fonts use mathematical curves that look poor at low screen resolutions
CSS3 font-smooth wouldn't solve the problem because turning off anti-aliasing on an outline font looks even worse than anti-aliasing it — you need the actual bitmap version
The bug was likely unintentional, caused by Safari using lower-level Core Graphics APIs for performance that don't respect the OS font smoothing settings
Gruber distinguishes his subjective preference from his objective critique: Safari violates an established Mac OS X convention, regardless of whether one likes anti-aliased text
money_quotes: My gripe from last week is that Safari anti-aliases small monospaced fonts such 9- and 10-point Monaco. This is true, but somewhat misguided. The anti-aliasing is an effect of the problem, not the cause.
In critical thinking, separating cause from effect is, well, critical.
If Safari simply turned off anti-aliasing for Monaco, but still used the outline version of the font, the results would be awful.
When all you're programming is a style system, does every bug start looking like a missing CSS feature?

But when I say Safari is wrong to use anti-aliasing for Monaco 9 and 10, I say so not because I don't like it (even though I don't), but because it's treating these fonts differently than do all other well-written applications on Mac OS X.
It's just that bitmap Geneva is beautiful, a classic for the ages, but outline Geneva is simply a dreadful Helvetica rip-off.
On any issue of subjectivity, it is all too easy to dismiss criticism from someone on the other side of the fence. But not all criticism on subjective issues is itself subjective.
posts.key_insight: Safari's font rendering problem isn't about anti-aliasing preferences but about using the wrong font version entirely — outline instead of bitmap — which violates established Mac OS X conventions that every other application follows.

posts.summary: Gruber publishes corrections and follow-ups to his earlier Anti-Anti-Aliasing article about font rendering on Mac OS X. Dave Hyatt from the Safari team confirms that Safari was indeed using the wrong fonts for screen rendering, validating Gruber's observations. Gruber corrects his claim about ImageWriter resolution (144 DPI, not 72), acknowledges that his description of TrueType hinting was oversimplified, and fixes a 2-bit vs 1-bit error. The post serves as both a technical errata and a demonstration that well-hinted fonts look better even when anti-aliased.
key_points: Safari's Dave Hyatt confirmed the browser was using wrong fonts for screen rendering due to missing a font substitution step in lower-level APIs
The ImageWriter had 144 DPI resolution at 'Best' quality, not 72 DPI as Gruber originally claimed, using 2x screen fonts
TrueType hinting is far more sophisticated than simply embedding bitmaps — it gives designers precise pixel-level control over letterform rendering
Well-hinted fonts like ProFont look better than unhinted fonts even when anti-aliased, suggesting hinting quality matters regardless of rendering method
money_quotes: Safari *is* using the wrong fonts for rendering to the screen.
I can't believe Hyatt has already responded to my article.
It is hinting that allows Microsoft fonts like Verdana and Georgia to looks so good on-screen without anti-aliasing.
Well-hinted fonts seem to look better than unhinted fonts, even when anti-aliased.
If Monaco looked that good in Safari, I probably wouldn't have bothered writing about this in the first place.
A black-and-white display is just 1-bit. Two colors, but just one bit.
posts.key_insight: Font hinting quality matters for screen rendering regardless of whether anti-aliasing is applied, and Safari's early font rendering issues were confirmed as genuine bugs rather than design choices.

posts.summary: Gruber defends Apple's decision to cancel a developer seed program for Safari after pre-release builds were repeatedly leaked to the public in violation of NDAs. He systematically dismantles three common complaints: that Apple should release nightly builds since they already release public betas, that Safari's use of open-source KHTML obligates Apple to open development, and that leaked builds helped Safari through better bug reporting. He argues that normal users inevitably download pre-release software and then blame the product when it's buggy, harming Safari's reputation. The leaked builds actually degraded bug report quality by flooding Apple with complaints about unfinished features like tabbed browsing. Gruber concludes that the real motivation behind the complaints was simple impatience dressed up as altruism.
key_points: Public nightly builds would harm Safari's reputation because normal users can't resist downloading them and then blame the software when it's buggy
Safari the application is not open source — KHTML is an open-source rendering library, but the browser built around it is Apple's proprietary software, and the motor is not the car
Leaked pre-release builds actually decreased the quality of bug reports by flooding Apple with feedback on obviously unfinished features
Apple was left with only two real options once leaks became widespread: embrace public seeds with slower development, or cancel the program entirely
The complaints are fundamentally about impatience for tabbed browsing, disguised as principled arguments about open source and software quality
money_quotes: These reasons are all crap.
People are drawn to publicly released beta software like insects to bug zappers.
Normal users can't resist. They will get hurt, data may be lost, and the software's reputation will be harmed.
The motor is not the car; the rendering library is not the browser.
Oh, I get it — Slashdot readers know better how to develop quality software than the Safari engineering team does.

It's a bit hypocritical to excuse impatience under an umbrella of altruism — I'm doing Apple a favor by using v67 — while ignoring the larger moral issue: that some of the seeded Safari testers acted as though their NDAs were printed on Charmin toilet paper.

If you think Safari, the application, is nothing more than a 'simple wrapper around KHTML', then we'll surely see one or more Safari clones pop up within a few days of the forthcoming release of the WebKit API. Don't hold your breath.
posts.key_insight: When pre-release software leaks to a mass audience, the developer loses control of both their reputation and the quality of their feedback loop, making cancellation of the program the only rational response.

posts.summary: Gruber conducts an extensive email interview with Brent Simmons, creator of NetNewsWire, the breakout RSS aggregator for Mac OS X. The conversation covers Simmons's design philosophy, his experience working for Dave Winer at UserLand Software, the business decisions behind splitting NetNewsWire into free Lite and paid Pro versions, and his views on Apple's complicated relationship with third-party developers. Simmons articulates a clear philosophy of ruthless simplicity in software design, learned directly from Winer, and explains why he chose to develop exclusively for Mac despite conventional wisdom favoring Windows. The interview also explores the emerging RSS ecosystem, pricing psychology for indie software, and the merits of public beta testing for small developers.
key_points: Simmons learned his core design philosophy — being ruthless about simplicity — from working under Dave Winer at UserLand, exemplified by reducing Manila's site creation form from a dozen fields to just four

The free/paid split between NetNewsWire Lite and Pro follows a read-only vs. read/write distinction, and Simmons deliberately refused to impose arbitrary subscription limits that would make the Lite version feel like mere advertising
Simmons chose to avoid screen-scraping in NetNewsWire to encourage sites to adopt RSS, avoid legal trouble, and prevent support nightmares from broken scrapers
Gruber argues that software users split into two groups — those willing to pay and those who never will — and that developers should ignore the vocal cheapskates entirely
Simmons worries Apple might create a competing RSS aggregator but hopes they've learned from the Watson/Sherlock debacle to either make deals with indie developers or leave the space alone
money_quotes: One of my philosophies is that any piece of software should be describable in a sentence.
One of the best lessons I learned from working for Dave Winer was to be utterly ruthless about simplicity.

He kept at me to keep making it simpler. And I did, piece by piece, until finally it's the form you may be familiar with today, where it asks you for a site name, a theme, and your username and password.
I'm lucky, I get to write software for John and Jane Einstein. I get to take risks that big companies can't.
The basic unit of measure on the web isn't the file, it's the link.

These people are morons, and they can't be pleased. You can put software users into two distinct groups: those who will consider paying for commercial software, and those who will never pay for software.
On the Mac, however, it's design that counts most. The best-designed app usually wins.
I prefer less ideology and more generosity. For me, Mac OS X is the only OS that seems to have in its DNA that spirit.
posts.key_insight: Great software comes from ruthlessly eliminating complexity until simplicity feels inevitable, a philosophy Simmons learned at UserLand and applied to make NetNewsWire succeed by being familiar, focused, and describable in a single sentence.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights a humorous April Fools' Day promotion from Bare Bones Software offering a $250,000 BBEdit license that includes hand delivery by an employee and a year of personal service. The joke offer includes absurd perks like reading the manual aloud and ripping the cellophane off the CD. Gruber notes the additional requirement of providing microbrewed beer, with a surcharge for mass-market beer. He adds his own suggestion to stock quality coffee, linking to Peet's and Torreo, showing his familiarity with the Bare Bones team's tastes.
key_points: Bare Bones Software posted a comedic $250,000 BBEdit license offer as an April Fools' joke
The offer parodies enterprise software pricing with absurd personal service perks
The beer and coffee details reflect the indie Mac developer culture Gruber is embedded in
money_quotes: Now available is the option to purchase a single user license of BBEdit with hand delivery by a Bare Bones Software employee and one full year of personal service
a refrigerator full of microbrewed beer (20% surcharge for mass-market or 'lite' beer)
posts.key_insight: Bare Bones Software's April Fools' joke lampoons enterprise software pricing while showcasing the personality and humor of the indie Mac developer community.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights John Siracusa's Ars Technica article dissecting the failings of the Mac OS X Finder and proposing a redesign. While not agreeing with all of Siracusa's proposed solutions, Gruber affirms the diagnosis is spot-on and emphasizes that critics aren't simply demanding a return to the Mac OS 9 Finder. He argues that the browser-based Finder metaphor serves computer nerds well but fails ordinary users by shifting spatial complexity from the screen into the user's mind. Gruber contends that Apple could design a Finder satisfying both spatial and browser camps, but the current implementation is a failed hybrid.
key_points: Siracusa's critique of the OS X Finder is accurate even if his proposed redesign isn't perfect — it's still better than what exists
Critics of the OS X Finder are not simply nostalgic for Mac OS 9; they want a genuinely better design
Every person who wrote Gruber expressing satisfaction with the current OS X Finder was a computer nerd, revealing a user-sophistication bias
The browser metaphor forces users to mentally track hierarchical file system structure, trading visual clutter for cognitive burden
A Finder that satisfies both spatial and browser camps is possible — the current Finder's failure is that it's purely browser-based with fake spatial features
money_quotes: Humans are much more adept at dealing with visual/spatial clutter than mental clutter.

By compromising the user's ability to manage files and folders based on familiar spatial cues and behaviors, all the complexity of the file system hierarchy is simply moved from the screen into the user's mind.
Every single person who wrote expressing satisfaction with the current OS X Finder had one thing in common: they are all computer nerds.
The browser metaphor the OS X Finder relies on requires you to understand and to be conscious of the hierarchical structure of your volumes.
It's absolutely possible that Apple could design a Finder with features that make both camps happy.
The problem with the current Finder is that every Finder window is a browser, and the spatial features are really just browser windows masquerading as spatial windows.
posts.key_insight: The OS X Finder's browser-based design serves power users while failing ordinary users by moving file system complexity from visible spatial relationships into invisible mental models.

posts.summary: Gruber translates Quark's PR announcement for QuarkXPress 6 from marketing-speak into blunt English. He systematically takes each claim from the press release and rewrites it to expose the emptiness of the corporate language. The post highlights Quark's vague feature promises, its shot at Adobe with built-in PDF support, and its misguided emphasis on web design features. Gruber implies that Quark is distracted and vulnerable to Adobe InDesign, which is focused purely on page layout. The format is simple but effective: quote the PR, then demolish it.
key_points: Quark's press release is empty marketing language that says almost nothing concrete about QuarkXPress 6
The built-in PDF creation is a direct competitive shot at Adobe, which Gruber reduces to a two-word message
Quark's emphasis on web design features is misguided, since users want a page-layout tool, not a web editor
Adobe InDesign is positioned as the real threat that Quark is trying to distract attention from
No pricing or ship date means the product will be expensive and late
money_quotes: Quark Inc. today released some vague statements about QuarkXPress 6, amounting to little more than verbal hand-waving.
People love to think that deadlines today are tighter than they were 'back in the day'. This isn't true, of course, but we'll assume you're kind of dumb.
Dear Adobe, Fuck you. Cheers, —Quark.
We are plowing full steam ahead under the delusion that our users want to use a print-oriented page-layout program for web design.
By placing extra emphasis on these unwanted web features, we hope to distract your attention from a certain upstart page layout application, which is focused squarely and solely on page layout.
QuarkXPress 6 will be expensive and late.
posts.key_insight: Quark's bloated PR language and misguided feature priorities reveal a company losing focus while a more disciplined competitor closes in.

posts.summary: Gruber methodically dismantles John C. Dvorak's recurring prediction that Apple will switch the Mac platform to Intel processors. He argues that Dvorak is a provocateur, not a reporter, whose predictions are designed to generate controversy rather than reflect insider knowledge. Gruber identifies several major obstacles Dvorak ignores: the impossibility of a fast PowerPC emulator for existing software, Apple's identity as a hardware company that depends on proprietary margins, and the absurdity of maintaining three simultaneous platforms. He emphasizes that Apple would never release Mac OS X for standard PCs because it would cannibalize their hardware business, as the clone licensing era proved. Even if Apple did adopt Intel chips, they would use proprietary hardware, not commodity PCs.
key_points: Dvorak's Intel prediction is based entirely on conjecture — two thin facts and hand-waving — with no actual sources or scoops
The 68K-to-PowerPC transition only worked because Apple built an exceptional emulator; a comparable PowerPC-to-Intel emulator seems implausible
Apple is fundamentally a hardware company, and the clone era proved that licensing the OS destroys hardware revenue
Even an Intel switch would mean proprietary Apple hardware, not Mac OS X on commodity PCs
Dvorak's proposal of three simultaneous platforms (PowerPC, Itanium, and standard PCs) contradicts Apple's strength in product line clarity
money_quotes: Dvorak's modus operandi is to instigate. He is a button-pusher, seldom if ever trying to inform, preferring instead to inflame.
Just remember that Dvorak is pulling all of this out of his ass.
Note the use of 'Will' in lieu of 'Would', subtly casting his conjecture as fact.
Sure you would, you cheap bastard. Apple's Switch campaign is an attempt to get PC users to buy thousands of dollars of Apple hardware, not hundreds of dollars of Apple software.
The vast majority of Wintel PCs are sold as disposable business plumbing — the adding machines and typewriters of the 21st Century. Apple wants no part of this low-margin market.
The only way to see the Mac as unsuccessful is to compare it to Windows on Microsoft's terms — market share and raw profit.
Part of Dvorak's sucker argument is the notion that it would be easy for Apple to make the switch. Don't be fooled. Possible? Maybe. But it would be both technically difficult and financially risky.
posts.key_insight: Switching to Intel would require solving the impossible emulation problem for existing software while threatening Apple's hardware-dependent business model — obstacles Dvorak conveniently ignores in favor of controversy.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that competition is the essential driver of software progress, using the page layout market as his case study. QuarkXPress rose by outcompeting PageMaker, then stagnated for a decade without a rival. Adobe InDesign arrived as a superior, cheaper alternative that can even read Quark files, yet Quark still dominates. Gruber attributes this to 'stickiness' — the accumulated switching costs of UI familiarity, document formats, corporate inertia, and workflow automation. He suggests InDesign may have been too late to unseat an entrenched incumbent, though its momentum is slowly building.
key_points: Competition drove rapid improvement in page layout software; Quark's monopoly after PageMaker's decline led directly to stagnation
InDesign is widely acknowledged as superior, cheaper, and from a more liked company, yet still hasn't displaced QuarkXPress — illustrating that product quality alone doesn't win markets
Software 'stickiness' operates at multiple levels: UI familiarity, document format lock-in, corporate procurement inertia, and investment in automated workflows
Large enterprises are the strongest source of stickiness because switching costs compound across training, licensing, and production workflow changes
InDesign has slow but real momentum — designers who switch are happy, and those who haven't are considering it
money_quotes: What necessity is to invention, competition is to progress.
It was competition that gave designers a better page layout application.
InDesign may not be too little, but it might have been too late.
Serious application software gets sticky over time. It starts slowly, but eventually it's difficult to peel users and their apps apart.
Document formats are another source of stickiness (most spectacularly used by Microsoft to attach users to the Office suite).
It's not just general conservatism that causes large companies to be reluctant to switch software platforms, it's cost.
A lone designer might be able to switch from Quark to InDesign rather easily; but a large company likely has an investment in automated workflow.
Slow and steady sometimes does win the race.
posts.key_insight: Superior software doesn't automatically win markets because entrenched applications accumulate multiple layers of stickiness — UI familiarity, document formats, corporate inertia, and workflow dependencies — that collectively outweigh any single product's technical advantages.

posts.summary: Gruber extends his analysis of software stickiness from page layout to web browsers, arguing that Internet Explorer's dominance is uniquely vulnerable because Microsoft has no revenue incentive to keep improving it. Unlike Microsoft's paid software, where the company competes against its own older versions, IE was built to kill Netscape rather than generate money, so once it achieved dominance Microsoft lost interest. Gruber observes that the browser market is now resurgent with superior alternatives like Safari, Camino, and OmniWeb 4.5, which just switched to Apple's WebCore rendering engine. He argues browsers are the least sticky major application category because switching costs are minimal — you only need to move bookmarks. The feared lock-in through proprietary Microsoft web technologies never materialized, and web standards are gaining ground.
key_points: Microsoft's genius is competing against older versions of its own products, something Quark failed to do — but IE is the exception since it generates no revenue and Microsoft has no incentive to improve it post-dominance
The browser market is resurgent precisely because IE stagnated after achieving its goal of killing Netscape, creating openings for Safari, Camino, OmniWeb, Opera, and Phoenix
OmniWeb's switch to Apple's WebCore engine demonstrates how separating the rendering engine from the browser application enables rapid competitive shifts
Browsers are uniquely low-stickiness software because switching costs are minimal and the feared IE proprietary lock-in of the web never materialized
Web standards gaining popularity over proprietary technologies is a victory for common sense and keeps the browser market contestable
money_quotes: After killing competing products and companies, it competes with older versions of its own products.
The message Quark has been trying to sell is 'The new version of XPress does new things in addition to page layout.' What they should have been selling is 'The new version does page layout better.'
A large part of IE's market share consists of people who just don't care, or don't even know, which web browser they're using.
Internet Explorer's purpose was to dominate the browser market, not to generate revenue. Once it achieved dominance, many declared the browser market dead.
The demise of the browser market has been greatly exaggerated. It is in fact resurgent, with more competition now than in 1996.
It's hard to drop your own code, but this is clearly the right move for The Omni Group.
I can't think of any other major application category where popularity can rise and fall as quickly as web browsers.
posts.key_insight: Internet Explorer's dominance is uniquely fragile because, unlike Microsoft's paid software, there is no revenue incentive to keep improving it — and browsers have the lowest switching costs of any major application category.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights three useful command-line tools for Mac OS X. First, he discusses Jason Perkins's PHP command-line installer that complements Marc Liyanage's PHP Apache module package. Second, he covers Gary Kerbaugh's 'Born Again Remove Function,' a shell script that moves files to the Trash instead of permanently deleting them, echoing Gruber's earlier argument about the Finder's behavior. Third, he spotlights Nicholas Riley's appswitch utility, which allows fast application switching and listing from the command line, far outperforming AppleScript for the same tasks.
key_points: PHP 4.3 gained official command-line support, and Jason Perkins created an easy installer to add it to Mac OS X
A shell script implementation of rm that moves files to the Trash instead of permanently deleting them brings safer Unix behavior to Mac OS X
Nicholas Riley's appswitch utility provides near-instantaneous application switching and process listing from the command line, far faster than AppleScript alternatives
The post reflects the early Mac OS X ethos of bridging Unix command-line power with Mac usability conventions
money_quotes: The best thing I can say about it as a language is that it's like a very simplified version of Perl.
Download, install, done. It's that easy.
The idea isn't that you should replace the real rm command with Kerbaugh's version, but that you should put it somewhere like ~/bin/.
And best of all, appswitch is very, very fast. Like instantaneous fast.
posts.key_insight: The best Mac OS X command-line tools are those that bridge Unix power with Mac conventions, like making rm respect the Trash or switching GUI apps instantly from the terminal.

posts.summary: Gruber critiques Dan Gillmor's San Jose Mercury News article about NeXT developers thriving on Mac OS X, arguing it unfairly implies that NeXT-derived parts of OS X are superior while classic Mac OS contributions are mere legacy baggage. He pushes back on the notion that Mac OS X is simply NeXT with a new coat of paint, insisting it is a genuine fusion of both platforms. Gruber also challenges the widespread myth that Cocoa apps are inherently better than Carbon apps, using iTunes as a counterexample of excellent Carbon engineering. He argues that the quality of software depends on the quality of programmers, not on which framework they use, and that the NeXT-vs-Mac tribalism is misguided and harmful to the platform.
key_points: Mac OS X is not merely NeXT with a Mac compatibility layer — it genuinely incorporates major technologies from both NeXT and classic Mac OS, like QuickTime and Apple events.
Calling Mac OS customers 'legacy' is absurd when the leading Mac apps (Office, Photoshop, BBEdit) transitioned directly to OS X rather than being replaced.
The belief that Cocoa is inherently superior to Carbon is a myth — iTunes, the best-performing app in Apple's iLife suite, is Carbon, built by veteran Mac programmers.
Small developer teams have always been the backbone of the Mac software ecosystem, not a new phenomenon enabled by NeXT/Cocoa tools.
The NeXT-vs-Mac tribalism among developers is counterproductive when all Mac OS X developers share a commitment to quality software and the Mac market.
money_quotes: I forget: which group brought DOS-style filename extensions to the party? And which group brought in a 30-year-old Unix file permissions model that resulted in thousands of Mac OS X users being disallowed from emptying their Trash?
It's absurd to claim that Apple could have screwed things up by 'accommodating' its existing Mac OS customers. Without those customers, who did they have? The massive NeXT market?
Mac OS X is, amazingly and successfully, something much more. It is NeXTStep and it is the Mac OS.
You really don't want to know how your sausage is made; you just want it to taste good.
The fact is, good programs are fast, and poor programs are slow, regardless if they're Cocoa or Carbon.
Even with the best application framework in the world, good apps are only going to come from good programmers.
Mac users have been seeded with this idea that Cocoa is a magic bullet, and Carbon is a rusty bucket. This myth needs to be dispelled, not promulgated.
posts.key_insight: The quality of Mac software depends on the talent of its programmers, not on whether it uses Cocoa or Carbon, and the tribalism between NeXT and classic Mac communities undermines a platform that draws real strength from both traditions.

posts.summary: Gruber critiques Andrew Stone of Stone Design for marketing his FontSight utility by claiming Cocoa apps are the only 'truly native' Mac OS X applications, thereby dismissing Carbon apps as inferior. He argues this is misleading bigotry that exploits users' lack of technical knowledge, since Carbon is a deep, legitimate part of Mac OS X, not merely a compatibility layer. Gruber notes that major Mac developers like Adobe, Microsoft, Bare Bones, and Macromedia all ship Carbon applications, and that dismissing their work as non-native is insulting to both developers and users. He explains that Cocoa is simply one application framework among several valid approaches, with its own shortcomings like mediocre AppleScript support. The piece concludes that applications should be judged by what they do, not by which framework they use.
key_points: Stone Design's marketing of FontSight deliberately equates 'Cocoa' with 'native OS X,' implying Carbon apps are inferior imitations rather than legitimate Mac software.
Carbon is not a stop-gap compatibility layer but a deep part of Mac OS X, and many premier Mac developers (Adobe, Microsoft, Bare Bones) ship Carbon apps for sound technical reasons.
The word 'native' is vague enough to be weaponized — it sounds obvious but has no precise technical definition, allowing Stone to mislead non-technical users.
Cocoa has its own shortcomings, including mediocre AppleScript support, while established developers have invested years building their own frameworks optimized for their specific needs.
Applications should be evaluated on what they do for users, not on which development framework they were built with.
money_quotes: That's called promoting your features. But instead, Stone took the opportunity to besmirch Carbon applications with the claim that they're 'not native'. That's called bigotry.
Just because Carbon developers aren't taking advantage of Cocoa does not imply they aren't taking advantage of Mac OS X; Cocoa is a part of the OS, not the OS itself.

By equating Cocoa with the adjective 'native' — a word whose meaning is seemingly obvious, but actually vaguely defined — Stone creates the impression that anything other than Cocoa is inherently inferior, not genuine, faking it.

If you accept as fact that Carbon applications are inherently inferior to Cocoa ones, you must therefore accept that Mac developers shipping Carbon applications are either incompetent or are knowingly shipping inferior products to their customers.
Shouldn't we decide which applications to use — like, say, whether to use Adobe Illustrator or Stone Design's Create — based on what they do, rather than how they're made?
No matter how good Cocoa is, it's a bit narrow-minded to assume it's the one and only legitimate application framework for Mac OS X.
And by the way, isn't Cocoa's standard Font Panel palette supposed to obsolete the old-fashioned Font menu? Just curious.
posts.key_insight: Exploiting the vague term 'native' to equate one framework with legitimacy is dishonest marketing that insults both developers and the users it claims to serve.

posts.summary: Gruber describes his discovery of a hidden RTF-to-plain-text conversion tool buried inside Apple's FileMerge application package. After failing to find a suitable Perl module on CPAN for converting RTF files to plain text, he stumbled upon the convertRichTextToAscii utility while exploring FileMerge's preferences. He explains how to use it from the command line and integrate it as a BBEdit shell filter, noting that despite its name, it actually outputs UTF-8 rather than ASCII.
key_points: CPAN surprisingly lacks a simple Perl module for converting RTF to plain text, despite having many RTF-related modules
Apple's FileMerge app contains a hidden convertRichTextToAscii utility buried in its application package resources
The tool works well with RTF files from both Word and TextEdit and can be integrated into BBEdit as a Unix filter
Despite its name, the tool converts to UTF-8 rather than ASCII, which is actually preferable
money_quotes: Most surprisingly, there don't seem to be any Perl modules on CPAN that can do this; there are a bunch that have something to do with RTF (e.g. creating RTF files from scratch), but none that will simply take an RTF file as input and spit out a plain text translation.
The convertRichTextToAscii tool doesn't actually convert RTF to ASCII — it converts to UTF-8. This is a good thing, since technically, the ASCII character set only includes 128 characters.
posts.key_insight: Useful developer utilities are sometimes hidden inside Apple's application packages, waiting to be discovered by anyone curious enough to poke around inside them.

posts.summary: Gruber examines three aspects of the newly released iTunes 4. First, he highlights a scripting bug where shared music libraries break AppleScript's ability to query the current track, affecting popular widgets. Second, he pushes back against Rafe Colburn's claim that Apple's DRM-protected music store is a 'fiendish' lock-in scheme, arguing that Apple delivered the most consumer-friendly option possible given the music labels' requirements. Third, he briefly notes the mystery of how Apple solved the credit card micropayments problem for $0.99 song purchases.
key_points: iTunes 4's music sharing feature breaks the AppleScript interface for querying currently playing shared tracks, affecting third-party widgets
Critics calling Apple's DRM strategy 'fiendish lock-in' ignore that music labels would never have agreed to sell DRM-free files
Apple's music store adds a new option without restricting existing freedoms — users can still rip and play MP3s as before
Given the constraints of rights management, Apple's terms (no subscription, no time limits, CD burning, three computers) are remarkably consumer-friendly
The $0.99 per-song pricing raises interesting questions about how Apple solved the credit card micropayments problem
money_quotes: While the iTunes Music Store is getting much of the press, my favorite feature in iTunes 4 is the music sharing. It's fast, it's simple, and works great.
"Fiendish" seems like a terribly loaded word here. What could Apple possibly have done differently?

From a consumer standpoint, sure, it'd be great if Apple's music store were selling music files in a completely open cross-platform format, with no rights management at all. But the music labels would never have agreed to this.
Fiendish would be if the new version iTunes somehow forced or tricked you into creating a music library dependent on Apple — like if it translated all your MP3 files into a proprietary format.
The fact is, there's nothing in iTunes 4 that prevents you from listening to digital music the same way you did before the iTunes Music Store.
The iTunes Music Store is something more, not something else.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iTunes Music Store isn't a lock-in scheme but an additive feature that offers the most consumer-friendly deal possible within the constraints imposed by music labels.

posts.summary: Gruber notes a reader's observation that iTunes uses the same interface pattern he had previously discussed in Safari: displaying a grayed-out text label inside an edit field when a search filter is selected. This brief post serves as a correction or addendum to his earlier comparison of iTunes and Safari interfaces, acknowledging that the two apps share more UI patterns than he initially realized.
key_points: iTunes uses the same grayed-out text label inside search fields that Safari does when a filter is selected
This observation came from reader Erik Barzeski, showing the value of reader feedback in interface analysis
The post is a brief correction to Gruber's earlier piece comparing iTunes and Safari interface details
money_quotes: when you choose anything other than "All" in the pop-up menu in the Search field, iTunes does the same thing Safari does, putting a grayed-out text label inside the edit field
posts.key_insight: Even a careful observer of Apple's UI details can miss shared interface patterns across the company's own apps, as iTunes and Safari used the same search field labeling convention.

posts.summary: Gruber compares the interface details of iTunes 4 and Safari, arguing that iTunes gets numerous small design decisions right where Safari fails. He highlights four specific areas: color scheme consistency with graphite/blue themes, foreground/background window state distinction, click-through behavior, and search field labeling. The centerpiece argument is against Safari's click-through support, which Gruber considers a terrible design choice that saves negligible time while creating costly accidental clicks. He also praises iTunes's adaptive window resizing on slower machines and its proper use of external labels for text fields.
key_points: iTunes properly dims its toolbar in background windows, making window state obvious at a glance, while Safari's foreground and background states are nearly indistinguishable
Click-through in Safari is a net negative: it saves a fraction of a second but creates costly mistakes when users accidentally invoke buttons while trying to bring a window forward
Background windows effectively serve as large Fitt's Law targets for activation — click-through undermines this by forcing users to carefully avoid controls
iTunes adapts its window resizing behavior to hardware capability, using live resizing on fast machines and outline resizing on slow ones
Safari's practice of placing labels inside text fields violates standard control behavior and is not justified by space savings
money_quotes: Sure, it's just cosmetic. But isn't cosmetic appeal the entire point of offering two color schemes?
Safari's support for click-through is a terrible idea. You might think otherwise, especially if you come from a non-Mac background, but trust me on this.
So click-through doesn't really save you any time at all — but it can cost you plenty.
What happens is that you click in a background window trying just to bring it forward, but inadvertently your click invokes a button.
In effect, the entire visible area of a background window becomes a large button you can use to bring that window forward. Fitt's Law at work once again.

Click-through ruins this. Instead of being able to click anywhere in a window to bring it forward, you need to carefully check to make sure you're not clicking on a button or other click-through enabled control.
"Saving space" is not a valid excuse to completely ignore standard control behavior.
posts.key_insight: Good interface design is built from accumulating small details done right — consistent theming, clear window states, restrained click-through, and proper labeling — and Safari's failures in these areas reveal a lack of attention to Mac interface conventions that iTunes exemplifies.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier iTunes 4 observations with three additional minutiae items. He corrects his previous guess about live resizing being tied to Quartz Extreme, noting it's actually determined by processor type (G4 vs G3). He shares a reader tip about suppressing live resizing with the Command key, addresses reports of ugly fonts caused by anti-aliasing settings, and investigates a hidden menu in iTunes 4 that appears to be a workaround for implementing keyboard shortcuts without cluttering the standard menus.
key_points: iTunes 4 live resizing is determined by processor (G4 vs G3), not Quartz Extreme as previously guessed
Holding Command suppresses live resizing even on G4 Macs — a trick Gruber wishes were system-wide
A hidden menu in iTunes 4 is likely not a bug but a workaround for implementing keyboard shortcuts for non-standard menu items
Apple maintained a legacy Cmd-Y shortcut for Preferences alongside the new standard Cmd-comma, creating redundancy Gruber thinks should have been cut
money_quotes: Even on a G4, you can suppress live resizing in iTunes by holding down the Command key. This would be quite useful system-wide.
This is definitely a little squirrelly.
My guess, however, is that this is not a bug, per se, but rather it's how iTunes is implementing keyboard shortcuts for commands that aren't regular menu items.
I think they would have been better off just getting rid of the old prefs shortcut.
posts.key_insight: Even Apple's own flagship apps resort to slightly hacky workarounds when UI conventions don't cleanly accommodate desired functionality, and the right solution is usually to cut legacy baggage rather than hide it.

posts.summary: Gruber argues against pervasive click-through (where clicking controls in background windows activates them without first bringing the window forward) as a default UI behavior, responding to readers who disagreed with his earlier criticism of Safari's click-through support. He dismantles the argument that click-through mimics real-world behavior by pointing out that the real world lacks the concept of an 'active application,' making the analogy fundamentally flawed. The piece expands into a broader comparison of Mac and Windows UI paradigms, arguing that the Mac's application-centric model with clear foreground/background window distinctions serves users far better than Windows' attempt to blur application boundaries. Gruber contends that Windows' pervasive click-through is a major reason most Windows users work in maximized full-screen mode, unable to comfortably manage multiple windows. He concludes that click-through should be off by default and enabled only for non-destructive actions like drag-and-drop, citing the Finder's policies as the gold standard.
key_points: The frontmost window concept is essential to Mac fluency, and click-through undermines it by making background windows look active and harder to distinguish from the foreground

Windows' attempt to blur the application/window distinction and enable pervasive click-through forces most users into maximized full-screen mode because context becomes impossible to maintain otherwise
The Mac's application-centric paradigm with a single menu bar reinforces context and identity far better than Windows' window-centric approach

Click-through saves one mouse click but risks destructive or annoying unintended actions, making it a poor default—the Finder's selective approach (drag-and-drop yes, toolbar buttons no) is the correct policy
Mac OS X currently suffers from anarchy in click-through policies, with each application establishing its own inconsistent rules
money_quotes: This is yet another example of why blind emulation of the real world doesn't make for good human interface design.
The Mac paradigm enforces a three-level hierarchy: you've got the system, which runs applications, which display windows.

The Windows paradigm tries to eliminate the middleman, presenting a system, which displays windows — i.e. the idea is not that your windows belong to applications, but that they belong to the Windows system itself. The problem with this is that it's an illusion.
Apple chose to make these task-level distinctions at the application level, and in my opinion, it's a distinction that has served Mac users extremely well for almost 20 years.
In short, with Windows-style pervasive click-through, you need to click very carefully if your intention is merely to bring a window from the back to the front.

In short, the most that can be said for enabling click-through for buttons in background windows is that it saves you one measly extra mouse click. In most cases, the window you're clicking in is coming to the front anyway. The downside, however, can be significant.
Better is a default policy where click-through is off by default, and enabled only for exceptions where there is little or no downside when click-through isn't intended.
posts.key_insight: Click-through should be off by default because the small convenience of saving one click is far outweighed by the risk of unintended actions and the erosion of the visual distinction between foreground and background windows that makes multi-window workflows possible.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Mac OS X's inconsistent click-through behavior is a systemic problem that only Apple can fix. He builds on earlier debate and Matthew Thomas's arguments to make the case that pervasive click-through violates Fitts's Law by turning background windows from large, easy click targets into minefields of unintended actions. He proposes a three-part solution: mandate correct behavior in Apple's Human Interface Guidelines, update Cocoa frameworks to default to proper behavior, and ensure Apple's own apps follow the guidelines. He emphasizes that these small usability details, while invisible to most users, shape behavior through conditioning and are exactly the kind of thing that distinguishes the Mac experience.
key_points: Click-through defeats Fitts's Law by turning large background windows into irregularly-shaped regions where users must carefully avoid hitting active controls
The current inconsistency across Mac OS X apps is worse than either universal approach because users can't form reliable expectations
Only Apple can fix this systemically through three steps: updating the HIG, changing Cocoa defaults, and fixing their own apps
Users who suffer from accidental click-through perceive the resulting annoyances as random computer misbehavior, never identifying the root cause
Command-click exists as a nifty shortcut for users who deliberately want click-through behavior, making pervasive click-through unnecessary
money_quotes: You can't plug leaks in this dam one hole at a time.
It is not so much about being better, as it is about sucking less.
The details do matter, at least on the Mac OS, the entire success of which is based on getting small details right.
A policy of You can implement click-through if you want to promotes neither consistency nor usability.

The problems that result from unintended click-through appear random to most people. This is not because they're stupid, but because they're simply not at all interested in learning the details of how their computer works.
A policy of Do what I say, not what I do does not make for credible leadership.
Pervasive click-through leads to usability paper cuts.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's inconsistent click-through behavior is a systemic usability problem that only Apple can solve by updating the HIG, fixing Cocoa defaults, and leading by example in its own apps.

posts.summary: Gruber evaluates Safari's tabbed browsing implementation, praising its custom UI controls as superior to Camino's standard Aqua tabs, but identifies a critical flaw: tabs are completely unscriptable via AppleScript. Safari's scripting dictionary treats each window as containing only one document, making it impossible to access, create, or even count tabs programmatically. This breaks his previously published Safari scripts for URL management. He proposes a hierarchical object model where browser windows contain lists of documents corresponding to tabs. A brief postscript notes that Safari's WebCore engine fixed monospaced font anti-aliasing bugs he had previously reported.
key_points: Safari's custom tab controls are superior to Camino's standard Aqua tabs because they're left-justified, open in intuitive order, and have close boxes
Safari's tab close boxes should resemble standard Aqua close buttons rather than branching out with a custom design, since widgets that act similarly should look similar
Safari's AppleScript dictionary is completely unaware of tabs — 'every document' only returns the frontmost tab per window, making all other tabbed pages unreachable
Gruber proposes a hierarchical scripting model with browser window objects each containing a list of documents for each tab
money_quotes: There's nothing wrong with custom widgets, if the widgets are meant to provide features not provided by any standard controls.
Widgets that act similarly should look similar.
Those are reasons for Apple to improve the standard close buttons, not for Safari to branch out on its own.
There's a huge problem with Safari's tabbed browsing, and it has nothing to do with how they look. It's that they're utterly unscriptable.
Needless to say, this sucks.
The other 18 documents are unreachable and unknowable via AppleScript.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed UI feature is fundamentally incomplete if it lacks corresponding scriptability, because power users depend on automation to extend the browser beyond its built-in capabilities.

posts.summary: Gruber announces SmartyPants 1.3, a bug-fix release for his typographic quote-curling plugin for Movable Type. The post focuses on a particularly tricky technical problem: when HTML tags split quote characters into isolated single-character tokens, SmartyPants had no surrounding context to determine whether quotes should curl left or right. The solution was to have the tokenizer remember the last character of the previous text token, providing the missing context. Gruber walks through the tokenization process with clear examples showing how the bug manifested. He admits the fix was obvious in retrospect.
key_points: SmartyPants 1.3 fixes edge cases where quote characters were being curled in the wrong direction
The core challenge is tokenizing HTML to process text while leaving attribute quotes inside tags untouched
When HTML tags isolate quote marks into single-character tokens, there's no adjacent context to determine curl direction
The fix is elegantly simple: remember the last character of the previous text token and use it as context for the next token
money_quotes: The main trick with educating quotes in HTML markup is that you need to take care not to touch the quotes inside tags — the quotes surrounding HTML attribute values need to remain plain ASCII straight quotes.

The problem is that the quote characters end up getting split into single-character tokens, all by themselves. With no preceding or succeeding characters to provide context, SmartyPants has no hint which way the quote should be curled.
Even a two-character token usually provides enough context to make an accurate guess as to whether a quote is opening or closing. But it's just not possible with only one character.
The solution seems so obvious, I'm embarrassed not to have thought of it months ago.
What SmartyPants now does is cheat by remembering the last character of the previous text token.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best solutions to persistent bugs are embarrassingly simple — in this case, carrying one character of state between tokens was all that was needed to resolve a long-standing context problem in typographic quote processing.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews Jeffrey Zeldman's 'Designing With Web Standards,' praising it as a practical guide that answers 'how' and 'why' rather than serving as a dry reference. He uses the review as a springboard to argue that professional web designers should learn to hand-code HTML and CSS rather than relying on WYSIWYG tools like Dreamweaver and GoLive. He draws a key distinction between print and web design: in print, the output is ink on paper, but on the web, the markup itself is the end product, making WYSIWYG a misnomer. Gruber praises Zeldman's pragmatic rather than dogmatic approach to web standards advocacy, noting the book focuses on practical benefits rather than moral arguments. The review also touches on the hand-coding workflow, where designers still use visual tools like Photoshop for design but translate to markup by hand.
key_points: WYSIWYG web editors like Dreamweaver and GoLive are a misnomer because web designers cannot control final presentation the way print designers can — the markup itself is the end product.
Hand-coding web designers still design visually using tools like Photoshop; they simply don't expect visual tools to generate production-quality markup.
The most effective arguments for web standards are pragmatic (easier for designers, better for clients), not moral or dogmatic.
Good web design is about finding the sweet spot between speed, usability, attractiveness, and compliance — all design involves trade-offs.
Dreamweaver and GoLive are arguably more complex than the HTML and CSS languages they claim to simplify.
money_quotes: You can lead a web designer to standards, but you can't make him drink. Designing With Web Standards will make him thirsty.
In web design, however, the end result is the markup itself. The final presentation of the markup is out of your hands.

It is thus a misnomer to describe Dreamweaver and GoLive as "WYSIWYG" applications. What you see is what you get implies that you have complete control over the final presentation of your project. In print, this is possible. On the web, this is impossible.
When your argument boils down to "just because", it is indistinguishable from dogma, and dogma turns many people away.
An architect draws extensive sketches first, before producing actual blueprints. So too with web designers.
All design is about trade-offs. A web site that looks great but is slow to load, or only works in certain web browsers, is a failure.
Mr. Zeldman is a master craftsman; reading his book is like serving as his apprentice.
I am likely to squander these earnings on beer and poker.
posts.key_insight: On the web, the markup is the end product — not a means to an end like print's Postscript — which is why hand-coding HTML and CSS is a non-negotiable professional skill for web designers.

posts.summary: Gruber reports on his experience with SpamAssassin after his web host added support for it, finding it catches 90% of spam on its own and 97% when combined with his personal Mailsmith filters. He had been skeptical, expecting that smart spammers would simply test their messages against the open-source tool to evade detection. However, he found that 'smart spammers' are rare and SpamAssassin's frequently updated ruleset stays ahead of most countermeasures. Despite the strong results, he warns that a 97% detection rate becomes inadequate as spam volume scales from hundreds to thousands per week.
key_points: SpamAssassin alone catches 90% of spam; combined with personal filters, the detection rate reaches 97%
The open-source nature of SpamAssassin theoretically lets spammers test and evade it, but smart spammers are apparently rare
SpamAssassin's constantly updated ruleset helps it stay ahead of spammer countermeasures
A 97% detection rate is fine at 500 spams per week but falls apart at 5,000 — the direction things are heading
money_quotes: As I type these words, I've received exactly 450 spams in the last 7 days.
A smart spammer, quite obviously, would do well to test their messages by sending them through SpamAssassin, then tweaking their content until they slip through undetected.
"Smart spammers", apparently, are a very rare breed.
My concern, however, is that "very good" is not good enough.
