97 percent is a fine detection rate when you receive 500 spams a week. It's not so good if you receive 5,000 spams a week — and that's where it seems we're heading.
I do get a few false positives, but they're almost always borderline spam anyway — marketing messages from online retailers, or five-times forwarded "jokes" from distant relatives who use AOL.
posts.key_insight: Even a 97% spam detection rate becomes inadequate when spam volume grows exponentially, making 'very good' filtering fundamentally insufficient against a scaling problem.

posts.summary: Gruber amplifies complaints from Chris Nandor and Buzz Andersen about Mac OS X's persistent bugs, particularly in the Finder, arguing that Apple keeps shipping flashy new features while fundamental problems remain unfixed. He contends that the OS X Finder is not merely a poor design but a poor design poorly implemented, riddled with minor bugs that have persisted across multiple major releases. He illustrates this with a specific example: AppleScript's inability to reliably get the Finder selection in column view, a bug that has existed for nearly the entire lifespan of OS X. He dismisses the perpetual hype cycle where each new OS X release promises Finder improvements that never materialize, noting Apple has had seven years on the project and deserves no more slack.
key_points: Apple keeps prioritizing showy new features like transparent graphics and piles over fixing fundamental, longstanding bugs in the Finder and other core apps
The OS X Finder suffers from both poor design and poor implementation — the bugs compound the design problems
A specific AppleScript bug where getting the Finder selection fails in column view has been broken across nearly every OS X release
The hype cycle around each new OS X release creates false hope that the Finder will finally be fixed, deflecting criticism into speculation about the next version
The classic Mac OS Finder may not have been universally loved, but it was reliable and not buggy in the way the OS X Finder is
money_quotes: The OS X Finder isn't just a poor design — it's a poor design poorly implemented.
Guess what — it's been seven years since Apple started the project. They don't deserve any slack whatsoever regarding the quality of the Finder.
I'd probably shut the fuck up about it if it weren't for all the bugs.

The hype cycle for upcoming major Mac OS X revisions inevitably includes rumors that the Finder will see huge performance improvements. And then the update ships, and the improvements are less than encouraging.
But, hey, why gripe when we can start speculating about the next major Mac OS X update? Surely the Finder will be improved eventually the thinking goes.
Maybe you didn't like the old Finder, but you sure couldn't call it 'buggy'.
Great, so I will be able to have more transparent graphics. Yippee. I just don't care.
posts.key_insight: Apple's pattern of shipping flashy new OS X features while leaving the Finder riddled with longstanding bugs represents a fundamental failure of priorities that no amount of hype-cycle optimism can excuse.

posts.summary: Gruber defends Apple's decision to disable Internet-wide music sharing in iTunes 4.0.1, arguing it was a rational response to third-party tools like iLeech and iSlurp turning iTunes into Napster-style piracy software. He acknowledges the feature wasn't accidental but characterizes it as a social experiment that failed when abuse put it in direct conflict with the iTunes Music Store. He methodically rebuts critics like Cory Doctorow and Steve Mallett, pointing out factual errors in their arguments while maintaining that Apple's move only prevented iTunes itself from being a piracy tool, not P2P sharing in general. Gruber frames the choice as pragmatic: Apple could either disable the feature or wage a costly, foolish legal battle with the RIAA.
key_points: iTunes 4.0's Internet sharing was intentional, not accidental — Apple's own help documentation described how to connect to specific IP addresses outside local subnets
Third-party apps like iLeech and iSlurp turned iTunes into copying software, putting Internet sharing in direct conflict with the iTunes Music Store
Apple's decision prevented iTunes from being used as a piracy tool, but did not prevent users from sharing music via other software
Cory Doctorow mischaracterized the iTunes 4.0.1 release notes as calling the removal an 'enhancement' when Apple clearly listed it as a separate clause
The iTunes Music Store was not yet a 'major revenue stream' — at a million songs per week it represented roughly $15 million quarterly against $1.475 billion in total revenue
money_quotes: I'm baffled — not by Apple's decision, but by much of the reaction to it.
Any industry that expresses outright hostility toward its most enthusiastic customers is in trouble.
Apple is not the RIAA, nor is Apple in any position whatsoever to dictate to the RIAA.
Choose your battles wisely, the saying goes, and regardless of righteousness, a P2P battle against the RIAA would be just about the most foolish thing Apple could do.
There is no logical way you can claim that iTunes could act, simultaneously, as an RIAA-sanctioned front end to the ITMS, and as bootlegging software the RIAA deems illegal.
Apple's decision was not about preventing bootlegging; it was about preventing the use of iTunes as a bootlegging tool. There is a huge difference.
It's a world I don't want to live in. — Terrific. Might I suggest starting here?
posts.key_insight: Apple's removal of Internet sharing from iTunes was not capitulation to the RIAA but a rational act of self-preservation — you cannot simultaneously sell music and distribute it for free through the same application.

posts.summary: This link-roundup post covers several Mac OS X topics from mid-2003. Gruber highlights a hack restoring iTunes internet sharing after Apple removed it in 4.0.1, critiques Adobe's bloated new Acrobat Reader, amplifies Jonathan Rentzsch's exposé of the Finder's deceptive file extension hiding, documents another Finder inconsistency in List view, and shares a useful drag-and-drop tip. The thread running through these items is frustration with software that obscures information or behaves inconsistently, particularly Apple's own Finder.
key_points: A developer quickly hacked around Apple's removal of internet-wide music sharing in iTunes 4.0.1, illustrating the 'Steve giveth, Steve taketh away' dynamic
Adobe Reader 6 is criticized as bloated corporate software that betrays its purpose as a simple document viewer
The OS X Finder's file extension hiding is called out as actively deceptive, renaming files behind the user's back
Multiple Finder inconsistencies in List view show Apple failing to maintain the Mac's traditional interface polish
A useful hidden feature: Command-Tab works mid-drag-and-drop in OS X
money_quotes: Steve giveth, and Steve taketh away.
The product that should be a simple document viewer now looks and feels like it was from Microsoft.
Try to rename Foo.txt back to Foo.bar. You can't.
Oh sure, the Finder will list the file's name as Foo.bar again, but the Finder is lying to you.
Personally, I think these file suffix-hiding shenanigans are the single most un-Mac-like aspect of Mac OS X.

It's like a smelly deposit of dog shit on the carpet. Mac OS X users have learned to hold their breath and walk around it; whereas Mac OS 9 users gag, and demand to know why Apple hasn't cleaned this up.
posts.key_insight: The OS X Finder's file extension hiding represents a fundamental betrayal of the Mac's tradition of honest, transparent user interfaces.

posts.summary: Gruber argues against TrackBack, Movable Type's notification protocol for linking between blogs, on the grounds that it is unnecessarily complex and exclusionary. He contends that TrackBack's requirement for both parties to run compatible software creates an unfair barrier that leaves out most of the web, including static sites, Blogger-powered blogs, and desktop publishing tools like Radio UserLand. Instead, he advocates for simpler alternatives: HTTP referrer tracking (which he has just implemented on Daring Fireball) and Technorati's link-indexing service, both of which work with plain HTML links and require no special software. He acknowledges TrackBack's richer data but argues that for communication protocols, lowest-common-denominator universality is a virtue, not a weakness.
key_points: TrackBack is a two-way protocol that only works between sites running compatible software, excluding the vast majority of the web from participation.
Simple HTTP referrer tracking and Technorati's link cosmos provide similar functionality to TrackBack without requiring any special software or protocols from either party.
For communication protocols and web standards, universal accessibility (lowest-common-denominator) is a feature, not a bug — unlike desktop software where differentiation is valuable.
TrackBack has practical usability problems: it's painful to send, relies on cryptic RDF for auto-discovery, and conflicts with static site generation.
The web's fundamental strength is that creating and linking between sites is simple; TrackBack undermines this by raising the barrier to participation.
money_quotes: A list of TrackBack pings on your site is only going to reflect incoming links from other web sites running TrackBack software. But most web sites don't speak TrackBack.
No dispute, it's useful and interesting to see a list of web sites linking to a particular article or web site. But it makes no sense to limit such a list to a small subset of the web.
The entire reason the web took off so quickly, and has continued to grow, is that it's really quite easy to create your own web site.
TrackBack is unnecessary as a connector between web sites. We already have something to connect web sites: the link.
The key to understanding why Technorati is so damn cool is that all you need to do to get tracked is link.
All you need to do is link. Technorati does the rest.
For communication protocols and web standards, lowest-common-denominator status is generally a good thing. The opposite is true for desktop software and hardware.
If you decide to display TrackBacks on your web site instead of referrers, you are excluding all of the people who aren't able or willing to use TrackBack for their web sites.
posts.key_insight: The link is the web's native connection protocol, and any system that requires special software on both ends to track connections between sites is solving the wrong problem at the cost of the web's fundamental openness.

posts.summary: Gruber clarifies his earlier criticism of TrackBack, emphasizing that his objection is specifically with using TrackBack for link tracking, not with the protocol itself. He acknowledges that TrackBack has legitimate creative uses, like Matt Haughey's WinAmp integration, but maintains that referrer tracking is superior for knowing who links to you because it captures everyone. He also addresses the criticism that TrackBacks should be compared to comments rather than referrers, arguing that in practice TrackBack excerpts make poor comments because they're written as article synopses, not responses. When intermingled with genuine comments, TrackBacks read like non sequiturs that disrupt the conversational thread.
key_points: The criticism is specifically about using TrackBack for link tracking, not about the TrackBack protocol itself, which has other valid uses
TrackBack's advantage of using individual posts as units of measure (via canonical permalinks) is real but still doesn't make it appropriate for link tracking
Referrer tracking is better for link tracking because it includes everyone, not just those who actively send a ping
TrackBack excerpts serve double duty as RSS synopses and as comments on other blogs, making them poor at both when optimized for one
TrackBacks intermingled with actual comments read like non sequiturs that break the conversational thread
money_quotes: The problems with using TrackBack for link tracking are manifold.
Many people think TrackBack is only for link tracking, which is an easy mistake to make, since it's the only thing most people use it for.
So there's TrackBack being used for something very cool, but also totally unrelated to link tracking.
If you simply want to track who is linking to your web site, referrer tracking is a better indication than TrackBack, because it includes everyone.
It's hard to write a single blurb that is appropriate in both contexts, and most people don't even seem to try.
TrackBacks intermingled with actual comments tend to read like non sequiturs.
posts.key_insight: TrackBack is a useful protocol being misapplied as a link-tracking tool, and its dual-purpose excerpt field makes it a poor substitute for both referrer logs and genuine comments.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the death of Internet Explorer for Mac, arguing that the real explanation lies in economics rather than Microsoft PR statements. He traces how the browser wars between Netscape and Microsoft destroyed the commercial browser market by making browsers free, which eliminated any incentive for sustained development. Mac IE 5 was the best browser in the world when it debuted in 2000, but with no revenue stream and no competition, Microsoft had no motivation to improve it. Gruber concludes that Safari's arrival was a consequence, not the cause, of Microsoft's abandonment of Mac IE.
key_points: The browser wars permanently crippled the commercial browser market by establishing free as the default price, making it impossible for companies to fund ongoing development through sales
Mac IE 5's Tasman rendering engine made it the best browser in the world at launch, but being free and bundled meant there was zero financial incentive to keep improving it
Microsoft's abandonment of Mac IE was inevitable and predated Safari — follow the money, and there was no money
Open source is not a free lunch either, as Mozilla was largely developed by full-time paid AOL/Netscape engineers
Apple's independence from Microsoft-controlled software was essential for the platform's survival
money_quotes: Of course, there's no such thing as a free browser. What we saved in software license fees, we lost in the competitive marketplace.

You need full-time effort from top-notch programmers to produce a quality web browser, but you can't make money to pay the engineers by selling the browser, because your competitors are giving away their browsers free of charge.
Don't look for the answer in statements from Microsoft PR. Instead, use the advice Deep Throat gave Woodward and Bernstein: Follow the money.
In the case of IE Mac, there is no money. Neither directly nor indirectly did Microsoft stand to profit by continuing to fund the development of Mac IE.
With no competition, and no upgrades to sell, what motivation did Microsoft have to continue to move the product forward? None.
Open source is no free lunch, either. The Mozilla project has been in large part developed by full-time AOL/Netscape engineers.
posts.key_insight: The death of Mac IE was not a strategic decision but an economic inevitability — when you destroy the market for commercial browsers by giving them away free, you also destroy the incentive to keep developing them.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews the release of Mailsmith 2.0, a Mac OS X-only email client from Bare Bones Software, highlighting its notable features including SpamSieve integration, PGP support, and Address Book integration. He acknowledges its main limitation—lack of IMAP support—before pivoting to a broader argument about why serious email users shouldn't settle for Apple Mail just because it's free. He critiques the common practice of calling Apple's email client 'Mail.app' with a detailed aside on why filename extensions shouldn't constitute application names. His core argument is that Apple Mail tries to serve everyone and consequently serves power users poorly, and that refusing to pay for a better tool out of principle is foolish.
key_points: Mailsmith 2.0 is a solid, Mac OS X-only update with SpamSieve spam filtering, PGP, and Address Book integration, free for existing 1.5 users
The practice of calling Apple's email client 'Mail.app' is wrong—the .app suffix is filesystem metadata, not part of the application's name, and an embarrassing holdover from NeXT
Most people treat email as ephemeral and Apple Mail serves them fine, but power users who complain about it yet refuse to pay for alternatives are being stubborn
Apple Mail suffers from specific design shortcomings: mandatory top-posting, weak AppleScript support, and the gimmicky drawer interface for mailboxes
By trying to serve everyone from casual grandmothers to mailing-list power users, Apple Mail ends up too complicated for beginners and too underpowered for experts
money_quotes: If you absolutely need to use IMAP, that's unfortunate, because you're missing out on what is truly a crackerjack email client.
E.g. imagine if Safari were instead named "Web".
Using the filename as a stash for file system metadata is a terrible idea, and is an embarrassing holdover from legacy NeXT systems.
Email must be free, Apple Mail is free, thus I must use Apple Mail.
Most people aren't very serious about their email.
You're nothing more than a stubborn goof if you refuse to consider coughing up a few bucks for something better designed for your needs.
Isn't it a bit odd to expect a single email client to meet the needs of everyone from three-messages-a-week grandmothers to subscribed-to-20-mailing-lists nerds?

Attempting to be all things for all emailers, Apple Mail is stretched very thin indeed — overly complicated for beginners and those with simple needs, yet underpowered and clumsy for those who seek a precise and powerful tool.
posts.key_insight: Free bundled software that tries to serve everyone inevitably becomes too complex for casual users and too limited for power users, and refusing to pay for a better tool out of principle is self-defeating stubbornness.

posts.summary: Gruber offers rapid-fire, irreverent reactions to Apple's WWDC 2003 announcements. He's impressed by the Power Mac G5 and Exposé, dismayed by the metallic Finder windows, and amused by the implications of iChat video conferencing and FileVault encryption. He notes the irony that Preview is only now becoming a competent PDF viewer despite OS X's PDF-based graphics layer, and expresses mixed feelings about Safari 1.0's WebCore progress versus its lack of AppleScript support.
key_points: The metallic Finder windows are a design regression that Gruber finds appalling
Preview finally displaying PDFs well is ironic given that OS X's entire graphics layer is built on PDF
Apple's IDE strategy shift from Project Builder's single-window metaphor to Xcode contradicts earlier positioning
Safari 1.0 ships with strong rendering but still lacks AppleScript support for tabs, a sore point for Gruber
money_quotes: Good god, shoot me.
Everyone is saying: Video conferencing. Everyone is thinking: Amateur porno!
The operating system whose graphics layer is "based on PDF" will now have an application that actually does a good job displaying PDF files? Pinch me.
Teens: protect your porno from your parents' prying eyes.
But I thought the everything-in-one-window Project Builder metaphor was the best thing to hit IDEs since the THINK Pascal debugger?
posts.key_insight: WWDC 2003's announcements are a mixed bag of genuinely impressive technology and baffling design decisions, best appreciated through irreverent one-liners.

posts.summary: Gruber announces an experiment with Google's new AdSense program on Daring Fireball. He notes the irony that Google's contextual ads are pitching Mac OS X Jaguar when his latest article is about its successor, Panther. He reports earning roughly $0.10-0.30 per click but finds the ads aesthetically offensive because Google doesn't allow CSS customization. He frames it as a temporary experiment, inviting readers to click but not to game the system.
key_points: Google AdSense's contextual targeting is imprecise, serving ads for Jaguar on a site discussing its successor Panther
The inability to customize ad appearance via CSS is a dealbreaker for a design-conscious site
Per-click rates vary significantly ($0.10-$0.50) across different publishers with no transparency from Google
Gruber frames monetization as an experiment, prioritizing site aesthetics over revenue
money_quotes: If you haven't bought Jaguar already, somehow I doubt you're going to spend $120 on it now.
The downside, of course, is that the ads are hideously ugly.
I wouldn't hesitate to keep the ads if I were allowed to customize their display via CSS.
But as it stands, they'll have to pay me quite a bit of scratch to tempt me to keep the ads.
Remember, it's an experiment. Feel free to send me a dime by clicking an ad, but please don't game it by clicking madly.
It's humorous to see that Google thinks Hivelogic is a web site about cigars and wedding speeches.
posts.key_insight: Early web advertising forced a tension between monetization and design standards, with Google's AdSense offering revenue at the cost of aesthetic control that design-minded publishers found unacceptable.

posts.summary: Gruber methodically dismantles a Slate article by Paul Boutin claiming that Linux's growing desktop market share poses a serious threat to Apple. He argues the article relies on unsourced statistics from unnamed analysts, makes dishonest price comparisons (a $1,999 Power Mac G5 vs. a $248 Walmart PC), and fundamentally misunderstands who competes with whom. Gruber contends that Apple sells computers, not operating systems, so free Linux software is Microsoft's problem, not Apple's. He further notes that Unix geeks are actually flocking to Mac OS X, not away from it, and that Linux's fragmented desktop environments (KDE vs. GNOME) make it misleading to even call it a single platform. The piece is a case study in how media laziness and the impulse to manufacture conflict produce deeply misleading tech journalism.
key_points: The article's premise relies on unsourced statistics and unnamed analysts, with neither Boutin nor his cited columnist linking to actual data
Apple sells computers, not operating systems — free Linux software threatens Microsoft's licensing revenue, not Apple's hardware business
Unix geeks are switching to Mac OS X in large numbers, the opposite of Boutin's claim that Linux is pulling users away from Macs
Boutin's $1,999 vs. $248 price comparison is a straw man; Apple's actual low-end (eMac at $799) vs. a comparable Walmart setup ($400 with monitor and modem) is far less dramatic
Linux is not a single desktop platform — KDE and GNOME are incompatible environments, making 'Linux desktop market share' a misleading metric
money_quotes: What kind of journalism bases entire articles on statistics without sources?
Linux is like an opponent that doesn't need to breathe. There is nothing to cut off.
Apple does not compete against 'Linux', which is neither a computer manufacturer, or a company, or even a product.
The truth is that Apple doesn't sell 'low-end' machines; they stoop no lower than mid-range, and their mid-range computers are very competitively priced.
Apple might be competing against cheap computers, but Microsoft is competing against free software.
Trying to sell QuarkXPress or Photoshop to Linux users would be like trying to sell Cadillacs to farmers in China.
The difference is that Microsoft used to get a nice cut on every one of these, and now they're not.
Did Boutin find a single actual person who considered a Mac, but went to Linux instead? If so, shouldn't the article mention them? If not, then isn't this entire article utter bunk?
posts.key_insight: Free Linux software is a competitive threat to Microsoft's software licensing business, not to Apple's hardware business, and media narratives that conflate the two reveal a fundamental misunderstanding of both companies' business models.

posts.summary: Gruber issues a correction to his previous claim that KDE and GNOME desktop environments for Linux were incompatible. A reader explained that while they are different desktops with different toolkits, users can run apps from both environments simultaneously. Gruber accepts the correction gracefully but maintains that his broader point still stands: even with cross-desktop compatibility, Linux remains an unattractive market for major commercial software companies like Quark and Adobe.
key_points: KDE and GNOME apps can run alongside each other, similar to how Java Swing apps can run on OS X despite not being native
Gruber acknowledges the factual error while maintaining his broader argument about Linux's commercial viability
Cross-desktop compatibility on Linux doesn't solve the fundamental problem of attracting major commercial software vendors
money_quotes: And so I stand corrected.
It's also beside the point I was trying to make.
It's good for everyone that apps written for KDE can run under GNOME and vice versa, but that still doesn't make Linux an attractive market for commercial software companies like Quark and Adobe.
posts.key_insight: Technical interoperability between Linux desktop environments doesn't address the fundamental problem of Linux's unattractiveness as a commercial software market.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the release of SmartyPants 1.4, his typographic punctuation plugin. The update contains no new features but includes numerous small improvements, particularly better parsing of HTML comments and PHP code blocks. He also notes a correction: the initial zip archive was botched and asks early downloaders to re-download.
key_points: SmartyPants 1.4 released with incremental improvements rather than new features
Improved parsing support for HTML comments and PHP code blocks
Initial release had a botched zip archive that was quickly corrected
money_quotes: No new features, but lots of small improvements, including much better support for parsing HTML comments and PHP code blocks.
posts.key_insight: A brief release announcement for SmartyPants 1.4, reflecting Gruber's ongoing commitment to refining his typographic tools through incremental quality improvements.

posts.summary: Gruber eulogizes Casady & Greene, a beloved Mac software publisher closing after 19 years. He recounts their catalog of stellar titles including Crystal Quest, Glider, SoundJam (which Apple acquired and turned into iTunes), and their flagship Conflict Catcher. He explains why Conflict Catcher was indispensable for managing classic Mac OS extensions, then acknowledges the inevitability of their demise: their key product served a dead operating system, and the web had eliminated the need for small software publishers entirely.
key_points: Casady & Greene published landmark Mac software including SoundJam (which became iTunes) and the indispensable Conflict Catcher utility
Conflict Catcher solved a fundamental problem of classic Mac OS—extension conflicts that caused instability—so well it seemed too good to be true
Their closure was inevitable: their flagship product served an OS Steve Jobs publicly buried, during an economic downturn
The web fundamentally changed software distribution, making small publishers like Casady & Greene obsolete as developers could reach users directly
They represented a standard of quality in publishing—good software with outstanding documentation—that was becoming rare
money_quotes: Crystal Quest was one of those games that was so addictive and time-consuming that it probably had a noticeable drag on the U.S. gross national product.
Conflict Catcher was to Apple's Extensions Manager what BBEdit is to SimpleText.
When people complain that the old Mac OS was unstable, what they really meant is that it was too easy to make the Mac OS unstable by adding conflicting third-party extensions.

When the economy is bad and your flagship product is a troubleshooting tool for an operating system that Steve Jobs put in a coffin onstage over a year ago, well, let's just say that's not a strong position.
It's not just a different market than 1984, it's different than 1994.
SoundJam was so good and so popular that Apple bought the rights to it and hired Robbin, and turned it into iTunes.
posts.key_insight: Casady & Greene's demise illustrates how the twin forces of platform obsolescence and web-enabled self-publishing made the small Mac software publisher—once essential for connecting developers with users—an extinct species.

posts.summary: Gruber traces the evolution of Textile from Dean Allen's original web tool into a broader ecosystem of implementations, highlighting the important distinction between Textile as a markup language and Textile as software. He notes how Brad Choate's MT-Textile plugin for Movable Type expanded the syntax beyond the original, creating ambiguity about what constitutes 'official' Textile. The good news is that Allen and Choate are collaborating to unify the language. The post's second half pivots to a plea for help finding a job for Brad Choate's friend Ron Pacheco, which becomes an occasion for Gruber to deliver a pointed critique of the American health insurance system's cruelty toward the uninsured.
key_points: Textile is both a markup language and a software implementation — an important distinction that gets blurred as multiple implementations diverge
The organic, seat-of-the-pants growth of Textile (PHP original, Perl plugin, Python port) shows how open-source tools evolve through individual initiative
Dean Allen and Brad Choate collaborating on syntax improvements resolves the fragmentation problem
The U.S. health insurance system's employer-dependent model creates catastrophic gaps when people lose jobs, especially for those with pre-existing conditions
money_quotes: That's an important but easily overlooked distinction.

The whole thing is a little seat-of-the-pants (and I mean that in a good way): Dean just wanted to write a tool to make it easier to write for the web; Brad just wanted to use Dean's clever idea with Movable Type; and Mark ports software to Python for kicks.
Those of you who live in civilized nations may not be aware of the depraved state of health insurance here in the U.S.
Worse is what happens if you ever experience a discontinuity in coverage.
Anything that happens when you're without insurance — illness or accident — will be deemed a 'pre-existing condition' and may not be covered even if you obtain health insurance again.
I have some personal experience with exactly this sort of situation, and though I do not know Mr. Pacheco, I know how he must feel.
This is not a sob story (it makes me angry more than anything else, frankly).
posts.key_insight: The organic growth of Textile illustrates how open-source tools evolve through individual passion projects, but the post's real punch comes from Gruber's furious aside on how America's employer-tied health insurance system turns job loss into a medical catastrophe.

posts.summary: Gruber makes the case for independent web publishing as superior to corporate media, using his own site Daring Fireball as exhibit A. He argues that corporate media sites sacrifice design, readability, and content quality in pursuit of ad-driven revenue, while independents can focus on quality because they answer only to readers. He introduces Google AdSense as a less invasive funding mechanism for independent publishers, contrasting it with the animated banner ads and bloated HTML of corporate sites like Business Week. The post also dissects how impression-based advertising incentivizes sensationalism over substance, and explains his plan to fund Daring Fireball through a combination of AdSense clicks and voluntary reader donations.
key_points: The hairpiece analogy illustrates the fundamental tension in designer-client relationships: clients pay for more, but more undermines quality — directly applicable to web design where less markup, fewer graphics, and omitting needless words produces better sites.

Corporate media web sites are objectively worse than independent ones: bloated code, animated ads, unreadable layouts, and invalid HTML — yet they're the ones getting paid, while independents work for free.
Impression-based advertising creates perverse incentives toward sensationalism, rewarding controversial clickbait over insightful analysis, as exemplified by columnists like Dvorak and Haddad.

Google AdSense represents a fundamentally better advertising model for independents: text-only, pay-per-click, no pop-ups, and an order of magnitude more revenue per reader than traditional banner ads.

The web is the only medium where independents consistently produce superior work to corporations, because the barriers to entry are negligible — a text editor and Apache server put you on equal footing with anyone.
money_quotes: Less markup. Less scripting. Fewer navigation elements. Fewer colors. Fewer graphics. Omit needless words. This is how you make a good web site. I know this for a fact.
A good web site costs a lot of money, and for a lot of money, most clients want a lot of web site.
My goal for Daring Fireball is not to attract the largest possible audience. Rather, I'm trying to attract the smartest possible audience.
The distinction worth making is not between professional vs. amateur web sites. That just means whether you're getting paid. The distinction worth making is corporate vs. independent.
Independent web publishers — individuals and small teams — have never considered the web, as a medium, to be a failure. On the contrary, they view it as a roaring success.
With a text editor and an Apache web server, you're on equal footing with any web site in the world.
The lone natural resource in the media landscape is attention.
Any traffic is good traffic when you're charging advertisers per impression.
posts.key_insight: The web is the one medium where independent publishers can outperform corporate media on quality, and Google's pay-per-click advertising finally offers a funding model that doesn't require sacrificing that quality.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Avie Tevanian's tenure as Apple's software chief following his transition to a new role, framing it as a mixed legacy. On the positive side, Tevanian delivered remarkable project management: Mac OS X shipped and improved on a steady annual schedule, while simultaneously maintaining momentum on classic Mac OS 8-9. However, Gruber argues Tevanian's deep loyalty to NeXT technology over Apple's own led to serious usability failures, including the infamous Technote #2034 and the iTunes 2.0 installer disaster. The piece concludes by hoping his successor Bertrand Serlet will prioritize pragmatism and usability over technical dogma.
key_points: Tevanian's greatest achievement was establishing a reliable annual release schedule for Mac OS X, contrasting sharply with Microsoft's multi-year gaps between Windows releases.
Under Tevanian, Apple successfully maintained two major operating systems simultaneously (classic Mac OS 8-9 and Mac OS X), a significant management feat.
Tevanian's dogmatic preference for NeXT technologies over Apple's own led to concrete usability disasters, including the iTunes 2.0 installer bug that deleted entire volumes.
The withdrawn Technote #2034, reportedly written by Tevanian personally, exemplified his tin ear for Mac developer culture by mandating NeXT conventions over established Mac practices.
The choice of Mach kernel over Apple's NuKernel was made without apparent consideration of NuKernel's advantages, reflecting Tevanian's personal attachment to technology he co-created.
money_quotes: But where he's gone wrong, he's gone terribly wrong, flashing a spiteful streak that has been largely detrimental to Mac OS X's overall usability.
His technical acumen may be undisputed, but neither is his tin ear for usability.
The general perception is that Mac OS X isn't just good, but that it's good and getting better.
Even if Longhorn does ship in 2005, Apple will likely have shipped two major updates after Panther in that same time.
All too often, Tevanian's decisions and edicts have been dogmatic rather than pragmatic.
Mac developers tend to be independent thinkers, and flatly rejected the precepts of Tevanian's 'Guidelines'.
Many of Tevanian's design decisions have not been made with the best interests of Mac users in mind.
posts.key_insight: Tevanian's tenure at Apple exemplifies how brilliant technical management and reliable execution can be undermined by dogmatic loyalty to one's own technology at the expense of usability.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to reader criticism of his earlier mixed review of Avie Tevanian's tenure as Apple's VP of software engineering. He uses the Jaguar Finder's omission of HFS file type and creator code searching as a concrete example of how Tevanian's anti-legacy-Mac policies hurt real users. Despite type/creator searching being useful, fast, easy to implement, and present since System 7.5, Jaguar replaced it with redundant filename extension searching. Gruber argues this was driven by spite and technical politics rather than user benefit, emblematic of Tevanian's pattern of prioritizing NeXT's legacy over Mac usability.
key_points: The Jaguar Finder replaced useful type/creator code searching with redundant 'extension' searching that duplicates existing filename search functionality
File type and creator metadata is stored in HFS directory entries, not resource forks, making searches fast and implementation trivial via existing Carbon APIs
Deprecating a technology means moving away from it going forward, not pretending it never existed — hundreds of billions of files with type/creator codes exist on Macs running Jaguar
The omission exemplifies Tevanian putting technical politics (NeXT vs. classic Mac) ahead of practical usability for real users
Panther 10.3 restored type/creator searching, validating Gruber's criticism but not excusing the Jaguar-era omission
money_quotes: Something useful (type and creator searching) was replaced by something utterly and obviously redundant ("extension" searching).
So (1) type and creator searching is useful; (2) it's been around since System 7.5; (3) it's fast; and (4) it's easy to implement. So why doesn't the Jaguar Finder support it? Spite.
Deprecating only implies moving away from something as you go forward — not pretending that it never existed.
The filename-extension vs. type/creator argument is a political one, and all too often Tevanian has put technical politics ahead of usability.
It's as though it isn't enough that Mac OS X has been deemed a success — but that the old NeXT system must be proven to have been superior all along.
That amounts to hundreds of billions of files with type and creator codes that can't be searched for. How could this limitation possibly be in the interests of regular users?
posts.key_insight: Removing useful legacy features out of ideological hostility rather than genuine technical reasoning is a failure of engineering leadership that puts politics ahead of users.

posts.summary: Gruber shares an updated version of his popular 'Grab HTML' AppleScript for BBEdit, which fetches web page source code via the command-line curl utility. The script is a Mac OS X replacement for Dan Crevier's 1996 HTML Grabber plug-in that only worked in Mac OS 9. It demonstrates how AppleScript's do shell script command bridges the Mac GUI and Unix command line, offering a dialog box to enter a URL and optionally include HTTP headers. Gruber walks through the implementation details, explaining how BBEdit properties like auto-language detection and window naming enhance the workflow.
key_points: The script replaces a 1996 BBEdit plug-in by combining AppleScript's GUI capabilities with the Unix curl command
Mac OS X's do shell script command puts the entire Unix command-line toolset at the scripter's fingertips, making it an easy-to-use programming interface
Setting window contents as a property during creation triggers BBEdit's automatic language detection and syntax coloring
The script remembers the last URL entered between invocations by using an AppleScript property
money_quotes: This sort of AppleScript/shell hybrid shows Mac OS X at its best.
While a command line by itself does not make for a good user interface, it can be an amazingly easy-to-use programming interface.
The do shell script command puts the whole Unix command-line toolset at your fingertips.
Sort of like the View Source command in a web browser, except much more convenient if you're already working in BBEdit.
I'm aware that "With Headers" is an awkward name for a button because it isn't a verb; but the display dialog command insists on making all three buttons the same width.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's ability to bridge AppleScript's GUI dialog boxes with Unix command-line tools like curl exemplifies the platform's unique strength as both a user-friendly and programmer-friendly environment.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's declining overall PC market share is a misleading statistic because it includes vast market segments where Apple doesn't compete and doesn't want to compete. He cites an NPD analyst who compares measuring Apple against the entire PC market to comparing Acura sports cars against Taurus wagons. Gruber traces the history of anti-Mac punditry through the decades, noting how critics have constantly shifted their arguments while consistently predicting Apple's demise. He contends that in the market segments Apple actually targets, its share is meaningfully higher than the oft-cited 2 percent. While acknowledging Apple needs to grow, he rejects the narrative that the company is heading toward irrelevance.
key_points: Overall PC market share is a bogus metric for Apple because it includes segments like network terminals and low-end business machines where Apple doesn't compete
Anti-Mac critics have shifted their arguments decade by decade — from dismissing GUIs, to claiming Windows parity, to citing declining market share — while always predicting Apple's demise
The better analogy isn't car market share but motor vehicle market share — counting everything from golf carts to tractor trailers in one category
Apple's situation is OK, not dire — it needs growth but not hand-wringing
Industry analysts are finally starting to acknowledge that Apple cannot be measured like other PC makers
money_quotes: Apple is just not like other PC makers.
Nobody expects that you can sell Acura TSX sports cars as Taurus wagons.
A small but vocal class of very-small-weinered self-proclaimed PC experts has been attempting to delegitimize the Mac ever since it came out.

It's like counting everything from golf carts to tractor trailers as a single category, thus making the 'overall market share' look worse than it is for a company that only makes actual passenger cars.
Overall PC market share covers large market segments where Apple isn't competing — including markets where Apple doesn't want to compete.
In the markets where Apple actually wants to compete, the Mac's market share is certainly higher than 2 percent.
posts.key_insight: Apple's overall PC market share is a meaningless metric because it lumps together market segments Apple deliberately avoids with those it actively targets, making its position look far more dire than it actually is.

posts.summary: Gruber announces he's moved his Google ads from a vertical sidebar position to a horizontal placement below article content. He acknowledges this defies conventional advertising wisdom by placing ads 'below the fold' and showing fewer ads at a time. His reasoning is that the vertical format was too visually distracting, and readers who actually read articles will encounter the ads naturally at the end. He bets that engaged readers are more valuable than casual visitors who never scroll.
key_points: Gruber relocated ads from vertical sidebar to horizontal placement below articles, prioritizing reading experience over ad visibility
He acknowledges this violates conventional advertising wisdom: fewer ads shown, positioned below the fold
His core argument is that readers who actually read content are the only ones likely to click ads anyway
The design change reflects his belief that reader experience should take priority over maximizing ad impressions
money_quotes: The men in suits would surely deem this a foolish idea.
I think the vertical format was overly distracting, and I couldn't stand subjecting you to it any longer.
I suspect that most of you tend to actually *read* these articles.
If you do, when you get to the end of an article, the ads will be there waiting for you.
Anyone who comes to this site (or any site for that matter), and then leaves without scrolling is unlikely to be clicking any ads, anyway.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing for reader experience over conventional ad placement wisdom is the smarter long-term bet, because engaged readers who finish articles are the only audience worth monetizing.

posts.summary: Gruber compares the economics of Apple's product line, noting that buying both a 12" iBook and a 17" iMac costs less than a single 17" PowerBook while covering more use cases. He argues that for users who spend most of their time at a desk, the two-machine combo offers better value than one do-everything laptop. He highlights the iBook line as an exceptional bargain, noting that a capable portable for $1,100 was remarkable given that PowerBooks recently cost $3,000+. He extends the value-comparison theme to Apple's display line, citing Nathaniel Irons' observation that two smaller Cinema Displays offer more screen area than one 23" for the same price.
key_points: A 12" iBook plus a 17" iMac cost $100 less than a single 17" PowerBook while providing both a desktop and a portable
The 17" PowerBook and iMac were remarkably comparable in display resolution and processor performance, with only minor differences in bus speed and ports
For users at their desk 90%+ of the time, the two-machine approach eliminates paying a premium for portability they rarely use
The iBook line represented exceptional value at $1,100 compared to $3,000+ PowerBooks just a few years earlier
The same value-math applies to Apple's display line, where two smaller panels beat one large one in both area and cost
money_quotes: You can buy a low-end iBook and a 17" iMac — complete with extra RAM and an Airport card for the iBook — for less than the cost of a 17" PowerBook.
It certainly seems very tempting, to me at least, to walk out with two new computers rather than a lone use-it-everywhere PowerBook.
The iBook doesn't have a SuperDrive, but the iMac does — do you burn DVDs on the road that frequently?
Once you're off the desk, the lunchtray PowerBook is indisputably a more powerful machine with a much nicer display.
The iBook is two pounds lighter and runs cooler — making it a better machine for literal lap-top use.
I mention this comparison not to damn the 17" PowerBook for being overpriced, because it isn't (or at least not by much). What I think is noteworthy is the value in the iBook line.
Can you tell I just bought an iBook?
posts.key_insight: The best value in Apple's lineup often comes from combining two less expensive products rather than buying one premium device, because the price premium for top-tier specs rises faster than the actual utility gained.

posts.summary: This is a link-post roundup covering three unrelated Mac topics. Gruber highlights Matt Neuburg's positive review of Mailsmith 2.0, praising Bare Bones Software's thorough revision of the email client. He shares a clever hack from a German magazine that enables external display support on iBooks (normally limited to mirroring). Finally, he notes that John Calhoun has released Glider Pro as freeware after the rights reverted from bankrupt publisher Casady & Greene.
key_points: Mailsmith 2.0 represents a significant improvement over 1.5, with Bare Bones doing painstaking professional work on speed, reliability, and interface organization
Recent iBooks have video hardware capable of driving external displays but are artificially limited to mirroring — a simple AppleScript hack unlocks the full capability
Glider Pro became freeware after publisher Casady & Greene went bankrupt and rights reverted to creator John Calhoun
money_quotes: Where I suffered through Mailsmith 1.5's drawbacks to take advantage of its special strengths, 2.0 is in every respect a delight to use.
The result is that Mailsmith 2.0 feels peppy, clean, crisp, and totally reliable.
When connected to an external display, iBooks are limited to mirroring, whereas PowerBooks can drive an external display in addition to their built-in screens.
But recent iBooks are equipped with powerful video cards that are capable of handling multiple displays.
Since Casady & Greene Inc. went bankrupt, the rights to the game Glider have reverted to me, the author.
posts.key_insight: Apple's Mac software ecosystem in 2003 was rich with craftsmanship from independent developers, clever community hacks to unlock hidden hardware capabilities, and games finding new life as freeware after publisher bankruptcies.

posts.summary: Gruber revisits his earlier criticism of Andrew Stone, the NeXT veteran and Cocoa evangelist whose FontSight utility only works with Cocoa applications — a crippling limitation given that professional design software is Carbon-based. Gruber initially accused Stone of deliberate dishonesty but now concludes Stone is genuinely delusional, stuck in a 1997 mindset where NeXT's framework was supposed to replace the Mac OS entirely. Stone refuses to support Carbon apps despite customers explicitly telling him that's what they need, while competitor Unsanity is building FontCard to work with both APIs. Gruber argues that end users don't care about developer APIs — they care about features and compatibility with their existing tools. The result is that Stone Design loses a market opportunity and Mac users lose a potentially useful utility.
key_points: Stone's FontSight only works with Cocoa apps, excluding nearly all professional design software, which is its natural market
Stone's worldview is frozen circa 1997, when Apple's original plan was to replace the Mac OS with NeXT's framework and no Carbon layer existed
Competitor Unsanity's FontCard will support both Cocoa and Carbon apps, making FontSight's limitation a fatal competitive disadvantage
End users choose software based on features and compatibility, not which developer API was used to build it
Stone is actively refusing customer requests to support Carbon, which is irrational from both a business and technical perspective
money_quotes: Andrew Stone is out of his fucking mind.
Potential customers are telling him exactly what they want FontSight to do to make it useful for them; and Stone is responding by saying he absolutely will not do that. That simply isn't rational.
People aren't going to abandon their software from Adobe, Microsoft, and Macromedia just to use a Cocoa-only menu font utility.

Stone's argument that Carbon applications are irrelevant and/or deprecated hinges on the word of Gil Amelio? Gil Amelio? What Gil Amelio said in 1997 is about as relevant today as something uttered by Gerald Ford in 1975.
Carbon and Cocoa are developer APIs; they are of no interest whatsoever to normal Mac users. None. Really.

Normal Mac users choose their software based on tangible surface qualities — the features, the human interface, their existing toolset, etc. They don't care what programming tools were used to create their software any more than they care what CAD program was used to design their automobiles.
Stone's entire argument against supporting Carbon applications in FontSight is about developer APIs that users don't care about.
It's not something to get angry about. It's just sad.
posts.key_insight: Letting ideological attachment to a developer framework override what customers actually need is both bad business and a fundamental misunderstanding of what users care about.

posts.summary: Gruber recounts his experience with Microtech's Zio USB CompactFlash card reader, whose Mac OS X kernel extension driver causes reproducible kernel panics when waking from sleep. He explains how Mac OS X's memory protection prevents applications from crashing the system, but kernel extensions like device drivers operate outside that sandbox and can still cause system-wide crashes. The insidious nature of the bug is that the crash occurs during wake-from-sleep, making it difficult for average users to connect it to the Zio driver. Gruber then details the surprisingly difficult process of uninstalling the driver, which required using command-line tools to find the obscurely named kernel extension and root permissions to delete it.
key_points: Mac OS X's memory protection prevents apps from crashing the system, but kernel extensions (device drivers) bypass this protection and can cause kernel panics
The Zio driver bug is insidious because the kernel panic occurs on wake-from-sleep, not during direct use of the device, making the cause hard to identify
Mac OS X's Installer creates receipts with bills of materials (.bom files) that can be parsed with the lsbom command-line tool to find what files were installed
Uninstalling kernel extensions requires root access, putting it beyond the ability of most Mac users, meaning buggy drivers tend to persist indefinitely
The driver was installed as 'USBATCF.kext' — an opaque name that makes manual discovery nearly impossible
money_quotes: "Kernel panic" is a fancy term, but the end result is just as disastrous as an old-fashioned system-wide crash — your only option is to reboot, and hope that you didn't have anything important open with unsaved changes.
The problem is that while applications can't bring down the system on Mac OS X, other kinds of software can. Such as, say, device drivers implemented as kernel extensions.
What's insidious about it is that it's not necessarily obvious the Zio driver is causing the kernel panic.
I suspected the Zio immediately, because I know that only certain kinds of software can cause kernel panics, and the Zio driver was the only such software I'd installed recently.
I want Microtech's crummy software off my machine, on general principle.
"USBATCF.kext"? That's so obvious! How did I miss it?

For those who aren't sufficiently savvy regarding root permissions — which describes the vast majority of Mac users — something like Microtech's crummy Zio driver is likely to sit there stinking up the Extensions folder forever.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's robust memory protection for applications doesn't extend to kernel extensions, and the difficulty of identifying and removing buggy drivers exposes a significant usability gap for ordinary users.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier piece about Microtech's problematic Zio driver with a detailed exploration of Mac OS X's filesystem hierarchy, installer etiquette, and kernel panic logging. He explains the distinction between /System/Library/ and /Library/, noting that while third-party software installing in /System/ feels wrong, Apple's own documentation actually recommends it for kernel extensions. He advocates for better installer practices, including full disclosure of what gets installed where and built-in uninstall capabilities. The post also covers the evolution of kernel panic reporting from OS X 10.1's raw console dump to Jaguar's friendlier screen, and notes the ironic limitation that large panic logs fail to get written to disk.
key_points: The /System/Library/ folder is owned by the OS and shouldn't normally be modified by users, while /Library/ is configurable by admins — but Apple's own docs recommend kernel extensions go in /System/Library/Extensions/
Good installer etiquette requires listing everything that will be installed and where, with explanations for privileged locations, plus ideally an uninstall option
Drag-and-drop installation is preferable but not feasible for components requiring privileged filesystem locations
Mac OS X's kernel panic logging has a ~2KB size limit, meaning the most catastrophic panics with the most diagnostic information may fail to log anything at all
money_quotes: Once granted admin privileges, an installer can stick things pretty much anywhere it wants.
Good installer etiquette calls for a complete listing of what will be installed and where, displayed in the readme information before anything actually gets installed.
Sure, most users will ignore it — but higher-functioning users tend to care very much about such things.
And so while Microtech's Zio driver is damnable for numerous reasons, installing itself within /System/ isn't one of them — no matter how hard it is to get it out of there once installed.

Since you cannot take a screen shot in this situation, type the information on another computer or write it out by hand. If you have the ability to photograph the computer screen without a flash, this may be preferred.
The likely reason my Zio panics weren't logged is that the panic generated a large amount of info.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's filesystem hierarchy enforces a clear separation between system-owned and user-configurable components, but the practical reality of kernel extensions and installer behavior creates tensions that only good etiquette and transparency can resolve.

posts.summary: Gruber explains why third-party kernel extensions on Mac OS X must be installed in /System/Library/Extensions/, despite that folder belonging to the System domain which is normally reserved for Apple-only files. He walks through Mac OS X's four file-system domains (User, Local, Network, System) and their corresponding Library folders, clarifying that the System domain under /System/ is meant to be controlled exclusively by Apple. The exception exists because the boot process only checks one location for kernel extensions, a deliberate engineering decision to keep the system simple and reduce boot-time risk. He cites Apple engineer Dean Reece's mailing list post explaining the tradeoff. Non-boot kernel extensions can technically live anywhere, though /Library/Extensions/ has no special Apple-sanctioned status.
key_points: Mac OS X uses four file-system domains (User, Local, Network, System) to control access, with /System/ reserved exclusively for Apple's own components
/System/Library/Extensions/ is the sole exception to the rule that nothing third-party belongs in /System/, because the boot process only checks this one location for kernel extensions
Apple deliberately chose a single extensions location over supporting /Library/Extensions/ to minimize boot complexity and reduce risk of filesystem corruption making the system unbootable
Non-boot kernel extensions loaded at runtime by applications can reside anywhere, but /Library/Extensions/ has no special Apple-sanctioned properties
money_quotes: The rule of thumb: Everything in /System/ is from Apple, and is considered an essential component of Mac OS X.
This is the only folder that Mac OS X checks for kernel extensions that need to load during the boot process.

The reasons we don't support [/Library/Extensions/] now are not really technical showstoppers, but adding another place for both BootX and the kext tools to look, plus managing two separate caches, adds some complexity with little real (end-user) benefit.
It also adds some (small) risk to the bootability of the system, since the more paths the booter has to traverse, the more chances that a corrupt root filesystem will be unbootable.
We decided that both the risk and the benefit were small, so we decided on the simpler solution (one location).
posts.key_insight: The single kernel extensions folder in /System/ is a deliberate engineering tradeoff where Apple chose simplicity and boot reliability over architectural purity in domain separation.

posts.summary: Gruber surveys notable updates in the Mac text editor landscape. TextWrangler 1.5 adds full AppleScript support and expanded syntax coloring, significantly closing the gap with its pricier sibling BBEdit. He highlights Michael Tsai's BBAutoComplete, a free auto-completion tool that works across multiple scriptable editors via Apple events. He also notes the collaborative editor Hydra's update with WebKit-powered live HTML previews, while lamenting its forced name change due to a legal dispute.
key_points: TextWrangler 1.5's addition of AppleScript support is a major upgrade that moves it much closer to BBEdit's capabilities for just $49
AppleScript support matters even for non-scripters because it enables third-party tools like BBAutoComplete to communicate with editors via Apple events
BBAutoComplete's on-demand approach to completion is superior to editors that automatically suggest completions as you type
Hydra's Rendezvous-based collaborative editing and WebKit live previews represent cutting-edge features, though the editor faces a forced name change
money_quotes: Using a scriptable text editor can be incredibly useful — even if you never write a single script yourself.
The addition of AppleScript support is huge.

Many people mistakenly felt that TextWrangler 1.0 was little more than a renamed commercial version of the free BBEdit Lite; the addition of full scriptability clearly moves TextWrangler's capabilities much closer to BBEdit's.
I despise auto-completing editors which automatically pop-up or flash suggested completions on-the-fly as you type. It's like trying to write while someone else is typing in the same window.
Regardless if you like BBAutoComplete's style of auto-completion, you can't argue with the fact that it wouldn't be possible if the aforementioned editors didn't offer terrific AppleScript support.
And of course if you really do want multiple authors typing in the same window simultaneously, Hydra has been updated to version 1.1.2.
posts.key_insight: Scriptability in text editors isn't just a power-user feature — it's the foundation that enables an entire ecosystem of third-party tools and workflows, making AppleScript support a transformative addition to any editor.

posts.summary: Gruber announces his article in the September 2003 issue of Macworld magazine, covering BBEdit 7.0's built-in CVS integration on Mac OS X. The article focuses on the one thing BBEdit doesn't handle — creating and setting up initial source code repositories. He notes that while CVS is notoriously cryptic, BBEdit makes it simple to use. He encourages readers to buy the physical magazine and write to the editors requesting more of his work.
key_points: Gruber published a print article in Macworld covering BBEdit 7.0's CVS integration
BBEdit simplifies CVS, which is notoriously cryptic even by Unix standards, but doesn't handle initial repository setup
The article exists only in print, not online — a notable detail for a web-native writer
Gruber self-promotes with characteristic dry humor, asking readers to request 'more articles from the same clever author'
money_quotes: While CVS is notoriously cryptic even by Unix standards, BBEdit's integration makes it really simple to use.
If you enjoy it, it probably wouldn't hurt if you wrote an email to their editors to let them know you'd like to see more articles from the same clever author.
The screenshot is intended as a Kubrick reference, not a Stephen King one, but you're free to interpret it either way.
posts.key_insight: BBEdit 7.0's CVS integration tamed one of Unix's most cryptic tools for Mac users, and Gruber used his Macworld debut to establish himself as a bridge between Unix power and Mac sensibility.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes the fourth annual MDJ Power 25 list of influential figures in the Mac industry, arguing that Steve Jobs is so dominant that no one else is within an order of magnitude of his influence. He critiques the list's simple ordinal format for obscuring the vast gap between Jobs and everyone else, notes that 16 of 25 spots are held by Apple employees, and observes the near-total absence of women. Gruber shares his own ballot, which included Jobs, Jon Rubinstein, Avie Tevanian, Dave Hyatt, and Brent Simmons, defending each choice with characteristic specificity about their contributions to the Mac platform.
key_points: Jobs's influence on Apple is not merely a function of his CEO title — his micro-management and taste define every product, making him incomparably more powerful than anyone else on the list
Jobs's greatest weakness is his inability to recognize when he's wrong, exemplified by his insistence on the metallic UI theme in violation of Apple's own Human Interface Guidelines
Apple's growing dominance as both hardware maker and application developer has concentrated power within the company, with 16 of 25 slots occupied by Apple employees
The absence of women from the list reflects a real industry problem, not voter bias, and the situation has actually worsened since the 1990s when women held prominent Apple leadership positions
Gruber's ballot reveals his priorities: hardware execution (Rubinstein), OS architecture (Tevanian), web rendering technology (Hyatt), and killer Mac-only software (Simmons)
money_quotes: Anyone who doesn't rank Jobs first doesn't have enough of a clue to deserve a vote in the poll.
Jobs does not run Apple via hand-waving and delegation — he is a micro-manager with an unrivaled attention to detail.
He has impeccable taste, and an amazing gift for defining new products that people will crave.
His biggest fault is equally obvious: he is oblivious to any of his weaknesses. He may be right 95 percent of the time, but when he's wrong, he stays wrong.
In terms of power and influence, no one else is even within an order of magnitude of Jobs.
Comparing them to Jobs is like comparing Roger Ebert to Steven Spielberg.
Jobs's attention seems to be binary — it's either completely on or off.
The lack of a good, system-wide HTML renderer was one of the biggest holes in the Mac OS.
posts.key_insight: Steve Jobs's power over the Mac industry is so absolute that the MDJ Power 25 is really a Power 1 list plus 24 others whose order barely matters.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Microsoft's dominance in corporate computing is sustained not by product quality but by the self-interest of IT departments, who choose complex, high-maintenance systems to justify their own existence. He begins by explaining how most people buy computers based on recommendations from tech-savvy friends, then extends this dynamic to corporate IT, where the people choosing technology benefit from its unreliability. Using a vivid plumbing analogy, he argues that computing should be held to the same reliability standards as other infrastructure. He concludes that if companies demanded high reliability and low maintenance, alternatives to Microsoft — particularly Macs — would become far more attractive.
key_points: Microsoft achieved monopoly not by winning over the general population directly, but by winning over the nerds who make purchase recommendations for everyone else
Outlook/Exchange is the worst email system ever made by security metrics, yet the most popular — because it serves IT departments' interests, not users'
Corporate IT staffs have a perverse incentive to deploy high-maintenance technology, since their budgets and influence grow with headcount
Computing infrastructure should be held to the same reliability standards as plumbing, but society has been convinced to accept lower expectations for computers
Macs are more common in publishing precisely because those industries cannot tolerate downtime — reliability is a feature that undermines IT empire-building
money_quotes: Microsoft has always understood this. They've achieved an overwhelming monopoly not by targeting an overwhelming majority of the general population, but rather by targeting an overwhelming majority of people who are 'into computers'.
Fear of blowing $1000 or $2000 on a 'bad' computer motivates them to buy whatever is recommended by their closest nerd.
It is thus in the interests of corporate IT staff to deploy technology that requires a large IT staff for maintenance.

The corporate IT field is in large parts comprised of men who are not smart enough to program, but yet wanted a career in computers. They are the fifth wheel of corporate America — serving no practical purpose but their own employment.
The only difference between 'most people' and 'most corporate IT professionals' is that the IT guys aren't intimidated.
So am I claiming that Apple's market share suffers because its products are too reliable and require too little maintenance? Well, yes.
If you insist upon high reliability and low maintenance, solutions other than 'all-Microsoft, all-the-time' start to look very good indeed.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's dominance is sustained by a perverse incentive structure where the people who choose corporate technology benefit professionally from its complexity and unreliability.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier 'Good Times' post about the sorry state of computing reliability, addressing counter-arguments about PC complexity, software monocultures, and Exchange/Outlook's role in virus epidemics. He rejects the notion that PCs are inherently too complex to work reliably, arguing that broken into component tasks they're no more complicated than other solved problems. He dismantles the claim that any dominant email client would face the same security problems, arguing that Microsoft's monoculture is an anomaly and that platform diversity is the natural state. He also challenges Cringely's claim that Linux adoption is driven by IT self-interest, arguing instead that Linux advocates are the 'Good Guys' pushing for genuinely better solutions from outside the entrenched IT culture.
key_points: PCs broken into component tasks (like email) are no more inherently complex than other single-purpose systems we expect to work reliably
Microsoft's software monoculture is an anomaly, not the natural state — platform diversity with open standards is the norm and provides natural resistance to widespread attacks
Exchange/Outlook's proprietary bundling of calendaring with insecure email makes it a menace not just to its users but to the entire internet
Linux's growing adoption is driven by competent people pushing for better solutions from outside the existing IT culture, not by IT departments protecting their headcount
Incompetent IT professionals genuinely believe unreliable computing is inevitable because they only know Microsoft's ecosystem
money_quotes: Someone who only knows Microsoft software can't conceive of a platform where computers just work.
Nature tends to favor heterogeneity, not homogeneity.
The Exchange/Outlook platform is quite simply a menace — not only to the organizations that use it, but to the world at large.
To respond to this by sticking with Exchange/Outlook is outrageous.

The people I see pushing for Linux aren't saying 'Let's do the same thing we're doing now, only without paying license fees to Microsoft.' They're instead saying, 'This is madness, we can and should do better.'
Complexity is not an excuse for low expectations.
We've strapped men into giant rockets loaded with jet fuel, propelled them into space, and landed them on the moon. That was complicated. And our expectation was that we'd get them back.
Why we don't expect our email to work is beyond me.
posts.key_insight: The widespread acceptance of unreliable, insecure computing is not an inevitable consequence of complexity but a failure of standards enabled by Microsoft's monoculture and the incompetent IT culture that sustains it.

posts.summary: Gruber revisits his earlier characterization of the native Mac OS X port of OpenOffice as vaporware, defending the label after the project announced a 2006 target date. He critiques both the open source community's outrage at the term and Andrew Orlowski's Register column wondering why more developers don't volunteer for the project. Gruber argues the answer is obvious: creating great Mac software is hard work that deserves compensation. He further argues that Apple has no incentive to staff an open source cross-platform office suite, preferring to build its own Mac-native software like Keynote and Safari.
key_points: The native Mac OS X port of OpenOffice fits the dictionary definition of vaporware, regardless of community objections to the label
The lack of volunteer developers is not a mystery — creating excellent user experiences is hard work that people reasonably expect to be paid for
Apple's strategic interest lies in building Mac-native software it owns, not contributing to cross-platform open source projects
Apple's pattern with Safari and Keynote shows it will leverage open source foundations but build proprietary products on top
Volunteer labor is unlikely to produce a top-notch office suite to rival Microsoft Office on Mac
money_quotes: Open source vaporware is no better than commercial vaporware.
Creating a top-notch user experience is extremely hard work, and it takes a lot of time.

Wondering why your friendly neighborhood Mac developer doesn't devote his nights and weekends to volunteering for OpenOffice is like wondering why your friendly neighborhood barber doesn't spend his spare time giving free haircuts.
How about the upstream benefit of getting paid for their time?
Apple's business is making Macintosh computers, and writing Macintosh software.
If they respond by assigning Apple developers to write office software, it's going to be Mac software, designed and owned by Apple.
I wouldn't hold my breath waiting for it to be produced by volunteer labor.
posts.key_insight: Great software requires real investment, and neither volunteer labor nor cross-platform open source projects are likely to produce the kind of polished Mac-native experience that Apple or its users actually want.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his VaporOffice piece about the stalled Mac port of OpenOffice.org by exploring why some open source projects attract developers while others don't. He cites Chris Nandor's observation that open source volunteers typically work on projects that directly benefit them — meaning developer tools, not end-user software. He also links to Steve Crandall's biological analogy about monocultures being vulnerable to attack, implicitly connecting software monocultures (Windows/Office) to the same fragility seen in agriculture.
key_points: Open source volunteers gravitate toward developer tools because they directly benefit from them, leaving end-user software like office suites underserved
The Mac port of OpenOffice.org lacks developers precisely because the people who could build it aren't the people who need it
Software monocultures share the same vulnerability as agricultural monocultures — uniform defenses make them susceptible to widespread attack
money_quotes: People do NOT normally put a lot of free time into anything that does not give them direct benefit.
Developers tend to volunteer to write developer software, which is a very different genus than software for normal people.
The problem with monocultures is that they are extremely sensitive to attack.

In a heteroculture, diseases and infestations can be stopped when they don't have a nearby host to attack — in a monoculture, every adjacent plant is an inviting host, waiting for an attacker to get lucky.
posts.key_insight: Open source thrives at building tools for developers but struggles with end-user software because volunteers naturally work on what benefits themselves.

posts.summary: Gruber describes a rendering discrepancy in Safari that causes a two-pixel white stripe at the bottom of his Google AdSense ads. The issue stems from Google specifying a table height of 250 pixels using a non-standard HTML attribute, which Safari renders two pixels short. He notes that Google's markup is embarrassingly non-compliant with web standards, using a junk attribute first supported by ancient Netscape versions. While he filed a bug report with Apple, he acknowledges Safari shouldn't be blamed for imperfect rendering of invalid HTML. He also suggests Google could fix the issue on their end by setting the background color on the body tag rather than just the table.
key_points: Safari renders a Google AdSense table two pixels shorter than specified, creating a visible white stripe that other browsers don't show
The root cause is Google using the non-standard 'height' attribute on a table tag — markup that has no correct rendering behavior
Google could easily fix the issue by applying the background color to the page body instead of just the table element
Safari's attempt to render non-standard HTML like legacy browsers is heroic but can never achieve 100% compatibility with invalid markup
money_quotes: There is no "correct" rendering for non-standard markup.
It's rather incongruous to see a company as clever as Google writing such embarrassingly bad markup.
Safari makes a heroic and largely successful attempt to render non-standard HTML markup just like crummy old versions of Netscape and IE do, but it's impossible to achieve 100 percent compatibility.
It's a junk attribute first supported by some ancient version of Netscape.
Their markup for Google.com is similarly utterly non-standards-compliant.
posts.key_insight: When browsers try to faithfully render non-standard HTML, there is no objectively correct behavior to aim for — the real fix is writing valid markup in the first place.

posts.summary: Gruber traces the evolution of Apple's relationship with IBM, from the iconic 1984 Super Bowl ad that cast IBM as Big Brother to the 2003 PowerMac G5 partnership where IBM supplies Apple's processors. He argues that Apple's polarizing brand identity — the 'us vs. them' marketing — invited strong opinions from non-users and created lasting perceptions of Apple as a corporate loner. The piece examines how the real enemy was always Microsoft, which undermined both Apple and IBM, while Motorola's decline as a semiconductor company left Apple needing a stronger hardware partner. Gruber concludes with the ironic observation that the new G5 Macs are literally more 'IBM-compatible' than any Dell or HP.
key_points: Apple uniquely attracts strong opinions from people who have never used its products, partly due to its culturally polarizing brand identity
The 1984 ad's Big Brother was IBM, not Microsoft — Microsoft was just one of several software companies at the time
Apple and IBM were never true enemies but opposites; their real common enemy was Microsoft, which deceived both companies
Motorola's decline from semiconductor leader to second-rate phone maker left Apple needing a credible hardware partner
The IBM-Apple G5 partnership gives Apple both a superior processor and the industry credibility that comes with IBM's name
money_quotes: One of the biggest differences between Apple and the vast majority of tech companies is that everyone tends to have an opinion about them.
Part of this is just foolishness, a by-product of the ignorant notion that everyone is entitled to an opinion on anything.
If there's an 'us', there must be a 'them'.
The ad said nothing about what the Macintosh was. It didn't even show a Macintosh. It served only to establish sides.
IBM and Apple circa 1984 weren't so much enemies as they were opposites.
Microsoft, which bamboozled the Sculley regime at Apple into inadvertently granting them carte blanche to blatantly steal elements of the Macintosh look and feel.
