With the G5, Apple is getting much more than a terrific new processor. They've also gained a powerful and respected ally.
The new PowerMac G5 is, in a literal sense, more 'IBM-compatible' than a Wintel PC from Dell or HP.
posts.key_insight: Apple's journey from casting IBM as Big Brother in 1984 to partnering with them on the G5 in 2003 illustrates how the tech industry's alliances are shaped not by ideology but by the pragmatic need for credible, powerful partners.

posts.summary: Gruber defends AppleScript against its reputation for being hard to write, arguing the difficulty stems from inconsistent application scripting dictionaries rather than the language itself. He highlights the 'whose' clause as an elegant feature that allows filtered references without explicit loops. Through practical examples using BBEdit and the Finder, he demonstrates how 'whose' clauses can filter collections of objects declaratively. The post serves as a brief tutorial teasing a follow-up where these clauses will be put to practical use.
key_points: AppleScript's difficulty reputation comes from inconsistent app scripting dictionaries, not the core language
The 'whose' clause enables declarative filtered references, eliminating the need for manual loops
Filtered references work across any well-scripted application, from BBEdit to the Finder
AppleScript's core language is actually small and offers elegant features when understood properly
money_quotes: AppleScript gets a rap for being easy-to-read, but hard-to-write. And it's a somewhat deserving reputation.
But the problem isn't with the language itself, per se, but rather with the syntactical vagaries of disparate application scripting dictionaries.
The AppleScript language itself is rather small, and in fact offers some very neat features.
posts.key_insight: AppleScript's real elegance lies in features like 'whose' clauses that allow declarative filtering, and its notorious difficulty is better blamed on inconsistent application dictionaries than on the language itself.

posts.summary: Gruber walks through building a 'Select Word' AppleScript command for BBEdit, starting from a simple two-line script and iteratively refining it for performance. The key optimization involves narrowing the search scope from the entire document to the current display line, preventing AppleScript from building enormous temporary word lists. He uses a robot-and-restaurant analogy to explain why unscoped queries don't scale. The final script includes error handling for edge cases like empty lines. The post doubles as a tutorial on AppleScript whose clauses and BBEdit's scripting model, while also critiquing AppleScript's inability to optimize obviously bounded queries.
key_points: A naive AppleScript whose clause that searches every word in a document becomes painfully slow in large files, even though it works fine in small ones
The fix is to narrow the scope from the entire document to the current display_line, which bounds the search to what's visible on screen
BBEdit's distinction between hard lines (separated by returns) and display_lines (soft-wrapped) matters for scripting performance
AppleScript lacks the optimization intelligence to short-circuit a 'last word whose...' query, forcing it to evaluate every element before returning the last match
The iterative refinement process — working script, then performant script, then robust script — is a model for practical scripting
money_quotes: I use both these commands frequently; editing in an application that doesn't support them drives me nuts.
You don't want to see that script; it's junk.
The command, as given, doesn't scale.
Find the closest restaurant that serves fresh-cut fries, within five miles of home.

Through the power of the whose clause, we, as scripters, do not need to allocate a temporary list variable to hold every word of the window, nor do we need to test each word to see if its offset meets our criterion. But this work still must be done — it's performed behind the scenes, by AppleScript.

Even without any sort of 'common sense' AI, given that we asked for the closest restaurant that serves fresh-cut fries, once the robot finds a single such restaurant, it could in theory be smart enough not to venture any further from home.
That's not how AppleScript works, and so neither does our hypothetical robot.
posts.key_insight: Elegant scripting requires not just correct logic but deliberate scope constraints, because abstractions that hide complexity don't eliminate it — they just move it out of sight where it can silently kill performance.

posts.summary: Gruber introduces Michael Tsai, a Mac software developer behind utilities like DropDMG and BBAutoComplete, and editor of the About This Particular Macintosh e-zine. The post serves as a preface to an email interview with Tsai, primarily focused on promoting his latest release, SpamSieve 2, a Bayesian spam filtering utility. Gruber shares his personal experience using SpamSieve 2 for over a month, reporting it caught all but 10 of 1,981 spams. He gives a strong endorsement of the software's ease of use, speed, and broad email client compatibility.
key_points: Michael Tsai is a prolific Mac developer and publisher of the ATPM e-zine
SpamSieve 2 uses Bayesian statistical filtering among other strategies to combat spam
Gruber's personal testing showed SpamSieve catching 99.5% of spam over a month
SpamSieve works with every major Mac OS X email client except Apple Mail
money_quotes: Its efficacy has bordered on the incredible: flagging all but 10 of 1981 spams.
SpamSieve is easy to set up, runs quickly, and works with every major Mac OS X email client except Apple Mail.
Needless to say, I highly recommend SpamSieve.
posts.key_insight: SpamSieve 2 represents the kind of thoughtful, effective Mac utility software that solves a real problem with near-perfect accuracy.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his BBEdit 'Select Word' script with responses to reader feedback. He addresses the objection that Option-Shift-Arrow already selects words by noting the script requires fewer keystrokes when the cursor is mid-word. He shares his keyboard shortcut choice (Control-L) and its rationale. He also highlights Sven-S. Porst's tip for enabling custom text-editing keybindings across all Cocoa apps.
key_points: Option-Shift-Arrow selection requires two keystrokes when the cursor is mid-word, while the script needs only one
Small conveniences like Select Word aren't essential but are worth having, just like BBEdit's built-in Select Line command
Control-L was chosen as the shortcut for its analogy to existing BBEdit conventions and ease of typing
Cocoa has a hidden built-in feature for custom text-editing keybindings across all apps
money_quotes: The point is not that the Select Line command and Select Word script are essential or necessary, but simply that they're very small conveniences.
So, yes, I completely agree that my Select Word script is not a big deal. It's just a nice little deal.
And, also, a nice example for teaching how to script BBEdit.
posts.key_insight: Small workflow conveniences that save even a single keystroke are worth building, both for their practical value and as teaching examples.

posts.summary: This is a link-roundup post covering several tech topics: Dan Benjamin's new web agency Automatic Labs, a Safari rendering bug with PRE elements, criticism of Aladdin's StuffIt Deluxe 8 for using a kernel extension to patch the Finder (which caused kernel panics), and a CNet report about a security researcher being fired after criticizing Microsoft. Gruber adds his own commentary to each link, particularly skewering Aladdin's heavy-handed engineering approach and Microsoft's response to security criticism.
key_points: Aladdin's StuffIt Deluxe 8 used a kernel extension to enable archive creation via Finder renaming — an approach criticized as massively disproportionate when simpler alternatives existed
The kernel extension approach proved dangerous in practice, causing kernel panics on multiple OS X versions
A security researcher was fired from a Microsoft consulting partner after co-authoring a paper critical of Microsoft's security
Safari had a rendering bug with PRE element width calculation when nested containers followed uncontained text
money_quotes: This feature — which was available in older versions of StuffIt for the original Mac OS, but which until now was not available for Mac OS X — has always struck me as weird and unintuitive, regardless of how it's implemented.
the approach can very well be compared to killing a mosquito with a nuke blast. Sure it works, but the impact is just *slightly* bigger than *needed*.
Ah, yes — security through shooting the messenger.
posts.key_insight: Technical overkill in engineering and corporate intimidation of security researchers are both symptoms of organizations prioritizing control over doing the right thing.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Dell's entry into the MP3 player market with its Digital Jukebox poses no real threat to Apple's iPod, despite PC-industry conventional wisdom suggesting cheaper knock-offs always win. He dismantles the narrative that Apple invariably loses to low-cost imitators, pointing out it only truly applied to the GUI PC market. The iPod succeeds not just as engineering but as a brand — a low-cost luxury item with massive mind share, akin to Coca-Cola. Dell's brand strength lies with corporate bean counters, which is worthless in consumer electronics. Gruber surveys potential real competitors (Sony, Microsoft, Nokia) and finds each hobbled by their own strategic limitations. The piece concludes that without an iPod-caliber brand, cheaper knock-offs aren't even competing.
key_points: The conventional wisdom that Apple always loses to cheaper knock-offs is based on a single case (the GUI PC market) and doesn't generalize
Dell's corporate-focused brand is worthless in the consumer electronics market where individual taste and coolness matter
The iPod is a low-cost luxury item with unmatched mind share — it's the Coca-Cola of music players
Potential real competitors like Sony and Microsoft are each hamstrung by conflicting corporate interests (record labels, rights management, Xbox losses)
Market share follows mind share: a competing player without iPod-caliber branding isn't competing at all
money_quotes: Guess what: Dell is still fucked.
The Dell brand stands for computers that are just like the other guys', except a little cheaper. This brand message was and is hugely popular with corporate bean counters.
Corporations buy computers for their employees, but they don't buy MP3 players.
It's not an expensive computer peripheral — it's a low-cost luxury item.
If the iPod is Coke, its current competition consists of nothing but store brands, those cans that just read 'Cola'. Dell, to be blunt, is no Pepsi.
Any competing player that doesn't establish an iPod-caliber brand isn't even competing at all.

PC industry pundits continue to assert that Apple can't possibly succeed by selling excellent products at premium prices — that they'll inevitably succumb to mediocre products at discount prices. Tell that to Nike.
Apple's Newton epitomized the difference between superior technology and a superior product.
posts.key_insight: Brand dominance in consumer electronics is won through mind share and product excellence, not corporate purchasing leverage — making Dell's discount-commodity playbook useless against the iPod.

posts.summary: Gruber presents three loosely connected vignettes organized around words ending in 'ck'. He highlights Giles Turnball's MacDevCenter article surveying what apps people keep in their Mac OS X Dock, noting it became a useful discovery tool for third-party software. He then calls out a Windows developer for shamelessly copying LaunchBar, his preferred app launcher. Finally, he shares Mark Morford's SF Gate column celebrating the unboxing experience of a new Mac, praising it as the best description of that experience he's ever read.
key_points: The 'What's on Your Dock?' article became an organic way for Mac users to discover obscure but useful third-party utilities
LaunchBar by Objective Development is Gruber's preferred app launcher, popular enough to have been copied by a Windows developer
Mark Morford's column captures the distinctive Apple unboxing experience — attention to packaging details that makes the product feel like art rather than commodity electronics
The post's playful structure (Dock/Dick/Lick) is classic early Gruber: irreverent framing around substantive Apple enthusiasm
money_quotes: Someone new to Mac OS X, with little idea of where to start looking for useful third-party software, would do well to check out the apps appearing in these Dock screenshots.
LaunchBar is both clever and original, and I highly recommend it.
Popular enough, in fact, to have been shamelessly ripped-off by a Windows developer.

No screaming colors and no garish cartoon graphics and no massive corporate logo and no bullet-point exclamation points listing the outrageous features you'll never use and you're like, wait a minute, what they hell does Apple think they're hawking here, art?
posts.key_insight: Apple's obsessive attention to design details — even in packaging — sets it apart from the commodity PC world, inspiring both devoted users and shameless imitators.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier piece about Dell and Apple's market positioning, addressing reader pushback on his claim that Apple fumbled the 1980s corporate market. He cites Guy Kawasaki's 'The Macintosh Way' to show that while Apple did target business users, they marketed to end users rather than IT decision-makers who controlled purchasing. He argues Apple could have pursued both audiences simultaneously, as they eventually did with Mac OS X. A secondary section highlights Sony's internal conflict between its electronics and music divisions, which prevented it from competing with the iPod.
key_points: Apple marketed the original Mac to 'knowledge workers' who would use the machines, but neglected the IT/MIS directors who authorized corporate purchases
Anyone who claims Apple could have been Microsoft with a few simple changes is naive — the corporate market failure involved many complex factors
Kawasaki's 'pick one' framing of Apple's target market was a false choice; Apple could have pursued both consumers and corporate IT simultaneously
Sony's music division actively prevented its electronics division from competing with the iPod due to copy-protection concerns
Apple with Mac OS X eventually figured out how to target both end users and corporate buyers at the same time
money_quotes: Anyone who glibly claims that Apple could have been Microsoft if they had simply done this and this instead of that and that is naive.
Apple targeted the Macintosh to the people who would use them, but not to the people who would decide whether to buy them.
In every other potential market, the Macintosh was a stellar success.
When the world leader in consumer electronics takes a pass on the hottest portable music player out there, you have to wonder what gives.
Now Apple is delivering the innovation while Sony studies the matter.
Sony became a global giant on the basis of innovative devices manufactured by the millions on nothing more than a hunch that people would buy them.
posts.key_insight: Apple's historic corporate market failure wasn't about ignoring business users entirely, but about marketing to the people who would use Macs rather than the IT gatekeepers who decided whether to buy them.

posts.summary: Gruber notes the irony in Apple's partnership with Pepsi for an iTunes music giveaway, given that Steve Jobs famously recruited John Sculley away from Pepsi by dismissing it as 'sugar water.' He finds it amusing that Apple is now partnering with the very company Jobs once disparaged. He ties this back to his earlier argument that the iPod transcends geek culture, positioning it firmly in the mainstream pop culture space.
key_points: Apple's Pepsi partnership for iTunes giveaway is ironic given Jobs's famous 'sugar water' recruitment line to Sculley
The deal reinforces Gruber's earlier argument that the iPod is a pop culture phenomenon, not just a tech product
The Pepsi tie-in adds further irony to his previous Coke-vs-iPod brand comparison in 'Dell's Dud'
money_quotes: Do you want to sell sugar water for the rest of your life?
Who's selling sugar water now, eh?
The iPod is not about geek culture, it's about pop culture.
posts.key_insight: Apple partnering with Pepsi to promote iTunes is a delicious irony that also proves the iPod has become a mainstream pop culture brand, not just a tech gadget.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects a Microsoft PR interview with Dave Fester about digital music services, translating each talking point into what Microsoft actually means. Fester frames the Windows Media ecosystem as offering consumer 'choice' and 'openness' while calling Apple's iTunes/iPod a 'closed system.' Gruber methodically exposes the Orwellian inversion: Microsoft's 'open' means locked into WMA format and Microsoft licensing, while Apple's supposedly 'closed' system actually supports the truly open MP3 format. The piece highlights Microsoft's strategy of redefining language to cast its proprietary format monopoly as consumer freedom.
key_points: Microsoft frames WMA lock-in as 'choice' because consumers can pick among multiple stores and devices — all using Microsoft's proprietary format
The word 'open' is inverted: Apple supports MP3 (an open format) but is called 'closed' because it doesn't use WMA; Microsoft's proprietary ecosystem is called 'open'
Microsoft's real goal is to replicate the Windows PC model in digital music — commodity hardware makers competing while all paying Microsoft licensing fees
Fester's entire Q&A never once mentions MP3, revealing the deliberate framing that excludes the one truly open format
Gruber draws a direct line between Microsoft's digital music strategy and its broader monopolistic business practices
money_quotes: Where by "legitimate", we mean "under Microsoft's control", which is also why we think it's so "exciting".
Where by "products and services", we of course mean "Microsoft products and services".
And where by "love", we mean "have grown to accept".
We're hoping that WMA is to portable music players as Windows is to PCs — dozens of manufacturers fighting each other for commodity hardware sales, all of them paying licensing fees to Microsoft.
What we mean by "open" is "based on Microsoft's proprietary formats".
In fact, we don't even mention "MP3" in this entire "Q&A".
Antitrust, schmantitrust — this discussion wouldn't be happening if we had put Apple out of business when we had the chance.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's framing of its proprietary WMA ecosystem as 'open' and Apple's MP3-compatible iTunes as 'closed' is a textbook example of Orwellian corporate language designed to disguise monopolistic ambition as consumer choice.

posts.summary: Gruber shares his third installment of Daring Fireball browser usage statistics, breaking down unique IPs by browser for October 2003. Safari leads among web browsers with 9,883 unique IPs, nearly matching IE Windows at 9,353, while NetNewsWire RSS readers dominate overall traffic. He notes that 13 percent of Safari users were already running Panther before its official release day, which he finds surprisingly high. He also explains his methodology for counting unique IPs and its inherent limitations.
key_points: Safari has overtaken IE Windows among Daring Fireball readers, reflecting the site's Mac-heavy audience
NetNewsWire (Lite and Full combined) accounts for more traffic than any individual web browser, showing early RSS dominance among tech-savvy readers
13 percent of Safari users were already on Panther before launch day, suggesting significant early adoption among the DF audience
IP-based counting is acknowledged as imperfect — DHCP inflates counts while NAT/proxies deflate them — but serves as a reasonable popularity gauge
money_quotes: Something I don't get: out of the 9,883 unique IPs for Safari, 1,282 are already using Panther's Safari update.
In other words, 13 percent of Daring Fireball readers who use Safari were already using Panther before today. That seems awfully high.
The point is not to count readers, but to gauge the popularity of various browsers.
posts.key_insight: In late 2003, Daring Fireball's audience was already heavily Mac and Safari-oriented, with RSS readers like NetNewsWire generating more traffic than any single web browser.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Proteron developer Samuel Caughron's claim that Apple ripped off his LiteSwitch X utility for Panther's improved Command-Tab application switcher. He points out that Command-Tab switching originated in Windows 3, was brought to Mac by third parties as early as 1994, and was even implemented by Apple itself in Mac OS 8.5. Gruber argues that Caughron's real grievance is simply that Apple filled a gap in OS X that his utility had exploited. He criticizes the tech press for amplifying the complaint without scrutiny and notes that other independent Mac developers have called out Caughron's hypocrisy rather than rallying to his defense.
key_points: Command-Tab switching originated in Windows 3 and was brought to Mac by third parties nearly a decade before LiteSwitch X existed
Caughron falsely attributes the phrase 'Apple innovation' to Apple when no one at the company actually made that claim
The real issue is that Apple's previous Command-Tab implementation was poor, creating a gap that third-party developers filled temporarily
Other independent Mac developers like Michael Tsai and Erik Barzeski sided against Caughron, undermining his narrative of Apple victimizing small developers
Tech press outlets amplified Caughron's claims uncritically, feeding an 'Apple's done it again' narrative that Gruber finds unwarranted
money_quotes: For hundreds of millions of users, the feature has existed for over 10 years, after it debuted in version 3 of Microsoft Windows.
The idea that Proteron deserves credit as the 'original authors' of this feature is patently absurd. It's like claiming to have recently invented drag-and-drop.
Mac OS X's Command-Tab switching used to stink, thus leaving an opening for third-party developers to fill. Starting with Panther, that opening is gone, and Caughron is pissed.
Proteron did a fine job designing how LiteSwitch X looks, but it is not a novel design — it looks good because it looks like something designed by Apple.
When a utility is designed to compensate for a hole in Mac OS X, the developer should not expect the hole to remain unfilled by Apple forever.
There's a lot to argue about regarding Apple's relationship with third-party Mac developers, but not this.
posts.key_insight: When a third-party utility exists only to fill a gap in the OS, the developer has no legitimate grievance when the platform vendor eventually fills that gap itself.

posts.summary: Gruber uses Safari version strings in his web server logs to track how quickly Daring Fireball readers upgraded from Mac OS X Jaguar to Panther. Since Safari 1.1 shipped exclusively with Panther, the user-agent string serves as a reliable proxy for OS adoption. Within nine days of Panther's release, over half of his Safari-using visitors had already upgraded. He also notes the open question of whether Apple will back-port Safari updates to older OS versions.
key_points: Safari 1.1 is exclusive to Panther, making the browser version a reliable proxy for OS X upgrade tracking in server logs
Within 24 hours of Panther's release, 23% of Daring Fireball's Safari users had already upgraded
By nine days post-release, Panther users surpassed Jaguar users at 59% of Safari traffic
The Panther-only status of Safari 1.1 raises concerns about whether Apple will tie browser updates to OS releases going forward
money_quotes: If you're using Safari 1.1, you're running Panther. If you're running Safari 1.0, you're using Jaguar.
Will Safari 1.1 be released for Jaguar users? Answer unclear, ask again later.
It's pretty impressive how many of you installed Panther within 24 hours of its release.
These figures are only Safari users. The Percent column indicates the percentage of Safari users who are running Panther.
Readers are either seeded developers or bootleggers.
posts.key_insight: Safari's version-to-OS coupling provides a clever natural experiment for measuring how aggressively Apple's core audience adopts new OS releases.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Xcode's external editor support is inadequate and worse than its predecessor Project Builder's implementation. Many programmers are deeply attached to their preferred text editors and want to use them within Apple's IDE rather than the built-in editor. Apple's response to requests for external editor support was to ask what features users wanted added to the built-in editor instead — missing the point entirely. Despite eventually adding external editor support, no one seems happy with the implementation, and Gruber contends it would be far easier to build good external editor support than to make Xcode's mediocre built-in editor great.
key_points: Programmers who are attached to their text editors want to use them within IDEs, not have their features poorly replicated in built-in editors
Apple's Project Builder team initially responded to external editor requests by offering to copy features into their own editor, fundamentally misunderstanding the ask
External editor support got worse going from Project Builder to Xcode, despite being a long-requested feature
Xcode's built-in editor is 'rather pedestrian' and lacks basic features like regex search in its Find dialog
Building good external editor support would require far less effort than turning Xcode's editor into a great one
money_quotes: Some programmers grow very attached to their favorite text editor. (Yours truly: guilty as charged.)
"What features in your favorite editor would you like to see us add to Project Builder?"
"I'm not asking you to add features from my favorite editor to Project Builder; I just want to write code using my favorite editor."
But, are any Project Builder/Xcode users actually happy with this external editor support? Anyone?
It's not because Xcode's built-in editor is like totally rad, because it's really rather pedestrian.
It would be a lot less work to add terrific external editor support to Xcode than it would be to turn Xcode's built-in editor into a terrific text editor.
posts.key_insight: When programmers ask to use their own editor, the right response is to support that choice well — not to attempt replicating their editor's features in your inferior built-in one.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the release of SmartyPants 1.4.1, a minor bug-fix update to his typographic punctuation plugin. The release fixes a previously broken patch for dashes followed by quotes without intervening spaces, and adds support for treating   as whitespace. He credits Greg Knauss for contributing a patch.
key_points: SmartyPants 1.4.1 is a minor bug-fix release with no new features
Fixes a regression where the 1.4 dash-and-quote bug fix didn't actually work
Adds   recognition as whitespace for correct typographic handling
money_quotes: The bug fix from 1.4 for dashes followed by quotes with no intervening spaces now actually works.
posts.key_insight: Even small typographic tools require careful attention to edge cases like whitespace handling to produce correct results.

posts.summary: Gruber provides an exhaustive technical examination of Panther's (Mac OS X 10.3) improved on-screen text rendering. He builds his argument from first principles, explaining why computer displays are fundamentally poor for typography due to their low resolution compared to print, then walks through the history of pixel fonts versus outline fonts, anti-aliasing techniques, and sub-pixel rendering. The core observation is that Panther's anti-aliasing improvements are subtle — less vertical fuzziness, sharper letterform edges — but meaningful in the same way a half-size-too-small shoe matters by end of day. He frames the anti-aliasing debate fairly, acknowledging both camps (fuzzy vs. smooth), while arguing the train has left the station on Apple's direction.
key_points: Computer displays at 90-100 DPI are fundamentally terrible for typography compared to even cheap printers at 600+ DPI, and resolution is two-dimensional so the gap is worse than it sounds
Pixel fonts (like Susan Kare's Chicago 12) match screen resolution perfectly but can't scale, while outline fonts scale beautifully to print but must be shoe-horned into low-resolution screens

Anti-aliasing is inherently a tradeoff: it produces smoother, more faithful letterforms but introduces fuzziness — both camps are right, and only higher-resolution displays can truly resolve the debate
Panther's text rendering improvements are subtle but real: reduced vertical fuzziness, sharper tops on letterforms, and better-formed curves, visible clearly at magnification
The piece credits Mark Pilgrim's Panther overview as the best available, then deliberately goes the opposite direction — one change examined in minute detail rather than breadth
money_quotes: Computer displays are a terrible medium for typography.
Text from a $70 inkjet is much more legible than text displayed on a $2000 Apple Cinema Display.
In a nutshell, this is the fundamental truth of screen reading: the type looks terrible, but the content is extraordinarily convenient.
Non-anti-aliased text is dorky but sharp. Anti-aliased text is elegant but fuzzy.
You can't win, because truly winning would require on-screen text that is both elegant and sharp, and that requires higher-resolution displays than those we have today.
Helvetica looks just like it did in the old days. Helvetica Neue looks like poop.
You might be tempted to wonder whether it matters, given that you can barely perceive the difference without magnification. Well, consider a pair of shoes which are a half-size too small.
Panther's improved anti-aliasing epitomizes the best of Apple. The smallest details matter. Perfection is worth striving for.
posts.key_insight: Panther's subtly improved text anti-aliasing exemplifies Apple's philosophy that the smallest details matter — imperceptible improvements accumulate into meaningfully better experiences.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights the release of BBEdit 7.1, which includes 102 fixes and three significant new features: built-in Webkit HTML previewing, SFTP support, and Rendezvous support. He notes that all three new features only work on Mac OS X, even though BBEdit 7.1 still supports Mac OS 9. He frames this as a natural consequence of progress rather than abandonment of legacy users, since the features depend on OS X-only system services. He closes with a humorous note about his own easter egg in BBEdit's About box.
key_points: BBEdit 7.1's three major new features—Webkit preview, SFTP, and Rendezvous—are all Mac OS X-only despite the app still supporting OS 9
The OS X exclusivity of new features reflects the reality of platform capabilities, not developer laziness
Backwards compatibility should not come at the expense of progress
money_quotes: Backwards compatibility is noble, but not at the expense of progress.
That's not a sign of disdain or laziness on the part of Bare Bones's programmers — it's just reality.
Also, more importantly, the "Gruber" easter egg in the About BBEdit box still works.
posts.key_insight: Maintaining backward compatibility is admirable, but the best software moves forward by embracing the capabilities of modern platforms rather than being held back by legacy ones.

posts.summary: Gruber shares a brief, playful post teasing readers about a hidden easter egg menu in BBEdit's About box while refusing to reveal how to access it. He also links to an interview with himself at Under the Iron. The post is characteristic of Gruber's affection for BBEdit and software craftsmanship details.
key_points: BBEdit contains a hidden easter egg menu in its About box
Gruber deliberately teases but refuses to reveal the easter egg, respecting the tradition of discovery
He links to an interview with himself at Under the Iron
money_quotes: No, I will not tell you how to get it. It's an easter egg!
OK, it's an easter egg *menu*. No more hints.
posts.key_insight: Easter eggs are meant to be discovered, not revealed — the delight is in the hunt.

posts.summary: Gruber recounts wasting a Sunday afternoon trying to change his Movable Type URL structure to remove .html extensions. He wanted cleaner URLs without breaking existing links, a classic web perfectionist's dilemma. After struggling with his own approach, he concludes that Mark Pilgrim's existing instructions were the right solution all along. The post is a self-deprecating admission of reinventing the wheel.
key_points: Clean URLs without file extensions are preferable to .html-suffixed ones, per web design best practices
Backward compatibility with existing URLs is infinitely more important than URL aesthetics
Following established solutions beats trying to roll your own, a lesson Gruber learned the hard way on a wasted Sunday
money_quotes: let's say you're the type of perfectionist who cares about things such as exactly what your URLs look like.
it's a bit crufty to end them all with ".html".
Breaking links is way worse, infinitely worse, than a bit of cruftiness.
As opposed to, say, wasting a Sunday afternoon trying to do it your own way.
posts.key_insight: Backward compatibility with published URLs matters more than aesthetic perfection, and following proven solutions beats reinventing the wheel.

posts.summary: Gruber praises John Siracusa's Ars Technica review of Mac OS X Panther, focusing especially on Siracusa's masterful coverage of the Finder. He argues that Mac OS X can simultaneously be excellent and deeply flawed, pushing back against fans who treat these as mutually exclusive. Gruber performs a close reading of a single introductory paragraph from Siracusa's Finder section, dissecting how it methodically deflates Apple's marketing spin that the Panther Finder was brand-new. He highlights Siracusa's strategic organization — covering cosmetic changes, then objective performance problems, then subjective design critiques — as a model of persuasive technical writing. The piece is ultimately a meditation on what makes great technology criticism: honesty, structure, and the courage to call out flaws in products you admire.
key_points: Mac OS X Panther is very good overall, but that excellence doesn't preclude serious flaws — particularly in the Finder
Apple marketed the Panther Finder as brand-new, but the changes were cosmetic (brushed metal, sidebar) while the underlying UI design remained fundamentally unchanged
Siracusa's strategic ordering — new features, then objective performance problems, then subjective design critique — is a masterclass in persuasive structure
A subset of Mac users dogmatically refuse to acknowledge that Mac OS X can be both excellent and flawed, treating the two as mutually exclusive
Great reviewing means writing about what deserves to be written about, both good and bad, rather than following a formula of praise with token criticism
money_quotes: So many times with reviews of any nature, one gets the sense that the reviewer is following a formula: spend the first two-thirds of the review saying what's good, then sprinkle in a few negative points to make it seem 'balanced'.
For whatever reason, there exists a subset of Mac users who dogmatically insist that these two points are mutually exclusive — that if Mac OS X is excellent, then it therefore has no serious flaws.
That if Mac OS X has become a much better system than the old Mac OS was, then it therefore must be better in every single regard.
Siracusa starts with a crisp pinprick, deflating the Apple/Jobs spin that the Panther Finder is totally new. No fanfare, no gloating, no fuss.
The brushed metal windows are, literally, only skin deep.
He's saying, Look, I welcome you to take what I have to say about the Panther Finder with a grain of salt. Just hear me out.

These three sections of Finder coverage seem so logical, so obvious — but achieving this sort of 'obvious' organization is the real ballgame when you're writing something as long and wide-ranging as a comprehensive review of an operating system.
It's gotten to the point where I don't know what I look forward to more: Apple's annual Mac OS X update, or Siracusa's review of it.
posts.key_insight: The best technology criticism acknowledges excellence and flaws simultaneously, and persuades through strategic structure rather than rhetorical force.

posts.summary: Gruber documents a specific click-through bug in the Panther Finder's sidebar eject buttons. When the Finder is the active app, eject buttons in background windows visually highlight on hover (suggesting click-through support) but don't actually eject when clicked. Conversely, when the Finder is not the active app, the buttons don't highlight (suggesting no click-through) but clicking them actually ejects the volume. This contradictory behavior is almost certainly a bug, as the Finder otherwise handles click-through well. Gruber also praises the sidebar eject buttons as a conceptual improvement over the confusing drag-to-Trash unmount gesture.
key_points: The Panther Finder's sidebar eject buttons exhibit contradictory click-through behavior depending on whether the Finder is the active application
The sidebar eject buttons are a genuine improvement over the classic Mac gesture of dragging volumes to the Trash to unmount them
Click-through is most dangerous for controls that perform irreversible actions, and accidental ejection wastes time that can't be undone
The hard-coded blue gradient selection style in the Finder sidebar matches iTunes and iCal but not the user's system preference, adding to the inconsistency across Apple's own apps
The bug only manifests when the window is in icon view and the volume is already selected in the sidebar
money_quotes: It's a Daring Fireball Thanksgiving-week tradition: griping about the OS X Finder.
If you drag files and folders to the Trash to delete them, it seems natural to drag a volume to the Trash to *erase* it.

The trash-as-unmounter gesture is a bit less scary on Mac OS X than it was on Mac OS 9, because once you initiate such a drag, the Trash icon in the Dock turns into an eject button. But even that's somewhat awkward, because the eject icon doesn't appear until after you've started dragging the disk icon.

When the Finder is the active app, the eject buttons in background windows look like they support click-through, but they don't; when the Finder is not the active app, the eject buttons look like they don't support click-through, but they do.
This is so bizarre that it's got to be a bug.
The least suitable controls for click-through are the ones which are the most dangerous.
Click carefully.
posts.key_insight: The Panther Finder's eject buttons exhibit perfectly inverted click-through behavior — lighting up when they won't work and silently working when they don't light up — a bug so precisely backwards it reveals how easily complex UI state interactions can produce dangerous contradictions.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier analysis of Mac OS X Panther adoption rates among Daring Fireball's Safari-using readers. Tracking daily server logs from Panther's October 23 release through the end of November, he shows adoption climbing from 16% on launch day to nearly 80% within five weeks. He also shares full browser usage stats for November, revealing Safari as the top browser among his readers, with IE Windows close behind and Mozilla/Firebird variants making a notable showing. The data paints a picture of an enthusiastic, early-adopter Mac audience upgrading at a remarkable pace.
key_points: Panther adoption among Safari users went from 16% on launch day to ~79% within five weeks, showing extraordinarily fast OS adoption
By the end of the first week, Panther had already reached 50% of Safari users — half the audience upgraded within days
Safari was the single most-used browser on Daring Fireball with 18,382 unique IPs, narrowly beating IE Windows at 15,237
The Mozilla family (Camino, Firebird, Mozilla) collectively represented a significant alternative browser presence alongside Safari
The data is explicitly limited to Safari users, making it a measure of enthusiasm among Mac-focused early adopters rather than the general population
money_quotes: Remember, these figures are *only* Safari users. The Percent column indicates the percentage of Safari users who are running Panther.
While I'm at it, here are the Daring Fireball browser usage stats for the month of November.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X Panther was adopted at a strikingly rapid pace, with nearly 80% of Daring Fireball's Safari audience upgrading within just five weeks of release, reflecting the enthusiasm of Apple's core user base.

posts.summary: Gruber mocks the Neistat Brothers' viral iPod battery complaint campaign (iPodsDirtySecret.com), which involved spray-painting protest stencils on iPod ads and filming it. He reframes their sensational claims as mundane truths: the warranty lasts exactly as long as stated, the battery is difficult but not impossible to replace, and most iPod batteries work fine after two years. He caps it off by pointing out they filmed themselves committing vandalism and published the evidence.
key_points: The Neistat Brothers' complaint about iPod battery replacement was exaggerated and sensationalized
The 'unreplaceable' battery was actually replaceable, just somewhat difficult to swap
Engineering tradeoffs like form factor explain why the battery isn't easily swappable
The brothers filmed themselves committing criminal vandalism and released the footage publicly
money_quotes: iPod's "1-Year" warranty only lasts 12 months
Where by "unreplaceable" we mean "somewhat difficult to replace"
The vast majority of two-year-old iPod batteries still work just fine, but I'm extraordinarily perturbed about the bad luck I've had with mine
iPod would almost certainly require a significantly larger form factor to accommodate an easily-swappable battery
I'm so fucking dumb I've not only videotaped myself committing criminal vandalism, I've released the footage publicly
posts.key_insight: The iPod battery controversy was manufactured outrage built on reframing ordinary product tradeoffs as corporate malfeasance.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses reader feedback on two recent posts. First, he responds to skepticism about whether the Neistat brothers' defacement of iPod ads was criminal, maintaining that even if the ads were guerrilla marketing, the activity likely isn't legal and recording it on video was unwise. Second, he responds to requests for graphical displays of his Safari browser usage statistics, acknowledging that good graphs are hard to make because they need to accurately represent data, not just look nice. He shares reader-submitted graphs while noting the inherent inaccuracy of web server statistics.
key_points: Even if iPod wild posters were unofficial guerrilla marketing, defacing them is likely illegal even if unenforced, and filming yourself doing it is foolish
Good data visualization is difficult because it must accurately represent the data, not just present numbers in a prettier format
A simple line graph of browser percentages would be misleading without accounting for varying daily traffic volumes
Web server statistics are inherently approximate, which somewhat justifies producing approximate visualizations to match
money_quotes: This is not the sort of activity a wise person commits to videotape.
The reason I only posted numbers isn't that I dislike graphs, but that good graphs are hard to make.
Not just in terms of aesthetics, but in terms of presenting truly useful and accurate representations of the data.
The malleable nature of web server stats means even the raw numbers should be considered only roughly accurate, so why not produce a roughly accurate graph to accompany them?
posts.key_insight: Good data visualization requires more than aesthetics — it demands accurate representation of the underlying data, including its limitations and uncertainties.

posts.summary: Gruber provides a detailed tutorial on adding PHP syntax checking to BBEdit using PHP's command-line tool and AppleScript. He explains how PHP's -l flag performs rudimentary parse-error detection, then walks through building an AppleScript that handles unsaved files via temporary copies, displays errors in alerts with line-number navigation, and integrates with BBEdit's existing HTML syntax checker through menu script attachability. The script uses dual handlers so it works both as a standalone Scripts menu item and as a supplement to BBEdit's built-in Check Document Syntax command.
key_points: PHP's -l flag only catches parse errors and stops at the first one found, making it rudimentary but still useful
The script handles edge cases like unsaved changes, untitled documents, and non-Unix line endings by writing temporary files
BBEdit's menu script attachability allows combining PHP syntax checking with the built-in HTML checker through a single script with dual handlers
The script extracts line numbers from PHP error messages to select the offending line in the editor
money_quotes: I say "rudimentary" because, as far as I can tell, PHP's syntax checker only detects parse errors — errors so egregious they cause PHP to be unable to parse the code.
Still, it's better than no checker at all.
However, step 1, as stated above, is a bit lazy. What if the front BBEdit window has unsaved changes?
We could have the script save the file automatically if it has unsaved changes. But that's still lazy — we should be able to syntax-check a file with unsaved changes.
Thus, if BBEdit displays its "No errors were found" dialog, we know that the file passed through both syntax checkers without complaint.
posts.key_insight: BBEdit's extensibility through AppleScript and menu script attachability enables developers to build sophisticated tool integrations that handle real-world edge cases like unsaved files and combined syntax checking.

posts.summary: Gruber discusses two lingering habits from classic Mac OS that persist in OS X: reaching for the File menu to quit apps and using Cmd-N to create new folders instead of new Finder windows. He argues Apple was wrong to change the Cmd-N shortcut since millions of users had it deeply ingrained, while simultaneously advocating that users should generally adapt to OS X rather than fighting it with third-party hacks. He resolves the tension by distinguishing between stubbornly clinging to OS 9 behavior and simply needing a deeply wired keyboard shortcut, then provides an AppleScript hack to swap the shortcuts. The piece ends with the amusing observation that he immediately developed a new conflicting muscle memory for the same shortcut.
key_points: Apple was wrong to reassign Cmd-N in the Finder because established keyboard shortcuts become deeply ingrained muscle memory that shouldn't be changed lightly
Developers should carefully consider keyboard shortcuts from the outset, and if defaults must change, users should be able to reconfigure them
Users should generally embrace OS X on its own terms rather than using hacks to mimic OS 9 behavior — 'When in Rome, do as the Romans'
There's a meaningful distinction between stubbornly resisting change and needing to preserve a deeply wired keyboard shortcut
Muscle memory operates at different depths — years of Cmd-N for new folders is wired more deeply than months of Cmd-N for new windows
money_quotes: But they weren't starting from scratch. Millions of Mac users had been using Cmd-N to create new folders for years. This shouldn't even have been on the table.
The whole point of keyboard shortcuts is that you commit them to memory.
Mac OS X is offering you a round hole. Third party hacks will let you put your square peg inside, but it's not going to work as smoothly as using the round peg designed for the hole.
People in hell want ice water, but they're not going to get it.
It's a hack, but I consider it a clean hack, because it doesn't modify the Finder itself or any of its resources.
Cmd-N for making a new folder is wired into a much deeper part of my mind than it is for making a new window.
posts.key_insight: Keyboard shortcuts become deeply ingrained muscle memory that developers should treat as a contract with users — changing them breaks trust and workflow, even when the new assignment is more logical.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects the deeply flawed implementation of Panther's (Mac OS X 10.3) custom keyboard shortcuts feature in System Preferences. He catalogs its major deficiencies: requiring users to type exact menu item names instead of selecting from a list, arbitrary restrictions on allowed key combinations, no conflict detection with existing shortcuts, and no ability to remove default shortcuts. He traces these problems to an architectural decision where Apple layered user customizations on top of the old system rather than replacing it, meaning the preferences panel has no way to query applications for their menu items or existing shortcuts. He concludes that Apple needs a proper system-wide mechanism for applications to register all their shortcuts, even if it means only explicitly supporting apps would benefit.
key_points: Panther's custom keyboard shortcuts require typing exact menu item names (including distinguishing ellipses from three periods), which is fundamentally un-Mac-like
The feature allows nonsensical shortcuts (Option-key only) while blocking useful ones (Cmd-letter), and provides no conflict detection or ability to remove existing shortcuts
The root cause is architectural: Apple layered customization on top of the old system rather than replacing it, so the prefs panel cannot query apps for their menus or detect conflicts
Third-party apps like BBEdit and Mailsmith already do keyboard shortcut customization correctly — with lists, immediate application, and conflict prevention
A half-assed feature is worse than no feature at all; Apple needs a proper system-wide mechanism for apps to register all their shortcuts
money_quotes: What could be less Mac-like than needing to type the exact name of something to use it?
When the human interface starts following the lead of the command-line interface, we might as well pack up our bags and move to Redmond.
Which means when I type a bullet in the Finder, the front window is sent into the Dock. Nice.
A half-assed feature usually is worse than none at all, and half-assed might be a generous description in this case.

What Apple has done is implement a new user-customizable architecture for storing and determining menu key shortcuts, but instead of replacing the old architecture, the new way is just sitting on top of the old one.
It not only doesn't know the app's built-in shortcuts, it can't know.

If that's not possible given the current underlying implementation, it's not an argument for forcing users to tolerate these limitations — it's an argument that we need a new underlying implementation.
posts.key_insight: Apple's Panther keyboard shortcut customization is fundamentally broken because it was layered on top of the old architecture rather than replacing it, making proper menu discovery and conflict detection architecturally impossible.

posts.summary: Gruber describes his recurring formula at Daring Fireball: someone writes something dumb, it gets unwarranted attention, and he responds. He notes that the real problem isn't the existence of bad takes but that reputable publications amplify them. In this case, PC Magazine's Lance Ulanoff wrote a gloating response to a DHCP security vulnerability on Mac OS X, and Gruber points to John C. Welch's rebuttal as a perfect takedown, making his own retort unnecessary.
key_points: Gruber codifies his recurring three-step blogging pattern: jackass writes something dumb, it gets attention, he retorts
The real problem isn't that bad takes exist but that credible publications give them unwarranted amplification
PC Magazine's Lance Ulanoff used a Mac DHCP vulnerability as an opportunity for anti-Mac gloating
Gruber delegates the rebuttal to John C. Welch, calling his response 'perfect'
money_quotes: Some jackass writes something really dumb. They get a lot of attention. I politely retort.
There's always some new jackass — or serial offender — publishing some sort of misleading / misinformed / self-aggrandizing crap.
The problem is with #2, wherein publications which ought to know better give the jerks from #1 unwarranted attention.
What's nice, though, is that sometimes #3 is unnecessary, because someone else does the retorting.
John C. Welch has written a perfect response. Really, I mean it: perfect.
posts.key_insight: The real damage in tech media isn't that bad takes exist, but that otherwise credible publications amplify them with unwarranted attention.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects the absurdity of Reuters and other major media outlets repeatedly quoting technology analyst Rob Enderle despite his track record of consistently wrong predictions about Apple. Using the example of a Reuters article about rumored lower-cost iPods, Gruber shows how Enderle injects his own baseless speculation (flash-memory-based players) that contradicts basic economic logic. Gruber argues that journalists use analysts like Enderle as quote mills to lend false credibility to their reporting, actually diluting accuracy in the process. The piece also offers Gruber's own analysis of why cheaper iPods with hard drives make sense while flash memory does not.
key_points: Rob Enderle has a long track record of wrong predictions about Apple, yet remains a go-to source for mainstream tech reporters
Enderle's flash-memory prediction contradicts basic economics: flash memory costs significantly more than small hard disks, undermining the premise of a cheaper iPod
Professional journalists use analyst quotes as a credibility veneer, but sourcing from unreliable analysts actually makes reporting less accurate
The original iPod rumor from Think Secret has logical merit — with 40GB at the high end, the natural expansion is downmarket on price rather than upmarket on capacity
Assembly-line journalism that relies on 'quote mills' like Enderle is a systemic problem, not just an individual failing
money_quotes: One would hope these two facts would be mutually exclusive — that a self-professed industry expert whose pronouncements about Apple were nearly always wrong would eventually stop being asked for his opinion about the company and its products.

An amateur writer would just read the Think Secret article and regurgitate it; whereas your professional capital-J Journalist wire service reporter reads the article, regurgitates it, and then calls an 'industry expert' professional quotester like Enderle for comment.
What I find fascinating is to see a Reuters reporter diluting the accuracy of his article by fishing for quotes from a 'respected' industry analyst.
Martell quotes Enderle to make his reporting sound credible — at what is almost certainly the expense of genuine accuracy.
If the new iPods are cheaper, why would Apple switch to a more expensive storage medium?
Enderle's presence is a warning sign. I see a quote from him I get the message. The reporter is out of ideas and has decided to cut corners.
If you want to find a way out, start digging for real stories, real news, and for that you can't go to the quote mills.
posts.key_insight: Mainstream tech journalism undermines its own credibility by reflexively quoting analysts with proven track records of being wrong, prioritizing the appearance of expert sourcing over actual accuracy.

posts.summary: Gruber discovered that several IP addresses were hitting his RSS feed over 100 times per day, and every single abusive poller was using Shrook, a Mac OS X news aggregator. The culprit turned out to be a feature, not a bug: Shrook allowed users to set checking intervals as low as every minute. While Shrook respects HTTP 304 codes so bandwidth isn't wasted, Gruber argues the behavior is still rude and unnecessary. He notes that Shrook's default Distributed Checking mode is actually well-designed, coordinating updates centrally so individual clients rarely need to poll. He encourages Shrook users to either use the default distributed mode or set intervals of 30 minutes or more.
key_points: Every abusive RSS poller in Gruber's logs was using Shrook, which allowed checking intervals as frequent as every minute
Even though HTTP 304 responses prevent bandwidth waste, excessively frequent polling is still inconsiderate server behavior
Shrook's default Distributed Checking feature is actually a smart solution that provides near-real-time updates without hammering individual servers
NetNewsWire caps its minimum polling interval at 30 minutes, which Gruber considers a more responsible design choice
money_quotes: That's not to say every Shrook user is polling at an abusive clip, but that every one of my subscribers who is abusive, is using Shrook. Every one.
So, I think, there must be a bug in Shrook. Ends up, it's a feature.

These Shrook users are behaving like annoying little kids in the back seat of the car, except instead of ceaselessly asking "Are we there yet? Are we there yet?", they're asking "Has the feed changed yet? Has the feed changed yet?"
304 or no 304, however, it simply rubs me the wrong way to see people checking for updates this frequently.
You get the same results as with frequent polling, without abusing the server.
posts.key_insight: Allowing users to set aggressively frequent polling intervals is a design failure, even when bandwidth-efficient protocols mitigate the worst technical consequences.

posts.summary: Gruber catalogs the most persistent broken UI habits reported by readers transitioning from Mac OS 9 (and Windows) to Mac OS X. He organizes them into categories: Command vs. Control key confusion for switchers, the new window-by-window layering replacing app-by-app layering, clicking the desktop no longer bringing all Finder windows forward, system-wide keyboard shortcut reassignments like Cmd-M and Cmd-H, the loss of the Finder's Put Away command, and proxy icons no longer behaving as aliases. Throughout, he distinguishes between changes that are genuinely worse and those that are better designs requiring painful adjustment, ultimately arguing that Mac OS X 10.0 was the right moment for Apple to make sweeping changes even at the cost of muscle memory.
key_points: The transition from Mac OS 9 to OS X broke deep muscle-memory habits, but many of the changes were objectively better UI designs despite being painful to adjust to
Apple's decision to claim system-wide keyboard shortcuts (Cmd-M, Cmd-H, Cmd-`) was justified because Mac OS X 10.0 was effectively a new operating system, not an incremental update
The loss of the principle that anything looking like an alias should act like an alias represents a deeper design regression in OS X, affecting the Dock, Cmd-Tab switcher, and Recent Items
Window-by-window layering replaced app-by-app layering, and while many prefer it, clicking the desktop to bring all Finder windows forward remains a sorely missed shortcut
The Finder's Put Away command was more useful than OS X's Undo, since it could restore any individual item from the Trash to its original location
money_quotes: It's not a list of "bad" UI changes in Mac OS X — it's simply a list of changes which have resulted in hard-to-break habits for long-time users.

Version numbers are as much driven by marketing as engineering; the truth is, there have been two versions of the Macintosh operating system: the first evolved from the original system in 1984 through Mac OS 9.2; the second is Mac OS X.
As the visual bedrock of the entire Mac GUI, it already gets special treatment in numerous places.
In fact, perhaps a better question is why not? Why shouldn't the Finder pop all its windows forward when you click the desktop?
The broken habit is that you can no longer expect items that look like aliases to act like aliases.
Something that could be an alias, either is an alias, or acts like an alias.
Uncomfortable though it may be during the adjustment period, I'm going to cast my vote that Apple was right to do this.
posts.key_insight: The transition to Mac OS X broke countless muscle-memory habits, but the deeper design loss was not the changed shortcuts — it was the abandonment of the principle that visual representations should behave as the real objects they depict.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that GarageBand perfectly encapsulates Apple's core identity: making computers for people who want to create, not just consume. He contends that Apple's small market share is misleading because Apple sells to the most interesting, creative segment of the PC market. The piece contrasts the excitement of creative tools like GarageBand with the boring utility of Microsoft Office, noting the keynote audience's tepid response to Office 2004. He also offers quick takes on the iPod Mini, arguing its value proposition is size rather than price.
key_points: GarageBand distills Apple's identity into a single product: empowering users to be producers, not just consumers
Apple's small market share is misleading because most Wintel PCs are just office equipment, while Apple captures the most creative users
The keynote audience's silence during Microsoft's Office presentation revealed that productivity software can't compete with creative tools for excitement
The iPod Mini's value is its size, not its price — and initial pricing skepticism mirrors the original iPod's reception
money_quotes: People who want to use their computers to make cool things. People who want to be producers, not just consumers.
If it's possible to distill into a single thought what it is that makes Apple Apple, and what has made the Macintosh so enduringly popular, that's it.
The vast majority of Wintel PCs are used as little more than modern-day typewriters. They're just office equipment.
The answer is simply that they're selling the best computers, to the most interesting people.
Maybe it is only two percent of the total PC market, but it's the most interesting two percent.
It's not because Mac users are biased against Microsoft, it's just that spreadsheets are fucking boring.
Compared to being able to make your own movies and music, it's hard to get excited about easier print previews in Excel.
GarageBand epitomizes what's different and better about the Mac. Everyone wants it, and there's nothing like it for any other platform.
posts.key_insight: Apple's seemingly small market share is irrelevant because the company dominates the creative segment of computing — the only segment that actually matters culturally.

posts.summary: Gruber argues against the widespread consensus that the iPod Mini is too expensive at $249, just $50 less than the 15 GB iPod. He contends that critics are wrong to frame the Mini as a budget model — it's a small-end model, not a low-end one. Miniaturization requires more engineering, not less, and the Mini is competitively priced against comparable players like the Rio Nitrus. He argues that even if the 15 GB iPod cost the same $249, some people would still choose the Mini for its significantly smaller size. While acknowledging the price is close to being too high, Gruber bets Apple has done its homework on consumer demand for a smaller, lighter player.
key_points: The iPod Mini was misframed by rumor sites and critics as a low-end model when it's actually a small-end model — miniaturization costs more, not less
Compared to competitors like the Rio Nitrus (same price, same capacity) and Rio Cali ($149 for only 128 MB), the Mini is competitively priced
Size and weight matter enormously for pocket-sized devices: the Mini weighs 33% less and takes up 41% less space than the standard iPod
The near-universal criticism of the Mini's pricing represents a contrarian opportunity — Gruber bets it will sell just fine
money_quotes: If you want to take a contrary-to-popular-opinion position that has a good chance of paying off down the road, tell people you think the iPod Minis are going to sell just fine.
The iPod Mini is not about being a new low-end model — it's a new small-end model.
I agree that the $50 price difference between the Mini and 15 GB iPods is negligible. That's exactly the point.
If hard disk capacity were the only factor that mattered, we'd all be using brick-sized players from Creative Labs.
When you're talking about pocket-sized objects, a few fractions of an inch in each dimension can add up to a big difference in volume.
I'm convinced that the iPod Mini was designed based on more than just a Jobsian hunch.
posts.key_insight: The iPod Mini's value proposition is miniaturization, not price — critics who focus only on the $50 gap to the 15 GB iPod misunderstand that smaller is a feature people will pay for.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier 'Die Hard' post about Mac switcher habits with two practical tips. First, he highlights uControl, a free kernel extension that can swap Command and Control keys for Windows switchers struggling with keyboard shortcuts. Second, he reveals that Command-dragging app icons from the Dock enables useful drag-and-drop behavior beyond just removing them. He also credits Sven-S. Porst's blog for surfacing both tips earlier, while disagreeing with the suggestion to use Classic Window Management for all Finder windows.
key_points: Windows switchers' most commonly broken habit is using Ctrl instead of Cmd for keyboard shortcuts on Mac
uControl can swap Command and Control keys, though as a kernel extension it carries some fragility risk across OS updates
Command-dragging Dock icons enables real drag-and-drop behavior, such as opening an app's scripting dictionary
Gruber draws a distinction between wanting classic window layering for desktop clicks versus wanting it globally for all Finder windows
money_quotes: Whether you really want to do something as radical as swapping your Command and Control keys, I don't know.
If you plan on using a Mac for the long haul, you'd certainly be better served getting accustomed to the standard key arrangement.
Especially for switchers from Windows who still use Windows in addition to Mac OS X, I can see how this might be a devilishly difficult habit to break.
This is a great tip. I don't think it's right that you need to hold down Command to get useful drag-and-drop behavior, but I'm happy as a clam that it's possible at all.
I don't want classic window layering for all Finder windows, I just want it for when I click on the desktop.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's small interaction design choices — like requiring Command-drag for Dock icons — reveal a tension between discoverability and clean interface design that affects both switchers and longtime users.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes the 2004 HP-Apple iPod licensing deal, arguing it represents Apple's transformation of the iPod from a product into a platform. He frames the deal as a massive win for both companies: HP gets a market-leading digital music solution that differentiates it from Dell, while Apple secures long-term platform dominance. Crucially, the deal bypasses Microsoft entirely, denying Redmond its usual strategy of leveraging Windows monopoly into adjacent markets. Gruber draws a careful distinction between Apple's failed 1990s Mac OS cloning and this limited licensing strategy, arguing the iPod can achieve market leadership without monopoly. He dismisses Paul Thurrott's speculation about WMA support as nonsensical, since the entire point of the deal is independence from Microsoft.
key_points: The HP-Apple deal transforms the iPod from a standalone product into a three-pronged digital music platform (iPod/iTunes/iTMS), analogous to how Windows functions as a PC platform.
Limited licensing to premium partners is the correct strategy for the iPod — widespread licensing commoditizes, while selective partnerships preserve brand value and expand market share.
The deal's most significant dimension is that it completely bypasses Microsoft, denying them their usual monopoly-leveraging strategy in the emerging digital media market.
Apple's digital music platform runs on Windows but is independent of it, making Apple a far more appealing partner than Microsoft for companies tired of being commoditized.
The real money in digital music is in hardware (iPods), not software or per-song sales — making the licensing economics compelling for both companies.
money_quotes: The HP deal changes the iPod from product to platform.
Windows-style widespread licensing is for commodity items. Limited licensing is for premium items.
The market leader doesn't have to hold a monopoly.
Anyone who claims Apple could have achieved greater than 50 percent OS market share without sacrificing what makes the Mac the Mac has no comprehension of how Microsoft built the Windows empire.
Apple's music platform runs on Windows, but it is independent of Windows. It is therefore independent of Microsoft.
If they took all your potatoes the last time you invited them to dinner, why invite them again?
They might as well pull down their pants and get out the Vaseline while they're at it.
The surprise isn't that HP said yes, the surprise is that Apple made the offer in the first place.
posts.key_insight: The HP-Apple iPod deal represents Apple's first successful platform licensing strategy — limited, premium, and crucially independent of Microsoft — transforming the iPod from a product into an industry platform.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the birth of his first child, Jonas Henry Gruber, born on January 15, 2004 after 35 hours of labor. The baby weighed 9 pounds, 0.6 ounces. The post is a brief, joyful personal announcement with Gruber's characteristic dry humor woven into the happy news.
key_points: Jonas Henry Gruber was born after 35 hours of labor
The baby weighed 9 pounds, 0.6 ounces
Gruber expresses uncharacteristic, unguarded happiness about fatherhood
money_quotes: Thursday, 15 January 2004, at 11:44 p.m., after a mere 35 hours of labor, my wife Amy gave birth to our first child.
The whole family is home, healthy, and happy. One-third of the family just took a dump in its pants.
Without an iota of hyperbole, I can honestly say I've never been happier.
posts.key_insight: A rare personal post where Gruber drops the tech commentary to announce his son's birth with characteristic dry wit and genuine warmth.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights Andy Hertzfeld's Folklore.org, a new website documenting the development of the original Macintosh through first-person accounts from the people who built it. He praises Hertzfeld as both a titan of Mac history and a great writer, noting that the site includes contributions from other key figures like Bruce Horn, Susan Kare, and Donn Denman. Gruber frames the site as a gift to the Mac community timed around the Mac's 20th anniversary.
key_points: Andy Hertzfeld launched Folklore.org to preserve first-person stories about the original Macintosh development
The site's software is designed to host stories about multiple projects, though only the Mac history is live so far
Contributors include key original Mac team members like Bruce Horn, Susan Kare, and Donn Denman
money_quotes: Andy Hertzfeld is one of the genuine titans of Mac history.
Not only did he write an enormous chunk of the original toolbox APIs and a ton of innovative Mac software, but, by all accounts, he's also a nice guy.
It's a great web site full of great stories.
It's like a giant 20th-anniversary-of-the-Mac birthday present for the entire Mac community.
Insanely great, indeed.
posts.key_insight: Andy Hertzfeld's Folklore.org is an invaluable first-person archive of original Macintosh development history, arriving as a fitting 20th-anniversary gift to the Mac community.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights a well-known and indisputable problem with the Mac OS X Finder: its display frequently falls out of sync with the actual file system state. New files don't appear and modifications aren't reflected until manually prompted. He notes that the real fix must come from Apple's Finder team, possibly using FreeBSD's asynchronous event notification as John Siracusa suggested. In the meantime, he recommends Rainer Brockerhoff's freeware contextual menu plugin 'Nudge,' which forces the Finder to refresh its view.
key_points: The OS X Finder's failure to stay in sync with the file system is an indisputable bug, not a matter of debate
Even ardent Finder defenders must acknowledge the stale display problem
The proper fix requires Apple to implement low-level file system event notification, as Siracusa outlined
Brockerhoff's Nudge plugin provides a practical workaround by forcing Finder view refreshes
money_quotes: Some of the problems with the Mac OS X Finder are debatable. Others aren't.
One of the OS X Finder's indisputable problems is that its display is often woefully out of sync with the actual state of the file system.
Even the most ardent defenders of the OS X Finder have to admit this stinks.
The Finder should have automatically re-sorted the window, moving the just-modified files to the top of the list — but, of course, it didn't, because it never noticed that the files changed.
posts.key_insight: The OS X Finder's inability to reflect the current state of the file system is not a matter of preference but an objective failure that even its defenders cannot excuse.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews the first public beta of OmniWeb 5.0, a Mac web browser built on Apple's WebCore rendering engine. He identifies two revolutionary features: workspaces that automatically save and restore browser state across sessions, and per-site preferences that let users customize font sizes and other settings for individual websites. While praising the browser's ambition and power-user features, he notes a significant drawback: OmniWeb uses a forked version of WebCore rather than Apple's Web Kit framework, leaving its rendering engine about eight months behind Safari's. He concludes that OmniWeb 5 targets high-functioning users who need more than Safari offers, comparing the relationship to Final Cut Express versus iMovie.
key_points: OmniWeb 5's workspace feature automatically saves and restores all browser state — windows, tabs, positions — across sessions and even crashes, a capability no other browser offers

Per-site preferences let users permanently customize font size, user-agent string, and JavaScript settings for individual websites, solving the persistent problem of designers overriding user preferences
OmniWeb's tab implementation uses visual thumbnails in a sidebar drawer rather than traditional tabs, trading screen space for better tab identification
By forking WebCore instead of using Apple's Web Kit framework, OmniWeb gains flexibility for custom features but falls eight months behind Safari's rendering improvements
The market for commercial browsers is small, but OmniWeb targets power users willing to pay for features Safari lacks — much as Final Cut Express serves users who outgrow iMovie
money_quotes: OmniWeb 5 is the first and only web browser I've ever seen that places any value whatsoever on your state — the position, placement, and content of your open browser windows.
Site Preferences are more than just great. They're *fucking* great.
What's the point of having a default text size preference if web designers are going to assume you're too stupid to know about it?
Experience has taught me not to second-guess engineering decisions from the other side of the source code.
Like any hard design decision, either choice — WebCore or Web Kit — involves trade-offs. (If there weren't any trade-offs, and one choice was clearly superior to the others, there is no decision.)
OmniWeb is to Safari as Final Cut Express is to iMovie.
You simply can't expect one browser to work as well for grandmothers as it does for web programmers.
posts.key_insight: OmniWeb 5's willingness to treat browser state as something worth preserving — through workspaces and per-site preferences — represents a fundamentally different philosophy about respecting the user's investment in their browsing environment.

posts.summary: Gruber explores the practical tradeoffs of using different scripting languages on Mac OS X to change HFS file and creator type metadata. He compares AppleScript, tcsh shell scripting, and Perl implementations of the same task, triggered via Brent Simmons's Big Cat Scripts contextual menu plug-in. While he prefers Perl's syntax, he concludes that AppleScript is superior for this use case because it works on stock Mac OS X installations and, crucially, causes the Finder to immediately update its file views. Shell and Perl scripts modify metadata at the Unix level, leaving the Finder's display out of sync until it notices the change on its own.
key_points: Mac OS X uniquely bridges AppleScript and Unix scripting worlds, with system support for both via the Script Menu and tools like Big Cat Scripts
The same file metadata task (setting HFS file and creator types) can be accomplished in AppleScript, tcsh, or Perl, each with different dependency requirements
AppleScript is the only option that works on a stock Mac OS X install without developer tools or CPAN modules
AppleScript's killer advantage is that the Finder immediately reflects metadata changes because it's the Finder itself making the modification
Practical convenience sometimes trumps language preference — Gruber chooses AppleScript over his preferred Perl when the Finder needs to stay in sync
money_quotes: Mac OS X brings together two worlds of scripting — traditional Mac OS AppleScript, and traditional Unix scripting.
Purely in terms of language syntax, I prefer the Perl version. (So shoot me.)
The AppleScript version is the only one that will work on a stock Mac OS X installation.
It's highly unsatisfying to run a script that says "make this a BBEdit text file", and but not have the file's icon changed immediately to reflect that the script actually worked as intended.
The Finder obviously notices that the file's metadata has changed, because it's the Finder itself that changes the file's creator and file type info.
Even in cases where I feel like it would take less time for me to write a script using Perl, I'll use AppleScript instead if I plan to invoke the script from Big Cat's contextual menu.
posts.key_insight: When scripting file operations on Mac OS X, AppleScript's integration with the Finder — ensuring immediate visual feedback — makes it the pragmatic choice over syntactically preferred languages like Perl.

posts.summary: Gruber explains how to remove HFS file and creator type metadata from files via scripts, complementing his earlier post on setting them. He describes Mac OS X's hierarchy for determining file-app bindings: per-file Finder bindings first, then HFS creator types, then filename extensions. The core technical problem is that HFS types are four-byte OSType values, not strings, so you can't set them to empty strings. The solution, provided by Jim Correia, is to set them to four null bytes (ASCII character 0 repeated four times).
