Gruber provides working solutions in both AppleScript and Perl, and explains his personal workflow of using creator types for files he edits and extensions for files he only reads.
key_points: Mac OS X uses a three-tier hierarchy for file-app binding: per-file Finder assignments (stored in resource forks), HFS creator types, then filename extensions
HFS file and creator types are not stored in resource forks but are metadata fields defined by the file system itself, contrary to widespread misconception
OSType values are always exactly four bytes, so you cannot coerce a zero-length or arbitrary-length string into one — the 'empty' value is four null bytes
The practical workflow distinction: use creator types for files you actively edit (so they open in your editor) and let extensions control files you only read
Historical workarounds like '????' or four spaces function but are not genuine solutions compared to four null bytes
money_quotes: Supporting file type and creator metadata isn't mutually exclusive with supporting Launch Services-style filename extension app assignment.
If it's an HTML file I'm editing, I want it to open in BBEdit; if it's an HTML file I just want to read, however, then I want it to open in my default web browser.

Contrary to widespread misconception, HFS file and creator types are not stored in the resource fork, they're simply metadata fields defined by the file system itself, just like the filename and date fields.
The trick is to think like a C/C++ programmer, not a scripter.
What looks like an 'empty' OSType is actually four bytes, each byte with a value of zero.
posts.key_insight: HFS file types are four-byte OSType values, not strings, so clearing them requires setting four null bytes rather than an empty string — a solution that requires thinking at the byte level rather than the scripting level.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights Matthew 'mpt' Thomas's new blog post documenting his first 48 hours switching from Mac OS 9 to Mac OS X. Thomas, known as a thoughtful usability critic, compiled 48 specific criticisms of the OS X experience. Gruber endorses the piece as recommended reading, noting he only disagrees with 1.5 of Thomas's 48 points. The brief post serves as a signal boost for quality usability criticism of Apple's then-current operating system.
key_points: Matthew Thomas, a respected usability critic, switched from Mac OS 9 to OS X and documented 48 specific complaints
Gruber finds Thomas's criticisms almost entirely valid, disagreeing with only 1.5 out of 48 points
Gruber values informed, detailed usability criticism of Apple products even when it's largely negative
money_quotes: It's always fun when a thoughtful usability critic switches from Mac OS 9 to OS X.
Out of his 48 points, I disagree with one and a half — all of #28, and the parenthetical aside in #3.
Recommended reading, even if you wind up disagreeing with more than 1.5 of his criticisms.
posts.key_insight: Gruber's near-total agreement with 48 usability criticisms of Mac OS X shows his willingness to acknowledge Apple's design shortcomings when the critique is well-reasoned.

posts.summary: Gruber describes a recurring problem where his muscle memory for Cmd-E (Enter Search String in BBEdit) causes him to accidentally send unfinished emails in Mailsmith, where Cmd-E means Send Now. Rather than reassigning either shortcut — both of which are well-established conventions — he devises an AppleScript menu script that detects when text is selected (indicating he likely intended to search, not send) and presents a confirmation dialog. The solution elegantly resolves a rare but embarrassing problem he'd suffered for nearly six years. He also discusses why both shortcuts are correctly assigned, defending Mailsmith's choice of Cmd-E for Send as the closest thing to a standard for Mac email clients.
key_points: Cmd-E is a long-established standard for Enter Search String in Mac text editors, while also being Eudora's established shortcut for Send in email clients — both assignments are reasonable
The real problem isn't bad shortcut design but conflicting muscle memory between two apps built on the same editing engine
The solution uses AppleScript to detect context: if text is selected when Send Now is invoked, it's likely a mistaken search intent, so a confirmation dialog appears
Deeply ingrained shortcuts like Cmd-S and Cmd-P should never be repurposed, even if an app doesn't save or print anything
Mailsmith and BBEdit's shared editing DNA is both Mailsmith's greatest charm and the source of this specific hazard
money_quotes: I'm writing in Mailsmith; I select a word; I hit Cmd-E with the intention of entering a search string — but fuck, by hitting Cmd-E, I just sent the message before it was finished.
Even if your app doesn't actually save or print anything, you should never reappropriate deeply ingrained shortcuts like Cmd-S or Cmd-P.
The problem isn't the shortcuts themselves — it's the fact that I have muscle memory for both of them.
What I want to defend against is that one time in a thousand when I hit Cmd-E in Mailsmith with the intention of invoking Enter Search String.
Cmd-E is certainly a better shortcut for sending a message than Apple Mail's contorted Shift-Cmd-D.
It has completely solved a rare but highly annoying problem I've suffered for over five years.
posts.key_insight: When two well-designed conventions conflict through muscle memory, the best fix isn't reassigning shortcuts but adding context-aware safeguards that detect likely user intent.

posts.summary: Gruber points readers to Tomas Jogin's scathing critique of RealPlayer, endorsing it as devastating and spot-on. The brief post serves as a link recommendation during a quiet period on Daring Fireball. An update notes that Jogin published follow-up posts containing internal memos and emails from RealNetworks employees, adding further weight to the critique.
key_points: Gruber endorses Tomas Jogin's critique of RealPlayer as 'utterly scathing' and 'devastating'
The post is a brief link blog entry pointing readers to external commentary
Follow-up posts from Jogin included internal RealNetworks communications that corroborated the critique
money_quotes: His conclusion is so spot-on, it's devastating.
posts.key_insight: RealPlayer had become so user-hostile that even internal communications from RealNetworks employees couldn't defend its design choices.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the release of SmartyPants 1.5, noting minor improvements and teasing integration with an unannounced new plug-in coming soon. He playfully hints that readers should check the release notes if they want to be early adopters. An update follows announcing version 1.5.1, which fixes a bug related to the not-yet-announced plug-in causing a blank menu item in Movable Type. The post is notable primarily as a teaser for what turned out to be Markdown, as indicated by the 'Next' link.
key_points: SmartyPants 1.5 released with minor improvements and integration hooks for an upcoming plug-in
The post serves as a deliberate teaser for Markdown, Gruber's most influential creation
A quick 1.5.1 bugfix followed to address a blank menu item caused by the unreleased plug-in's absence
Gruber uses playful secrecy to build anticipation for the announcement
money_quotes: Just a couple of minor improvements — plus one more thing.
Check the release notes if you can't wait for the next announcement, or if you want to be able to tell your grandchildren you were in on it from before it was actually announced.
posts.key_insight: This brief teaser post is historically significant as the immediate precursor to the announcement of Markdown, with Gruber borrowing Steve Jobs's 'one more thing' cadence to build anticipation for what would become one of the most widely adopted text formatting tools on the web.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the release of Markdown, his new text-to-HTML formatting tool. He describes it as a simple, readable plain text syntax that converts to valid XHTML or HTML. He acknowledges the comparison to Textile but distinguishes Markdown as different. The tool works as a Movable Type and Blosxom plugin, a standalone Perl script, and a BBEdit text filter, released under the GPL.
key_points: Markdown is a plain text formatting syntax designed for readability that converts to valid XHTML/HTML
It works as a blog plugin (Movable Type, Blosxom), standalone Perl script, and BBEdit text filter
Gruber preemptively addresses the Textile comparison with characteristic brevity: it's sort of like Textile, except different
The announcement is deliberately understated for what would become one of the most influential writing tools on the web
money_quotes: Markdown allows web writers to compose text using a simple, readable, plain text formatting syntax; Markdown takes care of translating it to valid XHTML (or, if you prefer, HTML).
So, yes, before another person asks, it is sort of like Textile. Except that it's different.
If you're not interested, I'll try to change your mind in a bit.
posts.key_insight: This casually brief announcement introduced Markdown — a tool that would go on to become the de facto standard for plain text writing on the web, making Gruber's preference for readable simplicity one of his most lasting contributions to technology.

posts.summary: Gruber makes the philosophical case for Markdown by weaving together two arguments: first, that changing your mind often comes from looking at different facts rather than having the wrong ones (illustrated by the Postel's Law debate in XML feed parsing); second, that just because something isn't difficult doesn't mean it's the right way to do it (illustrated by how even expert web developers use blogging software instead of hand-coding). He applies both points to writing in raw HTML — conceding HTML is easy to learn, but arguing it's wrong for composing and reading prose. Markdown is positioned not as a replacement for HTML but as a complement, targeting the sweet spot where plain text conventions suffice while allowing raw HTML when needed.
key_points: Changing your mind usually happens not because you had the wrong facts, but because you were looking at the wrong facts — as illustrated by the Postel's Law vs. XML strictness debate
Just because something isn't difficult doesn't mean it's how it ought to be done — even expert web developers use blogging software to avoid monotonous repetitive tasks
HTML is easy to learn but fundamentally wrong as a writing and reading format for prose composition
Most text-to-HTML filters fail because they replace HTML tags with equally unreadable custom syntax; Markdown instead targets a sweet spot between plain text and raw HTML
The goal of Markdown is to preserve the 'feel' of reading and writing prose, not force you to 'think' about markup
money_quotes: When I end up changing my mind on a matter of opinion, it usually is not because I had the facts wrong; it's because I was looking at the wrong facts.
Just because something isn't difficult doesn't mean it's the way it ought to be done.
Monotonous repetitive tasks are exactly what computers are good at, and what humans are bad at.
Doesn't it strike you as odd to write in a format that isn't readable? Suddenly, it struck me as absurd.
Why is it that desktop weblog editors need to provide 'preview' modes? You don't need to 'preview' an email before you send it — you write it, you read it, you edit it, right there.
Other filters are aimed at replacing HTML. Markdown is aimed elsewhere.
When you write and read text that's marked-up with HTML tags, it's forcing you to concentrate on the think of it. It's the feel of it that I want Markdown-formatted text to convey.
The tags that most weblog apps provide shortcuts for — italics, bold, links, paragraphs, blockquotes — are the ones you shouldn't need to worry about 'tagging' in the first place.
posts.key_insight: Markdown succeeds not by making HTML easier, but by recognizing that prose composition and markup tagging are fundamentally different activities that shouldn't be forced into the same mode.

posts.summary: Gruber announces Markdown 1.0b4, a bug-fix release with improved Blosxom integration. He highlights Aaron Swartz's html2text Python script, which converts HTML back into Markdown-formatted plain text, noting he plans to use it to convert his old Daring Fireball articles written in raw HTML. He acknowledges that perfect round-trip fidelity between Markdown and HTML isn't possible but praises html2text for coming close. He also mentions Gust's HumaneText, which makes Markdown available through Mac OS X's Services menu.
key_points: Markdown 1.0b4 is a bug-fix release with improved Blosxom integration, recommended for all earlier beta users
Aaron Swartz's html2text enables reverse translation from HTML to Markdown-formatted plain text, filling a gap users had been asking about
Complete round-trip fidelity between Markdown and HTML is acknowledged as impossible, but html2text comes close enough for practical use
The Markdown ecosystem is growing with third-party tools like HumaneText bringing it to Mac OS X Services and editors like SubEthaEdit and TextEdit
money_quotes: A handful of people have asked if there's a way to translate Markdown in reverse — to turn HTML back into Markdown-formatted plain text.
html2text is a Python script that convers a page of HTML into clean, easy-to-read plain ASCII. Better yet, that ASCII also happens to be valid Markdown.
html2text works so well that I'm planning to use it to convert most of my old Daring Fireball articles (the ones I wrote in raw HTML).
That sort of complete round-trip fidelity simply is not possible, but html2text comes pretty close.
Markdown and html2text are now available from the Mac OS X Services menu, thanks to Gust's HumaneText.
posts.key_insight: The Markdown ecosystem was rapidly maturing beyond Gruber's original tool, with third-party developers building complementary utilities like HTML-to-Markdown conversion and OS-level integration that expanded its reach and utility.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Eric S. Raymond's essay about failing to configure a shared printer on Linux and his subsequent proposals for improving open source usability. Gruber argues that Raymond's framing — that open source just needs minor attitude adjustments to reach 'Aunt Tillie' — fundamentally misunderstands the depth of the problem. The real issue isn't that Linux is almost there; it's that even expert Linux users can't figure out the software, and UI design is an order of magnitude harder than Raymond acknowledges. Gruber contends that the open source model is structurally ill-suited to producing good end-user software because great UI requires sustained work by talented, paid professionals under strong creative direction. He points to the success of commercial software (Mac OS X, Windows) and developer-facing open source tools as evidence that the market has already sorted this out. The essay concludes that desktop Linux is cheap and fast but not good, and you generally get what you pay for.
key_points: Raymond's inability to configure his own printer undermines his framing of usability as an 'Aunt Tillie' problem — the real issue is that even Linux experts struggle with Linux desktop software
UI design is not a finishing touch or attitude adjustment; it's an order of magnitude more work than building the underlying functionality, requiring rare talent and sustained investment
The open source model works well for developer-level tools (Apache, Python, GCC) but structurally works against good end-user software, which requires strong unified direction like filmmaking

The business model matters: selling 'services and support' for software that's supposed to be so intuitive it needs no support is a contradiction, which explains why the best UIs come from commercial software
Unix nerds who care about usability are switching to Mac OS X, draining the pool of talented developers who might otherwise improve desktop Linux
money_quotes: UI development is the hard part. And it's not the last step, it's the first step.
If there's a glib, nutshell synopsis for why Linux desktop software tends to suck, it's this: Raymond and his ilk have no respect for anyone but themselves.
Great software developers don't design for morons. They design for smart, perceptive people — people just like themselves. They have profound respect for their users.
The idea that GUI software needs to be designed for 'dumb users' is completely wrong.
The open source revolution has done nothing to change the fact that the best-designed, most-intuitive user interfaces are found in closed-source commercial software.
Movies are collaborative art, but require strong direction. So it is with end user software.
It's pretty hard to sell 'services and support' for software that fits that bill. The model that actually works is selling the software itself.
There's an old engineering adage: 'Fast, good, cheap: pick two.' Desktop Linux software is cheap and fast, but it's not good.
posts.key_insight: Good UI isn't a coat of paint you apply after building the real software — it's the hardest part of the work, requiring rare talent, strong direction, and sustained investment that the open source model is structurally unable to provide.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier 'Spray-On Usability' piece about desktop Linux, addressing reader complaints and clarifying his arguments. He corrects his Mozilla example after being shown that Camino and Firefox succeeded despite Netscape's corporate backing, not because of it. He reaffirms his core argument that good software must be designed UI-first rather than wrapping GUIs around back-end implementations. He distinguishes between Unix users who care about usability (and are switching to Mac OS X) and those who prioritize tweakability. He argues that desktop Linux can succeed but only with top-down design direction and commercial support, not through community consensus.
key_points: Good UI must be designed first, with implementation built to support it — not the other way around, as traditional Unix development does
Mac OS X uses open source components to implement Apple's designs, not as the basis that dictates the design — an important distinction
Unix users who value usability over tweakability are switching to Mac OS X; those who remain on Linux prioritize configurability and replaceability
Desktop Linux will never produce a great GUI through community consensus — it requires top-down, design-first direction with commercial backing
Linux culture's emphasis on diversity and replaceability is fundamentally incompatible with producing a coherent, unified user experience
money_quotes: Most aspects of Mac OS X are not designed to be configurable or replacable; they are designed to be usable, and to fit in with the design of the rest of the system.
People who want a Unix system that just works, so they can get on with their real work — those are the ones who are switching.
Good UI design will never be forged by community consensus. Bottom-up design won't lead to a coherent and unified whole.
They want to build a GUI on top of an OS. If they want to compete on the desktop, they should be building an OS to power their GUI.
They need to think of what they want the user experience to be, and then design a system that provides that experience. Period.
Trying to create a cohesive GUI system that appeals to these guys is like trying to write music that appeals to the tone deaf.
It's not worth listening to the opinions of assholes; and but once you shut them off, they just get angrier.
posts.key_insight: Great user interfaces require top-down design direction, not bottom-up community consensus — which is why desktop Linux struggles and Mac OS X succeeds despite both using the same underlying Unix foundations.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a new Preferences feature for Daring Fireball, addressing the site's most common complaint: body text that's too small due to pixel-based CSS font sizing that Internet Explorer won't let users resize. The font size switcher offers granular control from 10-16px rather than a simple small/medium/large choice. He also moves referrer listings behind a preference toggle, defaulting to off, as a countermeasure against referrer spammers trying to gain Google PageRank juice from DF's search engine reputation. The post closes with a self-deprecating pointer to an article pitting Gruber against Jakob Nielsen.
key_points: The #1 design complaint about DF was small body text, caused by pixel-based CSS sizing that IE (used by 90-95% of users) wouldn't let users resize
The font switcher uses a single dynamically-assembled PHP stylesheet rather than switching between multiple stylesheets
Referrer spammers exploit sites with public referrer listings to gain PageRank juice from search engines that spider those links
Hiding referrers by default doesn't prevent spam but guarantees spammers gain no SEO benefit from it
money_quotes: However, like any preferences system, over the course of the next few years it will expand to several hundred options.
One popular browser won't let users resize text specified using px; and that browser happens to be used by 90-95 percent of the world.
I don't think these scum bags have any interest or hope that Daring Fireball readers are particularly likely to click a link to 'gay-hitchhiker.top-porn-sites.net' (not made up).
This will have no preventive effect whatsoever to keep the spammers out, but it guarantees they won't benefit from their scum-bag behavior.

Humility almost, but not quite, prevents me from directing your attention to Andrei Herasimchuk's 'Gurus v. Bloggers', wherein yours truly is pitted against Jakob 'Two Wide Columns of Text Are Better Than One' Nielsen.
posts.key_insight: Good web design means working around browser limitations your users are stuck with, and thoughtful defaults can neutralize spam without requiring any new technical defenses.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects the 'MP3Concept Trojan Horse' scare of April 2004, where security firm Intego issued a sensational press release about a proof-of-concept file that could masquerade as an MP3 while actually being a Mac application. He demonstrates that the underlying 'threat' — apps disguised with document icons — has always existed on Mac OS and requires no special technical exploit. Gruber systematically grades each participant in the saga, from the blameless creator of the proof-of-concept to the shameless Intego press release and the credulous media outlets that amplified it without scrutiny. He reserves particular scorn for CNN, Wired's initial report, and Mac news sites that simply regurgitated Intego's inaccurate claims. His core argument is that credibility comes from accuracy, not speed, and that tech journalists failed their readers by not investigating before publishing.
key_points: The MP3Concept 'threat' was a harmless proof-of-concept demonstrating that a single file could be both a Mac app and a valid MP3 — a clever trick but not a novel security vulnerability
Intego's press release was riddled with inaccuracies and exaggerations, essentially manufacturing a scare to sell anti-virus software
The actual issue — that Mac applications can be given icons and filenames resembling documents — has existed since Mac OS 9 and is not specific to OS X
Most Mac news sites failed journalistically by regurgitating Intego's claims without verification, prioritizing speed over accuracy
The real defense is user awareness that files from untrusted sources should be verified as actual documents, not anti-virus software
money_quotes: Imagine if the Greeks had built a gigantic wooden horse, but instead of filling it with army troops, they had simply left a note inside telling the Trojans to be careful.
Computer security is serious business; warnings about newly-discovered threats demand precise and accurate technical descriptions.
Linking is not reporting.
If these sites are news media, as they all claim to be, and not merely clearing houses for press releases, then they are obligated to investigate claims such as Intego's before publishing them.
Credibility stems from accuracy, not immediacy.
Anyone who couldn't smell the fishiness in Intego's press release is unfit to write important Mac OS security stories.
Anyone who did smell the fishiness, but published Intego's claims anyway, should be ashamed.
You don't need anti-virus software to defend against this threat — you simply need to be careful that documents you download from untrusted sources are, in fact, documents.
posts.key_insight: A security company manufactured a scare from a harmless proof-of-concept, and the tech press amplified it uncritically — revealing that credibility requires accuracy, not speed.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the Mac OS X Panther adoption rate among his readers, noting that about 10% held off for months after release. He uses Jeffrey Zeldman's disastrous Panther upgrade experience — which killed his PowerBook over three days — to illustrate why cautious users wait. While acknowledging that bugs are inevitable and Panther works fine for most people, Gruber argues that OS X has a troubling failure mode: when things go wrong, they go deeply wrong in ways that require Unix expertise to fix. He contends that Apple risks alienating the creative professionals who kept the company alive through its hardest years, and that OS X should never require command-line knowledge to recover from a software update.
key_points: About 90% of Gruber's readers had upgraded to Panther by April 2004, but the remaining holdouts were cautious professionals who couldn't risk downtime
Zeldman's Panther upgrade caused cascading failures — broken apps, kernel panics, and an unbootable machine — taking three days to recover
A post-upgrade printing bug caused by missing system users exemplifies how OS X's Unix underpinnings create failure modes that ordinary Mac users can't diagnose
Mac OS 9 failures were more recoverable by non-technical users; OS X failures happen at deeper system levels requiring Unix knowledge
Creative professionals who kept Apple profitable through hard times deserve an upgrade experience that never demands command-line intervention
money_quotes: The fact the Panther upgrade worked perfectly for you, and for most of us, is not proof that Mr. Zeldman must have done something wrong.
Bugs happen. Some will slip through even the tightest QA tests. It has always been the case, and always will be, that every upgrade of your OS ought to be preceded by a full backup.
I no longer remember trouble-shooting tips, I trust Google to remember them for me.
When things go wrong on Mac OS X, they often happen at a deeper level. File permission and ownership problems, for example, are not something a typical Mac user can deal with.
It's not enough that Mac OS X doesn't require any Unix nerdery whatsoever for day-to-day use — it should never require Unix nerdery to recover from software updates, either.
There is a huge 'it just works' advantage that Mac OS 9 holds over OS X, and Zeldman's recent experience exemplifies it.
It's also the case that design professionals tend to purchase Apple's higher-margin, top-of-the-line hardware. They're not just loyal Apple customers, they're profitable Apple customers.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X should never require Unix expertise to recover from a failed software update, because the creative professionals who depend on it — and who kept Apple alive — deserve better than that.

posts.summary: Gruber shares a curated collection of links covering Mac software and history. Highlights include a Rich Siegel interview from Mac Developer Journal, a BBEdit update with scripting improvements, Andy Hertzfeld's Folklore story about Switcher and Bill Gates, a John Calhoun interview about the classic game Glider, and Jim Heid's detailed iTunes 4.5 coverage. The post is a straightforward link roundup with brief editorial commentary on each item.
key_points: BBEdit 7.1.4 adds scriptable search and replacement strings, which Gruber highlights as the best new feature
Andy Hertzfeld's Folklore site documents early Mac history, including how he negotiated Switcher rights directly with Bill Gates
The post takes a dig at Zinio's digital magazine format while sharing a workaround PDF of a paywalled interview
Gruber curates links from the tight-knit Mac developer and blogger community, crediting sources like Michael Tsai
money_quotes: Switcher was so cool, and the Mac so important to Microsoft, that Hertzfeld negotiated with Bill Gates one-on-one for the rights. And, of course, Gates tried to screw him.
If you have any sense at all, you're obsessively reading each new article as it's published.
Just a Bunch of Links, but They're Good Links, I Swear
posts.key_insight: A link roundup that reflects Gruber's role as a curator of the Mac community, connecting readers to the people, tools, and history that define the platform.

posts.summary: Gruber documents a frustrating troubleshooting journey where his default FTP client preference became corrupted and couldn't be reset to Interarchy. After systematically eliminating the application itself, its preferences, and the Internet Config plist as suspects, he discovered the problem lay in corrupted Launch Services cache files. The fix involved deleting the Launch Services cache from /Library/Caches/ and restarting. Along the way, he provides a detailed history of Internet Config on the Mac, from its third-party origins through Apple's adoption and eventual deprecation of the UI in Panther.
key_points: Internet Config, originally created by Quinn 'The Eskimo!' and Peter N Lewis, was adopted by Apple but had its System Preferences UI removed in Panther, fragmenting helper app settings across individual applications
Systematic troubleshooting eliminated the app, its preferences, and the Internet Config plist before identifying corrupted Launch Services cache files as the culprit
Launch Services and Internet Config are deeply connected — on Mac OS X, Internet Config calls through to Launch Services under the hood
Apple's removal of the Internet preferences panel created a messy situation where users must open Safari to set Firefox as their default browser
money_quotes: It is a bit weird to have to open Safari to tell the system that you'd rather use FireFox.
You know you're in deep shit when Google doesn't have the answer to a troubleshooting issue.

Internet Config wasn't invented by Apple. It was designed, implemented, and released for free by Quinn "The Eskimo!" and Peter N Lewis. It was useful, popular, and widely supported by the developers of Internet client software for the Mac. Thus, Apple adopted it and rolled into the system.

IE not only provides a UI to modify these system-wide Internet Config settings, but it also stores its own IE-specific private preferences inside the Internet Config preferences. This always struck me as a very odd decision on the part of IE's engineers.
Now that the Internet System Prefs panel is gone, it's up to individual applications to provide a way to set default protocol handlers.
posts.key_insight: When Mac OS X helper app preferences become inexplicably corrupted, the problem often lies not in the visible preference files but in the underlying Launch Services cache that Internet Config calls through to.

posts.summary: Gruber documents his methodical debugging of a bizarre Mac OS X bug where the Energy Saver preferences panel refused to load, accompanied by system freezes and wake-from-sleep failures on his iBook. Through a process of elimination involving user account isolation, preference file bisection, and finally discovering hidden dot files that the Finder couldn't show him, he traced the problem to a corrupted .GlobalPreferences.plist file. After the problem recurred, he dug deeper and isolated the specific culprit: a custom time format setting in the International preferences panel that omitted the 'M' from AM/PM. The bug is fully reproducible — any custom time format using just 'A' or 'P' instead of 'AM' or 'PM' prevents Energy Saver from loading. He publishes the fix primarily so Google indexes it for others with the same problem.
key_points: A custom time format removing the 'M' from AM/PM in International preferences caused Energy Saver to fail to load — a completely unrelated subsystem
The Finder's inability to show hidden dot files led Gruber astray during his binary search through preference files, until he switched to Terminal
Gruber's debugging methodology was systematic: isolate to user account, then preference folder, then individual files
The post doubles as a deliberate SEO strategy — publishing the fix so Google can serve it as a troubleshooting resource
money_quotes: Googling not only didn't turn up an answer, it didn't even turn up other instances of the problem.
That's obviously just placeholder text, which is supposed to be set to the actual status.
Silly me, trying to do this from the Finder.
An invocation of 'ls -AF' turned up a handful of hidden dot files. I.e. files with names that start with a dot, the Unix man's way of turning files 'invisible'.
Yes, yes — I'll file a proper report with Apple about this. But first things first — blog it so it gets into Google, the world's best troubleshooting database.
posts.key_insight: A custom time format in Mac OS X's International preferences that drops the 'M' from AM/PM inexplicably prevents the Energy Saver panel from loading — a perfect example of how seemingly unrelated system preferences can interact in catastrophic and opaque ways.

posts.summary: Gruber explains that he sometimes writes articles not for his regular Daring Fireball audience, but specifically for search engine discoverability. After publishing a Mac OS X troubleshooting article, it became the top Google result within five days, helping strangers solve their problems. He offers practical tips for writing search-friendly content: use descriptive titles matching likely search queries, and front-load factual details in the opening paragraphs. He acknowledges that Google's indexer might be smart enough to surface the right results regardless, but argues the approach is worth trying.
key_points: Gruber distinguishes between writing for his regular audience and writing for search engine discovery, treating Google as 'the world's best troubleshooting database'
His troubleshooting article reached the #1 Google result within five days, validating the approach
Practical SEO advice: use clear, query-matching titles and front-load factual content in early paragraphs
The real payoff is helping strangers who find the article through search — one reader was saved from a needless OS reinstall
money_quotes: Blog it so it gets into Google, the world's best troubleshooting database.
But it's only funny because it's true.
They were written for Google. Or, more specifically, for people using search engines to find an answer to the same problems I had.
If you happened to enjoy them anyway, well, that's just gravy.
Don't get cute with the title.
The point is, get to the point quickly.
It's possible that Google's indexer is so smart that it would still provide the same search results, even if I had titled the article 'The .GlobalPreferences.plist File Can Bite Me'.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable blog posts aren't always written for your regular audience — sometimes you write for the stranger desperately searching Google for the exact problem you just solved.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Six Apart's botched Movable Type 3.0 launch, which angered even loyal paying users. He identifies two critical mistakes: releasing a paid upgrade with almost no new features, and pre-announcing it as a 'free upgrade' months earlier only to reverse course on pricing. He argues that people pay for features, not engine improvements, and that pre-announcements almost always lead to disappointment. While acknowledging MT remains good software, he warns that the PR damage threatens Six Apart's credibility, which is essential for any commercial software company.
key_points: Paid upgrades must deliver new features — users won't pay for engine improvements and bug fixes alone, no matter how valuable they are technically
Six Apart's own co-founder admitted MT 3.0 was not a feature release, yet they still tried to charge for it
Pre-announcing MT 3.0 as a 'free upgrade' then launching with restrictive paid licensing destroyed user trust
The best policy is to never announce ship dates, features, or pricing until software is ready to ship
With commercial software, users put their faith in the company — unlike open source where they can fork — so credibility is existential
money_quotes: That's not to say, however, that Six Apart didn't fuck this up, big time.
Everyone loves bug fixes. Everyone loves performance improvements. But what people will pay for are features.
Microsoft is sitting on $50 billion in cash on the basis of this premise.
You can certainly argue that the world would be a better place — or at least filled with better software — if people were willing to pay for non-feature release software. But they're not.
"Under-promise and over-deliver" is good advice for developing a loyal user base. The best way to achieve this, however, is not to promise anything at all.
A company that announces software after it is ready to ship will never be wrong.
The more time you spend talking about future versions of your software, the worse it makes the current version look.
With commercial software, users put their faith in the company behind the product.
posts.key_insight: Charging for upgrades without new features and breaking a public promise of free pricing is a credibility-destroying combination for a commercial software company.

posts.summary: Gruber notes the release of Movable Type 3.0's developer edition by Six Apart and confirms that both of his MT plug-ins, SmartyPants and Markdown, are fully compatible with the new version. This is a brief, informational announcement aimed at users of his tools.
key_points: Six Apart released a developer edition of Movable Type 3.0
Both SmartyPants and Markdown plug-ins are confirmed compatible with MT 3.0
The post assumes widespread awareness of the MT 3.0 release among his readership
money_quotes: In the incredibly unlikely case that you haven't heard, Six Apart has released a "developer edition" of Movable Type 3.0.
posts.key_insight: A brief compatibility notice confirming Gruber's Markdown and SmartyPants plug-ins work with the newly released Movable Type 3.0.

posts.summary: Gruber alerts readers to a serious Mac OS X security vulnerability involving custom URI protocol handlers that could allow malicious websites to mount disk images and execute scripts on users' systems. He recommends downloading RCDefaultApp, a free System Preferences panel, to disable the dangerous 'help', 'disk', and 'disks' protocols. He explains why RCDefaultApp is superior to alternatives like MisFox and More Internet, noting that it shows all protocol handlers registered through Launch Services, not just those visible via the legacy Internet Config system. The post serves as both a practical security guide and a technical explanation of the underlying architecture.
key_points: A real exploit chain exists where malicious web pages can mount disk images via 'disk' protocol and then execute scripts via 'help' protocol in Help Viewer
RCDefaultApp is the recommended fix because it exposes all URI protocols registered through Launch Services, while alternatives only show legacy Internet Config registrations
The 'disk' and 'disks' protocols are invisible in MisFox and More Internet because they're registered directly in Launch Services, not through Internet Config
Safari's 'Open safe files after downloading' setting should be disabled as an additional protective measure
The Help Viewer script execution feature was designed for interactive help books, but Gruber is unaware of any help books that actually use it
money_quotes: This is for real, and potentially nasty.
I have yet to see any reports of the exploit actually being used maliciously, but it's worth protecting against.
Disabling the 'help' URI protocol's connection to Help Viewer will not affect your normal use of the Help Viewer application.
RCDefaultApp shows all the protocol handlers registered on your system; MisFox and More Internet only display the protocols that are registered through Internet Config.
This is a more elegant solution than assigning these protocols to dummy applications, such as Mac OS X's Chess game.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's layered URI protocol architecture created a security blind spot where dangerous handlers registered directly in Launch Services were invisible to most management tools, making RCDefaultApp uniquely capable of closing the vulnerability.

posts.summary: Gruber documents a serious security vulnerability in Mac OS X's default handling of 'telnet://' URIs, where the system passes URI content directly as arguments to the telnet shell command. By exploiting telnet's '-n' switch (which specifies a log file), an attacker can craft URIs that create or overwrite arbitrary files in a user's home folder. Using URL-encoded directory separators, the exploit can reach files outside the home folder's root level, including critical preference files. Gruber recommends disabling the telnet protocol handler using RCDefaultApp and notes that the ssh protocol does not share this vulnerability due to different command-line switches.
key_points: Mac OS X's telnet URI handler passes URI content directly as shell command arguments, including option switches like '-n' for specifying log files
The exploit can overwrite any file the user has write privileges to, which includes nearly everything in the home folder
URL-encoding directory separators allows the attack to target files outside the home folder root, such as Finder preferences
The ssh protocol is not vulnerable to an analogous attack because its command-line switches differ entirely from telnet's
Gruber recommends using RCDefaultApp to disable the telnet protocol handler entirely
money_quotes: The problem with Mac OS X's default handling for 'telnet://' URIs is that it treats whatever follows the slashes as an argument to the telnet shell command.
This file is empty, and it isn't executed, but the fact that it will overwrite an existing file with the same name is some serious shit.
It will not overwrite folders, and it will not overwrite files to which you don't have write privileges, thus it can't be used to overwrite essential system components.
But it can be used to overwrite any file on your computer to which you do have write privileges, which includes pretty much any file within your home folder.
Now we get nasty; this one will overwrite your Finder preferences file.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's naive passing of URI content as shell command arguments turns the telnet protocol handler into a file-overwriting exploit that can destroy any user-writable file on the system.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's Security Update 2004-05-24, which fixed the Help Viewer 'help:runscript' URI vulnerability by preventing script execution from external applications. He walks through how the exploit chain works — remote servers mounting disk images via 'disk' URIs, registering custom URI schemes, and launching malicious applications — and explains that disabling dangerous URI protocols via RCDefaultApp is sufficient protection. He argues against using Unsanity's Paranoid Android haxie, which injects code into every running application to intercept URI calls, contending that every vulnerability it addresses can be closed through supported configuration changes. He directly challenges Unsanity's claim that 'only Paranoid Android provides protection,' demonstrating that disabling 'disk', 'disks', and 'afp' protocols and reassigning 'ftp' away from the Finder closes all known attack vectors.
key_points: Apple's security update surgically fixed Help Viewer to block 'help:runscript' URIs from external apps while preserving safe 'help:search' functionality
The generalized vulnerability chain involves remotely mounting volumes, having the Finder register custom URI schemes from malicious apps, then launching those apps via the newly-registered schemes
Disabling 'disk', 'disks', and 'afp' URI protocols and reassigning 'ftp' away from the Finder closes all known exploit vectors without third-party software
Unsanity's claim that 'only Paranoid Android provides protection' is factually incorrect — supported configuration changes accomplish the same thing
Paranoid Android works by injecting code into every running application via unsupported mechanisms, creating potential for conflicts that simple Launch Services configuration changes avoid
money_quotes: Apple has prevented precisely what was dangerous, and nothing else.
It is simply not true that 'only Paranoid Android provides protection from it.'
Why run a haxie if you can close these vulnerabilities without one?

RCDefaultApp merely modifies your Launch Services settings, and should therefore conflict with nothing. Paranoid Android uses completely unsupported mechanisms to inject code into every running application, and therefore has the potential to conflict with anything.
If there is a demonstrable exploit that cannot be disabled by supported means, I'd like to see it.
At this point, the user's goose is potentially cooked — once the application is launched, it has free reign.
The key to prevention is to nip it at step #1, and prevent remote servers from automatically mounting volumes in your file system.
posts.key_insight: Surgical, supported configuration changes that disable dangerous URI protocols are preferable to injecting unsupported code into every application, because the simplest fix that closes the vulnerability is always the right one.

posts.summary: Gruber provides a consolidated, step-by-step guide for Mac OS X users to close all known URI-related security vulnerabilities that surfaced in mid-2004. The post walks through installing Security Update 2004-05-24, upgrading to 10.3.4 (Panther) or applying the equivalent Jaguar fix, and installing Security Update 2004-06-07. He notes the document was revised after Apple's June update closed the remaining holes. He also recommends keeping Safari's 'Open safe files after downloading' preference disabled as a precaution, and includes an FAQ addressing earlier workarounds like RCDefaultApp protocol disabling.
key_points: Three specific steps—two security updates and an OS point release—close all known URI-related Mac OS X vulnerabilities
Jaguar users must upgrade to 10.2.8 to receive any security patches; all earlier versions remain permanently vulnerable
Gruber recommends keeping Safari's 'Open safe files after downloading' disabled, arguing no downloaded file should be considered automatically safe
Apple's June 2004 update removed the 'disk' and 'disks' URI protocols entirely from Launch Services, eliminating the attack vector rather than just patching it
money_quotes: I think it's unwise to think there exists such a thing as a 'safe file' that can be opened automatically after downloading it.
This page is intended to serve as a consolidated, comprehensive, and to-the-point list of instructions for closing all known URI-related vulnerabilities affecting Mac OS X.
If new information or exploits are identified, I plan to revise this document in-place.
All previous versions of Jaguar, 10.2.0 - 10.2.7, will remain vulnerable to these exploits.
Security Update 2004-06-07 removes the 'disk' and 'disks' protocols from your Launch Services database.
posts.key_insight: No file downloaded from the internet should be considered automatically safe to open, and users should apply all available security updates while maintaining a cautious default posture.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that while Mac OS X's recently publicized URI-related security vulnerabilities are serious, the real problem is Apple's response to them, not the vulnerabilities themselves. He distinguishes between Mac OS X's solid security design/implementation and Apple's poor response/communication practices, noting that Apple sat on a privately reported Help Viewer vulnerability for three months before acting. He criticizes Apple's marketing-driven approach to security communication, including Phil Schiller's misleading press statement and the company's deliberately vague security update documentation. Gruber contends that Apple's spin-control approach is backfiring, inviting press criticism that conflates Apple's communication failures with Mac OS X's actual security posture, and that simple honesty would have avoided the negative coverage entirely.
key_points: The URI-related vulnerabilities are serious but remain unexploited; the distinction between vulnerability and exploit matters.
Apple's Design/Implementation of Mac OS X security scores well, but its Response/Communication is abysmal — treating security as a marketing problem rather than a technical one.
Apple ignored a privately reported Help Viewer vulnerability for three months, only acting after it became public, undermining the responsible disclosure process.
Apple's deliberately vague security documentation prevents administrators from making informed decisions about which updates to prioritize.

Apple's spin-control approach to security is backfiring — press criticism of Apple's communication gets conflated with criticism of Mac OS X itself, damaging the platform's reputation more than honesty would.
money_quotes: The problem is that Apple has been revealed as a company that treats security vulnerabilities as marketing problems, rather than as technical problems.
"Sweep them under the rug" is not a serious security policy, and the IT press is taking notice.
This vulnerability was "theoretical" in the same sense that gravity is theoretical.
Saying Apple "takes security very seriously" is meaningless. Judging by Apple's actions, they do not.
Just because the vulnerability is critical doesn't change the amount of time it takes to put together a good and properly-tested solution.
Trust but verify is good advice; Apple's euphemistic approach to documenting security updates makes verification difficult at best, and in some cases, impossible.
Apple does not need to spin security issues as they appear; they simply need to address them head-on, with plain language and the straight truth.
The entire negative slant to McCarthy's article could have been avoided if Apple had simply stated the straight truth.
posts.key_insight: Apple's attempt to spin security vulnerabilities as marketing problems rather than addressing them with transparency is not only dishonest but counterproductive, generating worse press coverage than the vulnerabilities themselves would have.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that the massive security disparity between Windows and Mac isn't primarily about technical vulnerabilities or market share — it's about community tolerance. Windows has become a 'bad neighborhood' where users accept crapware as inevitable, creating a permissive environment that invites ever-worse exploitation. The Mac community, by contrast, maintains zero tolerance for any form of malicious software, which functions like the 'broken windows' policing theory: maintaining order prevents escalation. He concedes the market share argument purely for rhetorical purposes, then turns it around — if smaller share means fewer exploits, the Mac is 'doomed' to be more secure forever. The piece dismantles Windows apologist arguments while celebrating the Mac community's intolerance of compromise.
key_points: Windows crapware is epidemic (28 instances per PC scanned) while Mac crapware is effectively nonexistent — even expert Windows users like Paul Thurrott get defeated by malware
Mac OS X's simpler architecture offers fewer hiding places for malicious software compared to Windows' opaque Registry and services system
The Mac community's zero tolerance for vulnerabilities and crapware functions like the 'broken windows' policing theory — refusing to tolerate small problems prevents larger ones
The market share argument for Windows' insecurity is irrefutable but self-defeating: if true, the Mac is permanently 'doomed' to superior security
A fair Mac-vs-Windows comparison should be made after six months of real-world use, not under factory-fresh conditions
money_quotes: Crapware is a problem of epidemic proportions on Windows, but it is almost completely non-existent on the Mac.
We all benefit from the fact that the Mac community has zero tolerance for vulnerabilities. Not just zero tolerance for security exploits, but zero tolerance for vulnerabilities.
Zero tolerance, on the part of the user community, is the only policy that can work.
Windows is like a bad neighborhood, strewn with litter, mysterious odors, panhandlers, and untold dozens of petty annoyances.
You don't need bars on your windows in a good neighborhood; you don't need anti-virus software on the Mac.
The security disparity between the Mac and Windows isn't so much about technical possibilities as it is about what people will tolerate.
And Mac users don't tolerate shit.
Isn't it thus only logical to conclude that the Mac is forever 'doomed' to be significantly more secure than Windows?
posts.key_insight: Security is ultimately a cultural problem, not a technical one — the Mac's freedom from malware stems from a community that refuses to tolerate any compromise, applying the 'broken windows' principle to software.

posts.summary: Gruber reports on Apple's Security Update 2004-06-07, which closes all the URI/Launch Services vulnerabilities that had been publicized over the previous month. He praises the update for being well-documented, noting it meets the standard he called for in his earlier piece 'Security Cannot Be Spun.' The fix works by presenting a confirmation dialog when Launch Services tries to automatically launch an app the user has never manually opened. Gruber updates his own vulnerability instructions and notes that while the known holes are patched, he still recommends leaving Safari's 'Open safe files' preference disabled as a precaution.
key_points: Apple's security update closes every known URI/Launch Services vulnerability from the preceding month
The release notes are praised as genuinely well-documented, a marked improvement over Apple's usual security communication
Apple's solution adds a confirmation dialog for auto-launching unknown apps, fixing the vulnerability without removing functionality
The 'disk' and 'disks' URI protocols have been completely removed rather than merely patched
Gruber recommends keeping Safari's 'Open safe files' preference off despite the fixes, arguing that no automatically opened file should be considered truly safe
money_quotes: It's not just better-documented than usual, it's just flat-out well-documented.
This solves the vulnerability where unknown apps could be launched automatically, and does so without removing any functionality.
I think one of the lessons of this saga has been that it's unwise to think there exists such a thing as a 'safe file' that can be opened automatically after it's been downloaded.
In the spirit of 'better safe than sorry', I'm leaving this preference off.
Documentation like this is exactly what I wished for in 'Security Cannot Be Spun'.
posts.key_insight: Apple delivered a well-executed, well-documented security fix that closed every known vulnerability without sacrificing functionality, though the episode proved that trusting any file type as automatically 'safe' is fundamentally unwise.

posts.summary: Gruber debunks a Register article claiming Apple's Security Update 2004-06-07 failed to block URI-based exploits. He explains that the update works exactly as intended — it only prompts for confirmation when Launch Services encounters never-before-launched applications. The Register's test failed because they had previously run the exploit apps, which were already trusted by Launch Services. Gruber verified this by deleting Launch Services cache files and restarting, after which the exploits were properly blocked. He also corrects the article's characterization of Paranoid Android and notes that even Unsanity considers their tool obsoleted by the update.
key_points: The Register's Tony Smith incorrectly reported that Apple's security update failed to block known exploits, without investigating why his test produced that result
Launch Services tracks previously-launched applications and implicitly trusts them — the security update only prompts for never-before-launched apps
Security Update 2004-06-07 makes a surgically precise change to Launch Services, unlike Paranoid Android's sweeping approach of blocking all non-standard URI protocols
Even Unsanity, the creators of Paranoid Android, acknowledged that Apple's update properly addresses the vulnerability their tool was designed to fix
The Register's recommendation to keep Paranoid Android alongside the update contradicts Unsanity's own guidance
money_quotes: Never one to let actual research or reporting get in the way of a good story, The Register's Tony Smith published an article about yesterday's security update.
To be clear: Security Update 2004-06-07 blocks these example exploits in exactly the way it is supposed to.

Security Update 2004-06-07, on the other hand, makes a surgically precise modification to the previous (vulnerable) behavior of Launch Services: it only kicks in when Launch Services has been asked to automatically launch a never-before-launched application.
Sorry for all the wavering vagueness here in this parenthetical; suffice it to say that Launch Services is more or less a black box.
Of course, Unsanity doesn't tell you how to uninstall Paranoid Android.
But you shouldn't have to worry about that, because you didn't install Paranoid Android.
posts.key_insight: The Register's flawed testing methodology — running exploit apps before installing the update — led to an incorrect report that Apple's security fix was broken, when in fact it worked exactly as designed by only flagging never-before-launched applications.

posts.summary: Gruber challenges the conventional wisdom that Windows's overwhelming market share alone explains why it suffers virtually all security exploits while Mac OS X suffers almost none. He acknowledges market share is a factor but argues the disproportionality is too extreme to be explained by share alone. He cites the Witty worm, which devastated only 12,000 Windows machines running specific firewall software — a population far smaller than Mac OS X's user base — as evidence that attackers target vulnerabilities regardless of population size. He concludes Mac OS X's security record likely stems from superior engineering, cultural factors, and some luck.
key_points: The conventional wisdom that monopoly market share naturally attracts a disproportionate share of exploits has some truth, but doesn't fully explain Windows's near-total dominance in security incidents
The Witty worm infected all 12,000 vulnerable machines worldwide in 45 minutes, proving attackers will target populations far smaller than the Mac user base when vulnerabilities exist
Classic Mac OS suffered serious viruses despite never having double-digit market share, undermining the pure market-share argument for Mac OS X's immunity
Mac OS X's security record likely comes from a combination of superior engineering, cultural factors, and luck — in roughly that order
Google Zeitgeist data from April 2004 shows Windows at 92% and Mac at 4%, providing concrete market share numbers for context
money_quotes: The question I'm interested in is whether this also explains why security exploits against Mac OS X are practically non-existent.
I don't believe that this explains the extraordinarily disproportionate share of security exploits that Windows suffers.
Twelve thousand machines was the entire vulnerable and exposed population, and Witty infected them all — worldwide — in 45 minutes.
Apple once had 16+% market share, but that was back in the Apple II era; to my knowledge, the Mac has never had double-digit market share.
Despite the fact that Mac OS X is relatively secure, Mac OS X users should not grow complacent. Witty devastated a target population vastly smaller than the overall Mac OS X population.
There are factors other than market share that have led to the remarkable paucity of security exploits on Mac OS X.
posts.key_insight: The Witty worm's devastation of just 12,000 machines proves that attackers will target small populations when vulnerabilities exist, undermining the argument that market share alone explains Mac OS X's near-absence of security exploits.

posts.summary: Gruber announces Daring Fireball's first fundraising drive, asking readers to buy t-shirts or donate for memberships. He explains that despite two years of writing and 200,000+ published words, the site generates only hobbyist-level income that doesn't justify the time invested. He frames the appeal around what deeper funding would unlock: more software reviews, developer interviews, and the depth that distinguishes Daring Fireball from typical weblogs. The piece is remarkably vulnerable for Gruber, stepping out from behind the curtain to make a direct, personal appeal while insisting the site will remain free regardless of the outcome.
key_points: Daring Fireball has been running at a loss due to the opportunity cost of Gruber's time, with AdSense revenue covering hosting but not his labor
The membership model offers a full-content RSS feed as a perk, but Gruber frames it as supporting his work rather than buying a product
Success would mean more in-depth software reviews and developer interviews — the kind of long-form pieces that take days or weeks but distinguish the site
Gruber draws an analogy between independent journalism and independent software development, arguing readers should think of donations like shareware registration fees
He explicitly rejects the equation of journalism with objectivity, arguing that subjectivity done honestly is more engaging than corporate neutrality
money_quotes: I want Daring Fireball to be my real work. No air-quotes.
I am trying to write the shit out of this stuff.
Don't conflate "journalism" with "objectivity". Striving for objectivity — taking no sides, casting no opinions — has taken all the life out of modern corporate journalism.
No matter how lovely the ones and zeroes are, they're never as interesting as people.
No stagecraft. No artifice. Just me, your humble author, hat in hand, heart in throat, asking for your support.
I tend only to play games which I'm already good at, and only to undertake endeavors which I feel certain will be successful.
When I'm grinding away on Daring Fireball, am I working, or am I "working"? Am I starting a career as a writer, or am I just dicking around?
Daring Fireball is unabashedly subjective, but, I hope, never at the expense of accuracy or genuine insight.
posts.key_insight: Gruber makes the case that independent, opinionated web journalism is worth paying for — not because readers owe him anything, but because depth and personality require time that only direct reader support can sustain.

posts.summary: Gruber provides a FAQ-style update on the Daring Fireball membership and T-shirt funding drive. He addresses international shipping concerns, confirms worldwide flat-rate pricing, and announces an alternative payment option via eSellerate for readers who had trouble with PayPal. He shares T-shirt specifications and playfully addresses reader questions about cat pictures prompted by his earlier funding drive announcement. The post reflects the early days of Gruber attempting to turn Daring Fireball from a hobby into a sustainable endeavor.
key_points: International readers can order T-shirts at the flat price of $30.72 USD with no extra shipping required, though some generous readers voluntarily donated extra
PayPal issues prompted Gruber to add eSellerate as an alternative payment processor, while still praising PayPal's low fees and API capabilities
The funding drive is showing promise but hasn't yet reached the level needed to make Daring Fireball a full-time pursuit
Gruber humorously deflects reader requests for cat and baby pictures while acknowledging posting his son's photo would be 'shameless manipulation'
money_quotes: Definitely something, but also definitely not yet capital-S Something.
Your support during this drive can make this a better site. It's that simple.
Such friendliness and generosity is remarkable.
I certainly don't want anyone to miss out on all the membership fun simply because they can't/won't/would prefer not to use PayPal.
They really do operate on a 'make it up on volume' business model.
Unfortunately, as the below example illustrates, posting a photo of the boy would leave me open to accusations of shameless manipulation.
posts.key_insight: This early membership FAQ captures the practical and emotional texture of an independent writer trying to turn a passion project into a livelihood, navigating payment logistics and reader goodwill with characteristic Gruber wit.

posts.summary: Gruber admits he was wrong in the 1990s when he dismissed the web's potential as an application platform, believing it could never match the quality of desktop apps. He now recognizes that web apps succeeded not by beating desktop apps on quality, but by offering zero installation, universal access, and effortless deployment. Drawing on Joel Spolsky's essay about Microsoft losing the API war, Gruber argues that web apps are gradually eroding Microsoft's Win32 monopoly by letting companies switch one app at a time. The deepest irony is that Microsoft destroyed Netscape to prevent the web from threatening Windows, yet the web itself — not Netscape — was always the real threat, and winning the browser war did nothing to stop it.
key_points: Web apps won not by matching desktop app quality but by offering zero installation, universal access, and effortless deployment — an entirely different set of terms.
Most people simply don't care about UI polish or rich feature sets; they want software that works and is easy to access.
Web apps erode Microsoft's Win32 monopoly gradually because companies can switch one app at a time without abandoning Windows entirely.
Microsoft's destruction of Netscape was strategically misdirected: the threat to Windows was the web itself, not any single browser company.
Gruber candidly admits his own past wrongness, comparing his dismissal of web apps to a theater critic in the 1950s dismissing television.
money_quotes: The location field is the new command line.
Most people simply don't care about the quality of an app's user experience. Not at all. They just want it to work, and to be "easy".
My saying that web apps would never become popular was like a theater critic in the early 1950s dismissing television.
Web apps don't need to beat desktop apps on the same terms. What's happened is that they're beating them on an entirely different set of terms.
It wasn't Netscape that was a threat to Windows as an application platform, it was the web itself.
They spent all that time, money, and development effort on IE, building a browser monopoly and crushing Netscape — but to what avail?
They're losing this war despite the fact that they won the browser war. Winning the browser war — destroying Netscape — was supposed to prevent there ever even being an API war.
It's easier for corporations to migrate to web apps than it is for them to stay Windows-only.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft won the browser war but lost the API war because the real threat to Windows was never Netscape — it was the web itself as an application platform, which succeeded by competing on entirely different terms than desktop software.

posts.summary: Gruber announces giveaways to promote the Daring Fireball membership drive. Brent Simmons has donated a full NetNewsWire license, and Gruber has Gmail invitations to distribute. The NetNewsWire license will go to a random paying member after the drive ends on July 1, while Gmail invitations are available to anyone who has donated any amount. The post serves as a straightforward promotional push to encourage donations during the membership drive.
key_points: Brent Simmons donated a NetNewsWire 1.x license that will also cover the upcoming free 2.0 update
Gmail invitations were still a scarce, desirable commodity worth using as giveaway incentives
The giveaway structure creates two tiers: full members eligible for the software license, any-amount donors eligible for Gmail invites
Community support from other Mac bloggers helped boost the Gmail invitation pool from 2 to 25
money_quotes: Brent Simmons has generously offered a full license to the award-winning NetNewsWire 1.x for my use in promoting the Daring Fireball membership drive.
I've got a couple of Gmail invitations burning a hole in my pocket.
The NetNewsWire license will be awarded July 1 to one random member after the membership drive is over.
If you've already joined, you're already entered.
I now have a staggering 25 Gmail invitations to give away.
posts.key_insight: In 2004, a Gmail invitation and a $25 RSS reader license were valuable enough incentives to drive a blog membership campaign, reflecting the scarcity economics of the early web 2.0 era.

posts.summary: Gruber announces an expanded set of prizes for the Daring Fireball membership drive, listing software donations from numerous Mac developers including Ranchero, Bare Bones, The Iconfactory, Rogue Amoeba, and others. The total retail value exceeds $1,600. He extends the membership drive through July 2 and explains the random selection process using a Perl script. Members can optionally rank their prize preferences via email.
key_points: The membership drive prizes grew from a single NetNewsWire donation to over $1,600 worth of Mac software from numerous indie developers
The prize list reads as a who's-who of mid-2000s Mac indie software: BBEdit, NetNewsWire, Path Finder, Audio Hijack Pro, VoodooPad, and more
The contest uses a simple, transparent random selection via Perl script, with an optional preference-ranking system
The breadth of donations reflects the tight-knit Mac developer community rallying around Gruber's independent writing
money_quotes: The retail value of all the above software is a little over $1,300 $1,600 (!).
Winners will be selected randomly, using a simple Perl script.
Each member can only win once. I.e. if there are 21 prizes, there will be 21 winners.
posts.key_insight: The generous response from Mac indie developers to Gruber's membership drive illustrates the strong community bonds that characterized the mid-2000s Mac software ecosystem.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a new feature for Daring Fireball: the Linked List, a daily collection of interesting links with brief commentary. The key innovation is its distribution model — the RSS feed is exclusively available to paying Daring Fireball members, while web archives are free but delayed by one day. He positions it as a members-only perk rather than a punishment for non-members. The launch is timed to coincide with Apple's WWDC announcements.
key_points: The Linked List is a new linkblog format — frequent but not frenetic short-form link commentary alongside Daring Fireball's longer essays
The RSS feed is members-only, establishing an early model of paid content gating tied to RSS rather than paywalled web pages
Web archives are free but delayed by one day, creating a timeliness incentive to subscribe rather than a hard paywall
Gruber frames the restriction as an added benefit for members rather than a restriction on non-members — a deliberate positioning choice
money_quotes: Call it a "link log", or "linkblog", or just "a good way to dick around on the Internet for a few minutes a day".
The one-day delay isn't intended to be punitive toward non-members; the idea is more that the Linked List is intended as a nice members-only extra.
posts.key_insight: Gruber pioneered the linkblog format and an early model of indie content monetization by making timeliness — not access — the premium feature.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's Dashboard is not a rip-off of Konfabulator, tracing the concept of mini-applets back to the original 1984 Macintosh desk accessories. He demonstrates that Dashboard and Konfabulator are fundamentally different under the hood: Dashboard is built on WebCore (HTML/CSS/JavaScript via WebKit) and Exposé, while Konfabulator uses a custom XML format with an embedded SpiderMonkey engine. He contends that Dashboard's web-standards approach makes gadget development accessible to vastly more people, and that Apple had clear technical reasons to build their own solution rather than acquire Konfabulator. He dismisses the notion that Apple owes compensation for ideas that weren't original to Konfabulator in the first place.
key_points: The concept of mini-applets dates back to 1984 Mac desk accessories, not Konfabulator — Apple isn't stealing an original idea
Dashboard is built on WebKit (HTML/CSS/JS) and Exposé, making it architecturally completely different from Konfabulator's custom XML and SpiderMonkey engine
Dashboard's web-standards foundation makes gadget development accessible to hundreds of thousands of web developers, unlike Konfabulator's closed platform
Konfabulator widgets each run as separate processes with heavy memory consumption, while Dashboard leverages existing system technologies
Apple cannot be expected to leave holes in its OS unfilled just because a third-party developer got there first
money_quotes: Dashboard is not a rip-off of Konfabulator. Yes, they are doing very much the same thing. But what it is that they're doing was not an original idea to Konfabulator.
The answer to the question What do they do? is the same. But the answer to the question How are they doing it? is completely different.
Do you believe reasonable engineering opinions can be drawn by looking at screenshots?

There are only a handful of people in the world who could write a puzzle game in the form of a Macintosh device driver using just 600 bytes of 68000 assembler; there are hundreds of thousands who can write one using HTML/CSS/JavaScript.
Apple may hire you to work for them. They may buy your code if they want it. But they will not pay you off just for an idea, nor should they be expected to.
When a utility is designed to compensate for a hole in Mac OS X, the developer should not expect the hole to remain unfilled by Apple forever.

In the lean years between John Sculley's exit and Jobs's return, Apple indeed seemed to follow this dictum of not stepping on Mac developers' toes. And you know what we got? Years of stagnation with System 7.5.
There aren't that many brand-spanking new ideas under the sun, and Konfabulator isn't one.
posts.key_insight: Apple built Dashboard on fundamentally different and superior technology than Konfabulator, and the concept of mini-applets was never Konfabulator's to own — it traces back to the original Mac desk accessories of 1984.

posts.summary: Gruber makes his case for direct reader support of Daring Fireball as the membership and T-shirt drive reaches its deadline. He explains why advertising-based revenue would compromise the site's quality, arguing that ads incentivize quantity over depth. He acknowledges the Catch-22 of needing revenue to build features while readers want features before paying. The post is both a fundraising pitch and a manifesto for independent, reader-supported journalism that prioritizes nuance and detail over mass appeal.
key_points: Advertising revenue models incentivize quantity (more page views) while readers value quality (depth, nuance, attention to detail)
A one-man publication can't maintain the editorial-advertising wall that larger media companies use to preserve independence
There's a Catch-22 in building a membership site: you need revenue to build features, but readers want features before they'll pay
Direct reader support had already outperformed AdSense revenue but still fell short of a full-time income
Gruber positioned Daring Fireball as serving readers unsatisfied with typical tech media journalism
money_quotes: What's most appealing to advertisers is quantity. More readers, more hits. More traffic. More page views.
What most appealing to you, the regular readers of this site, is quality. Attention to detail. Appreciation of nuance. Depth.
An advertising revenue model leads to trying to appeal a little bit to very many people.
What I'm trying to do is appeal very much to fewer people — in particular, people who aren't satisfied with typical tech media journalism.
People who appreciate that some ideas are subtle, and take more than a few paragraphs to express.
Most publications maintain at least some semblence of a wall between the advertising and editorial departments; such a wall obviously isn't plausible for a one-man show.

I can't promise to add features without the revenue, but you — the reader sitting on the fence with regard to coughing up the money to join — are unlikely to be motivated by these as-yet-unimplemented features until they're actually available.
posts.key_insight: Advertising-supported media optimizes for breadth while reader-supported media can optimize for depth, making direct funding the only viable model for independent writing that values nuance over traffic.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a discount for purchasing multiple Daring Fireball t-shirts, offering additional shirts at $20.48 each after the first at full price ($30.72). He walks readers through a manual PayPal-based ordering process since he never built a proper multi-item order form. International buyers pay a $25 per-shirt rate to cover shipping costs. The post is a straightforward logistical announcement for his merchandise operation.
key_points: Additional Daring Fireball shirts available at $20.48 each after purchasing one at $30.72
The ordering process is deliberately low-tech, using PayPal donations and manual processing
International orders carry a higher per-shirt cost ($25) to cover shipping
Buyers can optionally request additional membership keys for each extra shirt
money_quotes: I never got around to setting up a form where you can specify multiple shirt sizes, so we'll do it low-tech-style.
I can't eat the cost of overseas shipping for this price.
posts.key_insight: A minor logistical post about Daring Fireball merchandise, notable mainly as an early example of Gruber's indie business model and his willingness to use low-tech workarounds rather than over-engineer solutions.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on the Dashboard-vs.-Konfabulator controversy, clarifying that his criticism targeted the bystanders crying foul rather than Konfabulator's creators personally. He systematically dismantles arguments that Apple 'ripped off' Konfabulator by tracing Dashboard's lineage through desk accessories and HyperCard, arguing that none of the constituent ideas — scriptable runtimes, single-purpose applets, skinnable UIs — were novel to Konfabulator. He acknowledges Brent Simmons's concern that Apple bundling similar functionality discourages third-party developers, but counters that Apple buys technology and implementations, not ideas. He concludes that Dashboard's real significance is making the Mac a platform for client-side web applications, built on open standards anyone already knows.
