key_points: Dashboard's lineage traces directly through desk accessories (scope) and HyperCard (ease of creation), both predating Konfabulator by years or decades.
Konfabulator's widget aesthetics derive from the broader skinning/theming movement pioneered by Winamp and others, not from any Konfabulator innovation.
Apple routinely acquires technology it considers superior (Final Cut, SoundJam/iTunes, etc.) but has no obligation to pay for ideas alone.
Apple's adoption of 'widgets' over 'gadgets' was unnecessarily antagonistic and contributed to the perception of copying.
Dashboard's use of web standards (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) makes it fundamentally more inclusive than Konfabulator's proprietary platform, which is the real story being missed.
money_quotes: Making it easier to write client-side software using scripting languages is clearly something that makes Mac OS X a better platform. That Konfabulator offered something similar first doesn't entitle them to anything.
I see Konfabulator as a well done implementation of existing ideas. If you see it as something deeply innovative, then we've reached a point where we'll have to respectfully disagree.
What Apple doesn't pay for are ideas. I suppose Simmons's argument is that it might be a good idea if they did — but I'm unaware of any company that does.
If Apple saw Konfabulator as a good idea but not a good implementation — what would they have been paying for? Good will? Karma?
Where does the line get drawn? Should Apple have bought the rights to iCab before shipping Safari?

Whatever the reason for Apple's apparent switch to and appropriation of 'widget', it's unfortunate. To the casual observer, it certainly contributes to the perception that Dashboard is Konfabulator knock-off, and, worse, it's unnecessarily antagonistic.
Arlo Rose is a hyper-talented visual designer; his widgets possess a certain je ne sais quoi. But they're still just skins, albeit extraordinarily well-done ones.
posts.key_insight: Konfabulator assembled existing ideas well but invented nothing new, and Apple's real contribution with Dashboard is opening client-side app development to anyone who knows web standards.

posts.summary: Gruber defends HFS+ against years of disrespect from Unix purists who dismissed it in favor of UFS, arguing that Apple's file system was always better-suited to Mac OS X. He reveals that Dominic Giampaolo, hired from Be Inc., wasn't building an HFS+ replacement but rather Spotlight — a metadata search system built on top of HFS+. The post details Spotlight's architecture from a WWDC attendee's report, explaining its file-centric design, importer-based indexing, and real-time metadata updates. Gruber notes that Tiger will actually expand HFS+ support rather than deprecate it, vindicating those who stuck with it.
key_points: Unix enthusiasts who reformatted their Mac OS X drives as UFS were making an ideological rather than technical choice, and all eventually switched back to HFS+
Dominic Giampaolo wasn't hired to replace HFS+ but to build Spotlight's metadata layer on top of it, adding capabilities rather than starting over
Spotlight is file-centric at its core — its email search only works because Tiger Mail switched to one-message-per-file storage, potentially leaving third-party mail clients unsearchable
Spotlight uses three data sources: its own metadata store, HFS+ catalog calls, and SearchKit's full-text index, with importers firing automatically on every file change
Tiger 10.4 will ship with updated Unix tools that properly support HFS+ resource forks and metadata, fixing a long-standing gap
money_quotes: The gist of it boiled down to I'm a Unix tough guy; I have no need for that deprecated Mac OS baby stuff.
For typical desktop use, there were never any actual technical reasons for using UFS in lieu of HFS+; it just sounded like a nerdy thing to do.
The only negative thing that can be said about this is that it's about fucking time.
Updating the tools to support HFS features is a much better solution than trying to ram the less-featured UFS down everyone's throat as a sop to the limitations of 30-year-old command-line tools.
Ah-hah, exclaimed the peanut gallery, Apple hired Dominic Giampaolo to write a brand-new file system to replace HFS+!
Spotlight's ability to show results from Apple Mail archives on Jobs' machine was tantamount to a sham.
Spotlight is going to kick a great deal of ass.
posts.key_insight: Apple's strategy with Spotlight was to build a powerful new metadata layer on top of HFS+ rather than replace it, vindicating the file system's design and proving the Unix purists wrong.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a new self-service feature for Daring Fireball members to retrieve lost membership keys used for accessing members-only RSS feeds. Members can enter their email address to have key information resent. He notes that most cases of 'missing' keys are likely due to spam filters catching the original confirmation emails. He provides practical guidance on whitelisting the support email address.
key_points: A self-service membership key retrieval system has been added for Daring Fireball members
Membership keys are required for accessing members-only RSS feeds
Most missing key complaints are likely caused by spam filters flagging confirmation emails
PayPal orders are processed automatically while eSellerate orders require manual processing
money_quotes: My guess is that in the cases where members 'never got' their keys, overzealous spam filters mis-flagged the messages containing the membership confirmation and key information.
posts.key_insight: A simple self-service tool for key retrieval solves both the user frustration of lost membership keys and the support burden of manually handling retrieval requests.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that Markdown won third place in Six Apart's Plug In To Movable Type 3.0 Developer's Contest. His prizes included a 40 GB iPod, a $100 iTunes gift certificate, and Adobe Creative Suite Premium. He notes Jay Allen won the grand prize for MT-Blacklist. Unable to attend the BlogOn conference where awards were announced, Gruber wrote an absurdly grandiose acceptance speech comparing his plug-in award to the 1980 U.S. Olympic hockey team's Miracle on Ice.
key_points: Markdown placed third in Six Apart's MT 3.0 developer contest, an early formal recognition of the tool
Jay Allen's MT-Blacklist, a comment-spam blocker, won the grand prize — reflecting the era's blog infrastructure priorities
Gruber's mock acceptance speech hilariously equates a plug-in contest win with the Miracle on Ice, perfectly capturing his deadpan humor
money_quotes: A young, rag-tag team of American hockey players — more boys than men, really — faced off against the quote-unquote "unbeatable" Soviet Red Army.
With this award for Markdown, my Movable Type plug-in, my answer to that question is "Yes". Thank you.
posts.key_insight: Gruber celebrates Markdown's early contest win with a comically overblown acceptance speech that makes winning third place for a text-formatting plug-in sound like the greatest upset in sports history.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that the Daring Fireball t-shirts have arrived and are being shipped out to buyers. He credits his wife for handling the logistics of mailing several hundred shirts. Some recipients have already received theirs, while the rest will ship in the coming days. It's a brief, personal update post typical of early blogging.
key_points: Daring Fireball merchandise (t-shirts) arrived and began shipping to buyers
Gruber's wife handled the substantial logistics of mailing several hundred orders
Some early recipients were already sharing photos of their shirts online
money_quotes: Thanks to the diligent work of The Wife, several hundred shirts are now in the hands of the Postal Service.
posts.key_insight: A brief logistics update on Daring Fireball t-shirt fulfillment, showing the early days of indie blog merchandising as a one-family operation.

posts.summary: Gruber uses the conceit of a Magic 8-Ball Q&A to dissect RealNetworks' Harmony software, which reverse-engineered Apple's FairPlay DRM to allow Real's music store purchases to play on iPods. He exposes the contradiction in Real CEO Rob Glaser's earlier plea for Apple to open the iPod while ignoring that iPods already play unprotected music. The piece frames Harmony as a desperate publicity stunt rather than a legitimate business move, noting that Real bypassed licensing negotiations entirely. Gruber highlights Apple's thinly veiled threat to break Harmony in future iPod updates and treats Real's talk of licensing the technology to others as laughable.
key_points: RealNetworks reverse-engineered Apple's FairPlay DRM without permission or licensing, translating Real's proprietary format into Apple's proprietary format
Rob Glaser's claim that the iPod only works with iTunes purchases was misleading, ignoring CD ripping and non-DRM files entirely
Real's true concern was its own declining format market share, not consumer choice on the iPod
Apple's press release was a clear signal it would deliberately break Harmony in future software updates
The whole Harmony initiative reads more as a publicity stunt than a viable business strategy
money_quotes: Isn't what he really means that it's the market share for RealNetworks' protected file format that will go down if it doesn't work on the iPod?

Do you think RealNetworks honestly expected Apple to allow them to unofficially support the iPod, without paying Apple a cent in licensing fees, after Apple refused to allow them to officially support it?
Doesn't this seem more like a desperate publicity stunt than a legitimate business strategy?
Apple's (market) share will go down if they continue to do this. The only way to presently put songs on an iPod is to (buy) them from iTunes.

We strongly caution Real and their customers that when we update our iPod software from time to time it is highly likely that Real's Harmony technology will cease to work with current and future iPods.
posts.key_insight: RealNetworks' Harmony was a desperate attempt to piggyback on iPod dominance by reverse-engineering Apple's DRM, but it was transparently more about saving Real's own declining relevance than serving consumers.

posts.summary: Gruber discusses the Perl Email Project, a new set of CPAN modules under the Email:: hierarchy that replace the older, over-engineered Mail:: modules for parsing email files. He argues that despite Perl's natural fit for text processing and the plain-text nature of mbox email storage, the existing Mail:: modules were needlessly complicated and slow. The new Email:: modules by Simon Cozens deliver dramatically better performance and simpler APIs. Gruber demonstrates with a concrete code comparison showing how a multi-step body extraction became a single method call, and reports a 10x speed improvement in his own scripts.
key_points: Perl should be ideal for mbox email parsing given its text processing strengths and Unix heritage, but the existing Mail:: CPAN modules were over-engineered
The new Email:: module hierarchy created by the Perl Email Project offers simpler APIs and dramatically faster performance — over 10x in Gruber's testing
The concrete code comparison illustrates Larry Wall's principle that 'easy things should be easy' — reducing two lines of unintuitive code to one clean call
Gruber recommends Simon Cozens' Perl.com article as an introduction to the new modules
money_quotes: They reek of over-engineering — complicated APIs and slow performance.
One of Larry Wall's guiding precepts for Perl is, "Easy things should be easy, and hard things should be possible."
The modules in the Mail:: hierarchy fail the "easy things should be easy" test.
I replaced Mail::MboxParser with Email::Folder in a script I wrote to process mbox files full of T-shirt orders, and it ran over 10 times faster.
Even better, I found the syntax more intuitive — more, well, Perlish.
posts.key_insight: Good library design should follow Larry Wall's principle that easy things should be easy — over-engineering common tasks is a failure of API design, not a sign of sophistication.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles the conventional wisdom that Apple's failure to license the Macintosh in the 1980s was a catastrophic strategic error. He argues that IBM-compatible PCs were technically incapable of running a Mac-like OS in 1984, making software-only licensing impossible. He reframes the key difference between Apple and Microsoft not as open-vs-closed licensing, but as pragmatism-vs-idealism: Microsoft parlayed each success into the next (DOS to Windows to Office), while Apple repeatedly built revolutionary but incompatible new platforms (Apple II to Mac to Newton). The Mac's low market share likely stems not from licensing decisions but from Apple's failure to parlay the Apple II's success, though doing so would have produced something far less revolutionary.
key_points: IBM-compatible PCs in 1984 were technically incapable of providing a Mac-like experience — much of the Mac OS was in ROM and depended on proprietary hardware, making software-only licensing impossible.

The real strategic difference between Apple and Microsoft was not open-vs-closed but pragmatism-vs-idealism: Microsoft relentlessly parlayed each success into the next, while Apple built revolutionary but incompatible new platforms.

Microsoft's Windows monopoly is an anomaly born from IBM's decision to license DOS rather than proof that markets inherently favor open platforms — as evidenced by closed console gaming platforms like Nintendo, Sony, and Xbox.
Apple repeated the pattern of non-parlaying with the Newton, which had only tenuous ties to the Mac instead of being designed as a must-have Mac peripheral.
A hypothetical Mac built on the Apple II would have been evolutionary rather than revolutionary — pragmatic but fundamentally un-Apple.
money_quotes: Toss it back and forth long enough and a nugget of conventional wisdom eventually comes to be treated as fact.
Companies which compete directly against Microsoft have a tendency to go out of business quickly and gruesomely.
Microsoft parlayed their DOS command-line-era monopoly into the Windows GUI-era monopoly, then parlayed their Windows monopoly into the Office monopoly.
That they ended up on different sides of the open/proprietary divide is an effect, not the cause.
The difference between Microsoft and Apple wasn't about open-vs.-closed; it was pragmatism-vs.-idealism.
The real Mac was revolutionary; a hypothetical Mac based on the Apple II would have been evolutionary.
We might have ended up with something merely 'new and improved', which isn't nearly as exciting as 'insanely great'.
I posit that the Windows monopoly is an anomaly, and exists only because of IBM's decision to license the DOS operating system from Microsoft, rather than buying it or writing their own from scratch.
posts.key_insight: The Mac's low market share resulted not from Apple's refusal to license but from their idealistic refusal to parlay existing successes into new platforms — a pattern that produced revolutionary products at the cost of incremental market dominance.

posts.summary: This is a humorous meta-post where Gruber preemptively addresses reader objections to his previous essay 'The Art of the Parlay' about platform licensing and market share. Rather than engaging with each counterexample, he simply lists technologies and platforms that readers likely emailed him about — the Apple III, Lisa, OS/2, the Amiga, GEM, and others. The joke is in the absurdly long title and the deliberate refusal to discuss any of these items substantively. He also gets in a dig at GEM enthusiasts who overstate its resemblance to the Mac. The format itself is the argument: these historical footnotes don't undermine his thesis.
key_points: Gruber preemptively deflects predictable reader objections by listing them without rebuttal
The listed items are all failed or marginalized platforms from computing history that readers likely cited as counterexamples
The parenthetical on GEM reveals Gruber's actual knowledge depth — he's not ignorant of these, he's deliberately excluding them
The post's form (a bare list with a comically overwrought title) is itself the rhetorical argument
money_quotes: Things Unmentioned in the 'Art of the Parlay' Article, but of Which You Must Take My Word Were Left Out Because They Were Beside the Point, and Not Because I Was Unaware of Them
GEM (early '80s GUI OS for PCs from Digital Research; which was not nearly as 'Mac-like' as GEM aficionados would have you believe)
posts.key_insight: The best way to handle predictable objections that miss your point is to acknowledge them without dignifying them with a response.

posts.summary: Gruber systematically dismantles the mainstream media narrative that Apple is 'repeating its Macintosh mistakes' with the iPod. He argues that the iPod's position is fundamentally different from the 1980s Mac because the iPod is built on compatibility — it works with Windows, plays MP3s and CDs, and parlays off existing platforms rather than demanding users abandon them. He dissects a USA Today column point-by-point, exposing factual errors and logical fallacies in the 'closed system' critique. He argues that RealNetworks' Harmony offers no benefit to Apple and that the real consumer choice the iPod provides is the freedom to avoid DRM entirely. The HP licensing deal further undermines the 'Apple won't license' narrative. Gruber concludes that Apple is strategically positioned to win the nascent DRM format war while losing nothing if DRM fails.
key_points: The iPod's compatibility with Windows, MP3s, and CDs makes it fundamentally different from the 1980s Mac, which was an incompatible platform requiring incompatible software
Media coverage consistently mischaracterizes the iPod as a 'closed system' by ignoring that DRM-protected music is optional — the iPod fully supports unprotected formats
RealNetworks' 'choice' argument is hollow: those worried about DRM lock-in won't trust Real or Microsoft either, and will buy non-DRM music that the iPod already plays
Apple's HP licensing deal directly contradicts the claim that Apple refuses to license its technology
Apple is strategically betting on winning the DRM format standard, which could become a massive recurring revenue stream and parlay into DRM video — while losing nothing if DRM fails entirely
money_quotes: Apple's entire music platform is a pure parlay.
But there are other metrics than design. One being compatibility. And in terms of compatibility, DOS/Windows was better than the Mac.
The only proprietary aspect of the iPod is with protected audio formats, a.k.a. DRM.
But what Apple provides is a larger and more important choice: the choice not to use DRM protected audio at all.
DRM lock-in is indeed a serious issue — but the people who are concerned about lock-in aren't going to trust RealNetworks or Microsoft any more than they trust Apple.
If you ignore MP3 files, the iPod only plays Apple's own proprietary iTMS songs. But that's a little like arguing that if it weren't for cash, you'd have to use credit cards for every purchase.
It's foolish to think that every public statement from Apple's executives is the plain truth about their actual strategy.
Establishing a de facto standard format for DRM audio, on the other hand, is the type of success that keeps on succeeding.
posts.key_insight: The iPod is not the Mac repeated — it's a compatibility-first platform that parlays off existing ecosystems, making the 'Apple is making the same mistake' narrative not just wrong but structurally backwards.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews the final release of OmniWeb 5.0, having used it as his primary browser for two months. He praises its unique features — saved session state, draggable thumbnail tabs, and per-site display preferences — as genuine innovations that other browsers should adopt. However, he identifies a critical weakness: OmniWeb's rendering engine is based on an outdated version of WebCore (v85, corresponding to Safari 1.0 from June 2003), which causes compatibility problems with modern sites like Gmail. He frames OmniWeb's situation as a race against time, since competitors like Safari and Camino will inevitably copy its best UI ideas while maintaining superior rendering.
key_points: OmniWeb 5.0's session-saving, draggable thumbnail tabs, and per-site preferences represent genuinely useful browser innovations
Omni's decision to build on low-level WebCore rather than the higher-level Web Kit created a rendering engine that's over a year behind Safari
The outdated WebCore means real-world incompatibility — Gmail works in Safari 1.2 but fails completely in OmniWeb 5.0
Catching up is structurally difficult because Omni customizes WebCore with their own features, making updates non-trivial
OmniWeb faces a competitive threat: Safari and Camino developers can copy its UI innovations while maintaining current rendering engines
money_quotes: OmniWeb saves the state of your entire browsing session when it quits. When it relaunch, your windows, tabs, and page contents are restored. Every browser should work this way.
OmniWeb 5.0 still doesn't feel quite as snappy as Safari or Camino, but it's fast enough.
OmniWeb's rendering capabilities are woefully behind the times.
When web developers test for browser compatibility, they test against the latest version of Safari. This means that some sites that work in Safari 1.2 do not work in OmniWeb 5.0.
The result is that using OmniWeb requires a trade-off. You get some very nice browser application features, but at the expense of using a slightly outdated and less-compatible rendering engine.

The Omni Group's challenge is to catch up to the current version of WebCore, and to stay there. Because other browser developers — like say Safari's and Camino's — are surely taking a long look at OmniWeb's best features.
posts.key_insight: OmniWeb 5.0 demonstrates that innovative browser UI features mean little if your rendering engine can't keep up, and the clock is ticking because competitors will adopt your best ideas while maintaining technical parity.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the 1.0 release of Markdown, his plain text formatting syntax and Perl-based converter. He details key changes from the beta, including simplifying the code block syntax (dropping colons in favor of indentation only), abandoning a confusing dual GPL/commercial licensing experiment in favor of straight GPL, and adding flexible bullet markers for unordered lists. He also celebrates the rapid emergence of ports and re-implementations in PHP, Ruby, AppleScript, and other languages, noting Markdown's adoption across multiple content management systems including WordPress, Drupal, and Moodle.
key_points: Markdown 1.0 simplifies code block syntax to just indentation (4 spaces or 1 tab), dropping the colon requirement from the beta
The dual GPL/commercial licensing experiment was both confusing and unsuccessful, so Markdown is now purely GPL
Gruber distinguishes Markdown as two things: a plain text formatting syntax and the Perl software that converts it to HTML
Multiple ports already exist in PHP, Ruby, and AppleScript, with adoption in WordPress, Drupal, Moodle, and other CMS platforms
The only backward-incompatible syntax change is the code block simplification, which may leave superfluous colons in existing documents
money_quotes: This experiment was both (a) confusing and (b) unsuccessful, so Markdown is now simply licensed under the GPL, no strings attached.
"Markdown" is really two different things: (1) a structured plain text formatting syntax for writers; and (2) software I wrote in Perl that translates Markdown-formatted text into HTML.
I had hoped that others would port/re-implement Markdown in other scripting languages and weblogging/content management systems, and it didn't take long.
Donations to support Markdown's development are happily accepted, but are not required.
This is the only significant syntax change that breaks compatibility with the initial public beta.
posts.key_insight: Markdown 1.0 represents Gruber's willingness to simplify ruthlessly — dropping a failed licensing scheme and a needlessly complex syntax rule — while the rapid proliferation of third-party ports validates the core idea of separating a writing format from any single implementation.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a codeless language module he created for BBEdit 8 that provides syntax coloring for Apache configuration files. BBEdit 8's major new feature is support for codeless language modules via XML property list files, replacing the previous requirement for compiled C/C++ plug-ins. The module highlights comments and Apache server directives for .conf and .htaccess files. He iteratively updated it based on community feedback, eventually building a comprehensive keyword list covering all Apache 1.3 and 2.0 server directives.
key_points: BBEdit 8 introduced codeless language modules as XML property lists, replacing the need for compiled C/C++ plug-ins
Gruber created an Apache configuration syntax coloring module as a practical demonstration of this new capability
The module was developed during the beta period, not hastily after release
Community feedback drove multiple updates, expanding from an incomplete keyword list to a comprehensive superset of all Apache 1.3 and 2.0 directives
money_quotes: Creating codeless language modules is a cinch — each module is an XML property list file.
I was a beta-tester for BBEdit 8; I put this together a few weeks ago, not in the hour since BBEdit 8 was publicly released.
posts.key_insight: BBEdit 8's shift to codeless language modules via simple XML property lists dramatically lowered the barrier to extending the editor's syntax support.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights the absurdity of Microsoft's MSN music executive Yusuf Mehdi claiming their music service was not a response to Apple's iTunes Music Store. He points to Microsoft naming their newsletter 'Music Tuesday' — the same name concept as Apple's 'iTunes Tuesday' — as evidence of direct imitation. After readers correct him that Tuesday is the industry-standard release day, Gruber pivots to the stronger point: Mehdi's own subsequent answers contradict his initial claim, admitting Apple launched before Microsoft had even considered the idea and that the decision to sell music was only made a year prior.
key_points: Microsoft's MSN music VP claimed their music service was independently conceived and not a response to iTunes Music Store
The naming similarity between Microsoft's and Apple's Tuesday-themed music newsletters suggests direct imitation rather than independent development
Mehdi's own interview answers contradict each other — first claiming independence, then admitting Apple launched before Microsoft even considered the idea
Microsoft's pattern of following Apple's lead while publicly denying it is presented as both transparent and faintly ridiculous
money_quotes: No, of course it's not in response to the iTunes Music Store.
I mean, what's Microsoft supposed to do? Release their new music on *Wednesdays*?
My 'Release their new music on Wednesdays?' crack about the day of the week is off-base, but come on — the *exact* same newsletter title?
Apple had already gotten out before we had even thought about doing our own particular service.
Both of which answers seem to contradict the first, that they were 'on a path already to provide great music services' before the ITMS.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's executive couldn't even maintain the fiction of independent innovation within a single interview, with his own answers revealing that their music service was plainly a reaction to Apple's iTunes Music Store.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Microsoft's MSN Music help page, which blamed Apple for iPod incompatibility with Windows Media DRM while positioning Microsoft's own proprietary format as the open 'consumer choice.' He notes the hypocrisy of calling Windows Media 'popular' while labeling Apple's market-leading AAC/FairPlay as merely 'proprietary DRM.' He further reveals that Microsoft revised the help page from a genuinely useful answer (explaining how to burn and re-import music) to pure spin that offered no actual solution. The revision replaced helpful instructions with blame-shifting talking points, making the help page less useful but more aligned with Microsoft's messaging.
key_points: Microsoft selectively applied 'popular' to Windows Media and 'proprietary DRM' to AAC/FairPlay, when both descriptors apply equally to both formats — except Apple held 70% market share
Microsoft's 'consumer choice' rhetoric required Windows Media format, Windows OS, and Internet Explorer — no actual choice at all
The original MSN help page contained a genuinely useful workaround (burn to CD, reimport via iTunes) that was removed in favor of pure anti-Apple messaging
Microsoft's claim that Apple 'refuses to allow other companies to integrate' was demonstrably false, given HP's iPod hardware deal and Audible.com's partnership
money_quotes: The only one they choose for themselves — 'popular' — is the least apt for Windows Media, given that Apple's AAC/FairPlay has 70 percent market share.

There are more than 70 portable audio devices that depend on technology licensed and controlled by Microsoft today, and we hope that someday Apple gives up on their own market-leading technology, bends over, and uses ours.
The 'consumer choice' championed by Microsoft involves no choice at all regarding: Media format (Windows Media), Operating system (Windows), Web browser (Internet Explorer).
But other than that, Microsoft is all for choice.
Help pages are more useful when they're helpful, rather than spiteful.
It's no more or less standard than AAC/FairPlay, unless you subscribe to the school of thought that standards are whatever Microsoft says are standards.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's 'consumer choice' campaign against the iPod was pure spin — they replaced a genuinely helpful answer with blame-shifting talking points, revealing that their real goal was lock-in to Microsoft-controlled technology, not interoperability.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a codeless language module he created for BBEdit 8 that provides syntax coloring for Movable Type configuration files. The module supports all documented MT directive keywords plus six undocumented ones found in the default config file. After publication, Phil Ringnalda pointed him to the canonical list of directives in MT's Perl source code, which yielded 25 additional undocumented keywords. Gruber updated the module accordingly and pointedly noted that Six Apart should document these directives.
key_points: The BBEdit 8 language module provides syntax coloring for Movable Type's mt.cfg configuration files
Six undocumented directives were discovered in the default config file beyond what MT's manual listed
The canonical source of truth for MT directives lives in the Perl source code (ConfigMgr.pm), not the documentation
A total of 25 additional undocumented keywords were found in the source, highlighting a significant documentation gap
money_quotes: It provides syntax coloring for comments and MT directive keywords; by default, it claims any file whose name ends with 'mt.cfg'.
Note to Six Apart: It'd be nice to have these documented.
posts.key_insight: When documentation falls short, the source code becomes the only reliable reference — and tools like syntax-coloring modules end up doing the documentation work that the software vendor should have done.

posts.summary: Gruber notes that Microsoft's MSN Music help page regarding iPod compatibility has been rewritten again, this time removing overt propaganda. The updated page now straightforwardly states that iPod doesn't support WMA format and directs iPod owners to send feedback to Apple asking them to add Windows Media support. Gruber finds this framing notable, as Microsoft positions Apple as the obstacle to interoperability. The brief post is a follow-up to his earlier piece on the topic.
key_points: Microsoft rewrote its MSN Music iPod help page to remove overtly propagandistic language
The new page frames Apple as the party responsible for iPod owners' inability to play MSN Music purchases
Microsoft directs users to pressure Apple to add WMA support rather than acknowledging its own format lock-in strategy
money_quotes: The MSN Music help page regarding iPod support has been rewritten yet again, this time, sans propaganda.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's revised MSN Music page dropped the overt propaganda but still cleverly framed Apple as the obstacle to interoperability, deflecting attention from its own proprietary format strategy.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews BBEdit 8.0, Bare Bones Software's flagship text editor, arguing that its enduring appeal stems from the deep integration of powerful features with a genuinely Mac-like interface — not mere aesthetics, but interactive ergonomics. He distinguishes between superficial visual polish and true usability, pushing back against critics who dismiss BBEdit for not being flashy enough and Unix purists who reduce interface quality to feature checklists. The review covers major new features including a multi-document interface, ATSUI-powered Unicode rendering, Text Factories for batch processing, codeless language modules, and the significant architectural shift of dropping Mac OS 9 support entirely. Gruber frames the OS X-only requirement as liberating rather than limiting, enabling BBEdit to fully embrace modern platform conventions throughout the application. He also makes a surprisingly fair case for Emacs and Vim's cross-platform portability as their strongest selling point.
key_points: The user interface IS the application — features that aren't presented via an intuitive, discoverable interface might as well not exist
BBEdit's appeal lies in the balance of powerful text editing and genuinely Mac-like interaction design, where 'interface' means interactive ergonomics, not cosmetic appeal
The Mac's main appeal is not that it is easy-to-use but easy-to-learn — a significant but often overlooked distinction
Dropping Mac OS 9 support enabled BBEdit 8 to fully embrace OS X conventions throughout, including multi-document windows, ATSUI text rendering, and proper domain-layered support files
The most compelling case for Emacs or Vim is not their feature sets but their cross-platform interface portability
money_quotes: The user interface is the application — whereas most non-application software doesn't even have a user interface.
Features which aren't presented via an intuitive, discoverable, usable interface might as well not even exist.
Some would have you believe that such is the price of power — that easy-to-use and powerful are mutually exclusive.
The Macintosh's main appeal is not that it is easy-to-use, but rather that it is easy-to-learn. The difference is significant, but often overlooked.
The point is not to impress you; the point is to enable you to get work done.
In short, the point of BBEdit is not just what it looks like or what it does, but how it feels.
It's certainly nice to have a chair that looks good; but if you're going to be sitting in it 8 or more hours every day, ergonomics are much more important than aesthetics.
Sure, it's nothing a short Perl or Python script couldn't do — but if you think everyone can or should learn to program, you're missing the point.
posts.key_insight: A great application is defined not by its feature list or visual polish but by how deeply its interface enables users to work — the distinction between looking good and feeling right is what separates tools you admire from tools you depend on.

posts.summary: Gruber shares an AppleScript he wrote to solve a specific workflow problem with BBEdit 8's new multi-document text windows. He wants documents to open individually by default, but also wants the ability to open a group of related files together in a single new window from the Finder. The script uses Brent Simmons's Big Cat Scripts contextual menu plug-in to achieve this. He walks through the script's logic in detail, explaining the workaround needed because BBEdit can't create empty text windows. Chris Nandor later ported the script to Perl for command-line use.
key_points: BBEdit 8's multi-document windows offer all-or-nothing defaults, but Gruber wants selective grouping of related files
The script exploits BBEdit's 'open...opening in' syntax to batch-open files into a specific window, which is more efficient than looping
A key workaround involves tracking the auto-created untitled document's unique ID so it can be closed after the real files are loaded
The script integrates with the Finder via Big Cat Scripts contextual menu or can be adapted as a drag-and-drop application
money_quotes: But what I want is something in-between. By default, I want documents to open using the traditional individual-window model. But I also want to be able to open a group of related files all at once, together in a single new window — in a single action from the Finder.
There is no way to create an 'empty' text window containing no documents; when you close the last document in a window, the window goes away.
There's no need to loop through the list and open them one at a time — in fact, for a long list of files, it's considerably more efficient to tell BBEdit to open the list in one statement.
We couldn't close this document before opening the files in file_list because it was the only document in w at the time, and so the entire window would have gone away when the document was closed.
The in w is all it takes to open these files in the new text window we just created.
posts.key_insight: When software defaults don't match your workflow, a small script can bridge the gap between all-or-nothing settings and the nuanced behavior you actually want.

posts.summary: Gruber announces he's replacing Google AdSense with his own direct sponsorship program for Daring Fireball. He argues that AdSense's algorithmically targeted ads were poorly matched to his readership and undervalued his audience. The new model offers just one text-only ad per page with a maximum of five rotating sponsors, priced transparently at $200/week. He makes the case that text ads are superior for publisher, reader, and sponsor alike, citing Poynter Institute eye-tracking research showing text ads receive far more attention than display ads. The post is both an announcement and a sales pitch, framed as a principled stand for quality over clutter.
key_points: Google AdSense served poorly targeted ads that didn't match Daring Fireball's audience, undervaluing the site's readership
The sponsorship model limits ads to one text-only placement per page with a maximum of five sponsors, emphasizing exclusivity
Gruber publishes rates transparently ($200/week, $500/four weeks), criticizing the industry norm of hiding pricing as foolish
Text-only ads benefit all three parties — publisher, reader, and sponsor — by keeping the design clean and the attention focused
Eye-tracking research shows text ads receive nearly 4x more viewing time than the best display ads
money_quotes: It's not so much that AdSense is bad, per se, but simply that it isn't very good for a site like Daring Fireball.

Ad-driven web sites seldom publish their rates, apparently driven by paranoia that doing so will put them at a competitive disadvantage. This has always struck me as foolish; hiding information, especially the price, never makes people happy.
If demand is low, I'll lower the prices, if it's high, I'll raise them. Welcome to microeconomics.
I don't want this site to look like a tramp.
What's essential to remember is that the web is not print, and it's not TV.
The value in Daring Fireball's sponsorships is that they're exclusive.
If you're skeptical about the effectiveness of text ads, please consider The Poynter Institute's Eyetrack III.
Yes, engineers, I'm asking you to visit someone in marketing.
posts.key_insight: By rejecting algorithmic ad placement in favor of curated, exclusive, text-only sponsorships, Gruber pioneered the indie web advertising model that values audience quality and design integrity over volume.

posts.summary: Gruber transparently breaks down Daring Fireball's traffic numbers and readership estimates to support his new sponsorship initiative. He reports roughly 7,000 daily page impressions and estimates around 10,000 regular readers based on unique IP addresses and RSS subscriber counts. He calculates CPM rates for potential sponsors and acknowledges the inherent difficulty of translating server logs into accurate readership figures. Crucially, he argues that Daring Fireball's value to advertisers lies not in raw traffic volume but in the quality and specificity of its audience — savvy Mac enthusiasts who know what an RSS feed is.
key_points: Daring Fireball averages about 7,000 total daily page impressions across all sections, translating to roughly $12.75-$20 CPM for sponsors
Readership is estimated at 10,000+ regular readers based on unique IP addresses, though converting IPs to actual people is inherently imprecise
The site's longer-form, less-frequent posting format generates fewer page views than high-volume link blogs, and the lack of comments further reduces raw traffic
Daring Fireball ranked 99th on Bloglines' most popular blogs list, placing it competitively among technically savvy audiences
The pitch to advertisers is audience quality over quantity — a focused Mac-enthusiast readership that few higher-traffic sites can match
money_quotes: To be clear, the appeal of Daring Fireball as an advertising forum is not the raw page view counts — there are scads of sites, including many weblogs, with significantly more traffic.
Extrapolating web server logs into readership numbers is right up there with political election polling in terms of exemplifying the old "lies, damned lies, and statistics" aphorism.
Unique IP addresses are easy to count, but again, there are all sorts of reasons I can't state with certainty how many people are subscribed to my feed.

If humility didn't stand in the way, I might point out that very few of the sites in Bloglines' rankings listed ahead of Daring Fireball would be appropriate venues for advertisers targeting Daring Fireball's savvy Mac-enthusiast audience.
That's certainly not to claim that Daring Fireball is one of the 100 most popular web sites in the world. I mean, come on.
posts.key_insight: A niche site's advertising value comes from the specificity and engagement of its audience, not from raw traffic numbers — quality readership beats quantity.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights USA Today columnist Kevin Maney's experience with a devastating Windows virus that left his PC in an endless reboot loop, and the wave of 'get a Mac' reader mail that followed. Maney acknowledged that Macs and Linux machines 'hardly get infected or invaded at all,' though he hedged with 'for whatever reasons.' Gruber seizes on that phrase as the crux of the issue: regardless of why Windows is less secure, the practical reality is that Windows users are plagued by malware while everyone else is not. He notes that despite Microsoft's years of security promises, new viruses were up 400% year over year. Gruber argues that ordinary, non-technical users are beginning to realize the cycle of patches and anti-virus software will never end, and the best solution is to stop using Windows altogether.
key_points: Kevin Maney's personal Windows malware nightmare illustrates the typical home user experience with Windows security failures
The reasons why Windows is more vulnerable than alternatives are debatable, but the practical outcome is not — Windows users suffer disproportionately from malware
Despite years of Microsoft's security improvement promises, the situation was getting measurably worse, with new viruses up 400%
Non-technical users are reaching a tipping point where they recognize the patch-and-protect cycle is futile
The best solution to the Windows security problem, for ordinary users, is simply not to use Windows
money_quotes: "For whatever reasons" is the key point I tried to make in "Broken Windows" back in June. The reasons why are subject to argument. But you can't argue about the net effect: Windows users, especially with their home computers, are plagued by insidious malware; everyone else is not.

New Windows updates, new anti-virus software, new ad-blocking software — regular people are starting to realize that the cycle never ends, that they are never going to successfully secure Windows, and that the easiest and best solution to the Windows security problem is not to use Windows at all.
The masses are restless.

Kevin Maney is not a nerd. Enthusiast, maybe, but not a nerd. What's interesting about these two columns isn't technical insight into the nature of the problem; he has none. But the vast majority of Windows users aren't nerds, either, and Maney seems to have his finger on their pulse.
Microsoft has been talking about improved security in Windows for the last few years. But the situation continues to get worse.
posts.key_insight: The debate over why Windows is less secure than alternatives is beside the point — what matters is the undeniable reality that Windows users are drowning in malware, and ordinary people are finally realizing the only real fix is to stop using Windows.

posts.summary: Gruber clarifies his earlier hypothesis that security problems are driving regular (non-technical) people to consider alternatives to Windows. He emphasizes this isn't about a mass exodus but a subtle shift of a few percentage points — something unprecedented for Windows, which has only ever gained market share. He argues most switchers will likely move to desktop Linux rather than Mac, but the mere fact that ordinary people are becoming aware alternatives exist is enormously beneficial to Apple. He concludes that rising Linux market share comes at Windows' expense, not the Mac's.
key_points: Windows has never before experienced a loss in market share momentum, making even a small decline historically significant
Most regular (non-technical) computer users don't even realize alternatives to Windows exist — security concerns are changing that awareness
The majority of Windows refugees will likely switch to desktop Linux rather than Mac, but increased awareness of alternatives benefits Apple regardless
Desktop Linux gaining market share comes at Windows' expense, not the Mac's, despite press framing it as bad news for Apple
Apple's biggest historical challenge isn't losing head-to-head comparisons but getting people to consider the Mac at all
money_quotes: Windows has never really suffered a loss in momentum. Its market share has only ever gone in one direction: up.
Most regular people don't even realize that there exist any choices other than Windows.
Nerds are people who at least realize Windows is a choice. I've never seen any sign that large numbers regular people are even aware of this. Until now.

The simple fact that more people are becoming aware that there are choices other than Windows, and that there are easy-to-understand reasons why one might do so, could be the best thing to happen to Apple's market share in a decade.
Here's a simple 'regular person' test: Can you explain the difference between RAM and a hard disk? The vast majority of computer users can't.
Rising desktop Linux market share comes at the expense of Windows' market share, not the Mac's.
And anything that raises awareness that there exist alternatives to Windows is a good thing for everyone other than Microsoft.
posts.key_insight: The unprecedented fact that ordinary, non-technical people are becoming aware that alternatives to Windows exist — driven by security frustrations — represents a subtle but historically significant shift in momentum that benefits everyone except Microsoft.

posts.summary: Gruber shares an AppleScript he wrote for BBEdit 8 that enables renaming an open document in a single step, a feature he notes no application offers natively. The script presents a dialog box with the current filename and lets users type a new name. It handles both saved files (by telling the Finder to rename) and unsaved documents (by changing BBEdit's internal name property). He walks through the technical implementation, explaining how BBEdit's scripting objects and AppleScript's dialog commands work together.
key_points: No application Gruber knows of offers in-place document renaming, requiring a tedious multi-step process through the Finder instead
The script handles two cases elegantly: renaming files on disk via the Finder, and renaming unsaved documents by changing BBEdit's name property
The script works with BBEdit 8's multi-document windows, renaming only the active document
Gruber provides a detailed technical walkthrough of the AppleScript implementation
money_quotes: A feature I've always wanted, but which no application I'm aware of offers, is the ability to rename an open document.
"Save As" is not the same thing — because while it allows you to save a document with a new name, it leaves behind the previously-saved copy of the file with the old name.
This has always struck me as something that ought to be doable in a single step.
I use this for temporary scratch documents which I don't need to keep, but for which I'd like to have more descriptive names than something like "untitled text 5".
posts.key_insight: Renaming an open document should be a single-step operation, and the gap between what users expect and what applications provide can often be bridged with scripting.

posts.summary: Gruber explains how to toggle soft wrapping in BBEdit using keyboard shortcuts, covering both the built-in BBEdit 8 method and his preferred AppleScript approach. He describes three distinct soft-wrap states he uses depending on context: off for source code, fixed-width (80 characters) for prose, and window-width for HTML markup. He shares an AppleScript that cycles through all three modes with a single keyboard shortcut, using BBEdit 8's new Page Guide feature as a visual indicator of the current wrapping state.
key_points: BBEdit 8 added built-in keyboard shortcut assignment for Status Bar menu items, making soft wrap toggling easier than the old AppleScript method
Gruber uses three different soft-wrap states depending on context: off for code, fixed-width for prose, and window-width for HTML
The Page Guide feature in BBEdit 8 replaces the old Philip Bar and adapts its color to be slightly darker than whatever background color is set
His AppleScript cycles through all three wrap modes with one shortcut, using the Page Guide visibility as a visual state indicator
money_quotes: When I'm writing source code, I typically leave soft wrap off. When I'm writing prose, I turn soft-wrapping on, with the wrapping width set to 80 characters. When I'm editing HTML markup, however, I tend to turn soft-wrapping on, but wrapped to the entire width of the window instead of a fixed width.
The Page Guide isn't necessarily light gray; it's a slightly darker shade of whatever color you've specified as your text window background color.
Thus the Page Guide provides a visual indication of the current soft wrapping status.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed text editor workflow maps different wrapping modes to different writing contexts — code, prose, and markup each benefit from distinct wrapping behavior — and a single keyboard shortcut can cycle through them all.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the return of Daring Fireball t-shirts due to remaining stock from the first run and continued reader demand. The shirts are priced at $29 and include a one-year membership to the site. He outlines shipping details, notes that ladies' sizes are no longer available, and warns that stock is first-come, first-served with a second printing run planned. Existing members can purchase shirts at a significant discount. The post is a straightforward commerce announcement tied to his membership-driven business model.
key_points: Daring Fireball t-shirts are back due to leftover stock and ongoing reader requests
Each shirt purchase includes a one-year Daring Fireball membership with perks like members-only RSS feeds
Existing members get a steep discount on shirts, rewarding early supporters
The membership model is Gruber's approach to sustaining independent publishing
money_quotes: Current stock will be shipped first-come, first-served.
Everyone who purchases a shirt also gets a one-year membership to Daring Fireball.
To everyone who's already become a member: my sincere thanks.
posts.key_insight: Gruber uses merchandise as both a revenue stream and a membership incentive, tying physical goods to his independent publishing business model.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Steve Ballmer's claims about Apple, iPods, and DRM by examining four quotes from a recent interview. He challenges Ballmer's assertion that most iPod music is stolen, arguing most users rip from legally purchased CDs. Gruber points out the fundamental contradiction in Microsoft's DRM strategy: making DRM 'easier' doesn't solve the problem because DRM's difficulty IS the point — it restricts what users want to do. He argues Apple is already leading the digital entertainment convergence precisely because it doesn't enforce draconian DRM, while Microsoft's corporate-market instincts are irrelevant in consumer electronics. The post draws historical parallels to Hollywood's failed opposition to the VCR to illustrate that consumer platforms succeed by pleasing consumers, not industry executives.
key_points: Ballmer's claim that most iPod music is stolen is likely wrong — most iPod owners have large CD collections they've legally ripped
The only thing 'hard' about DRM is when users want to do things DRM forbids, so making DRM 'easier' is a contradiction
Ballmer's own 12-year-old son instinctively understands the problem with DRM better than Microsoft's strategy acknowledges
The iPod's success on Windows already disproves Ballmer's claim that Apple can't achieve critical mass beyond the Mac
Microsoft's corporate-market instincts are a liability in consumer entertainment, where pleasing users matters more than pleasing executives
money_quotes: The most common format of music on an iPod is 'stolen.' — I'd love to see his source for this.
The only thing 'hard' about using DRM media is when you want to use it in ways that the DRM forbids.

Ballmer's 12-year-old son is, I'm guessing, smart enough to realize that there's no technical reason that would prevent him from putting 'all the music that he wants in all of the places that he would like it'.
If this is a joke, I get the feeling Apple will be laughing all the way to bank.
The iPod strikes me as existence proof that Apple can succeed outside the Mac user base.
Apple is already leading the way in terms of music — in large part because they don't enforce draconian DRM measures.
Successful consumer platforms are designed to make consumers happy, not clueless entertainment industry executives.
The film industry fought against the VCR, but it became wildly successful anyway, because consumers loved it.
posts.key_insight: Consumer platforms win by delighting users, not by enforcing restrictions that please corporate executives — and Microsoft's instinct to serve the enterprise makes it fundamentally misread the consumer electronics market.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his previous analysis of Steve Ballmer's comments about iPod users and stolen music. After controversy arose about whether Ballmer's quotes were taken out of context by Silicon.com, Gruber examines a fuller transcription published by The Guardian Unlimited. He concludes that even with additional context, Ballmer clearly claimed most music on iPods is stolen and implied that support for non-DRM music is a problem. Gruber argues this reveals Ballmer fundamentally misunderstands why the iPod is popular.
key_points: Silicon.com had to retitle their article after misquoting Ballmer with fabricated direct quotes, though the substance remained accurate
Even with full context restored, Ballmer's core claim that most iPod music is stolen holds up as reported
Ballmer frames the iPod's support for non-DRM music as a 'problem' rather than a feature
Gruber argues Ballmer's DRM-centric worldview blinds him to understanding the iPod's actual appeal to consumers
money_quotes: Ballmer clearly claimed that most music on iPods is "stolen", and implies that the iPod's support for non-DRM-protected music is a "problem".
If this is really what he believes, he has no idea why the iPod is so amazingly popular.
Most people still steal music.
The fact that you can buy it and it's protected doesn't affect the fact that most people still steal music.
As long as there's alternate forms of music acquisition, there still will be ways for people to steal music.
posts.key_insight: Even with full context restored, Ballmer's comments reveal Microsoft's fundamental misunderstanding of why consumers love the iPod — it's not about DRM, it's about the user experience.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that he is available for hire as a freelance web developer, designer, and technical writer. After two years of publishing Daring Fireball, he acknowledges that while the site's revenue from T-shirts, memberships, and sponsorships exceeds typical blog income, it remains firmly short of a full-time salary. He admits that self-promotion doesn't come naturally to him but recognizes the practical necessity. The post lists his skills — technical writing, web standards development, Perl/PHP coding, and Mac expertise — and opens the door to both contract work and full-time employment.
key_points: Daring Fireball's revenue has grown beyond a typical blog but remains insufficient as a primary income source
Gruber frames his site as a 'very lucrative hobby' still in the 'don't quit your day job' category
He overcomes his natural aversion to self-promotion out of practical financial necessity
His skill set spans technical writing, web standards development, Perl/PHP, and Mac expertise
The post marks a pivotal moment where Gruber publicly seeks to turn his reputation into sustainable work
money_quotes: The revenue from this web site is well beyond that of a typical weblog — but, it's way short of a full-time salary.
It's turned into a very lucrative hobby, but the numbers are still firmly in the "don't quit your day-job" category.
Self-promotion does not come naturally to me. I'd much prefer to let my work speak for itself.
Unfortunately, you can't pay rent with a well-written weblog, or with consulting services no one is aware of.
I am currently free, where by "free", I mean under-employed and available.
I am a technical writer who can present and explain complex subject matter engagingly.
posts.key_insight: Even with a successful and influential blog, Gruber found that reputation alone couldn't pay the bills — a remarkably honest admission from someone who would later prove that independent publishing could become a full career.

posts.summary: Gruber reports that PayPal's website has been experiencing a major outage since the weekend, remaining mostly down through the day of posting. The outage has directly impacted his Daring Fireball T-shirt sales, preventing readers from completing orders. He apologizes to affected customers and suggests they either keep trying during intermittent windows of availability or wait for PayPal to resolve the issue.
key_points: PayPal suffered a multi-day outage affecting its web service from the weekend through Monday
The outage directly impacted Gruber's merchandise sales by blocking T-shirt orders
The service was intermittently available rather than completely down, making it frustrating but occasionally usable
money_quotes: Which in turn has prevented a bunch of you from ordering T-shirts.
If you've tried to order and couldn't get through, I'm terribly sorry.
posts.key_insight: Even a brief operational post reveals Gruber's early dependence on PayPal as sole payment infrastructure for his independent publishing business, highlighting the risks of relying on a single third-party service.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that the real problem with Mac OS X's brushed metal window theme isn't whether it looks good, but that Apple uses it inconsistently, violating its own Human Interface Guidelines. He distinguishes between the superficial aesthetic debate and the deeper consistency argument, noting that the Mac's usability advantage has historically depended on apps looking and feeling uniform. Safari's adoption of brushed metal despite fitting none of the HIG's criteria was a watershed moment proving Apple ignores its own rules. Gruber contends the HIG should either be enforced or rewritten to reflect Apple's actual capricious policy, because a guidelines document that doesn't match reality has no credibility.
key_points: The brushed metal debate has two distinct layers: personal aesthetic preference and the deeper issue of UI consistency across the platform
The behavioral difference of metal windows (drag from anywhere) makes inconsistency worse — if it's a good idea, all windows should support it
Safari was the watershed moment where Apple clearly ignored its own HIG criteria for brushed metal usage
The HIG itself undermines consistency by framing the choice in aesthetic terms ('too heavy') rather than emphasizing strict consistency rules
If Apple won't follow the HIG, the HIG should be rewritten to honestly reflect actual policy — guidelines are only credible when they match practice
money_quotes: The HIG states, 'Don't use the brushed metal look indiscriminately', but indiscriminate is precisely the word to describe Apple's use of it.
A large part of the Mac's historical usability advantage is that Mac applications all look and feel the same.
If it's a good usability idea, all windows should work like this; otherwise, none should.
It's quite obvious that the one and only reason Safari uses brushed metal is that someone at Apple thinks it looks better that way.
The HIG is only credible if it accurately reflects Apple's actual policy. If the policy isn't going to change, then the HIG should.
To those of you who think this state of affairs is just fine, I ask this: If two themes are OK, why not three or four?
posts.key_insight: Platform guidelines that the platform owner itself ignores are worse than no guidelines at all, because they undermine the credibility of the entire consistency framework.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Mac OS X's UI consistency problem is worse than the brushed metal debate suggests, identifying at least three (possibly four) distinct visual themes Apple uses across its software. Beyond Aqua and brushed metal, Apple's 'Pro' apps use an entirely custom dark theme with replacement controls unavailable to third-party developers. Tiger introduces yet another theme with unified title bars. Gruber contends that Apple is undermining its own platform's consistency — a foundational Mac principle since 1984 — by proliferating themes based on aesthetics rather than clear guidelines. He fears that if Apple won't lead on consistency, the Mac's advantage over Windows in this area will erode.
key_points: Apple's Pro apps (Final Cut, Motion, DVD Studio Pro, Logic) use a completely custom dark theme that replaces standard Aqua controls, not just window chrome like brushed metal does
The Pro theme is unavailable to third-party developers, implying Apple considers its own default appearance insufficient for premium software — undermining the platform brand to build a product brand
Tiger introduces a fourth theme with unified title bars and toolbars, but Apple has given no official guidance on its name or intended use
Consistency is valuable for its own sake, independent of aesthetics or usability — and Apple is sacrificing it without any principled framework
If Apple won't follow its own Human Interface Guidelines, third-party developers will inevitably create their own custom themes, accelerating the chaos
money_quotes: The point is consistency. Not usability, not how easy something is to learn, but consistency for its own sake.

If your application is a professional production tool, and your company headquarters are at 1 Infinite Loop, Cupertino CA, you may use the Pro theme to provide a special appearance for your application.
Is it not reasonable to conclude that Apple deems Mac OS X's default appearance not good enough for its own use?
Apple's Pro theme is replacement of the default theme, not a supplement to it.
Apple's apparent desire to assert its Pro line of software as being special by using a custom suite-wide theme comes directly at the expense of system-wide consistency.
My fear, of course, is that it's just a third window frame, and that the only factor determining when it will be used is whether it looks cool.
Consistency in and of itself has been a fundamental pillar of the Mac user experience from 1984 onward. But with Apple no longer leading the way, it's fading.
"At least it's still more consistent than Windows" is not high praise.
posts.key_insight: Apple's proliferation of custom UI themes across its own software — unavailable to and unjustifiable by third-party developers — is eroding the platform-wide consistency that has been a core Mac value since its inception.

posts.summary: Gruber revisits his criticism of Apple's inconsistent use of brushed metal windows in Mac OS X, honestly admitting he's unsure whether the inconsistency truly matters or if it's merely an aesthetic preference. He engages with Mike Manzano's counterargument that the differences are purely cosmetic and don't affect widget behavior or placement. While conceding the point has merit, Gruber then pivots to an actual usability complaint: brushed metal windows look nearly identical whether active or in the background, making it easy to mistake which window has focus and invoke wrong keyboard shortcuts.
key_points: Apple has no consistent policy governing brushed metal window usage, and third-party developers have followed Apple's inconsistent example rather than the HIG
The strongest counterargument is that consistency only matters when it affects usability, and brushed metal differences are merely cosmetic
Brushed metal windows fail to visually distinguish between active and background states, unlike standard Aqua windows which use gradient, darkness, and depth cues
The argument that brushed metal helps identify apps in Exposé doesn't scale — taken to its logical conclusion, every app would need its own custom window style
The active/background window distinction is fundamental to the Mac experience and should be visually significant, not subtle
money_quotes: This isn't a retraction, per se, but an admission of vacillation. I just don't know.

Maybe it is just aesthetics — merely the color of pixels, rather than how the interface truly works — and I'm looking for serious implications that aren't there, simply because I happen to think brushed metal windows look stupid.
The texture of brushed metal windows is exactly the same when they're in the foreground and background.
The logical conclusion of this line of thinking is that every app should use its own custom window style.
The active window is an essential concept in the Macintosh user experience.
If there is a case to be made for brushed metal, usability certainly isn't it.
I've received more email this week from brushed metal aficionados than I have from gloating Red Sox fans.
posts.key_insight: Even when the broader consistency debate is uncertain, brushed metal has a concrete usability flaw: it fails to visually distinguish active windows from background ones, undermining a fundamental element of the Mac interface.

posts.summary: Gruber writes a satirical fake announcement for an 'iPod Daring Fireball Special Edition,' riffing on Apple's real U2 iPod Special Edition announced at their October 2004 event. He jokes that he was supposed to announce it at the Apple event but got bumped when the U2 segment ran long. The fictional iPod is slate gray with his autograph on the back and comes bundled with a Daring Fireball T-shirt. He further jokes that it includes the entire Daring Fireball archive as audiobooks narrated by an absurd cast including Christopher Walken and Donald Rumsfeld.
key_points: Parodies Apple's U2 iPod Special Edition announcement with a fictional Daring Fireball version
Pokes fun at celebrity branding partnerships by inserting himself as the celebrity
The audiobook narrator lineup (Walken, Rumsfeld, DeGeneres, HAL 9000's voice actor) is deliberately absurd comedy
Subtly promotes his existing Daring Fireball T-shirt merchandise through the joke
money_quotes: This was supposed to have been announced at this morning's Apple event, but the U2 segment ran long (private message to Bono: thanks, jerko) and I ended up getting bumped.
Like no other, iPod Daring Fireball Special Edition stands out.
Slate gray, it bears a subtly lighter gray Apple Click Wheel, and, on the flip side, my autograph.

As an extra special treat, it includes the entire Daring Fireball archive in audiobook format, featuring recordings narrated by Christopher Walken, Douglas Rain, Ellen DeGeneres, Donald Rumsfeld, Dr. Joyce Brothers, and Apple Computer co-founder Steve Wozniak.
posts.key_insight: Gruber uses self-deprecating humor to lampoon the celebrity-branded iPod concept while affectionately mocking Apple's product marketing language.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the iPod's explosive growth after Apple shipped 2 million units in Q4 2004, outpacing Mac sales by nearly 3-to-1. He systematically dismantles Paul Thurrott's track record of wrong iPod predictions — from calling the Mini 'way too expensive' to championing the Dell DJ as an 'iPod Killer' — using Thurrott's latest dismissal of the iPod Photo and U2 Edition as a contrarian buy signal. Gruber analyzes the prospects for both new models, attributing the iPod's dominance to hardware design, software quality, and cultural momentum. He also addresses why Apple chose photos over video (legal barriers to DVD ripping, not a Steve Jobs conflict of interest) and why iTunes handles photo syncing instead of iPhoto (pragmatism over architectural purity).
key_points: Apple shipped 2 million iPods in Q4 2004 versus 836,000 Macs, with market share rising to 92% of hard-disk players despite increasing competition
Paul Thurrott's perfect record of wrong iPod predictions — Mini pricing, WMA support, Dell DJ as iPod killer — makes his dismissal of new models a reliable contrarian indicator
The iPod's success rests on three pillars: hardware design, iTunes software superiority, and pop-culture momentum driven by word-of-mouth and the 'it just works' factor
Apple chose photos over video not due to Steve Jobs' Pixar conflict of interest, but because ripping DVDs is illegal and there's no legal video content ecosystem yet
iTunes handles photo syncing instead of iPhoto because Apple pragmatically avoids porting Mac apps to Windows that won't sell more iPods
money_quotes: Now it's not even close — 2 million iPods vs. 836,000 Macs.
Thus, judging by nothing other than Thurrott's perfect 0.000 batting average with regard to all things iPod, Apple's latest additions to the iPod line-up will sell through the roof.
Considering the iPod now holds 92 percent of the market for hard-disk-based players — higher than when the Dell DJ debuted — one must assume the DJ is a very sneaky assassin indeed.
People aren't shopping for 'digital music players', they're shopping for iPods.
Undergirding all three is the secret sauce: the 'it just works' factor.

But today's Apple is a pragmatic company. They are in the business of selling iPods and selling Macintosh computers; they are not in the business of developing and giving away Windows application software.
Since all Apple has on Windows is the iTunes hammer, every iPod synching problem is treated like a nail.
posts.key_insight: The iPod's dominance stems not from any single feature but from the compounding effect of superior hardware, superior software, and cultural momentum — and its critics' consistent wrongness only confirms the strength of Apple's position.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the 'poof' animation in Mac OS X — the effect when dragging items off the Dock, Finder sidebar, or toolbars — arguing it represents genuine UI inconsistency far more important than the brushed metal debate. He traces how Apple pioneered safe deletion via the Trash, then undermined that clarity by introducing poofing as a second, inconsistent deletion gesture. The Dock's poofing prevents useful unmodified drags (like creating aliases), the Finder sidebar wastes the entire drag concept on poofing alone, and only toolbar poofing is appropriate since toolbar icons have no meaning outside the toolbar. He argues items should behave as aliases with drag-to-Trash for removal, as third-party apps like DragThing already demonstrate.
key_points: Poofing conflicts with normal drag-and-drop, preventing useful unmodified drags from the Dock and Finder sidebar
The Dock's behavior requires unintuitive Command-drag modifier keys to get useful drag functionality that should be the default
DragThing demonstrates the correct approach: treat dock items as aliases, use drag-to-Trash for removal
The Finder sidebar is even worse than the Dock — no modifier key combination allows dragging an actual reference to the item
Toolbar poofing is the only appropriate use because toolbar icons have no meaning outside the toolbar itself
money_quotes: It's a cute effect, made for demoing how cool Mac OS X looks — but it's shitty user-interface design, and Apple has implemented it inconsistently.
In the Finder sidebar, the entire concept of dragging — one of the most useful actions of the Mac's UI vocabulary — is wasted entirely on poofing.
In the old Mac OS, there was one clear way to delete something using a mouse gesture, that being to drag it to the Trash. This worked remarkably well.
You were never more than one bad wildcard away from wiping out essential files.
This is just a confusing side-effect of the Dock's conflated roles as both a launcher and process manager.
Much better would be if sidebar items were aliases. Then they could serve both as drop targets and draggable items.
Knowing this Command-drag shortcut makes the Dock much more useful. However, it's very much counter-intuitive.
posts.key_insight: Apple's poof animation sacrifices the fundamental utility of drag-and-drop for a cute visual effect, and the solution is obvious: treat proxy icons as aliases and use drag-to-Trash for removal.

posts.summary: Gruber continues his examination of Mac OS X's 'poof' animation, cataloging additional inconsistencies across Safari, Menu Extras, Disk Utility, and the Finder's Find dialog. He finds that Safari dangerously breaks the unwritten rule that poofing should be non-destructive by actually deleting bookmarks rather than just removing references. Menu Extras support poofing for removal but lack drag-and-drop for installation. Disk Utility uses poofing as the only removal method but restricts where you can drop, while the Finder's Find dialog fails to support poofing at all, instead moving actual folders when you drag items from the list. The piece argues that these inconsistencies undermine user trust in a gesture that should have reliable, predictable behavior.
key_points: Safari's Bookmarks Bar violates the core poof principle by deleting actual data (bookmarks/bookmarklets) rather than just removing a reference
Menu Extras support poof removal via Command-drag but have wildly inconsistent methods for being added in the first place
Disk Utility's sidebar uses poofing as the sole removal mechanism but restricts the drop zone to within the app window, breaking the standard behavior
The Finder's Find dialog is the most dangerous case: dragging from the search places list moves the actual folder rather than removing the reference, potentially causing data loss
OmniWeb correctly refuses to poof bookmarks, demonstrating that third-party developers sometimes get Apple's conventions more right than Apple does
money_quotes: When you poof a Safari bookmark, you are deleting the actual bookmark itself. In every other instance of poofing, you're only removing a reference to something, not deleting actual data.
It's a bad idea because it breaks the unwritten rule that poofing is non-destructive, removing only a reference to a thing, not the thing itself.
OmniWeb 5, on the other hand, gets this right.
Poofing is the only way to remove a disk image file from the Disk Utility sidebar. Judging from my email, this is utterly non-intuitive.

Dragging a folder out of the list moves the original folder to wherever you drop it. This includes dropping items on the Trash — a gesture one might reasonably assume would simply remove the folder reference from the list.

This list of specific search places is exactly the sort of list-of-references where Apple typically uses poofing to remove items. That you're instead dragging a reference to the original folder is both unexpected and dangerous.
posts.key_insight: Even small interaction patterns like the poof animation need rigorous consistency across the OS, because when the same gesture sometimes removes a reference and sometimes deletes data or moves files, users can no longer trust any instance of it.

posts.summary: Gruber enthusiastically recommends Cabel Sasser's 'The True Story of Audion,' published by Panic Software. He considers it so exceptional that it deserves a full post rather than a quick link. He urges readers to set aside distractions and read it carefully, including the pop-up footnotes. He calls it one of the greatest Mac stories he's ever read.
key_points: Cabel Sasser of Panic Software wrote a detailed history of Audion, their Mac audio player
Gruber considers the story so good it deserves more than a quick link post
The piece is described as capturing the full emotional range of indie Mac software development — joyous, exciting, and heartbreaking
Gruber's enthusiastic endorsement signals the story's importance to Mac culture and indie development history
money_quotes: Ordinarily, this is the sort of thing I'd post to the Linked List, but it's simply too good — extraordinarily, wonderfully good — and thus deserves your full attention.
One of the greatest Mac stories I've ever read.
Joyous, exciting, heartbreaking.
Get yourself a lovely beverage, disconnect the phone, quit your IM client, and enjoy The True Story of Audion.
Seriously, read this. You'll thank me.
posts.key_insight: Some stories about the Mac community are so good they demand you stop everything and read them — and Cabel Sasser's history of Audion is one of them.

posts.summary: Gruber reports on an MDJ exclusive claiming that Tim Bucher, who had been leading Apple's Macintosh hardware engineering division for six months, abruptly left the company. MDJ reported that Bucher's departure was announced internally without explanation and that he was removed from Apple's systems. However, Gruber introduces doubt by noting a source who says Bucher is still listed in the company directory. The post serves as both signal-boosting of MDJ's scoop and a subtle fact-check of its details.
key_points: MDJ reported Tim Bucher's sudden departure from Apple's hardware engineering division with no public announcement or explanation
