Bucher had only been in the role for six months after replacing Jon Rubinstein, who moved to lead the iPod division
Gruber's own source at Apple contradicts part of MDJ's report, noting Bucher still appeared in the company directory
The story had not been reported outside MDJ or even widely known within Apple beyond the hardware division
money_quotes: This week, without warning, Apple's hardware engineering group was told that Bucher had left the company.
Not only has this not been reported anywhere other than MDJ, but it hasn't even been announced outside Apple's hardware division.
Confidentiality, of course, is guaranteed.
posts.key_insight: Gruber amplifies a significant insider scoop about Apple executive turnover while carefully noting where the reporting doesn't fully check out, demonstrating his role as a critical filter for Apple news.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that his Linked List, previously available only to paying members via RSS, is now publicly accessible as a separate weblog. He explores where to place link-log content on Daring Fireball's front page, surveying how other prominent web designers handle sidebars for links. After testing a Kottke-style approach of interspersing linked items with full articles in the main column, he decides it undermines Daring Fireball's signature single-column simplicity. He concludes that what works brilliantly for one site's identity can detract from another's, and keeps the Linked List separate while offering the combined view as an optional preference.
key_points: The Linked List is now publicly available on the web after six months as a members-only RSS feature, though the feed itself remains members-only
Sidebar placement, the de facto standard for link-logs, doesn't suit Daring Fireball because the commentary is too long for a narrow column
Gruber tested Jason Kottke's innovation of interspersing links and articles chronologically in the main column, but found it diluted Daring Fireball's identity
The core design philosophy of Daring Fireball is a single column with one place to look for content and minimal distraction
Design trade-offs are context-dependent: what enhances one site's character can diminish another's
money_quotes: The evaluation of trade-offs is the hardest part of design.
At Kottke.org, the intertwining of links and articles made the site even more Kottke-ish. Here, however, it made the site less Fireball-ish.
The idea is that there's only one place where you need to look for new stuff to read, and there's little else to distract you.

Gained was a bit of vibrancy, a home page which could remain current even during periods when I don't have time to write full articles. Lost, however was a certain element of virtuous simplicity, a quietness of presentation.
If you want to know which weblogs I recommend, just pay attention to the sites I link to.
How in the world would anyone notice a new entry in a list that long?
posts.key_insight: Good design isn't about copying what works elsewhere — it's about understanding your own site's identity well enough to know when a clever idea would undermine it.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the rampant speculation about Apple releasing a flash-memory-based iPod, arguing that the rumors fundamentally misunderstand product design by treating storage technology as a feature rather than an implementation detail. He dissects the market share statistics used to justify the rumor and points out that consumers don't choose players based on storage mechanism — they choose based on price, size, and experience. He considers three possible rationales (cheaper, smaller, sport-oriented) and finds each problematic: a cheap iPod risks the brand, a smaller one faces interface constraints, and a sport model wouldn't necessarily be cheaper. Gruber concludes that if Apple does release a flash-based iPod, the flash memory itself is the least interesting part — what matters is the purpose it serves.
key_points: Storage mechanisms are implementation details, not features — 'hard-drive player' and 'flash-memory player' are not real product categories that consumers shop by
A low-capacity, low-cost flash iPod would generate short-term sales at the expense of the iPod brand, since generous storage is central to the iPod experience
The iPod Mini was misunderstood at launch as a 'cheaper iPod' when it was actually a 'smaller iPod' — the same confusion drives flash iPod speculation
Apple has never indicated they want to corner the entire MP3 player market — just the market for good ones
The market share statistics cited by rumor promoters conflate U.S. and global numbers in misleading ways
money_quotes: Storage mechanisms are implementation details, not features.
No one goes shopping for a music player and decides against an iPod because they want a 'flash-memory player'. Many, however, decide they want a cheap music player.
The iPod isn't popular because it's cool — it's cool because it's so well-designed.
The idea that Apple can just slap the iPod brand on a low-capacity, low-cost player and 'make it work' is backwards.
Demand for lower-cost iPods exists. But, duh, demand for lower-cost everything exists. Perhaps you'd like to see a $15,000 BMW 3-series model, but you're not going to get one.
Apple has never indicated that they intend to corner the entire market for MP3 players — just the market for good ones.
No matter what the case, if there is a flash memory iPod in the pipeline, the fact that it uses flash memory is the least interesting part of the story.
Flash memory, in and of itself, is not a feature. Size, weight, and price — those are features.
posts.key_insight: Technology choices like flash memory are implementation details, not product features — what matters is the user experience they enable, and Apple would never compromise the iPod brand for market share in a low-end segment.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that Daring Fireball is adding Amazon affiliate links as a new revenue stream, after previously pioneering blog monetization through advertising and paid memberships. He explains the mechanics of the affiliate program, noting that referral bonuses come from Amazon's cut rather than increasing prices for buyers. He highlights Amazon's role as a competitive Apple reseller with prices sometimes significantly below Apple's own, making the affiliate links mutually beneficial for readers shopping for Apple hardware. The post is framed as a holiday shopping season pitch, with practical details about international Amazon stores and user preferences for controlling the links.
key_points: Daring Fireball adds Amazon affiliate links as a third revenue stream alongside advertising and paid memberships
Amazon's affiliate bonuses come from Amazon's margin, not from increased prices to the buyer
Amazon sells Apple hardware at prices sometimes significantly below Apple's own retail prices, with mail-in rebates saving $100-150 on Macs
International readers can choose between Amazon US, UK, and Canadian stores, or disable affiliate links entirely via a preferences page
money_quotes: This site has been somewhat of a trailblazing effort in terms of generating revenue from a weblog via advertising and via paid memberships and T-shirt sales.
For absolutely no good reason, however, I've never attempted to make some scratch via the most common means for weblogs: affiliate links.
It's worth noting that the referral bonuses come out of Amazon's end, not yours — you pay Amazon's regular prices, and Amazon sends a portion (generally around 5 percent) to me.
A 5 percent cut on the price of a book might not seem like much, but I'm hopeful it might add up in the aggregate.
More intriguingly, Amazon is a lot more than just a bookstore now. Point of fact, they're a terrific Apple reseller.
Even though Amazon caps the affiliate bonus at $25 on computer hardware purchases, it's easy to see how this might add up.
posts.key_insight: Gruber frames Amazon affiliate links as a natural and reader-friendly extension of his blog monetization experiments, sweetened by Amazon's surprisingly competitive Apple hardware pricing.

posts.summary: Gruber methodically dismantles a rumored screenless flash-memory iPod design leaked by TheMacMind, arguing that no screen, FireWire-only connectivity, and a circular gamepad controller are all implausible for Apple. He critiques a Japanese designer's alternative mock-up that places a screen inside the clickwheel as impractical due to scale problems. He addresses Slashdot readers who prize 'no moving parts' over hard drives, arguing iPod hard drives are reliable enough and store far more per dollar. He notes that Toshiba confirming flash memory sales to Apple is the only credible data point, but warns against assuming any specific rumored design will materialize.
key_points: A screenless iPod would require an audio-only interface, which would be frustrating for navigating any meaningful music library
FireWire-only connectivity on a budget iPod makes no sense since many PCs lack FireWire but have USB 2
The 'no moving parts' argument from Slashdot readers is theoretically valid but practically irrelevant — iPod hard drives are reliable enough and offer far superior storage per dollar
Sanada's screen-inside-the-clickwheel concept fails on scale: the screen would be smaller than a postage stamp, and scrolling would obscure the display
Toshiba's named-source confirmation of flash memory sales to Apple is credible, but doesn't validate any specific rumored iPod design
money_quotes: I just don't see no screen at all as a feasible design.
Storage mechanisms are implementation details, not features.
If everyone who understands the basic difference between a hard disk and solid-state memory stopped buying iPods, it'd barely register as a blip on the sales chart.
Solid state technology may be more reliable than hard drives, but hard drives — or at least the ones used by Apple — are more than reliable enough.
Here we go again with rumors setting expectations for radically lower-priced iPods, echoing last year's iPod Mini conjecture.
What fan fiction is to TV shows, these guys are to industrial design.
My point is that I don't think they'd make a crummy iPod just to hit a significantly lower price point.
Don't let the rumormongers say 'I told you so' if Apple unveils a new iPod that happens to use flash memory, but otherwise resembles none of the currently-rumored details.
posts.key_insight: Apple wouldn't sacrifice the iPod's core user experience — screen, intuitive controls, broad compatibility — just to hit a lower price point with flash memory, and rumor sites conflate 'Apple will use flash memory' with wildly speculative design details.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the release of Markdown 1.0.1, a maintenance update focused on bug fixes and resolving ambiguities in Markdown's formatting syntax. The most significant change treats code blocks and spans as literal content, no longer processing backslash escapes within them. The release also tightens rules around link definitions and indentation, fixes numerous edge cases with lists, HTML comments, and horizontal rules, and corrects invalid HTML nesting. Several changes are explicitly noted as breaking from previous Markdown behavior.
key_points: Code blocks and spans are now treated literally with no backslash escape processing, a breaking change from all previous versions
Link definitions must now occur within three spaces of the left margin, with four-space indentation making them code blocks instead
A tricky bug where hard-wrapped paragraphs could accidentally trigger list creation is partially fixed but acknowledged as potentially unfixable for sub-lists
The release fixes invalid HTML output like improperly nested strong/em tags and spurious paragraph tags around HTML comments and nested lists
A subtle boolean evaluation bug where the string '0' was treated as empty input demonstrates the perils of Perl's truthiness semantics
money_quotes: This release was primarily focused on fixing bugs and eliminating certain ambiguities and confusing edge cases in Markdown's formatting syntax.
Code blocks and spans are now treated literally, with no special rules to worry about regarding backslashes.
This was never officially supported, but neither was it explicitly disallowed.
Given Markdown's list-creation rules, I'm not sure this can be fixed.
if the input to any function was the single-character string "0", it would also evaluate as false and return immediately. How silly. Now fixed.
posts.key_insight: Markdown 1.0.1 prioritizes predictability and correctness over backward compatibility, simplifying code block handling and tightening syntax rules even at the cost of breaking existing content.

posts.summary: Gruber explains why he switched Markdown's license from GPL to a BSD-style license starting with version 1.0.1. The GPL was originally chosen with a vague idea of keeping Markdown free for open source while charging commercial users, but in practice no commercial users offered to pay and BSD-licensed open source projects felt they couldn't include a GPL script. The irony was that closed-source apps like Ecto and MarsEdit were happily granted free permission while open source BSD projects felt excluded. The new BSD license simplifies everything: use it freely, just give credit and don't misuse the name. For revenue, Gruber added a PayPal donation box with a live tally, letting users set their own price.
key_points: The GPL created an unintended barrier where BSD-licensed open source projects felt they couldn't include Markdown, while closed-source commercial apps were granted free permission anyway
Gruber's original dual-licensing notion — free for open source, paid for commercial — never materialized because no commercial users volunteered to pay
The BSD license removes friction for all developers with only two conditions: attribution and trademark protection on the Markdown name
Gruber introduced a pay-what-you-want donation model with a live running tally as an experiment in monetizing open source
A roadmap for Markdown 1.1 includes definition lists, tables, blockquote cite attributes, and smarter HTML handling
money_quotes: Licensing discussions make me sleepy, so I'll try to keep this short.
The reason I originally chose the GPL for Markdown was that I had the vague, poorly-thought-out notion that Markdown could be available freely to users who simply wanted to use it.
No commercial developers or business users volunteered to pay any license fees anyway.

The irony of this is that non-open-source software like Ecto and MarsEdit are including Markdown for free, but the authors of several BSD-licensed open source projects feel they can't include Markdown.
What this means is that you get to set the price for Markdown. If you think it ought to be free, that's fine, go ahead and use it for free, no strings attached.
Can you make money giving away open source software? We'll see.
posts.key_insight: The GPL's restrictions on Markdown created the perverse outcome of excluding friendly open source projects while commercial apps got free passes, making a permissive BSD license the obviously correct choice.

posts.summary: Gruber examines Jeffrey Zeldman's multi-step procedure for safely updating Mac OS X system software, separating genuinely useful precautions from superstitious rituals. He shares his own five-step update process — wait a day, back up, log out clean, don't multitask during install, restart — then critiques Zeldman's additions like repairing permissions, clearing font caches, and re-prebinding the system. His central argument is that Repair Permissions is not preventive maintenance but a troubleshooting tool, and that many of the extra steps are harmless but unnecessary voodoo. He draws a direct parallel to classic Mac OS rituals like rebuilding the desktop and zapping PRAM.
key_points: Waiting a day or two after a system update release is wise insurance against bad installers slipping through Apple's QA
Repair Permissions is widely misunderstood — it's a troubleshooting tool, not preventive maintenance, and can sometimes create new problems
Many pre- and post-update rituals (clearing font caches, re-prebinding, running cron scripts) are modern equivalents of classic Mac voodoo like rebuilding the desktop
The key distinction is between 'harmless voodoo' that wastes only time and rituals that can actually cause harm
Full backups before and after updates are the one piece of advice that is unequivocally correct
money_quotes: Repair Permissions is not a periodic maintenance task or a preventive measure.

Just remember that there's no reason to invoke it unless you're experiencing problems; it's not a preventive measure, and even when it does make 'repairs', it's not necessarily a sign that there was anything wrong.
Bits of harmless voodoo from the days of the old Mac OS included rebuilding the desktop and zapping the PRAM.
Even if this is just superstitious voodoo, it's harmless voodoo. It only costs about a minute of your time.
Doing this again now is like taking a shower after taking a shower.
Ignore this advice only if you don't care about your data.
In fact, sometimes running Repair Permissions can create problems.
posts.key_insight: Most Mac OS X update rituals are superstitious voodoo inherited from the classic Mac era — the only steps that truly matter are waiting for bug reports, backing up your data, and letting the installer run undisturbed.

posts.summary: Gruber examines a Think Secret rumor that Apple will announce an office suite called 'iWork' at Macworld Expo, consisting of Keynote 2.0 and a new app called Pages. He notes that IGG Software quietly renamed their existing 'iWork' app to 'iBiz,' offering only a vague explanation. Gruber connects this to USPTO filings showing Apple registered the 'iWork' trademark after IGG held it, and concludes Apple likely paid IGG for the name under NDA.
key_points: Think Secret reported Apple would announce an office suite called iWork with Keynote 2.0 and a new word processor called Pages
IGG Software's sudden, unexplained rename from 'iWork' to 'iBiz' strongly corroborates the rumor
USPTO filings show Apple filed for the 'iWork' trademark in August 2004 after IGG previously held it
Gruber infers Apple paid IGG for exclusive rights to the name, with Gillespie under NDA about the deal
money_quotes: Lending significant credence to this rumor is the fact that IGG Software has just renamed their time management/invoicing app, from 'iWork' to 'iBiz', offering no explanation other than a small print statement.
What seems likely to me is that Apple paid IGG for exclusive rights to the name, and but that Gillespie is under NDA regarding the deal.
posts.key_insight: The circumstantial evidence — a quiet app rename, evasive non-answers, and USPTO filings — points convincingly to Apple acquiring the 'iWork' trademark for an upcoming office suite announcement.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's lawsuit against Think Secret for publishing accurate rumors about the headless iMac and iWork suite before Macworld Expo 2005. He notes the curious timing of filing suit before the keynote, which effectively confirms the rumors. The core of his analysis examines Apple's legal strategy: the suit isn't really about stopping these specific leaks but about forcing Think Secret to reveal its sources so Apple can punish the leakers and deter future ones. Gruber argues Think Secret likely has strong First Amendment protections but may lack the resources to fight Apple in court. He references a 2000 precedent where MacInTouch's lawyer made clear Apple had no legal standing against publishers of leaked information. Ultimately, Gruber sees this as Apple playing a long game — making examples of leakers to discourage others from talking.
key_points: Filing suit before the Expo effectively confirms the rumors, suggesting Apple's legal strategy prioritizes identifying leakers over protecting specific secrets
Think Secret likely has strong legal standing since publishers aren't bound by NDAs they didn't sign, but the cost of fighting Apple in court creates enormous pressure to settle
Apple's real goal is twofold: identify the sources through legal discovery, and publicly punish leakers to deter future ones
Cease-and-desist letters from Apple Legal have become an ironic accuracy gauge — receiving one confirms a rumor is real
Apple doesn't need to shut down rumor sites; they just need to make it known that sources won't be protected
money_quotes: These cease-and-desist letters are such a regular occurrence that they've turned into an unofficial gauge of accuracy — if a rumor is posted with screenshots of unreleased Apple software, and the site does not receive a letter from Apple Legal, then the images are widely regarded as fakes.
If you break an NDA to supply me with information, I'm not bound by the terms of the agreement you signed.
Apple doesn't need to shut Think Secret down; they just need to make it known that Think Secret is unwilling to protect sources' confidentiality.
They'll do this by pursuing legal recourse against the leakers in public view, making it clear that loose lips sink careers.

Shutting down individual rumor sites won't do Apple any good; dozens of new ones would replace them. But by making harsh examples out of whoever leaked these rumors, Apple stands a good chance of discouraging other would-be leakers from unsealing their lips in the future.
If Think Secret wasn't prepared for this, it's because they're foolish, not because they weren't fairly warned.

This suit is not about stemming the rampant speculation regarding these specific rumors in the remaining days before the Expo. Cat's out of the bag, the train has left the station, don't cry over spilled milk, etc.
posts.key_insight: Apple's lawsuit against Think Secret isn't about suppressing specific rumors but about identifying and punishing leakers to create a chilling effect that deters future sources from talking.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's lawsuit against Think Secret just days before Macworld Expo 2005, arguing that the leaks are genuinely harmful to Apple rather than secretly beneficial PR. He dismantles the conspiracy theory that Apple planted the rumors, noting that filing bogus lawsuits is illegal and that Think Secret's track record includes damaging inaccuracies. Using the iPod Mini launch as a case study, he shows how Think Secret's wrong $100 price prediction created disappointment when the actual $249 price was announced. He argues that Macworld excitement comes from Apple's track record of great products, not from rumor sites, and that false rumors about pricing or timing can seriously damage product launches.
key_points: Apple's lawsuit against Think Secret is not a PR stunt — filing bogus lawsuits is illegal, and Think Secret's past inaccuracies have demonstrably hurt Apple
Think Secret's iPod Mini price prediction ($100) versus reality ($249) created palpable audience disappointment at the 2005 keynote, proving rumors can set damaging false expectations
Macworld Expo excitement stems from Apple's track record of great product debuts, not from rumor sites — in the leaky pre-Jobs era, there was more accurate rumor reporting but less hype
False rumors are especially dangerous when the real price is higher than rumored, or when rumored products aren't ready to ship, shifting press coverage to what wasn't announced
Gruber correctly predicts the headless Mac won't be called 'iMac' and won't be G5-based, speculating it might be called 'iMac Mini'
money_quotes: One does not use courts of law for PR stunts. It's illegal to file bogus lawsuits.
The rumor sites don't build excitement for Macworld Expo, they take advantage of it.
The excitement surrounding Macworld Expo in the Jobs 2.0 era is because of Apple's — and Jobs's — track record of debuting exciting new products at the show.
When Steve Jobs announced the $249 price during the keynote, the audience's disappointment was palpable.
It is unreasonable to believe that they don't cause Apple any harm, simply because you enjoy reading the rumors.
Trust me, there is no fucking way Apple is releasing a G5-based iMac — screen or no screen — for only $499.
The Jobs 2.0 regime has always struck me as the 'don't get mad, get even' sort.
posts.key_insight: Rumor sites don't generate excitement for Apple — they parasitize it, and their inaccuracies create false expectations that can turn a great product launch into a disappointment.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes the two headline products from Macworld Expo 2005: the iPod Shuffle and the Mac Mini. He argues both are defined by their price points and represent Apple's first serious push into the low end of the market. He admits he was wrong to dismiss pre-release rumors of a screenless iPod, acknowledging he underestimated Apple's ability to redefine component pricing through sheer volume. The Mac Mini, he argues, cleverly cuts corners outside the box (no display, keyboard, or mouse) rather than inside, preserving quality while hitting $499. He sees both products as Apple cashing in its brand equity to dominate the full market spectrum, calling the pair the most aggressive venture of the Jobs 2.0 era.
key_points: The iPod Shuffle is the first Apple product that is cheaper megabyte-for-megabyte than its competition, thanks to economies of scale from anticipated massive sales volume.
Apple's strategy with the Mac Mini was to cut corners outside the box (no peripherals) rather than inside, avoiding the junk quality of competing low-end PCs.
The iPod Shuffle deliberately trades features for storage capacity — offering double the memory of competitors at the same price by eliminating the display entirely.
The Mac Mini ships with inadequate RAM (256 MB) and a user-hostile upgrade path, representing the one significant compromise in an otherwise well-executed product.
Both products represent Apple cashing in accumulated brand value to make its first serious play for the low end of both the music player and computer markets.
money_quotes: For the first time in its history, Apple has begun to make gorgeous, inexpensive things.
Megabyte-for-megabyte, the iPod Shuffle is cheaper than its competition. To my memory, this is the first product in Apple's history where this is so.
My mistake was not realizing that a low-cost iPod wouldn't entail Apple joining the current market for low-end players, so much as Apple redefining the market for low-end players.
What it means to be an iPod is less now than it was a week ago.
It was, to paraphrase one Apple executive, to solve the problem of how to make a $499 computer without it being a piece of junk.
What Apple has done, rather ingeniously, is cut corners outside the box: no display, no keyboard, no mouse.
The one-two punch of the Mac Mini and iPod Shuffle comprise the most aggressive, boldest venture in the Jobs 2.0 era at Apple.
The thinking being that it's better not to offer a feature at all than to offer a frustrating implementation.
posts.key_insight: Apple's genius with the iPod Shuffle and Mac Mini was redefining what gets stripped away at low price points — eliminating the display from one and peripherals from the other — preserving quality where it matters while using brand strength and volume economics to dominate markets they'd never before contested.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier Software Update article with reader feedback and corrections. He addresses two main debates: whether Repair Permissions should be run as routine maintenance (he says no, it's a troubleshooting tool), and whether combo updaters are superior to delta updaters (he says Software Update handles it correctly). He also corrects his claim about the iPod Shuffle being Apple's first price-per-megabyte leader by noting the Xserve RAID, and observes Apple confusingly reusing the term 'dashboard' for both Tiger's widget layer and editing palettes in Motion and iPhoto 5.
key_points: Repair Permissions is a troubleshooting tool, not routine maintenance — if permissions keep changing, something deeper is wrong with your installation
Delta vs. combo updaters should be a non-issue for users who run Software Update regularly, as it automatically selects the right one
Users who mix and match system files between OS revisions create unsupported configurations and then blame the updaters when things break
The Xserve RAID was another Apple product that competed favorably on cost-per-megabyte, contradicting Gruber's iPod Shuffle claim
Apple is confusingly using 'dashboard' to describe both Tiger's widget layer and editing palettes in Motion and iPhoto 5
money_quotes: File permissions and ownership don't have a half-life — they don't rot or 'go bad' or even change over time. If they've changed, some software had to have changed them.
My guess is that most of the people who run into problems with delta updaters have diddled with files they ought not have diddled.
This is madness. I'm not denying that this worked for Mr. Marks, but what he's ended up with is a completely unsupported system configuration.
Apple designs and tests the delta updaters to work against the standard installation of the previous OS revision — once you've started diddling with system files, all bets are off.
Mixing and matching individual files between OS revisions is a recipe for disaster, although the disaster might not occur immediately.
In short, if you use Software Update — and I recommend that you do — the Right Thing should just happen.
posts.key_insight: Most Mac OS X maintenance superstitions — routine Repair Permissions, mandatory combo updaters — are unnecessary for users who keep standard installations and let Software Update do its job.

posts.summary: Gruber provides a business update on Daring Fireball's monetization efforts, covering three areas: T-shirt sales and memberships, sponsorships, and a Bloggies nomination. He frames his goal as becoming a successful full-time independent Mac writer, analogous to independent Mac developers. Membership revenue has exceeded expectations but isn't yet a full salary, and he's adding perks like a members-only mailing list to attract more supporters. The sponsorship program, featuring tasteful text-based ads from Mac-ecosystem companies, is also growing. He emphasizes that the core content remains free and memberships are a way to support that work.
key_points: Gruber is attempting to make independent Mac writing a viable full-time career, modeled on the success of independent Mac developers
The membership model provides perks (RSS feeds, mailing list access, T-shirts) as token rewards, but the real value proposition is supporting free content
His advertising philosophy insists on small, text-based, non-animated ads with a maximum of one per page and only five sponsor slots total
Membership revenue has exceeded expectations but still falls short of replacing a salary
He's adding a members-only mailing list partly to improve signal-to-noise ratio and partly to incentivize fence-sitters to join
money_quotes: If there are successful full-time independent Mac developers, why not successful full-time independent Mac writers, too?
The more readers who support this site by becoming members, the more time I'll be able to spend writing.
The intention isn't that these members-only perquisites are, in and of themselves, worth the price of a membership. What's truly valuable here is the content, and that is free for everyone.
Becoming a member is a way for you to support this free content, and the members-only features are token rewards for doing so.
By keeping ads relatively small, text-based, and non-animated, they're noticeable without being distracting.
I will only accept sponsorships for products or services that I believe will be of interest to Daring Fireball readers, and which are worth their attention.
posts.key_insight: Gruber articulates an early blueprint for sustainable independent web publishing built on reader memberships and tasteful, limited sponsorships — a model that would prove prescient for the creator economy.

posts.summary: Gruber uses a Magic 8-Ball Q&A format to dissect Napster's $30 million Super Bowl ad campaign for its 'Napster To Go' subscription service. He argues that Napster's '$10,000 to fill an iPod' marketing claim is fundamentally dishonest, since almost no one fills their iPod exclusively with purchased music. He points out that $15/month for a subscription actually exceeds what most people spend on music annually, undermining Napster's value proposition. Gruber further argues that Apple could easily launch its own subscription service if the model proved viable, and that the iPod's market dominance creates a self-reinforcing loop that competitors like Napster simply cannot break. The piece concludes that Napster's subscription pivot is essentially an admission of defeat in the pay-per-download market.
key_points: Napster's claim that it costs $10,000 to fill an iPod is misleading because virtually no one purchases all their music from iTMS — most iPod music comes from ripped CDs
At $180/year, Napster's subscription costs more than what most iPod owners actually spend on music, undermining the value argument
Apple's pay-per-download model creates stronger customer lock-in than subscription services, where switching costs are minimal
The iPod's market dominance creates a self-reinforcing loop: iPod popularity drives iTMS success, and iTMS purchases keep users on iPod
Napster's subscription push signals they've conceded the pay-per-download market to Apple
money_quotes: These companies are going out of their way to draw unrealistic comparisons to the iPod and iTunes, and the mainstream media are giving them play not because they really pose a credible threat to Apple, but just because it's an easy way to write a story.
Yes, if you wanted to fill a 40 GB iPod only using songs purchased from iTMS, yes, it would cost $10,000. But it's a ridiculous comparison, because no one is doing that.
$15 a month is $180 per year. That's a lot more than most people spend on music, isn't it?

Apple could easily jump right in and launch their own iTunes subscription service. They'd just make it simpler, and perhaps even cheaper, than everyone else's, and then Steve Jobs would annoy the hell out of the industry by declaring it a 'brand-new' and 'innovative' way to listen to music.

With subscription services, there's very little barrier to switching from one service to another. In short, a popular pay-per-download service seems like a better long-term business than a popular subscription service.
The popularity of the iPod and iTunes has led to the success of the iTunes Music Store; and the more songs people purchase from iTMS, the more likely they are to stick with the iPod and iTunes.
There's nothing that a company like Napster can do that Apple can't match, and there's little Napster can offer to appeal to the customers Apple already has.
posts.key_insight: Napster's subscription marketing is built on a dishonest comparison that ignores how people actually use iPods, and the iPod's dominant market position creates a self-reinforcing ecosystem that subscription competitors cannot penetrate.

posts.summary: Gruber clarifies a widely misunderstood aspect of subscription music services like Napster To Go: when you cancel your subscription, all downloaded music stops working. He argues that $180/year is far more than most people spend on music downloads, noting that most iPod owners spend under $100/year on iTunes. He points out that subscription music still requires per-track fees for burning CDs, and that Napster's marketing deliberately obscures these limitations in fine print. He concludes that people who invest in subscriptions will spend thousands over a decade and own nothing, while CD and iTunes buyers retain their purchases permanently.
key_points: Subscription music services are rental, not ownership — all music stops working when you cancel
Napster To Go's marketing buries the critical limitation that continued access requires maintaining a subscription
Most iPod owners spend well under $100/year on iTunes, making a $180/year subscription a poor value comparison
Most adults already own the bulk of the music they'll ever want from prior CD purchases
A decade of subscription payments yields thousands spent with nothing owned, while purchased music persists indefinitely
money_quotes: If you drop your subscription, all of the music you've downloaded ceases to function.
In other words, with a subscription service, you're renting music, not buying it.
But there's no free lunch.
I buy singles online, but when I buy albums, I buy CDs. I.e. I use iTMS in addition to buying CDs, not instead of.
I think $180 per year is just way more than most people are willing to spend for rented music downloads, regardless how many songs they can download.
Most of us over the age of, say, 25 or so, already own most of the music we're ever going to want.
A kid today who puts his money into a subscription service like Napster To Go is going to wind up spending a couple of thousand dollars over the next decade, and yet own nothing.
posts.key_insight: Subscription music services obscure through marketing what is fundamentally a rental model, and for most consumers the economics of ownership — whether through CDs or iTunes — are far more favorable than perpetual subscription fees for music you never actually own.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that Daring Fireball's members-only RSS feeds are switching from URL-based membership keys to HTTP authentication. The previous system embedded access tokens in the feed URL, which made it trivial for non-members to find and use other people's keys through public aggregators like Bloglines. After consulting with Bloglines, Gruber learned that HTTP authentication was the only way to mark feeds as truly private. He provides detailed instructions for subscribers using various RSS readers, and closes with a measured appeal to freeloaders, noting that $19/year membership isn't much but matters in aggregate.
key_points: URL-based access tokens were inherently insecure because public aggregators like Bloglines exposed the full subscription URLs to anyone who searched
HTTP authentication was initially avoided because many RSS readers didn't support it well, but by early 2005 most major Mac aggregators had caught up
Gruber provides a pragmatic workaround for older aggregators by embedding credentials directly in the URL using the http://user:pass@host format
The members-only feeds don't contain exclusive content — they're a convenience offering full-text and linklog access via RSS as a thank-you to paying members
Gruber addresses bootleggers directly with a tone that's firm but not hostile, framing the issue as one of scale rather than principle
money_quotes: The downside to this scheme, however, is that all of the information needed to access the feeds was available in the URL itself.
Currently the only way that a feed is marked private is if it contains a username/password used for HTTP authentication.
There is no members-only content at Daring Fireball; these feeds are merely a members-only means of accessing the same content that everyone can access for free via the web site.
They're a convenience I offer to members as a token of my thanks for supporting Daring Fireball.
If you're determined to keep using these feeds for free, I'm sure you'll find a way, and I won't stop you.
That works out to less than $1.60 a month — which isn't much to you, but, in the aggregate, means a lot to me.
posts.key_insight: Security through obscurity in URLs fails when public aggregators index those URLs, making proper HTTP authentication necessary even at the cost of user convenience.

posts.summary: Gruber alerts Daring Fireball members-only RSS feed subscribers who use Bloglines that they need to manually mark their subscriptions as private. Even after embedding authentication credentials in the feed URL, Bloglines defaults to making all subscriptions public, which would expose members' login credentials. The post provides step-by-step instructions for both new and existing subscribers to fix this privacy issue.
key_points: Bloglines does not default to treating authenticated feeds as private, exposing membership credentials publicly
Users must manually change the Access setting to 'Private' when subscribing to password-protected feeds
The post provides specific instructions for both new subscriptions and updating existing ones
money_quotes: One would think that a web-based aggregator would default to treating feeds which require a username and password as private — but one would be wrong.
posts.key_insight: Bloglines' default of making authenticated feed subscriptions public is a surprising and problematic design choice that exposes users' credentials.

posts.summary: Gruber presents his four favorite Mac apps of 2004: Interarchy 7 (FTP/SFTP client), Affrus 1.0 (Perl debugger), SpamSieve 2.2 (spam filter), and BBEdit 8.0 (text editor). He provides detailed histories and evaluations of each, with particular emphasis on Interarchy's evolution from Anarchie and its superior attention to detail, Affrus's extraordinary documentation, and SpamSieve's near-perfect accuracy enhanced by AppleScript-based score thresholds. Honorable mentions go to NetNewsWire 2.0, Quicksilver, and OmniWeb 5, though the first two are excluded for being technically in beta and OmniWeb lost out due to performance issues compared to Safari.
key_points: Interarchy 7's browser-style interface, tabs, disclosure triangles, and AppleScript support made it the first satisfying file-transfer app for Gruber in nearly a decade
Affrus 1.0's help documentation, written by Matt Neuburg, is singled out as the finest software documentation Gruber has ever encountered, truly leveraging hypertext
SpamSieve 2.2's accuracy reached 99.8%, and using AppleScript to access raw spam scores rather than binary spam/not-spam classification dramatically reduced manual review burden
Public betas like NetNewsWire 2.0 and Quicksilver stretch the concept of beta software to the point where the distinction from release software may not be meaningful
OmniWeb 5 lost to Safari as Gruber's primary browser due to performance and rendering issues, despite superior features like per-site preferences and tab management
money_quotes: What's great about Interarchy isn't that it has tons of features (although it does), but that its features are so well done.
It's the attention to detail that makes Interarchy a pleasure to use.
There are only two types of 1.0 products: those which ship with missing features, and those which never actually ship.
Help is ignored because it tends to utterly suck. (Apple, I am looking in your direction.)
Ignoring Affrus's help would be tragic, because it not only doesn't suck, it is quite simply the finest software documentation I have ever encountered.
SpamSieve epitomizes the idea of utility software that does one thing, and does it well.
I'm not sure how it can be a 'beta' if more people are using it than are using the non-beta 1.0.x releases.
That assumes Quicksilver is ever going to come out of 'beta'.
posts.key_insight: The best software is defined not by feature count but by attention to detail and doing its core job exceptionally well.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the shared characteristics of his 2004 Mac apps of the year — Interarchy, Affrus, SpamSieve, and BBEdit — and finds three common traits: excellent documentation, strong scripting/extensibility, and tabbed interfaces. He argues that good documentation remains undervalued, that AppleScript support builds loyal user communities even when most users don't write scripts themselves, and that tabbed interfaces are proliferating rapidly but inconsistently across Mac apps. He calls on Apple to standardize tab widgets and interface guidelines before implementations diverge too far. The post closes with the pointed observation that none of these standout apps came from Apple itself.
key_points: All the top apps have thorough, well-written documentation — wikis alone are insufficient for documenting complex software
Scripting and extensibility let power users create custom workflows, and those users share scripts that build community around an app
Tabbed interfaces spread rapidly from browsers to other app categories, but implementations vary wildly in appearance and behavior
Apple should provide standardized tab widgets and guidelines before the inconsistency becomes permanent
None of the year's best Mac apps were made by Apple
money_quotes: Making an app scriptable isn't just a check-off feature — it's about allowing users to check-off their own individual customized features.
Wikis seldom produce complete documentation; they're a good way to aggregate user-contributed tips and tricks, but they're a poor way to completely document a complex app.
Users who do write scripts are almost always willing to share, posting scripts to forums, mailings lists, and weblogs. It helps build a loyal community of users around an app.
Only Apple can prevent this from turning into a permanent free-for-all of disparate and inconsistent appearances and behaviors.
One last thing worth noting about these apps, by the way: none of them came from Apple.
I've written scripts for my own use with these apps that I would never ask for as built-in functions in the apps themselves, but which save me significant amounts of time.
posts.key_insight: The best Mac software distinguishes itself not through flashy features but through the fundamentals — documentation, scriptability, and thoughtful UI conventions — and notably, Apple itself isn't leading by example.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the fallout from Apple's decision to stop including FireWire cables and other accessories with new iPods, arguing that the controversy is more symbolic than substantive. He shows that the price cuts roughly offset the now-à-la-carte accessories, making it a packaging change rather than a technical one. He's more critical of the iPod Shuffle's FAT32-only disk format, which imposes Windows-era file naming restrictions on Mac users. He argues this is a genuinely un-Mac-like compromise that undermines Apple's 'it just works' philosophy, even if most users won't notice because the Shuffle is primarily a music player.
key_points: The FireWire cable removal is a marketing/packaging change, not a technical one — iPods still support FireWire, and price cuts offset the accessory costs
The real constituency being taxed is Mac users with older machines lacking USB 2.0, while the change saves Apple tens of millions on cables most Windows iPod users never used
The à la carte pricing is reasonable for the low-cost iPod Mini but feels stingy for the premium $449 iPod Photo
The iPod Shuffle's FAT32-only format is the more insidious issue, forcing Mac users to deal with archaic file naming restrictions that contradict Apple's ethos
Apple's own suggested workaround of creating zip archives to avoid file name limitations is an un-Mac-like kludge that typical users will never discover
money_quotes: Those cables never were free — you paid for them as part of the total price of the iPod kit.

A couple of bucks here, a couple of bucks there, and with over a million iPods being sold per month, you're talking about tens of millions of dollars on FireWire cables that most iPod users weren't using.
The a la carte accessory pricing seems reasonable with the low-cost iPod Mini; it seems stingy with the high-cost iPod Photo.
With apologies to Sigmund Freud, sometimes a cable is just a cable.
Remember the days when DOS/Windows file name restrictions were a target of mockery? With the iPod Shuffle, Windows' limitations are now ours, too.
But since when is Apple a company that takes the easy way out?

The 'it just works' factor has been one of the hallmarks of the Mac user experience since day one; using an iPod Shuffle as a disk drive forces you to be aware of arcane and bizarre file naming restrictions, and thus is utterly un-Mac-like.
I'm just saying it's a little sad when Apple Computer, of all companies, tells us that punctuation such as '=' and ';' are 'special characters'.
posts.key_insight: The FireWire cable controversy is overblown symbolism, but the iPod Shuffle's FAT32-only format represents a genuine, if minor, betrayal of Apple's 'it just works' philosophy by importing Windows-era limitations onto the Mac.

posts.summary: Gruber documents an irritating quirk in Movable Type 3.0 where the blogging software automatically creates a tiny image file called 'nav-commenters.gif' at the root level of every weblog during rebuilds, regardless of whether the site uses TypeKey authentication or even displays comments. The file persists even when manually deleted, as MT recreates it on every rebuild. Gruber traces the root cause to a design shortcoming: MT had no built-in way to reference images in its static folder from published weblogs, so developers took a shortcut by copying the file into every weblog's public directory. He wrote a plug-in to suppress this behavior, preferring the plug-in approach over directly modifying MT's source code for maintainability. Six Apart later addressed the issue in MT 3.16 with a built-in configuration option.
key_points: Movable Type 3.0+ created nav-commenters.gif at the root of every weblog on every rebuild, even when TypeKey and comments were disabled
The root cause was an architectural gap: MT had no mechanism to link published weblogs to its static images directory, so developers copied the file as a shortcut
Gruber's plug-in works by deleting the file after MT creates it and rewriting the template tag to reference the static images folder instead
Plug-ins are strongly preferred over source code patches because they survive upgrades and can be cleanly uninstalled
The file's ubiquity made it a reliable fingerprint for detecting sites running Movable Type 3.0+
money_quotes: In the grand scheme of things, this is, obviously, not a big deal. But to me, it was maddening, like a sliver of popcorn caught in my teeth.
The presence of 'nav-commenters.gif' is in fact a half-decent way to tell whether a given site is powered by Movable Type 3.0 or later.
MT litters your web space with no other such images. Just 'nav-commenters.gif'.
Ever since MT 1.x, I've been wary of any advice that instructs one to modify MT's source code.
Using plug-ins is a much cleaner way to hack MT's internals. This way, instead of modifying MT's source code, you're simply modifying MT's behavior.
posts.key_insight: Small software design shortcuts — like copying a file everywhere instead of building a proper reference mechanism — create disproportionate irritation, and the right fix respects the architecture rather than working around it.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that the iPod FireWire cable controversy revealed a deeper anxiety among Mac users: the fear that the iPod's success will come at the Mac's expense. He traces how Apple's identity became conflated with the Mac during its years as a one-product company, and explains that the Mac's loyal but static user base of 10-20 million has sustained profits without delivering growth. The iPod's explosive success means the Mac is now one division among several rather than the whole company, which some users experience as abandonment. Gruber dismisses this fear as irrational, noting the Mac remains profitable, and dismantles the argument that Apple should have kept the iPod FireWire-only to boost that standard, pointing out the iPod only became a phenomenon after adding USB support.
key_points: Mac users conflate Apple with the Mac, so iPod success feels like Mac abandonment rather than company growth
Mac OS X sustained the Mac as profitable but never grew the installed base beyond the long-claimed 25 million users
The iPod became a mass-market phenomenon only after adding USB 2.0 support — FireWire-only iPods sold one million in 18 months versus one million in six months after USB
Telling Apple to 'become a consumer electronics company' without specifying products is like telling someone to 'become a millionaire'
Apple's obligation is to shareholders, not to subsidize FireWire adoption through iPod bundling decisions
money_quotes: They say they love Apple, but what they love is the Mac.
If you take it personally that the Mac has perhaps slipped to Apple's second-most-profitable division, you're weird.
Saying that Apple should 'become a consumer electronics company' is like telling someone they should 'become a millionaire'.
This is completely backwards, confusing the horse for the wagon.
People aren't buying new computers just to use with their iPods — they're buying iPods to use with their existing computers.
FireWire, much like the Macintosh, is neither a failure nor a phenomenon. It is simply successful in a nice-sized niche, but not the entire market.
Apple's interest is in selling iPods, not raising the average quality of de facto standard data-transfer buses.
Great means growth, and growth is what drives a stock price up.
posts.key_insight: The iPod's success doesn't diminish the Mac — it transforms Apple from a one-platform company into a multi-division one, and users who conflate their loyalty to the Mac with loyalty to Apple are misreading the situation.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Think Secret's argument that Apple's trade secret lawsuit is merely a bullying tactic against a small publication. He admits his own initial legal analysis was wrong — Apple's case rests on trade secret law, not NDA enforcement, and is likely strong. He specifically targets Think Secret's claim that Apple would never sue The New York Times for publishing the same information, arguing this comparison is nonsensical because the Times would never publish unsubstantiated product rumors in the first place. He further undercuts Think Secret's credibility by documenting their refusal to issue corrections when their iPod Mini color screen prediction proved completely wrong. Gruber concludes that Apple would actually be more likely to sue a major publication, since it would avoid the David-vs-Goliath optics.
key_points: Apple's case against Think Secret is based on trade secret law, not NDA enforcement — a much stronger legal foundation than most commentators assumed
The comparison to The New York Times is fallacious because the Times would never publish unsubstantiated product rumors in the first place
Think Secret's refusal to issue corrections when proven wrong (e.g., the iPod Mini color screen prediction) undermines their claim to legitimate journalism
Apple would likely be more inclined to sue a deep-pocketed publication because it would eliminate the negative David-vs-Goliath publicity
Gruber openly corrects his own earlier analysis, modeling the intellectual honesty he finds lacking in Think Secret
money_quotes: First and foremost, not only *didn't* The New York Times publish such information, they *wouldn't*.
The Times doesn't print unsubstantiated or dubiously-sourced gossipy conjecture on unannounced or unreleased products. That's why they're The New York Times.
Most publications, including The New York Times, would consider a report that "contradicts with reality" to be, you know, an *error*, and thus in need of correction.
At Think Secret, it's good enough that "some" of what they published was "in line with" reality.
My initial armchair legal analysis was way off-base, however.

If The New York Times, or any other deep-pocketed mainstream publication, had published the same information, obtained in the same way, Apple might have been *more* likely to file suit than they were against Think Secret.
A lawsuit against The New York Times would be seen as Apple picking on someone its own size.
When you hear someone claim that if they did, Apple wouldn't sue them, don't believe it.
posts.key_insight: Think Secret's invocation of The New York Times as a shield is doubly hollow: the Times would never publish such material, and if it did, Apple would have even more reason to sue.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses reader pushback on his earlier argument that Apple would have sued even The New York Times over trade secret violations, not just Think Secret. He acknowledges the Times' credibility problems — the Jayson Blair fabrication scandal and the far more damaging Judith Miller/WMD reporting — but argues these are irrelevant to the Apple litigation comparison. The key distinction is that Think Secret's legal trouble stems from accurate reports based on illegally obtained trade secrets, not from bad journalism. Gruber contends that trade secret law offers no exemptions for journalists, and that Times reporters engage in speculation rather than inducing NDA violations.
key_points: The Judith Miller/Chalabi WMD reporting was far more damaging to the Times' credibility than the Jayson Blair fabrication scandal
Think Secret's legal trouble stems from accurate reporting based on trade secrets, not from inaccurate journalism — making comparisons to the Times' credibility problems a red herring
Trade secret law contains no special exemptions for journalists, regardless of how they are defined
The distinction between speculation (legal) and inducing someone to break an NDA (illegal) is the crux of the Apple v. Think Secret case
A court ruling in the related Apple v. Does case confirmed that journalist status is irrelevant under trade secret law
money_quotes: Think Secret's legal troubles don't stem from its numerous *in*accurate reports; they're in trouble over stories that were spot-on accurate.
It is also illegal to publish or share information which you know to be a trade secret, regardless how you learned about it.
There are no special exemptions under trade secret law for journalists.
The bottom line is there is no exception or exemption in either the (Uniform Trade Secrets Act) or the Penal Code for journalists — however defined — or anyone else.
Speculation is not a violation of trade secret law. Inducing someone to break a non-disclosure agreement is.
My natural inclination is to expand every tangent, no matter how digressive. This makes for more comprehensive, less predictable, and more interesting fireballing.
While the Blair scandal was certainly embarrassing, it was nowhere near as damaging to The Times' reputation for trustworthiness as the Judith Miller/Ahmad Chalabi situation.
posts.key_insight: The credibility of a news organization is irrelevant to trade secret litigation — what matters is whether sources were induced to violate legal obligations, not whether the publication is considered journalism.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Judge Kleinberg's ruling in Apple v. Does, which denied the motion to quash Apple's subpoena of PowerPage's email provider. He explains that while the ruling is narrow in scope, its reasoning strongly favors Apple's position that trade secret protections outweigh First Amendment claims by rumor sites. The court found that an 'interested public' is not the same as the 'public interest,' and that journalists have no special license to traffic in stolen trade secrets. Gruber notes the rumor sites' lawyers largely failed to address the trade secret question, focusing instead on broad First Amendment arguments. He frames the core tension as whether trade secret statutes should constitutionally outweigh press freedoms, acknowledging this could become a landmark case.
key_points: The ruling covers only whether Apple can subpoena PowerPage's email provider, but its reasoning has broad implications for Apple v. Think Secret
The court held that California trade secret statutes apply to everyone regardless of profession, with no carved-out exception for journalists or bloggers
Apple's product secrecy has real economic value—leaked products lose the exclusivity and surprise that generate premium publicity like Time magazine covers
The movants' lawyers failed to address the intersection of trade secrets and journalistic privilege, which the court viewed as the essential issue
The distinction between an 'interested public' and the 'public interest' was central to the court's rejection of the rumor sites' arguments
money_quotes: An interested public is not the same as the public interest.

Rights are not absolute. Recall Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes: 'The right to swing my fist ends where the other man's nose begins.' What the court is saying here, more or less, is that the right to publish information about Apple ends where their trade secrets begin.
Just because you have a desire to know doesn't mean you have a right to know.
There is no inherent 'right' for the public to know what Apple, or any other private enterprise, is up to behind closed doors.
When skilled lawyers largely ignore an essential issue that the Court specifically inquires about, it sends a message they have little to say on the subject.

The rumor sites, the EFF, and their attorneys apparently want to argue these cases solely in terms of the First Amendment. But Apple sees this as a matter of trade secret law, and with this ruling, the court agrees with Apple.
This is the judge's way of saying that the movants are making a very broad argument, and pretty much have nothing.
posts.key_insight: The court's distinction between public curiosity and genuine public interest undermines the rumor sites' First Amendment defense, reframing the case as a straightforward trade secret violation rather than a press freedom battle.

posts.summary: Gruber documents his troubleshooting of a 90-second login delay on his iBook running Mac OS X 10.3, caused by a corrupted font cache. After ruling out login items and corrupt preferences, he used ssh from a second Mac to run top during login and identified ATSServer (Apple Type Services) consuming excessive CPU. Deleting the bloated 64MB fontTablesAnnex cache file restored normal login times, though the file kept growing back suspiciously. He notes this kind of Unix-level debugging is beyond most Mac users' abilities, and readers suggested fast user switching as an alternative to needing a second computer.
key_points: A corrupted or bloated fontTablesAnnex cache file (64MB vs normal 1MB) caused ATSServer to consume excessive CPU during login, producing a 90-second SPOD delay
Standard troubleshooting steps (disabling login items, deleting preferences) failed — only Unix tools like ssh and top revealed the real culprit
The fix may be temporary: the cache file grew from 1MB back to 32MB within two days, suggesting an underlying font issue rather than simple cache corruption
Fast user switching provides a single-machine alternative to ssh for monitoring login processes with top
The episode highlights the gap between Mac OS X's friendly surface and the Unix internals sometimes needed to diagnose problems
money_quotes: Under ordinary use, it's convenient and natural to think of Mac OS X as "the system". But on the inside, in terms of how things really work, there's no such thing.
As a Unix-y operating system, the Mac OS X "system" is really a bunch of small processes that work together, each of them doing their own little thing.
This strikes me as a troubleshooting session that most Mac users never could have managed.
Most have never even heard of top or ssh, let alone would have known how to use them to see what was going on while the SPOD was displayed.
Those of you who religiously delete font caches every time you install a system or security update, feel free to smirk smugly.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's user-friendly surface sometimes conceals problems that can only be diagnosed by diving into its Unix underpinnings, exposing a gap between the Mac experience and the expertise required to fix it.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier piece about Mac OS X login delays caused by damaged font caches, synthesizing reader reports to identify the root cause. The bug was introduced in ATS.framework version 1.8.5, shipped with Mac OS X 10.3.6 in October 2004, which causes fontTablesAnnex cache files to grow without bound. The bug disproportionately affects designers and others with PostScript fonts installed, with one reader reporting 1 GB of cache files and another suffering 14-minute login times. Gruber presents a reader's thorough investigation confirming ATS now scans every font in all font folders regardless of activation status. He advises waiting for Apple's fix in the expected 10.3.9 release rather than replacing system framework files, and recommends manually clearing font caches in the interim. An update confirms 10.3.9 shipped with the fix.
key_points: The font cache bug was introduced in ATS.framework 1.8.5, shipped with Mac OS X 10.3.6, as a side effect of other font-related fixes
The bug causes fontTablesAnnex files to grow without bound but does so slowly, making the adverse effects sneak up on users over weeks or months
The bug appears to only affect users with PostScript fonts installed, hitting designers disproportionately hard since they almost invariably use PostScript fonts
One reader confirmed via fs_usage that ATSServer was scanning every single font regardless of activation status, and successfully worked around it by reverting to the older ATS.framework
Gruber advises against replacing system components and recommends waiting for Apple's 10.3.9 fix while manually managing font caches
money_quotes: These font caches grow endlessly, but they tend to grow slowly, and so the adverse effects tend to sneak up on you.
Designers are the hardest-hit demographic simply because they almost invariably have numerous PostScript fonts installed.

This is not to say there's anything wrong with PostScript fonts. Or, if you've been hit, that there's anything damaged or corrupt with your PostScript fonts. This is a bug in Mac OS X, plain and simple.
That it worked for him is proof that it's a bug in version 1.8.5 that is causing these problems; that doesn't mean replacing OS components with versions from previous OS updates is a wise idea.
Feel free to substitute 'issue' for 'bug' if you prefer the euphemistic parlance of Apple's release notes.
One reader reported finding over 1 gigabyte of 'fontTablesAnnex' files; and in terms of login delays, designer Cameron Moll reported that his login time had grown to 14 minutes.
posts.key_insight: A single framework update in Mac OS X 10.3.6 introduced an unbounded font cache growth bug that silently degraded performance over months, disproportionately punishing the design community that Apple most depends on as advocates.

posts.summary: Gruber provides follow-up notes on a Mac OS X 10.3 font cache bug he previously reported. He clarifies that PostScript fonts aren't the sole cause of bloated cache files, as some readers experience the issue without them. He discusses third-party utilities like Font Finagler and OnyX that can clear font caches, while noting he prefers manual deletion. The key technical insight is that ATSServer opens every font in system font folders on login, which triggers the bug, meaning third-party font managers that store fonts outside system folders effectively sidestep the issue.
key_points: PostScript fonts exacerbate the font cache bloating bug but aren't the only cause, contrary to initial assumptions
ATSServer opening every font in system font folders on login is the root mechanism behind the cache bloating
Third-party font managers like Suitcase avoid the bug by storing fonts outside the system's font folders
Apple's Font Book stores even disabled fonts in system font folders, so it doesn't protect against the bug
money_quotes: I personally can't see spending $10 to delete a few files which are easily removed by hand (and, frankly, I feel more comfortable trashing them manually anyway).
The cache bloating is somehow related to the fact that on login, ATSServer opens every font located in the system's font folders.

Apple's Font Book does not itself cause this problem (at least I don't think it does), but it doesn't avoid it, either, because when you use Font Book, it stores all of your fonts, including your disabled fonts, in the system's font folders.

The point here is not that you should buy Suitcase just to avoid this bug, but rather to explain why, if you're already using Suitcase or a similar utility, you probably haven't been affected by this bug.
posts.key_insight: The Mac OS X font cache bug stems from ATSServer loading every font in system folders at login, which means third-party font managers that store fonts elsewhere are inadvertently immune.

posts.summary: Gruber promotes an Amazon pre-order deal for Mac OS X 10.4 Tiger that includes a $35 rebate, bringing the price to $95. He has placed an affiliate link in the site's banner, framing it as a way for readers to save money while supporting Daring Fireball. He estimates around 90 percent of his readership plans to upgrade. The offer is US-only, with no availability yet on Amazon's Canadian or UK stores.
key_points: Amazon offers Mac OS X Tiger for pre-order with a $35 rebate, bringing the cost to $95
Gruber uses affiliate links as a revenue model for Daring Fireball, being transparent about it
He assumes the vast majority (90%) of his audience will upgrade to Tiger, reflecting the enthusiasm of the Mac community
money_quotes: If you're planning on upgrading to Tiger (and I'm guessing somewhere around 90 percent of you are), this is a good way to save yourself $35 and support Daring Fireball at the same time.
posts.key_insight: Gruber leverages his audience's near-universal enthusiasm for Mac OS upgrades to transparently promote an affiliate deal that benefits both readers and his site.

posts.summary: Gruber updates a previously posted AppleScript for opening multiple files together in a single new BBEdit text window. The original script required workarounds due to BBEdit 8.0's limited scripting support for its documents drawer. BBEdit 8.1 added an 'opening in' parameter to the open command, making the script much simpler. The new version is more concise, intuitive, and faster than the original. He also notes Chris Nandor's updated Perl version of a similar script.
key_points: BBEdit 8.1 added an 'opening in' parameter with options for front_window, separate_windows, and new_window, simplifying multi-file opening
The updated AppleScript is significantly shorter and cleaner than the BBEdit 8.0 workaround version
Gruber uses the script with Ranchero's Big Cat Scripts plug-in for Finder contextual menu integration
money_quotes: BBEdit 8.1 makes this a lot easier, by adding an optional opening in parameter to the open command.
This script is not only more concise and intuitive than the version from September, but executes a bit quicker as well.
posts.key_insight: BBEdit 8.1's improved scripting support for the documents drawer eliminated the need for workarounds, turning a clunky multi-step AppleScript into an elegant one-liner.

posts.summary: Gruber examines two contrasting perspectives on Mac OS X from prominent technologists: Paul Graham's essay celebrating hackers switching to Macs, and Tim Bray's threat to 'unswitch' away from the platform. While largely agreeing with Graham's thesis, Gruber corrects the implication that talented programmers only recently discovered the Mac, noting GUI programmers were there all along. He then dismantles Bray's case for leaving Mac OS X, arguing that Bray's complaints about Apple's secrecy are hollow and his specific technical gripes (Safari issues, screen quality) don't justify abandoning the platform. Gruber methodically uses Bray's own previous enthusiastic writings about the Mac against him, highlighting the inconsistency of his position.
key_points: Mac OS X's dual nature makes it uniquely appealing: it's the Mac OS with Unix under the hood for traditional Mac users, and Unix with a superior GUI for hackers migrating from Linux

Graham's thesis that hackers are switching to Macs is correct, but his implication that the Mac user base was reduced to 'graphic designers and grandmas' in the '90s ignores the developers who were writing the software those users relied on
Bray's case for 'unswitching' rests on Apple's corporate secrecy rather than any actual technical deficiency of the platform, which Gruber finds absurd as a basis for choosing a computing environment
The Unix hackers switching to Mac OS X are specifically those who care about usability and want their tools to just work, as opposed to tinkerers whose hobby is the computer itself
Gruber turns Bray's own enthusiastic 2002 PowerBook review against him, showing that none of the qualities Bray praised have deteriorated
money_quotes: From the perspective of your typical user, it is the Mac OS with a modern Unix architecture encapsulated under the hood; from the perspective of the hackers Graham writes of, it is Unix with a vastly superior GUI.
GUI programming is hard, and for GUI programmers, the Mac has always been, in Brent Simmons's words, 'The Show'.

The problem hackers had with Mac OS 7-9 wasn't that it was bad — but that it wasn't good for the tasks they cared about. And in a hacker's mindset, if a computer isn't good for his particular brand of hacking, that computer isn't good, period.
Rather than picking a system based on its quality or suitability for your needs, pick it based on the level of 'secrecy' maintained by the company that makes it.
I said 'Unix nerds who care about usability'. People who want a Unix system that just works, so they can get on with their real work — those are the ones who are switching.
It is the Mac and it is Unix, at the same time.
Using Firefox doesn't make you less of a Mac OS X user.
'Infofascism' seems like an awfully loaded term considering the rather hollow charges Bray has levied.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's unique power is that it doesn't force a choice between Mac usability and Unix capability — it delivers both simultaneously, which is why hackers who care about getting work done are adopting it while those whose hobby is tinkering with the OS itself never will.

posts.summary: Gruber discusses the fundamental difference between how Mac OS X's Finder and Windows handle folder replacement: the Finder deletes the old folder entirely, while Windows merges the two folders' contents. He notes this difference frequently catches platform switchers off guard, citing Matt Mullenweg's loss of a photo folder as a recent example. Gruber reiterates his earlier proposal that the Finder should move replaced items to the Trash rather than permanently deleting them, which would prevent catastrophic data loss and enable undo. He also criticizes Windows for inconsistently using 'replace' to mean 'merge' for folders but 'overwrite' for files.
key_points: Mac's Finder deletes the old folder when replacing, while Windows merges folder contents — a difference that surprises platform switchers in both directions
Matt Mullenweg lost a folder of photos because he assumed Mac's Finder would merge folders like Windows does
Gruber proposes that Finder replacements should move old items to the Trash instead of permanently deleting them, enabling both safety and undo
Windows' behavior is internally inconsistent: 'replace' means merge for folders but overwrite for files, which Gruber considers a terrible design
money_quotes: In the Finder, when you replace one folder with another, the old folder is deleted. On Windows, the two folders are merged.
For reasons I've never seen anyone even attempt to explain, Windows calls this action 'replacing.'
When the Finder replaces a folder or file, it should move the replaced items to the Trash instead of instantly nuking them.
Not only would this eliminate catastrophic mistakes, but it would also allow replacements to be undoable.
It's a terrible idea for 'replacing' to mean two entirely different things, depending solely on what it is you're replacing.
posts.key_insight: Folder replacement should move old items to the Trash rather than permanently deleting them, making the operation both safe and undoable.

posts.summary: Gruber notes that Apple has announced April 29 as the ship date for Mac OS X 10.4 Tiger, and Amazon now offers the family pack 5-user version for pre-order alongside the single-user version. He highlights a $50 rebate form available but not prominently advertised on the family pack product page. The post serves as both a news brief and an affiliate pitch, encouraging readers to purchase through his Amazon links to support Daring Fireball.
key_points: Apple set April 29, 2005 as the official Tiger release date
Amazon made the 5-user family pack available for pre-order alongside the single-user version
A $50 rebate form exists but is not mentioned on the family pack product page, only linked from the single-user page
Gruber promotes purchasing through his Amazon affiliate links to support the site
money_quotes: Amazon's product page for the family pack doesn't mention it, but the rebate form here can save you $50.
Buying the Tiger family pack via the links at the top of this page will both save you some money and help support Daring Fireball via Amazon's filthy associates lucre.
posts.key_insight: A brief consumer tip pairing Tiger pre-order news with an easy affiliate pitch, delivered with Gruber's characteristic self-aware candor about monetization.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Adobe's official FAQ about their acquisition of Macromedia, translating each piece of corporate PR-speak into blunt English. He argues the deal is fundamentally about eliminating competition rather than creating synergy, noting that Adobe is buying its only significant rival in several product categories. He highlights the inevitable job cuts hidden behind euphemisms, the death of competing products like Freehand, and the meaninglessness of promises to serve customers better. The piece also takes shots at the Bush administration's permissive stance on mergers and Adobe's ironic use of PDF to distribute the FAQ.
key_points: The acquisition is primarily about Adobe buying out its only significant competitor in design software, not about innovation or synergy
Corporate euphemisms like 'reduction in force' and 'complementary product lines' mask inevitable layoffs and product eliminations
The Freehand-vs-Illustrator rivalry, one of the longest-running in the design industry, will simply end through consolidation rather than competition
Adobe's repeated non-answers about which products will be eliminated reveal the uncomfortable truth they're trying to avoid stating publicly
The merger will reduce customer choice and competition, despite PR language claiming the opposite
money_quotes: Dude, we just bought the only significant competitor to several of our flagship applications. We didn't buy Macromedia, we bought the market.
Where by 'complementary' we mean 'the two leading technologies that irritate people when they're used in lieu of regular web pages.'
Macromedia's board sees this as an opportunity to cash out.
Please also note that PDF is an excellent format for sending out résumés.
Competition is overrated — it only benefits customers.
This isn't good for customers at all.
You think the current version of Acrobat Reader takes too long to launch, runs too slowly, and uses too much memory? You ain't seen nothing yet.
We know that what you really want to know is which programs are going to get the axe. But we're not going to tell you — that's why we've repeated this same exact blurb twice.
