posts.key_insight: Corporate acquisition FAQs are exercises in saying nothing while obscuring the uncomfortable truth that buying your competitor eliminates the competition that benefits customers.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that the Amazon affiliate links on Daring Fireball now properly support international Amazon stores for Canadian and U.K. readers. Visitors who set their preferred Amazon location in the site's preferences page will see Tiger pre-order links pointing to the correct regional store. He notes that while international discounts aren't as generous as the U.S. pricing, they still beat Apple Store prices in those regions. The post is essentially a brief site-housekeeping update combined with a thank-you to readers who support the site through affiliate purchases.
key_points: Daring Fireball's Amazon affiliate links now respect user-selected regional preferences for Canada and the U.K.
International Amazon discounts on Tiger aren't as large as U.S. ones but still undercut Canadian and U.K. Apple Store prices
Gruber thanks readers who support the site by purchasing through his affiliate links
money_quotes: The discounts aren't quite as sweet as those offered by Amazon.com here in the U.S., but they still beat the prices from the Canadian and U.K. Apple Stores.
It's a great way to support this site and save yourself a few bucks at the same time.
posts.key_insight: A minor site update reflecting Gruber's attention to detail in serving his international readership while transparently leveraging affiliate revenue to sustain Daring Fireball.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Adobe's decline as a great software company traces directly to CEO Bruce Chizen, a marketing executive whose main passion is salesmanship rather than technology or design. Using an Economist profile of Chizen published days before the Macromedia acquisition, Gruber contends the deal reflects Adobe's drift toward becoming bigger rather than better. He draws a parallel to Steve Jobs's critique of Apple during his exile — that 'sales guys' ruined the company by prioritizing profits over innovation. Gruber points to the cynical 'Creative Suite' rebranding, Adobe's embarrassingly poor web presence and typography, and stagnating products as evidence that Chizen's Adobe has lost the engineering passion that made it great. Rather than expanding into untapped creative markets, Adobe is chasing the enterprise software market at the expense of the creative professionals who built the company.
key_points: Adobe's Macromedia acquisition reflects years of strategic drift under a sales-focused CEO, not a bold new direction
The 'Creative Suite' rebranding and version number games insult Adobe's core audience of creative professionals who see through marketing gimmicks
Adobe's own website and PDF materials demonstrate shockingly poor typography and design from the company that invented digital typography
Steve Jobs's diagnosis of Apple's decline under 'sales guys' maps precisely onto Chizen-era Adobe: monopolist behavior, profit-chasing, and product mediocrity
Adobe's best products were built by engineering-driven leadership; its acquired products (PageMaker, GoLive, FrameMaker) have languished or died
money_quotes: His passion isn't for creating great products; it's for convincing us that Adobe's products are great.
What's wrong with being a nicely profitable $2 billion-a-year company that dominates the software market for creative professionals?

Rather than expand into untapped creative markets, Adobe seems hell-bent on expanding into the jerks-wearing-suits market, a market that's completely at odds with the creative market they've dominated for nearly two decades.
The entire 'Creative Suite' bundle reeks to high hell of marketing for the sake of marketing.
The entire 'CS' campaign has the feel of a company that is marketing down to its customers, rather than treating them respectfully as peers.
Like most sales guys, Chizen seems like the sort of person who believes that what matters most is not the quality of the product itself, but merely the marketing in which you package it.
If that spirit continues to wither, Adobe will continue its slide into mediocrity, and will become just another software company.

How is this anything other than wretched? In what way is it acceptable for Adobe Systems, the company that more or less invented digital typography as we know it, to publish a web site that borders on illegibility?
posts.key_insight: When a company built on engineering excellence and creative passion hands the reins to a sales executive, the rot starts at the top and the products inevitably follow.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a new format for covering Mac OS X 10.4 Tiger: a continuously updated list of interesting details and observations compiled on a single page over the course of a week. Rather than writing a traditional review, he's creating a temporary mini-weblog dedicated to non-obvious Tiger details, complete with its own RSS feed. He invites reader submissions but sets clear editorial standards, emphasizing that he's looking for details Apple hasn't already touted — not keynote demos or well-known features. He assumes readers have already consumed John Siracusa's comprehensive Ars Technica review as baseline knowledge.
key_points: Gruber experiments with a continuously-updated single-page format rather than a traditional review, reflecting early blogging's evolving editorial forms
The focus is specifically on non-obvious details — things Steve Jobs didn't demo and Apple hasn't marketed — valuing discovery over coverage
He sets unusually specific submission guidelines, including that well-written notes get priority and email to this address is attributable by default
The reference to Siracusa's Ars Technica review as assumed reading establishes a shared baseline among the Apple-focused readership
money_quotes: In other words, it's sort of like a temporary mini-weblog dedicated to details about Tiger.
What we're looking for here are interesting details that aren't obvious and which Apple hasn't touted.
If it's something Steve Jobs demoed during his Macworld keynote, it's not what we're looking for.
And let's assume everyone has already read John Siracusa's Tiger review at Ars Technica.
Notes that are well-written are more likely to be used than those which aren't.
I'm trying to list only the most interesting details, but what I find interesting is obviously highly subjective.
posts.key_insight: The most interesting things about a major OS release aren't what the company promotes but the non-obvious details discovered through careful use.

posts.summary: Gruber details his quest to integrate Mac OS X 10.4's new Dictionary app with BBEdit, Mailsmith, and TextWrangler, since Dictionary's built-in lookup features only work in Cocoa text fields. He catalogs the limitations: no contextual menu in Carbon apps, no useful AppleScript dictionary, and broken dict:// URL support. He presents two workarounds — assigning a keyboard shortcut to the Dictionary Services menu item, and a more sophisticated AppleScript that uses GUI Scripting to automate lookups from the current word at the insertion point. He also recommends PreFab UI Browser as essential for writing GUI scripts.
key_points: Dictionary.app's lookup features (contextual menu, keyboard shortcut) only work in Cocoa NSTextView fields, excluding popular Carbon-based editors like BBEdit
The Services menu workaround is simple but requires manual text selection; GUI Scripting enables automatic current-word detection without selection
Dictionary lacks a proper AppleScript dictionary for lookups, and its dict:// URL scheme is unreliable — a clear oversight by Apple
PreFab UI Browser is practically essential for GUI scripting work, far surpassing Apple's own UI Element Inspector
BBEdit 8.2.1 later added native support for the Command-Control-D Dictionary shortcut, making Bare Bones the first third-party developer to do so
money_quotes: Dictionary's primary advantage over online dictionaries — including Sherlock's — is that its database is stored on your computer, and thus is always available; online dictionaries are useless to an offline laptop.
To be truly useful as a writing aid, you need to be able to invoke your dictionary easily from the app in which you're writing.
I'm so lazy that I don't even want to have to select a word before doing a look-up on it.
Trying to accomplish something with GUI scripting without using UI Browser is like trying to walk around blindfolded.
It's a completely valid gripe that Dictionary ought to provide a proper AppleScript command for performing look-ups, and I hope this gets addressed in a future update.
But in the meantime, GUI scripting gets the job done today.
posts.key_insight: When Apple's tools fall short of proper integration, resourceful scripting workarounds can bridge the gap — but the need for such workarounds reveals real shortcomings in Apple's developer story.

posts.summary: Gruber shares his experience with Mac OS X 10.4.0 (Tiger), which he considers the buggiest release since the 10.1.x days. He highlights a specific bug encountered while logging out, where a dialog box appeared after all applications had quit — presumably asking if it was OK to quit when nothing was running. He finds philosophical humor in the absurdity of the empty confirmation dialog.
key_points: Mac OS X 10.4.0 was notably buggy, the worst since the 10.1.x era
Logout process was particularly problematic, triggering nonsensical dialog boxes
An empty or absurd confirmation dialog appeared after all apps had already quit
money_quotes: In my experience, 10.4.0 has been the buggiest release of Mac OS X since the 10.1.x days.
Admittedly, there is a sort of zen perfection to this dialog.
Perhaps it wasn't a bug at all, but rather, a life lesson.
posts.key_insight: Even Apple's software quality stumbles sometimes, and the most absurd bugs can take on an unintentionally philosophical quality.

posts.summary: Gruber dismisses the latest round of Apple-to-Intel switch rumors, which stem from a Wall Street Journal report citing unnamed industry executives. He acknowledges Apple has legitimate grievances with IBM's G5 production — Steve Jobs promised 3 GHz within a year of WWDC 2003, and two years later they're stuck at 2.7 GHz — but argues the enormous software compatibility hurdles make the switch implausible. The core dilemma: Apple can't pre-announce the switch without killing current hardware sales, but can't spring it without software. He suggests the rumors are more likely planted leaks to pressure IBM, and predicts he'll be writing about the same rumors again in 2007.
key_points: The WSJ sources are unnamed 'industry executives' not confirmed to work at Apple or Intel, making this a rumor column item at best
Software compatibility is the insurmountable hurdle: all Mac OS X software would need recompilation, and emulation of G5 on Intel would be unacceptably slow
Apple faces an impossible marketing paradox — pre-announcing kills current sales, but not pre-announcing means no third-party software at launch
The rumors likely represent planted leaks to pressure IBM into improving G5 production rather than genuine switch plans
Apple could plausibly use Intel chips for non-Mac products like iPods or next-gen AirPort hardware
money_quotes: Every 18-24 months, the tech industry will be captivated by unsubstantiated rumors that Apple is switching the Macintosh to Intel processors.
So, yes, there's a motive for Apple to consider such a switch. But that doesn't make it plausible.
The minute Apple announces they're moving to x86 processors, sales of current hardware dry up. Who's going to spend $3000 for a deprecated CPU architecture?
My advice is to pay no heed whatsoever to any Macintosh-to-Intel rumors that don't address this issue.
The fact that it's technically possible doesn't mean there's a good business case for such a move.
Reading between the lines, I think this is less about whether Apple actually intends to switch processors, and more about planted leaks intended to spur IBM.
My prediction: I'll be writing about this again in 2007.
posts.key_insight: Technical feasibility is not the same as business plausibility — the marketing paradox of pre-announcing a processor switch makes the Apple-Intel rumors far less credible than they appear.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Mac OS X Tiger's adoption rate among Daring Fireball's Safari users by mining his server logs for user-agent strings. The data shows Tiger climbing from near-zero on its April 29 release day to roughly 70% of Safari visitors within a month. He compares this to the same analysis he did for Panther in 2003 and calls the adoption rates roughly a tie. He also cross-references Omni Group's software update statistics, which show similar but slightly lower Tiger adoption at 44.5%. The post closes with a playful note that Intel CPU share among Mac users sits at zero — 'for now' — foreshadowing the imminent Intel transition announcement.
key_points: Safari's user-agent string reveals which OS version a visitor runs, enabling OS adoption tracking from server logs alone
Tiger reached ~70% adoption among DF's Safari users within one month of release, comparable to Panther's adoption curve
Gruber is careful to caveat that his audience skews heavily toward early-adopting Mac enthusiasts and is not representative of the broader Mac user base
Omni Group's independent data corroborates the trend but shows lower Tiger adoption (44.5%), likely reflecting a more typical user base
The closing Intel CPU remark — 'zero, for now' — is a deliberate tease days before Apple's Intel switch announcement at WWDC 2005
money_quotes: Daring Fireball readers almost certainly do not comprise a representative sample of the overall Mac user base.
Web server log analysis is an inexact science.
The adoption rate for Tiger seems to be slightly less than that of Panther, but, considering the sloppy accuracy of server log analysis, I'm scoring it as a tie, for now.
Which, if you do the math, leaves Intel at zero, for now.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X Tiger was adopted by the enthusiast Mac user base at roughly the same rapid pace as Panther, with about 70% of Safari-using DF readers upgrading within a month of release.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the rumors of Apple switching to Intel processors days before WWDC 2005, raising skeptical questions about the reports from CNet and The Wall Street Journal. He notes a semiconductor analyst's claim that Intel chips would actually cost Apple more than IBM/Freescale processors. He questions the lack of technical details in the reports, suggests the leak may have been strategically planned to build WWDC hype, and mocks Robert Scoble's vague 'confirmation.' Gruber's own prediction is that Intel will produce PowerPC chips for Apple rather than Apple switching to x86, though he hedges with a one-dollar bet.
key_points: A semiconductor analyst argues Apple pays less for IBM/Freescale chips than Intel would charge, undermining the cost-saving rationale for switching
Neither CNet nor the WSJ provided any technical details about the supposed switch, which Gruber finds suspicious
The Friday-before-WWDC timing suggests a deliberate leak designed to generate keynote buzz
Switching to little-endian x86 would create significant byte-order headaches for developers whose code assumes big-endian PowerPC
Gruber's prediction: Intel will produce PowerPC chips for Apple, not a full architecture switch
money_quotes: On Friday morning, no one was talking about WWDC; by Friday night, everyone was.
How could someone 'confirm' this story but yet not know the answers to these questions? I suspect that if Apple announces anything related to Intel, Scoble will claim he was in on it.
Whatever they do announce will have to compete for headline space with 'Apple Not Switching to Intel Chips'.
Here's my bet: Intel is going to produce PowerPC chips for Apple. But I'm only betting one dollar.
Switching to the little-endian x86 would be extremely irritating for any developer with a code base that makes assumptions about byte order.
posts.key_insight: The Apple-Intel rumors lack technical substance, and the strategic timing of the leak suggests Apple is orchestrating buzz rather than genuinely having its plans exposed.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes the weekend reports from CNet and the Wall Street Journal that Apple will announce a switch from PowerPC to Intel processors at the Monday WWDC keynote. He emphasizes that both outlets are reporting this as fact, not rumor, meaning either Apple is making the biggest hardware transition in its history or two respected publications have committed catastrophic reporting blunders. While he previously considered the switch improbable, he finds it even less likely that the Wall Street Journal would be this wrong. He argues the reported switch makes no sense on its own — performance and cost arguments don't hold up — so there must be something more to the story. He speculates about alternatives like Intel manufacturing PowerPC chips or software emulation via Transitive Technologies, noting a NeXT connection on Transitive's board.
key_points: Both CNet and the Wall Street Journal are reporting the Intel switch as fait accompli, not rumor — leaving no wiggle room if wrong
The reported transition timeline (Mac Mini mid-2006, Power Mac mid-2007) risks the Osborne effect and contradicts Apple's historical pattern of introducing new processors at the high end first
Neither performance nor cost arguments for switching hold up under scrutiny — G5 clock speeds have actually increased faster percentage-wise than Pentium 4
Gruber speculates Intel might manufacture PowerPC chips instead of Apple switching to x86, which would avoid developer disruption and OS-on-commodity-hardware risks
The NeXT connection to Transitive Technologies, a binary translation company, hints at how emulation could make an x86 transition feasible
money_quotes: No matter how unlikely I think it is that Apple is about to move the Mac to Intel processors, I think it's even less likely that the Wall Street Journal would publish a story this wrong.

What this means is that something extraordinary must occur: either Apple is going to announce the biggest hardware transition in company history (and, arguably, industry history), or, two well-respected news organizations have committed enormous blunders.
If it doesn't happen, this is a catastrophic reporting blunder. There is no 'maybe' in either report.

The only way this makes any sense is that there's something else. Something big. Not that CNet and the Journal have the story wrong, but that they only have part of the story — and the part they don't have is what's going to knock our socks off.
If there's nothing more to the story — if it's just a case of Apple switching the Mac from PowerPC to x86 Intel processors — it makes no sense.
Some argue that it's about cost, but I have yet to see a single such argument backed by actual numbers — the cost per unit Apple would save after switching.
posts.key_insight: The certainty of the reporting forces acceptance that the Intel switch is real, but the reported facts alone don't add up — meaning the real story must be bigger than what's been leaked.

posts.summary: Gruber reports a detail buried on page 67 of Apple's Universal Binary Programming Guidelines: Classic (Mac OS 8/9) applications will not be supported under Rosetta on Intel-based Macs. He notes this hasn't been reported elsewhere or officially announced by Apple. While he doesn't find it surprising, he recognizes it as a definitive end to the Classic Mac era. He later confirms Apple has no plans for Classic support on Intel Macs.
key_points: Apple's Universal Binary Programming Guidelines explicitly list Mac OS 8/9 apps among software Rosetta will not translate
Rosetta's limitations also exclude AltiVec code, G4/G5-specific apps, kernel extensions, and certain Java applications
The omission of Classic support on Intel Macs marks the definitive end of the Classic Mac OS era
Apple made no official announcement about dropping Classic — it was buried in developer documentation
money_quotes: I don't think this is surprising, but it's certainly the end of the Classic Mac era.
I have confirmed that Apple has no plans for Classic support on Intel-based Macs.
posts.key_insight: Apple quietly buried the death of Classic Mac OS compatibility in a developer PDF, marking the definitive end of an era without fanfare.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's announced transition from PowerPC to Intel processors, examining why he was wrong to doubt it would happen. He had believed Apple couldn't emulate PowerPC software at acceptable speeds on Intel and wouldn't risk the Osborne Effect of pre-announcing a platform shift. Rosetta, Apple's dynamic binary translation technology, solved the first problem by allowing existing PowerPC apps to run on Intel Macs. The Osborne Effect remains a real concern but Apple is absorbing the hit from a position of financial strength, buoyed by iPod revenue. Gruber concludes the switch was a pragmatic choice: IBM couldn't deliver on its PowerPC roadmap, and Apple decided to act from strength rather than wait and be forced to act from weakness.
key_points: Rosetta's dynamic binary translation is the essential enabling technology for the Intel transition, acting as an 'unsung hero' that lets existing PowerPC apps run without user intervention
The Osborne Effect will hurt Apple's Mac sales in the short term, but Apple's financial position and iPod revenue make it survivable rather than existential
Apple chose to transition from a position of strength rather than wait and risk being forced into it from desperation, as IBM failed to deliver promised G5 speeds or a G5 PowerBook
Universal Binaries benefit both Intel and PowerPC Macs, meaning current Mac owners aren't being abandoned
Rosetta is far less comprehensive than the old 68K emulator — it handles only applications, not drivers or system extensions, and is all-or-nothing per process
money_quotes: Rosetta is not just a side note or afterthought; it is an essential component in the transition strategy. It is not going to be marketed with trumpets and banners. Rosetta's role is that of the unsung hero.
Apple is marching into this transition chin-first, and they're simply going to take their lumps.
I'm guessing for anyone genuinely familiar with the semi-conductor industry, it was as ludicrous as a proposal for Coca-Cola to start producing Pepsi.

The thinking seems to be: better to act now, from a position of strength, and absorb the costs of the transition while the company is doing well, rather than wait a few years and risk being forced to act from a position of weakness or desperation.
I think it boiled to a choice between two difficult options: either initiate a painful and expensive transition to Intel processors, or stick with PowerPC and fall behind.
This transition period is going to be hard on Apple, not hard on Mac users.
Unlike the Classic environment — which runs apps in a visual ghetto — most users may not even notice that a few of their apps are running under Rosetta.
posts.key_insight: Apple's Intel transition was a calculated gamble from a position of strength — absorb the pain of platform migration now rather than be forced into it later when IBM's PowerPC roadmap fails to deliver.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's switch to Intel processors represents a strategic partnership, not a prelude to acquisition as Robert X. Cringely speculated. He dismisses the idea that Intel would buy Apple or that Steve Jobs would ever cede control, noting that Jobs sees himself as a company-founding visionary in the mold of Walt Disney, not a hired executive. The partnership is complementary: Intel gains an innovative hardware partner willing to break new ground, while Apple gains reliable chip supply and competitive performance. Gruber draws a parallel to how Apple built Mac OS X on Unix — giving up building everything from scratch to concentrate on what differentiates them. He acknowledges the deal could lead to a powerful alliance against Microsoft, but insists a merger would add nothing beyond what a symbiotic partnership already provides.
key_points: Steve Jobs would never accept a subordinate role in an acquisition — he sees himself as a company founder, not a hired CEO, making any buyout scenario implausible
Intel and Apple are natural complements: Intel needs an innovative partner to push chip usage beyond incremental PC upgrades, while Apple needs reliable supply and competitive performance
The Intel switch mirrors Apple's adoption of Unix for OS X — outsourcing the commodity layer to focus engineering on the user experience that differentiates them
Apple's previous PowerPC benchmarks weren't dishonest but were selectively timed to moments when they had a performance lead
The partnership could challenge Microsoft's dominance, but a full merger would only antagonize Intel's existing customers and add no real benefit over a symbiotic relationship
money_quotes: Jobs doesn't want to take over Disney — he wants to turn Apple into a Disney-esque company.
Jobs thinks of himself as a peer to Walt Disney, a visionary who creates something of his own.
Intel would like to see a bit less evolution and a bit more revolution in the way its chips are being used. Apple is that type of company.

By switching to a mostly industry-standard Intel architecture, Apple obviously has no chance of ever surpassing the PC industry in performance. But the other side of that argument is equally true: they'll never again fall behind, either.
In a nut: if you can't beat them, join them.
This doesn't mean Mac hardware will simply be 'a PC with a pretty case' any more than that Mac OS X is merely 'Unix with a pretty UI'.
In the end, it's the product that matters, not the underlying technology.
Cringely, as he often does, started with a good idea but took it one click too far, turning the pundit dial to 11 when 10 would have done just fine.
posts.key_insight: The Apple-Intel partnership mirrors Apple's adoption of Unix — outsourcing commodity infrastructure to concentrate on the user experience that actually differentiates the product.

posts.summary: Gruber announces his inclusion in Joel Spolsky's compilation 'The Best Software Writing I,' with his essay 'The Location Field Is the New Command Line.' He notes the essay is freely available online and he received only nominal payment, but considers the inclusion an honor. He praises the book's typography, particularly its use of Sabon typeface. The post also celebrates a Father's Day gift of 'The Stanley Kubrick Archives' and humorously notes his friend Jim Coudal received the same book, and closes with a playful endorsement of a children's book.
key_points: Gruber's essay was selected by Spolsky, not self-nominated, and he considers it one of his pieces with lasting relevance
All essays in the book are freely available online, making the purchase purely about the curated collection and physical artifact
Gruber's standard for agreeing to participate was the book's visual design quality — he'd rather not be published than be published in a poorly-designed setting
The book is set in Sabon, Gruber's favorite typeface, leading to his formula: good writing + good typesetting = a pleasure to read
The Stanley Kubrick Archives is celebrated as a masterpiece of physical book design, complete with actual film strips from 2001
money_quotes: I'd rather not see my work published at all than see it published in a poorly-designed setting.
Good writing + good typesetting = a pleasure to read.
Sabon is the font family I'd choose to take with me to a desert island.
This book is why they make coffee tables.
It's one of those cases where it really is an honor just to be included.
He's writing acclaimed essays at an age when I did little more than sit around getting baked and playing NHL Hockey '93 on Sega Genesis.
Just in case Spolsky subsequently edits a second volume, I will say he's a very handsome and clever fellow.
posts.key_insight: For Gruber, the physical design and typography of a book are not secondary concerns but essential to whether the writing is worth publishing at all.

posts.summary: Gruber documents his hack of Apple's Dashboard Weather widget to display the time of its last data update. His frustration stemmed from not knowing whether the displayed temperature was current or stale, since the widget only syncs periodically while visible. The hack rearranges the layout to combine high/low temperatures on one line and adds a timestamp showing when data was last fetched. He provides detailed step-by-step instructions for modifying the widget's HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files, while emphasizing the importance of working on a copy of the original. The post doubles as a mini-tutorial on DOM scripting and widget architecture.
key_points: The Weather widget only updates while displayed, meaning users often see stale data for several seconds after invoking Dashboard
The hack repurposes existing HTML elements to show high/low temps on one line and adds a last-updated timestamp below the city name
Gruber emphasizes keeping variable and ID names accurately descriptive even in short hacks, treating it as a best practice against confusion
Dashboard widgets are architected as empty HTML frameworks filled dynamically by JavaScript via DOM scripting
The data object already contained the update time internally but Apple's original widget never displayed it to the user
money_quotes: My frustration was that I wanted to know at a glance whether I needed to wait for updated data. So I hacked it.
I can't emphasize this strongly enough: Make a copy, then hack the copy.

Even in a short hack such as this one, it's a good idea to keep your variable / ID names accurately descriptive; it's a recipe for confusion to have a div named "lo" which contains a time, not a temperature.
This is pretty much how most widgets are architected: the HTML is an empty framework, and the contents are filled in by JavaScript that modifies the HTML document dynamically via DOM scripting.
The original widget never displays this time, but it uses the time to decide whether the current data is old and needs to be refreshed.
posts.key_insight: Apple's Weather widget already tracked when its data was last refreshed but inexplicably hid this from the user, a small UX oversight that a simple three-file hack could fix.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's decision to merge the iPod Photo line into the standard iPod lineup, eliminating the monochrome models and simplifying the product range. He speculates the merge happened because color screens became cheap enough to replace monochrome ones, or because Apple wanted to harvest early-adopter margins before dropping prices. The piece pivots into a broader argument about simplification as the core of iPod's success, contrasting Apple's streamlined lineup with Creative Technology's bloated roster of over 20 form factors. Gruber dismantles Creative CEO Sim Wong Hoo's belief that Apple wins through marketing spend, arguing instead that Apple wins through product quality, simplicity, and ruthless pruning of older models.
key_points: The iPod Photo merger was less about the Photo flopping and more about color displays becoming cheap enough to be the default across the entire lineup
Apple's simplified product lineup — three sub-brands with two sizes each — is a deliberate competitive advantage that's deceptively hard to achieve
Creative Technology's CEO fundamentally misdiagnoses Apple's success as marketing spend rather than product simplicity, leading to a doomed 'more features' strategy
Every decision forced on a customer is another chance for them to walk away — Creative's 20+ form factors exemplify this problem
The retirement of Chicago 12 as the iPod system font marks the end of a visual lineage to the original Macintosh, replaced by Apple's corporate font Myriad
money_quotes: The next time Apple comes out with a next-generation iPod for $500 or $600 (can you say "wireless"?), wait nine months and see if you can save $200.
Every decision you force a customer to make is another chance for them to decide to just walk away.
Creative would be better off flushing that $100 million right down the fucking toilet than spending it on advertising that promotes their products as "packed with more features".
Emphasizing the length of your feature list works when you're selling to the corporate IT market. But "more features" is a terrible message for the consumer market.
This is the major reason behind the iPod's success: not because it has a lot of buttons, but because it only has a few, and they feel good when you press them.
It's not enough to add new models — you've got to discontinue old ones, even ones which are still selling modestly.
The music on FM radio pretty much totally sucks, which is a big reason why people are buying iPods in the first place.
It seems so easy from the outside, but I suspect it's very difficult to achieve, as evidenced by the muddied and complicated product lineups at most PC and consumer electronic companies.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iPod dominance stems not from marketing budgets or feature lists but from the discipline of simplification — fewer products, fewer buttons, fewer decisions for the customer.

posts.summary: Gruber examines how Apple successfully kept its iPod lineup refresh and Intel processor switch secret from the rumor mill. He dismisses claims that Apple's lawsuits created a 'chilling effect' on rumor sites, arguing instead that Apple simply plugged its leaks effectively. He mocks Think Secret for retroactively claiming credit for predicting the iPod changes based on a vague, inaccurate earlier report. Most remarkably, the Intel switch — arguably the biggest Mac news in a decade — was kept almost entirely secret, with only Paul Thurrott catching wind of it as an aside at a Windows conference. Gruber graciously credits Thurrott for the scoop despite their adversarial history.
key_points: Apple's iPod refresh completely escaped the rumor mill, proving effective leak-plugging rather than any legal 'chilling effect'
Think Secret's attempt to claim prescience about the iPod changes is undercut by the vagueness and inaccuracy of their original prediction
The Intel switch was kept secret from the entire Mac community — developers, rumor sites, and commentators alike — until the Wall Street Journal broke it
Paul Thurrott, an unlikely source for Apple scoops, casually mentioned the Intel switch a month before it became public, likely from Intel-side sources
Gruber admits he was wrong about the Intel rumors and gives credit to Thurrott despite their long-running disagreements
money_quotes: Note to crackpots: this is not a "chilling effect" of Apple's lawsuits; to say so implies the rumor sites had information, but were intimidated from publishing it.
This is, simply, the result of good old-fashioned leak-plugging.
In Think Secret's world, this story qualifies as accurate.
This was quite probably the biggest Mac news in 10 years, and the Mac rumor sites had nothing.
Yes, the same Paul Thurrott whom I've long-berated as being always wrong regarding all things Apple. Except, this time, he was right. (And I was wrong.)
The entire Mac community was genuinely in the dark about this.
There were literally gasps at the WWDC keynote when Jobs unveiled the slide that said, "It's true."
But at least in some small circles that don't (or didn't) intersect with the Mac universe, it was an open secret.
posts.key_insight: Apple's ability to keep major announcements secret was the result of disciplined leak-plugging, not legal intimidation, and the biggest scoops sometimes come from the most unexpected sources outside the usual echo chamber.

posts.summary: Gruber examines Apple's rushed but strategically brilliant entry into podcasting with iTunes 4.9, arguing that shipping early with bugs was the right call because it instantly made Apple the dominant player. He catalogs the shortcomings — broken ITMS feeds, missing UI options, iPod firmware regressions — but contends these are acceptable tradeoffs for speed to market. He notes the enormous branding windfall of the word 'podcasting' itself containing 'pod,' which forces competitors into impossible naming dilemmas. He also surveys the gaps in podcast creation tools and RSS standards compliance issues, concluding that Apple's willingness to fix problems after shipping is more important than getting everything right upfront.
key_points: Apple shipped podcasting support faster than expected for a company its size, accepting known bugs as the cost of beating competitors like Odeo to market
The word 'podcasting' is a permanent branding advantage for Apple, leaving competitors to choose between using a rival's name, coining an alternative nobody will adopt, or ignoring the trend entirely
Apple's podcasting strategy is a loss-leader: free iTunes and free podcast downloads drive iPod hardware sales, where the real profits are
iTunes's RSS parser quirks matter enormously because its dominance means 'works with iTunes' will become the de facto standard, much as IE defined HTML compatibility
Apple's lack of podcast creation tools is a minor gap — most people want to consume podcasts, not produce them — but it represents an opportunity for indie Mac developers
money_quotes: Real artists ship, and one of the main tricks to shipping software is knowing when to say, 'This is good enough.' Recognizing what qualifies as 'good enough' is art, not science.
All of a sudden, iTunes 4.9 has turned an entire class of software into an unnecessary middleman.
It's not because Odeo moved slowly; it's because Apple moved fast.
Apple has flipped the old Gillette maxim — they're making money selling the razors (iPods), not the blades.
Can you imagine Apple using a term like 'walkmancasting', 'dellcasting', or 'wincasting'? It's embarrassing.
If podcasting continues to grow and turns into something big, the simple fact that the name includes 'pod' is a significant and permanent advantage in Apple's favor.
It's easier to ask forgiveness than to seek permission — and, let's face it, Apple was not going to seek permission from anyone on something like this.
Apple is in a position to do whatever they want regarding podcasting, but for the most part, it appears that what they want to do is the right thing.
posts.key_insight: Apple's decision to ship podcasting support early with known bugs was strategically correct because speed to market — combined with the branding windfall of the word 'podcasting' itself — created a dominant position that no amount of polish could have matched.

posts.summary: Gruber explains his approach to implementing footnotes on Daring Fireball, addressing the unique challenges of footnotes in web content versus print. In print, readers can glance to the bottom of a page and back, but web articles scroll continuously, making footnote navigation disorienting. His solution uses clickable superscript references that jump to notes at the bottom, with a Unicode leftwards arrow with hook (↩) at the end of each note to jump back. He considered dynamic HTML approaches like tooltips or inline expansion but preferred the traditional bottom placement because it feels right and works in RSS readers without requiring CSS or JavaScript.
key_points: Web footnotes face a fundamental UX problem that print footnotes don't: there are no discrete pages, so readers lose their position when scrolling to notes
The back-link solution using Unicode ↩ is elegant because it adds no visual noise while solving the navigation problem
Gruber deliberately chose the simplest implementation (relative anchors only) over dynamic HTML techniques to ensure compatibility with RSS feed readers
The design philosophy prioritizes quiet, standards-based solutions over clever JavaScript tricks
money_quotes: A weblog post — especially longish ones like those here at Daring Fireball — is very different from a printed page in a book.
By placing the notes at the bottom of the article, they're in some way more like endnotes than footnotes.
Rather than add needless noise to the page, instead of using a textual link, I'm simply using a bit of Unicode typography.
This works pretty well and looks pretty good, and it solves the 'how do you get back to the text from the footnote' problem concisely and quietly.
I like the traditional placement at the bottom. It just feels right to me.
This design has the added benefit of requiring no CSS or JavaScript whatsoever, just relative anchors.
Some users of pre-XP versions of Windows may just see a box instead of a hooked arrow. My heart bleeds.
posts.key_insight: The best web design solutions often involve adapting print conventions with minimal, standards-based mechanisms rather than reaching for complex dynamic techniques.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses a common complaint about Apple's Dashboard Weather widget: users outside the U.S. often get weather for the wrong city because same-named cities aren't disambiguated. The fix is to press Return after typing a city name, which triggers input validation and presents matching options. However, the widget gives no indication that this step is necessary, and its mouse-oriented design leads users to click Done without validating. Gruber argues this is a clear UI design failure, since the right way to do something should be the obvious way. He proposes that validation should happen as-you-type, like Google Suggest, and the Done button should only enable after validation completes.
key_points: The Weather widget shows weather for the wrong same-named city if users don't press Return to trigger validation before clicking Done
The problem disproportionately affects non-U.S. users who can't use Zip codes and have been led to believe the widget is U.S.-only
The widget's mouse-oriented Dashboard design actively misleads users away from the keyboard-based validation step
Gruber proposes as-you-type validation and disabling the Done button until input is validated as the proper solution
money_quotes: The right way to do something should be the obvious way.
One of the core principles of UI design is that you should never allow for invalid or ambiguous input.
The confusion here is caused by a decidedly poor UI design.
The Weather widget provides absolutely no indication that you need to hit return after typing your city name.
Input validation has to be invoked by the keyboard, but dismissing the configuration panel with the Done button has to be done with the mouse.
posts.key_insight: When a UI requires a non-obvious step to function correctly, that's not a user error — it's a design failure, because the right way to do something should always be the obvious way.

posts.summary: Gruber examines different approaches to presenting notes on the web, comparing sidenotes (margin notes) and footnotes. He highlights two well-done CSS-based sidenote implementations while explaining why he chose footnotes for Daring Fireball. He argues that web designers should learn from centuries of print typography rather than defaulting to pop-ups and tooltips. He also responds to Joe Clark's critique of his footnote design, agreeing that HTML lacks proper semantic markup for notes, but pushing back on Clark's claim that his back-arrow glyph choice was wrong.
key_points: Sidenotes eliminate the problem of helping readers connect notes to their referenced text, but break down when notes are long or closely spaced
Both footnotes and sidenotes are superior to pop-up and tooltip-style inline notes, drawing on hundreds of years of proven print design
HTML's lack of semantic markup specifically for footnotes and sidenotes is a glaring omission that standards bodies have neglected
Unicode characters like ↩ and ★ offer a useful middle ground between punctuation and iconography, but poor Windows rendering has limited their adoption on the web
The left-pointing hooked arrow ↩ means 'back' in web convention (dating to Mosaic), not 'move left' as Clark argued
money_quotes: No, the web is not print, but that doesn't mean web designers can't build upon and learn from traditional typography and print design.
Both footnotes and sidenotes are well-represented in good design from the past few hundred years.
That there exists no HTML tag syntax specifically appropriate for notes is a glaring omission, and but one that standards wonks don't seem to care about.
I love using Unicode characters for purposes such as this — it feels like a cross between punctuation and iconography.
The only reason these characters exist in Unicode is that they're supposed to be, well, used.
I chose '↩' not because it implies moving left, but because it implies moving back. And a left-pointing arrow has meant 'back' ever since Mosaic.
posts.key_insight: Good web typography should build on centuries of print design convention rather than reinventing annotation from scratch, and the web's failure to provide semantic markup for notes remains a significant oversight.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Cory Doctorow's alarm over Apple's use of a TPM chip in the Intel Developer Transition Kit. The TPM is being used solely to prevent Mac OS X from running on non-Apple hardware — exactly what Apple publicly stated it would do. Doctorow extrapolated this into a dystopian scenario where applications would use TPM to lock users out of their own data, but Gruber argues there is zero evidence Apple intends anything of the sort. He points out that both Windows Vista and the Linux kernel also include TPM support, undermining the idea that its mere presence is sinister. Gruber compares the unfounded hysteria to McCarthyism and criticizes Tim O'Reilly for amplifying it uncritically.
key_points: Apple's TPM usage in the Developer Transition Kit serves a single purpose: tying Mac OS X to Apple hardware, exactly as Apple publicly announced.
Doctorow's scenario — where TPM enables apps to lock users out of their own files — is entirely fabricated with no supporting evidence.
Both Windows Vista and the Linux kernel include TPM support, making it absurd to single out Apple as uniquely threatening.
Good intentions don't excuse spreading unfounded fear — influential voices carry responsibility for accuracy.
Open file formats matter more than free software, and Apple's TPM usage does nothing to threaten them.
money_quotes: There is no indication, none, zero, not even a whiff, that Apple intends to enable, let alone encourage, developers to create software with the TPM file-access authorization-locking described by Doctorow above. None.
This is not about third-party software developers limiting access to your data. This is about Apple limiting access to their operating system.
There is a word for these people. That word is asshole. No, wait, zealot. OK, there are two words for these people.

The idea that Doctorow's critique, despite being unfounded, was good for the community simply because of its anti-DRM slant is like saying McCarthyism was good for the country simply because of its anti-Communist slant.
If a file is written to disk in an open format, no TPM chip can prevent you from opening it in another program.
TPM support, in and of itself, is no more or less evil than support for any other chips on a motherboard.
The only 'proposed DRM' that would get in the way of any 'freedom to switch' exists only in completely fabricated scenarios from Doctorow himself.
posts.key_insight: The mere presence of a TPM chip used as a hardware dongle for OS licensing is fundamentally different from using TPM to restrict users' access to their own data, and conflating the two is irresponsible fearmongering.

posts.summary: Gruber documents a rendering inconsistency in Safari where Unicode black star characters (★) used as permalink markers on Daring Fireball's Linked List displayed differently depending on which fonts were installed on the system. The culprit was the Armenian font Mshtakan, which contained an ugly, differently-shaped star glyph that Safari would use as a fallback instead of the better glyph from Hiragino Kaku Gothic Pro. Firefox and Camino handled this correctly by always choosing the same star glyph regardless of available fonts. Gruber's workaround was to specify preferred font families in CSS for the permalink class. He also notes that Safari's ATSU rendering option fixes the issue but introduces poor kerning elsewhere.
key_points: Unicode fallback font selection in Safari/WebKit varied by system depending on which fonts were installed, producing inconsistent rendering of the same character
The Mshtakan Armenian font contained the worst black star glyph of all available fonts, yet the system preferentially selected it over better alternatives like Hiragino Kaku Gothic Pro
Gecko-based browsers (Firefox, Camino) handled this more gracefully by always selecting the same glyph regardless of installed fonts
The fix was a targeted CSS font-family declaration to guide WebKit toward better font choices before it could fall back to Mshtakan
The underlying issue was likely in Mac OS X's font fallback system rather than WebKit specifically, as TextEdit exhibited the same behavior
money_quotes: It's a less angular, more childish star.
What sucks about this is that of all the fonts that contain a glyph for this character, Mshtakan is the only font that could be considered a poor choice.
Only in Mshtakan is it significantly smaller, and only in Mshtakan is it a differently-shaped star.
I consider this more desirable than Web Kit's 'I'll show you different stars depending on which fonts you have available' behavior.
I can't recommend turning Safari's ATSU rendering option on, however, as it results in generally unsightly kerning.
posts.key_insight: Even a single misguided font fallback choice in the operating system's Unicode rendering can undermine visual consistency across browsers, revealing how much typographic detail matters in web design.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights GUIdebook, Marcin Wichary's comprehensive graphical user interface gallery that documents screenshots of UI elements across platforms. He announces an interview he gave to GUIdebook discussing Mac OS X's user interface, covering topics like the first-run experience, the Dock, brushed metal windows, and the Finder. He notes that Wichary even unearthed a screenshot of Apple's proposed purple 'single window mode' button from the original Aqua demo.
key_points: GUIdebook is praised as a uniquely comprehensive and obsessive collection of GUI screenshots and documentation
Gruber participated in an interview covering key Mac OS X interface elements including the Dock, brushed metal, and the Finder
The interview surfaces obscure UI history like Apple's proposed purple 'single window mode' title bar button from the first Aqua demo
money_quotes: GUIdebook is both interesting and amazingly comprehensive, and there's nothing else like it.

Marcin even managed to dig up a screenshot of the purple 'single window mode' button that Apple had proposed putting in the title bar of every single window in the first public demo of the Aqua user interface.
posts.key_insight: GUIdebook represents the kind of obsessive, comprehensive documentation of GUI design history that Gruber values, and the interview surfaces forgotten details from Mac OS X's interface evolution.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Google is fundamentally an advertising company, not a platform company or software company, despite widespread speculation about Google challenging Microsoft's dominance. He dismantles the NYT's claim that Google is building an indispensable developer platform by defining what a true platform is — something that can't be removed without breaking what's built on it — and noting that any app using Google search could trivially switch to Yahoo. He contends that the real competitive battle is Google vs. Yahoo in advertising, not Google vs. Microsoft in software, and that newspapers with their classified ad monopolies should feel more threatened by Google than Microsoft does.
key_points: A true platform is something you can't remove without breaking what's built on it — Google search doesn't meet this definition since apps can trivially switch to Yahoo or MSN
Companies should be defined by where their profits come from: Apple is a hardware company, Microsoft is a software company, and Google is an advertising company
Google's software and search results serve the same purpose as editorial content in newspapers — they're an excuse to show ads
The real competitive battle is Google vs. Yahoo in advertising, not the hypothetical Google vs. Microsoft rivalry everyone obsesses over
Newspapers with artificially high classified ad prices from local monopolies are the incumbents most threatened by Google's targeted local advertising
money_quotes: What makes something a platform is that you can't take it away without the stuff that's built on it falling down.
"Follow the money" is as good a way as any to define a company: the point of business is to profit.
This is why Apple is not, and has never been, a software company: their profits come from hardware sales — computers, and, now, iPods.
Business-wise, Google's software is just an excuse to show ads.
Google's search results and apps like Gmail serve the same purpose as the editorial content in magazines and newspaper.
Google-vs.-Microsoft may well be the battle everyone wants to see; but the battle that's actually going on, for real, today, is the obvious one: Google-vs.-Yahoo.
Ignoring it to focus on a purely-hypothetical-at-this-point Google-Microsoft rivalry is like ignoring astronomy to gab about flying saucers.
posts.key_insight: Defining companies by their profit source rather than their products reveals that Google is an advertising company competing with Yahoo and newspapers, not a platform company threatening Microsoft.

posts.summary: Gruber announces his new project: CSS syntax checking scripts for BBEdit and TextWrangler that integrate with the W3C's CSS Validation Service. The scripts combine Perl and AppleScript to send CSS content to the W3C validator and display errors in BBEdit's results browser. He explains that he delayed building this for years due to his aversion to screen-scraping, which was necessary because the W3C validator lacks a proper web services API. Despite the fragility of parsing HTML output, the tool works well enough to ship.
key_points: The scripts provide W3C CSS validation integrated directly into BBEdit/TextWrangler's results browser interface
The implementation relies on screen-scraping because the W3C CSS Validation Service has no proper API
Screen-scraping is fragile — the W3C's output HTML isn't always well-formed, and format changes could break the parser
Gruber delayed the project for years specifically because of his distaste for screen-scraping as a technique
money_quotes: I've had the idea for this for a couple of years, but I put off hacking it together because I have a strong aversion to screen-scraping.
The W3C CSS Validation Service is a wonderful tool, but, alas, it has no proper API for calling it as a web service.
The problems with screen-scraping are obvious: if the W3C changes the HTML format of the validator's output, my parser may break.
Believe it or not, their output isn't always well-formed HTML.
A real web-services API for the validator would be welcome.
That said, it seems to work pretty well.
posts.key_insight: Even useful developer tools sometimes require ugly compromises like screen-scraping when proper APIs don't exist, and the irony of the W3C's own validator producing malformed HTML is not lost on a detail-oriented developer.

posts.summary: Gruber writes a comedic screenplay-style piece dramatizing the announcement of iTunes 5's new UI theme, which replaced the Brushed Metal look with a new, unnamed dark interface. The piece anthropomorphizes Mac OS X's UI themes as Hollywood characters — Brushed Metal as a panicking actor learning he's been replaced, his agent Mike breaking the news, and Aqua as a drunk, gloating has-been. The humor comes from mapping the real design politics of Apple's evolving interface guidelines onto entertainment industry backstabbing and ego. It's a sharp commentary on Apple's inconsistent UI philosophy during the mid-2000s transition period, when multiple competing visual styles coexisted without clear rationale.
key_points: iTunes 5 introduced a new unnamed UI theme that replaced Brushed Metal, signaling a shift in Apple's design direction
The new theme is described as a hybrid — like Unified Title and Toolbar but darker, smoother, with custom window corner radii that violate HIG conventions
Apple's UI inconsistency is satirized: Brushed Metal still clings to Finder, iChat, and Calculator while losing flagship apps
The piece highlights how Apple's own apps routinely flouted the Human Interface Guidelines they published for developers
The Aqua theme's drunken cameo underscores how far Apple had drifted from its original OS X visual identity
money_quotes: Whatever happened to 'Once you go metal, you never go back?'
This guy trashes the HIG the way Johnny Depp trashes a hotel room.
He even sports a custom radius on his window corners. No other window on the system has a shape like this.
When is the Special Event scheduled for the next version of Calculator? Oh, that's right, there is none, because no one gives a shit about Calculator.
I'm the bad-ass theme. I'm the one who flouts the Human Interface Guidelines.
Man, he doesn't even have a name. How's that for bad-ass?
He's like Unified but darker. His color is a lot like yours, but smooth and shaded like Unified. And thinner. No borders. It's a very chic look.
posts.key_insight: Apple's mid-2000s UI inconsistency — multiple competing visual themes with no unifying rationale — was so absurd it could only be properly captured as a Hollywood backstabbing drama.

posts.summary: Gruber documents a subtle but maddening bug in AppleScript where the 'path to' command returns incorrect results when placed inside a 'tell application "System Events"' block. The command ignores the 'from user domain' parameter and always returns the Local domain folder instead. This is particularly problematic because System Events is the recommended way to script file operations, yet placing 'path to' inside its tell block silently produces wrong results without any error. The workaround is to call 'path to' outside the tell block and pass the result in. Gruber notes that the Finder does not have this bug, and files a Radar report.
key_points: The 'path to' command inside a System Events tell block silently ignores the 'from user domain' parameter, always returning the Local domain path instead
System Events is the recommended alternative to Finder for scripting file operations, making this bug especially problematic since it affects the canonical approach
The bug produces no compilation or runtime error — it silently returns the wrong folder, making it extremely difficult to diagnose
A simple code cleanup like moving a statement into a tell block can introduce this bug without any visible indication that behavior has changed
money_quotes: This is particularly insidious because inside a 'tell application "System Events"' block is exactly where you're likely to want to get the path to a folder.
This sort of bug is precisely why so many people despise AppleScript.
Not only does it make no sense, but something like this can be maddeningly difficult to track down.
Now your script is broken — but you may not notice immediately, because it doesn't break with a compilation or runtime error.
Instead, just by moving the 'path to' statement inside the System Events tell block, it silently starts returning a different (and wrong) 'Application Support' folder.
I have to assume this is a bug in System Events. If this isn't a bug, but instead expected behavior, I'd love the explanation.
posts.key_insight: AppleScript's worst bugs are the silent ones — where syntactically valid code produces wrong results without any error, and where following best practices (using System Events over Finder) is exactly what triggers the failure.

posts.summary: Gruber methodically documents his experience discovering Dell's new flash-memory music player, the DJ Ditty, intended to compete with the iPod Shuffle. Through a numbered list format, he notes that Dell barely promotes the device on its own website, that it bears an unfortunate resemblance to a cheap lighter, and that its advertised storage capacity relies on encoding music at an absurdly low bitrate. The piece culminates in smug satisfaction that the tech industry's long-held advice that Apple should be more like Dell has proven spectacularly wrong.
key_points: Dell released the DJ Ditty to compete with the iPod Shuffle but barely promoted it on their own website, suggesting even Dell wasn't proud of it
The Ditty's claim of fitting 220 songs in 512MB relies on 64kbps WMA encoding — half Apple's default bitrate — making it a misleading marketing comparison
The contrast between Apple's prominent, luscious product photography and Dell's hidden, lighter-shaped device perfectly illustrates the design gap between the companies
A decade of punditry urged Apple to be more like Dell, yet Dell's attempt to be more like Apple produced an embarrassment
money_quotes: Begin to suspect that even Dell is not very proud of this device.
Note prominent and primary emphasis on luscious product porn of new iPod Nano.
Note vague resemblance to a 50-cent Bic lighter.
Roughly equivalent in fidelity to that of transmissions carried over tin cans and string.

Anyone with such profoundly bad taste in industrial design who would consider purchasing this device probably also has such bad taste in music as not to notice that their 64 kbps-compressed songs sound like mush.

Sit back and recall, with tremendously smug satisfaction, a decade's worth of tech industry punditry holding that superior design would never get Apple anywhere, and that Apple should instead, you know, be more like Dell.
posts.key_insight: Dell's embarrassing attempt to compete with the iPod — a product so bad even Dell wouldn't promote it — vindicated Apple's longstanding bet that design and taste matter more than commodity economics.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects an AppleScript bug where System Events returns the wrong folder for a `path to` command, revealing it as a scripting dictionary terminology conflict between StandardAdditions and System Events, both of which define `user domain` with the same Apple Event code. He walks through several workarounds of increasing complexity, ultimately requiring knowledge of raw four-character Carbon folder codes. The bulk of the essay is a sustained critique of AppleScript's English-like syntax, arguing that its resemblance to natural language creates more confusion than it resolves by obscuring the actual computational semantics. He contrasts AppleScript unfavorably with Python and JavaScript, whose more abstract syntaxes make the underlying mechanics clearer and thus are actually easier to use.
key_points: The bug is a terminology conflict: System Events and StandardAdditions both define `user domain` with the same Apple Event code but different semantics, and System Events' definition takes precedence inside its tell block.
AppleScript's English-like syntax makes two fundamentally different operations — a global command with parameters vs. accessing a property chain — look nearly identical to human readers.
Python and JavaScript are actually easier than AppleScript despite being less English-like, because their more abstract syntaxes make the underlying mechanics obvious rather than obscured.
Scripting additions pollute the global namespace, creating a minefield of potential terminology conflicts that produce no compile-time or runtime errors.

The complete workaround requires both knowledge of the terminology conflict and looking up raw four-character Carbon codes in developer documentation — a far cry from AppleScript's promise of approachability.
money_quotes: It was a grand and noble idea to create an English-like programming language, one that would seem approachable and unintimidating to the common user. But in this regard, AppleScript has proven to be a miserable and utter failure.
The language syntax is optimized for English-likeness, rather than being optimized for making it clear just what the fuck is actually going on.

This is why Python and JavaScript, two other scripting language of roughly the same vintage as AppleScript, are not only better languages than AppleScript, but are easier than AppleScript, even though neither is very English-like at all.
AppleScript's slavish devotion to English-likeness gives us two very different syntax constructs that read, to humans, as though they're semantically similar.
So you've got `to path to`, and one of the `to`'s is a language keyword, and the other is an application keyword.

The way AppleScript lets you throw in superfluous `the`'s and `a`'s makes it seems as though AppleScript just doesn't care about 'little words', which is true in a handful of cases, but completely untrue in others.
AppleScript is actually a tiny language, with extraordinarily few keywords, but the standard scripting additions turn it into a bigger language, ripe for terminology conflicts.
posts.key_insight: AppleScript's English-like syntax, designed to make programming approachable, instead obscures the underlying computational semantics so thoroughly that it makes the language harder to use than more abstract alternatives like Python and JavaScript.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that he has been working full-time at Joyent, a startup building web-based applications for small businesses, since the beginning of 2005. He clarifies that Daring Fireball is not his full-time occupation, joking that readers might expect more regular posting if it were. He mentions Joyent's presence at the Web 2.0 Conference and credits collaborators Cameron Moll and Paige Pooler for their work on the Joyent website.
key_points: Gruber confirms Daring Fireball is a side project, not his full-time job
He joined Joyent full-time in early 2005, shifting away from freelance design and development
Joyent is building web-based applications for small businesses in the Web 2.0 era
The Joyent website was a collaborative effort with designer Cameron Moll and illustrator Paige Pooler
money_quotes: No, I do not write Daring Fireball full-time. (One would hope the publication schedule would be slightly less erratic if I were.)
I'm no longer working as a freelance designer and developer.
We're so "Web 2.0" that we're at the Web 2.0 Conference.
posts.key_insight: In 2005, Gruber transitioned from freelancing to full-time startup work at Joyent while maintaining Daring Fireball as a side project, offering a glimpse into the early Web 2.0 startup era.

posts.summary: Gruber catalogs his observations from Apple's October 2005 special event, covering the new iPod with video, the Apple Remote and Front Row software, iTunes 6, and the new iMacs. He notes significant hardware trade-offs like dropping FireWire support and the remote jack in favor of a thinner design. He's skeptical of the $1.99 TV show pricing at 320x240 resolution, pointing out the irony of Apple declaring it 'the year of HD video.' He praises Apple's iPod hardware engineering trajectory and observes that Front Row's full-screen UI is clearly a precursor to Apple's TV ambitions.
key_points: The new iPods dropped FireWire entirely and removed the remote jack, sacrificing peripheral compatibility for a thinner form factor
TV show downloads at $1.99 for 320x240 resolution seem like a poor value proposition, especially given Apple's own 'year of HD video' marketing
Front Row's full-screen media UI is clearly designed for television use, suggesting Apple's incremental strategy toward a media entertainment platform
Jon Rubinstein's move from Mac hardware chief to iPod division head, once seen as a demotion, now looks prescient given the iPod division's extraordinary engineering output
iTunes 5 lasted only one month before being superseded by iTunes 6, an unprecedentedly short version lifecycle
money_quotes: iTunes 5, we hardly knew you. I'm not sure I've ever seen an entire x.0 version number series go by so quickly.
The full-screen UI of Front Row is just begging to be hooked up to a TV. Begging.
Apple's long-term strategy for a media entertainment platform is unfolding incrementally.
$1.99 for each TV show, in only 320 × 240 resolution, doesn't seem like a good deal to me.
Of course, I think ringtones sound like a bad deal, too, but people buy billions of them.
And so much for 'the year of High Definition Video' — 320 × 240 not only isn't HD, it's too low to look good on regular (non-HD) TVs.
I'm sitting here looking at my year-old 40 GB iPod and the thing looks like a relic.
I'm hard-pressed to think of any other hardware company doing as much innovative product design and engineering as Apple's iPod division.
posts.key_insight: Apple's October 2005 event revealed a company making deliberate hardware trade-offs to push thinner designs while incrementally building toward a media entertainment platform, though the initial video offering's low resolution and pricing undercut the vision.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the economics and realities of being an independent Mac developer, using Brent Simmons's sale of NetNewsWire to NewsGator as a case study. He argues that the romantic notion of 'The Life' — independently selling software and coding full-time — breaks down at scale because support costs grow with every sale. Simmons was so successful that he could no longer spend his time writing code, instead drowning in customer support. The NewsGator acquisition, rather than being a sellout, actually gets Simmons closer to The Life by freeing him to focus on programming while NewsGator handles the business overhead. Gruber supports this with detailed RSS feed statistics showing NetNewsWire's dominant market position among Mac users.
key_points: Software sales follow a power-law distribution, but unlike books, each sale brings incremental support costs that consume development time
The indie developer paradox: by the time you sell enough licenses to quit your day job, you have so many users that you spend most of your time on support instead of coding
NetNewsWire held a staggering 51-74% share of RSS aggregator usage across Daring Fireball's various feeds, demonstrating massive market dominance
Simmons's wife Sheila was the 'secret ingredient' handling support and business operations — without her, Ranchero couldn't have lasted as long as it did
The NewsGator acquisition paradoxically moved Simmons closer to 'The Life' than independence did, by letting him focus on code while a company handles the rest
money_quotes: Once a developer gets enough paying users to consider quitting his day job so he can devote full-time effort to writing code, he's quite possibly got so many paying users that he'll spend much of his time helping customers in ways other than writing code.
Novelists aren't expected to help people read their books; musicians don't help fans play their CDs; but programmers are expected to help you with their software.
The whole point of The Life is the idea that you can just sit around all day hacking on code and watch the money roll in through Kagi or eSellerate.
By selling his company and working on NetNewsWire as a NewsGator employee, Brent has actually gotten closer to The Life than he was as an indie.
What it really boils down to is a crackerjack programmer carving out a space where he can continue focusing on one of the best apps the Mac has ever seen.
NetNewsWire's market share among Daring Fireball members is simply titanic.
People who are willing to pay money to support Daring Fireball are more likely than average to be willing to pay money to purchase software.
posts.key_insight: The romantic ideal of the solo indie developer breaks down at scale because success creates a support burden that prevents the very coding work that made the product successful in the first place.

posts.summary: Gruber offers detailed observations on Apple's October 2005 product announcements: Aperture, updated Power Macs, and new PowerBooks. He praises Aperture's UI design and photographer-focused approach while noting it's not a Photoshop competitor but rather targets a specific segment. The Power Mac updates get credit for serious performance gains via dual-core processors, though he notes Apple is further from the promised 3 GHz than ever. The PowerBook updates are mostly display improvements with little performance gain, raising questions about the Osborne Effect as Intel transition looms.
key_points: Aperture represents Apple targeting a specific Photoshop market segment (photographers) rather than competing with Photoshop directly as a design tool
Aperture's single-window UI philosophy versus Photoshop's palette-heavy approach signals the future direction of Mac application design
The new dual-core Power Macs deliver major real-world performance gains (76% faster Xcode builds) but clock speeds have actually regressed from 2.7 to 2.5 GHz
PowerBook updates are mostly display improvements, making them a real test of the Osborne Effect as customers weigh buying now versus waiting for Intel-based models
Apple's online store is experimenting with Ajax-based configuration UI that replaces pop-up menus, though it's inconsistently deployed
money_quotes: Aperture looks like what Mac apps are going to look like in the future; Photoshop looks like what Mac apps used to look like years ago.
If Aperture is fast, then why is iPhoto still so slow?
Not only have the Power Mac G5s still not gotten to 3 GHz, they're actually further away than before, topping out at 2.5.
Finally, Power Mac owners with thousands of dollars budgeted for RAM can comfortably open both Safari and iPhoto at the same time.
I think well-designed single-main-window designs are a better way to implement 'workflow' applications.
If you're tempted to wait for Intel-based PowerBooks, at this point, I think you might be waiting for a long time.
Aperture's revision control only stores the instructions, not entire files for each version.
If you've ever used Xcode, or listened to a developer who does, you'll realize that there are a lot of Mac developers out there clicking 'buy' today.
posts.key_insight: Apple's October 2005 updates reveal a company pushing UI design forward with Aperture while squeezing the last viable performance from PowerPC hardware as the Intel transition approaches.

posts.summary: Gruber posts a brief housekeeping note for Daring Fireball members about two common issues with the membership system. Gmail users may find their confirmation emails caught in spam filters. Returning members who renewed need to update their RSS subscription passwords to use their new membership key. He provides specific instructions for updating credentials in NetNewsWire and Safari/Keychain Access.
key_points: Gmail spam filters were catching Daring Fireball membership confirmation emails
Renewed memberships require manually updating RSS feed passwords with the new membership key
Instructions provided for NetNewsWire and Safari/Keychain Access password updates
money_quotes: If you've signed up but haven't received your membership information, please check your spam box.
posts.key_insight: A brief administrative note addressing common friction points in Daring Fireball's early membership and paid RSS feed system.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the prizes for Daring Fireball's 2005 membership drive, listing software licenses and goods donated by prominent Mac indie developers and companies. The prizes total over $3,000 in value and include offerings from Panic, Bare Bones, 37signals, Rogue Amoeba, and many others. He outlines the rules: members who joined or renewed after June 2005 are eligible, winners are selected randomly via Perl script, and members can optionally rank their prize preferences by email. The post is essentially a promotional announcement to encourage last-week signups before the October 31 deadline.
key_points: Over $3,000 in prizes donated by prominent Mac software companies and indie developers
The prize list reads as a who's-who of mid-2000s Mac indie software: Panic, Bare Bones, Rogue Amoeba, 37signals, Flying Meat, Shirt Pocket, and others
Winners are selected randomly using a simple Perl script, with each member winning only once
Members can optionally email a ranked preference list to increase their chances of getting a prize they want
money_quotes: This is somewhere north of US$3,000 in prizes; my sincere thanks to all of the above companies for their support of Daring Fireball.
Winners will be selected randomly, using a simple Perl script.
posts.key_insight: The breadth and generosity of prizes from Mac indie developers illustrates the tight-knit ecosystem around Daring Fireball and the mid-2000s Mac software community.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a 1.0.1 update to his CSS Syntax Checker for BBEdit and TextWrangler, primarily adding Mac OS X 10.3 compatibility. The update required replacing the HTML::Entities Perl module with CGI.pm's unescapeHTML() since the former wasn't installed by default on 10.3. The AppleScript component needed workarounds for the path to me command and display alert command, both of which behave differently on Tiger versus earlier OS versions. Gruber details the version-checking approach using AppleScript's binary-coded decimal version system.
key_points: The CSS Checker update adds backward compatibility with Mac OS X 10.3 by working around Tiger-specific features
AppleScript's path to me command behaves differently on 10.3 vs 10.4, returning the application path rather than the script path on older systems
Version checking uses AppleScript's binary-coded decimal version number rather than the OS version directly
The display alert command introduced in Tiger produces nicer dialogs but requires fallback to display dialog on older systems
money_quotes: Asking people to learn to use CPAN just so they can syntax-check their CSS files is a bit much to ask.
It's better practice to define a constant with a sensible name than to compare against a seemingly arbitrary value like "17826208" repeatedly.

I say "sort of" because that's actually a screenshot of the display dialog command taken on 10.4, because I'm just too lazy to bother getting a screenshot from the old iMac I have that's still running 10.3.
Even the output from display dialog looks better on 10.4 than it did before, so the dialog will look even crustier when actually running on 10.3.
Once compiled, the script works just fine on 10.3, but to compile it under 10.3, you need to use the raw Apple event syntax for the display alert command.
posts.key_insight: Backward compatibility with older OS versions requires careful version-checking workarounds, particularly when AppleScript commands behave differently across Mac OS X releases.

posts.summary: Gruber transparently shares Daring Fireball's membership numbers, revealing that the current drive has yielded only 540 sign-ups compared to last year's 910, despite readership doubling. He analyzes possible reasons for the drop-off: no new t-shirt design, readers willing to pay once but not annually, and a bystander effect where readers assume others will contribute.
