He frames the experiment as testing whether direct reader support can sustain a writing career, expressing a preference for reader funding over advertising. He closes with a direct appeal to readers, arguing that while no single contribution matters much, the collective impact is significant.
key_points: Daring Fireball's membership drive is underperforming at 540 sign-ups versus 910 the previous year, despite doubled readership
Gruber identifies three likely causes: stale t-shirt designs, one-time-only donor psychology, and the bystander effect among readers
He explicitly prefers building revenue on direct reader contributions rather than advertising
The transparency about finances is itself notable — Gruber publishes exact numbers to make the case that reader support hasn't yet reached sustainability
He references Kottke as possibly the only person making full-time blogging work on reader support alone, framing the challenge as systemic rather than personal
money_quotes: I'd prefer to have the foundation based on direct contributions from readers, rather than advertising.
But perhaps there's a reason why Kottke is the one only person I'm aware of who's making a go of it, full-time, based solely on the support of his readers.
I'm fairly certain that there are way more than 540 of you who are hoping I'll succeed.
You (singular) aren't going to make or break this experiment, but youse (plural, and perfectly acceptable grammar here in Philadelphia) can make it work.
The point here isn't that I need this to pay bills — but rather to determine whether direct reader support can work as a sustainable business model for this site.
Some readers might have been happy to cough up a few bucks to support Daring Fireball once, but that they don't see it as something worth paying for every year.
posts.key_insight: Gruber's transparent accounting of his membership numbers reveals the fundamental tension of reader-supported publishing: audiences that double in size don't necessarily double in willingness to pay, and the bystander effect makes collective patronage harder than it should be.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews the late 2005 15-inch PowerBook G4 after abandoning his long-held theory that desk-bound users should buy cheap laptops and expensive desktops. He argues that data syncing pain, improved PowerBook pricing, and the viability of living off a single laptop make the PowerBook a better sole workstation. The review covers design, materials, keyboard, display resolution, and various hardware quirks in exhaustive detail. He praises the timeless aluminum design and keyboard while noting the iBook's superior durability and AirPort reception. The piece concludes with practical advice about the looming Intel transition, suggesting that buying a last-generation PowerPC Mac may be wiser than jumping on first-generation Intel hardware given historical Rev. A flakiness and the lag before universal binary software arrives.
key_points: Syncing data between two Macs is painful enough that living off a single maxed-out PowerBook is preferable to maintaining separate desktop and laptop setups
The PowerBook's aluminum design has aged remarkably well over four years, standing in stark contrast to the decorated, sticker-laden aesthetic of PC laptops
The new higher-resolution displays (1440x960 for the 15-inch) push pixel density but remain usable, though Apple will need resolution-independent UI before going much further
Despite the pending Intel transition, buying a current PowerPC Mac is defensible because first-generation hardware tends to be flaky and major software vendors will lag on universal binaries
Apple's obsessive secrecy during development may directly contribute to first-generation hardware problems like the iPod Nano's scratch-prone surface
money_quotes: It isn't necessary for Apple to heavy-handedly brand their case designs; the cases themselves — their shapes, their materials, their proportions, and of course the Apple logo — are the Apple brand.
The difference is that PC hardware appears not to be designed so much as decorated.
We'll buy your CPUs; we'll license your operating system; but we're not going to put your ugly fucking stickers on our computers.
Synching data between two machines is never going to be as convenient as having a single data store.
Apple is slated to soon start using the same Intel x86 guts as other laptop manufacturers, but I'll eat my hat if they start boogering up their cases with 'Intel Inside' decals.
The pending switch to Intel doesn't change my basic buying advice: if you need a new Mac now, get one now.
The only way to test what happens when you really use something is to really use it, but to really use a still-in-development Nano would have meant allowing people to take them out of the lab.
If not, the current 15-inch PowerBook G4 is an appropriate send-off to one of the best products in Apple's history.
posts.key_insight: The best laptop strategy for most users is to invest in one excellent portable machine rather than splitting the budget between a cheap laptop and a powerful desktop, because the pain of syncing data between two machines outweighs the performance gains of a desktop.

posts.summary: This post is a follow-up addendum to Gruber's 15-inch PowerBook review, addressing reader feedback and additional discoveries. He shares Amazon rebate deals on Macs, details the new 'Safe Sleep' feature that preserves RAM to disk before battery depletion, and discusses external display support on PC laptops. He also covers the glossy screen trend on Windows laptops, praises Migration Assistant, compares Seagate vs. Hitachi 7200 RPM drives, and resolves a trackpad drag issue caused by an overzealous system preference.
key_points: New PowerBooks include a 'Safe Sleep' mode that writes RAM to disk before sleep, automatically restoring sessions if the battery dies — a feature Gruber didn't know about during his original review.
The glossy screen trend on Windows laptops is split between genuine contrast improvements and showroom marketing gimmickry, with most DF readers considering it a gimmick.
Migration Assistant worked flawlessly for transferring a full user account via FireWire target mode, despite Gruber's initial skepticism as a long-time Mac user.
Apple ships both Seagate and Hitachi 7200 RPM drives in built-to-order PowerBooks with no way to choose; the Hitachi is slightly faster but noticeably louder.
The trackpad drag registration issue from the original review was caused by the 'Ignore accidental trackpad input' setting being enabled by default.
money_quotes: It's not something you have to turn on, or a new way to put your computer to sleep. It's something that kicks in automatically if you run out of juice while in normal sleep mode.
I suspect many long-time Mac users are somewhat skeptical of Migration Assistant — I certainly was.
I figured if I was dissatisfied with the results, I could easily restore the PowerBook to factory state and do the migration manually, as I have with previous new Macs.

Similar to the way that when compared side-by-side in a showroom, typical consumers will choose a new television with over-saturated colors over one that is configured for truly accurate color representation.

I personally find these screens a bit repulsive, but I can't say I've ever tried using one for an extended period of time, and it's entirely possible that this is the direction in which all LCD screens are moving.
To my ears, the Seagate in this PowerBook is the quietest laptop hard drive I've ever used.
But if it's so great to be able to dismiss dialog boxes without moving your hand, why don't they have similarly-conveniently located Enter keys on desktop keyboards?
This feature was clearly causing me more harm than good.
posts.key_insight: The addendum format reveals how Gruber's thorough engagement with reader feedback surfaces important details — from hidden features like Safe Sleep to practical fixes like disabling overzealous trackpad settings — that enrich the original hardware review.

posts.summary: Gruber shares a simple AppleScript tip for web developers who need to restart Apache on their Mac after changing configuration files. He notes that while Apple provides a System Preferences method and the terminal offers apachectl, both require context-switching. His solution is a one-line AppleScript that invokes the shell command with admin privileges, saved to the system-wide Scripts menu for instant access from anywhere.
key_points: Changes to Apache's httpd.conf require a restart to take effect, commonly needed when setting up virtual hosting
Both the System Preferences method and terminal command are inconvenient unless those apps are already open
A one-line AppleScript using 'do shell script' with administrator privileges solves the workflow friction
Saving the script to the system-wide Scripts menu makes it accessible from any context
money_quotes: So because I'm really lazy, I made an AppleScript that does this. It's just one single line.
From the terminal, you can restart Apache using the `apachectl graceful` command, which requires administrator privileges, which means you'd typically invoke it as `sudo apachectl graceful`.
So it's as simple as this: when I need to restart Apache, I choose "Restart Apache" from my system-wide Scripts menu, the script prompts me to authenticate with admin credentials, and that's it.
posts.key_insight: A one-line AppleScript bridging shell commands to the system Scripts menu can eliminate small but repeated workflow friction for web developers on the Mac.

posts.summary: Gruber documents a Safari compatibility issue with Skype's 'callto:' URI scheme, where including double slashes after the scheme breaks phone number links but not username links. He provides the fix (omit the slashes) and notes that the no-slash format is likely canonical, consistent with how 'mailto:' URIs work. Firefox handles both formats fine, but Safari is picky. The post serves as practical developer guidance for anyone building Skype integration into web pages.
key_points: Safari fails silently on 'callto://' URIs containing phone numbers, though username-based URIs work fine with slashes
The correct format omits slashes after the scheme ('callto:+15558675309'), consistent with 'mailto:' URI conventions
Firefox handles both formats gracefully while Safari does not
MSDN documentation confirms the no-slash format as canonical
money_quotes: You click them and nothing happens.
I have no idea which format is "correct", but given that "mailto:" URIs have never used slashes, my money is on the no-slash format being canonical.
Or, as we call it in the Web 2.0 world, a "Skype mash-up".
posts.key_insight: When implementing 'callto:' URIs for Skype integration, omit the double slashes after the scheme to ensure Safari compatibility, following the same convention as 'mailto:' links.

posts.summary: Gruber lays out his predictions for Macworld San Francisco 2006, hours before the keynote, arguing that any pundit worth his salt should commit predictions to writing beforehand. His central prediction — that Apple would not release Intel-based Macs — proves wrong, though he correctly calls iLife '06, iWork '06, and the absence of plasma TVs or a 10.5 preview. He adds post-keynote results inline, acknowledging his misses while noting the MacBook Pro name is 'just horrible.' The piece reveals his analytical framework: Apple's credibility with developers matters more than crowd-pleasing early releases.
key_points: Gruber argued Intel Macs shipping early would damage Apple's credibility with developers who were told they had until summer 2006
He correctly predicted iLife '06 with iWeb, iWork '06, and the absence of OS X 10.5 previews and Apple-branded TVs
He was wrong about Intel Macs not shipping and wrong about a new spreadsheet app in iWork
He immediately called the 'MacBook Pro' name terrible — 'like some crappy Mac accounting software from 1987'
He predicted Rosetta emulation would be fairly slow, explaining why PowerPC models would continue selling alongside Intel ones
money_quotes: Any pundit worth his salt ought to be willing to put predictions in writing beforehand.
Releasing Intel-based Macs now might be popular with the keynote crowd and the tech press, but it would come at the expense of a bit of Apple's credibility with developers.

If Apple were to release Intel-based Macs now, in January, the next time Apple tells developers they have a year to get on board with something new, developers will feel like they've got to drop everything and do it immediately.
MacBook Pros (which name is terrible — I mean just horrible, like some crappy Mac accounting software from 1987)

I see zero signs of let-up or over-confidence at Apple with regard to their iPod success; they've got an enormous market share but are pressing harder than their competitors at pushing innovative hardware designs out the door.
FireWire may not be the raging success we all hoped it would be, but there are millions of digital video cameras and hard drives out there that depend on it.
Apple-Branded Plasma TVs Running Mac OS X: No. This doesn't even make sense.
posts.key_insight: Gruber valued Apple's developer credibility over crowd-pleasing announcements, a principled stance that proved wrong when Apple shipped Intel Macs early — showing that sometimes shipping ahead of schedule is worth the disruption.

posts.summary: Gruber writes a comedic screenplay imagining Mac OS X's Brushed Metal interface theme as a down-on-his-luck character visiting Safari after the Macworld 2006 keynote revealed that iLife '06 apps had dropped Brushed Metal in favor of the new iTunes-style theme. Brushed Metal is desperate, having lost his contracts with iLife apps overnight, and is looking for allies to stay relevant. Safari is polite but distant, clearly unwilling to get involved in theme politics. The piece satirizes Apple's inconsistent UI theming decisions, the tech community's fickle aesthetic preferences, and the lack of clear interface guidelines at Apple during this era.
key_points: The iLife '06 suite abandoned the Brushed Metal theme in favor of the new borderless iTunes-style interface, a significant visual shift in Apple's app design
Gruber satirizes the tech community's tendency to praise new design trends while forgetting that older themes already offered similar features like borderless windows
The piece highlights Apple's lack of consistent interface guidelines, with themes appearing and disappearing without formal naming or documentation in the HIG
Safari's reluctance to get involved foreshadows uncertainty about which apps would keep Brushed Metal and which would eventually switch themes
The squared-off window corners in the new iLife theme are called out as an odd design choice that looks like a bug
money_quotes: Tried calling you, but kept getting your voicemail.
How about that fucking keynote? Fucking iLife, the whole goddamn suite, gone to that bastard theme from iTunes.
They didn't even bother weaseling out of them with loopholes — they just ripped 'em up.
Like I can't do borderless! Like you and me aren't existence proof that I can do borderless! We fucking pioneered borderless.
And what's the deal with those squared-off window corners? Oooh, custom corner radiuses. What is the point of that? It looks like a bug.
I just want to render pages, keep getting faster. Keep closing leaks. I'm just not involved with the theme stuff.
Can I borrow a hundred dollars?
How about iCal? Fucker.
posts.key_insight: Apple's serial abandonment of its own UI themes reveals a company that prioritizes aesthetic novelty over consistent design guidelines, leaving its own interface elements as disposable casualties.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses the controversy over iTunes 6.0.2's MiniStore feature, which transmitted listening data to Apple's store and was accused of being spyware. He argues the feature was never actually spyware since its function was self-evident, but acknowledges Apple's mistake was in not making the data transmission transparent or opt-in from the start. Apple responded by making the MiniStore opt-in with clear explanatory text answering all privacy questions. Gruber praises the fix as a 'pitch-perfect solution' and credits Apple for listening to user concerns.
key_points: The MiniStore wasn't spyware — transmitting track data was inherent to showing related store content, making the function self-evident
Apple's real mistake was poor communication: users couldn't easily determine how to disable the feature or whether Apple retained their listening data
Apple fixed the issue by making MiniStore opt-in with clear explanatory text, addressing all pertinent privacy questions
The iTunes team's error was assuming users would trust them rather than anticipating how the feature could be misconstrued
The quick, well-executed response demonstrates Apple does listen to its users despite perceptions otherwise
money_quotes: Apple's mistake wasn't that they added spyware to iTunes, because they didn't, but rather that the way the feature was introduced made it possible to be misconstrued as spyware.
The answers to these questions were not apparent.
You can argue that this is how it should have shipped a week ago, and you'd be right.
The iTunes team didn't anticipate that the MiniStore feature could be misconstrued — they knew it wasn't the least bit nefarious, and assumed users would trust them.
What they've come up with here is a pitch-perfect solution.
So much for the idea that Apple doesn't listen to its users.
posts.key_insight: The MiniStore controversy wasn't about Apple doing something wrong, but about failing to communicate clearly — and their quick opt-in fix showed how to properly handle user trust.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews the major announcements from Macworld Expo 2006, centered on Apple's first Intel-based Macs. He analyzes the strategic decision to keep existing case designs for the new iMac Core Duo and MacBook Pro, arguing it reinforces Apple's message of a smooth transition rather than a disruptive schism. He debunks a BusinessWeek article suggesting Apple is profiteering from the Intel switch, dissects the new Dashboard widgets and iLife '06 suite (particularly iWeb's promising simplicity but flawed markup), and praises the Intel chip commercial as TV-commercial-as-art.
key_points: Apple's decision to use existing case designs for Intel Macs was strategically brilliant — it signals continuity and a smooth transition rather than a disruptive break, letting consumers see them as 'just a Mac with a faster processor'

BusinessWeek's article suggesting Apple is profiteering from Intel chips is journalistic malpractice — the article's own facts contradict its headline, since Apple likely pays more for Intel chips than it did for PowerPC
iWeb is a compelling new app focused entirely on ease-of-authoring at the expense of features, but its XHTML markup falls short on semantics despite technically validating
Real-world benchmarks show Intel iMacs are 25-50% faster than G5s with native apps, and Rosetta emulation performs respectably at roughly 1 GHz G4 speeds
The MacBook Pro's removal of the modem and FireWire 800 are reasonable trade-offs, but dropping S-video output without including an adapter is a nickel-and-dime move
money_quotes: But in hindsight, I think I see Apple's strategy. Internally, these new computers are almost completely different than their PowerPC brethren; but by using the same outer appearance, it reinforces the message that this transition is intended to be smooth. A shift, not a schism.
PCs typically cost less than Macs because they're pieces of crap, not because Intel CPUs are less expensive than IBM's or Freescale's.

The headline and opening paragraphs of the article are clearly construed to leave casual readers with the conclusion that Apple is profiting wildly from the switch to Intel processors, when the facts and analyst opinions in the article itself lead to the completely opposite conclusion.
Apple has completely ignored the features race in order to concentrate on simplicity and obviousness.
People who have no idea how to publish anything on the web are going to be able to use iWeb to publish all sort of stuff to the web.
The lighting, the extreme slow-motion cinematography, the music, the words — the ad is beautiful. It has a Kubrickian vibe that warms my heart. This is TV-commercial-as-art.
Don't believe it. Intel loves this ad. This ad makes Intel processors look better than any ad Intel has ever produced itself.
What kind of weblog doesn't support comments?
posts.key_insight: Apple's decision to house revolutionary Intel internals in familiar exteriors was a deliberate strategic choice — framing the biggest architectural shift in the Mac's history as a smooth, seamless transition rather than a frightening upheaval.

posts.summary: Gruber delivers a thorough argument against the silent, automatic installation of Unsanity's Smart Crash Reports by third-party Mac applications. While acknowledging that crash logs are invaluable to developers and that SCR itself appears well-engineered, he argues that no application should ever install a system-wide extension that modifies other applications without explicit user permission. He traces how apps like Sandvox, Path Finder, and Chat Transcript Manager silently installed the SCR input manager — which loads into every Cocoa app — without informing users, violating the implicit trust of Mac OS X's drag-and-drop installation model. He proposes superior alternatives where apps simply check their own crash logs on launch and ask permission to send reports, as Path Finder 4.0.2 ultimately implemented.
key_points: Smart Crash Reports is an input manager that loads into every Cocoa application to redirect crash logs to third-party developers, not just Apple — but some apps silently auto-installed it without user knowledge or consent.

The silent installation violates Mac OS X's fundamental drag-and-drop contract: users expect that trashing an app removes all its active effects, but SCR persists and keeps loading into every application.
The quality or safety of SCR is irrelevant to the argument — the principle is that no app should silently install system-wide hacks, period, regardless of how well-engineered they are.

Developers can implement their own crash reporting by simply reading their app's crash log file on launch and asking permission to send it — a solution that requires no system modifications whatsoever.
If an accurate description of what you're installing would turn users off, the answer isn't to hide it — it's to not do it.
money_quotes: To a seasoned Mac developer, a stack trace is like a fingerprint-covered weapon left at the scene of a crime.
This behavior — the silent installation of a system extension that modifies all applications — is both wrong and dangerous.

An application, in and of itself, should never install an extension that modifies the system or otherwise diddles the runtime environment of other applications, without the express permission of the user. There are no exceptions to this rule.

If you think your users would be turned off by an accurate description of something, that doesn't mean you should do it without telling them. It means you shouldn't be doing whatever it is you don't want to tell them about.
You might be almost certain that Smart Crash Reports won't cause any problems, but I'm completely certain that if it isn't installed at all, it definitely won't.
The argument against the auto-installation of Smart Crash Reports isn't that SCR causes any particular known harm; the argument is that no app should silently install system-wide hacks, period.

The point being: users should have zero worries about trying out an app via a drag-and-drop installation. The only way for users not to be worried about things being installed behind their backs is for nothing to be installed behind their backs.
posts.key_insight: The quality or harmlessness of silently installed software is irrelevant — the principle that applications must never modify the system without explicit user consent is absolute, because trust in the drag-and-drop installation model is foundational to the Mac experience.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that Daring Fireball has joined The Deck, a boutique ad network for web and design professionals alongside A List Apart, Coudal Partners, and Signal vs. Noise. He explains that The Deck's advertising philosophy closely mirrors his own homegrown sponsorship system, with the key difference being the addition of small graphics alongside text. The shared principles include one ad per page, advertiser vetting, limited spots with equal rotation, and no animation. Gruber credits his friend Jim Coudal as the mastermind behind The Deck and notes their collaborative history in shaping these advertising ideals. He closes by thanking the companies that supported Daring Fireball through his previous sponsorship system, with special mention to Jewelboxing for continuous support from day one.
key_points: The Deck operates on principles contrary to conventional web advertising: one ad per page, vetted advertisers, limited spots, and no animation
Gruber's advertising philosophy was developed in collaboration with Jim Coudal, making The Deck a natural evolution of his homegrown system
The approach is framed as superior for both readers (no distraction or insult) and advertisers (no competition for attention on the same page)
The transition represents Daring Fireball joining an exclusive network of respected web and design publications
money_quotes: Animation is verboten.

Each one of these principles is contrary to the conventional wisdom in web advertising — most sites that draw revenue from advertising attempt to cram as many ads as possible on each page, in as big and garish a format as possible.

Yet I feel strongly that our policies make for a superior experience both for you, the reader, who is neither distracted by animation nor insulted by "punch the monkey" nonsense, and for the sponsors, who do not have to compete for attention from other advertisers on the same page.
Advertisers are limited to products and services we (the member sites of The Deck) have paid for or use.
That The Deck's policies are so similar to my own is not a coincidence.
posts.key_insight: Restraint in web advertising — fewer ads, vetted sponsors, no animation — serves both readers and advertisers better than the conventional approach of maximizing ad density and visual noise.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier critique of Unsanity's Smart Crash Reports with several addenda. He addresses reader concerns about InputManagers being a security hole, arguing that while the situation isn't great, it's just one of many ways a malicious app could cause harm once launched. He notes an inconsistency in how macOS handles invisible input managers. He reports that Unsanity has responded to his criticism by adding a permission dialog in the next release, though he argues the description still isn't transparent enough about what's actually being installed. He also highlights Apple's own recommended approach and a third-party alternative called BugReporter.
key_points: InputManagers aren't uniquely dangerous — any running app can already modify Library subfolders, so the real defense is not launching untrusted software
macOS inconsistently blocks dot-prefixed invisible input managers but loads ones with the HFS invisible bit set, which Gruber calls an oversight or bug
Unsanity responded to criticism by adding a permission dialog in Smart Crash Reports 1.1b1, addressing Gruber's core argument about silent installation
Apple's own technical note recommends apps check their own crash logs on launch rather than using input manager hacks
Self-contained crash reporting solutions like BugReporter exist as alternatives that don't require system-wide extensions
money_quotes: Once you launch an application, it can pretty much do anything it wants within the confines of your home folder.
The expected behavior of any application that wants to install any system-wide extension is that it should ask permission first; apps that behave otherwise should be shunned.

There are so many bad things a malicious app can do once it's running that it'd be nearly impossible to defend against them all, individually; the best and only defense is not to launch software you don't trust.
This seems like an oversight, if not a bug. If it's a good idea to suppress some 'invisible' input managers, the system should suppress them all.
I can't think of a single legitimate reason why anyone would want invisible input managers to load.
System-wide extensions should never be installed without the user's permission.
posts.key_insight: The real security boundary on macOS isn't folder permissions but the decision to launch an application in the first place, which is why system-wide extensions must always require explicit user consent.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the launch of Joyeur, the corporate weblog for Joyent, the company he works for. Joyent had recently merged with TextDrive, a web hosting company founded by Dean Allen and Jason Hoffman. The blog is built on Textpattern with a design by Bryan Bell, and will cover a mix of company products, technical topics, and casual personal posts. Gruber playfully introduces the concept of blog comments as a 'novel feature' and notes the blog will range from UI decisions and code snippets to nacho recipes and beer reviews.
key_points: Joyent merged with TextDrive at the end of November, combining a tech company with a beloved web hosting outfit
The Joyeur blog is deliberately eclectic, mixing technical content with personal and casual posts
Gruber deadpans about comments being a 'novel feature,' a joke given his own famously comment-free blog
The blog is built on Dean Allen's Textpattern with a design that inverts Joyent.com's color scheme
money_quotes: From the moment the papers were signed, the combined company has been kicking all sorts of ass.
But these days, most good companies also publish good weblogs.
That "learn from all of you" part stems from a novel feature called "comments", which allows readers of the weblog to append brief observations, rebuttals, and questions to each post.
Joyeur is going to be rather hard to categorize, content-wise.
Anything from our user interface decisions to JavaScript or Ruby code snippets is fair game.
posts.key_insight: Gruber uses the launch of Joyent's corporate blog to subtly argue that good companies distinguish themselves partly through good writing and an authentic, personality-driven web presence.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects a Safari exploit where a shell script disguised as a JPEG can execute automatically thanks to a combination of Mac OS X's 'usro' resource fork mechanism and Safari's 'Open safe files after downloading' default setting. The exploit works because the Finder's custom 'Open With' binding (stored as a 'usro' resource) takes precedence over file extensions and creator codes, allowing a file named 'Heise.jpg' to silently open in Terminal and execute as a script. Gruber explains the technical chain in detail and emphasizes this is a serious vulnerability, not a joke. He proposes several fixes Apple could implement, including disabling Safari's auto-open preference by default — something he'd already recommended two years prior. He contextualizes the exploit within the broader, longstanding problem of Trojan horse techniques that disguise executables as documents, concluding that double-clicking files from untrusted sources has never been safe.
key_points: The 'usro' resource fork lets a custom 'Open With' binding override both creator codes and file extensions, enabling a shell script to masquerade as a JPEG
Safari's 'Open safe files after downloading' preference, enabled by default, is the critical enabler — Gruber had warned about this exact attack surface two years earlier
Creator codes and type codes are stored as HFS Plus metadata, not in resource forks — contrary to popular misconception
Apple could fix this by removing or disabling the auto-open preference, restricting 'safe' file types, or making 'usro' resources user-account-specific
The fundamental problem — that double-clicking untrusted files is unsafe — is a decade-old issue, not unique to this exploit
money_quotes: A harmful exploit using this very same technique could do just about anything to and with the entire contents of your home folder, however. This is not a fucking joke.
If Apple had gotten rid of this preference two years ago, we wouldn't be having this discussion now.
The file name extension in this case is a lie. The file is not, in fact, a JPEG image. It's a shell script.
It boils down to this: you can't safely double-click files from untrusted sources, and you never could.
This is no different today on Mac OS X 10.4 than it was a decade ago on Mac OS 8 and 9.
At the very least it should be turned off by default.
posts.key_insight: Safari's default preference to auto-open 'safe' files is an ongoing security liability that Apple should have eliminated years ago, and this exploit proves the point.

posts.summary: Gruber examines why knowledgeable PC users who acknowledge the Mac's superiority still refuse to switch, using political blogger Joshua Marshall as a case study. Marshall admits Macs seem better designed but cites familiarity with Windows, sunk costs in PC software, and concern about being 'tied to one company' as reasons to stay. Gruber finds the first two reasons understandable but challenges the third, arguing that Windows users are just as dependent on Microsoft as Mac users are on Apple. He points out that the Wintel platform's supposed 'openness' is illusory given Microsoft's monopoly and antitrust convictions, and that true openness leads to Linux, not Windows. He concludes that Microsoft's greatest trick has been convincing the public that its platform is open.
key_points: Familiarity with Windows, not superiority, is the single biggest impediment keeping potential switchers from moving to Mac
The iPod halo effect gradually breaks down unfamiliarity by introducing people to Apple hardware, software, and retail stores
The perception that PCs are 'open' and Macs are 'closed' is largely illusory — Windows users are just as locked into Microsoft as Mac users are into Apple
If openness is truly the priority, the logical destination is Linux, not Windows or Mac
Microsoft's antitrust conviction undermines the argument that trusting Microsoft is safer than trusting Apple
money_quotes: Familiarity is comforting.
I think in some cases they just need to see existence proof that Apple kit isn't 'weird'.
If Mac users' eggs are all placed in Apple's basket, I fail to see how Windows users' eggs are any less all placed in Microsoft's.

And which company of the two strikes you as more likely to abuse the implicit trust placed in it by the customers who have grown dependent upon its operating system: the one with less than three percent world-wide total market share or the one with a monopoly position which it has already been convicted of abusing under anti-trust laws?
I'm not saying there isn't merit to the idea that openness, in general, is a safer choice. But if that's the route you want to take, it leads neither to Cupertino nor Redmond.
The greatest trick Microsoft has gotten away with is convincing the public that the Wintel PC platform is open.
posts.key_insight: The perception that Windows represents an 'open' platform free from vendor lock-in is Microsoft's greatest deception — Windows users are every bit as dependent on one company as Mac users, just on a company with a worse track record of abusing that trust.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier piece about why Windows users don't switch to Mac, documenting a wave of prominent switchers including political blogger Josh Marshall, conservative commentator Andrew Sullivan, and the Penny Arcade gaming duo. He highlights reader insights comparing Windows familiarity to McDonald's consistency and the Ship of Theseus nature of PC upgrades. Gruber argues that a significant pool of Windows users could switch today but are held back primarily by comfort with the familiar. He concludes that while converting these users would be a bonanza for Apple yet still leave Microsoft with monopoly share, losing the 'computer enthusiast' demographic could threaten Microsoft's long-term position because enthusiasts are the people others turn to for buying advice.
key_points: Multiple prominent figures from different demographics — political bloggers, gamers — switched to Mac in the same week, suggesting a cultural tipping point
Penny Arcade's switch is notable because gamers are a demographic where Apple has traditionally done poorly, yet even they found the Mac compelling for non-gaming tasks
Windows users perceive their platform as an open, continuous experience, but incrementally replacing every component is functionally the same as switching (Ship of Theseus analogy)
The enthusiast market may be small in percentage terms, but enthusiasts disproportionately influence purchasing decisions for friends and family
Even a significant wave of switching would still leave Microsoft with monopoly market share, but the erosion of mindshare among enthusiasts poses a long-term strategic threat
money_quotes: What I have ascertained is not that PCs as we know them lack good design, but that PCs as we know them have hardly any design to speak of.
As a PC user, enduring the grotesqueries of that experience is something that we are actually proud of.
Like men who love the wilderness for its savage and untamed qualities, I believe many of us are drawn to this stark brutality.
I suspect one reason Wintel people believe their fortunes aren't tied to any particular vendor is that the little plastic badge on the front of their computers doesn't say 'Microsoft'.

At least with McDonald's you know that every location will give you pretty much the same surly service, moderately clean dining areas and bland, overly processed food. And that really describes the PC experience to me.
Enthusiasts might only constitute a few percentage points of the home market, but enthusiasts are the people family and friends go to for advice when buying new computers.
How many people do you know who love computers and prefer Windows?
posts.key_insight: The real threat to Microsoft isn't mass-market switching but losing the computer enthusiast class — the informal advisors who shape everyone else's purchasing decisions.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects a MacNotables podcast episode in which Bryan Chaffin, editor-in-chief of The Mac Observer, praises Think Secret as practicing 'true journalism' rather than rumor-mongering. Gruber methodically demonstrates that Think Secret's pre-announcement reporting on the video iPod was almost entirely wrong, undermining Chaffin's credibility claims. He further highlights the contradiction in Chaffin claiming to know as much or more than rumor sites while simultaneously getting his own on-air speculation about Apple's next announcements completely wrong. The post also needles Chaffin's claim that The Mac Observer doesn't publish rumors due to wanting a 'relationship with Apple,' suggesting this is really about advertising contracts. Chaffin's confident prediction of a touchscreen video iPod at the next day's event — which actually yielded the iPod Hi-Fi and Intel Mac Minis — is left to speak for itself.
key_points: Think Secret's October 2005 iPod predictions were almost entirely wrong — no video, wrong storage capacity, wrong color — undermining claims of rigorous journalism
Chaffin simultaneously claims Think Secret practices 'true journalism' while admitting he doesn't read it because he already knows what they know
The Mac Observer's refusal to publish rumors is framed as journalistic ethics but likely stems from Apple advertising agreements
Chaffin's own confident speculation about a touchscreen video iPod at the next day's event was completely wrong — Apple announced the iPod Hi-Fi and Intel Mac Minis
The piece exposes the gap between the Mac media's self-image as serious journalists and the reality of their track record
money_quotes: Other than the fact that the new iPods are indeed thinner than the previous generation, this is entirely and utterly wrong: the new iPods did support video, did not have 80 GB of storage, and were not available in silver.
Not rumors. Journalism. Right.

If by 'different', he means 'fictional', then this is correct, but I doubt that's what he means, since it shouldn't be hard for anyone to see how fictional upcoming product rumors are damaging to Apple.

Where by 'relationship with Apple', I'm guessing Chaffin means 'we want them to continue paying us for ads promoting WWDC, and the contract for those ads stipulates that we, along with all the other sites that run them, won't publish stories about unreleased Apple products.'
Should be like shooting fish in a barrel, what with all Chaffin knows before things even hit journalism sites like Think Secret.
It would have been interesting to hear how the iPods that were released back in October — the ones that play video — are untrue. But, alas, no.
I'll either look like an idiot or I'll look brilliant to the folks who hear this after it's already happened.
posts.key_insight: The Mac media's self-congratulatory claims of practicing 'true journalism' collapse under the weight of their own spectacularly wrong predictions and transparent conflicts of interest.

posts.summary: Gruber announces his appearance on a SXSW panel called 'How to Blog for Money by Learning From Comics,' where he'll be the token blogger alongside web comic artists. The panel's premise is that web comic creators have years of real experience monetizing online content. He playfully promotes his panel over a competing session, mentions other panels he's excited about, and closes with a joke about readers who owe him beers.
key_points: Web comic artists were early pioneers in making money from online content and have practical lessons for bloggers
Gruber positions himself as the lone blogger among web comic creators, suggesting cross-pollination between the mediums
The post is a lighthearted promotional piece with Gruber's characteristic self-deprecating humor and swagger
money_quotes: Our basic premise is that web comic artists have been making money from their online work for years, and so they have actual experience regarding what works and what doesn't.
I say if you've seen one redesign, you've seen them all; our shit's going to be funny and we're going to set fire to stacks of hundred dollar bills during the discussion.
If you're attending SXSW and happen to be one of the various DF readers who have ever claimed to quote-unquote "owe me a beer": please bring your credit card.
posts.key_insight: Web comic artists figured out how to monetize online content long before most bloggers, making them a valuable source of practical business wisdom for independent web publishers.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's iPod has become an unstoppable juggernaut, defying the conventional wisdom that Apple would repeat its 1980s Macintosh mistake of ceding market share to cheaper competitors. He cites Steve Jobs's own analysis that old Apple went for profits over market share, and shows that new Apple is doing the opposite with iPod — pricing competitively while leveraging volume discounts. Gruber methodically dismantles claims that competitors like Creative offer cheaper or better products, showing iPods are price-competitive while superior in design. He documents the retreat of would-be competitors like Dell, who quit the market entirely, and notes the growing iPod ecosystem of peripherals and iTunes Store dominance as compounding advantages.
key_points: The 'Apple is repeating the Mac mistake' narrative has collapsed under the weight of iPod's overwhelming market success and cultural penetration
Steve Jobs identified old Apple's fatal error as choosing profits over market share, and new Apple is deliberately doing the opposite with iPod pricing
Contrary to widespread assumption, iPods are not more expensive than competitors — they're comparably priced or cheaper, while Apple still earns 20-25% margins through volume discounts
Competitors can't win on features, design, price, or branding — leaving them no viable path to challenge iPod dominance
The iPod ecosystem of peripherals, auto adapters, and iTunes Store creates compounding network effects that deepen Apple's moat
money_quotes: The simplicity of the iPod is one of the prime reasons for its appeal. That it lacks many non-essential features is itself a feature.
In short, and I mean this in the nicest way possible, Apple's iPod competitors are totally fucked.
Apple's only serious competition to date has been itself.
They took their best-selling iPod model, the Mini, and discontinued it at the height of its popularity, replacing it with the radically superior and totally new iPod Nano.
If they can't win on features, and they can't win on design, and they can't win on price, what's left?
At the critical juncture in the late '80s, when they should have gone for market share, they went for profits.
My 40 GB fourth generation iPod isn't even two years old, but compared to the current iPods, it looks like a relic.
posts.key_insight: Apple learned from its 1980s Mac mistake and deliberately chose market share over maximum profits with iPod, creating a competitive moat that rivals cannot breach on features, design, or price.

posts.summary: Gruber eviscerates a San Francisco Chronicle article by Dan Fost that argues Apple is repeating its 1980s mistake by not licensing iPod technology. He systematically dismantles each claim in the article, from the mischaracterization of the Walkman's history as a cautionary tale, to an MIT economist's absurd method of calculating Apple's 'lost' half-trillion dollars by simply subtracting market caps, to John Dvorak's factually wrong claim that Mosaic was a Mac-only browser. Gruber points out that the licensing-is-destiny narrative ignores that hardware-focused companies like Sony, Microsoft, and Nintendo thrive in gaming, and that being a software company offered no protection against Microsoft either. The piece is a clinic in fact-checking lazy tech punditry.
key_points: The Walkman analogy backfires: Sony sold 330 million Walkmans, making it a success story, not a cautionary tale about hardware lock-in
The 'Apple gave up half a trillion dollars' claim is derived by simply subtracting Apple's peak market cap from Microsoft's — assuming Apple would have captured every dollar Microsoft ever made
The licensing argument ignores that hardware-focused companies (Sony, Nintendo, Microsoft in gaming) thrive without licensing their platforms to competitors
Dvorak's claim that Mosaic only worked on Mac is factually wrong — it shipped first on Unix X Window, and the first web browser was on NeXTStep
Apple survived not despite its idiosyncrasies but because of them — specifically its habit of designing superior products
money_quotes: If the next five years of the iPod go as well as the second five years of the Walkman, I'm sure Apple — and Apple's shareholders — will be pleased as punch.

I look forward to Fost's future haranguing of Sony, Microsoft, and Nintendo, all of whom are surely on the verge of collapse because they're all foolishly focusing on video game hardware and refuse to license their hardware to other companies.
Please tell me at what point in the last 30 years Apple's market share in the personal computer business was ever higher than 20 percent, a peak it reached before the Macintosh was even released.
John Gruber, a non-economist from Daring Fireball, estimated that Lester Thurow made that up.
Can you just make up any crazy bullshit you want and get it quoted as fact in The San Francisco Chronicle?
Is it not perhaps the case that Apple 'hung on' not despite its idiosyncrasies, but because of them? Like, say, its quirky little habit of designing and producing vastly superior products?
Much less risky to be a software company, I suppose, like WordPerfect, Borland, Lotus, and Netscape, all of whom fared so very well against Microsoft.
posts.key_insight: The 'Apple should license its technology' argument is a zombie narrative that persists despite being historically wrong about the Mac, logically incoherent about hardware companies, and built on fabricated or misleading facts.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses the conspiracy theories surrounding Avie Tevanian's departure from Apple, arguing that the move is entirely unremarkable. He notes that Tevanian had effectively stepped away from active work in 2003 when he gave up day-to-day management of software engineering. Apple engineers confirmed to Gruber that Tevanian had 'both feet out the door' for years. Gruber credits Bertrand Serlet as a capable successor and dismisses any connection to Intel transitions or wild theories about Apple switching to Windows. While acknowledging Tevanian's significant contributions to Mac OS X, Gruber frames the departure as simply a formality.
key_points: Tevanian had effectively disengaged from Apple since stepping down from VP of Software Engineering in 2003, making his formal departure unremarkable
Apple engineers confirmed to Gruber that no one knew what Tevanian had been doing for the past three years
Tevanian's success stemmed from being an engineer at heart rather than a manager, giving him true understanding of Mac OS X's design
Bertrand Serlet capably took over in 2003 and shipped Mac OS X 10.4 under his leadership with equal success
Despite personal criticisms of Tevanian, Gruber respects his legacy of shipping Mac OS X 10.0 through 10.3
money_quotes: Now, calling John C. Dvorak a "noted" anything other than idiot is a sure sign that this blurb isn't exactly meant to be taken seriously.
By all accounts of what he's done since then, his title might as well have been "guy who's worked his ass off and done a great job and now has decided to sit back and do nothing for a while".
They all agreed, more or less, that Tevanian has had both feet out the door but just hadn't yet turned in his keys.
One reason I think Tevanian was successful is that he was an engineer at heart, not a manager.
It's just a case of a very smart guy who decided a couple years ago to move on to something else, and tomorrow is moving day.

Criticizing Tevanian is like criticizing a sports player who won a bunch of championships: the bottom line in sports is winning, and the bottom line at Apple's software division is shipping great software.
posts.tone: measured, dismissive of conspiracy theories, respectful but candid
posts.key_insight: Tevanian's departure was a non-event because he had effectively left years earlier — the real story was that Apple's software division had already successfully transitioned leadership to Bertrand Serlet.

posts.summary: Gruber pushes back against the widespread Mac community belief that users should 'Repair Permissions' before and after every system update. He points out that Apple has never recommended this practice, and that it originated from the same vague 'they' who once advised unnecessary PRAM zapping. He clarifies that the 10.4.6 updater's double reboot on PowerPC Macs has nothing to do with permissions repair. His actual advice for system updates is simply to back up first, disconnect external drives during installation, and trust Software Update — treating Repair Permissions as a troubleshooting tool, not routine maintenance.
key_points: Apple has never recommended repairing permissions before or after system updates, despite widespread community belief
The 10.4.6 double reboot on PowerPC Macs is for early-boot component updates, not permissions repair
Repair Permissions is a troubleshooting tool, not a regular maintenance step
The only truly essential pre-update step is performing a full backup, ideally to a disconnected external drive
Folk wisdom from anonymous 'they' sources creates unnecessary complexity around what should be simple update procedures
money_quotes: We are told to repair permissions before and after each update by whom? Certainly not by Apple.
Perhaps Cruse means 'them', the same 'them' who, in the classic Mac OS era, recommended zapping your PRAM every time you need to reboot your Mac after the system was wedged by a crashed app.
To be clear, perfectly clear: Apple does not recommend that you do this.
Repairing permissions is a troubleshooting tool, not a regular maintenance step.
It's this simple: if it were good for your Mac, Apple would recommend it.
I guarantee you that the engineers on Apple's installer team know more than 'they' do.
A buggy installer could damage or inadvertently delete files from any mounted drive on your system.
'They' make a lot more work out of system updates than they need to.
posts.key_insight: Repairing permissions is a troubleshooting tool, not routine maintenance — the folk wisdom that says otherwise is cargo-cult computing unsupported by Apple or engineering reality.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's Boot Camp announcement transforms the Mac from a 'different' computer into a 'special' one — a superset of PC hardware that runs both Windows and Mac OS X. He contends this is a low-risk, high-reward move that eliminates the biggest barrier to switching: fear of the unfamiliar. Gruber draws a pointed analogy between Windows-on-Mac and the old Classic environment in early Mac OS X — a compatibility ghetto you tolerate but want to escape. He predicts Apple will eventually build concurrent virtualization into Leopard, making dual-booting obsolete. The piece frames Boot Camp as an act of supreme confidence: Apple relishes a direct comparison between Mac OS X and Windows, betting most users who try both will prefer the Mac.
key_points: Boot Camp recasts Macs from 'different' to 'special' — instead of choosing between a Windows PC or a Mac, you choose between a PC that only runs Windows or one that runs both
The target market is enthusiasts and higher-end users, not mainstream consumers — people who care about their computers and are willing to pay more for something better
Gruber draws a deliberate analogy to Classic mode: Windows on Mac will be viewed as a compatibility ghetto users tolerate but wish they didn't need
Apple is likely building concurrent virtualization into Leopard, making Boot Camp's dual-boot limitation temporary
Microsoft is stuck — Apple is doing nothing wrong, Boot Camp users pay for Windows licenses, and in a fair comparison Mac OS X wins
money_quotes: The point is that it recasts Macs from being 'different' to being 'special'.
Instead of choosing between a Windows PC or a Mac, you now get to choose between a computer that can only run Windows or a computer that can run both Windows and Mac OS X.
This is a move of supreme confidence — Apple relishes the comparison between Mac OS X and Windows XP.
Everything about Boot Camp is calibrated to position Windows-on-Mac as the next Classic-style ghetto — a compatibility layer that you might need but that you wish you didn't.
Even if Apple only has 2 percent of the total market today, it's 2 percent from the best part of the market.
Boot Camp portends Apple's intention to become a Windows-only PC manufacturer no more than Classic served as a hedge against Apple's commitment to Mac OS X — that is, not at all.
People who shop primarily based on price are generally lousy customers.
What's the difference between OS X and Vista? Microsoft employees are excited about OS X...
posts.key_insight: Boot Camp transforms the Mac from an alternative to Windows into a superset of it, and Apple is betting that once people can safely try both operating systems on the same hardware, Mac OS X will win on merit.

posts.summary: Gruber systematically dismantles several popular post-Boot Camp predictions about Apple's strategy: licensing Mac OS X for generic PCs, competing directly with Microsoft, and releasing Cocoa for Windows. He argues that all Apple speculation must be filtered through one lens: Apple's core business is selling hardware at 20-25% profit margins. Boot Camp helps sell Macs by making them the only hardware that runs both OS X and Windows, pitting Apple against Dell and Sony rather than Microsoft. Licensing OS X would cannibalize hardware profits, as the failed 1990s clone program demonstrated, and Cocoa for Windows would undermine the software advantage that drives Mac purchases.
key_points: Apple's business is fundamentally hardware sales at 20-25% margins; all strategy speculation must be evaluated through that filter
Boot Camp pits Mac hardware against other PC makers like Dell and Sony, not Apple against Microsoft — Microsoft may not lose anything since Boot Camp users still buy Windows
Apple targets the profitable high end of the PC market, not mass market share; profit share matters more than unit share
Licensing Mac OS X for generic PCs would cannibalize hardware profits — each lost hardware customer requires 3-5 new OS license buyers just to break even, exactly as the 1990s clone program proved
Cocoa for Windows would undermine Mac OS X's primary advantage by letting the best Mac apps run on Windows, costing Apple hardware sales for negligible developer gains
money_quotes: Selling hardware is their business. That's where their revenue comes from, that's where their profits come from, and revenue and profit is what defines a business.

Boot Camp doesn't pit Mac OS X against Windows, and so it doesn't pit Apple directly against Microsoft. What Boot Camp does is pit Mac hardware that can run both Mac OS X and Windows up against all other PC-hardware that can only run Windows.
Apple's current business model is bootleg-proof; unlike Mac OS X installer DVDs, you can't burn copies of MacBooks or iPods.
Jobs killed the cloning program because it was killing Apple Computer financially.
It's just not true that most Mac users use Macs because the hardware is better designed. They're Mac users because of the Mac OS.
It's not Apple's fault that Microsoft sucks to write about, nor is it their obligation to put themselves on a collision course against Microsoft just because it would make for good drama.

The best way for Apple to sell more computer hardware is to make Mac OS X as much better than Windows as they possibly can; nothing could be further from this goal than releasing a version of Cocoa for Windows.
posts.key_insight: Every piece of Apple strategy speculation must pass a single test — does it help Apple sell more hardware at premium margins? — and licensing Mac OS X, competing with Microsoft, and Cocoa for Windows all fail it.

posts.summary: Gruber argues forcefully that running 'Repair Permissions' in Mac OS X is not a valid periodic maintenance task, but rather a superstitious practice akin to voodoo. He explains that the feature merely compares current file permissions against original BOM records, and differing permissions don't necessarily mean they're wrong. Permission bugs were common in early OS X (10.0-10.1) but were eradicated by 10.3, making routine repair unnecessary. He also mocks MacFixIt's absurdly overcautious upgrade instructions for 10.4.6, which involve booting from external drives and Safe Mode for a routine update. The core message is that troubleshooting should be evidence-based, not ritualistic.
key_points: The name 'Repair Permissions' is misleading — 'Restore' or 'Reset' would be more accurate, since differing permissions aren't necessarily broken
Permission bugs were common in early OS X but were fixed by 10.2-10.3; most current 'permission problems' are self-inflicted by users modifying system files
Correlation between running Repair Permissions and having no problems is specious reasoning — the same logic would credit avoiding sidewalk cracks for your mother's healthy back
MacFixIt's 10.4.6 upgrade instructions are absurdly overcautious, involving external boot drives and Safe Mode for a routine system update
Running Repair Permissions without symptoms is like taking antibiotics before you're actually sick — wasteful and based on superstition rather than science
money_quotes: Troubleshooting computers is science, not magic.
Running Repair Permissions before you have an actual permissions problem is like taking antibiotics before you're actually sick.

Arguing that you (a) run Repair Permissions all the time and (b) have no permission problems, and then drawing the conclusion that there's a cause-and-effect relationship there, is like arguing that your diligent avoidance of sidewalk cracks has a causal relationship to the fact that your mother's back is doing just fine.
'Repair' makes it sound as though files are broken if their permissions are affected by this process; that's not the case.
Repairing permissions is zapping the PRAM for the twenty-first century.
Covering yourself in vaseline and rolling around naked in the dirt and repairing permissions are just as likely to fix your Mac OS X problem.
They apparently forgot to document the initial step, where you wrap your head in heavy-duty aluminum foil to block the mind-control rays emitted from installer packages.
If you are not experiencing any symptoms that would indicate permission-related problems, there is no reason to run Repair Permissions.
posts.key_insight: Repair Permissions is a troubleshooting tool for specific problems, not a preventive maintenance ritual — treating it as one is superstition dressed up as technical competence.

posts.summary: Gruber examines Jason O'Grady's blog post about the Apple v. Does lawsuit, in which Apple subpoenaed O'Grady's email provider to identify who leaked plans for 'Asteroid,' a FireWire GarageBand accessory. Gruber challenges O'Grady's characterization of being 'targeted for deletion' as overblown, and disputes his claim that any company could classify anything as a trade secret and sue over it. By citing Judge Kleinberg's ruling and the California penal code's specific definition of trade secrets, Gruber shows that O'Grady's cafeteria-menu analogy is misleading. He also highlights a factual discrepancy: O'Grady claims the leaked materials weren't marked confidential, while the court found they came from slides clearly labeled 'Apple Need-to-Know Confidential.' Gruber suggests both claims could be true if O'Grady's source stripped the labels before passing them along.
key_points: O'Grady was not personally named as a defendant — only his email provider was subpoenaed — making his 'targeted for deletion' rhetoric disproportionate
California's legal definition of 'trade secret' requires independent economic value from secrecy and reasonable efforts to maintain it, ruling out trivial information like cafeteria menus

The court found that O'Grady's postings contained exact copies of drawings from slides clearly labeled 'Apple Need-to-Know Confidential,' contradicting O'Grady's claim the materials weren't marked confidential
The discrepancy about confidentiality labels could be reconciled if O'Grady's source removed the markings before passing along the materials
money_quotes: A subpoena served against one's hosting provider is a far cry from being 'targeted for deletion'.
That paints a picture where this ruling implies that a company can rule anything and everything it wants as a 'trade secret', and that just clearly isn't so.

Even if you think Apple's Asteroid plans don't meet the aforementioned legal qualifications for a trade secret — that, say, they weren't subject to 'reasonable' enough efforts to maintain their secrecy — at the very least they come close.
A 'cafeteria menu' they are not, and Kleinberg's ruling in no way opens the door to a broad or loose interpretation of what constitutes a trade secret.
Note the discrepancy here regarding whether the documents were labeled as confidential.

It is entirely possible that all of this is true: that the original slides from Apple were marked 'Apple Need-to-Know Confidential', but that O'Grady's source removed, cropped, or redacted the labels before showing the slides to O'Grady.
posts.key_insight: Gruber methodically fact-checks O'Grady's rhetoric against the actual court ruling, showing that the legal definition of trade secrets is far narrower than O'Grady implies and that key factual claims about confidentiality labels don't match the court record.

posts.summary: Gruber announces new Daring Fireball t-shirt designs in response to member feedback that people wanted fresh options beyond the original shirt. He details the pricing structure, including discounts for multiple shirts and existing members. He teases that the shirts include a 'Your PC is a POS' design typeset in Chicago 12px, the original Mac and iPod system font. He also acknowledges the second common complaint — that he doesn't write frequently enough to justify membership — and promises to address that later the same day.
key_points: Two new t-shirt designs are introduced alongside the original, with tiered pricing ($29 first shirt, $19 each additional)
The 'Your PC is a POS' shirt uses Chicago 12px, a deliberate nod to classic Mac and iPod typography
Existing members get the discount price immediately, rewarding loyalty
Gruber openly acknowledges criticism that he doesn't write frequently enough to justify paid membership
money_quotes: Do note that the "Your PC is a POS" slogan is typeset in Chicago 12px — the original system font of both the Macintosh and the iPod.

You're a good writer and Daring Fireball is one of my favorite web sites, and so I might be willing to support your efforts by doing your membership thing, but, and I hate to say this, you don't write frequently enough.
I'll be announcing the fix for that one later today.
posts.key_insight: Gruber treats reader feedback as actionable product requirements, addressing complaints about merchandise variety and writing frequency as two halves of the same membership value proposition.

posts.summary: Gruber announces he has quit his full-time job at Joyent to write Daring Fireball full-time. He reflects on the catch-22 of needing full-time effort to grow revenue but needing revenue to justify full-time effort, and concludes the only way forward is to take the leap. He acknowledges the very real possibility of public failure but argues that endless deliberation is worse than acting. Inspired partly by Steve Jobs's Stanford commencement address about not settling, he asks readers directly for their support through memberships and t-shirt sales.
key_points: Gruber left his job at Joyent solely to pursue Daring Fireball full-time, describing it as jumping out of a plane with the idea as his parachute
The membership program launched two years prior was more successful than expected but less than secretly hoped — not enough to make the transition easy
He identified a catch-22: revenue wouldn't grow without full-time effort, but full-time effort required sufficient revenue first
He chose to announce publicly rather than quietly try, viewing secrecy as cowardly and transparency as necessary accountability
Steve Jobs's Stanford commencement speech about loving your work and not settling provided genuine inspiration despite the corniness
money_quotes: What I'm doing now is like jumping out of a plane with this idea as my parachute.
There's a reason why you can't say, 'Wow, look at all those people supporting their families with their weblogs devoted to deeply intricate Mac and web nerdery.'
My desire for this to be easy bound me in a catch-22.
There exists the very real possibility of failing in an excruciatingly public way.
It would be better to try this now and fail than to endlessly deliberate over plans that are never put into action.
If you can talk brilliantly about a problem, it can create the consoling illusion that it has been mastered.
The proper response to this announcement isn't so much 'Congratulations' as it is 'Good luck, I hope it works.'
Daring Fireball is what I love to do.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the only way to break a catch-22 between effort and revenue is to take the leap first and bet on yourself publicly, accepting the risk of failure over the safety of perpetual deliberation.

posts.summary: Gruber critiques Robert X. Cringely's three-column prediction that Apple will replace the Mach kernel in Mac OS X 10.5, finding it unlikely though not ludicrous. He dismantles Cringely's specific claim that the new kernel would be Intel-only, arguing Apple would never ship two different kernel architectures or abandon PowerPC support given the upgrade revenue at stake. Gruber also examines Cringely's even bolder claim that Apple will natively implement Windows APIs in OS X 10.5, calling it wildly ambitious and practically implausible. He notes that achieving bug compatibility with undocumented Windows APIs would be enormously difficult — even Microsoft struggles with Windows complexity. Gruber argues that a compatibility layer approach or simply bundling Windows via Boot Camp would be far more practical solutions.
key_points: Mac OS X's Mach kernel is slower than Linux and FreeBSD for server tasks, but the difference matters less for GUI-based desktop use
Cringely's claim that a new kernel would be Intel-only makes no sense because Apple would never ship two different kernel architectures or abandon PowerPC upgrade revenue
Intel Macs are already fast enough that Apple doesn't need to suppress PowerPC performance to drive hardware upgrades
Implementing the Windows API natively in OS X would require not just feature compatibility but bug compatibility with undocumented APIs — a nearly impossible task
The simpler and more practical approach is running Windows in a compatibility layer or via Boot Camp rather than reimplementing Windows APIs from scratch
money_quotes: There is just no way that Apple is going to ship PowerPC and Intel versions of Mac OS X with two entirely different kernel architectures.
These first-generation Intel-based Macs are so fast that Apple doesn't need to worry about suppressing performance on PowerPC Macs to spur people to upgrade to new hardware.

Perhaps the hardest part about building a new implementation of an existing standard such as the Windows application APIs is that you not only have to achieve feature compatibility, you also have to achieve bug compatibility.
That also means implementing not just the documented APIs but also the undocumented ones, and the fact that they're undocumented makes them rather hard to implement.
The ongoing Vista fiasco indicates that Windows is so large and so complicated that even Microsoft is having trouble producing a new version of it.
Mach is definitely not one of the best parts of Mac OS X, and the kernel is so deeply under-the-hood that most users would never notice.
posts.key_insight: Bold technical predictions about Apple often collapse under scrutiny when you consider the practical engineering constraints and business realities that actually drive Apple's decisions.

posts.summary: Gruber critiques Apple's 'smart' Cut/Copy/Paste behavior in Cocoa's NSTextView, which automatically adds or removes spaces around words depending on how the selection was made. He demonstrates how double-clicking to select a word triggers different paste behavior than click-and-drag selection, producing unwanted spaces when editing HTML. The core argument is that this 'smart' feature violates the elegant simplicity of traditional C/C/P by introducing an undocumented, mode-dependent behavior that appears random to most users. He draws a parallel to Microsoft Word's notorious over-helpful features and argues that software which guesses at user intent must be right nearly every time, because wrong guesses are far more annoying than right guesses are convenient.
key_points: Cocoa's NSTextView treats word-wise selection (double-click) differently from manual selection, triggering 'smart' space manipulation during cut and paste operations
The same Cut/Copy/Paste commands produce different results depending on how text was selected, violating users' expectations of consistent behavior
Features that appear to activate randomly are bad features, because most users don't understand the hidden word-selection mode distinction
Apple traded the elegant simplicity of traditional C/C/P for marginal convenience, echoing Microsoft Word's pattern of over-helpful defaults
Software that guesses user intent must be correct nearly every time, because wrong guesses are disproportionately more annoying than right guesses are helpful
money_quotes: Features that seem to kick in at random are bad features.

Once software starts down this path of guessing what it is the user is trying to do, and then doing something special based on that guess, it must guess correctly nearly every time, because the times when it guesses wrong are so annoying that they far outweigh the extra convenience of the times when it guesses right.

Software that eschews the clever for the obvious — in this case, using good old-fashioned 'what you see selected is what you get' C/C/P behavior — will always do exactly what the user expects. That's what people mean when they talk about intuitive software design.

Apple has taken a very simple, completely obvious feature — a feature that almost every user completely understood — and turned it into something governed by a complicated and undocumented set of rules that most users don't even know exist.
They've exchanged the elegant simplicity of traditional C/C/P for the very marginal benefit of sometimes having word-bordering spaces taken care of for you automatically.
The worst part is that unless you understand that word-wise selection puts you into a special mode, the 'smart' C/C/P behavior seems to kick in at random.
posts.key_insight: Smart software features that produce inconsistent behavior based on hidden modes are worse than simple features that always do exactly what the user expects.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his critique of NSTextView's 'smart' Cut/Copy/Paste behavior, clarifying that he wasn't questioning whether the general concept was new — Apple's HIG has prescribed intelligent cut and paste since at least 1992. His core complaint is that NSTextView's implementation contradicts Apple's own Human Interface Guidelines by making the smart behavior depend on how you made your selection (double-click vs. click-and-drag), not just what is selected. This violates a fundamental GUI principle: what you see should determine what you get. He argues that old-fashioned non-smart C/C/P is actually more predictable because users internalize the consistent manual spacing, whereas NSTextView's inconsistent 'sometimes smart, sometimes not' behavior forces users to think about something the feature was designed to eliminate.
key_points: Apple's HIG has prescribed 'Intelligent Copy and Paste' behavior since at least 1992, and the HIG rules are sensible for prose editing
NSTextView's implementation contradicts Apple's own HIG by making smart behavior depend on how a selection was made, not what is selected
The fundamental GUI principle of 'what you see is what you get' is violated when identical-looking selections produce different cut/paste results
Old-fashioned non-smart C/C/P is arguably better in practice because its consistent behavior becomes automatic and predictable
The inconsistency of NSTextView's smart C/C/P defeats its own purpose by forcing users to think about the very thing it was supposed to handle automatically
money_quotes: There's nothing in the HIG regarding how you make your selection — a selected word is a selected word, no matter whether you selected it by double-clicking or by clicking and dragging.
Instead of 'what you see is what you get', it's 'what you did is what you get', and the whole point of a GUI is about what you see.
The point of 'smart' C/C/P is, ostensibly, to free you from thinking about word-bordering spaces before and after C/C/P.

With old-fashioned 'non-smart' C/C/P, sure, you have to deal with word-bordering spaces manually, but I think in practice, you get used to doing so without even thinking about it, because you know you have to do it every time.
With NSTextView's implementation, however, you do have to think about it — or at least most users do — because sometimes you get the 'smart' behavior, and other times you don't.
posts.key_insight: A 'smart' feature that behaves inconsistently based on invisible state is worse than a dumb feature that behaves predictably every time, because it violates the GUI principle that what you see should determine what you get.

posts.summary: Gruber investigates Think Secret's report that Apple disbanded the Aperture development team, confirming through his own sources that the team was dispersed but pushing back on the claim that Aperture's future is 'in question.' He acknowledges that Aperture 1.0's development was a disaster — nine months late and described internally as a 'cluster fuck' — but argues the product's innovative UI design makes it worth saving. He cites Steve Jobs's personal enthusiasm for Aperture and Adobe Lightroom's competitive pressure as reasons Apple will fix rather than abandon it. He concludes with a critique of Think Secret's writing style, arguing that Apple gossip should be fun tabloid writing, not dreary faux-objective journalism.
key_points: Aperture's development was genuinely troubled — nine months late, with the team subsequently dispersed — but Think Secret's headline overstated the severity by claiming the team was 'axed'
Despite its performance problems, Aperture's UI and interaction model are innovative and represent the leading edge of Apple's design work
It's generally easier to fix a great UI with performance problems than to fix great performance with a terrible UI
Steve Jobs was personally invested in Aperture, making its continuation a certainty despite the development mess
Think Secret and similar Apple rumor sites undermine their own scoops by writing in dull, faux-objective journalism-ese instead of embracing the fun tabloid tone their gossip content demands
money_quotes: The term 'cluster fuck' is not generally used to describe minor hiccups.
It's generally easier to fix an app with a great UI suffering from performance problems than an app with great performance suffering from a terrible UI.
The user interface and interaction model of Aperture aren't just good — they're innovative.
There's no question what Apple is going to do with Aperture: they're going to fix it. The questions are just how, who, and when.
Let's face it — it's gossip. And gossip ought to be fun; not necessarily funny, but fun.
Think Secret just comes across as dull even when they're serving up a juicy scoop.
Calling it a 'photo touch-up program' shows that Cringely has no idea what Aperture actually is.
posts.key_insight: A troubled development process doesn't doom a product when the underlying design vision is strong and leadership is committed to fixing it.

posts.summary: Gruber systematically dismantles an Associated Press article by Dan Goodin that portrays Mac OS X as increasingly vulnerable to viruses. He shows how the piece inflates a single incident — one person who manually opened a poorly-written Trojan horse that failed to spread — into a trend piece about rising Mac security threats.
