Gruber exposes the article's rhetorical tricks: conflating theoretical vulnerability with actual risk, elevating unproven claims from 'may allow' to 'potentially allowing criminals,' and treating a chemical engineer's uninformed opinions as expert testimony. He notes the irony of comparing Apple's security situation to Microsoft's when the entire malware epidemic is essentially a Windows problem. The piece is timed to counter Apple's new 'Get a Mac' ad campaign highlighting Mac security advantages.
key_points: The AP wire story created a false impression of multiple corroborating reports when it was a single article republished by dozens of outlets under different alarming headlines
The actual incident involved one person who manually downloaded and opened a Trojan horse (Oompa-Loompa) that was so poorly written it couldn't successfully spread — affecting at most two people
The article conflates theoretical vulnerability (Macs could in theory get malware) with practical reality (Macs are not under attack), which is the central dishonesty of the piece
Security bugs reported by Tom Ferris were escalated from 'may allow code execution' (unproven) to 'potentially allowing criminals to gain access to passwords' — a journalistic sleight of hand
The claim that Intel processors make Macs more vulnerable is nonsensical, since Linux and FreeBSD run on the same processors without Windows-like malware problems
money_quotes: What's great about an AP story like this one is that if you're only paying attention to the headlines, it creates the impression that there are multiple reports from all over the web corroborating the same point, when in fact it's just one story, repeated many times over by news publications that regurgitate whatever comes in over the AP wire.
Good god, now they're going after entire companies.
Perhaps someday I'll have an opportunity to make equally informed statements regarding chemical engineering — a subject about which I am utterly ignorant — in an Associated Press report.
And oomph — not facts or accuracy — is at the heart of every good story.
"Blasted" is a great word. Much more exciting than something more accurate, such as "sent as an attachment".

If Goodin wanted to be reasonable or accurate, he could have written a story titled 'Some Guy Double-Clicked a Trojan Horse Virus for Mac OS X but It Didn't Actually Spread to Anyone Else', but what kind of story would that be? OK, it'd be a true story, but it wouldn't be a good story.
I'll bet that means all the other operating systems that run on Intel-compatible x86 processors, such as Linux and FreeBSD, are just as susceptible to malware as Windows.
posts.key_insight: The AP story exemplifies how tech journalism manufactures fear by conflating theoretical vulnerability with actual risk, inflating a single failed Trojan horse incident into a narrative about rising Mac security threats.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier report about Apple's Aperture development team troubles, now armed with first-hand sources including an original team engineer. He reveals that no engineers were fired — they left voluntarily due to spectacularly bad management. The core problem was a classic Mythical Man-Month blunder: the team ballooned from 20 to 150 people in weeks during the 1.0 homestretch, making everything worse. The only people who were actually fired or pushed out were the engineering manager and project manager. Gruber uses this to directly contradict Think Secret's reporting, which he says unfairly blamed the engineers rather than management.
key_points: Original Aperture engineers left voluntarily due to bad management, none were fired or removed
The team suffered a textbook Mythical Man-Month disaster, growing from 20 to 150 people in weeks in a misguided attempt to ship faster
The only people fired were the engineering manager and project manager, not any engineers
Aperture was never without an engineering team — the new team was assembled before the original left
Think Secret's original report was heavily slanted against the engineering team and factually wrong in its lead sentence
money_quotes: No one from the original Aperture engineering team was fired or removed from the team. Yes, most members of the original engineering team left the project — but they did so on their own volition. Why? Because of what can only be described as spectacularly bad management.
It is astounding that there are software engineering managers who are either unaware of the Mythical Man-Month theory, or who are but believe it doesn't apply to their project.

The product team grew from about 20 to 150 in just a few weeks, comprising about 100 engineers, 40 QA people, and the rest manager types. And not surprisingly, this is precisely when things went from bad to worse with regard to the quality of the product.
The only person from the Aperture team who was fired from the company was the engineering manager.
I'll leave any conclusions regarding the identity of Think Secret's sources to your imagination.
posts.key_insight: Apple's Aperture debacle was a management failure — a textbook Mythical Man-Month disaster — not an engineering failure, and reporting that blamed the engineers got the story exactly backwards.

posts.summary: Gruber explains the .htaccess configuration he uses to standardize Daring Fireball's URLs, covering two specific cases: redirecting 'www.daringfireball.net' to the prefix-less 'daringfireball.net', and redirecting 'daringfireball.com' to 'daringfireball.net'. He walks through the Apache mod_rewrite and mod_alias syntax in detail, explaining RewriteCond, RewriteRule, and RedirectMatch directives. The post is a practical tutorial but filtered through Gruber's characteristic opinions about web conventions, including his dislike of the 'www' prefix and '.com' TLD.
key_points: The 'www' prefix is a goofy, vestigial convention from when people briefly called it the 'World Wide Web' before settling on just 'the Web'
Apache mod_rewrite with permanent (301) redirects is the proper way to canonicalize domain names and eliminate www prefixes
RedirectMatch (mod_alias) and RewriteRule (mod_rewrite) achieve similar results but differ in how they handle the leading slash in request strings
Registering alternate domain variants defensively prevents domain squatters from exploiting common user typos
Permanent 301 redirects are preferable to temporary 302s because intelligent clients will remember and skip the redirect on subsequent requests
money_quotes: I've always rather disliked the "www" prefix for web servers. It has become so common that everyone is accustomed to both seeing and saying it, and so it's sort of invisible now, but it really is a goofy convention.
But why be lazy?

I can't quite explain why I decided to use "daringfireball.net" instead of "daringfireball.com" as the canonical domain for Daring Fireball. Suffice it to say I have some sort of irrational dislike for ".com".
The last thing I wanted was for some asshole domain squatter to register "daringfireball.com", so I registered the ".com" version too.

People only called it the "World Wide Web" for about a month or so back in 1994 before we all agreed to just call it the "Web", but yet we're stuck with the lame "www" prefix for the rest of our lives.
I'd never use a web host who didn't enable mod_rewrite.
posts.key_insight: Canonical URL standardization through permanent redirects is both a matter of web hygiene and an expression of taste in how you present your domain to the world.

posts.summary: Gruber provides a three-week update on his decision to go full-time with Daring Fireball, sharing a membership revenue graph and explaining his financial goal structure. He plans to fund the site through roughly equal thirds: memberships/t-shirts, advertising, and other initiatives. Revenue has reached about 80 percent of his initial membership drive goal. He then pivots to a broader observation about online monetization: people are far more willing to pay for physical goods (atoms) than digital content (bits), which explains why t-shirt sales dominate his revenue. He contrasts his freely-available content model with the New York Times's TimesSelect paywall, noting that even he was reluctant to pay $50/year for content he read daily.
key_points: Gruber's financial plan divides Daring Fireball revenue into roughly equal thirds: memberships/shirts, advertising, and other initiatives
T-shirt sales consistently outperform pure membership/donation revenue, illustrating the 'atoms vs. bits' principle
People have a deep psychological reluctance to pay for digital content even when the per-use cost is trivial
Gruber's model keeps all content free, making memberships voluntary support rather than gated access — a key distinction from paywalls
Even at 80 percent of his goal, Gruber is transparent about needing continued support, countering assumptions that he's already set
money_quotes: People are more willing to spend money for atoms than for bits.
You can't touch ones and zeroes.

Despite the fact that the reason you fine readers are buying these shirts is that you want to support my work, I don't think I'd be nearly as successful if I were only asking for donations or selling memberships.
This isn't criticism; it's just an observation about human nature.

$50 a year isn't a lot of money for something I read at least 300 times per year — it works out to like 15 cents a day — but yet I was reluctant to sign-up because it just didn't seem right to have to pay for something to read on the web.
That's what's so gratifying about the membership system here — readers aren't signing up because they have to. They're signing up because they want to.
posts.key_insight: People will readily pay for physical goods to support digital work they value, but resist paying for digital content directly — a fundamental tension in online monetization that favors hybrid models over pure paywalls.

posts.summary: Gruber tears apart Real Networks CEO Rob Glaser's interview in The Guardian, where Glaser dismisses Apple as a competitor while positioning Microsoft as Real's only rival. Gruber highlights how Glaser's strategy amounts to waiting for non-Apple portable devices to become compelling, which only gives Apple more time to dominate. He catches Glaser contradicting himself — admitting iPods drive music sales while blaming the music industry for allowing non-interoperable devices. Gruber is particularly scathing about Glaser's claim that piracy is the only way to get interoperable music, pointing out DRM-free stores like eMusic exist. The piece exposes Glaser's talking points as recycled Microsoft propaganda and his business strategy as wishful inaction.
key_points: Glaser conspicuously avoids acknowledging Apple as a competitor, referring only to Microsoft as a rival despite Apple's 65% European download market share
Real's stated strategy of waiting for better non-Apple portable devices is self-defeating — every year of waiting lets Apple sell more iPods and lock in more customers
Glaser contradicts himself by admitting iPods drive iTunes sales while arguing the music industry erred by allowing non-interoperable devices
The claim that piracy is the only way to get interoperable music is false — DRM-free stores like eMusic already existed
Accusing prospective customers of being thieves is borrowed from Microsoft's playbook and is unlikely to be an effective business strategy
money_quotes: I'm always left with the impression that he's either a moron or a liar. (My money is on liar.)

I'm willing to cut him some slack because it's his job as CEO to be an advocate for his company and its shareholders. I certainly don't expect him to stand up and admit that Apple has them licked. But that's no justification for just making shit up.
Translation: 'We're fucked, because there aren't any cool gadgets that play our DRM-protected audio formats.'
Just keep waiting. The longer Real waits, the more iPods Apple sells, and, uh, somehow that'll make it easier to succeed at some unspecified point in the future.
And Real's core business is what? 'Waiting'?
The music industry has had no role in the success of the iPod.
And these so-called 'friends' are probably shoplifting their CDs, right?
I'm intrigued by Glaser's strategy of accusing millions of prospective customers of being dishonest thieves.
posts.key_insight: Rob Glaser's strategy of ignoring Apple, waiting indefinitely, and parroting Microsoft talking points about piracy reveals a CEO who has already lost but won't admit it.

posts.summary: Gruber reports that his Daring Fireball membership drive is just one pixel short of its fundraising goal, with only hours remaining. He thanks supporters for a late-week boost in signups and notes he'll likely reach the goal through continued t-shirt sales. An update confirms he made the goal. The remainder of the post provides practical sizing details for the three Daring Fireball t-shirt designs available to members.
key_points: The membership drive reached 97% of its goal with hours to spare, literally one pixel short on the progress graph
A late-week boost in signups helped close the gap, and he ultimately hit the goal by Saturday morning
Three t-shirt designs were available: a charcoal 'classic,' a black 'Your PC is a POS,' and an 'I Love DF' ringer tee
money_quotes: I swear, with no fudging at all, it's *one pixel* short of my goal.
97 percent of the way there — that's pretty good.
It'd be nice to get that pixel and hit the goal before the drive officially closes.
posts.key_insight: Gruber's membership drive to go full-time with Daring Fireball came down to the wire but succeeded, marking a milestone in independent web publishing as a viable career.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Tim Bray's criticism of iCal after Bray lost his calendar data to file corruption following a system lockup. While agreeing the data loss is unacceptable, Gruber methodically investigates Bray's specific technical claims and finds some of them off-base — iCal's use of UUIDs for folder names is defensible, and its file-saving behavior already appears to use atomic writes rather than in-place updates. However, Gruber agrees with Bray's core argument: iCal should keep backup revisions of calendar data so that corruption doesn't mean total loss. The real failure isn't the corruption itself but iCal's lack of recovery capabilities.
key_points: iCal's use of UUIDs for calendar folder names is a reasonable design choice that prevents name collisions and survives renames, not the design flaw Bray suggests
Investigation using hfsdebug shows iCal already uses atomic saves (writing to new files rather than updating in-place), contrary to Bray's assumption
The real problem is iCal's failure to keep backup revisions of calendar data files, which would make corruption recoverable
Calendar data is high-value and small — there's no excuse for not maintaining at least one backup revision for disaster recovery
Most users wouldn't know where to find calendar data files or how to recover them, making iCal's lack of built-in recovery especially harmful
money_quotes: Bray's basic criticism as to why this shouldn't be possible is sound. But I don't think he has it quite right.
These iCal data file folders aren't meant to be end-user-serviceable files; you're meant to interact with them solely through iCal, not the Finder.
Bray's fourth point is the crux of the matter, however. There's no reason iCal couldn't keep the previous file around.
Losing an entire calendar is potentially devastating; losing the changes from your current iCal session would be, in most cases, merely annoying.
Disk space is cheap, calendar files are small, and calendar data is, in many cases, very important.
The problem is its meager data corruption recovery capabilities.
Wise users take this into their own hands, and back up all of their important data on their own, every day. But most people don't back up regularly.
posts.key_insight: The real failure in iCal's design isn't that data corruption can happen, but that it offers no recovery path when it does — keeping backup revisions of small, high-value calendar files is an obvious safeguard that's inexcusably absent.

posts.summary: Gruber investigates whether Apple's web rendering framework should be spelled 'Web Kit' or 'WebKit', documenting rampant inconsistency across Apple's own documentation and developer pages. He traces the pattern of Cocoa framework naming conventions, noting that official names like Application Kit, I/O Kit, and PDF Kit all include spaces, while their abbreviated forms (like AppKit) close the gap. He concludes that 'WebKit' is likely an error born from the AppKit abbreviation convention and the influence of the closed-up 'WebCore', but ultimately just wants Apple to pick one and stick with it.
key_points: Apple's own developer pages use both 'Web Kit' and 'WebKit' nearly side by side, with different teams preferring different spellings
Cocoa naming convention consistently uses spaces in kit names (I/O Kit, PDF Kit, Search Kit), suggesting 'Web Kit' is technically correct
The closed-up 'WebKit' likely arose by analogy with 'AppKit', the accepted abbreviation of Application Kit, but unlike that case it doesn't actually abbreviate anything
The open source WebKit developers have settled on the closed-up spelling, which dominates in Google results despite being arguably incorrect
money_quotes: As a stickler for detail and consistency, this has been driving me nuts ever since the framework was announced.
Apple's inconsistency regarding whether there's a space in the name is borderline comical.

But unlike Application Kit/AppKit, which feels like a legitimate and useful abbreviation in that the short form is both easier to pronounce and to type, Web Kit/WebKit just looks like inconsistent spelling.
They're pronounced the same, and unless you have two broken thumbs, it'd be hard to argue that "WebKit" is easier to type.
Plug them into GoogleFight and "WebKit" absolutely kicks "Web Kit"'s ass; but that doesn't make it right.
My gut feeling is thus that "WebKit" is a mistake. But I don't really care which spelling Apple settles on, I just want it settled.
posts.key_insight: Apple's failure to consistently spell its own framework's name reveals a tension between formal naming conventions and developer culture that only consistency can resolve.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Microsoft and Apple, while not direct competitors, are 'orthogonal rivals' whose fortunes have inversely tracked each other. In the mid-2000s, Microsoft suffers from a deep malaise around Windows Vista and a loss of corporate identity now that it lacks clear external enemies to defeat. Apple, meanwhile, is brimming with confidence — exemplified by the new MacBooks that offer flagship-level performance at consumer prices, showing Apple is pursuing market share over profit margins. Gruber contends that Microsoft's problems stem from middle management complexity and a culture that values bureaucratic decisions over design clarity, as evidenced by Vista's seven confusing editions versus Apple's simplified product line. He provocatively argues that a strong Vista would actually benefit Mac users by pressuring Apple to innovate.
key_points: Microsoft's greatest competitor is now its own older products — it must convince users to upgrade rather than defeat external rivals, which undermines the competitive identity that historically drove the company.
A weak Windows Vista is bad for Mac users because competitive pressure from Windows is what forces Apple to improve Mac OS X.

Apple's confidence is demonstrated by the new MacBooks offering near-MacBook-Pro performance at $1099, pursuing volume over per-unit profit margins — a reversal of the old iBook-era strategy of deliberately crippling cheaper machines.
Microsoft's plan to ship Vista in seven editions epitomizes a company that values management complexity over design clarity.
Microsoft's malaise is a management problem, not a talent problem — too many dependencies and too little accountability make it impossible to identify which teams are actually failing.
money_quotes: But it won't be good for Mac users if Vista turns out to be a dud. It might be good for Apple, but it won't be good for Mac users.
Microsoft, as a company, defines itself by its rivalries.
To sell new copies of Microsoft Office, they need to beat older copies of Microsoft Office.
Microsoft is suffering from a lack of confidence, and Apple is brimming with it.
Microsoft is turning into a company that values management decisions that increase complexity over design decisions that increase clarity.
This is how a company with so many talented programmers can spend six years developing an operating system that no one is excited about.
It is difficult to get a man to understand something when his salary depends upon his not understanding it.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's loss of external rivals has created an identity crisis and management bloat that produces complexity instead of innovation, while Apple's confidence is driving it toward simpler products, lower prices, and greater market share.

posts.summary: Gruber uses the analogy of high-end bicycles and cameras to frame Mark Pilgrim's decision to switch from Mac OS X to Ubuntu Linux. He argues that evaluating 'better' is inherently subjective and multi-dimensional — what matters depends on which criteria you value. Pilgrim's central concern is openness and long-term data preservation, not that Mac OS X has a bad UI. Gruber criticizes Mac users who dismiss Pilgrim's argument out of hand, comparing their dogmatism to Windows users who dismiss the Mac's UI advantages. He concedes Pilgrim has valid points about Apple's calculated indifference to openness, while noting that if Apple's lack of openness were truly disastrous, the disaster would have already occurred after 22 years.
key_points: Evaluating 'better' is subjective and multi-dimensional — a cheap bike can be better than an expensive one depending on your needs, and the same applies to operating systems
Most commenters missed Pilgrim's central argument about openness and long-term data preservation, instead fixating on trivial points about backups and UI quality
There's an unbecoming tendency among Mac users to deny that any other OS could be better in any way, mirroring how Windows users dismiss Mac UI advantages
Apple's stance on openness appears highly calculated — open only so far as necessary to be perceived as being open, a marketing stance rather than an engineering one
The strongest counter-argument to Pilgrim is that if Apple's lack of openness were truly catastrophic, it would have manifested already in his 22 years of use
money_quotes: It's generally pretty easy for most people to perceive the difference between the mediocre and the good, but it often requires expertise or finely-honed sensibility to perceive the difference between the good and the great.
"Better" is a complicated adjective, because it is subjective.
Just because evaluating "better-ness" is subjective does not mean it is arbitrary.

I'm deeply suspicious of Mac users who claim to be perfectly happy with Mac OS X. Real Mac users, to me, are people with much higher standards, impossibly high standards, and who use Macs not because they're great, but because they suck less than everything else.

Telling Pilgrim that he's making a mistake because Ubuntu doesn't have as refined or cohesive a UI as Mac OS X is like telling someone who is switching from a Chevy Tahoe to a Toyota Prius that he's not going to have as much cargo room. He knows it.
Apple's stance on openness is in fact highly calculated, and that the calculation is that Apple should be open only so far as necessary to be perceived as being open.
An ideology is an organized system of beliefs; just because you don't share them doesn't mean they aren't valid.
"Better" for Mark Pilgrim doesn't mean better for you, and nowhere has Pilgrim implied that it does.
posts.key_insight: Dismissing someone's informed decision to leave your preferred platform, rather than engaging with their actual arguments, reveals the same dogmatism you accuse the other side of having.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Tim Bray's suggestion that Apple should open source its bundled applications like Mail, Safari, iCal, and iChat. While acknowledging Bray's valid points — that the apps' competitive advantage lies in UI design rather than source code, and that the code would be valuable for developer education — Gruber argues Apple won't do it because these apps serve as upgrade incentives. New features in bundled apps are key selling points for each Mac OS X release, and open sourcing them would allow outside developers to backport features to older versions, undermining Apple's lucrative upgrade cycle. He frames this as the fundamental dichotomy between closed and open source development models.
key_points: Apple's bundled apps serve as major selling points for OS upgrades — open sourcing them would let developers backport new features to older versions, reducing upgrade incentive

Bray is correct that the apps' competitive strength lies in UI design, not source code — anyone can copy the best ideas just by looking at them — but that misses the real competitive threat: competition with Apple's own previous OS versions
WebKit being open source while Safari remains closed perfectly illustrates Apple's strategy: share the framework, keep the product
Open source software tends to improve in small steady increments while commercial software improves in large gulps designed to entice paid upgrades — fundamentally different development rhythms
The bundled apps transformed Mac OS X releases into events with midnight release parties, something the classic Mac OS never achieved
money_quotes: He overlooks another form of competition: existing versions of Mac OS X.
The role these apps play isn't just to make Mac OS X look good compared to Windows or Linux, but also to help make each new version of Mac OS X look better than the previous one.
This is one reason why Web Kit, the rendering framework, is open source, but Safari, the application, is not.
How many software products can you think of that have users lining up around the block for a midnight release party?

In short, releasing the source to these apps would be a risk. Not a risk with a catastrophic downside, but a risk nonetheless. And the potential upside — the best case scenario from Apple's perspective — wouldn't result in any additional sales.
While open source software is, by definition, eminently tweakable, it also, in general, is less likely to get to the point of being very cool and really good in the first place.

Open source software tends to improve in small, steady, frequent increments. Established commercial software tends to improve less frequently but in large gulps so as to entice users to pay for upgrades.
But don't hold your breath.
posts.key_insight: Apple won't open source its bundled apps not because competitors might copy them, but because doing so would let developers backport new features to older OS versions, undermining Apple's most effective upgrade incentive.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles the British Phonographic Industry's call for Apple to make iTunes DRM 'interoperable' with other music players, arguing that interoperability and DRM are fundamentally incompatible. He walks through the practical options — Apple licensing FairPlay, Apple adopting Windows Media DRM, or dropping DRM entirely — and shows that only the last one actually achieves interoperability. He points out the irony that the record industry itself insisted on DRM, which handed Apple its dominant position, and now complains about the lock-in they created. The real solution is obvious: sell music without DRM, which already works everywhere.
key_points: DRM is inherently opposed to interoperability — you cannot have both, because DRM's entire purpose is restricting playback to authorized systems
The record industry created Apple's lock-in advantage by insisting on DRM in the first place, then complained about the resulting market dominance
The only practical path to interoperability is removing DRM and selling music in standard formats like AAC or MP3
Mainstream media uncritically parrots the entertainment industry's calls for 'DRM interoperability' as though it were a reasonable technical request
Record industry executives refuse to accept they will never regain complete control over music distribution, and this delusion drives their incoherent policy demands
money_quotes: Apple can't 'just play music from other stores'; the whole point of DRM is that there's secret juju encrypting the data in the files.
The music industry's insistence upon DRM is what put the ITMS in the position that Apple now enjoys; the record industry is decrying a lock-in advantage that they themselves handed to Apple.
The very nature of DRM is that it is diametrically opposed to interoperability.
Record industry executives refuse to believe what is patently obvious to anyone with a clue — they are never ever going to regain complete control over the distribution of recorded music.
Calling for 'interoperability' without any practical suggestion as to how it could be achieved is just an empty platitude.
But unlike the energy problem or cancer, digital media interoperability is not an intractable problem. There's an obvious solution staring everyone in the face.
You're just fucking nuts if you think Apple is going to do that.
posts.key_insight: The record industry's simultaneous demands for DRM and interoperability are logically contradictory — they created the lock-in they now decry by refusing to sell music without copy protection.

posts.summary: Gruber challenges the widely-held belief that Mac OS X caused a dramatic surge in Mac users, arguing with quarterly sales data that Apple sold roughly one million Macs per quarter both in the late 1990s and mid-2000s. He contends that Apple's financial struggles in the '90s were caused by inept management, not an inability to sell Macs, and that the Mac never lost its stronghold in creative markets. The real difference with Mac OS X, he argues, is not that it replaced something bad with something great, but that it broadened the Mac's appeal to new audiences like Unix nerds and programmers. He acknowledges that genuine switching is underway but hasn't yet reached a mainstream tipping point. He also pushes back against both the myopia of recent switchers who assume no one cared about Macs before OS X, and the smugness of long-time Mac users who dismiss anyone who wasn't already on board.
key_points: Apple's quarterly Mac sales were roughly one million units in both the late 1990s and mid-2000s, debunking the narrative of a dramatic OS X-driven sales surge
Apple's financial crisis in the '90s was caused by bad management, not declining Mac sales — the creative market stronghold never wavered
Mac OS X's real achievement was broadening appeal rather than replacing something broken — its Unix core attracted nerds while Aqua attracted aesthetes
The Mac was already dominant in web authoring and creative fields in the late '90s, with BBEdit commanding 50% of all web designers
Both recent switchers and long-time Mac users hold myopic views of Mac history that overstate their own group's significance
money_quotes: Apple isn't selling significantly more computers now than they were 10 years ago.
Apple's financial struggles weren't caused by an inability to sell Macs — they were caused by inept management.
The Mac users of today are, by and large, the Mac users of 10 years ago.
The difference between the old Mac OS and Mac OS X isn't that it used to suck but now it's great. The difference is that Mac OS X's appeal is broader; it is good in more ways than the old Mac OS was.
What makes nerds 'nerds' is that they care to an irrational depth about certain specific things; Mac OS X covers more of those things than did Mac OS 9, and so it appeals to many more nerds.
Today, it's hard to find someone serious who thinks Macs 'suck', or that old chestnut that they're 'toy computers'.
You can't appeal to all people all the time, but Mac OS X comes remarkably close. The old Mac OS, as insanely great as it was, did not.
Switching is afoot — but it hasn't yet reached the tipping point where it goes mainstream.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X didn't rescue Apple from irrelevance — it broadened the Mac's appeal beyond its already-loyal creative base to include the nerd and programmer markets that had previously dismissed it.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Tim Bray's argument that Apple should open-source its apps by acknowledging Bray's point that it wouldn't hurt upgrade sales, but arguing the practical complications (forking, Software Update collisions, inter-app integration breakage) make it unworkable. He then reframes the debate entirely: Mac OS X is already deeply hackable through scripting, plug-ins, and Cocoa's dynamic runtime — open source isn't the right mechanism for the tweaks power users want. The real problem is that Apple's AppleScript support is inconsistent and many apps lack proper scripting dictionaries or documented plug-in APIs. Gruber concludes that Apple should invest in better scriptability and extensibility rather than opening source code, because scripts and plug-ins are easier to share, don't interfere with system updates, and solve the actual user need.
key_points: Open-sourcing Apple's apps would create practical nightmares: Software Update collisions, broken inter-app integration, and pressure to replace rather than extend official apps.

Mac OS X is already highly hackable through AppleScript, plug-in APIs, Cocoa's dynamic Objective-C runtime, and Unix scripting — the same extensibility model that makes Firefox and Emacs successful despite being open source.

Even open source flagships like Firefox and Emacs are primarily extended through scripting and plug-ins, not by users compiling custom binaries — proving that extensibility APIs matter more than source access.
Apple's real failing is inconsistent AppleScript support across its own apps, with some apps like Preview lacking even basic scripting dictionaries.
The argument that 'most users don't write scripts' misses the point — one person writes kGTD and thousands of OmniOutliner users benefit.
money_quotes: Mac OS X is a very hackable platform; it just isn't hackable by means of the raw source code to the software.
I don't want the source code to their software; I just want to be able to script the hell out of it.
The benefit of good scripting support isn't derived only by those users who write scripts, but also by those who use them.
Firefox would be a lot more hackable if its source code were closed but the extension interface remained than it would be if the source were kept open but the extension interface were removed.
'Try my script' is a lot more appealing than 'try my custom binary of iCal'.
It only took one Ethan Schoonover to write kGTD, but there are now thousands of OmniOutliner Pro users using it.
Software license agreements are full of bullshit restrictions that aren't enforceable and are contrary to established copyright law.

For Apple and Mac OS X, the sweet spot is providing simple, clear, consistent default features and behaviors for typical users, but providing as much extensibility as possible for hackers to play with.
posts.key_insight: The real solution to power users' frustrations with Apple software isn't open source — it's consistent, well-documented scripting and plug-in APIs that let one person's small hack benefit thousands of users without the chaos of forking.

posts.summary: Gruber uses his recurring Magic 8-Ball Q&A format to dissect Microsoft's newly confirmed Zune project — an integrated music player, store, and software platform clearly modeled after Apple's iPod/iTunes ecosystem. He highlights the absurdity of Microsoft launching a closed platform that's incompatible with their own PlaysForSure initiative, effectively betraying their licensing partners. He skewers the transparently staged 'leaks,' the shameless iPod-mimicking industrial design, and the implausibly ambitious feature list for a November ship date with no working prototype. While acknowledging Microsoft's one real advantage — effectively unlimited cash to subsidize hardware and buy out iTunes libraries — he concludes that money can't buy taste or the simplicity that makes Apple's products successful.
key_points: Microsoft's Zune is wholly incompatible with their own PlaysForSure platform, effectively abandoning partners they recruited to that ecosystem
The Zune's leaked design is such a transparent iPod copy that it can only aspire to match current iPod coolness while Apple moves ahead with new designs
The ambitious November ship date and extensive feature list with no working prototype shown suggests classic Microsoft vaporware tactics designed to freeze the market
Microsoft's real competitive advantage is its cash hoard — capable of subsidizing hardware losses and buying replacement copies of users' iTunes purchases
Despite billions in resources, Microsoft has never demonstrated the design taste and simplicity needed to compete with Apple in consumer electronics
money_quotes: Isn't that like inviting guests to your home for dinner and serving them hot dogs while you yourself eat a steak?
Does anyone at Microsoft realize that rip-offs aren't considered cool?
They can't buy taste, but they can burn money.
There's something perverse about the fact that the only recent brand-new product from Microsoft that's considered a success is the Xbox, which lost $4 billion from 2001-2005.
If their $60 billion bank account hasn't yet helped Microsoft develop a penchant for the simplicity/clarity/elegance thing, it never will, will it?

So we hear that you're worried about the growing leverage that Apple is accumulating in the digital media space, and that you're looking for a partner who can be trusted not to use such an advantage unfairly or to bludgeon competitors. How about Microsoft?
The best they can do with such a design strategy is to approach the coolness of the current iPods; whereas Apple itself is always at work on new designs that make their own current ones look dated.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's Zune project exemplifies how even unlimited financial resources cannot substitute for taste and design coherence — copying Apple's integrated model while simultaneously betraying your own partners and shipping vaporware is a strategy doomed by its own contradictions.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Brian Krebs's Washington Post reporting on a supposed MacBook Wi-Fi exploit, questioning the credibility of claims that the MacBook's built-in AirPort drivers are vulnerable. The security researchers demonstrated the exploit using a third-party wireless card while merely claiming the built-in drivers were also affected, without proving it. Gruber highlights the critical distinction between reporting what someone told you and reporting verified facts. He argues that Krebs's follow-up raises more questions than it answers, leaving Mac users without actionable information about which configurations are actually vulnerable. Gruber also questions the implausible narrative that Apple 'leaned on' the researchers to use a third-party card while still allowing them to publicly claim the built-in drivers were exploitable. The sensationalist headline and anti-Mac quotes from the researchers further undermine the reporting's credibility.
key_points: Krebs reported researchers' claims about built-in AirPort vulnerability as fact without personally verifying the exploit worked on stock hardware
The narrative that Apple pressured researchers to use a third-party card while allowing them to publicly claim built-in drivers were exploitable makes no logical sense
Using a third-party card in the demo actually makes the situation worse by falsely reassuring most MacBook users they're safe
Krebs's reporting fails to answer basic security questions: which models are affected, whether default configurations are vulnerable, and what users can do to protect themselves
The sensationalist headline and hostile anti-Mac quotes from the researchers contradict Krebs's claim that the story wasn't meant to pick on Macs
money_quotes: "I stand by that they told me the built-in driver is exploitable" is very different than "I stand by that the built-in driver is exploitable".
If they're willing to say that the built-in driver is exploitable, why are they not willing to prove it?

Basing their demo video on a third-party card makes matters worse, not better, because it creates the perception that the majority of MacBook users are safe because they aren't using third-party cards.
Krebs's shoddy reporting leaves nearly all the important questions regarding this exploit unanswered.
Aren't these questions completely obvious?
What does this mean, that Apple "leaned on Maynor and Ellch pretty hard"? Were they threatened? By whom at Apple, exactly? And how? With a lawsuit? With violence?
With a headline like "Hijacking a Macbook in 60 Seconds or Less," where would anyone get the idea that the point of Krebs's post was to pick on Macs?
posts.key_insight: Reporting unverified claims as established facts—especially about security vulnerabilities—is not journalism but amplification, and the burden of proof lies with those making extraordinary claims.

posts.summary: Gruber presents a detailed technical argument that Mac OS X's default keyboard-based list selection model (unanchored, where Shift-arrow always extends the selection) is inferior to the anchored model (where reversing direction shrinks the selection). He documents that nearly all Mac software uses the unanchored model because it's the default in Cocoa's NSTableView and Carbon's Data Browser controls, yet he received zero emails from anyone who actually prefers it. The core problem is that unanchored selection makes it impossible to recover from overshooting without starting over or reaching for the mouse. He argues for consistency with text selection (which is anchored) and mouse-only selection (also anchored), and calls on Apple to update the HIG and default controls accordingly. In a postscript, he notes Apple did exactly this in Leopard.
key_points: Unanchored selection (the Mac OS X default for lists) makes it impossible to correct overshooting with the keyboard alone — you must start over or use the mouse
Keyboard-only text selection and mouse-only list selection are both anchored, making the unanchored default for keyboard list selection an inconsistency within Apple's own frameworks
The theoretical justifications for unanchored selection (no ambiguity about anchor point, ability to extend both ends) are contrived scenarios that rarely arise in practice
The ease of recovering from mistakes is a fundamental barometer for software usability, and unanchored selection fails this test
Apple eventually validated Gruber's argument by switching NSTableView to anchored selection in Mac OS X 10.5 Leopard
money_quotes: The ease with which you can recover from mistakes is a good barometer for the overall usability of software.
I received not a single email from anyone who claims to prefer this behavior. Not one.

If you're willing to break with the default visual appearance for something that looks better, I don't see why you wouldn't be willing to break with the default behavior for something that works better.
It's not controversial; it's just baffling.

Think of the rubber-band selection mode in the Finder's icon view: it grows and shrinks as you drag back and forth. It would be goofy if it only grew and never shrank — but yet that's exactly how the unanchored keyboard-only selection shortcuts behave.
You suffer a large penalty (when you overshoot your intended range) in exchange for a small benefit (being able to grow both ends of the selection range).
AppKit is not the HIG.
posts.key_insight: Software defaults that make it hard to recover from small mistakes represent a fundamental usability failure, even when those defaults are sanctioned by the platform vendor.

posts.summary: Gruber offers his night-before predictions for Apple's WWDC 2006 Leopard announcements, while self-deprecatingly noting his poor track record. He expects a dramatically improved Spotlight (calling the current version terrible), the most significant UI overhaul since Mac OS X's debut, and enhanced Dashboard widgets. He predicts Apple will deliberately make Vista look dated by comparison. He also makes negative predictions: no system-level tabbed document windows despite developer demand, and no tabs in Finder or iChat.
key_points: Spotlight in 10.4 is deeply flawed in UI, performance, and reliability, and Gruber expects a major overhaul
Leopard's UI changes will be the most dramatic since 10.0, strategically timed to make Vista look outdated
Despite widespread developer demand for standardized tabbed document windows, Apple likely won't provide them since only Safari uses tabs among Apple's own apps
Apple will push Dashboard forward with desktop widgets and a Dashcode IDE to outcompete Vista and Yahoo widgets
money_quotes: Spotlight, as it exists in 10.4, pretty much totally stinks. The user interface stinks, performance stinks, and it's buggy as hell.
It's time to zig now that Microsoft is zagging with Vista.
They're going to try to make Vista look as out-of-fashion and goofy and childish as possible.
A lot of the things Apple has already moved away from in Aqua have been goofy, ugly, and childish, frankly — how about those stripes in 10.0 and 10.1?
My prognosticatory track record is rather spotty. Please, no wagering.
An attempt to blow away the competing widget implementations from Windows Vista and Yahoo (née Konfabulator).
posts.key_insight: Apple's Leopard strategy will be to make the biggest visual leap since OS X's debut, deliberately positioning Vista as already outdated before it ships.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes the hardware announcements from WWDC 2006, covering three angles: Apple's surging notebook market share (6% to 12%), the Mac Pro launch, and Apple's naming conventions. He argues notebook market share is a more meaningful metric than overall PC market share for gauging Apple's health, and that Boot Camp is driving switcher sales. He notes the Mac Pro was inevitable but its aggressive pricing was the real surprise. The bulk of the piece dissects Apple's consolidation of its product line under the 'Mac' brand, questioning why Xserve was excluded and why 'iMac' now feels slightly inconsistent.
key_points: Apple's notebook market share doubling from 6% to 12% is the most meaningful indicator of platform health, since notebook buyers are in Apple's actual competitive segment
The Intel switch makes Apple's hardware roadmap predictable, removing the element of surprise from system architecture announcements
The Mac Pro's aggressive pricing was the real surprise — Apple deliberately set out to 'bust the myth' that Macs cost more than comparable PCs
Apple's 'Mac' naming consolidation is complete except for Xserve, likely because Xserve customers may run non-Mac operating systems
The 'i' prefix has drifted from meaning 'Internet' to meaning 'fun consumer-level Apple product,' creating a subtle branding inconsistency with the iMac
money_quotes: All hail the Boot Camp 'even if I don't like the Mac OS I can just install Windows' safety net.
Notebook market share is much more indicative of the overall interest in the Mac platform.
I didn't even list 'Intel-based replacements for the Power Macs' on my WWDC predictions because it seemed too obvious, like predicting that Jobs would be wearing a black shirt.
I don't think anyone would have batted an eyelash if Apple had set the price on these new Mac Pros $500 higher. The actual prices strike me as very aggressive.

While nearly everyone agrees that in and of themselves, 'PowerBook' and 'iBook' are better-looking, better-sounding names than 'MacBook Pro' and 'MacBook', it's better for the overall platform brand to put all the hardware under the 'Mac' umbrella.
It's not like the Xserve is lost in a labyrinthian Dell-like product matrix: Apple sells two notebook brands, three desktops, and one server. That's it.
The 'i' has more or less come to mean 'fun consumer-level product from Apple', which really has no logical connection with the letter 'i'.
posts.key_insight: The Intel transition made Apple's hardware roadmap predictable, shifting the competitive surprise from architecture to pricing and branding — and Apple leveraged both aggressively at WWDC 2006.

posts.summary: Gruber posts a housekeeping update covering two topics: a bug fix for the site's cookie-based preferences system and an update on Daring Fireball t-shirt sales. The preferences feature, which lets readers set custom font sizes, had a bug causing cookies to expire at session end rather than persisting for years. He also reports that shirt and membership sales have been strong since his April announcement of going full-time, and announces that two shirt designs are being discontinued.
key_points: A long-standing cookie expiration bug in the site's preferences system has been fixed, allowing font size choices to finally persist across browser sessions
T-shirt and membership sales have been crucial to sustaining Daring Fireball as a full-time operation since Gruber's April 2006 announcement
Two shirt designs ('Your PC Is a POS' and 'I✪DF') are being discontinued to simplify inventory, with a final order deadline given
Gruber highlights the community aspect of DF readers sharing photos of themselves wearing the shirts on Flickr
money_quotes: Shirt and membership sales have been helping to keep this operation afloat, whilst I write and continue working behind the scenes on upcoming initiatives to generate more moola.
Still haven't seen one that tops Eric Case's, though — he took his DF shirt to the 20,000-foot-high summit of Stok Kangri in northern India.
So if you want one of these but have been procrastinating on ordering, you'll need to place your order by the end of the day tomorrow (Friday August 18) to guarantee availability.
My sincere thanks for everyone's continuing support (and patience with regard to shipping delays).
My thanks to everyone who has supported my work — especially to those of you who had to (or continue to) wait a bit longer than "several weeks" for your shirts to reach you.
posts.key_insight: This post captures the early bootstrapping era of Daring Fireball as an independent publishing business, sustained by reader merchandise purchases and memberships rather than traditional advertising.

posts.summary: Gruber awards Paul Thurrott his 'Jackass of the Week' honor for a hypocritical price comparison between Mac Pro and Dell Precision workstations. Thurrott initially configured the Dell with a cheaper processor, and even after correcting it, configured the Dell as a 32-bit system to keep its price lower. His justification that 64-bit 'isn't mainstream on Windows' directly contradicts his own writing from the previous week, where he praised Windows XP x64 Edition and criticized Apple for being two years behind on 64-bit support. Gruber highlights this blatant double standard as the defining reason for the award.
key_points: Thurrott's original Mac Pro vs. Dell comparison used mismatched processors, favoring Dell, and had to be corrected
Even after correction, Thurrott configured the Dell as a cheaper 32-bit system while the Mac Pro was 64-bit capable
Thurrott justified omitting 64-bit on the Dell by claiming it wasn't mainstream on Windows
Just one week earlier, Thurrott had touted Windows XP x64 Edition as proof Apple was two years behind Microsoft on 64-bit
The contradiction reveals a consistent bias: any framing that makes Apple look worse gets applied regardless of consistency
money_quotes: His original post, now corrected, showed the Dell coming out cheaper, but he inexplicably configured the Dell with a cheaper processor in that comparison.
In the updated post, with both systems configured with 3.0 GHz Xeon CPUs, the Mac Pro comes out $500 cheaper.
But even still, he has configured the Dell as a (cheaper) 32-bit system.
So when the feature was announced at WWDC, it was a catch-up feature that Thurrott claims put Apple two years behind Microsoft.
But when it would have made Dell look even worse in a side-by-side pricing comparison, it's 'not exactly a mainstream configuration'.
Jackass.
posts.key_insight: Thurrott's treatment of 64-bit computing as either a must-have advantage or an irrelevant detail depending on which framing makes Apple look worse reveals the intellectual dishonesty underlying his commentary.

posts.summary: Gruber meticulously dissects the saga of a supposed MacBook Wi-Fi security exploit demonstrated at Black Hat 2006 by researchers David Maynor and Jon Ellch. The demonstration used a third-party USB wireless card and third-party drivers, not the MacBook's built-in AirPort hardware, yet Washington Post reporter Brian Krebs reported it as a vulnerability in Apple's own drivers. Apple and Atheros both issued unequivocal statements that no exploit code or demonstration targeting built-in MacBook hardware had been shared with them. Gruber argues that either the researchers are frauds who exploited media credulity for publicity, or Apple is lying — and Apple has far more to lose from lying. He concludes that Maynor and Ellch likely failed to find an exploit against stock MacBook hardware and cynically chose the MacBook as a demo platform for maximum attention.
key_points: The Black Hat demo used a third-party USB Wi-Fi card and third-party drivers, not the MacBook's built-in AirPort hardware, making it irrelevant to actual MacBook users
Both Apple and Atheros issued unequivocal statements that Maynor and Ellch never shared exploit code or demonstrations targeting Apple's built-in wireless drivers
Brian Krebs's original reporting claimed Apple's own drivers were 'identically exploitable' based solely on Maynor's unverified claims, then Krebs quietly retreated from that position

ZDNet's George Ou defended the researchers with self-contradictory arguments, simultaneously claiming the exploit was only about third-party hardware while asserting built-in drivers were also vulnerable

Gruber applies Occam's Razor: the researchers likely tried and failed to exploit Apple's built-in drivers, then used a modified MacBook for publicity value while cultivating ambiguity about what was actually vulnerable
money_quotes: Someone, clearly, is either lying or incompetent (or both).

Not asking these questions indicates that Krebs is incompetent to write about computer security issues. It's like watching a magician perform a trick using his own deck of cards, and then not only not asking to see the trick performed with a deck of cards as-yet untouched by the magician, but not even asking whether the cards had been tampered with.
That this sort of basic middle-school-level logic should need to be painstakingly spelled out for the computer security columnist for the Washington Post is astounding.
Translation: 'I no longer stand by my own reporting.' Alternative translation: 'Oh boy am I fucked.'

This is one of the oldest tricks in the hack tech writer book: Because, yes, some small number of devoted Mac users are in fact kooks, when you begin receiving criticism after publishing some sort of false or inaccurate analysis regarding the Mac or Apple, you just dismiss it all on the grounds that all Mac users are irrational cultists.

It is a simple yes or no question: Have Maynor and Ellch found a vulnerability that affects MacBooks using Apple's built-in cards and drivers? That Maynor and Ellch haven't answered it speaks volumes.
A more reciprocally sensational but yet completely accurate headline might have been, say, 'Losing My Journalistic Integrity in 60 Seconds or Less'.
posts.key_insight: When security researchers refuse to plainly answer a simple yes-or-no question about what they actually found, and journalists report unverified claims as fact without demanding evidence, the resulting spectacle reveals more about the failures of tech journalism than about any actual vulnerability.

posts.summary: Gruber continues his skeptical examination of the alleged MacBook wireless security exploit demonstrated by David Maynor and Jon Ellch. He criticizes Washington Post columnist Brian Krebs for basing his reporting entirely on Maynor's claims without independently verifying the exploit. Gruber argues that computer security is a science requiring evidence, not just assertions. He warns that false alarms create a dangerous 'boy who cried wolf' effect that could cause Mac users to ignore genuine future vulnerabilities. He also highlights a suspicious technical detail in the demonstration video: the shell access landed in a user directory rather than root, suggesting the exploit may not be what was claimed.
key_points: Brian Krebs's Washington Post reporting on the exploit was based entirely on Maynor's claims without independent verification, making it worse than no reporting at all
False security alarms create a 'boy who cried wolf' effect that could cause Mac users to dismiss real future vulnerabilities
A technical detail in the demo video — the shell landing in a user directory rather than root — suggests the exploit was not a genuine root-level attack
Computer security claims require evidence and scientific rigor, not just someone's word
money_quotes: Computer security is a science. Science is conducted by gathering and evaluating evidence.
Just because someone says they've found a security flaw doesn't mean there is one.
Bad reporting on computer security is worse than no reporting at all.

A false alarm makes it more likely that if someone finds an actual serious security flaw against Mac OS X, Mac users will ignore it, thinking that it's another over-hyped non-issue like this one appears to be.
A root exploit would typically put the current directory at "/" — that is, the root level of the startup volume.
posts.key_insight: Security reporting based solely on unverified claims is worse than no reporting at all, because false alarms erode the credibility needed to respond to real threats.

posts.summary: Gruber tells a personal story about taking his two-and-a-half-year-old son Jonas on a boardwalk boat ride in Wildwood, New Jersey, during the final night of a family vacation. The boy is overtired and caught in a toddler's paradox of simultaneously wanting and not wanting to go on the ride. After brokering a compromise to return to their room and come back, Gruber carries Jonas back to the boardwalk for one last attempt. A tense moment arises when a woman tries to cut in line, threatening Jonas's chance at the one blue boat he's fixated on. Gruber silently stares her down, secures the boat, rides alongside his son holding hands, and carries the satisfied boy home.
key_points: The toddler paradox — wanting and not wanting the same thing simultaneously — is rendered with precise, affectionate observation
Gruber honors the deal with his son despite exhaustion and logistical pressure, because 'a deal's a deal'
The line-cutting confrontation is resolved through silent intensity rather than words, with Gruber's restraint masking a fierce protectiveness
The story builds narrative tension around something absurdly small — a seat on a children's boat ride — but the emotional stakes are completely real
The ending lands on quiet satisfaction rather than joy, perfectly matching the exhausted contentment of both father and son
money_quotes: The boy both wants and does not want to go on what he himself has named 'the boat ride'.
A deal's a deal.
She not only knew that we were in line before them, but she also knew that I knew that she knew this.
If you think you're getting in front of my boy you're out of your fucking mind.
It doesn't seem possible that a boy could be this tired but still awake.
He's too tired to be happy, but he is satisfied.
I squeeze him and kiss him, and I carry him home.
posts.key_insight: The fierce, quiet determination of parenthood reveals itself not in grand moments but in honoring small promises to an overtired toddler on a boardwalk carousel.

posts.summary: Gruber issues a formal public challenge to security researchers David Maynor and Jon Ellch, who claimed to have discovered a Wi-Fi vulnerability in MacBook's built-in AirPort card. He offers to buy a brand-new MacBook and let them keep it if they can remotely hijack it using only its default configuration. The challenge comes with detailed stipulations ensuring the test is fair and uses only out-of-the-box settings. Gruber frames this as a poker-style bet: he expects to be ignored because he believes the researchers demonstrated a class of potential exploit on a rigged machine rather than a real vulnerability. He argues the $1,099 cost is worth it either to confirm a genuine security risk or to call out what he suspects is inflated publicity at Mac security's expense.
key_points: Gruber believes Maynor and Ellch demonstrated a theoretical class of Wi-Fi exploit on a modified MacBook, not a real vulnerability in default hardware
The challenge's 14 stipulations are carefully designed to test only the stock MacBook configuration with no user intervention or rigged setup
Gruber frames the bet using poker logic: the knowledge gained is worth more than the money risked
The challenge is structured so the researchers would only accept if they can actually win, making non-acceptance itself informative
Gruber argues someone needs to hold security researchers accountable when they generate publicity at the expense of Mac's security reputation without proof
money_quotes: If you can hijack a brand-new MacBook out of the box, it's yours to keep.
The idea is that knowledge I'll gain from seeing my opponent's cards is worth more than the bet I expect to lose.
I don't expect to lose this particular bet — but I don't expect to win it, either. I expect to be ignored.
If I'm wrong, it'd be worth $1099 to know that MacBook users are in fact at risk.
If I'm right, someone needs to call Maynor and Ellch on their bullshit.

I think what they've discovered — if they've in fact discovered anything useful at all — is a class of potential Wi-Fi-based exploit, which they demonstrated on a rigged MacBook to generate publicity at the expense of the Mac's renowned reputation for security.
posts.key_insight: When security researchers make dramatic claims but refuse to demonstrate them under fair conditions, a public challenge with real stakes is the most effective way to separate genuine vulnerabilities from publicity stunts.

posts.summary: Gruber updates his challenge to security researchers David Maynor and Jon Ellch to demonstrate a Wi-Fi exploit on a stock MacBook, noting the bounty has doubled to two MacBooks. He methodically dismantles the claim that his challenge is unfair by tracing the contradictory and evasive statements Maynor and Ellch have made about whether the exploit affects built-in MacBook hardware. He argues their refusal to give a simple yes-or-no answer about the built-in card's vulnerability is itself suspicious. He also examines and largely debunks the theory that Apple Legal has silenced the researchers, noting the supposed threats don't appear in the published interview transcript.
key_points: Maynor and Ellch's own published Q&A contradicts itself — first implying the exploit affects the built-in MacBook card, then saying it doesn't, then vaguely referencing ongoing research.
The researchers have never simply stated they cannot perform the exploit on stock MacBook hardware, which would be the obvious response if the challenge were truly unfair.
Brian Krebs's interview transcript contains no mention of Apple pressuring the researchers, despite Krebs's original reporting claiming Apple 'leaned on' them.
Apple Legal gag-order speculation doesn't hold up: gag orders come from courts, apply to both sides, and Apple's own public statements suggest none exists.
The researchers' deliberate ambiguity about the exploit's scope is no more responsible than a clear confirmation would be.
money_quotes: If I lose — a big 'if' in my opinion — I'll set up a DropCash campaign for people to contribute to. But I issued this challenge fully willing to foot the entire bill, should I lose.
In fact, I'm pretty certain I could up the ante to a gold-plated MacBook at this point.
Refusing to even answer the yes/no 'Have you found such an exploit against the built-in MacBook card and driver?' is just silly.

Their ambiguity makes it every bit as likely that a malfeasant would investigate on his own than if they had came right out and said, 'We have identified a vulnerability in the default MacBook Wi-Fi driver and card.'
Why refuse to say 'yes' or 'no' but instead hint that there might be one?
Apple Legal can't simply impose a 'You won't talk about this exploit, nor will you even say that you're not allowed to talk about this exploit' gag order upon them.
And shouldn't a security research firm such as SecureWorks be prepared for bullying tactics from vendors?
In the meantime, the questions continue to come faster than the answers.
posts.key_insight: The security researchers' refusal to give a straightforward yes-or-no answer about the MacBook's built-in Wi-Fi vulnerability is more damning than any answer they could give.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his MacBook Wi-Fi hack challenge, which Maynor and Ellch declined to accept, and dissects the responsible disclosure debate surrounding their Black Hat demonstration. He argues that regardless of whether the researchers found a real exploit against stock MacBook AirPort cards, their handling was irresponsible — either they disclosed a real vulnerability recklessly by using a MacBook in their demo, or they left a false impression that one existed. He introduces William Carrel's 2003 DHCP vulnerability disclosure as a model of responsible behavior and argues for fuller disclosure practices. Gruber concludes that both the researchers and Apple are culpable for their silence, since neither side will clarify whether stock MacBooks are actually vulnerable.
key_points: Maynor and Ellch's use of a MacBook in their demo was irresponsible regardless of whether a stock AirPort exploit exists — it either revealed a real vulnerability or created a false impression of one
The 'responsible disclosure' defense falls apart because performing the demo on a MacBook already tipped off attackers to the possibility of a built-in card exploit
William Carrel's 2003 DHCP vulnerability disclosure demonstrates how responsible disclosure should work — report privately, give reasonable time, then go public
Apple shares blame by refusing to issue a clear statement confirming or denying the vulnerability, hiding behind careful PR language
Full disclosure, while debatable in its extremes, ultimately serves users better than indefinite silence that leaves them unknowingly vulnerable
money_quotes: But of course it wasn't fair — the unfairness was the entire point of the challenge.
Sometimes it's only effective to fight fire with fire, and make no bones about it, Maynor and Ellch's MacBook exploit demonstration video from the Black Hat conference was a cheap stunt.
This uncertainty, to me, is far worse than a flat-out statement that they've found an exploit against built-in MacBook AirPort cards.
I may or may not yet be proven wrong about what Maynor and Ellch have discovered and reported to Apple, but I see no way that they can come out of this seen as having acted 'responsibly'.
Waiting until Apple issues a patch is not exceedingly responsible. It is exceedingly irresponsible.
In short, that both sides remain mum means that both sides remain, to some degree at least, wrong.

Apple director of Mac PR Lynn Fox's statement last month that SecureWorks had 'provided no evidence' of such a flaw involving the built-in MacBook card and driver is not the same thing as saying that Apple was not aware of any such flaw.
posts.key_insight: When both security researchers and vendors choose silence over transparency, users are the ones left vulnerable — responsible disclosure requires accountability from all parties, not just researchers.

posts.summary: Gruber promotes Amazon affiliate links for purchasing the newly announced iPods, offering Daring Fireball memberships as an incentive for orders over $199. He explains the honor-system arrangement since Amazon's affiliate reports don't include buyer identity. He notes Amazon's initial shipping confusion, including a comically erroneous 2035 ship date for the new iPod Nanos.
key_points: Gruber offers readers a way to support Daring Fireball through Amazon affiliate purchases of the new iPods at no extra cost
A promotional deal offers one-year DF memberships for Amazon orders of $199+ through his affiliate link, run on the honor system
Amazon's product metadata was initially confused, listing the new Nanos with a 2035 ship date
money_quotes: You pay the same price as from Apple.com, and I get a nifty 5-6 percent kickback from Amazon.
The affiliate reports I get from Amazon only include the items, prices, and dates — there's no info about who, for obvious privacy reasons.
So this DF-membership-for-Amazon-patronage deal is on the honor policy.
Amazon's metadata is obviously a bit confused at the moment, considering that the new Nanos are marked both as "usually ships in 2 to 3 days" and "In stock on October 26, 2035".
I'm pretty sure it isn't going to take 29 years.
posts.key_insight: Gruber transparently promotes his Amazon affiliate partnership as a mutually beneficial way for readers to support independent tech publishing while buying products they already want.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's September 2006 'Showtime' event where they launched movie downloads on iTunes, new iPods, and previewed the iTV device. He frames the event around three major challenges Apple had to overcome simultaneously: video quality versus bandwidth/storage tradeoffs, iPod hardware limitations for movie playback, and resistant movie studio executives who prefer the status quo. He argues Apple came remarkably close to meeting sky-high expectations despite shipping only 75 Disney titles and delaying the iTV. Gruber also offers a theory about Steve Jobs's subdued WWDC performance — that Jobs simply hates presenting unfinished work like Leopard — contrasting it with his energized Showtime performance where he could show polished, shipping products. The piece concludes by marveling at the iPod/iTunes empire's incremental growth from a $399 Mac-only MP3 player into a juggernaut that competitors must now try to match all at once.
key_points: Apple faced three simultaneous challenges entering the movie business: video quality vs. bandwidth tradeoffs, iPod battery and screen limitations, and resistant movie studio executives clinging to the status quo.
Jobs's lackluster WWDC keynote wasn't about illness but about his hatred of presenting unfinished work — his reality distortion field only works when he genuinely believes in what he's showing.
The film industry's resistance to digital distribution mirrors the music industry's earlier futile resistance to the home video revolution.

The iPod/iTunes platform grew through steady, incremental steps — from a Mac-only MP3 player to a dominant ecosystem — and that methodical approach now creates an enormous barrier for competitors like Zune.
Apple's iTV pre-announcement was a calculated exception to their anti-vaporware stance, necessary to prevent the 'how do I watch on my TV?' question from overshadowing the actual product launches.
money_quotes: Their sense of entitlement regarding the preservation of the status quo is such that, when faced with any technology that threatens to upset it, no matter how cool, how clever, how convenient it might make things for us, their customers, they attempt to legislate it out of existence or to encumber it with odious DRM restrictions.
The mainstream corporate music industry, as represented by the RIAA, sees its customers as an enemy, as a horde of dishonest would-be thieves.

Jobs, in my opinion, is a terrible liar and a poor actor. When he's able to convince people of things that aren't true, or that are exaggerations of the truth, it's because he believes what he's saying.
The reality distortion field isn't something he projects willfully; it's an extension of his own certainty.

His contempt for the device was palpable; when he failed to successfully switch from song playback to accept a call, he seemed poised to just toss the thing off-stage and cry out that it was a piece of garbage.
Vaporware pre-announcements distract customers from currently available products, but in this particular case I think Apple felt they had to reveal this now.
It started with nothing more than a $399 5 GB Mac-only MP3 player and a Mac-only jukebox app.
Apple has had the benefit getting to this point slowly but surely; anyone who wants to compete with them now is forced to try to catch up all at once or settle for just one piece of the pie.
posts.key_insight: Apple's methodical, incremental expansion of the iPod/iTunes ecosystem — always shipping polished products that meet Jobs's standards — created an insurmountable lead that forces competitors to attempt in one leap what Apple built over five years of steady steps.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews the September 2006 iPod lineup refresh, working through each tier: Shuffle, Nano, and full-size iPod. He confesses his initial skepticism about the original Shuffle was wrong and declares it his favorite iPod, praising the new clip-based redesign. The new Nanos combine the best of the Mini and original Nano, while the updated full-size iPods deliver the battery life improvements essential for Apple's move into movie sales. He argues that dismissing current iPods as not 'real' video players is as absurd as saying the original 5 GB iPod wasn't a real music player. He closes by advocating for open game APIs on the iPod and musing that Apple's phone ambitions might simply be an iPod with phone features.
key_points: The iPod Shuffle alone generated roughly as much revenue as the entire iTunes Store, illustrating that Apple's hardware business dwarfs its content business in profitability.
Battery life was the essential upgrade for the full-size iPod — the jump from 2 to 3.5 hours of video playback makes movie watching viable and validates Apple's push into film sales.
Calling current iPods not 'real' video players because of screen size is as wrongheaded as saying the original 5 GB iPod wasn't a real music player — capabilities grow incrementally.
Apple's closed game APIs represent a missed opportunity; games should follow the music and video precedent where the iTunes Store competes on convenience rather than exclusivity.
Portable electronics are converging from different directions, and Apple's phone entry could simply be an iPod with phone features rather than an entirely new product line.
money_quotes: It ends up that the shuffle mode's randomizer does a better job picking music I want to hear than I do.

The iPod Shuffle epitomizes the appeal of Apple Computer — I can't think of any other gadget company that would make something this simple or put this much design effort into something so inexpensive.
It never ceases to amaze me just how horrible the buttons feel on so many gadgets; cell phone buttons, for example, are usually all mushy and wiggly, like loose baby teeth.
Claiming that these iPods aren't 'real' video players because their screens are too small is like saying the original iPods weren't 'real' audio players because they could only hold 5 GB.
Your eyes will burn out before the battery does on the 80 GB.
Let the games from the iTunes Store compete with the free market in terms of convenience, price, and quality, just like how the music and video from the iTunes Store do.
Instead of thinking of Apple's hypothetical entry into the mobile phone market as an entirely new 'iPhone' product line, couldn't it just be a new 'iPod Phone' — an iPod with phone features?
posts.key_insight: Apple's genius with the iPod line is putting extraordinary design effort into even its cheapest products, while incrementally expanding each tier's capabilities — a strategy that makes dismissing any current iPod as not yet 'real' a fundamental misunderstanding of how Apple iterates.

posts.summary: Gruber pushes back against tech media hype over Microsoft's announced Zune Wi-Fi sharing feature, pointing out that the Zune is still vaporware and the feature isn't even finished being designed. He quotes a TechCrunch writer breathlessly asking what Apple will do in response, then methodically dismantles the premise by noting the product hasn't shipped, isn't finished, and its key feature remains unresolved. He raises strategic questions about why Microsoft would reveal an unfinished killer feature months before launch, giving Apple time to respond. He fairly acknowledges the vaporware charge cuts both ways, noting Apple's own unshipped 'iTV' announcement.
key_points: The Zune's Wi-Fi sharing feature is being treated as a competitive threat to iPod despite the product being unfinished vaporware
Microsoft's own engineers admit the Wi-Fi power management problem isn't solved yet, undermining claims the feature is a game-changer
Strategically, announcing an unfinished killer feature early tips your hand to competitors and wastes media attention before consumers can buy
Apple's stock price was unmoved by the Zune announcement, contradicting the suggestion that investors were worried
Gruber acknowledges fairness by noting Apple's own 'iTV' announcement is equally vaporware
money_quotes: But the Zune is vaporware.
How about we wait until Microsoft decides "what they're going to do about" Zune's Wi-Fi sharing before we start scheduling dance lessons in preparation for a trip to the iPod's grave?
If they're "not done with it yet", on September 14, when will they be? How late can they wait to go into production and still hit shelves for the holiday season?
And if this really is a killer feature in a product they honestly expect to ship within the next month or two, why are they talking about it now? Why tip their hand to Apple in advance?
Why blow all this media attention before people can actually fork over their money for the thing?
The "vaporware" charge cuts both ways, of course — it would be just as unfair to ask now for Microsoft's response to "iTV".
And the most important question of all: Brown?
posts.key_insight: Tech media's eagerness to declare unfinished vaporware a competitive threat reveals a pattern of rewarding announcements over actual shipping products.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles a Gartner analyst report claiming Apple priced new iPods for profit margins rather than market share growth. He argues the analysts ignore supply constraints, the economics of maximizing total profit (not just margin), and the competitive landscape where Apple's prices match or undercut competitors like SanDisk. He points out that Apple's simplified product line is itself a strategic advantage, not a missed opportunity. The piece concludes that these analysts are operating from an outdated narrative that paints Apple customers as overpaying zealots, and that syndication of one flawed story creates a false impression of widespread concern.
