key_points: When demand outstrips supply, pricing higher is rational economics — Apple may have left money on the table during previous holiday shortages by pricing too low
Margin is only one component of profit; the goal is to maximize the product of margin and unit sales, not either one in isolation
Apple's iPod prices are competitive with SanDisk's comparable products despite Apple offering a superior device, undermining the 'overpriced' narrative
The simplicity of Apple's product line and clean pricing tiers ($150/$200/$250) is itself a strategic brand asset, not a failure to segment the market
Analysts and reporters are recycling an outdated script where Apple products are dismissed as overpriced and their customers as cultists
money_quotes: Simple economics dictates that when demand outstrips supply, the price is too low.
The goal isn't to maximize margin or unit sales, the goal is to maximize the product of margin and unit sales — the profit.
The relative simplicity of their product lines is a key part of the Apple experience.
To the victor go the spoils; when you've got a smash hit and a strong brand on your hands, you can sell for higher margins and increase (or at least maintain) your market share.
And why should Apple's prices be judged by the cost of their component materials, anyway? Shouldn't they be judged against the rest of the market?
I suggest they head to a nearby high school and ask a few students whether an iPod Nano is easily worth $40 more than a SanDisk e250.

What I think is going on is that these analysts, along with tech reporters like Dan Nystedt, are still reading from the old script where Apple products get written off as overpriced and their customers branded as mindless cultists.
The idea is to paint Apple as a company that milks its own loyal customers. What other company gets held to a standard like this?
posts.key_insight: Judging Apple's pricing by component cost rather than competitive positioning and total profit optimization reveals analysts still trapped in a narrative that dismisses Apple's brand power as customer exploitation.

posts.summary: Gruber meticulously catalogs the differences between the 5G and 5.5G iPods, revealing that Apple quietly shipped an intermediate hardware revision (which he dubs '5.25G') with new, more efficient displays before the official 5.5G announcement. He notes that the search feature was withheld from older 5G iPods despite no technical limitation, calling it a pure marketing decision. He wrestles with whether Apple should treat iPods like computers (with ongoing feature updates) or consumer electronics (frozen at purchase), ultimately concluding that Apple's approach is understandable even if imperfect.
key_points: Apple shipped an unpublicized intermediate iPod hardware revision with new displays before the 5.5G announcement, throttled to match older screen brightness
The search feature was withheld from 5G iPods despite identical hardware capabilities — a purely marketing-driven decision
Features that help Apple sell iTunes Store content (games, movies, higher-res video) were included in the firmware update, while the search feature was not
Gruber draws a distinction between treating iPods as computers (expecting ongoing updates) versus consumer electronics (features frozen at purchase), suggesting the latter framing is more appropriate
Gapless playback and quick scroll were included as free updates with no direct financial incentive, suggesting Apple does invest in goodwill for existing customers
money_quotes: There is no technical reason the search feature couldn't have been included in the firmware update for existing 5G iPods. I believe this was solely a marketing decision.

Apple switched to the new, brighter, more-efficient iPod displays at some point before September 12. No one noticed because these iPods throttle the display brightness to match the maximum brightness of the older 5G iPods.
This means there are really three 5G iPod hardware configurations.
My instinct is to view iPods as computers — combinations of hardware and software — and this instinct is probably wrong.
If you buy a new TV set or DVD player today, you almost certainly aren't going to get new features a year from now in a firmware update.

It's easy to see why Apple included in the firmware update the extended battery life for video playback, 640 × 480 video support, and the ability to play the new iPod games — these are features that allow Apple to sell games and movies from the iTunes Store to owners of older iPods.
I'd prefer to see Apple let the new 5.5G iPods be compared against the previous 5G ones based solely on their hardware improvements — the bigger 80 GB drive and the brighter screens.
posts.key_insight: Apple's decision to withhold software features from identical hardware reveals the tension between treating devices as updatable computers versus disposable consumer electronics — and how that choice is driven more by marketing than by technical constraints.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Apple's AirPort security update in the context of the earlier 'MacBook Wi-Fi Hack' controversy involving researchers David Maynor and Jon Ellch. Apple patched buffer overflow vulnerabilities in AirPort wireless drivers for both Broadcom and Atheros chipsets, but explicitly stated that no known exploits exist and that the fixes were not based on anything provided by Maynor, Ellch, or SecureWorks. Gruber methodically shows that while the researchers were broadly correct that AirPort drivers needed better frame validation, their original claims of having a working exploit against Apple's built-in drivers remain unproven. He lays out three logical possibilities: the researchers lied, they found an exploit but never showed Apple, or Apple is lying — and argues the last option is least credible. He concludes that the researchers' credibility is essentially spent unless they can produce proof.
key_points: Apple's AirPort update patched separate buffer overflow vulnerabilities in Broadcom (PowerPC) and Atheros (Intel) wireless chipsets, plus a third-party API flaw, all involving malformed Wi-Fi frame handling
Apple went on the offensive, explicitly stating that Maynor and Ellch never provided information identifying a specific vulnerability and that the fixes came from Apple's own internal audit

The distinction between finding potential vulnerabilities and demonstrating actual exploits is critical — Apple says no known exploit exists, which undercuts the researchers' dramatic Black Hat claims
Gruber identifies three mutually exclusive possibilities and argues that Apple lying would be an enormous risk for a public company, making the researchers' position untenable
Brian Krebs's conclusion that the drivers were 'indeed vulnerable to exploitation' overstates what the evidence actually shows — potential vulnerability is not the same as proven exploitability
money_quotes: "No known exploit" does not just mean that there aren't any attacks in the wild; it means no one has demonstrated to Apple a way to take advantage of these frame validation issues.
They fixed them to eliminate potential exploits, not to address actual, known exploits.

My translation of "we are open to hearing from security researchers on how to improve the security" is "We're not in the business of besmirching the reputations of security researchers who report problems in Mac OS X, but we are not going to sit back and take it when someone grossly exaggerates or lies about a threat."

Maynor and Ellch's demonstration video was the equivalent of an intruder entering the building, walking into the executive suite, and taking a dump on the CEO's desk. But according to Apple, Maynor and Ellch never demonstrated that they could get through the front door.
Apple's AirPort code is now performing additional validation to guard against such an attack, but there remains no proof that such an attack exists.
The only way Maynor and Ellch have any credibility remaining is if Apple is flat-out lying. And if that's the case Maynor and Ellch can simply step forward and prove it.

It is a simple yes or no question: Have Maynor and Ellch found a vulnerability that affects MacBooks using Apple's built-in cards and drivers? That Maynor and Ellch haven't answered it speaks volumes.
posts.key_insight: Patching potential vulnerabilities is not the same as validating claims of working exploits, and Apple's security update actually undermines rather than supports the researchers' unproven assertions.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles a Techworld article by Kieren McCarthy that mischaracterized Apple's AirPort security updates, finding factual errors in every single paragraph. He demonstrates that McCarthy falsely claimed Apple contradicted itself, when Apple's statements were actually consistent: they denied receiving evidence from SecureWorks, not that vulnerabilities existed. Gruber shows that the Black Hat demonstration explicitly used a third-party card and driver, not Apple's built-in AirPort hardware, contradicting McCarthy's central claim. He also notes that McCarthy misread which Macs were affected by the update, and that even McCarthy's own errors contradict each other within the article. Dalrymple and Macworld's editor both distanced themselves from the piece, which had improperly carried Dalrymple's byline.
key_points: Apple's statements about the Wi-Fi vulnerability were consistent: they denied receiving evidence from SecureWorks, not that flaws existed — a careful distinction McCarthy ignored
The Black Hat demonstration by Maynor and Ellch explicitly used a third-party Wi-Fi card and driver, not Apple's built-in AirPort hardware
McCarthy misread the security update, claiming it didn't affect MacBooks while simultaneously claiming it addressed a MacBook vulnerability — contradicting himself
Dalrymple's name was improperly attached to the Techworld article, which Macworld's editor called 'a snide opinion article masquerading as news'
Every single paragraph of McCarthy's article contains at least one factual error
money_quotes: Apple denied only that SecureWorks had provided them with evidence of Wi-Fi flaws affecting Apple products. No one from Apple said anything, one way or the other, about whether the company was aware of any such flaws.
Even if you're not aware of any flaws, it's impossible to know whether there exist flaws you aren't aware of. It's the difference between 'known unknowns' and 'unknown unknowns'.
We stand by our story; we wish Techworld would stand by theirs and not misuse the name of our reporter on its snide opinion article masquerading as news.
McCarthy deserves double jackass honors — once for the crummy article and again for putting Jim Dalrymple's name on it.
Amazingly, not only does every single paragraph of this article contain at least one factual error, but even the errors reported within the article itself contradict each other.
One fact that is undisputed by anyone is that their public demonstration did not involve Apple's card or driver. Undisputed.
It boggles the mind.
posts.key_insight: McCarthy's article fails at the most basic level of journalism — getting the facts right — with every paragraph containing errors and even his own claims contradicting each other.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Kieren McCarthy's follow-up defense of his reporting on Apple's AirPort security updates, identifying multiple factual errors in McCarthy's opening paragraph alone. He challenges McCarthy's claims that Apple denied security problems, that security experts issued unequivocal warnings, and that Apple's response followed a repeated pattern. Gruber acknowledges Apple's imperfect security practices, including sitting on non-public vulnerabilities, but argues McCarthy fabricates a pattern of behavior that doesn't exist. The core argument is that security reputations are earned through real-world results — Microsoft's is bad because millions of users face actual malware, while Apple's is good because virtually no Mac users have experienced real attacks.
key_points: McCarthy's response contains at least three factual errors in its opening paragraph alone, misrepresenting what security experts said, what Apple said, and what Apple's patches addressed
Apple's actual statement was that SecureWorks showed them no evidence of a problem, not that no problem existed — a crucial distinction McCarthy ignores
McCarthy claims Apple follows a repeated pattern of criticizing security researchers, but Gruber challenges him to name a single prior instance
The comparison to Microsoft's security failures is backwards: Microsoft's reputation suffers because of real-world malware affecting millions, not because of PR strategy
Gruber concedes Apple has real security shortcomings, particularly sitting on non-public vulnerabilities, while arguing McCarthy's criticisms are fabricated rather than legitimate
money_quotes: Already wrong again, three times in the opening paragraph.
That's not mere inaccuracy; it's flat-out making shit up.
The reason Microsoft's security image is terrible is that millions of their customers are plagued by actual security problems.

Many Mac users are smug about security, but it's not because they believe Mac OS X is magically invulnerable to malware — it's because there don't seem to be any Mac users with actual malware problems.
Just because you're happy that you have been healthy your entire life doesn't mean you irrationally believe you couldn't possibly get sick in the future.
But reputations are forged by results.
posts.key_insight: Security reputations are forged by real-world results, not PR strategies, and criticizing Apple's security posture requires actual evidence rather than fabricated patterns.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews the new 'Zoom using scroll wheel' feature introduced in Mac OS X 10.4.8, which allows users to magnify the screen by holding a modifier key and scrolling. He walks through the preference options and finds the feature generally well-implemented and convenient for examining pixel-level details. However, he takes issue with the system-wide ⌘⌥\ keyboard shortcut for toggling zoom smoothing, which conflicts with BBEdit's Hard Wrap command. He argues that minor system updates shouldn't claim keyboard shortcuts without making them configurable.
key_points: Mac OS X 10.4.8 adds a scroll-wheel zoom feature that's on by default with Control as the modifier key, offering a quick alternative to dedicated utilities like xScope
The 'Smooth images' option is misleadingly named since it anti-aliases the entire screen, not just images, making it counterproductive for examining pixel-level details
The ⌘⌥\ shortcut for toggling smoothing is applied system-wide even when not zooming, conflicting with established shortcuts in apps like BBEdit and Mailsmith
Most Universal Access keyboard shortcuts are adjustable in System Preferences, but this one is not, which Gruber considers an inconsistency Apple should fix
money_quotes: If you're using this feature as I intend to, to examine pixel-level layout details, it's best to leave this off.
Having zoom available at a moment's notice is a bit more convenient than firing up a utility like xScope or Art Directors Toolkit.
Minor system updates shouldn't just gobble up keyboard shortcuts like this.
Who's going to be toggling this smoothing option so frequently that they need a keyboard shortcut?
I've got about 10 years of muscle memory using this command with this shortcut every single day, it's a problem for me.
Most of the other system-wide default keyboard shortcuts can be changed or just plain turned off in the Keyboard Shortcuts tab; but, alas, not this ⌘⌥\ zoom-smoothing shortcut.
posts.key_insight: Apple's new scroll-wheel zoom is a welcome addition, but claiming a system-wide keyboard shortcut without making it configurable shows a lack of attention to the power users who depend on established shortcuts.

posts.summary: Gruber criticizes Adobe's decision to rebrand Lightroom as 'Photoshop Lightroom,' arguing it reflects marketing-driven thinking that hurts both brands. He contends the move dilutes Photoshop's specific meaning while implying Lightroom can't stand on its own, when in fact it's an innovative, distinct application. He connects this to Adobe CEO Bruce Chizen's 'sales guy' mentality and the broader pattern of great companies declining when marketing people replace product people. Gruber praises Lightroom's engineering — including its unconventional use of Lua and its cross-platform ambition — and argues the branding decision is unfair to the team that built something genuinely new.
key_points: Rebranding Lightroom as 'Photoshop Lightroom' hurts both brands: it dilutes Photoshop's specific meaning and implies Lightroom isn't significant enough to stand alone.
Good marketing rings true; slapping a strong brand name on unrelated products is marketing for the sake of marketing, not marketing that serves the product.
Adobe already owns the umbrella brand 'Adobe' — making 'Photoshop' also an umbrella brand creates unnecessary hierarchy.
Lightroom represents genuine engineering innovation at Adobe, including a new cross-platform framework and unconventional use of Lua, and its marketing should reflect that distinctiveness.
Companies decline when product people are replaced by sales and marketing people who don't understand or love what they're selling.
money_quotes: But it rankles.
The point is that good marketing rings true.
Some marketing people, when given control over a powerful, popular, long-standing brand, see it as a weapon to be wielded — they want to slap that brand on everything they can.
Other marketing people — the good ones — realize that even strong brands are fragile; that they're valuable, precious commodities that need to be nurtured and protected.
The implication is that Lightroom can't stand on its own, that it isn't significant enough to be considered a peer to Photoshop.
Specific brands are intense; umbrella brands are not.
It's marketing for the sake of marketing itself, as opposed to marketing that serves and respects the actual products.
You can't make a good new candy bar just by borrowing the name of an existing one.
posts.key_insight: Strong brands are fragile things that need protection, not weapons to be slapped on everything — and when marketing people override product people, both the brand and the product suffer.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Wil Shipley's essay complaining about Mac OS X APIs only available through Carbon. While sympathetic to Shipley's core point — that higher-level Cocoa APIs make developers more productive — Gruber argues Shipley undermines himself by framing Carbon as simply 'old' and Cocoa as 'new,' when both date from the 1980s. Gruber clarifies that the real distinction is low-level vs. high-level, not old vs. new, noting that much of Cocoa actually sits on top of Carbon's CoreFoundation. He also distinguishes Shipley's legitimate complaint from anti-Carbon purism, citing Daniel Jalkut's criticism of developers who refuse to cross API boundaries. Gruber ultimately distills Shipley's argument into Larry Wall's programming maxim: easy things should be easy, and hard things should be possible.
key_points: The Carbon-vs-Cocoa distinction is really about low-level vs. high-level APIs, not old vs. new — both frameworks date from the 1980s
Much of Cocoa sits on top of Carbon's CoreFoundation, making the 'Carbon is legacy' narrative misleading
Shipley's real complaint is valid: important Apple APIs lack high-level Cocoa wrappers, forcing developers into verbose, low-level Carbon code
API purism — refusing to mix Carbon and Cocoa — is counterproductive, but wanting higher-level APIs for common tasks is reasonable
Objective-C 2.0's additions like garbage collection and foreach loops reflect the same principle: making easy things even easier
money_quotes: Using Cocoa, at its best, is like opening a box and getting a toy. Using Carbon is like opening a box and getting a fistful of tiny pieces that you need to assemble into a toy.
The argument is that Carbon is lower-level and Cocoa is higher-level, and programmers are almost always more productive, write fewer bugs, and have more fun when working with higher-level APIs.
The problem is that you can't do it in Cocoa using Cocoa; you have to use Carbon, and compared to how it might be done if it were possible using Cocoa, it's a pain in the ass.
Easy things should be easy, and hard things should be possible.
Making hard things possible is good, but making hard things easy is even better.
If you count CoreFoundation as part of Carbon, then in a very real sense it's fair to say that most of Cocoa sits on top of Carbon.
posts.key_insight: The real distinction between Carbon and Cocoa isn't old versus new but low-level versus high-level, and developers rightly want Apple to provide high-level APIs that make common hard tasks easy.

posts.summary: Gruber announces BBColors 1.0, a command-line tool he created to fill a long-standing gap in BBEdit: the inability to save, switch between, and share text color schemes. Despite BBEdit supporting configurable syntax coloring since version 4.0 in 1996, it never offered a way to manage color schemes en masse. BBColors stores schemes as text files in Application Support and applies changes via the macOS defaults command. The tool works with both BBEdit and TextWrangler, and Gruber provides example color schemes on the project page.
key_points: BBEdit has supported configurable syntax coloring since 1996 but lacks any built-in way to save, switch, or share color schemes
BBColors is a command-line tool that fills this gap with simple save/load/share functionality
Schemes are stored as portable text files in ~/Library/Application Support/BBColors/, making sharing trivial
The tool works by manipulating BBEdit preferences via the macOS defaults command
money_quotes: One feature still lacking in BBEdit which I've wanted for years is a way to easily save and switch between different text coloring schemes.
BBEdit's text colors have been configurable ever since syntax coloring appeared in BBEdit 4.0 in 1996, but there's no means to save or change them en masse.
Nor is there any way to share color schemes with other users.
posts.key_insight: When a beloved tool lacks a feature you've wanted for years, sometimes the best solution is to build it yourself.

posts.summary: Gruber explains multiple techniques for determining if a Mac application is currently running, a problem he encountered while building his BBColors tool. He walks through AppleScript approaches using creator type, name, and displayed name properties, noting the pitfalls of each. He then demonstrates how to call AppleScript from Perl using osascript, the MacPerl module, and Mac::Processes, detailing escaping bugs and endian-related issues on Intel Macs. The post concludes that identifying apps by name is the most portable approach due to Intel compatibility bugs in Mac::Carbon's creator type handling.
key_points: AppleScript's System Events can identify running processes by creator type, name, or displayed name, but displayed name is unreliable because it changes based on Finder preferences
Calling AppleScript from Perl requires careful escaping of quotes and backslashes at both the Perl and shell levels
Mac::Carbon's Mac::Processes module avoids mixing AppleScript into Perl but has endian bugs on Intel Macs that reverse creator type byte order
The safest cross-platform approach is identifying apps by name rather than creator type, since name works correctly on all Macs running 10.4
money_quotes: So in a nut: the creator type and name properties are good bets; the displayed name property is not, because it changes depending on the user's Finder preference for displaying file extensions.

Except this code has a few subtle bugs: it won't work if it's passed a creator type string containing either a literal double-quote or a backslash character. I'm not aware of any creator type codes that use these characters, but they're legal.
We need one level of backslashing for Perl, and another level for the shell when we call the osascript tool.

Creator types aren't really four-character strings; they're four-byte integers, and treating each of the bytes as a MacRoman-encoded character is just a shortcut that makes them more readable and memorable.
Instead of "R*ch" for BBEdit, you get "hc*R"; instead of "MACS" for the Finder, you get "SCAM".
posts.key_insight: When bridging AppleScript and Perl on Mac OS X, identifying running applications by name is the most robust approach, avoiding both escaping complexity and Intel endian bugs that plague creator type lookups.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his previous article about detecting running applications, sharing reader feedback and improvements. He explores why System Events lacks bundle identifier support, presents a clever endianness workaround for the Mac::Processes Perl module on Intel Macs, and dives deep into why the seemingly simple ps-and-grep approach is trickier than most people think. The bulk of the post demonstrates how naive grep patterns fail (matching iTunesHelper when looking for iTunes, or matching 'Contents' folders in app paths), then arrives at an elegant solution using ps -xc -o command for clean output. He concludes with benchmarking showing the ps-and-egrep method is fastest for real-world single invocations, despite misleading microbenchmark results.
key_points: System Events inexplicably supports querying processes by name and creator type but not by bundle identifier, which is the canonical Mac OS X way to identify apps
Nearly everyone who suggested using ps and grep got it wrong — naive patterns match substrings like iTunesHelper or Contents folder paths
The key insight is using ps -xc -o command to output only process names, enabling simple and robust exact-match grep patterns
Microbenchmarks with 1,000 iterations were misleading due to caching effects; real-world single invocations showed ps-and-egrep was 3-5x faster than AppleScript methods
The problem with regular expressions is that it's easy to write one that usually works but hard to write one that always works
money_quotes: The problem with regular expressions is that it's often easy to write one that usually works, but hard to write one that always works.

Instead of just trying to match the part of the line that you're looking for, you should try to match the entire line, including both the part you're looking for and the parts you aren't otherwise interested in.
Lord knows I loves me the regular expressions, but avoiding crafting a pattern like the above is what led me to skip this technique originally.
That's both clever and robust.
Nearly everyone who emailed to suggest it, for example, got it wrong.
I suspect that what's happening is that there are some serious caching and 'everything is already loaded in memory' benefits to the repeated calls.
posts.key_insight: The seemingly simple task of checking if an app is running via ps and grep is deceptively error-prone, but using ps -xc -o command to isolate process names makes exact matching both simple and robust.

posts.summary: Gruber documents a workaround for a conflict between Mac OS X 10.4.8's new Zoom With Scroll Wheel feature and BBEdit's Hard Wrap keyboard shortcut (Command-Option-Backslash). The system claims the shortcut at a low level, preventing BBEdit from receiving it. He uses Keyboard Maestro, a $20 macro utility, to intercept the shortcut and redirect it to BBEdit's Hard Wrap command. After testing multiple approaches, he settles on reassigning the BBEdit shortcut to Command-Option-Return while using Keyboard Maestro as a bridge during the transition. The post reflects his philosophy of running minimal system modifications and eventually weaning off workarounds.
key_points: Mac OS X 10.4.8's Zoom With Scroll Wheel feature claims Command-Option-Backslash system-wide, breaking BBEdit's Hard Wrap shortcut with no way to reconfigure it in System Preferences
Keyboard Maestro can intercept keyboard shortcuts that the system has claimed, unlike regular applications, without using any haxie or input manager tricks
Two macro approaches were tested: 'Select Menu Item' (works but activates all app windows) and 'Simulate Keypress' (unreliable due to timing conflicts between real and simulated key events)
Gruber's philosophy favors minimal system modifications and breaking old habits rather than permanently relying on workarounds
money_quotes: I've got about 10 years of muscle memory using this command with this shortcut every single day, it's a problem for me.
No haxie or input manager trickery involved. The engine is lean and mean: it consumes only 696 kilobytes of private memory here on my PowerBook.

When the Zoom With Scroll Wheel feature is turned on, regular apps like BBEdit no longer 'see' that key sequence — the system consumes it at a lower level and doesn't pass it along. Keyboard Maestro, however, still sees it.
Ideally, I try to run with as few system modifications as possible, and when I'm using one simply to enable a habit, I try to break that habit.
I ran Unsanity's WindowShade X for at least a year back in the 10.1 era before my window-shading habit faded away.
My goal is to wean myself away from the old shortcut to the new one; but until the new muscle memory kicks in, the old shortcut will still work.
posts.key_insight: When the OS usurps a keyboard shortcut you depend on, macro utilities like Keyboard Maestro can intercept at a level between the system and applications, serving as a bridge while you retrain muscle memory.

posts.summary: Gruber announces improvements to the Daring Fireball membership renewal process. Previously, renewing required signing up for an entirely new membership, generating a new key and forcing subscribers to update all their RSS feed credentials. The new system allows members to log in and extend their existing membership seamlessly. He also acknowledges that early renewers were shortchanged on time and corrects this by extending all recent sign-ups to October 2007.
key_points: The old renewal system required creating an entirely new membership, which was confusing and forced users to update RSS feed passwords
Gruber admits the flawed system existed because he was lazy when first building it in 2004
The new process lets members log in and extend their existing membership without changing keys
Members who renewed early and lost remaining time have been compensated with extended expiration dates
money_quotes: The explanation for this is simply that I was lazy when I first cobbled the membership system together back in 2004.
This was both confusing and a pain in the ass.
Lesson: "good enough for now" will eventually not be good enough.
To make this right, I've changed the expiration dates for everyone who has signed up for a new membership in the last six weeks to 17 October 2007.
posts.key_insight: Technical debt from 'good enough for now' solutions inevitably compounds into user-facing problems that demand more effort to fix than doing it right the first time.

posts.summary: Gruber provides a candid update on his experiment of writing Daring Fireball full-time, revealing he's at about 50% of breaking even with revenue split evenly between Deck advertising and direct reader support. He announces a free RSS feeds week as a promotional strategy to convert more readers into paying members, comparing it to free HBO weekends. He also mentions site licensing options for group memberships. The post is a transparent look at the economics of independent blogging in 2006, framed with Gruber's characteristic mix of earnestness and humor.
key_points: Revenue is split almost exactly 50-50 between The Deck advertising network and direct reader contributions (memberships and t-shirts)
Gruber values reader-funded income more than advertising dollars as a writer, finding direct reader support 'indescribably satisfying'
The free feeds promotion is a strategic trial period to hook non-members on RSS feeds that 80% of existing members already use
Independent blogging funded significantly by voluntary reader contributions was still rare in 2006, with Kottke's micropatronage being a notable but short-lived experiment
money_quotes: As a businessman, I ought to be thinking that every dollar is as good as another. But I'm not a businessman, I'm a writer, and as a writer there's something indescribably satisfying about support that comes directly from my readers.
It's no longer all that novel to "go pro" with one's weblog, but there aren't many who are doing it based so significantly on voluntary contributions from readers.

Think of it like when there's free HBO for a weekend — if you like the shows, you pay to subscribe when the free period is over. Or, think of it like when a drug dealer gives out a free sample to get you hooked. No, wait, don't think of it like that.
The answer, in a nut, is "pretty good".
Those of you working at YouTube, I'm looking in your direction.
posts.key_insight: In the early days of professional blogging, Gruber bet that direct reader support could sustain independent writing — valuing the writer-reader relationship over pure advertising revenue.

posts.summary: Gruber eviscerates Gartner analysts Mark Stahlman and Charles Smulders for recommending that Apple abandon its computer hardware business and license Mac OS X to Dell — issued just two days before Apple announced record Mac sales and strong profits. He argues this advice was questionable even in 1997 when Apple was struggling, but is utterly absurd when Apple is posting record unit sales, high margins, and growing market share. Gruber dismantles Gartner's claim that Intel is 'propping up' Apple's hardware business, noting Apple is a valuable Intel customer selling only mid-to-high-end processors. He reframes the market share debate around profits rather than units, showing Apple's $546 million quarterly profit dwarfs competitors like Gateway (which lost money) and even Dell (which earned less despite five times the market share). He also points out that without hardware expertise, Apple could never have created the iPod.
key_points: Apple announced record Mac sales (1.6M quarterly, 5.3M annual) and $546M in profit just two days after Gartner recommended they abandon hardware
The meaningful metric for Apple's computer business isn't unit market share but share of total industry profits, where Apple vastly outperforms higher-volume competitors
Gartner's claim that Intel is 'propping up' Apple misunderstands the relationship — Apple is a valuable customer buying only mid-to-high-end chips with no AMD competition
Apple's hardware design and manufacturing expertise was the foundation that made the iPod possible — licensing Mac OS X would have eliminated that capability
Unlike attention-seeking columnists who profit from provocative takes, Gartner analysts' reputations depend solely on the quality of their analysis, making this recommendation especially damaging
money_quotes: Hello, Gartner? 1997 called and they want their advice for Apple back.
These Gartner analysts are supposed to be for real, though, which makes this all the more crazy.
Their reputations are built solely upon the quality of their analysis.
The interesting 'share' number for Apple's computer business isn't their share of the total units sold; it's their share of the total profits in the PC hardware industry.
Five times the market share, but less profit.
If Apple had listened to similar jackasses who told them to get out of the computer hardware business a decade ago, would they have been able to do the iPod?
Telling Apple to abandon their computer hardware business might just be the worst possible advice Gartner could plausibly offer.
It's hard to get a handle on the magnitude of the jackassery here.
posts.key_insight: The fixation on unit market share obscures what actually matters — profitability — and Apple's record profits while gaining share make the recommendation to abandon hardware not just wrong but possibly the worst plausible advice anyone could give them.

posts.summary: Gruber calls out CNN reporter Steve Hargreaves for writing an article about rising Mac security threats that uses an iPod virus incident as evidence — without mentioning the virus only affects Windows computers. The article's headline 'Mac Attacks Rare but Rising' is misleading because the cited example has nothing to do with Mac security. Hargreaves earns Gruber's 'Jackass of the Week' award, the third that week. CNN later updated the article to mention the Windows connection and softened the headline, but Gruber maintains the award stands because the fundamental framing remains dishonest.
key_points: CNN's article about rising Mac malware threats failed to mention that the iPod virus in question only attacked Windows computers
The omission is particularly egregious given the article's headline framing it as evidence of increasing Mac attacks
Even after CNN corrected the article to mention Windows, the core premise — that this incident signals rising Mac threats — remains flawed
This is the third 'Jackass of the Week' award Gruber has given out in a single week, suggesting a pattern of poor tech reporting
money_quotes: Hargreaves's article does not mention — not once — that the virus on these iPods only attacks computers running Microsoft Windows.
Not mentioning that the virus is a Windows virus is a rather egregious omission for an article headlined 'Mac Attacks Rare but Rising'.
So congratulations to Steve Hargreaves for completing the trifecta: the third Jackass of the Week of the week.
The Jackass award remains in place — pointing out that the iPod virus only affects Windows is good, but the point remains that it isn't a sign that Macs are increasingly under attack.
posts.key_insight: Using a Windows-only virus found on iPods as evidence that Macs are increasingly under attack is journalistic malpractice that reveals how poorly mainstream media understands the technology it covers.

posts.summary: Gruber awards his 'Jackass of the Week' designation to Rush Limbaugh for accusing Michael J. Fox of faking or exaggerating his Parkinson's disease symptoms in a political ad supporting stem cell research. Gruber emphasizes that Fox's commercial is honest and powerful, and that Limbaugh's attack represents an extraordinarily low cheap shot against genuine advocacy. He addresses potential criticism that the topic is too political for Daring Fireball, clarifying that his objection is to Limbaugh's cruelty, not his politics. Gruber further dissects Limbaugh's non-apology, in which he simultaneously claimed to apologize while pivoting to accuse Fox of being exploited. The post closes by noting Limbaugh fully doubled down the next day, retracting nothing.
key_points: Limbaugh accused Michael J. Fox of acting or deliberately skipping medication to exaggerate Parkinson's symptoms in a stem cell research ad
Gruber argues the issue isn't political disagreement but rather the cruelty and demeaning nature of Limbaugh's attack on a genuinely ill person's advocacy
Limbaugh's supposed apology was actually a pivot to a new attack — that Fox was allowing his illness to be 'exploited' by Democrats
Gruber points out the absurd logic that Fox's visible illness somehow disqualifies him from advocating for research into his own disease
Limbaugh ultimately retracted nothing, fully standing by his original remarks the following day
money_quotes: I'm calling him a jackass — and it's taking a lot of restraint not to call him something far worse — because his response to Fox's honest, genuine advocacy regarding an important, serious issue is one of the lowest, most demeaning cheap shots I've ever seen.
So, according to Limbaugh, Fox's condition disqualifies him from speaking on the issue.
I.e. that it's unfair to generate support for stem cell research by showing how sad this disease is.
I'm sure Michael J. Fox will feel terribly exploited if he lives to see the day that a cure for Parkinson's Disease is found.
I'm not calling Limbaugh a jackass because I disagree with his politics on this issue.
Fox is not distorting the position of the Republicans these ads are targeting, and he is not exaggerating the promise of stem cell research.
posts.key_insight: Attacking someone's visible suffering as performative in order to discredit their advocacy is not political disagreement — it's cruelty.

posts.summary: Gruber calls out The Washington Post for publishing an anti-iPod screed by mountaineer Neal Mueller without disclosing that Mueller receives gear from Creative Labs, a direct iPod competitor. Mueller's complaints — that iPods scratch, are expensive, use a closed system, and failed on Mount Everest — are systematically debunked as misleading comparisons between different product categories and outright FUD. The most absurd claim is that the iPod failed at Everest's summit, which is far beyond Apple's published operating specs for temperature and altitude. Gruber argues the real jackassery lies not with Mueller's opinions but with the Post's editors for failing to require disclosure of his Creative sponsorship, a basic journalistic standard.
key_points: Mueller unfairly compares a hard-drive iPod to a flash-memory Creative MuVo, ignoring that comparable flash-based iPods exist at similar prices
The 'closed system' complaint is factually wrong — iPods play more non-DRM formats than Creative's players
Complaining an iPod failed on Everest's summit is absurd given it's 20,000 feet above and 34 degrees below Apple's published operating limits
Mueller's undisclosed relationship with Creative Labs as a sponsor of his expeditions is a serious journalistic ethics failure
The Washington Post editors bear greater responsibility than Mueller for letting a sponsored opinion run without disclosure in a major newspaper
money_quotes: The openness gripe — man, how long are people going to keep trying to make this stick? iPods play more non-DRM-encumbered formats than Creative's players.

Complaining that it doesn't work 34 degrees below the low threshold of its operating temperature and almost 20,000 feet above its maximum altitude is like being upset because your car doesn't work underwater.
'iPods don't work in extreme high-altitude conditions' isn't exactly a significant concern for most people.
On its own, though, Mueller's rant is minor league jackassery. What makes it notable is its venue.
He is obligated to disclose his relationship with the company.
Something like this isn't supposed to slip through at a reputable publication.
posts.key_insight: The real failure isn't a biased opinion about the iPod — it's a major newspaper letting a sponsored advocate pose as an independent critic without disclosure.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights a lesser-known but long-standing Mac OS X feature: Cocoa's built-in auto-completion, triggered by the Esc or F5 keys, which suggests word completions from the system spelling dictionary. He walks through the mechanics of using it — navigating with arrow keys, selecting with Tab/Return/Space — and notes the practical difference between Esc and F5 in contexts where Esc means Cancel. He acknowledges the feature's limitation of not incorporating custom-learned words, and admits he struggles to build the habit because his primary editors (BBEdit, Mailsmith) don't support it.
key_points: Cocoa's text system has a built-in auto-complete feature tied to the system spelling dictionary, invoked via Esc or F5
The F5 shortcut has a practical advantage over Esc in contexts where Esc is bound to Cancel
The completion list draws from the system dictionary but ignores user-added custom words from Learn Spelling
The feature is extensible — apps like Xcode and SubEthaEdit enhance it for code auto-completion
Non-Cocoa editors like BBEdit, Mailsmith, and TextMate don't support this feature, making habit-building difficult
money_quotes: Here's one of those tips that's not new — it's been around for at least the last few major releases of Mac OS X, and probably even dates back to NeXTStep — but is very useful, and, I think, not widely known.
For example, hallelujah is one of those words I can never remember how to spell, but I do know that it starts with "halle".
Even better, you can use the Space Bar and you'll get the completion you selected and a space character — i.e. arrow down to the completion you want and then just continue typing.
The F5 shortcut has one significant advantage over Esc — it can be used in contexts where Esc means "Cancel".
One probable reason why I seem to have a hard time building a habit for this feature is that I spend so much time writing in BBEdit and Mailsmith, neither of which support it.
posts.key_insight: Mac OS X's Cocoa text system has a powerful but underused auto-completion feature that, despite being available for years, remains hidden from most users partly because popular third-party editors don't support it.

posts.summary: Gruber investigates why Adobe's new Soundbooth audio editor is Intel-only on Mac, when Apple's universal binary toolchain makes supporting both architectures straightforward for new applications. He systematically dismantles Adobe's official explanations — that it would take 'nearly double' the development and testing time — by pointing to counterexamples like Adobe's own Lightroom and tiny shops like Rogue Amoeba that managed universal binaries with far fewer resources. He concludes the real reason is likely extensive SSE-optimized code inherited from Adobe's Windows-only Audition app, but argues this is ultimately a marketing decision Adobe is trying to dress up in technical justifications. The piece makes the case that abandoning PowerPC users — especially G5 owners who bought high-end machines partly to run Adobe software — is premature and costs Adobe both goodwill and money.
key_points: For new Mac applications built with Xcode, creating universal binaries adds minimal overhead — the 'nearly double' claim doesn't hold for most developers
Soundbooth likely inherited Intel-specific SSE vector code from Adobe's Windows-only Audition, making the PowerPC port genuinely non-trivial
Adobe's own Lightroom disproves the company's argument that Intel-only is the obvious choice for new cross-platform media applications
Adobe bears special responsibility toward PowerPC users since its delayed Intel migration was a key reason creative professionals bought late-model G5 Power Macs
The decision is fundamentally a business/marketing call that Adobe is unconvincingly framing as a technical necessity
money_quotes: Easier cross-platform development is certainly of interest to Adobe, but it's not something most Mac users care about. This translates to 'we're too lazy'.
Perhaps Nack should ask the Lightroom team?
You either see the value in developing Mac software or you don't, but Mac users aren't going to thank you just for developing Mac software at all.
Adobe, more than any other software developer, is responsible for creative professionals buying late-model high-end PowerPC hardware.
I'm not saying there isn't hard work involved. Isn't that Adobe's job? Or, rather, isn't that the job of Adobe's engineers? To write difficult code?
Rogue Amoeba has six people, and we managed to port from one platform to another. Adobe has almost 6,000 people and they can't pull it off?
What galls is that this is a marketing decision, but the explanations from Nack and Shafer are attempts to cast it in technical terms.
If there aren't enough G5 users to make it worthwhile for Adobe to add PowerPC support to Soundbooth, why are so many people complaining about it?
posts.key_insight: Adobe's claim that Intel-only development was a technical necessity is undermined by every counterexample — including Adobe's own Lightroom — revealing it as a business decision the company lacks the honesty to own.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews Stikkit, a new web app from Rael Dornfest's Values of N startup that uses a sticky-note metaphor with automatic parsing of notes to identify events, people, to-dos, and bookmarks. He praises its use of Markdown for formatting and its ambitious 'everything is just text' approach, but criticizes its over-reliance on unlabeled icons, heavy Ajax usage that breaks browser URLs and bookmarking, and the limitations of its free-form parsing syntax. He draws comparisons to 37signals' Backpack and Delicious, noting Stikkit's unclear business model. Ultimately, Gruber concludes that Stikkit is a well-executed implementation of a fundamentally flawed premise — the lack of UI-enforced structure makes it confusing when the 'magic' parsing inevitably falls short.
key_points: Stikkit's icon-heavy UI without text labels creates a cryptic, Olympic-Village-pictogram feeling that hinders discoverability
Heavy Ajax usage breaks fundamental web conventions — URLs don't change as you navigate, making it impossible to bookmark pages in an app that is itself a bookmark manager
The free-form text parsing for events has unintuitive word-order requirements that undermine the 'magical' promise of automatic detection
Using spaces as tag delimiters is a poor design choice inherited from Delicious that penalizes normal typing behavior — commas or semicolons would be better
The 'everything is just text' concept is ambitious but fundamentally flawed: when you present features as magical, it's confusing and irritating when the magic runs out
money_quotes: All this label-less iconography-as-UI gives me the feeling of being somewhere like the Olympic Village, where all the signs are wordless pictograms.
That strikes me as a particularly egregious sin for a web app that features bookmarking as one of its primary features — it doesn't do you much good to have a bunch of bookmarks all named "stikkit".
Free cash from the Web 2.0 money tree?

People type spaces all the time. If you want to use any multiple-word sequence as a tag, you either have to use spaces, or you have to resort to punctuational kludges like CamelCasing or replacing spaces with underscores or dashes.
A good delimiter is one that's easy to type but not something you'd commonly want to use as a literal character within the name itself. Space passes the first test, but fails miserably at the second.
Stikkit strikes me as a very good implementation of a flawed premise.
When you present features as being magical — "just type a date and it knows it's an event" — it's confusing and irritating when the magic runs out.
For jotting down ephemeral notes and reminders, nothing can beat paper and pen.
posts.key_insight: Presenting software features as 'magical' text parsing creates a worse user experience than explicit UI structure, because users have no way to understand the rules until the magic fails them.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Microsoft's deal to pay Universal Music a per-unit royalty on each Zune player sold is not a cunning plot to undermine Apple, but rather the result of Microsoft's weak bargaining position. Universal exploited Microsoft's desperation to launch Zune with a full music catalog, knowing Microsoft couldn't afford to go to market without Universal's one-third share of worldwide music. Gruber contends this is Universal's power play, not Microsoft's strategy, and notes the irony of Microsoft — famous for leveraging dominant market positions — being on the receiving end of such a deal. He dismisses concerns that this precedent poses a serious threat to Apple, since Apple has the leverage of popularity that the music labels need.
key_points: Microsoft paid Universal a per-unit Zune royalty not as a strategic move against Apple, but because Universal had the leverage and Microsoft couldn't launch without their catalog
The music industry has long wanted a cut of hardware revenue, as evidenced by Warner's Edgar Bronfman demanding iPod revenue sharing a year earlier
Microsoft lacks a dominant position in the consumer music space to leverage against partners, unlike its historical playbook with Windows
The deal's piracy justification is logically absurd — it implies Zune buyers should feel entitled to bootleg Universal music
Apple can resist similar demands because it has what the labels need: the dominant platform and massive customer base
money_quotes: What we've got here is a nice, warm, shit sandwich from Universal Music, and Microsoft concluded that they had no choice but to eat it.

Logically, it makes no more sense for Universal to be entitled to a share of music player sales than it does for music player manufacturers to be entitled to a share of Universal's compact disc revenues.
Music industry executives aren't exactly known for their sense of fairness.
If the justification for this deal is that it's compensation for bootleg music, does it not imply that Zune users should now feel entitled to bootleg Universal music?
It's Microsoft who, historically, approaches partners with 'We know you need this, and if you want it, you're going to do whatever we tell you to do' deals.
Far from seeing this Universal dollar-per-Zune deal as some sort of nefarious Redmond plot, I think it's actually giving Microsoft a nice taste of its own medicine.

Apple, though, surely will continue to resist, if not outright laugh off, such deals. Partly out of general principle, but mainly because unlike Microsoft, Apple has leverage over the music labels because they have something the labels need: popularity.
posts.key_insight: The Zune royalty deal reveals not Microsoft's cunning but its weakness — Universal exploited Microsoft's desperate need for content, giving the bully a taste of its own medicine.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that labeling released, commercially available software as 'beta' does not shield it from criticism. He collects multiple examples of Disco app critics who hedged their negative reviews with 'but it's a beta' disclaimers, then dismantles the logic behind such restraint. He draws a clear line: unreleased software deserves slack, but software available for purchase is fair game regardless of labels. His central point is that 'beta' in this context is merely a euphemism for 'buggy,' and developers shouldn't hide behind it.
key_points: Released software available for sale is open for full criticism regardless of any 'beta' label
The traditional meaning of 'beta' refers to pre-release software that is feature-complete but unpolished — not software already on the market
When applied to commercial released software, 'beta' is simply a euphemism for 'buggy'
Critics should not feel obligated to dilute their reviews with 'but it's a beta' disclaimers for software people can buy
Knowing where to draw the line between shipping and not shipping is a core part of the art of software development
money_quotes: Released software that is labeled 'beta' is still released software, and is fair game for the same level of criticism as any released software.
You can't 'semi-release' your 1.0 just because you want it out there but aren't yet finished. Being semi-released is like being semi-pregnant.
No, what 'beta' means in this context is 'buggy'.
An app is either good enough to release and sell, or else development isn't yet done. A label doesn't affect this equation.

The home stretch is the hardest part of software development. You've already been working for months, often even a year or longer. But just because you can see the finish line doesn't mean you're actually near it.
Using 'beta' as a badge of honor for released commercial software makes no more sense than using 'buggy' in the same context, and it makes no more sense as an excuse, either.
My advice to developers is to realize that 'beta' is a warning, not an excuse.
posts.key_insight: Calling released, commercially available software 'beta' is just a euphemism for 'buggy' and grants no immunity from criticism.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Larry Seltzer's eWeek opinion piece claiming Mac OS X is 'as insecure as anything out there,' arguing that Seltzer commits a fundamental logical error by conflating potential vulnerabilities with actual security outcomes. Seltzer cited a Symantec report listing Mac OS X vulnerabilities, but Gruber notes the report never claims the vulnerability collection rivals other operating systems. Gruber highlights the glaring discrepancy: Mac OS X holds roughly 6% U.S. market share but effectively zero percent of actual malware, which the 'market share' explanation alone cannot account for. He systematically rebuts each alternative explanation Seltzer offers, showing they contradict observable facts about Windows security. Gruber concludes that the simplest explanation is the correct one: Mac OS X is genuinely better designed and more secure, not invulnerable, just better.
key_points: Seltzer's binary framing — either invulnerable or equally vulnerable — is a false dichotomy that ignores degrees of security
Mac OS X's share of actual malware is effectively zero, wildly disproportionate even to its small market share, which undermines the 'not worth targeting' argument
The market share explanation fails further when compared to Linux and Solaris, which have fewer users but more attacks, and Windows, which suffers massive non-server exploits
Seltzer overstates the Symantec report's conclusions — the report shows Mac OS X isn't magically invulnerable, not that it's equally insecure as Windows
The simplest explanation — that Mac OS X is genuinely better designed — is the one Seltzer refuses to consider
money_quotes: Seltzer's logic seems to be that an operating system is either invulnerable or vulnerable, and since Mac OS X is vulnerable, that means it's in the same position as Windows.
Mac OS X's share of the malware market isn't just disproportionately low — it's nearly zero.

If there's one firm conclusion to be drawn from the MacBook Wi-Fi hack fiasco in August, it's that you can get a hell of a lot more attention for a Mac OS X exploit that you never even release or prove actually exists than you can get for an actual released-into-the-wild Windows exploit.
They're unsatisfying because they don't make any sense at all.
The explanation that makes sense is the obvious one: that Mac OS X really is more secure and better designed.
Not that it's totally secure. Not that it's perfectly designed. Not that it is utterly impervious to attack because it's protected by magic leprechauns. Just that it's better.
posts.key_insight: The gap between Mac OS X's theoretical vulnerabilities and its near-zero real-world malware rate points to genuinely better design, not luck or obscurity.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that indie Mac developers should not underprice their software or participate in deep-discount giveaway promotions like MacZot. He cites developer Steve Harris's experience where giving away cheap copies of his app KIT generated a flood of support requests but zero increase in regular sales. Gruber contends that cheapskates will always want a lower price no matter what you charge, so developers should price confidently and let quality speak for itself. He posits that good software will break out of obscurity on its own merits without giveaway gimmicks, and that the real formula for indie success is hard work plus talent. The piece serves as both a critique of race-to-the-bottom pricing and an encouragement to indie developers to value their own work.
key_points: MacZot-style giveaways generate support burden without increasing regular sales, as Steve Harris's experience with KIT demonstrated
Cheapskates will always want a lower price — you literally cannot set a price low enough to satisfy them, so don't try
Professional-quality software should cost at least $20-25; if you don't think it's worth that, release it as freeware
Good software will break out of obscurity on its own merit without giveaway gimmicks, though crossing from enthusiast awareness to mainstream adoption requires some luck
Developers who want users to treat their software as valuable must first market it as though it's valuable
money_quotes: Don't underprice your software in an attempt to appeal to cheapskates.
The whole 'If you give away a few thousand copies you'll get tens of thousands of full-priced sales later on' idea just doesn't work.
If you charge $40 per license, they will say, 'This is a great app, but it's too expensive. I'd buy it if it were $25.' But if you had set the price at $25, they'd tell you it should be $15.
$10 is not enough money to charge for professional quality software. If you, the developer, don't think it's worth ten bucks, you really should just release it as freeware.
The point here is that you can't set a price low enough to please the cheapskates.
Hard Work + Talent = Success
If you want users to treat your software like it's valuable, you, the developer, need to market it as though it's valuable.
Far more budding indie developers fail because their prices are too low than too high.
posts.key_insight: Indie developers hurt themselves more by underpricing than overpricing — cheapskates will never be satisfied, and giveaway promotions generate support costs without converting free users into paying customers.

posts.summary: Gruber warns about a serious Mac OS X security vulnerability where a malicious .dmg disk image file can cause a kernel panic on any up-to-date Mac running 10.4.8. The exploit is especially dangerous because Safari's default 'Open safe files after downloading' preference means most users are vulnerable to attack simply by visiting a malicious website. Gruber has repeatedly warned about this preference and argues it should be removed or at least disabled by default. He directly challenges Apple to act on this recurring security issue.
key_points: A malicious .dmg file from the 'Month of Kernel Bugs' project can cause kernel panics on fully patched Mac OS X 10.4.8 systems
Safari's 'Open safe files after downloading' preference, enabled by default, makes users vulnerable to drive-by kernel panics just from visiting a website
Gruber has repeatedly flagged this Safari preference as a security liability, and this exploit validates his warnings
The preference compounds a kernel-level bug into a remotely exploitable attack vector requiring zero user interaction
money_quotes: You should be safe, of course, because you read Daring Fireball, and so you know that you should turn off Safari's incredibly foolish "Open 'safe' files after downloading" preference.
But given that this preference, which in my opinion shouldn't even exist, is on by default, most Mac users are vulnerable to attack via this exploit.
If you have the preference turned on, you'll get a kernel panic just by downloading the file — and any web site you visit can initiate a file download automatically.
Question for Apple: How many times must this Safari preference be exploited before you remove it from Safari, or at least turn it off by default?
posts.key_insight: Safari's default preference to automatically open downloaded files transforms every kernel-level file-handling bug into a remotely exploitable zero-click attack, and Apple's refusal to change this default is indefensible.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Palm CEO Ed Colligan's dismissive remarks about Apple's prospects in the mobile phone market, finding them laughably misguided on multiple levels. He notes the irony of Colligan calling Apple 'PC guys' when Apple has deep expertise in handheld consumer electronics, and of Palm itself licensing Microsoft's Windows Mobile. Gruber argues that Apple's approach would likely involve doing less than competitors but doing it exceptionally well, much like the iPod strategy. He draws a parallel to Rob Malda's infamous dismissal of the original iPod. While acknowledging uncertainty about how Apple would navigate carrier restrictions, Gruber finds Colligan's confidence that Apple would struggle to sell phones to be deeply misguided.
key_points: Colligan calling Apple 'PC guys' is absurd given Apple's unmatched expertise in handheld consumer electronics and UI design
Palm licensing Windows Mobile while dismissing Apple's design capabilities reveals a fundamental lack of self-awareness
Apple's advantage would be struggling with the problem privately rather than selling less-than-decent designs along the way like Palm did
An Apple phone would do less than existing smartphones but do it really well — the same strategy that made the iPod dominant
Colligan's dismissal echoes Rob Malda's infamous 'No wireless. Less space than a nomad. Lame.' reaction to the iPod
money_quotes: It's just that Apple hasn't been selling their less-than-decent designs along the way, like Palm has.
The whole point of an Apple phone would be to make something that doesn't suck and is actually cool; the carriers impose utterly sucky restrictions on every phone they sell.
An Apple phone wouldn't do more than a Treo or a BlackBerry or a Razr — it would do less, and what it would do, it would do really well.
An Apple cell phone is sure to be greeted by existing quote-unquote 'smartphone' aficionados as utterly underwhelming. It's hardly got any features! It's just well-designed!
Meanwhile, the line at your local Apple store will stretch out the entrance and down to the mall's food court.
It's also certainly the case that an Apple phone would aspire to something better than 'decent'.
posts.key_insight: Incumbents who dismiss Apple as outsiders consistently fail to understand that Apple's strategy is to do less but do it exceptionally well — the same blindspot that led critics to dismiss the iPod.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects the early sales claims around Microsoft's Zune MP3 player, contrasting a puff-piece CNN Money article with actual Amazon sales rankings. He shows the Zune languishing far behind multiple iPod models, SanDisk players, and even Creative's Zen Vision:M across several Amazon category rankings. The brown Zune sells worse than discontinued year-old iPods, and the top-selling black Zune ranks below a record player in Amazon's overall electronics list. Gruber argues Microsoft made a fundamental strategic error by building a hard-drive-based player when the market sweet spot had shifted to flash-based Nano-sized devices. He concludes the Zune's best sales week was likely its first, and the trajectory points downward.
key_points: The CNN Money article touting Zune's 9% market share in its first week reads like a regurgitated press release that doesn't hold up against actual sales data
Amazon rankings show the Zune trailing not just current iPods but also discontinued year-old iPod models and competitors like Creative's Zen Vision:M
Microsoft copied the wrong iPod form factor — the market sweet spot had moved to flash-based Nano-sized players, not hard-drive-based full-size ones
The top-selling Zune ranks lower than a record player on Amazon's overall electronics bestseller list
Black outsells white across all brands, a clear consumer preference visible in the Amazon data
money_quotes: CNN Money today ran an article titled 'Don't Cry for the Zune Just Yet', which reads like a regurgitated Microsoft press release.
Amazon is currently selling more year-old iPods than they are brand-new brown Zunes.
The top-selling Zune (black) is all the way down at #62, which is a dozen spots lower than this record player.
I can't help but think that Microsoft copied the wrong iPod.
The Nano is the sweet spot for Apple, and it's the Nano-ish players from competitors that are registering in the sales charts.
If the Zune's best week was its first week, it's in trouble — and it looks to me like its sales are sinking, not rising.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's fundamental Zune mistake wasn't just making a worse iPod — it was copying the wrong iPod entirely, building a full-size hard-drive player when the market had already shifted to flash-based pocket players like the Nano.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that The Omni Group's pre-announced OmniFocus application is, by any standard dictionary definition, vaporware — regardless of the company's objections to the term. After Omni's marketing manager Linda Sharps published a lengthy rebuttal insisting OmniFocus isn't vaporware, Gruber methodically cites six dictionary definitions to demonstrate that any announced-but-unshippable product qualifies. He contends that pre-announcements are inherently competitive moves designed to discourage potential customers from choosing existing alternatives, and calls out Omni's claim otherwise as disingenuous. He draws a parallel to Apple's iTV pre-announcement, noting that even Apple — famous for secrecy — resorts to vaporware when strategically necessary.
key_points: Every major dictionary defines vaporware as software announced but not yet available — OmniFocus fits this definition regardless of Omni's sincere intentions to ship it
Vaporware is considered pejorative because even well-intentioned pre-announcements create inflated expectations and discourage customers from exploring competing products
Omni's claim that they pre-announced OmniFocus purely to gauge interest, not for competitive reasons, is disingenuous given the thriving GTD software market
Apple's iTV pre-announcement demonstrates that even the most secretive company uses vaporware strategically when competitive circumstances demand it
The fewer details in a pre-announcement, the more room is left for imagination to fill — making expectations impossible to manage
money_quotes: This is why you shouldn't pre-announce apps.
Simply stating that OmniFocus is not vaporware doesn't alter the fact that OmniFocus is vaporware.
I didn't say it was hyped. I said it was vaporware.
The fewer the details in a pre-announcement, the more room is left for your imagination to fill.
Implicit in any pre-announcement is the idea that 'you should wait for this'.
Maybe it's going to be a complex, over-designed monster like OmniPlan. But today, it's vaporware.
I've got to call bullshit on the idea that Omni's decision to pre-announce OmniFocus wasn't at least partially for competitive reasons.
Competitive doesn't necessarily imply nefarious, but it's disingenuous to pretend it wasn't competitive at all.
posts.key_insight: Pre-announcing a product that doesn't yet exist is vaporware by definition, regardless of the company's good intentions or track record, and the competitive motivation behind such announcements shouldn't be denied.

posts.summary: Gruber writes a fictional transcript imagining negotiations between Apple's Steve Jobs and Universal Music CEO Doug Morris over a proposed per-iPod royalty fee. Morris, emboldened by his deal to extract $1 per Zune from Microsoft, tries to get $2 per iPod from Apple. Jobs appears to agree enthusiastically, then uses a reductio ad absurdum strategy — suggesting Universal should also get royalties on every Mac sold — before delivering a profane punchline that exposes the absurdity of the entire premise. Morris quickly retreats to the existing 99-cents-per-song deal. The piece satirizes the music industry's sense of entitlement to hardware revenue and highlights the vast power asymmetry between Apple and the labels.
key_points: Universal's Zune royalty deal emboldened the label to seek even larger per-device fees from Apple, framing it as Apple maintaining 'leadership'
Jobs's fictional strategy of enthusiastically agreeing, then extending the logic to Macs, exposes the absurdity of taxing hardware for hypothetical piracy
The power dynamic is starkly illustrated: Morris brings six attorneys and stacks of documents; Jobs brings one attorney and nothing on the table
The Zune's tiny market share is the running joke — seven units would 'double their sales for the week'
The piece argues that the existing iTunes Store deal (99 cents per song) is already fair, and hardware royalties are pure rent-seeking
money_quotes: On the table in front of Morris and each of the Universal attorneys are various neatly stacked folders, contracts, and legal pads. In front of Jobs the table is completely clear; he holds nothing in his hands.
A pittance, really. But what it is, is a small step toward compensating us for the stolen music that belongs to us which we all know is being stored on these sorts of devices.
You can't download music from P2P to an iPod. You download it to your Mac, and then it goes to your iPod. So maybe Universal deserves something for each Mac we sell, too.
Mr. Jobs is suggesting that you take a white Microsoft Zune 30 gigabyte digital music player and insert it into your rectum.
Seven Zunes — that should double their sales for the week.
And Universal Music will get seven dollars.
posts.key_insight: The music industry's demand for per-device royalties on iPods is absurd rent-seeking, and Apple's dominant market position gives it the leverage to simply refuse.

posts.summary: Gruber documents Microsoft using a direct copy of Apple's Workgroup Manager icon on their Vista Business edition purchase page. He walks readers through a step-by-step comparison of the two icons and notes Microsoft couldn't even copy it properly, introducing resizing artifacts. After the post gained attention, Microsoft hastily replaced the icon twice — first with a poorly anti-aliased substitute, then with something resembling Windows 95 clip art. Gruber also notes Microsoft misspelled 'Business' in the page title.
key_points: Microsoft's Vista Business page used an icon directly copied from Apple's Mac OS X Server Workgroup Manager application
Microsoft replaced the stolen icon twice in rapid succession after being caught, each replacement looking worse than the last
The hastily assembled replacement icons featured terrible anti-aliasing and dated visual quality, underscoring the lack of design care
Microsoft also misspelled 'Business' as 'Busines' in their web page title, compounding the embarrassment
money_quotes: Think of a snarky comment making fun of the fact that Microsoft can't even copy an icon without screwing it up by resizing it.
Judging by the appalling anti-aliasing, it would appear this replacement icon was rather hastily assembled.
Good thing I saved a copy of the original GIF file here.
By the way: GIF? In 2006?
An even crummier-looking replacement icon that looks straight out of Windows 95.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's copying of an Apple icon — and the increasingly embarrassing attempts to cover it up — perfectly illustrated the company's design deficit relative to Apple.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects the economics of the MacHeist software bundle, revealing that while it's marketed as a community benefit for independent Mac developers, the deal structure heavily favors the MacHeist organizers. Developers were paid flat fees (around $5,000 each) regardless of how many bundles sold, meaning as sales increased, virtually all additional profit went to MacHeist. Gruber calculates that if sales hit the $400,000 level, MacHeist would keep over 70% of profits while the average developer would receive less than 3%. He argues that a fair arrangement would give MacHeist an agent-like 10-15% cut, and proposes a profit-sharing model instead. The post's final update notes that MacHeist ultimately grossed $640,000, with the organizers keeping an estimated 85% of profits.
key_points: MacHeist paid developers flat fees (~$5,000 each) for unlimited license sales, creating a grossly lopsided profit structure as sales scaled up
The promotion was framed as supporting indie Mac developers, but the economic reality was that MacHeist's organizers stood to profit far more than the developers whose work they were selling
At final sales of $640,000, MacHeist kept an estimated 85% of profits while the average developer received about 1.5%
Gruber proposes a fairer model: profit-sharing where MacHeist takes an agent-like cut equivalent to one developer's share, roughly 10-15%
The charitable donation component (25% of gross) was used as a clever marketing device to frame sales milestones, making it harder to criticize the promotion
money_quotes: What's in dispute is whether the money is being distributed equitably. Just because someone is satisfied with a bad deal doesn't mean it isn't a bad deal.
How much worse for one of these developers would it be for you to pirate a copy of their app than to obtain a legitimate license through this bundle?

70 percent of the profit would go to the MacHeist team — almost 25 times more money than the average profit of a developer in the bundle, and more than double the profit of all 10 developers combined.
There's something silly about calling these apps 'hidden gems' in one paragraph, and then immediately proclaiming that the apps have been 'showered with awards and accolades'.

My problem with MacHeist is that they're couching this bundle as a service to the indie developer community, when the truth is that they stand to make a significant amount of money while paying comparatively little to the very developers whose interests they claim to be serving.
Respectable agents or managers take no more than a 15 percent cut of their clients' revenue, and usually not more than 10 percent.
The participating developers were also strongly encouraged not to share the details of their MacHeist deals with each other or the public.
posts.key_insight: MacHeist's flat-fee payment structure meant that as sales grew, developers' share of profits shrank toward zero while the organizers captured nearly all the upside — the opposite of the community-benefiting narrative they sold.

posts.summary: Gruber describes his quest to write an AppleScript that resizes and arranges browser windows to a consistent layout across different displays. He needed to programmatically determine screen dimensions, which AppleScript doesn't natively expose. Craig Hockenberry suggested querying the Finder for the desktop window bounds, which works perfectly for single-display setups. However, Gruber demonstrates that with multiple displays, the technique returns a single bounding rectangle encompassing all screens, making it unreliable for multi-monitor configurations. He documents the coordinate behavior across different display arrangements and notes that a proper solution for multiple displays requires a different approach.
key_points: AppleScript has no built-in way to query display dimensions, but asking the Finder for 'bounds of window of desktop' provides a workable workaround for single displays
The desktop coordinate system uses the top-left corner of the main display (with the menu bar) as the origin, so secondary displays can have negative coordinates
With multiple displays, the Finder returns one bounding rectangle spanning all screens, making it impossible to determine individual display sizes
Gruber uses leading underscores in AppleScript variable names to distinguish them from scripting dictionary terms, analogous to Perl's sigils
money_quotes: The main reason I wanted to write the script in the first place is that I switch between my 20-inch Cinema Display and the built-in display of my 15-inch PowerBook, which (the switching) can leave browser windows in weird sizes.

The leading underscores are something I've started using in my AppleScript variable names to make them easily distinguishable from terms in scripting dictionaries; think of them sort of like Perl's sigils.
The first number is negative because the origin (0, 0) of the coordinate system is the top left corner of the main display — the one with the menu bar.
So, in short, asking the Finder for the bounds of window of desktop works great if there's just one display.
