posts.key_insight: AppleScript's lack of native display-dimension queries can be worked around by asking the Finder for the desktop bounds, but this hack breaks down with multiple displays because it returns a single rectangle spanning all screens.

posts.summary: Gruber breaks down the MacHeist bundle revenue distribution to expose how lopsidedly the profits favored the MacHeist organizers over the participating developers. Of the estimated $760,000 in total revenue, MacHeist kept roughly $463,500 (87.5% of profit) while individual developers received an average of just 1.3% each. The post uses a stark numerical breakdown format to let the figures speak for themselves. Gruber closes by juxtaposing these numbers with MacHeist organizer Phill Ryu's celebratory claim that the event was 'one of the biggest successes in the history of Mac shareware,' highlighting the irony that the developers themselves saw very little of that success.
key_points: MacHeist generated $760,000 total but paid bundled app developers only an estimated $66,500 combined
The MacHeist team took home an estimated 87.5% of total profit while each developer averaged just 1.1-1.3%
Gruber stands behind his anonymous sourcing on the developer payment figures, noting they haven't been disputed
The post uses Phill Ryu's triumphant closing statement about Mac shareware's bright future as devastating ironic counterpoint to the actual revenue split
money_quotes: Estimated profit for the MacHeist team: $463,500
MacHeist's percentage share of the total profit: 87.5
Average percentage share of the total profit for each individual developer: 1.3
Average percentage share of the total profit for each developer whose company name does not rhyme with 'Malicious Donster': 1.1
MacHeist, which will go down as one of the biggest successes in the history of Mac shareware, you have sent a message loud and clear: the state of Mac shareware is stronger than ever.
posts.key_insight: MacHeist was celebrated as a triumph for Mac shareware, but the revenue split reveals it was overwhelmingly a triumph for MacHeist's organizers at the developers' expense.

posts.summary: Gruber awards his 'Jackass of the Week' distinction jointly to tech pundits Rob Enderle and Paul Thurrott. Enderle continued pushing a debunked Forrester Research report claiming iTunes Store sales dropped in 2006, even after Forrester itself disavowed that interpretation. When a ComScore report showed iTunes sales were actually up 84%, Enderle fabricated a claim that Apple had paid for the report — a claim ComScore's chairman personally refuted. Thurrott earned co-honors for calling Enderle's column 'excellent.' Gruber highlights an especially unhinged Enderle passage claiming the public Steve Jobs is a fictional character played by an actor, leading Gruber to wonder if Enderle is less a jackass than a genuine nutcase.
key_points: Enderle kept pushing a Forrester report's debunked interpretation of declining iTunes sales even after Forrester corrected the record
When confronted with contradictory ComScore data showing 84% iTunes sales growth, Enderle fabricated the claim that Apple had commissioned the report
ComScore's chairman personally refuted Enderle's insinuation, confirming it was invented
Thurrott earned criticism for endorsing Enderle's column as 'excellent' despite its fabrications
Enderle's claim that Steve Jobs is essentially a fictional character played by an actor reveals conspiracy-theory-level thinking
money_quotes: It's a Jackass of the Week dream team: Rob Enderle and Paul Thurrott.
It's apparently something Enderle just made up, disparaging ComScore's integrity.
The public "Steve Jobs" is a character created by Apple's agency played by a guy named "Steve Jobs" — that's why Apple doesn't like the Jobs biographies — they break the image.
Had they gone the Ronald McDonald route, they could probably keep the name and change the guy.
Sometimes I wonder if Enderle isn't so much a jackass as he is a nutcase.
posts.key_insight: Gruber exposes how anti-Apple pundits like Enderle fabricate claims and dismiss contradictory evidence to maintain their preferred narrative, while their peers in the tech press validate the dishonesty by calling it excellent analysis.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a live Daring Fireball session at Macworld Expo 2007, scheduled for Tuesday at 4:30 PM on the Macworld Live stage. His special guest will be Cabel Sasser, co-founder of Panic. They plan to discuss Steve Jobs's keynote news, UI design, and Panic's history. The post is a lighthearted promotional piece written in Gruber's signature self-deprecating comedic style.
key_points: Gruber is doing a 45-minute live session at Macworld Expo 2007 with Cabel Sasser of Panic
Topics will include Jobs's keynote, UI design, and Panic's company history
The session is accessible with just a cheap expo floor pass, not a full conference pass
The event will be recorded and made available for download
money_quotes: You're thinking, Daring Fireball is pretty good, but it would be better if instead of being a web site, it were more like a talk show, taking place in front of a live audience, with jokes and commentary and interviews with special guest Mac nerd celebrities.
I plan to spend most of the time bragging about how accurate my keynote predictions were.
Cabel and I will both be wearing shirts and pants, and neither of us will be drunk, where by "drunk" I mean "visibly drunk".
posts.key_insight: A promotional post for Gruber's first live Daring Fireball event at Macworld Expo, notable mainly for its comedic self-awareness about translating a beloved blog into a live format.

posts.summary: Gruber investigates which processor powers the newly announced iPhone, noting that Apple conspicuously avoided mentioning it during the keynote. Intel officially confirmed it's not involved, and Gruber's sources point to an ARM processor — a significant new architecture target for Apple's operating system. He explores why Apple might be keeping quiet: possibly to avoid drawing attention to having sold its ARM stake in the late '90s, or because final chip selection isn't locked down. He also distinguishes between 'Mac OS X' and the iPhone's 'OS X' as related but distinct operating systems, and warns Adobe that assuming x86 permanence may prove shortsighted.
key_points: Intel officially confirmed it is not supplying the iPhone's processor, and Apple provided zero information about the chip
Sources indicated the iPhone runs on an ARM processor, making it a new architecture target for Apple's OS
Apple once owned a significant ARM stake — acquired through the Newton — and sold it all in the late '90s, an ironic decision given the iPhone
Gruber distinguishes Mac OS X from the iPhone's 'OS X,' declaring them siblings but not the same thing
Adobe's assumption that they can now target x86-only may prove as shortsighted as the old assumption of PowerPC permanence
money_quotes: Sources familiar with the matter (as they say) hinted to me that the iPhone is powered by an ARM processor.
This in itself is intriguing, as it is an entirely new chip architecture that Apple's operating system is now apparently targeting.

Apple owned a big chunk of ARM because ARM was the company that supplied processors for the Newton; they dumped it, perhaps, not just for profit but because they didn't see the need to maintain a stake in a company that produced processors that were useful for things like portable touch-screen communication devices. Whoops.
The days of lazily referring to 'Mac OS X' as 'OS X' are now over.
The lesson most other Mac developers seem to have taken is to stop making assumptions about the underlying processor architecture.
Their 'Intel-only' Mac software may well bite them on the ass someday in the future.
posts.key_insight: Apple's silence about the iPhone's ARM processor hints at both an ironic reversal of a past divestment and a future where developers who assume architectural permanence will regret it.

posts.summary: Gruber recounts his live appearance at Macworld Expo 2007, where he shared the stage with Panic cofounder Cabel Sasser for a 45-minute session at the Macworld booth. They discussed the newly announced iPhone, Apple TV, Panic's company history, and the state of indie Mac software development. The session ran well past its allotted time and included audience Q&A on topics ranging from the iPhone's future to why Daring Fireball lacks comments. Gruber notes the audio recording is available as a Macworld podcast for those who missed it.
key_points: Gruber and Cabel Sasser held a live session at the Macworld booth covering the iPhone, Apple TV, and indie Mac development
The session took place on keynote day, meaning some major announcements (like iPhone third-party development details) were still unknown at the time of recording
Audience enthusiasm for the iPhone was overwhelming, with nearly every hand raised when asked who planned to buy one
The session ran well past its 45-minute slot, suggesting strong audience engagement
money_quotes: When Cabel Sasser agreed to appear as my guest, I thought it could turn out really fun.
Questions from the audience ranged from what we think the iPhone means for the future of the iPod to why Daring Fireball doesn't have comments.
It's audio-only, but we didn't use any slides, props, or visual aids, so the only thing you're missing out on are our hand gestures.
When Cabel asks for a show of hands for how many people plan to buy an iPhone, just about every hand went up.
posts.key_insight: The live Macworld session captured a moment of peak excitement around the original iPhone announcement, with the Mac community's enthusiasm palpable in the near-universal show of hands for buying one.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Apple's claim that the iPhone 'runs OS X,' addressing two opposing misconceptions: that it runs a full desktop Mac OS X, and that the claim is marketing BS. He explains that the iPhone runs a genuine subset of OS X — with the Mach kernel, Darwin underpinnings, and Cocoa frameworks — but with a completely new UI designed for a mobile device. He draws a useful distinction between 'OS X' as the underlying technology platform and 'Mac OS X' as the specific desktop product, arguing the iPhone claim is more accurate from an engineering perspective than a marketing one. He extends this analysis to Apple TV and explores the branding implications for Windows users and the Mac brand.
key_points: The iPhone runs a genuine but heavily trimmed subset of OS X — Mach kernel, Darwin, Cocoa AppKit — not a full Mac OS X installation, which would be far too large at ~2GB vs the iPhone's ~500MB OS.

Slashdot-style skepticism that the iPhone can't be running OS X (based on Darwin licensing and ARM support arguments) is wrong because Apple owns the Darwin copyright and can do whatever it wants with its own code.
The distinction Gruber draws: 'OS X' is the technology (kernel, drivers, frameworks), while 'Mac OS X' is the product (OS X plus Mac-specific apps and UI). The iPhone runs OS X but not Mac OS X.

The iPhone UI shares nothing with the Mac UI — no menu bar, no windows, no mouse cursor — so from a lay user's perspective it neither looks nor acts like a Mac, even though engineers would recognize it as OS X.
Apple's branding strategy deliberately separates iPhone from 'Mac' to avoid scaring Windows users and to keep the iPhone floating above the Mac-vs-Windows rivalry, much like the iPod.
money_quotes: It's more accurate to call the iPhone's system software 'OS X' from an engineering perspective than from a marketing one.
Mac nerds, and Cocoa programmers in particular, would look at the iPhone's system and agree, Holy crap, that really is Mac OS X.
It's a 'full' version of OS X not because it contains everything from Mac OS X, but because it contains everything you'd actually want from Mac OS X.

There is no menu bar. There are no windows. There is no mouse cursor. These three things have been the defining fundamental elements of the Mac UI ever since the original Macintosh, and none are present in the iPhone UI.
OS X is technology; Mac OS X is a product.
You wouldn't want to take a UI optimized for 20-inch displays and squish it onto a 3.5-inch screen.

On a technical level, yes, an iPhone does appear to be much more like a Mac than an iPod. But on a marketing level, the iPhone is very much like an iPod, floating high above the fray of the 20-year-old Mac-vs.-Windows rivalry.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone legitimately runs OS X in the computer science sense — same kernel, same frameworks — but Apple's careful distinction between 'OS X' the technology and 'Mac OS X' the product reveals a deliberate platform strategy that extends beyond the Mac.

posts.summary: Gruber shares a simple but practical AppleScript for use with Jesper's ThisService utility, which lets users create system-wide Mac OS X services from scripts without Xcode. The script counts words in any selected text and displays the result in an alert dialog. He walks through the code, explaining why the alert must be displayed in the frontmost app's context rather than taking a shortcut. He acknowledges AppleScript's word-counting limitations with HTML and punctuation but notes it's a good enough approximation that works across most apps including browser form fields.
key_points: ThisService lets anyone create system-wide Mac OS X services from AppleScript or Unix scripts without needing Xcode or Cocoa knowledge
The display alert command must run in the context of the frontmost visible application, otherwise the alert is invisible to the user
AppleScript's word counting is imprecise — it treats HTML tags as words and punctuation as delimiters, making URLs count as multiple words
Keyboard shortcuts for services are limited to Command-Shift combinations, a Mac OS X limitation rather than a ThisService one
money_quotes: You give ThisService a script — either AppleScript or any Unix scripting language such as Perl, Python, or Ruby — and it generates a system-wide service based on that script, right there in the Services menu, available in any app.
No Xcode or Cocoa required. You just write the script.
That's the entire script.
But that won't work, because the `display alert` command needs to appear in the context of some visible application, otherwise it runs in the context of the service itself, and you'll never see it.
I chose Command-Shift-K (K for kount; Command-Shift-C is the standard shortcut for the Show Colors command).
posts.key_insight: ThisService democratizes Mac OS X service creation by letting anyone turn a simple script into a system-wide tool, no developer tools required.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Rob Enderle's iPhone analysis on multiple fronts: Enderle's claim that the LG Prada phone is superior ignores software (the iPhone's core differentiator), his prediction that a DRM-laden music service called Ruckus will topple iTunes ignores that college students already have free DRM-free music, and his claim that Apple copied Philips 1998 prototypes is visually absurd. Gruber also addresses readers who claim Enderle's old predictions about Apple switching to Intel and releasing flash iPods eventually came true, arguing that predictions off by two or more years aren't predictions at all. The piece is a thorough takedown of Enderle's credibility as a tech analyst.
key_points: Enderle's comparison of the LG Prada to the iPhone ignores software, which is the iPhone's entire value proposition
The prediction that DRM-laden 'free' music downloads will topple iTunes ignores that college students already have unlimited DRM-free music via piracy
Enderle's claim that Apple copied 1998 Philips prototypes for the iPhone is visually absurd — the devices look nothing alike
Predictions that eventually come true years late don't validate the predictor — timing and specifics matter
Hindsight compresses timelines, making wildly wrong predictions seem prescient in retrospect
money_quotes: The software, of course, being the whole point of the iPhone.
Hint to Enderle: college students have had all the free music downloads they want for years, and the songs they download don't use DRM.
I've seen potato chips that look more like an iPhone than these things do.
Someone please explain to me why anyone takes this guy seriously.
That these things eventually came to pass does not make Enderle right.

If I predict today that later this year Apple is going to ship an iPod Nano-sized iPhone that gets 10 hours of battery life and supports 3G data networks, it wouldn't make my prediction correct if such a device were to debut in January 2009.
Two years in the future is, and feels, a long way off. Hindsight, on the other hand, has a condensing effect.
posts.key_insight: A prediction that eventually comes true years after its stated deadline isn't prescience — it's hindsight's condensing effect making bad analysis look better than it was.

posts.summary: Gruber interviews Peter N Lewis and Matthew Drayton about the transfer of Interarchy, one of the most popular indie Mac file transfer apps, from Lewis's Stairways Software to Drayton's new company Nolobe. Lewis explains he's ready to move on after a dozen years of development, while Drayton, who has worked on the app for six years, sees it as an opportunity to start his own company with an established customer base. The interview covers Interarchy's origins as an archie client in 1993, its unusual Pascal codebase, and the surprisingly workable toolchain built around BBEdit and GNU Pascal. Lewis reflects on the challenge of a successful product preventing you from pursuing anything else, and wisely declines to compete with Apple on a Finder alternative.
key_points: Interarchy changed hands from Stairways Software to Nolobe in an amicable employee buyout after over a dozen years of development
The app is still written primarily in Pascal, with a custom toolchain using BBEdit, GNU Pascal, and ctags that Lewis describes as working 'very nicely'
Lewis was a pioneer of the web-based indie Mac software distribution model, helping found payment processor Kagi in the early 1990s
Lewis wisely avoided building a Finder competitor, noting Apple could render such work obsolete with a single major update
Drayton enlisted his brother David, freshly graduated with a CS degree, mirroring how Drayton himself joined Stairways in 2001
money_quotes: The problem with having a successful program like Interarchy is it essentially stops you from working on anything else that doesn't succeed as well as it does.
Going head to head with Apple on what amounts to a straight user interface does not strike me as a good idea.
Who knows when Apple might decide it is time for a major change in direction for the Finder, so after a lot of work it would be very easy to be left behind in the dust if Apple ever got serious.
In other ways — and I mean this as a deep compliment — it works like the Finder used to, getting certain things right that the OS X Finder never has.
Peter was a pioneer of the modern web-based business and distribution model for indie Mac developers.
Matthew, if you accept as many of my feature requests as Peter did, I'm sure Nolobe is going to do just fine.
posts.key_insight: A successful indie Mac app can become a golden cage for its creator, making an amicable handoff to a trusted employee the best path forward for both the product and the developer.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Bill Gates's Newsweek interview claims that Apple copied Vista's features from Microsoft and that Mac OS X is riddled with daily security exploits. He methodically debunks Gates's security claims as false, noting the Month of Apple Bugs project produced only one serious unpatched exploit. He then tackles Gates's assertion that Apple copied the menu bar from Microsoft, tracing the history of GUI development from Xerox through Apple's Lisa to the Mac to show pull-down menus originated at Apple. Gruber argues the real dynamic is that Microsoft deals with being outpaced by reframing Apple's shipped features as copies of Microsoft's unshipped plans, citing WinFS vs. Spotlight as a prime example.
key_points: Gates's claim that Mac OS X suffers daily total exploits is demonstrably false — the Month of Apple Bugs produced only one serious remote exploit, which Apple patched
Gates's narrative inverts reality: he claims Apple copied Microsoft's UI features, when Microsoft had been shipping Windows features inspired by the Mac for decades
The File/Edit/View menu bar originated at Apple's Lisa group, not at Xerox or Microsoft, directly contradicting Gates's challenge to trace its origins
Apple's 1990s 'look and feel' lawsuit was wrong-headed because no one should own the basic concept of a GUI — Apple should have focused on being the best, not the only

Microsoft's coping mechanism for being outpaced is reframing Apple's shipping features as copies of Microsoft's announced-but-undelivered features, like claiming Spotlight ripped off the never-shipped WinFS
money_quotes: It's either an angry, slanderous lie, or Bill Gates is an uninformed jackass.
In Gates's view, Microsoft came up with these features, Apple copied them, and Apple got them into their shipping product first because Microsoft was spending so much time improving Vista's security.
What Apple needed to do was work on maintaining the Mac's lead as the best GUI; instead they attempted to protect their supposed right to be the only GUI.
Apple didn't invent the idea of the graphical user interface. They invented the first commercial implementation of that idea.
In Microsoft's view, it's not that Vista now offers features that appeared in Mac OS X in 2003; it's that Mac OS X has features that Microsoft talked about in 2001.
Spotlight, in this view, is a rip-off of WinFS — even though WinFS didn't even actually make it into Vista.
Windows, icons, menus — these are just ideas, and no one deserves to own ideas.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's response to being outpaced by Apple is to rewrite history — claiming Apple copied features that Microsoft announced but never shipped, while making demonstrably false security claims that go unchallenged by credulous interviewers.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Rob Enderle's advice that IT departments should ban iPhones from corporate networks due to security concerns. He points out that Enderle knows nothing about the iPhone beyond its public spec sheet, yet makes sweeping security proclamations. Gruber highlights the logical contradiction in Enderle's argument: claiming the iPhone is insecure because it lacks third-party security software, while simultaneously noting it's a closed platform that doesn't allow third-party software at all. He notes that a closed platform is inherently more secure against third-party threats, while acknowledging he personally hopes Apple opens iPhone development.
key_points: Enderle's security warnings about the iPhone are based on zero insider knowledge beyond Apple's public specs
The argument contains a logical contradiction: the iPhone is supposedly insecure because it can't run third-party security software, but the threat model assumes third-party software vulnerabilities
A closed platform that prohibits all third-party software is actually a strong defense against software-based security threats
Enderle's invocation of Sarbanes-Oxley compliance violations for connecting an iPhone is absurd fearmongering
money_quotes: What exactly does Rob Enderle know about the iPhone other than the tech specs on Apple's web site? (Hint: Nothing.)
Who are the non-principal analysts at The Enderle Group?

Enderle and Khanna are arguing that the iPhone is insecure because it doesn't (at least yet) allow for third-party software, which means you can't install third-party software designed to let you securely install additional third-party software.
One would think that if the problem is 'insecure third-party software', then not allowing for any third-party software at all is a relatively secure solution to the problem.
Or perhaps the IT department could declare that these iPhones have 'cooties', and have the devices quarantined.
Pretty hard to run a Trojan horse if you can't run any third-party apps at all.
posts.key_insight: Enderle's argument that the iPhone is insecure because it's a closed platform actually proves the opposite — a device that can't run third-party software is inherently protected against third-party software threats.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Steve Jobs's 'Thoughts on Music' essay, arguing it simultaneously challenges the major record labels to drop DRM, rebuffs European governments demanding Apple 'open up' FairPlay, and delivers a strategic blow to Microsoft. He highlights Jobs's framing of three options — status quo, licensing FairPlay, or dropping DRM — as a rhetorical move that eliminates the middle option, leaving only the status quo or DRM-free music. Gruber notes the tension between Jobs's anti-DRM rhetoric and Apple's refusal to let indie labels sell DRM-free music on iTunes, calling it a test of sincerity. He also observes that the music industry's response reveals fundamental confusion, with the RIAA bizarrely celebrating an offer Jobs explicitly rejected, and Microsoft's Zune team cycling through the stages of grief in two paragraphs.
key_points: Jobs's essay is strategically designed to serve multiple purposes: challenging labels, answering European regulators, and undermining Microsoft's position
DRM and interoperability are mutually exclusive — 'open DRM' is a contradiction, and the only real path to interoperability is no DRM at all
Apple's sincerity on DRM-free music would be proven by allowing indie labels who already want it to sell unprotected tracks on iTunes
The music industry's insistence on DRM is hypocritical given that 90% of music is already sold DRM-free on CDs
Eliminating DRM would also kill subscription-based music services, which is a competitive side benefit for Apple
money_quotes: If it's a bluff, all it will take to call them on it is a simple two-character statement from just one of the four major labels: 'OK'.
If you really want interoperability, then what you want is no DRM, not 'open' DRM.
Translation: We are not going to license FairPlay to anyone else. The only options that are actually on the table are the status quo and dropping DRM.
Translation: PlaysForSure sucks.
News flash, Mr. Anonymous: CDs are digital, unprotected, and higher-quality than the compressed format on iTunes.
In other words, the music industry wants a magical DRM format that gives them — not Apple, not Microsoft — complete control over all digital music. And a unicorn and a rainbow.
Apple is not Microsoft; the only competitive advantage Apple needs is the quality of its products.
The only thing the Zune team is doing 'on the ground with the industry' is squirting each other.
posts.key_insight: Jobs's essay is a masterful strategic move that frames the DRM debate so that the only viable options are the status quo or DRM-free music, putting the onus squarely on the record labels while deflecting regulatory pressure from Apple.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles two prominent criticisms of Steve Jobs's 'Thoughts on Music' anti-DRM essay: that Apple is merely bandwagon-jumping, and that Jobs didn't mean what he wrote. He argues that Paul Thurrott's suggestion that Apple license Microsoft's Windows Media DRM reveals a broken assumption that Microsoft matters in the digital music debate, when in fact Microsoft is a 'paper tiger' whose own DRM standards don't even interoperate with each other. Gruber contends that Apple's strategy has always been about maximizing control while minimizing dependencies, not about lock-in. He then takes apart Paul Kedrosky's Wall Street Journal op-ed, arguing that DRM elimination would actually benefit the music industry since piracy is already rampant and DRM only punishes honest customers. The core argument is that Apple's dominance comes from product quality, not DRM lock-in, and Jobs's call to drop DRM is genuine self-interest, not posturing.
key_points: Microsoft is irrelevant to the DRM debate despite Thurrott's assumption — their music DRM has failed to become a standard, and their own Zune and PlaysForSure DRM systems don't even interoperate
Apple's corporate strategy is maximizing control and minimizing dependencies, which is fundamentally different from Microsoft's embrace-extend-extinguish lock-in approach
Jobs's anti-DRM position is genuine self-interest: Apple makes money selling iPods and competing on product quality, not from iTunes Store margins or DRM lock-in
DRM-free music would actually benefit the industry because piracy is already rampant via P2P networks, and DRM only deters honest customers from buying
The music industry's real problem is obsessing over pirates while taking paying customers for granted
money_quotes: It's one thing when a peace activist calls for an end to nuclear weapon testing; it's something else when the leader of a country that has nuclear weapons does so.
Microsoft is a paper tiger in this realm.
Too much Microsoft on the brain can lead you to view Apple — or any other company — through Redmond-tinted glasses.
Apple's defining corporate desires are maximizing their control while minimizing their dependencies.

Microsoft is so hell-bent on switching places with Apple — so that they control an overwhelming DRM lock-in advantage — that they're missing out on the fact that Apple is offering to relinquish its own (actual, existing) DRM lock-in advantage.
Microsoft is so DRM-happy that they don't even offer cross-support between their own DRM standards.
They obsess over pirates while taking their honest customers for granted.

The reason the music companies are unpopular is that their actions and stated policies make people unhappy. The reason Apple is popular is that its actions and stated policies make people happy. This is not complicated.
posts.key_insight: Apple's call to eliminate DRM is genuine corporate self-interest — Apple profits from hardware and user experience, not lock-in — while Microsoft and critics who assume otherwise reveal their own inability to imagine a business strategy that isn't built on controlling customers.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights a quote from Rands about SXSW being uniquely positioned as a conference where designers and developers coexist and interact. He uses this to promote an upcoming SXSW panel called 'The Design Aesthetic of the Indie Developer,' which Rands is moderating and Gruber is participating in alongside Nick Bradbury and Shaun Inman. Gruber notes that the best panel topics are ones you could discuss all day, and this qualifies. He also puts out a call for an indie Mac developer to join the panel.
key_points: SXSW uniquely bridges the designer-developer divide that most conferences maintain
Designers and developers share the distinction of being the people who actually build things in organizations
The best conference panel ideas are topics that could sustain all-day conversation
The panel brings together notable indie developers across platforms including Windows and Mac
money_quotes: SXSW is the only conference I know where designers and developers hang out.
Designers and developers have a basic conflict of interest. They both believe that they are the only parties on the organizational chart who build real stuff.
Actually, everyone on the organizational chart believes that, but the designers and the developers are the only ones who actually do build real things. SORRY SALES.
One of my rules of thumb for panel ideas is whether it sounds like a topic that could be talked about all day long. This is one of those ideas.
posts.key_insight: The intersection of design and development in indie software is a rich and underexplored topic, and SXSW is one of the rare venues where that conversation naturally happens.

posts.summary: Gruber translates Macrovision CEO Fred Amoroso's official response to Steve Jobs's 'Thoughts on Music' open letter, revealing the self-interested corporate motives behind each piece of PR language. The post systematically deconstructs Macrovision's defense of DRM by restating each paragraph in blunt, honest terms. Gruber exposes that Macrovision's arguments for DRM are fundamentally about preserving its own business model rather than serving consumers or content creators. The translation reveals the absurdity of claiming DRM increases consumer value, that interoperable DRM is achievable, and that content owners would abandon digital distribution without copy protection. The piece is a masterclass in cutting through corporate doublespeak to expose naked self-interest.
key_points: Macrovision's defense of DRM is entirely motivated by protecting its own business as a copy protection vendor, not by consumer or industry benefit
The concept of 'interoperable DRM' is presented as a pipe dream that gives people none of the actual benefits of DRM-free media while enriching middlemen
Macrovision's offer to 'help' Apple with FairPlay is really an insult, implying Apple can't handle its own technology
The claim that abandoning DRM would increase consumer costs inverts reality — DRM exists to charge people repeatedly for content they already own
Macrovision's argument that content owners would flee digital distribution without DRM ignores the massive market incentives to sell digitally
money_quotes: We've been helping and encouraging the entertainment industry to annoy its paying customers for more than 20 years.
Remember those squiggly lines when you tried copying a commercial VHS tape? You can thank us for that.
We recognize that if getting rid of DRM works for the music industry, it's going to open the eyes of executives in other fields, and it could unravel Macrovision's entire business.
Up is down. Black is white.
The solution is more DRM. DRM everywhere.
Abandoning DRM will prevent us from forcing our customers to keep paying us over and over again for the same movies and songs they've already paid for.
I am high as a kite.

Just because we have sold the entertainment industry on the pipe dream of 'interoperable DRM' that can't actually be implemented does not mean they should stop paying Macrovision in a futile attempt to make it happen.
posts.key_insight: Macrovision's defense of DRM is a transparent exercise in corporate self-preservation disguised as consumer advocacy, and translating PR-speak into plain English instantly exposes the absurdity.

posts.summary: Gruber curates a roundtable of perspectives on using dynamic scripting languages like Python, Ruby, and Lua for Mac OS X desktop application development. He highlights that Adobe Lightroom already uses Lua for its UI, and that Python-Cocoa bridges have production history dating back to the NeXT era. The discussion centers on whether dynamic languages will complement or replace Objective-C for Cocoa development. A key technical insight is that Ruby and Python succeed as Cocoa bridges because they are more dynamic than Objective-C, unlike Java which was less dynamic. The counterargument is that Objective-C 2.0's new features in Leopard may reduce the appeal of scripting languages. Gruber presents the debate without taking a strong side, letting the linked arguments speak for themselves.
key_points: Adobe Lightroom's UI is already written mostly in Lua, proving dynamic languages for desktop apps isn't theoretical but happening in production
Python-Cocoa bridging has deep roots going back to NeXT in the mid-90s, making it a mature rather than experimental approach
Ruby and Python succeed as Cocoa bridges because they are more dynamic than Objective-C, whereas Java's bridge failed because Java is less dynamic
Objective-C 2.0 in Leopard adds garbage collection, properties, and foreach loops that may reduce the appeal of switching to scripting languages
money_quotes: What makes these languages interesting isn't that they are interpreted, but that they're dynamic.
Ruby and Python are both more dynamic than Objective-C; Java is less dynamic than Objective-C.
Objective-C 2.0, which adds garbage collection and some useful new syntax like properties and foreach loops, obviates many of the things that might draw Cocoa developers to Ruby or Python.
The most common performance hit comes when crossing the bridge.
This transition isn't in the future; it's happening now.
posts.key_insight: Dynamic scripting languages are natural complements to Objective-C and Cocoa because they share its dynamism, unlike Java whose static nature doomed its Cocoa bridge to obsolescence.

posts.summary: Gruber revisits his earlier criticism of Steven Levy's Newsweek interview with Bill Gates, in which Gates made objectively false claims about Mac OS X security being exploited 'every single day.' After initially accepting Levy's defense that interview time is better spent drawing out interesting responses than debating technical points, Gruber reconsiders after reader feedback and concludes he let Levy off too easy. He argues that journalists have a responsibility to challenge objectively false claims, distinguishing between subjective opinions (like 'Vista is more secure') and demonstrably untrue statements that mislead readers who trust the publication's expertise.
key_points: Gates's claim that Mac OS X security is exploited 'every single day' was objectively false, not merely subjective or an exaggeration
Levy defended his silence by arguing interview time is better spent drawing out interesting responses than debating technical points
The distinction between subjective claims (which can stand unchallenged) and objectively false statements (which demand pushback) is the crux of journalistic responsibility
Most Newsweek readers likely assumed that if Gates's claim were false, Levy would have challenged it — making silence a form of implicit endorsement
Being a 'passionate partisan' doesn't grant someone a license to make unchallenged false claims in a major publication
money_quotes: What Gates claimed was not subjective.
But what Gates said about Mac OS X security was objectively false. It wasn't his opinion, and it wasn't an exaggeration of something that was more or less true. It was flat-out false.
I think most readers assume that if it were false, then Levy would have called him on it — and so conversely, Levy's silence means it must be true, or at least arguably true.
That Gates is a 'passionate partisan' doesn't grant him a license to say whatever he wants, truth be damned, about his competition.
A simple, 'I haven't heard of any such exploits — what's your source for that?' would have sufficed.
I get far more complimentary emails than critical ones, but the critical ones are always welcome and appreciated.
posts.key_insight: When an interview subject makes an objectively false claim, journalistic silence functions as implicit endorsement, and no amount of reader sophistication excuses the failure to ask a simple follow-up question.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Peter N. Lewis's argument that Apple cannot sell a mix of DRM and DRM-free music at the iTunes Store due to user confusion. While acknowledging the valid concern about maintaining Apple-style simplicity, Gruber argues it's doable — Apple could keep the same usage terms for all music while only differing on enforcement, and could badge DRM-protected tracks similarly to how 'Explicit' tracks are already marked. He concludes that after Steve Jobs published 'Thoughts on Music,' Apple would face a serious PR problem refusing any major label willing to go DRM-free.
key_points: Apple could maintain a single set of usage rules (five-computer limit) for all music regardless of DRM status — the difference would only be in enforcement
The iTunes Store already badges tracks with 'Explicit' and 'Clean' labels, proving that per-track differentiation is technically and design-wise feasible
The real challenge isn't technical but design-related: most users don't pay attention to badges or format details
After Jobs's 'Thoughts on Music' essay, Apple would face a PR impossibility in refusing a major label willing to drop DRM
money_quotes: If they started allowing individual record labels to sell DRM-free music, they could still sell everything under the same terms: that you're allowed to play downloads on up to five computers. It's just that with the DRM-free music, there'd be nothing in place to enforce the policy.
Imperfect, but it's certainly not as confusing as multiple sets of rules for DRM-protected music.
That AAC isn't compatible with many third-party players isn't Apple's problem.
Selling a mix of DRM-laden and DRM-free music wouldn't pose any serious technical hurdles for Apple, but it would pose some design challenges.

If one of the major music companies were to step up and say, OK Apple, go ahead and sell our songs without DRM, I think it'd be hard, if not downright impossible, PR-wise, for Apple to say they won't allow it until the other companies agree, too.
Even with a single consistent set of rules, DRM is complicated.
posts.key_insight: The obstacles to Apple selling mixed DRM and DRM-free music are design challenges rather than technical ones, and after Jobs's public stance against DRM, Apple couldn't credibly refuse a willing label.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Jason Kottke's analysis showing very low female representation among speakers at tech/web conferences. Rather than attributing the disparity to conference organizer bias, Gruber argues the speaker ratios likely reflect the broader gender imbalance in the tech/design/web field itself. He draws a comparison to law and medicine, where removing barriers led to near-parity, but notes that computer science has seen women's participation actually decline despite open opportunity. He concludes the real question isn't about conference lineups but about why fewer women are drawn to web and tech fields in the first place.
key_points: Kottke's data showed most tech/web conferences had few or no women speakers, but Gruber questions what baseline these numbers should be compared against
The speaker gender ratio likely mirrors the gender ratio of attendees, developers, and bloggers in the field, suggesting the issue isn't organizer bias
Law and medicine achieved near-parity once barriers were removed, but computer science saw women's participation actually decline from 37% to 28% between 1985 and 2001
Gruber reframes the question from 'why aren't more women speaking at conferences' to 'why aren't more women interested in web nerdery'
He attributes the gap to lack of interest rather than lack of opportunity or aptitude, distinguishing tech from fields where explicit barriers existed
money_quotes: These numbers are indeed striking, but striking compared to what? The 50-50 gender split of the general population?

An overwhelming percentage of the speakers at these conferences are men might not be a sign of gender bias on the part of the conference organizers, but rather a reflection of the fact that an overwhelming percentage of the people in the tech/design/web field are men.

It seems entirely possible that most of these conference organizers are making an effort at gender diversity, and that the discrepency is a reflection of the disproportionate number of men who are interested and involved in this field.
It's not because of a lack of opportunity or aptitude; it's a lack of interest.
So the issue here isn't why there aren't more women speaking at web conferences, but why there aren't more women interested in web nerdery.
posts.key_insight: The gender imbalance among tech conference speakers likely reflects the broader field's demographics rather than organizer bias, and the deeper question is why fewer women enter the field despite open opportunity.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Dori Smith's argument that web conference organizers show gender bias by failing to invite women JavaScript experts. Smith pointed to 10 women among Amazon's bestselling JavaScript book authors as evidence of an available talent pool being ignored. Gruber does the math more rigorously, finding that women constitute 8.5% of the 106 unique authors on Amazon's JavaScript bestseller list. He then notes that women made up 16.5% of speakers at recent web conferences — nearly double the rate found among JavaScript book authors. He concludes that the numbers don't support the claim of gender bias by conference organizers.
key_points: Dori Smith identified 10 women among Amazon's top 100 JavaScript books as evidence conference organizers were ignoring qualified women
Gruber's more thorough analysis found 9 women out of 106 unique authors, or 8.5% of bestselling JavaScript book authors
Women comprised 16.5% of speakers at recent web conferences (excluding BlogHer and SXSW), nearly double the book author rate
The data, when examined proportionally rather than in absolute numbers, undermines rather than supports the claim of conference organizer bias
money_quotes: OK, I'll bite.
After removing non-persons (e.g., five of the books were authored by Adobe Systems Inc.) and duplicates (Danny Goodman was listed six times), we're left with 106 unique authors.
I count nine women (the same nine listed by Dori Smith), which works out to 8.5 percent.
There were 73 women out of a total of 441 speakers, or 16.5 percent — which is almost double the rate of women authors in Amazon's list of bestselling JavaScript books.
I'm not saying these numbers prove anything, but I am saying they certainly do not prove any sort of gender bias on the part of the conference organizers.
posts.key_insight: When you actually do the proportional math on Smith's own chosen metric, women are represented at web conferences at nearly double the rate they appear among bestselling JavaScript book authors, undermining rather than supporting the claim of organizer bias.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Joe Trotter's observation that classy blogs tend to run on Movable Type while WordPress blogs look generic. Gruber argues this conflates software with templates — what makes a site distinctive is original design, not the CMS behind it. He points to Zeldman and Matt Mullenweg as examples of WordPress sites with strong, unique identities. He then details his own design process for Daring Fireball, emphasizing that he started from a blank slate rather than default templates, carefully designing everything from layout to URL structure. The core argument is that default templates are fine for people who don't want to design, but terrible starting points for establishing a unique brand. True design means thinking about every detail, including things most people overlook like URL structure.
key_points: The perceived quality gap between Movable Type and WordPress sites is about original design versus default templates, not the underlying software
Gruber designed every aspect of Daring Fireball from scratch — visual design, markup, and URL structure — starting with blank files rather than default templates
URLs are a design element deserving careful thought: DF's /year/month/slug pattern was deliberately chosen for clarity, context, and minimal cruft
Starting from nothing forces you to think about everything, which is why sites built on default templates rarely achieve a distinctive identity
The CMS powering a site should be as invisible to readers as whether a magazine was laid out in QuarkXPress or InDesign
money_quotes: What makes Design Observer so good isn't that it feels Movable Type-y, but that it feels Design Observer-y.
It's just HTML and CSS; the software that generates it should be no more noticeable or relevant to you, the reader, than is whether a magazine was laid out using QuarkXPress or InDesign.
I did not design Daring Fireball by starting with one of Movable Type's default templates. I started the visual design with a blank sheet of paper, and then moved on to an empty Photoshop file.
Yes, even URLs are designed.
Designing is thinking, and if you care about design, every detail — the layout, the colors, the markup, the URL structure — deserves to be thought about.
Default templates are terrific for people who can't or don't want to design their own — but they're terrible starting points for anyone attempting to establish their own unique brand.
If you start with nothing, you're forced to think about everything.
It took me six weeks to choose the exact shade of Daring Fireball's background color, #4a525a.
posts.key_insight: A distinctive website comes from original design thinking applied to every detail — layout, markup, URLs — not from the choice of CMS, and starting from a blank slate forces the intentionality that default templates preclude.

posts.summary: Gruber revisits the ongoing MacBook Wi-Fi security exploit controversy involving researchers David Maynor and Jon Ellch, who demonstrated a purported wireless hijacking at Black Hat in August 2006. More than six months later, Maynor broke his silence but only demonstrated a denial-of-service crash against Mac OS X 10.4.6, not the full remote code execution hijack shown in the original video. Gruber methodically catalogs the still-unanswered questions: the unnamed third-party Wi-Fi card, the suspicious MAC address evidence, and why the exploit targets an OS version predating the original announcement. He argues that a kernel panic is far removed from the claimed machine hijacking and that Apple's boilerplate 'may cause arbitrary code execution' language proves nothing. Gruber concludes that while Maynor likely did discover a legitimate DoS vulnerability, there remains no evidence it can be expanded into a reliable hijack exploit.
key_points: Maynor's new demonstration only showed a kernel panic (DoS) on Mac OS X 10.4.6, not the root-privilege hijacking from the original Black Hat video
Jim Thompson's analysis of the video revealed the exploited network interface had an Apple MAC address, suggesting the unnamed third-party USB card may have been a distraction
The exploit was demonstrated against 10.4.6 despite 10.4.7 being released a month before the original announcement, raising questions about whether Apple's September patches were even related
Apple's 'may cause arbitrary code execution' language is boilerplate used for virtually all buffer overflow advisories and doesn't confirm a working hijack exists
Maynor's former employer SecureWorks apparently prevented him from presenting evidence to defend his claims, muddying the entire saga
money_quotes: Most of the time, when I report about something, the job is organizing, ordering, and explaining the facts. With the Maynor/Ellch/SecureWorks MacBook Wi-Fi security saga, however, it's always been about missing facts.
Many people remain under the impression that the controversy is about whether they used a third-party card or not, a fact which was never actually in dispute.

There is a real controversy that is converse of the false one — that their video purports to show an exploit against some still-unnamed third-party card but in fact shows a MacBook that is connected to the network using its built-in AirPort card.
That's bad, but it's far removed from the 'take over the machine' hijacking shown in their video.
Apple uses this language for just about any buffer overflow bug, however.
I say: Show me.
But I don't think this exploit can necessarily be expanded into a reliable hijack. We'll know more if and when Maynor releases code for his exploit, which he's promised to do.
posts.key_insight: A demonstrated kernel panic is a legitimate but far lesser finding than the sensational machine-hijacking claim, and without released exploit code or reproducible evidence, extraordinary security claims remain unproven.

posts.summary: Gruber reflects on SXSW Interactive 2007, arguing that the conference's unique value lies in its social and artistic atmosphere rather than its official programming. He notes a strong consensus that the 2007 sessions were weak, yet the conference was still considered worth attending — a paradox unique to SXSWi. He critiques the heavy reliance on panel discussions over lectures, arguing panels lack sustained narrative and convey far less information. He frames SXSWi as an art-focused conference for people whose work straddles art and science, and concludes that this artistic emphasis demands more structure and preparation from speakers, not less.
key_points: SXSW Interactive's amorphous name fits its nature: unlike Music or Film, it tracks whatever is happening now in the industry
SXSWi is unique among conferences in that its social value exceeds its programming value, and attendees accept this tradeoff
The 2007 sessions drew a collective 'Meh' yet the conference was still widely considered worth attending
Panels are inherently limited compared to lectures — they're entertaining but lack sustained narrative and convey far less information
SXSWi focuses on the art side of web work, which is what makes it distinctive but also puts more pressure on speakers to bring structure
money_quotes: An amorphous name for an amorphous concept.
Many clichés are true, and at SXSWi that applies to the one about the most interesting stuff happening in the hallways, restaurants, and bars.
At most conferences, the deal is that the content is great and the socializing is good. At SXSWi, the content is good, but the socializing is great.
I feel like I conveyed 50 times more information in my hour-long lecture at C4 in October than I did as one of three panelists in an hour-long session at SXSWi this year.
Panels are dessert, lectures are meals. The mix at SXSWi this year was far too sugary.
SXSWi, by nature of its audience, is chock full of interesting conversations in the hallways, at restaurant tables, and around the late-night pitchers of beer. We don't need them on stage, too.
posts.key_insight: SXSW Interactive's real value is its social and artistic atmosphere, but that doesn't excuse weak programming — the art side of web work demands more structure from speakers, not less.

posts.summary: Gruber shares a brief, humorous anecdote about his young son imitating a scene from Sesame Street where Oscar the Grouch's car won't start. The boy is visibly angry and muttering about the broken car. When Gruber comments that he looks upset, the boy corrects him with a word he clearly didn't learn from children's television. The post is a classic parenting vignette about kids absorbing language from their surroundings.
key_points: Children absorb and repeat adult language with perfect contextual usage
The humor comes from the contrast between innocent Sesame Street imitation and decidedly non-Sesame Street vocabulary
The title 'Like a Sponge' captures how children soak up everything around them, including words parents wish they wouldn't
money_quotes: "No, I'm *pissed*," he says.
Somehow, I doubt he learned that word from Oscar.
posts.key_insight: Kids absorb everything — including the words you wish they wouldn't — and deploy them with impeccable timing.

posts.summary: Gruber presents a practical developer tool he built: a Perl script that converts readable JavaScript source code into bookmarklet-ready URLs. The post explains the core problem — that bookmarklets require JavaScript to be crammed into URL-encoded single-line format, making development painful. His solution preserves the readable source code while generating the encoded bookmarklet as a comment at the top of the file and copying it to the clipboard. The script handles stripping comments, collapsing whitespace, and escaping special characters. It's designed to work as a BBEdit text filter but is editor-agnostic.
key_points: Bookmarklet development is irritating because JavaScript must be URL-encoded into a single line, making the code unreadable and hard to edit
The Perl script solves this by maintaining readable source code while generating the bookmarklet URL as a first-line comment, replaceable on each run
The script strips comments, collapses whitespace, removes newlines, and escapes special characters and unicode into a valid javascript: URL
The generated bookmarklet is also copied to the clipboard via pbcopy for immediate pasting into a browser's bookmark editor
Gruber deliberately keeps URL escaping minimal for readability, only escaping what's practically necessary rather than being maximally conservative
money_quotes: Developing or modifying bookmarklets can be irritating, to say the least, because of this requirement that the JavaScript code be in the form of a URL.
So the problem developing bookmarklets is this: You want to write and edit normal JavaScript code, but you need to publish hard-to-read URLs.
Personally, I prefer to keep the bookmarklet URL itself as readable as possible.
If it eats your source code, Undo is your friend.
posts.key_insight: A simple build tool that converts readable JavaScript into bookmarklet URLs solves the fundamental tension between writing maintainable code and publishing browser-compatible bookmarklets.

posts.summary: Gruber exposes LogoMaid, a cheap off-the-shelf logo shop, for selling a knock-off of Dan Cederholm's SimpleBits logo. Rather than quietly removing the stolen design, LogoMaid's owner Paul Viluda made things worse by accusing Cederholm of being the thief and creating sock puppet Flickr accounts to defend himself. Gruber uses his platform's influence to shine a light on the theft, noting that IP thieves operate like vermin who scatter when exposed.
key_points: LogoMaid sold a barely-disguised copy of Dan Cederholm's SimpleBits logo for $199
Instead of removing the offending logo, LogoMaid's owner accused Cederholm of theft and threatened a lawsuit — a textbook case of doubling down
Viluda created multiple sock puppet Flickr accounts to defend LogoMaid in the comment thread
Gruber deliberately leverages Daring Fireball's high PageRank to punish LogoMaid with unwanted exposure
money_quotes: They took his logo and added a fake mustache, as it were.
Intellectual property thieves have much in common with other vermin, such as cockroaches and rodents: they don't like the light.
Expose them and they hide.
The last thing a site like LogoMaid wants is a high-PageRank site — like, oh, say, this one — spreading the word that they peddle ripped-off logos.
He responded by accusing Cederholm of ripping his logo off from LogoMaid, and, laughably, threatening 'a lawsuit against SimpleBits.'
posts.key_insight: When caught stealing creative work, the worst response is to double down and threaten the victim — because sunlight is the best disinfectant for IP theft.

posts.summary: Gruber builds on Tantek Çelik's analysis of cognitive load in user interfaces, proposing a refined counting methodology. While Çelik counts individual actions like clicks and keystrokes, Gruber argues that what really matters is the number of distinct UI elements a user must 'deal with.' He illustrates this by comparing instant messaging to email, then applies it devastatingly to iCal's event entry (10 elements) versus Backpack's (2 elements). The post argues that raw keystroke counts are misleading — iCal and Backpack differ by only 5 keystrokes, yet the experience is dramatically different. Gruber concludes that counting UI elements you must deal with better captures the actual felt quality of an interface.
key_points: Cognitive load in UI comes more from the number of distinct elements (nouns) you must deal with than from the number of actions (verbs) you perform
Even skipping past an irrelevant field like CC or Subject carries some cognitive load — it's not nothing, even if it's small
Raw keystroke and click counts are misleading metrics for UI quality; iCal and Backpack differ by only 5 keystrokes but feel worlds apart
The right case to optimize for is the experienced repeat user, not the first-time user who benefits from explicit labeled fields
The 'deal with it' counting method — enumerating distinct UI elements requiring attention — better reflects the actual appeal of a design
money_quotes: Çelik's technique is to count the actions, or, if you will, the verbs. But I suspect it's the nouns that add the most to the cognitive load.
My counting methodology can be reduced to the form 'Deal with some specific user interface element'.

This is not about 'easy' vs. 'hard', which are clumsy, imprecise words for describing a user interface. Easy what? Easy to learn? Easy to understand? Easy to remember how to use? Easy meaning 'simple'?

There's nothing complex or confusing about iCal's event entry UI. And with specific fields for each item of data, it is more obvious than Backpack's — but only for a first-time user, which is the wrong case to optimize for.
It's as palpable as the difference between going uphill and downhill.
Counting the user interface elements you have to deal with produces a comparison that reflects the actual appeal of the two designs.
Imagine if there were five CC fields to skip in every message window. Or 100. It's a little tiny thing, but it is not nothing.
posts.key_insight: The cognitive load of a UI is best measured not by counting keystrokes or actions, but by counting the number of distinct interface elements a user must mentally deal with.

posts.summary: Gruber clarifies three points from his earlier 'Deal With It' essay about counting interface elements. His central clarification is that the theory is about what feels efficient, not what is objectively most efficient — and that perceived efficiency matters more because it drives user behavior. He explicitly notes the essay was not arguing IM is better than email or that Backpack is better than iCal, but rather explaining why simpler-feeling interfaces are preferred even when they aren't objectively superior.
key_points: Perceived efficiency matters more than measured efficiency because human behavior is guided by how things feel, not how they are
Users will prefer the interface that feels more efficient even when rigorous testing proves the alternative is objectively faster
The original essay was about explaining the psychology of interface preference, not making absolute claims about which tools are better
Gruber personally prefers email over IM but acknowledges IM can feel like the path of least resistance
money_quotes: It is about establishing what *feels* like the most convenient interface for a task. There's a big difference.
Human nature is guided more by how things feel than how they are.
It might be more important for an interface to *feel* efficient than to *be* efficient, because that's what drives human behavior.
Guess which interface the users will prefer?
'Deal With It' was my attempt to explain why sending an IM can *feel* like the quickest, easiest way to send someone a message, even if it actually isn't.
posts.key_insight: An interface that feels efficient will win over one that is efficient, because human behavior is driven by perception, not measurement.

posts.summary: Gruber announces the 2007 Daring Fireball membership drive as he approaches the one-year anniversary of writing the site full-time. He reflects on the positive reader response to his increased output since going professional, noting that memberships are the site's primary revenue source. The post includes an extensive prize drawing featuring over 120 prizes worth $6,300 from prominent Mac developers and design companies. He frames the membership model as a mutual exchange — readers get better content, and their support makes the full-time work possible.
key_points: Daring Fireball's membership system is the primary revenue source that enables Gruber to write full-time
Readers noticed a tangible improvement in output quality and consistency after Gruber went full-time in April 2006
Over 120 prizes worth $6,300 from prominent Mac developers serve as incentive for the membership drive
The prize list reads as a who's-who of mid-2000s indie Mac software: Panic, Bare Bones, Omni Group, Rogue Amoeba, and others
money_quotes: Starting today, we get to see.
The membership system is the primary source of revenue for Daring Fireball. This web site is better because of you.
Many of you have written to say that the difference since I've gone full-time has been both noticeable and welcome: more articles along with a more consistent and steady pace to the Linked List.
Last year's support was tremendous, and it's the reason I'm still here working on DF full-time today.
posts.key_insight: Gruber's membership drive demonstrates the viability of reader-supported independent publishing, with the Mac developer community rallying behind the model by contributing over $6,300 in prizes.

posts.summary: Gruber shares a lightweight AppleScript he wrote to create new text files directly from the Finder's contextual menu. Inspired by the NuFile contextual menu plugin highlighted on TUAW, he wanted a simpler solution focused solely on text files. The script uses Brent Simmons's free Big Cat Scripts Plugin to add a contextual menu item that prompts for a filename, creates the file, and opens it in your preferred text editor. He provides instructions for adapting the script to work with BBEdit, TextWrangler, TextMate, or SubEthaEdit.
key_points: Gruber built a minimal AppleScript alternative to the NuFile plugin, focused only on creating text files
The script leverages Brent Simmons's free Big Cat Scripts Plugin for contextual menu integration
It includes basic safety checks like verifying the file doesn't already exist before creating it
The script is easily customizable for different text editors by swapping the command-line tool name
money_quotes: I thought that sounded pretty cool, but I'd only ever use it for creating text files.
The `do shell script` line at the end of the script assumes that your favorite text editor has a command-line tool, and that the tool is available in your shell's `PATH`.
posts.key_insight: A simple AppleScript paired with a free contextual menu plugin can fill a small but real gap in the Finder's workflow for text-file-centric users.

posts.summary: Gruber provides a status update on Daring Fireball's members-only XML feed system, addressing a MySQL outage that morning, explaining how the tiered feed system works, noting Google Reader's incompatibility with HTTP authentication, and describing the in-feed expiration reminder system. He explains why he switched from URL-based secret keys to real HTTP authentication after non-members were accessing feeds via leaked URLs. The post serves as both a FAQ for confused new readers and a transparent look at the mechanics of running an independent subscription-based site.
key_points: 80-85 percent of active Daring Fireball members subscribe to at least one members-only feed, making it the site's most popular feature
The free feed contains only article headlines and excerpts, while members get full articles, Linked List items, or a combination feed with everything
Google Reader's lack of HTTP authentication support is the biggest limitation of the feed system, with no workaround available
Gruber switched from URL-based secret keys to real HTTP authentication because web-based readers were making feed URLs public, letting non-members outnumber members
Expiration reminders are delivered directly in the feed itself rather than via email, which Gruber considers clever but acknowledges startles some readers
money_quotes: When I look at your web site, I see a bunch of new links, but your RSS feed hasn't updated in days.
There were more non-members accessing these feeds via "found" URLs than there were members, so I switched to using real authentication.
Many web-based feed readers default to making the feed URLs you're subscribed to public.

You would never want to do this with a feed that contained genuinely private or sensitive information, but it was just fine for Daring Fireball, where all the content is freely available for everyone on the web site.
I think this is pretty clever, if I do say so — and far less intrusive than sending reminders via email.
My apologies for any skipped heartbeats.
posts.key_insight: Running an independent subscription site requires constant pragmatic tradeoffs between user convenience and protecting the value of membership, as Gruber discovered when the openness of URL-based feed keys was exploited by non-members.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a new Daring Fireball t-shirt design by illustrator Kevin Cornell and a new partnership with Oddica for printing and shipping fulfillment. The partnership aims to fix chronic delays in shirt delivery caused by Gruber's own poor inventory management. He acknowledges an embarrassingly large backlog of pending orders and support emails, promising all existing orders will ship before new ones. Both the new 'Daredevil Helmet' design and the classic DF logo shirt will be printed on American Apparel blanks and begin shipping in mid-April.
key_points: New 'Daredevil Helmet' t-shirt design by illustrator Kevin Cornell, who will share in profits
Outsourcing fulfillment to Oddica to replace Gruber's dysfunctional self-managed shipping process
Honest admission that previous order fulfillment took weeks or months due to his own mistakes
All pending backlogged orders promised to ship before new orders begin in mid-April
money_quotes: In a certain sense, I've wanted this t-shirt since I was about four years old.

This should take a process that previously took weeks — and in some cases, due to my own mistakes and poor inventory management, months — and turn it into a process that takes just a few days to get new shirts into your hands.
I have an embarrassingly large queue of support email regarding delayed and lost orders.
Expect this site to be relatively quiet for the next day or two as I work through these piles.
posts.key_insight: Gruber's willingness to publicly own his fulfillment failures and outsource to specialists reflects the same pragmatism he champions in software — recognize what you're bad at and delegate it.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to accusations of hypocrisy for defending Apple's 'Hello' iPhone commercial against charges it ripped off Christian Marclay's 1995 film Telephones, while simultaneously criticizing LogoMaid for copying SimpleBits' logo. He argues that rip-offs exist on a continuum rather than as a binary judgment, and draws a distinction between copying an idea versus copying a specific implementation. He classifies several Apple rip-off accusations (Dashboard vs. Konfabulator, iPod vs. Lugz, Intel commercial vs. Postal Service video) differently based on this framework. Ultimately, he concedes that even if Apple's commercial isn't technically a rip-off, a company that positions itself as a champion of creativity should hold itself to a higher standard.
key_points: Rip-offs exist on a continuum from wholly original to blatant copy, not as a binary yes/no judgment
The key distinction is whether work derives from the idea of an original or from the original work itself
Apple is hypocritical for mocking Microsoft's copying while appropriating others' ideas, though Vista's features are arguably original implementations of Mac OS X concepts
A company that presents itself as a champion of the creative class should hold its advertising to a higher originality standard than merely 'not a rip-off'
The thought experiment of Jonathan Ive designing for Sony illustrates the murky question of who owns a creative sensibility
money_quotes: Drawing the line between what's a rip-off and what's original, but inspired by something else, can be highly subjective.
To me, it's not a binary decision, yes or no, but instead a continuum, with wholly original works on one side and blatant bit-for-bit copies on the other.
My basic rule of thumb is to gauge whether the work in question is derived from the idea of the original work, or derived from the original work itself.
What if Sony hired Jonathan Ive and he designed for them an iPod-esque Walkman? Is it iPod-ishness or Ive-ness?

It's true that most of the new features in Vista are similar to long-standing features in Mac OS X, but none of them are rip-offs. They strike me as falling under the category of original implementations of the same idea. Lack of innovation, yes, but not theft.
"Not a rip-off" isn't good enough for a company that presents itself as a champion of the creative class.
Rip-off or not, if Marclay didn't want Apple to use the idea, they shouldn't have used it. Not because it was wrong, but because Apple can do better.
posts.key_insight: The distinction between copying an idea and copying its execution is the crux of creative originality, but a company that brands itself as a creative champion should aspire to more than just clearing the 'not a rip-off' bar.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses a common question from recent Daring Fireball members who signed up before the new Kevin Cornell-designed t-shirts were announced. He offers a simple upgrade path: pay the difference via PayPal and email with size and shipping details. The offer is limited to the membership drive period ending April 16. It's a brief, practical FAQ-style post handling membership logistics.
key_points: Members who signed up before the t-shirt announcement can upgrade by paying the price difference
The upgrade cost is $13 for US/Canada and $18 internationally
The offer expires at the end of the membership drive on April 16
money_quotes: Just to answer what is becoming a very common FAQ.
posts.key_insight: Gruber handles the logistics of his independent membership model with straightforward, reader-friendly customer service.

posts.summary: Gruber reacts to the announcement that EMI will sell DRM-free music through iTunes at higher quality (256kbps AAC) for $1.29 per track, with full albums DRM-free at no price increase. He sees this as vindication of Steve Jobs's 'Thoughts on Music' essay, which critics dismissed as posturing. He notes the clever compromise on pricing and speculates that if EMI's sales increase, other major labels clinging to DRM will face pressure to follow suit.
key_points: Apple accepted a $1.29 price point for singles in exchange for two major improvements: double the bitrate and no DRM, a pragmatic compromise from a company that long resisted price increases.
The EMI deal validates Jobs's 'Thoughts on Music' essay, proving Apple was genuinely willing to sell DRM-free music rather than using the essay as PR cover.
Apple committed to offering the same DRM-free deal to all major and independent labels, not just EMI.
The real test will be whether EMI's sales increase, which would undermine the other major labels' insistence that more DRM is the answer to declining revenue.
money_quotes: I'd buy that for thirty cents.
So much for accusations that Jobs was full of shit with his 'Thoughts on Music' essay.
Clearly, Apple *is* willing to embrace DRM-free music sales, and they're not going to wait for all of the major labels to agree before going forward with it.
Their worldview is based around the idea that they need *more* DRM, not less, and that DRM-free music sharing has been the source of their recent woes.
Cold hard cash might snap them out of it.
posts.key_insight: The EMI-Apple DRM-free deal proved Jobs's anti-DRM essay was genuine strategy, not posturing, and set up a market test that could force the entire music industry to abandon copy protection.

posts.summary: Gruber investigates what Google Desktop for Mac installs on a user's system, since Google's custom installer (Google Updater) lacks any transparency about what files it places where. He methodically catalogs every file installed by comparing before-and-after snapshots of a test machine. The findings reveal several concerning practices: an input manager hack injected into every Cocoa app, files installed in the /System/Library hierarchy (a major no-no for third-party software), a dynamically loaded kernel extension, and about 45 MB of background processes. While the uninstaller works cleanly, the lack of upfront disclosure about these deep system modifications is the core issue.
key_points: Google Desktop uses a custom installer (Google Updater) that provides no way to see what files will be installed or where, unlike Apple's standard installer
Google Desktop installs an input manager hack in the local Library that injects code into every Cocoa application for all users on the machine
Files are installed into /System/Library, which is considered off-limits for third-party software, and a kernel extension is loaded dynamically from a non-standard location
The software runs five background processes consuming about 45 MB of real memory
The uninstaller works properly, removing all significant software including the input manager hacks
money_quotes: I won't install software like this on my main machine without knowing everything that's going to be installed.
Google Desktop installs an input manager hack in the local domain Library folder. That means it is installed for all users on the machine, and injects code into every Cocoa application you launch.
Google Updater does not give you the option to install such files in your per-user Library folder.
My understanding is that it's a major no-no for third-party software to install anything in the /System/Library/ hierarchy other than kernel extensions.

It's a slick, easy-to-use piece of software, but alas, it lacks anything like the aforementioned Show Files feature of Apple's installer. There's no way to tell what files it's going to install, or where.
Mac software is usually distributed in a way that encourages simple drag-and-drop installation.
posts.key_insight: Google Desktop's slick installer conceals deeply invasive system modifications — input manager hacks, kernel extensions, and files in /System/Library — that users deserve to know about before installation.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that the handwringing over Apple dropping 'Computer' from its corporate name is misguided. Rather than abandoning computers for consumer electronics, Apple's advantage is that the entire consumer electronics industry is becoming computers — and Apple makes better computers, OS, and software than anyone. He uses Apple TV as proof: under the hood it's a Mac running a modified Mac OS X, offering both a simple mass-market interface and deep hackability for nerds. The device exemplifies Apple's enduring strategy of picking important features, implementing them well, and presenting them simply.
key_points: The consumer electronics industry is shifting toward making computers, which plays to Apple's core strengths in hardware, OS, and software design
Apple TV proves the point: it runs a modified Mac OS X under the hood, making it fundamentally a computer disguised as a consumer device
Apple TV strikes a balance between simplicity for regular users and deep hackability for nerds, contradicting the 'too closed' criticism
Apple is arguably more of a computer company now than 10 years ago, when pundits called for them to exit hardware entirely
The iPod strategy — pick important features, implement them well, present them obviously — extends naturally to Apple TV
money_quotes: The entire consumer electronics industry is shifting toward making computers; Apple's advantage is that they design better computers, have a better OS, and write better software than any of their competitors.
They're arguably more of a computer company now than they were 10 years ago.
For most of the 1990s, there were dozens of jackass columnists and 'industry analysts' calling for Apple to do just that.
Great software, well-integrated with cleverly-designed hardware — which pretty much describes every successful product Apple has shipped in the last 30 years.
