Pick the important features, implement them really well, and present them in an obvious UI.
Apple's stance on Apple TV hacking is clearly neutral, not antagonistic.
Regular people never have to get their hands dirty with complex nerdery, but those of us who want to easily can.
posts.key_insight: Apple dropping 'Computer' from its name isn't a retreat from computers — it's recognition that all consumer electronics are becoming computers, which is exactly where Apple's strengths lie.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles the persistent myth that AAC is a proprietary Apple format used for lock-in, arguing this misconception stems from critics projecting Microsoft's playbook onto Apple. He details how AAC is an ISO standard developed by Dolby and licensed through Via, with costs comparable to MP3 and no distribution royalties. The piece was prompted by Apple's announcement of DRM-free music sales with EMI, which should have ended lock-in arguments but instead shifted them to anti-AAC propaganda. Gruber uses thought experiments to contrast how Microsoft would have behaved in Apple's position, and draws an analogy between Apple's adoption of AAC and the iMac's pioneering use of USB — both cases where Apple pushed an industry standard forward by committing to it fully.
key_points: AAC is an ISO standard invented by Dolby engineers and licensed through Via, not an Apple-proprietary format — it's supported by Zune, PlayStation, Xbox, Nokia, Sony Walkman, and nearly every serious iPod competitor
Critics project Microsoft's well-documented proprietary lock-in strategy onto Apple, but Apple's behavior with DRM-free music proves the opposite
AAC licensing costs are comparable to MP3 (and actually cheaper per unit at scale), while offering better audio quality and no distribution royalties
WMA is genuinely cheaper to license but is controlled entirely by Microsoft, creating real lock-in risk — the opposite of what critics claim about AAC
Apple's adoption of AAC parallels the iMac's USB adoption: committing to an underused industry standard and catalyzing widespread adoption
money_quotes: Apple is not the 'Microsoft of digital music', and everyone ought to stop trying to view their actions as though they were.
Microsoft would have told EMI to stick their DRM-free tracks up their ass.
Can you imagine Microsoft volunteering to switch from DRM-protected songs to an unprotected industry standard file format? Me neither.
Keep your eye out for anti-AAC propaganda from those pushing an anti-iTunes or anti-Apple agenda.
In for a penny, in for a pound: once you license WMA audio, you're also on the hook to Microsoft for licensing fees for Windows Media DRM and Windows Media Video.
FairPlay DRM is an Apple format; regular AAC is not.
Default settings are the only settings for most users in a mass-market app like iTunes.
posts.key_insight: AAC is an open industry standard comparable to MP3, and critics who call it Apple's proprietary format are projecting Microsoft's lock-in playbook onto a company that has demonstrably rejected that strategy.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on reader feedback about audio codec formats, particularly AAC, MP3, and Ogg Vorbis. He notes that AAC support is still not universal among non-iPod players but predicts widespread adoption within years. The core argument centers on why 'free' codecs like Ogg Vorbis aren't truly free for companies like Apple—patent litigation risk makes them a liability despite their open-source intentions. He extends this patent-risk argument to explain Apple Lossless vs. FLAC. He also surfaces the interesting observation that many people mistakenly think AAC is an Apple format because of the acronym.
key_points: AAC is not yet universally supported by non-iPod players, but widespread adoption seems inevitable within a few years
MP3's patent situation is murkier than commonly assumed, with multiple companies claiming rights beyond Thomson
Ogg Vorbis's 'free' status is untested legally—no deep-pocketed company has risked using it in a mainstream product to find out if patent holders would sue
Apple likely created Apple Lossless instead of using FLAC not for lock-in but because FLAC's patent defensibility was uncertain
The 'AAC' acronym itself contributes to the misconception that it's an Apple-proprietary format
money_quotes: Do software patents suck? Yes. Is it possible that Ogg Vorbis does not actually infringe on anyone's patent, but that some patent holder could sue and win even though they shouldn't? Yes.
Ogg Vorbis is intended to be free, and it would be great if it were free, but no one with deep pockets has yet tested the water to see whether it really is.
You can argue about how big a risk it would be for Apple to use a free format like Ogg Vorbis, but you can't argue that it would be risk-free.

The most likely explanation for why Apple chose to create their own lossless format rather than use FLAC has nothing to do with technical quality or a lock-in strategy, but rather that they didn't feel FLAC was defensible patent-wise.
Many people assume that one of the A's in 'AAC' stands for 'Apple'.
posts.key_insight: Open-source audio codecs like Ogg Vorbis may be intended as free alternatives, but untested patent exposure makes them a real business risk that explains Apple's codec choices better than any lock-in conspiracy.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a limited edition Daring Fireball t-shirt produced in collaboration with Coudal Partners' Swap Meat project. He jokes that Daring Fireball began as a t-shirt design project in 1979 when he was six years old, with the website coming 23 years later. The run is limited to 75 shirts in three sizes, each including a one-year DF membership.
key_points: Limited run of 75 Daring Fireball t-shirts produced through Coudal Partners' Swap Meat
Each shirt includes a one-year Daring Fireball membership or extension
Gruber frames the announcement with self-deprecating humor about the site's origins
money_quotes: All great projects begin the same way: by designing a t-shirt.
So it was with Daring Fireball, which I started work on in 1979 as a six-year-old.
posts.key_insight: Even a brief merchandise announcement becomes an occasion for Gruber's signature deadpan wit.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Daniel Jalkut's criticism of Apple's explanation that iPhone development caused Leopard's delay. While acknowledging the delay is bad news, Gruber argues it's unfair to say Apple can't walk and chew gum at the same time, since Leopard and iPhone share the same OS X foundation — unlike the original iPod, which was developed alongside rapid Mac OS X releases without issue. Drawing on engineering sources at Apple, he reveals that the OS group under Bertrand Serlet has grown less than 10 percent in five years while being asked to power iPhone, Apple TV, and Mac. The real problem isn't that talent was pulled from OS X to iPhone, but from the Mac part of OS X to the iPhone part of OS X.
key_points: Leopard and iPhone aren't separate products — they share the same OS X foundation, making the 'can't walk and chew gum' criticism unfair
The original iPod was developed as a wholly separate project during Mac OS X's most productive stretch, proving Apple can handle parallel efforts when they're truly independent
Apple's OS group has grown less than 10 percent in five years despite OS X now powering iPhone, Apple TV, Mac desktops, and servers
The apps group has grown significantly and now outnumbers the OS group, revealing a resource allocation problem rather than a hiring problem
The PR spin is essentially true: talent was pulled from the Mac part of OS X to work on the iPhone part of OS X
money_quotes: Leopard and iPhone aren't two wholly separate products. They're two products built on the exact same foundation: OS X.
There's a difference between throwing new hires at a late project (which almost never works, and almost always in fact makes things worse), and allocating the OS team the resources it needs.
OS X is being asked to do far more — powering both iPhone and Apple TV while continuing its role as a desktop and server OS for the Mac — but with almost no additional engineering talent.
It's not so much that talent was pulled from OS X to work on the iPhone, but that they were pulled from the Mac part of OS X to work on the iPhone part of OS X.
There's still going to be a major new release of OS X in June. It's just not the one we'd been expecting.
posts.key_insight: The Leopard delay isn't about Apple's inability to multitask — it's about a shared OS X foundation being stretched across Mac, iPhone, and Apple TV without proportional growth in the engineering team that builds it.

posts.summary: Gruber showcases the Daring Fireball t-shirts, thanking designer Kevin Cornell and printer Oddica. He highlights memorable instances of readers wearing DF shirts in high-profile situations: a Food Network taping, a 20,000-foot Himalayan peak, and Sundance Film Festival promotional photos. The post serves as both a promotion for the shirts and a celebration of the community around the site.
key_points: Gruber promotes DF t-shirts by highlighting notable reader appearances wearing them
The shirts have appeared at a Food Network taping, atop a Himalayan peak, and at Sundance
Screenwriter John August wore his DF shirt for promotional photos of his Sundance film The Nines
money_quotes: Any high-profile situation where you need to look your best is a good time to wear your DF t-shirt.
Let's say you're a successful screenwriter who has written and directed a film that's playing at Sundance. What do you wear for the promotional photos? John August wore his DF t-shirt.
posts.key_insight: Gruber turns merchandise promotion into community storytelling by celebrating readers who wear DF shirts in increasingly impressive contexts.

posts.summary: Gruber reminds readers that the 2007 Daring Fireball Membership Drive ends today, with prizes to be awarded the next day. Signups are about 60 short of the previous year's tally but expected to surpass it given last-day surges. Members can join for $19 or buy a t-shirt for $29. He explains the prize selection process and announces late additions to the prize pool including Mint 2.0, 37signals Basecamp credit, and Ambrosia Software games.
key_points: The 2007 membership drive is closing with signups nearly matching the prior year's total
Members can specify prize preferences by emailing a ranked list
133 prizes will be randomly awarded to eligible members who joined or renewed since January 2007
Late prize additions include Mint 2.0, 37signals Basecamp credit, and Ambrosia Software games
money_quotes: So far, so good, at this writing we're about 60 signups short of last year's tally.
Given how many people usually wait until the last day, it's looking good to top last year.
If you've been waiting, and you want in on the prizes, today is the day.
If you've already signed up or renewed, you have my sincere thanks.
I'll pick 133 winners at random from the eligible members.
posts.key_insight: A straightforward membership drive reminder that illustrates Gruber's direct, reader-supported business model for independent publishing.

posts.summary: Gruber wraps up the 2007 Daring Fireball membership drive contest, noting that winners have been selected and notified. He thanks the companies that donated prizes and expresses gratitude to readers who financially supported the site. The post marks the one-year anniversary of his decision to write Daring Fireball full-time, and he reflects briefly on the success of that transition. It's a short, sincere thank-you note to his community.
key_points: The membership drive contest winners were selected and notified by email
Multiple companies donated prizes for the contest
April 20 marked one year since Gruber went full-time on Daring Fireball
Reader financial support made full-time independent writing viable
money_quotes: It's very fun to give away piles of cool stuff; I can see why Bob Barker keeps going.
April 20 marked the one-year anniversary of my announcement that I was going to write Daring Fireball full-time.
It's been quite a year, and I couldn't have done it without your continuing support.
posts.key_insight: One year into writing Daring Fireball full-time, Gruber credits reader support as the foundation that made independent tech commentary viable.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to David Maynor's prediction that he would dismiss the CanSecWest Mac OS X exploit. He draws a sharp distinction between Maynor's unproven MacBook Wi-Fi hack from the previous summer and Dino Dai Zovi's proven CanSecWest exploit, emphasizing that he judges security claims based on evidence, not tribal loyalty. He acknowledges the exploit fully counts and predicts Apple will handle it professionally, contrasting this with Maynor's cynical predictions about Apple's response.
key_points: Gruber rejects being labeled a 'Mac faithful' zealot, framing his stance as evidence-based rather than tribal
He distinguishes between Maynor's still-unproven Wi-Fi exploit and Dai Zovi's proven CanSecWest exploit as a matter of evidence, not platform loyalty
He makes specific, falsifiable predictions about Apple's professional response to Dai Zovi, directly countering Maynor's predictions of legal threats and smear campaigns
The rhetorical 'In my world' vs 'In Maynor's world' structure frames the disagreement as one between evidence-based reasoning and conspiratorial thinking
money_quotes: In my world, I look for proof and evidence.
Maynor and Ellch's supposed MacBook Wi-Fi exploit? Still unproven. Dino Dai Zovi's winning exploit in the CanSecWest contest? Proven. It's that simple.
Nobody serious is downplaying this bug or claiming it doesn't count. Of course it counts.
It's not the first such exploit against Mac OS X. It won't be the last.

In my world, Dai Zovi won't hear a word from Apple legal; Apple won't say a single bad word about him in the press; and, weeks from now, when the bug is fixed in a security update, he'll receive full credit for finding it.
posts.key_insight: The difference between legitimate security criticism and unproven claims isn't about platform loyalty — it's about whether you can show the proof.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews Panic's Coda, a new IDE that consolidates web development tools — text editor, FTP client, browser preview, terminal, CSS editor — into a single window. He argues that Coda's genius isn't in the features of its individual components (which are deliberately less capable than standalone alternatives like BBEdit or TextMate), but in the connections between them. Panic avoided bloat by not pursuing feature parity with dedicated apps, instead focusing on reducing context-switching and organizing work by project rather than by application. Gruber draws a comparison to Apple's upcoming Spaces feature in Leopard, noting both address the same problem of project-based workflow organization. He positions Coda as a conceptual descendant of OpenDoc, reimagined Panic-style.
key_points: Coda's appeal is not its feature set but how it feels to use — the connections between components matter more than the components themselves
Panic deliberately made each component less featured than standalone alternatives to avoid bloat, which Gruber considers the harder and more important accomplishment
The Mac and Unix share a philosophical preference for small, dedicated tools, and Coda challenges this by arguing web development should be treated as a single conceptual task
Coda organizes work by project rather than by application, solving a fundamental limitation of how Mac OS X handles window and context switching
Apple's upcoming Spaces feature in Leopard addresses the same project-based organization problem from the OS level, making it Coda's most interesting competitor
money_quotes: The appeal of Coda cannot be expressed solely by any comparison of features. The point is not what it does, but how it feels to use it.
The essential aspects of Coda aren't features in its components, but rather the connections between components.

The mistake that leads so many well-intentioned UI designers astray is underestimating the pull of bloat, creating designs that attempt to toe right up against the line. But when you get close, you get sucked in.
Trade-offs are inevitable; the trick is figuring out what to trade.
It's ambitious because Panic has taken into their own hands aspects of the user experience that are typically within the purview of the system itself.
Switching contexts on the Mac is conceptually based around apps, not projects. Coda is like OpenDoc, Panic-style.
One problem with application bloat is that it has a gravity-like pull. Once you start edging closer to bloat, the pull gets stronger.
posts.key_insight: The best IDEs succeed not by matching dedicated tools feature-for-feature, but by making deliberate trade-offs that prioritize the connections between components over the capabilities within them.

posts.summary: Gruber interviews security researcher Dino Dai Zovi, who wrote the winning exploit in the CanSecWest MacBook Pro hacking contest. Dai Zovi explains he found a Java-based QuickTime vulnerability in about nine hours overnight, achieving user-level access which is practically sufficient for most attacks. The interview covers the exploit's cross-browser and cross-platform nature, the reality that multiple people often independently discover the same vulnerabilities, and practical security advice for Mac users. Dai Zovi, who uses Macs as his primary computers, recommends running as a non-admin user and using encrypted disk images, but notably does not use anti-virus software. The conversation also touches on Apple's security posture, with Dai Zovi praising their proactive patching while urging adoption of ASLR and other modern protections.
key_points: The exploit was a Java-based QuickTime vulnerability affecting Safari, Firefox, and potentially Windows — not Safari-specific as initially reported
User-level access is practically as dangerous as root access on single-user machines like laptops, since an attacker can read, delete, or corrupt all user data
Dai Zovi believes it's a fallacy to think vulnerabilities are only discovered by one person, citing his own experience with independently discovered exploits
Even a top security researcher who hacks Macs for prizes uses Macs as his primary computers and doesn't run anti-virus software
Apple was praised for proactively patching internally-found vulnerabilities but urged to implement ASLR and better default account security
money_quotes: On a (primarily) single-user machine like a laptop or desktop, even non-admin user-level privileges are enough for most attacks.
You can replace your system software and apps with installer discs. If you don't have backups — which is true for most people — you can't replace your data.
I have been in this business, however, long enough to know the fallacy in believing that a vulnerability is only discovered by one person.
I sat down to begin looking for a web-based vulnerability at around 10 pm, had found one around 3 am, and had written a reliably working exploit around 7 am.
I use a Mac as my primary, secondary, and tertiary computers.
For me, the challenge, especially with the time pressure, was the real allure.
I do not, however, run any commercial anti-virus packages.
posts.key_insight: Mac security vulnerabilities are real and independently discoverable by multiple parties, but practical defense comes from sensible precautions like non-admin accounts and encrypted storage — not anti-virus software.

posts.summary: Gruber shares two Perl scripts he wrote for ThisService that automate Google 'I'm Feeling Lucky' searches via macOS's system-wide Services menu. The scripts let you select text in any application and either replace it with the resulting URL or open the result in a browser, all via keyboard shortcut. He walks through the before-and-after workflow for inserting a URL into an email, showing how a five-step process becomes three steps with no app-switching. The scripts use curl to follow Google's redirect and extract the final URL.
key_points: Two scripts automate Google Lucky searches: one replaces selected text with the destination URL, the other opens the result in a browser
Using macOS Services means the scripts work from any application without switching contexts
The workflow for inserting a URL drops from five steps (with app-switching) to three steps (type, select, shortcut)
The scripts are written in Perl and use curl to follow Google's I'm Feeling Lucky redirect to extract the final URL
money_quotes: I do this sort of thing every single day. It seems ripe for some sort of automation, especially because I just know that if I do a Google search for "imdb raiders lost ark", it's going to be the first hit in the results.
No switching between apps involved — just type what you're looking for and hit a shortcut. And because they're services, they work from any app.
posts.key_insight: Small workflow automations that eliminate app-switching and leverage system-wide services can dramatically streamline everyday tasks like inserting URLs.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Steve Ballmer's dismissal of the iPhone as too expensive to succeed, arguing that the price criticism fundamentally misunderstands Apple's market position. He points out that millions of people already spent $399-599 on iPods, and the iPhone does everything an iPod does plus much more. Gruber notes that Windows Mobile only holds 6% of the smartphone market despite years of effort, making Ballmer's confidence misplaced. He argues the iPhone is a consumer product like the iPod, not a business device, and that comparing it to cheap phones rather than premium gadgets is the wrong frame. Gruber predicts Apple could pass Microsoft in phone OS market share within two years, and that the real question about the iPhone is technical execution, not price.
key_points: The iPhone's price should be compared to iPod pricing history ($399-599 at launch), not to subsidized commodity phones
Windows Mobile only holds 6% of the smartphone market despite years of investment, undermining Ballmer's dismissal of Apple's potential share
The iPhone targets consumers who buy the nicest things they can afford, not businesses that buy the cheapest things possible for employees
The only real risks for the iPhone are technical (battery life, touch screen, UI), not price — if it works well, demand will be enormous
Microsoft and other phone makers have spent a decade selling phones with terrible UIs, showing they don't understand how much software experience matters for consumer appeal
money_quotes: There are millions of people who have already spent $399–599 on an iPod within the last few years. With the exception of storage capacity, the iPhone does everything these iPods do, and, well, a whole lot fucking more.
Maybe I'm the moron, but the way I see it, if the iPhone's initial price is wrong, it's too low, not too high.
Businesses, typically, want to buy the cheapest things possible for their employees to use. When buying for themselves, people want to buy the nicest things they can afford.
The iPod isn't marketed to businesses and Apple has sold 100 million of them. The iPod is marketed to people, and the iPhone is, too.
Some of these pundits and analysts are morons. Ballmer, however, is a very smart man, but what he's saying about the iPhone is going to make him look stupid if it's successful.
The executives running these companies clearly have no idea how much the software user experience matters when you're trying to create a gadget with iPod-like appeal.
We'll see how long Steve Ballmer thinks the iPhone's price is funny.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone's price critics were using the wrong comparison — measured against iPods rather than cheap phones, the iPhone was arguably underpriced for a device that did everything an iPod did and far more.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects reports that PC World's editor Harry McCracken resigned after a senior VP killed an anti-Apple story, arguing the narrative is far less clear-cut than reported. He notes that no source — named or anonymous — actually alleges Apple applied pressure, yet Paul Thurrott and others leap to that conclusion. Gruber questions the credibility of anonymous claims that Steve Jobs personally supervised Macworld's editorial content, calling the implication absurd. He constructs an equally plausible alternative scenario: Crawford simply killed a bad article, and a sympathetic staffer spun it as advertiser censorship. Gruber concludes that without seeing the killed article itself, no one can determine which version is true.
key_points: No source in any report actually alleges Apple applied pressure to kill the story, yet commentators like Thurrott assume Apple's involvement
The anonymous claim that Jobs would call Macworld about stories before publication implies pre-publication approval, which Gruber calls implausible for any publication with integrity
McCracken's stated willingness to continue freelancing for PC World undermines the narrative that he quit over a corrupt editorial policy
Gruber constructs an alternative scenario where Crawford killed a genuinely bad article and a sympathetic staffer spun it as censorship
The entire controversy hinges on an article no one outside PC World has actually seen
money_quotes: Nowhere in Zetter's weblog entry, nor in the CNet story Thurrott links to, is there any mention of any pressure coming from Apple whatsoever.
And we're supposed to believe Steve Jobs himself effectively supervises Macworld each month?
No publication with any integrity would send articles for approval in advance of publication to Apple or any other company.

If he quit as editor because the magazine's publisher is enforcing a 'You can't write anything bad about Apple or other major advertisers' policy, why would McCracken still be willing to be associated with the magazine in any capacity?
I have no sources for this, anonymous or otherwise. I made it up, based solely on Zetter's brief description of the article and PC World's history of publishing stupid articles about Apple.
I'm not saying it's true, I'm just saying it's plausible — or at least more plausible than Zetter's scenario.
Did Crawford kill a fair and truthful article because it was unflattering to Apple? Or did he kill it because he thought the article wasn't good?
Without seeing the article, it's impossible to say.
posts.key_insight: The rush to frame an editorial dispute as Apple censorship reveals how readily the tech press builds narratives from anonymous sources and assumptions, when the simplest explanation — a bad article got killed — fits the known facts just as well.

posts.summary: Gruber provides a brief update on the shipping timeline for new Daring Fireball t-shirts. The shirts, designed by Kevin Cornell and produced in partnership with Oddica, were originally expected to ship by the end of April but were delayed. The delay was caused by underestimating demand and needing extra time to determine print quantities. He notes the new designs have been very popular and confirms shirts are now in production with worldwide shipping expected by the end of the following week.
key_points: New DF t-shirts designed by Kevin Cornell were delayed past their original end-of-April shipping target
The delay was caused by unexpectedly high demand requiring more time to determine print quantities
Shirts are produced and fulfilled through a partnership with Oddica
Shipping worldwide was expected to begin by end of the following week
money_quotes: The new Kevin Cornell-designed shirts are a hit, to say the least.
posts.key_insight: A short logistical update noting that demand for new Daring Fireball merchandise exceeded expectations, causing a brief shipping delay.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the rumored Microsoft acquisition of Yahoo not from a business angle, but from a technical one: Yahoo's entire stack is built on open source, not Microsoft technology. He argues that Microsoft is unique in having built virtually all of its own developer tools and infrastructure, while modern web companies overwhelmingly use open source. Acquiring Yahoo would force Microsoft to either waste years rewriting Yahoo's codebase in Microsoft technology—as they infamously did with Hotmail—or finally embrace non-Microsoft web technologies. Gruber suggests the acquisition could be Microsoft's face-saving way to adopt open source without admitting their web strategy has failed.
key_points: Microsoft is uniquely insular in building all its own developer technology—compilers, languages, databases, web servers—unlike any other modern company.
Apple nearly died from the same 'do everything ourselves' approach in the '90s and was saved by embracing open source under Steve Jobs.
Web companies built on Microsoft technology are rare, reflecting an entire generation of developers who never considered Microsoft's stack.
Rewriting Yahoo's open-source codebase to use Microsoft technology would be insane given the urgency of countering Google.
Acquiring Yahoo could let Microsoft embrace open source web technologies without directly admitting their web strategy has failed.
money_quotes: One of the least remarked-upon remarkable things about Microsoft is just how much software they've written themselves.
There's no other company that does anything even remotely like this today.
By the '90s, that 'let's do everything ourselves' mindset nearly killed the company.
The biggest difference between the old Apple regime and the new post-NeXT-merger Steve Jobs regime is that Apple now focuses on just a few things.
When was the last time you saw a new hit web site developed using Microsoft's web stack?
There's an entire generation of developers who are growing up without ever even considering Microsoft developer technologies.
It would seem utterly insane to acquire Yahoo and then waste years of time rewriting Yahoo's existing code to use Microsoft technology.
Acquiring Yahoo could be Microsoft's way out of the corner they've painted themselves into.
posts.key_insight: A Microsoft-Yahoo merger would be less a business play than a technological reckoning, forcing Microsoft to finally embrace the open-source web stack it has spent decades trying to replace with its own.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Engadget editor Ryan Block's handling of a bogus internal Apple email claiming iPhone and Leopard delays. He argues the hoax should have been obvious because Apple had issued no actual press release — something required by law before such news could circulate internally. Gruber further criticizes Block's follow-up as self-justification rather than apology, and points out that Block didn't merely report what he knew (an internal email was circulating) but instead presented the delays as confirmed fact. The core failure was prioritizing a scoop over basic journalistic skepticism.
key_points: The hoax was detectable because the email referenced a press release that didn't exist — Apple is legally required to disclose such news publicly before or simultaneously with internal communication.
Block's follow-up framed his decision as responsible journalism, but Gruber reads it as rationalization driven by scoop anxiety rather than editorial judgment.
Block presented unverified internal email content as confirmed fact, using language like 'on authority' that implied executive-level sourcing he didn't have.
There was a defensible version of this story — transparently reporting that an internal email was circulating — but Block chose sensationalism over accuracy.
money_quotes: There is no way — none — Apple would first announce a dramatic change in iPhone availability via wide internal email. No way.
Apple cannot release news like this internally before issuing a public statement. It's not just good manners, it's the law.
The red flag here is the phrase 'issued a press release today' — which, you will note, is in the past tense. If they issued a press release, where was the press release?

I don't blame the Apple employees who received this email and believed it to be true, at least at first; they had no reason to be skeptical. But Ryan Block's job is to be skeptical of sensational news.
Translation: I jizzed my pants at the thought of having a scoop on this, and posted it because I thought some other site might post it first.
'On authority' implies just the opposite — that Engadget's source knew, firsthand, as in like executive-level knowledge, that this information was true.
I don't see how anyone can argue that what Block did post — which was completely and utterly false — was anything other than sensational jackassery.
posts.key_insight: A tech journalist's job is to be skeptical of sensational news, and presenting an unverified internal email as confirmed fact — while obscuring the actual source — is not reporting, it's reckless amplification.

posts.summary: Gruber shares AppleScripts he wrote to streamline sending information from Mailsmith and NetNewsWire into Yojimbo, Bare Bones Software's information management app. He explains that Yojimbo's core design philosophy is frictionless capture of information, and that writing custom scripts further reduces friction for repeated workflows. The scripts handle creating notes from email messages and bookmarks or web archives from RSS items, with tag support via dialog prompts. The post includes code snippets showing the AppleScript tag-parsing logic and Yojimbo's scripting interface.
key_points: Yojimbo's central design purpose is making it effortless to throw bits of information into it without deliberation
Custom AppleScripts can reduce friction even further for repeated workflows between specific apps and Yojimbo
The Mailsmith script intelligently handles both full messages and text selections when creating Yojimbo notes
The NetNewsWire bookmark script adds tag support via a comma-separated dialog prompt, improving on Fraser Speirs's original version
money_quotes: The central point of Yojimbo, as I see it, is that you can just throw bits of information into it without really thinking much at all.

It's a deceptively simple application, and most of the places where it gets particularly clever share the same fundamental purpose: to make it as easy and frictionless as possible to add new bits of information.
When I repeatedly send bits of information into Yojimbo from the same source application, I find that it's worthwhile to write AppleScripts to make it even easier.
Creating a version of the Mailsmith script that prompts for tags, or which uses Growl for notifications, is left as an exercise for the reader.
posts.key_insight: The best information management tools prioritize frictionless capture above all else, and custom scripting can push that friction even lower for repeated workflows.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Steve Jobs has been deliberately vague and misleading about third-party iPhone development, offering technical explanations that don't hold up to scrutiny. He contends that Apple's real reason for delaying an SDK isn't technical concerns about network stability or phone crashes, but aesthetic — Apple wants to establish the iPhone UI paradigm before letting third parties in. By setting expectations at 'never,' any eventual SDK announcement becomes good news. Gruber predicts that within two years, developers will have ways to write iPhone software, and that Apple is simply keeping its options open while shipping the product.
key_points: Jobs's stated technical reasons for no third-party development (network crashes, phone instability) don't hold up — other platforms allow third-party apps without these problems, and OS X's protected memory should prevent app crashes from taking down the phone
Apple's real motivation is aesthetic: let users internalize what true iPhone-style apps feel like before opening development, so user expectations force developers to build native-feeling apps
Apple is committing to nothing publicly because it preserves maximum flexibility — no promised APIs means they can change anything without breaking third-party code
The strategy is classic under-promise and over-deliver: if the official SDK ship date is 'never,' it can't be late
Gruber draws a direct parallel to the original Macintosh, which shipped without a terminal to force developers to write real Mac-style apps rather than character-mode ports
money_quotes: The simple truth is that Apple is committing to nothing because that allows them to keep all of their options open.
If the official ship date for an iPhone SDK is "never", it can't be late.
The main reason Jobs might want to delay allowing third-party software to run on the iPhone isn't technical, but aesthetic.
Let users grow accustomed to what true iPhone-style apps feel like to use, and they — the users — will demand that developers write true iPhone-style apps when development is eventually opened up.
Downplaying the prospects for third-party app development in the meantime is a way of under-promising and over-delivering.
While iPhone development remains private to Apple, Apple is free to change and shift the APIs as they see fit.
From Apple's (and Jobs's) perspective, that'd be about as cool as it would have been for a character-mode DOS or Apple II app to run on an original Mac back in 1984.
posts.key_insight: Apple's refusal to announce an iPhone SDK isn't about technical limitations but a deliberate aesthetic strategy — establish the platform's identity first, then let third parties in once users know what good iPhone apps should feel like.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's new iPhone commercials, noting that no other tech product is advertised simply by demonstrating its user interface. He breaks down the 'Calamari' ad step by step, showing that unlocking the phone, watching a movie, finding a restaurant on Maps, and calling it requires just one slide and eight taps. He argues this efficiency-per-tap metric, combined with obvious animated transitions, demonstrates a usability advantage comparable to the original Mac versus DOS. The piece also notes subtle changes in the iPhone's app list since January, including the browser being renamed from 'Web' to 'Safari.'
key_points: No other cell phone or tech product is advertised by simply showing its UI in action, which speaks to how far ahead the iPhone is
The 'Calamari' ad demonstrates unlocking, movie playback, map search, and a phone call in just one slide and eight taps — near-optimal efficiency
Every mode switch uses purposeful animated transitions that reinforce spatial understanding of the interface
The iPhone's usability advantage over competitors is compared to the original Mac's advantage over DOS, not just Mac vs. Windows
The interface is high on obviousness — anyone who can use an iPod or PC should be able to figure out the iPhone
money_quotes: No other cell phone is advertised by showing off the user interface.
It's a testimony to just how clever the iPhone UI is, and just how bad the UIs are on the competition.

I don't think it's possible to make a commercial for the Mac that makes any task seem that much cooler than doing the same thing on Windows. This is more like the overwhelming usability advantage from when the Mac was competing against DOS.
No matter how clever the iPhone is, it's hard to make 30-second spots that are both easy to follow and yet contain so much information.
That seem pretty close to optimal, in terms of efficiency per tap.
The experience isn't just short on screen taps, it's high on obviousness.
It seems obvious that anyone who can use an iPod or a PC ought to be able to figure out on their own how to use iPhone. The fundamental trick to learn is the magic home button.
Keep dreaming if you think Apple has secretly added GPS behind the FCC's back.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone's UI is so self-evidently superior that Apple can advertise the product by simply showing it in use — something no competitor could attempt.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier speculation about how the iPhone Maps app knows the user's location, as shown in Apple's 'Calamari' commercial. He had initially guessed it used a default zip code setting, ruling out secret GPS. Dozens of readers pointed out that cell tower triangulation (GSM localization) is a well-known technique that could provide location without GPS. The technology was already common in European mobile phones and could be quite accurate in urban areas.
key_points: The iPhone Maps app appeared to know the user's location in Apple's commercial, raising questions about how it worked without GPS
Cell tower-based positioning (GSM localization) can determine approximate location without GPS hardware
In dense urban environments, cell tower positioning can be accurate to within a few city blocks
The technology was already common in European mobile phones before the iPhone launch
money_quotes: Keep dreaming if you think Apple has secretly added GPS behind the FCC's back.

In a dense urban environment with plenty of nearby towers, it could be accurate to within a few city blocks, and even in rural areas it ought to be more than accurate enough for something like the Maps demo in the commercial.
posts.key_insight: Even without GPS, the iPhone could leverage existing cell tower infrastructure to provide useful location awareness for its Maps application.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses the controversy over Apple embedding user-identifying metadata in DRM-free iTunes Plus tracks. He highlights the EFF's complaint that personal information is stored unencrypted, then counters with Alastair Houghton's argument that privacy and anonymity are distinct concepts. Gruber implies that critics are missing the bigger picture: Apple removed DRM from music purchases, which was what everyone wanted. He closes with a joke about a grandmother who, after miraculously getting her grandson back from the sea, complains he lost his hat — drawing a parallel to those who got DRM-free music but complain about metadata.
key_points: The EFF complained that iTunes Plus DRM-free tracks contain unencrypted user-identifying metadata including name and email
Houghton draws a sharp distinction between privacy advocacy and anonymity advocacy, arguing the EFF is really pushing for the latter
Gruber uses a joke to illustrate that critics are ungrateful — they got DRM-free music (the miracle) but are complaining about minor metadata (the hat)
The implicit argument is that perfect shouldn't be the enemy of good when it comes to digital rights progress
money_quotes: What these complaints about the iTunes Plus metadata remind me of is a joke.
Most of them are not, in fact, advocating privacy. Instead, the majority advocate anonymity, which is quite different and has many effects that are detrimental to the rest of society.
The grandmother looks at the boy, then raises her head to the sky. She shouts, "He had a hat!"
posts.key_insight: Complaining about user metadata in DRM-free iTunes tracks misses the forest for the trees — Apple just gave people what they'd been demanding for years.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the new 17-inch MacBook Pro's high-resolution 1920×1200 display option, noting it packs the same pixel count as Apple's 23-inch Cinema Display into a 17-inch laptop screen. He provides a detailed comparison table showing pixels-per-inch across Apple's entire display lineup, revealing that the new laptop achieves 133 PPI — far exceeding any Cinema Display's roughly 98-102 PPI. He notes his personal wish for a 15-inch model with similarly upgraded resolution. He concludes that the display's exceptional density feels like preparation for the resolution independence feature expected in Mac OS X Leopard.
key_points: The new 17-inch MacBook Pro's 1920×1200 high-res option matches the pixel count of the much larger 23-inch Cinema Display
At 133 PPI, the new display dramatically exceeds the ~98-102 PPI of Apple's desktop Cinema Displays
Apple's notebook displays have consistently offered higher pixel density than their desktop counterparts
Gruber sees the high-res display as a leading indicator of resolution independence coming in Mac OS X Leopard
money_quotes: Which is still my main Mac today. Screw that little endian stuff.
At 1920 × 1200, they offer the same number of pixels as a 23-inch Cinema Display.
Notably absent from this week's new MacBook Pros is the configuration I'd personally be most interested in: a higher resolution 1680 × 1050 15-inch MacBook.
At 133 PPI, it's hard not to see it as a few months ahead of its time, optimized for the resolution independence coming (I hope) in Mac OS X 10.5.
posts.key_insight: The 17-inch MacBook Pro's 133 PPI display, far exceeding Apple's desktop monitors, signals that Apple is laying hardware groundwork for resolution-independent rendering in Leopard.

posts.summary: Gruber writes a comedic screenplay depicting the transition from Apple's Brushed Metal interface theme to the new dark gray theme introduced in Mac OS X Leopard. The piece personifies the UI themes as characters meeting outside Apple headquarters on the morning of WWDC 2007. Brushed Metal, once the dominant look of iTunes and many Mac apps, discovers he's been replaced system-wide by the younger, flashier dark gray theme from iLife '06. The twist comes when Brushed Metal learns he's not there for the iPhone team as he hopes, but is instead being led to a basement for what's clearly his execution. The piece satirizes Apple's habit of abruptly abandoning design elements and the awkward generational handoff between UI paradigms.
key_points: Apple's Leopard preview at WWDC 2007 replaced the polarizing Brushed Metal theme with a unified dark gray appearance across the entire OS
Brushed Metal's desperate pitching of metal scrollbars and buttons satirizes how aging design elements try to stay relevant
The piece captures the abruptness with which Apple discards once-prominent design choices, treating it as a corporate assassination
The iPhone reference is a red herring — Brushed Metal thinks he's getting a prestigious new assignment but is actually being led to his demise
money_quotes: I was huge fan of yours when I was a kid. You were my idol. Big poster on my bedroom wall of you in the original iTunes on Mac OS 9.
The windows switching to my chrome? The least remarkable change in the UI. The least!
Nothing says "slide me" like Brushed Metal.
Close/minimize/zoom — green/yellow/red tinted Brushed Metal against your smooth background. Beautiful!
This isn't about the iPhone, is it?
Not in the face, OK? It's all I've got.
You won't feel a thing.
posts.key_insight: Apple's design evolution is ruthless — yesterday's signature interface element becomes today's quietly eliminated embarrassment, with no sentimentality for what came before.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes the three main takeaways from Apple's WWDC 2007 keynote. He finds Leopard's previously secret features underwhelming, consisting mainly of cosmetic changes like a new window theme and Cover Flow in Finder. He sharply criticizes Apple's positioning of web apps as the development path for iPhone, calling it insulting to developers who want native app access. He also explains that Safari for Windows is primarily a money play, revealing that Apple earns roughly $25 million per year from Google search integration revenue, which could grow substantially with a Windows user base.
key_points: Leopard's 'secret features' turned out to be mostly cosmetic — a new window theme and Cover Flow in Finder — far less significant than the secrecy implied
Apple's claim that web apps are the path for iPhone development was received poorly by developers, especially since Apple doesn't use web apps for any of its own iPhone software
Safari for Windows is primarily a revenue play: Apple earns about $2 million/month from Google search toolbar integration, and a Windows version could push that to $100 million/year
The new Dock's 3D visual effect only works well at the bottom position, despite Jobs claiming it looks great on the side
Browser search bar integration is a quietly lucrative business model where developers earn money from ads users would have seen anyway
money_quotes: This is sort of like saying you're adding a secret new player to your baseball team and then revealing that it's one of the existing players wearing a new jersey.
Apple's 'you can write great apps for the iPhone: they're called web sites' message went over like a lead balloon.
It's insulting, because it's not a way to write iPhone apps, and you can't bullshit developers.
If all you have to offer is a shit sandwich, just say it. Don't tell us how lucky we are and that it's going to taste delicious.
If web apps running in Safari are a great way to write iPhone apps, why aren't web apps running in Safari a great way to write Mac apps?
Safari is a free download, but it's already one of Apple's most profitable software products.
Browser developers earn money — a lot of it — for ads that users were going to see anyway, just by performing the same search without the built-in integration.
posts.key_insight: Apple's WWDC 2007 revealed underwhelming Leopard secrets, insulted developers by pretending web apps were a real iPhone development platform, and quietly launched Safari for Windows as a search revenue cash grab.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to a Wall Street Journal article about the iPhone's lack of Microsoft Exchange support, arguing that the pressure to adapt will fall on corporate IT departments rather than Apple. He dismisses suggestions that Apple should license proprietary technology from Microsoft or RIM, pointing out that Exchange already supports IMAP but IT departments refuse to enable it. He frames the situation as a bottom-up revolution where employees buying iPhones will force IT departments to adopt open standards. Gruber argues Apple's strategy is to champion open protocols like IMAP and CalDAV rather than kowtow to Microsoft's Exchange hegemony.
key_points: The WSJ frames the iPhone's lack of Exchange support as Apple's problem, but Gruber argues it's actually a problem created by IT departments chaining themselves to proprietary systems
Exchange already supports IMAP, but corporate IT departments refuse to enable it out of vague security concerns that Gruber finds logically inconsistent
Employee demand for iPhones will create bottom-up pressure on IT departments to support open standards rather than top-down pressure on Apple to adopt proprietary ones
Apple's enterprise strategy is to push open protocols like IMAP and CalDAV rather than license technology from Microsoft or RIM
money_quotes: This suggestion isn't about making it easier for corporate users; this is about making it easier for corporate IT departments that have chained themselves to Exchange.

Odds of Apple doing this: nearly zero. The pressure isn't going to be on Apple to license proprietary technology from RIM or Microsoft. The pressure is going to be on IT departments to support open standards like IMAP, which the iPhone supports.
Translation: IMAP has cooties.

Think about it: the difference between IMAP and Exchange isn't that only IMAP is exposed to the public. If your Exchange server wasn't 'exposed', how would employees receive or send email while outside the office?
Self-important IT experts will continue to insist that the iPhone 'must' or 'needs to' support 'business software systems', but in the meantime, their employees will be buying iPhones on their own.

Apple's answer to the enterprise 'problem' isn't to kowtow to the Microsoft Exchange hegemony; it's to point in the opposite direction, and show how much better things can be with open industry protocols.
Like many successful revolutions, this one might come from the bottom.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone's lack of Exchange support isn't Apple's problem to solve — employee demand will force corporate IT to embrace open standards rather than the other way around.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Slate media critic Jack Shafer's argument that the press gives Apple undue, sycophantic coverage. He counters Shafer's claims point by point: the video iPod's massive sales justified its coverage, the iPhone battery news moved Apple's stock price $4, and comparing the iPhone launch to the obscure Helio Ocean ignores the unprecedented consumer phenomenon surrounding Apple's phone debut. Gruber argues that when hundreds of thousands of people line up to buy a product, covering it isn't sycophancy — it's journalism. He turns Shafer's own concluding insult back on him.
key_points: The video iPod's tens of millions in sales retroactively justified the press attention Shafer mocked
Apple's stock jumping $4 on battery news suggests the coverage reflected genuine market-moving information, not hype
Comparing the iPhone to Helio's Ocean on features misses the point — no one camped out or even knew when the Ocean launched
The iPhone launch is an unprecedented consumer phenomenon that merits coverage regardless of whether the product ultimately disappoints
Shafer's cynicism about Apple coverage mistakes genuine public interest for media sycophancy
money_quotes: Unless Shafer is willing to argue that the iPod's continuing success is the result of the media coverage, the video iPod's popularity justifies the press coverage.
People aren't just aware of what day the iPhone goes on sale, they know the hour.
It is, of course, entirely possible that the original iPhone will be a disappointment. But it's impossible to argue that there's ever been a phone debut like this.
What is Shafer's argument? That the press should ignore the fact that hundreds of thousands of people are going to line up hoping to buy an iPhone at 6pm on the first day it's available?
Is it not possible that the iPhone deserves tremendous media attention?
Shafer's right about one thing: someone needs a swift kick in the ass.
posts.key_insight: When a product generates unprecedented consumer demand and moves stock prices, covering it isn't media sycophancy — it's reporting on a genuine phenomenon.

posts.summary: Gruber highlights what he considers the pinnacle of pre-launch iPhone criticism by singling out Rob Enderle's comments to ABC News. Enderle claimed the iPhone's touchscreen could lead to class-action lawsuits because teenagers would get into car accidents while texting on it, since unlike physical keyboards, you have to look at a touchscreen. Enderle also suggested the iPhone's slick design would make it prone to dropping. Gruber mocks both the absurdity of the claims and ABC journalist Ashley Phillips for treating Enderle as a credible source without challenging any of his assertions.
key_points: Rob Enderle predicted the iPhone's touchscreen would cause car accidents and class-action lawsuits against Apple because teens couldn't 'blind text' on it
The journalist let Enderle's claim that phone vendors are 'often' held accountable for texting-while-driving accidents pass without asking for a single example
Enderle criticized a device he had clearly never touched, calling it 'pretty slick' and therefore prone to dropping
Gruber's broader point is about journalistic negligence in treating known contrarians as credible expert sources
money_quotes: We've been swimming in iPhone jackassery all week, but no one's ever going to out-jackass Rob Enderle.
ABC's Ashley Phillips, who quotes him as though he's anything other than a fool, should be ashamed.
"Rightly or wrongly" — I love that.
Phillips lets that slide without asking Enderle for a single example of such a vendor.
Enderle also believes that the iPhone, which he clearly has never touched, is "pretty slick" and thus will be prone to dropping. The mind boggles.
posts.key_insight: The pre-iPhone launch media cycle was marked by tech pundits making absurd predictions that went unchallenged by credulous journalists.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles a New York Times article collecting skeptical predictions about the iPhone days before its launch. He methodically quotes various analysts and competitors — a Verizon executive, equity analysts — who predict the iPhone will fail due to price, network issues, and limited appeal. Gruber rebuts each claim with cutting sarcasm, pointing out the absurdity of Verizon bragging about having 18 music phones, the naivety of thinking AT&T set the iPhone's price, and the foolishness of dismissing web browsing and email as 'fancy features.' The piece reads as a time capsule of spectacularly wrong iPhone predictions, with Gruber clearly confident history will prove the doubters wrong.
key_points: The Razr comparison actually highlights the iPhone's advantage: the Razr succeeded on hardware alone despite universally hated software, while the iPhone offered both
Verizon's strategy of offering 18+ similar music phones reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of what Apple was doing with a single, well-designed product
The analyst prediction that the iPhone would appeal to only 3-5% of wireless users exemplifies the establishment's inability to see paradigm shifts coming
Gruber reads Verizon's defensive posturing as regret over having rejected the iPhone partnership with Apple
money_quotes: I know a bunch of people who own Razrs and I don't know one who doesn't despise the software.
Someone has taken Econ 101. And isn't it cute the way he thinks AT&T set the iPhone's price?
Yes, that's right, what Verizon needs are more phones. Why stop at 18? I'd like to choose from at least 40 different similarly-featured phones.
Translation: "We're seriously regretting that we told Apple to go fuck themselves last year, and if you see Steve Jobs, can you tell him that we're really very sorry and that he should call me?"
Fancy features like "web browsing" and "email". Old people don't use crazy stuff like that.
Because if it actually worked as well as advertised, shit, even Edward Snyder would buy one.
They just made this "Edward Snyder" guy up, didn't they?
"I don't think Apple's going to be a big player in this at all." I am so writing that quote down.
posts.key_insight: The pre-iPhone skeptics revealed more about their own inability to recognize a paradigm shift than about any actual weakness in Apple's strategy.

posts.summary: Gruber examines a curious detail in Apple's iPhone activation demo video: a capacity meter showing 80 GB, far exceeding the announced 4 and 8 GB models. He methodically considers three explanations — a secret future iPhone, a hacked iPod standing in, or a Photoshop mockup — and urges readers toward the mundane explanations. He also notes an interesting detail about the iTunes UI scaling without jaggies, hinting at possible resolution independence technology.
key_points: Apple's activation demo video briefly showed an 80 GB capacity, sparking speculation about a larger-capacity iPhone
Gruber presents three explanations and argues the boring ones (hacked iPod or Photoshop mockup) are far more likely than a secret future device
Internal evidence in the video itself is contradictory — the bottom bar says 74 GB while the summary says 7.27 GB, suggesting a production error
The smooth, jaggie-free zoom of the iTunes UI hints at possible resolution independence work happening inside Apple
money_quotes: Before wetting yourself or slipping into a coma, please note that (b) and (c) are far more likely than (a).
Even if there exist pre-production future iPhone models, the odds that they'd just give one to someone on the Apple.com production team are nearly zero.
The more interesting detail in this demo screencast is that when it zooms in, the entire iTunes UI — buttons, text labels, even the mouse cursor — scales without jaggies.
Some sort of private-to-Apple-at-least-for-now resolution independence?
posts.key_insight: The most exciting-seeming detail in a product demo is usually a production error, while the genuinely interesting technical clues go unnoticed.

posts.summary: Gruber mocks David Platt, a self-proclaimed software usability expert, for his spectacularly wrong prediction that the iPhone would 'crash in flames.' Platt argued that combining multiple functions into one device would be essentially impossible for ordinary users, and that Apple's designers had forgotten fundamental usability principles. Gruber dismantles these claims by noting that switching between music and other apps is trivially simple, and that Apple's design team succeeds precisely because they design products they themselves want to use. He also punctures Platt's credibility by noting he's a Harvard Extension instructor, not a Harvard professor as Reuters reported.
key_points: Platt's core argument—that a multipurpose handheld device is inherently too complex for ordinary users—fundamentally misunderstands both the iPhone and Apple's design philosophy
Apple's success comes from engineers who design products they themselves want to use, not from condescending assumptions about 'stupid users'
The specific multitasking concern Platt raised (listening to music while using other apps) is trivially solved by the Home button
Platt's credentials were inflated by Reuters—he's a Harvard Extension instructor, not a professor
money_quotes: There are iPhone doubters, and then there's David Platt.
After starting a song, click the Home button, then click Safari or Mail. Baffling!
The iPod wasn't designed for 'those stupid users'. The iPod was designed by a small team that asked, 'What would we like in a music player?'
Platt's argument seems to be that most people are too stupid to use any multipurpose handheld device.
I'm not sure it's possible to more greatly misunderstand Apple's success this decade.
Somehow I doubt the iPhone designers had to forget any advice from David Platt.
I.e. it is in fact still safe to send your children to Harvard.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone's success was built on Apple designers creating what they themselves wanted, not on dumbing things down for users—making Platt's condescending usability framework exactly wrong.

posts.summary: Gruber endorses The Macalope's critique of John Heilemann's New York Magazine cover story profiling Steve Jobs, arguing the piece is deeply flawed and overreaches to portray Jobs negatively. He notes Heilemann had no access to Jobs or his inner circle, relying instead on quotes from Apple competitors. Gruber dismantles Heilemann's central thesis that the iPhone represents a risky 'Fourth Act' in Jobs's career, arguing instead that not making a phone would have been the real risk. He contends the iPhone is a natural convergence of Apple's two greatest successes—OS X and the iPod—rather than a dangerous new direction. Gruber concludes that the iPhone is so advanced it feels like a product from 2010.
key_points: Heilemann's profile of Jobs is undermined by having no access to Jobs or anyone close to him, relying entirely on Apple competitors for attributed quotes
The 'Apple fanatics' criticism is deflected: it's Heilemann and his editors who cast Jobs and Apple customers in religious terms like 'iGod'
Calling the iPhone a risky 'Fourth Act' is preposterous—not making a phone would have been the real risk for Apple
The iPhone is the natural convergence of Apple's two great successes (OS X software and iPod hardware), not a new strategic direction
Apple's advantage is asymmetric: adding a physical keyboard to a future iPhone would be far easier than competitors replicating Apple's software
money_quotes: But who's the fanatic? It's Heilemann and his editors who cast Jobs, and Apple's customers, in religious terms.
Arguing that the iPhone is a risky move for Apple is preposterous. What would be risky would be not to make a phone.

If it ends up there's truly demand for a physical keypad, it'd be a lot easier for Apple to produce a future slider-style iPhone than it would be for any of their competitors to produce their own iPhone-quality software.
Apple's two spectacular successes in the past decade are OS X (software) and the iPod (premium handheld consumer electronics). The iPhone is where they meet.
Clearly this is where Apple has been heading ever since Jobs came back to the company.
The only thing that's surprising is that they were able to produce a device this small, with these features and battery life, so soon. The iPhone seems like something from 2010.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone isn't a risky new chapter for Apple but the inevitable convergence of its two greatest strengths—OS X software and iPod-class hardware—making it the natural continuation of Jobs's comeback story.

posts.summary: Gruber shares his detailed first-day impressions of the original iPhone, calling it '95 percent amazing, 5 percent maddening.' He praises the responsive UI, gorgeous high-resolution screen, and thoughtful design decisions like prioritizing drag animation feel over rendering and the app-switching model that eliminates the concept of quitting. He identifies significant shortcomings including the lack of a clipboard, poor Notes app design, email-only sharing, and Mail's forced top-posting. The review reveals an observer deeply attuned to interaction design details, noting how the Calendar's list view solves a longstanding iCal frustration while the auto-correct system impressively knows the word 'fucking.'
key_points: The iPhone prioritizes responsiveness and feel over visual completeness — showing a checkerboard pattern rather than laggy drags, a fundamental design philosophy choice
Apple replaced the clipboard paradigm with purpose-built sharing commands, making email the de facto clipboard for moving data between apps
The Notes app is a cosmetic disaster that breaks the iPhone's otherwise consistent Helvetica UI with ugly Marker Felt on faux legal paper, likely designed assuming a Leopard launch
The app-switching model that eliminates quitting is praised as great design, even though muscle memory makes it hard to adjust to
The crash logs reveal internal framework names like LayerKit (original Core Animation) and app paths like /Applications/MobileMail.app, hinting at the OS architecture
money_quotes: Overall day one impression: the iPhone is 95 percent amazing, 5 percent maddening.
Helvetica has never, ever looked so good on screen.
iPhone prioritizes drag animation over the rendering of the contents; feel over appearance.
The auto-suggest correction system works pretty damn well, considering it hasn't yet had a chance to learn much about what and how I type. Most impressive touch: it knows the word 'fucking'.
"Use it then close it or quit it" isn't the metaphor on the iPhone, however. When you're done with an app, you just tap the Home button.
The iPhone solves this by not having a clipboard at all. No copy, no paste.
In fact, email is the iPhone's de facto clipboard.

The entire iPhone UI is set in one typeface — Helvetica — and it's gorgeous. But Notes, in a lame attempt to be 'friendly', displays a UI that looks like a pad of yellow legal paper, and uses the handwriting-esque Marker Felt as the font for note text.
posts.key_insight: The original iPhone's genius lay in prioritizing feel and responsiveness over feature completeness, making deliberate trade-offs like eliminating the clipboard and the concept of quitting apps in favor of a more direct, tactile interaction model.

posts.summary: Gruber endorses Craig Hockenberry's critique of the new Leopard Dock's changed perspective angle, which makes existing icons look wrong. He notes the irony that while most HIG complaints center on it being outdated, the icon guidelines were actually precise and well-maintained for Mac OS X. The Leopard Dock's new shelf angle breaks carefully designed icons that followed Apple's own documented perspective rules. Gruber argues the change seems arbitrary and thoughtless, and concludes that of the three most important cosmetic UI elements changed in Leopard, only window borders improved while the menu bar and Dock got worse.
key_points: The new Leopard Dock uses a different perspective angle than the documented HIG standard, making existing icons look tilted and wrong
Unlike most of the HIG which is woefully outdated, the icon guidelines were precisely updated for Mac OS X, making this break particularly frustrating
Top-notch icon designers obsess over pixel-level perfection, and Apple's own guidelines encouraged this perfectionism — then Apple broke the assumptions
Of Leopard's three major cosmetic UI changes (menu bar, Dock, window borders), only window borders are an improvement
money_quotes: What's interesting about this case is that most developer complaints about the HIG in recent years boil down to the core problem that it's woefully out of date.

Important new concepts like source lists are hardly mentioned at all; many of its cosmetic guidelines are obviously antiquated compared to what we can all plainly see are Apple's actual Mac OS X UI style guidelines.
Changing this in Leopard just seems arbitrary and thoughtless.
Top-notch icon designers sweat over each and every pixel.
The icon sections in the current HIG take this perfectionism into account; there's an assumption that yes, Macintosh icons really should look perfect.
The three most cosmetically important user interface elements on the Mac are the menu bar, the Dock, and the standard window borders.
All three have changed significantly in Leopard. One — window borders — is improved, but the other two are worse.
posts.key_insight: Apple undermined its own carefully documented icon design standards with the Leopard Dock's arbitrary perspective change, breaking the pixel-perfect work of developers who followed Apple's own rules.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Josh Quittner's reasons for returning his iPhone point by point, exposing the logical inconsistencies in each complaint. Quittner cited Verizon's better coverage, battery life, keyboard issues, lack of Flash, flaky Wi-Fi, and cost as reasons to go back to his Treo. Gruber notes that many of these complaints apply equally or worse to the Treo, such as the lack of Wi-Fi entirely and comparable monthly costs. The piece implies Quittner's return was more performative contrarianism than genuine consumer dissatisfaction.
key_points: Most of Quittner's complaints about the iPhone apply equally or worse to the Treo he's returning to
The battery replacement concern was publicly known before purchase, suggesting the return was premeditated theater
Quittner complains about missing Wi-Fi quality on a device while his Treo has no Wi-Fi at all
The monthly service cost complaint falls apart when Verizon's comparable plans cost the same or more
money_quotes: And this replacing-the-battery issue is something that didn't occur to you until *after* you bought your iPhone?
The Treo's screen needs to be bigger. Neener-neener.
And so Flash works great on your Treo?
How come no one else is complaining about poor Wi-Fi? I assume the Wi-Fi on your Treo is better. No, wait, Treos don't support Wi-Fi at all.
Please — I beg you — be sure to take photos of all the other people at the Apple Store returning their iPhones.
posts.key_insight: Tech pundits returning their iPhones for pageviews often cite complaints that apply equally or worse to the devices they're returning to.

posts.summary: Gruber calls out MSNBC journalist Bob Sullivan for grossly misrepresenting Apple's iPhone battery specifications. Sullivan claimed the iPhone battery would 'only survive about 300-400 recharges,' implying it would be completely dead after that many cycles. Gruber quotes Apple's actual battery page, which states the battery retains up to 80% capacity after 400 full charge cycles. Gruber awards Sullivan 'double jackass points' for both the misrepresentation and for failing to link to the source material.
key_points: Sullivan's report claimed the iPhone battery dies completely after 300-400 charges, when Apple actually says it retains 80% capacity after 400 cycles
The misrepresentation was the entire basis of Sullivan's 1100-word report, not a minor error
Sullivan didn't link to Apple's publicly available battery information page, making the error harder for readers to catch
The comment section showed readers fully believed the false claim, demonstrating the real-world harm of sloppy reporting
money_quotes: So Apple says that after 400 cycles, the battery should hold about 80 percent of its original capacity. But Sullivan reports that it'll be completely dead after "300-400 recharges".
Double jackass points for not even linking to Apple's web site.
It's not like this is a trivial little detail — the idea that iPhone batteries will die after 300 charges is the entire crux of his 1100-word report.
Note that his comment thread is chock full of remarks from readers who completely believe this.
posts.key_insight: Tech journalists who misrepresent easily verifiable specs to manufacture a scare story — and don't even link to the source — deserve to be called out for the misinformation they spread.

posts.summary: Gruber shares AppleScript workarounds he created to fix Apple Mail's default top-posting behavior when replying to emails. Forced to switch to Mail for IMAP support needed by the iPhone, he describes his long-standing dread of the application. The scripts use GUI Scripting to move the insertion point below quoted text, mimicking proper bottom-posting etiquette. He explains how FastScripts lets him override Mail's built-in Reply shortcut with his custom script. He also notes a limitation with signatures and offers a partial workaround.
key_points: The iPhone's requirement for IMAP forced Gruber to switch from Mailsmith to Apple Mail, which he had been dreading
Apple Mail's Reply feature is hard-wired for top-posting, which Gruber considers 'uncouth and illiterate'
The AppleScript workaround uses GUI Scripting to simulate keystrokes that reposition the cursor below the quoted text
FastScripts intercepts keyboard shortcuts before the application, allowing the custom script to override Mail's built-in Reply command
The script has a known limitation with Mail's built-in signature feature, placing the cursor below rather than above the signature
money_quotes: For the email accounts that I want to read on my iPhone, I need IMAP, so I'm switching those accounts to Apple Mail. I've been dreading this for years.
My first must-fix annoyance is that Mail's Reply feature is hard-wired to encourage top-posting, an uncouth and illiterate practice.

FastScripts "sees" the shortcut before the current application does, which means that if a script has the same shortcut as a regular menu item — Command-R in the case of Mail's Reply menu item — the script gets called, not the menu item.
I've long used TextExpander to include email signatures, rather than the built-in signature feature in Mailsmith (or, now, Mail).
posts.key_insight: Apple Mail's insistence on top-posting is a design flaw significant enough to warrant scripting workarounds, reflecting how seriously proper email etiquette matters to power users.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that the real problem with email etiquette isn't whether you top-post or bottom-post, but rather the practice of quoting entire messages in replies. He advocates for selectively quoting only relevant passages and interspersing your remarks between them, much like writing an article. He distinguishes between forwarding (where including the full message makes sense) and replying (where it doesn't). The post frames careful email composition as basic hygiene rather than optional effort.
key_points: The top-posting vs. bottom-posting debate misses the real issue: quoting entire messages is the fundamental problem
Email replies should work like articles — quote only relevant parts with your remarks interspersed
Forwarding and replying are different acts with different quoting conventions
The purpose of quoting is to provide context, not to maintain a complete thread archive in every message
money_quotes: The fundamental source of poor email style is the practice of quoting the entire message you're replying to.
Writing an email is like writing an article. Only quote the relevant parts, interspersing your new remarks between the quoted passages.
The point of quoting isn't to maintain a self-contained copy of the entire thread in each single message; the point is to provide context for your own remarks.
Does it take more time to edit the portions of quoted text included in your reply? Yes. So does spell-checking and proofreading. It also takes time to shower and brush your teeth each day.
The idea that each new reply in a thread ought to contain the entirety of each previous message in the thread is silly, wasteful, distracting, and unnecessary.
posts.key_insight: Good email quoting isn't about where you put your reply — it's about having the discipline to quote only what's relevant, treating email composition with the same care as any other form of writing.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Steve Jobs's admission that Apple is working on OS X-based iPods, arguing this was an inevitable evolution given how the iPhone's iPod app made the existing iPod interface feel obsolete. He highlights Jobs's philosophy that Apple should cannibalize its own products rather than let competitors do it, citing the iPod Mini-to-Nano transition as the defining example. Gruber argues that Apple's willingness to kill hit products while they're still on top is the company's defining characteristic. He dismisses skepticism about OS X iPods cannibalizing iPhone sales, noting Apple's interest is in the best product lineup regardless. The key open question is whether new iPods will be just music/video players or fuller devices with Wi-Fi, email, and web browsing.
key_points: The iPhone's iPod app made the existing iPod UI feel like old news, making OS X-based iPods inevitable rather than surprising
Jobs's statement that if anyone cannibalizes iPod sales it should be Apple itself reveals the company's core strategic philosophy
Apple's willingness to kill the best-selling iPod Mini to introduce the Nano exemplifies how they replace hits while still on top, unlike most companies that milk successful products
Apple treats its product lineup itself as a designed product — obvious, simple, and easy to understand, in contrast to competitors offering 18 music phones
The real question isn't whether OS X iPods are coming, but whether they'll be just media players or everything-but-a-phone devices with Wi-Fi networking
money_quotes: If you've used, or even seen, the iPod app on an iPhone, you know that it's old news. It's impossible to get excited about any "new" iPod that is less than the iPhone iPod app.
Jobs was asked if he was concerned that the iPhone would cannibalize sales of iPods. Jobs replied that if anyone was going to cannibalize iPod sales, it better be Apple itself.
This is the defining characteristic of Apple as a company, today: They replace their hit products while they're still on top.
Rather than building a lead over their competition and sitting on it, they just keep building.
Most companies wouldn't even consider killing a product like the iPod Mini while it was still a best-seller; instead, they milk hit products for all they're worth and ride them out for years.
Apple treats its product line-up like a product itself — it is designed to be obvious and easy to understand.
The simple truth is that the iPhone user experience doesn't just blow away the experience of other companies' cell phones — it blows away the experience of Apple's own iPods.
