posts.key_insight: Apple's defining strategic trait is its willingness to cannibalize its own best-selling products rather than milk them, treating the product lineup itself as a designed artifact that must remain simple and obvious.

posts.summary: Gruber debunks a Roughly Drafted article that claimed iPods have been running Mac OS X for years. The claim, picked up by Slashdot, was based on a 2004 MacTech article that was actually an April Fool's joke about developing QuickTime apps for iPods using a fictional programming language called 'SNOJOB.' Gruber points out that current iPods do not run OS X or anything resembling it, and that the supposed evidence — including a fake 'laf' option for the ls command — was entirely fabricated as a hoax. The piece is a straightforward takedown of tech rumor-mongering built on uncritical sourcing.
key_points: A Roughly Drafted article claimed iPods run Mac OS X with a Mach kernel and Unix userland, and Slashdot amplified the claim
The sole evidence was a 2004 MacTech article that was an April Fool's joke involving a fictional programming language called SNOJOB
Current iPods do not run OS X or anything resembling it — there is no hidden Mach kernel on iPod file systems
The story illustrates how tech journalism can go wrong when writers fail to verify obviously dubious sources
money_quotes: Current iPods do not run OS X, or anything even vaguely resembling it.
There is no programming language called SNOJOB.
There is no secret 'laf' option for the `ls` command-line tool that will reveal a 'hidden' mach kernel on iPod file systems.
This MacTech article was a joke, an unfunny April Fool's hoax.
posts.key_insight: A tech writer built an entire thesis on an April Fool's joke, and the story spread across major outlets because nobody bothered to check the source.

posts.summary: Gruber shares an AppleScript he wrote to automate inbox management in Apple Mail. The script sweeps all read, unflagged messages from each IMAP account's inbox into a corresponding 'Archive' mailbox. He explains his simple mailbox structure and the motivation: keeping inboxes clear of everything except unread and flagged messages. The post includes detailed notes on the script's efficiency, particularly its use of AppleScript's 'whose' clause to filter and move messages en masse rather than one at a time.
key_points: Gruber uses a simple mailbox structure with an 'Archive' mailbox for each IMAP account, sweeping read unflagged messages daily
The AppleScript uses a 'whose' clause to filter messages by criteria and move them in bulk, avoiding slow per-message loops
The script was updated in 2011 to work around a bug in Apple Mail on OS X 10.7.0
The post provides practical guidance on adapting the script for local mailboxes instead of IMAP server-side archives
money_quotes: The point is to make it as easy as possible to keep my inboxes clear of everything other than unread and flagged messages.
This is a nice example of using a whose clause to create a filtered reference to a list of items that meet certain criteria.
Mail's scripting support also allows us to move the entire list of messages en masse.
We don't need to loop through each message in the inboxes one at a time to find the read unflagged ones, and we don't need to move them one at a time to the Archive mailboxes.
It might take a few seconds for Mail to finish moving the messages out of your inboxes.
posts.key_insight: A well-crafted AppleScript using filtered references can efficiently automate inbox management in Apple Mail, moving bulk messages without slow per-message iteration.

posts.summary: Gruber critiques Apple's font selection on the original iPhone, arguing that while the device's 160 PPI display produces gorgeous typography, it's held back by a mere 11 typeface families — several of which are poor choices. He's particularly incensed that Apple included Courier New over Courier, kept both Arial and Helvetica when only Helvetica was needed, and chose Trebuchet and Marker Felt over superior options like Futura, Gill Sans, and Hoefler Text. He argues that even with storage constraints, better curation would have dramatically improved the iPhone's typographic quality. The piece serves as both a technical reference for web developers dealing with iPhone font rendering and a passionate plea for better typographic taste.
key_points: The iPhone ships with only 11 typeface families, fewer than Mac OS 8/9 systems from a decade earlier despite comparable storage and RAM
Including Courier New instead of Courier means all fixed-width text on the iPhone 'looks like complete ass'
Arial and Helvetica are both included despite Arial being a cheap knock-off — Apple should have shipped only Helvetica and substituted silently
Superior Mac OS X typefaces like Futura, Gill Sans, and Hoefler Text are absent while mediocre fonts like Trebuchet and Marker Felt are included
The iPhone's high-resolution 160 PPI display makes type look better than on a Cinema Display, making the poor font selection even more wasteful
money_quotes: But worse than that the list is so short is that some of the fonts Apple did include are so ugly.
Courier New is the only fixed-width font on the iPhone, which means all fixed-width text looks like complete ass.
Arial is a tawdry, inferior knock-off of Helvetica, but which, to the detriment of the world, Microsoft chose to license for Windows simply because it was cheaper.
The overwhelming majority of people would never notice, and those who would notice would be delighted.
To include Trebuchet but not Gill Sans or Futura is like choosing Mario Mendoza for the baseball Hall of Fame and omitting both Mickey Mantle and Willie Mays.
A designer stranded on a desert island with nothing but these three fonts could continue to produce a remarkable range of work.
Type looks noticeably better on my iPhone than it does on my Mac's Cinema Display.
It's a shame this wonderful display is being held back by such a poor selection of installed fonts.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iPhone had the hardware for stunning typography but squandered it with a poorly curated font selection that included mediocre knock-offs while omitting some of the finest typefaces already available in Mac OS X.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles iSuppli's widely-cited claims that Apple earns 55% profit margins on the iPhone, arguing their 'bill of materials' analysis is fundamentally misleading. He notes iSuppli published their cost estimate months before the iPhone shipped, then conveniently confirmed their own guess after teardown. Gruber points out that Apple's actual reported gross margins are 25-36%, far below iSuppli's claims, and that component costs ignore software development, build quality, packaging, shipping, and warranty expenses. He criticizes the press for uncritically parroting iSuppli's estimates as fact, with dozens of stories citing no other source. The $7 line item for iPhone OS X licensing perfectly encapsulates the absurdity of reducing a product to its physical parts.
key_points: iSuppli published iPhone cost estimates in January 2007, months before the device shipped, then claimed vindication after a July teardown — a suspiciously convenient confirmation of their own guesses
Apple's legally-required quarterly financial statements show gross margins of 25-36%, far below the 50%+ margins iSuppli consistently claims for individual products
Bill-of-materials analysis fundamentally ignores software development, build quality, packaging, shipping, and warranty costs — making the resulting 'margin' figures meaningless
Dozens of news outlets parroted iSuppli's estimates as fact without citing any corroborating source or questioning the methodology
The $7 line item for 'Operating System - OSX' reveals the absurdity of trying to reduce the iPhone's value to its physical components
money_quotes: I'm calling bullshit.
In early July, they took some actual iPhones apart, and, surprise surprise, concluded they were pretty much spot-on back in January, with Apple earning margins of 55 percent.
Warwick shifts from citing the figures as an estimate in one sentence to stating them as fact in the next.
We do not know that iPhone margins are 55 percent; what we know is that iSuppli, a company with no relationship to Apple whatsoever, claims Apple's iPhone margins are 55 percent.
The total cost of a product's physical components is not the total cost to produce the finished product.
The most precious iPhone ingredient, for example, is its software.
Try calling Apple and telling them you'd like to license OS X for your upcoming new cell phone, for $7 per unit.
Operating System - OSX: $7.00
posts.key_insight: A product's bill of materials tells you almost nothing about its actual profit margins, and the press's uncritical repetition of iSuppli's component-cost estimates as margin analysis is lazy reporting that fundamentally misunderstands how products are made and valued.

posts.summary: Gruber challenges BusinessWeek's reporting on iPhone component costs, specifically disputing the claim that Apple's gross margins have historically been around 50 percent. Using Apple's own publicly available quarterly financial statements, he shows the actual average gross margin over 15 quarters was 30 percent, and the longer-term average back to 1992 was 27 percent. He argues that subtracting component costs from retail price does not yield gross margin, since assembly, packaging, and shipping costs are significant. He also notes that the iPhone's custom components, like its touch screen, make cost estimates inherently speculative.
key_points: BusinessWeek's claim that Apple's gross margins have historically been around 50% is contradicted by Apple's own public financial statements showing a 15-quarter average of 30%
Component cost estimates from tear-downs don't represent the full cost of production — assembly, packaging, and shipping must be accounted for before calculating margins
The iPhone contains custom, non-commodity components like the Balda touch screen whose costs are largely educated guesses
A reasonable cost breakdown for Apple products is roughly 50% components, 20% assembly/packaging/shipping, and 30% profit
money_quotes: That's a lot of "minus" for "50 percent plus or minus a few points".
My dispute is that the sum of the component costs is significantly less than the cost to produce a product.
Subtracting the component costs from the retail price does not give you the gross margin.
Do Apple products assemble themselves in some sort of Willy Wonka-like magic factory?
So, regardless how reasonable and informed these tear-downs are, they need to be treated by the press as estimates, not facts.
"This screen is like nothing I've ever seen before," says Carey.
posts.key_insight: iPhone tear-down cost analyses are routinely misrepresented by the press, which confuses component costs with total production costs and overstates Apple's margins by ignoring assembly, packaging, and shipping expenses.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a major change to Daring Fireball's RSS feed structure, making the full content of the site — including Linked List items — freely available to all readers instead of restricting it to paying members. He explains two problems that motivated the change: non-members were confused when Linked List posts didn't appear in their feed, and paying members couldn't use the authenticated feeds with popular readers like Google Reader. He frames the open feed as a month-long experiment for August, funded by weekly exclusive sponsorships, with Rogue Amoeba as the debut sponsor. The move represents a significant shift in Daring Fireball's business model from membership-gated content to sponsorship-supported free distribution.
key_points: The free RSS feed previously only contained article headlines and descriptions, excluding Linked List items, causing reader confusion during periods without full articles
HTTP authentication on member feeds was incompatible with popular aggregators like Google Reader, frustrating the paying members the system was meant to serve
The solution opens the full-content feed to everyone for free, funded by exclusive weekly sponsorships rather than membership fees
The star (★) symbol is introduced to visually distinguish full articles from Linked List entries in the unified feed
The change is positioned as benefiting existing members as much as non-members, since it solves the Google Reader compatibility problem
money_quotes: On most weblogs, everything that appears on the web site also appears in the RSS feed; it's reasonable to assume things are supposed to work that way here, too.
The goal is simple: to make it as easy and convenient as possible for everyone who wants to follow Daring Fireball via a feed subscription.
This experiment with an open-to-all full-content feed is as much for the benefit of members as it is for non-members.
By far, the single most common request for the member feeds is to find a way to make them work with Google Reader. This is it.
For now, let's consider this an experiment for the month of August.
posts.key_insight: When the mechanism meant to serve paying customers actually frustrates them, and confuses everyone else, the right move is to tear it down and find a simpler business model that works with the ecosystem rather than against it.

posts.summary: Gruber continues his critique of TheStreet.com columnist Scott Moritz for pushing a fabricated narrative that Apple expected to sell one million iPhones during its opening weekend. Moritz reported a rumor about Apple cutting iPhone production orders, citing a note from Miller Tabak that turned out not to exist. Gruber dismantles the 'one million iPhones' claim by pointing out that Apple sold 270,000 units on the first night and weekend, and stores were nearly sold out by Tuesday — making it logically impossible that Apple expected to sell four times more. He argues that a segment of the business press is determined to frame the iPhone launch as a disappointment despite clear evidence to the contrary.
key_points: Moritz cited a Miller Tabak note about iPhone production cuts that the firm itself said doesn't exist
The 'whisper number' claim that Apple expected to sell one million iPhones opening weekend is mathematically impossible given supply constraints
Apple sold 270,000 iPhones Friday night and Saturday, with most stores sold out by Tuesday — they couldn't have expected 700,000 more sales with no inventory
Gruber identifies a pattern in business press coverage attempting to reframe the iPhone's strong debut as a disappointment
money_quotes: What Moritz published on TheStreet.com was false.
You can argue whether the iPhone's debut was great or really great, but you can't argue that Apple had actually hoped to sell more than twice as many of them as they actually did.
Clearly, though, there's a contingent of the business press attempting to argue just that.
How could Apple possibly have expected or even hoped to sell 700,000 additional iPhones over the opening weekend if they were nearly sold out of them by Tuesday?
Bad news seems even less likely to leak than good news, and Apple rarely leaks these days.
posts.key_insight: A segment of the financial press was manufacturing a narrative of iPhone disappointment by citing fabricated sources and making claims that were mathematically impossible given the actual supply and sales data.

posts.summary: Gruber critiques a Dateline NBC report that argues Apple should track stolen iPods using registration data. He points out the practical and privacy complications of tracking 110 million devices, notes Dateline's misleading framing that competitors don't do 'anything more' than Apple (meaning they do nothing at all), and flags the irony of Dateline praising an Apple patent that could prevent competitors from implementing similar theft-deterrence features.
key_points: Dateline's suggestion that Apple track all 110 million iPods ignores both the enormous engineering effort and the serious privacy implications
Dateline's phrasing that competitors don't do 'anything more than Apple' cleverly obscures the fact that no MP3 player maker tracks stolen devices at all
Apple's patent application for theft-deterrent charging could be used to prevent competitors from implementing similar features, undermining the very goal Dateline celebrates
The ex-partner scenario illustrates how a stolen-device tracking system would immediately become a privacy and harassment nightmare
money_quotes: Would it be possible for Apple to do something about this? Yes. Easy or simple? No.
How would it not be a privacy nightmare? Imagine all the iPods given to ex-boyfriends / ex-girlfriends / ex-spouses that would be reported as "stolen".
By which they mean that none of Apple's competitors in the MP3 player market do anything to track stolen players.

Dateline's approval of which is curious — or at least shortsighted — given that, if approved, Apple could use such a patent to prevent any other gadget maker from implementing theft-deterring device tracking.
Hooray for software patents.
posts.key_insight: Dateline's report on iPod theft inadvertently highlights both the media's tendency to demand simple solutions to complex problems and the perverse incentives of software patents.

posts.summary: Gruber marvels at Apple's stock trajectory, noting the company has reached a $120 billion market cap — nearly rivaling HP and Google, and closing the gap on Microsoft. He contrasts this with five years earlier, when Microsoft's market cap was 33 times Apple's. He then discloses that he owns no Apple stock and no stock in any company he covers, establishing a personal ethics policy modeled on traditional journalism standards. He argues this transparency is essential for reader trust.
key_points: Apple's market cap grew from $6.3 billion to $120 billion in five years, closing the gap with Microsoft dramatically
Gruber publicly discloses he owns no Apple stock or stock in any company he covers, establishing a journalistic conflict-of-interest policy
He argues that blogs can move stock prices, citing Engadget's false iPhone delay report as evidence
He models his disclosure policy on traditional newspaper and magazine standards for financial conflicts of interest
money_quotes: Five years ago, Microsoft's market cap was about 33 times greater than Apple's — $210 billion to $6.3 billion.
Needless to say, this is an interesting trend.
I do not own a single share of Apple Inc.; nor do I own stock in any other individual company I might write about on Daring Fireball.
Good newspapers and magazines prohibit their reporters from owning stock in the companies they write about, and for good reason.

I think it's worth stating this as policy so that you, my readers, can trust that what I write — as well as what I know about but choose not to write — is based solely on what I deem interesting, important, and true.
posts.key_insight: As Apple's stock surge made financial conflicts increasingly relevant for tech commentators, Gruber drew a line between blogging and journalism by voluntarily adopting traditional media's conflict-of-interest standards.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to the unmasking of the 'Fake Steve Jobs' blogger, revealed by The New York Times to be Forbes editor Daniel Lyons. He argues against two wrong reactions: pretending the anonymity can be preserved, and assuming the blog is now over. Gruber contends that Fake Steve's appeal was always rooted in the quality of the writing — scathingly funny and deeply insightful — not the mystery of the author's identity. He draws an analogy to SNL impressions, where the performance matters more than the secret. He suggests Lyons may actually write with more freedom now that he has nothing to lose.
key_points: Two common reactions to the unmasking are both wrong: trying to preserve the fiction of anonymity, and assuming the blog is finished
Fake Steve's appeal was the quality of the writing — funny, insightful, and cleverly metafictional — not the mystery of authorship
Lyons created a sophisticated narrative voice that simultaneously acknowledges being Fake Steve while never fully breaking character as Real Steve
Losing anonymity could actually liberate Lyons to write with even more ferociousness since he has nothing left to protect
money_quotes: Wishing the balloon had never popped is one thing. Trying to unpop it is another.
Knowing who the author is doesn't spoil Fake Steve any more than knowing which cast member is playing the president on Saturday Night Live. It's the performance that counts, not the secret.
That his identity was unknown certainly added a mystique, but it was nothing more than a distraction from the work itself.
Now that his identity has been revealed, he's got nothing to lose.
He might as well just let it fly.
It's not like he can simply go back to conducting boring-ass interviews with Steve Ballmer for Forbes.
posts.key_insight: Great satirical writing derives its power from the quality of the performance, not the mystery of who's behind it, and unmasking the author may actually free them to be bolder.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that the iPhone's lack of text selection and clipboard features, while its most vexing shortcoming, is analogous to the original Macintosh shipping without arrow keys — a deliberate constraint that forced better design solutions. He explains that Apple's decision to use tap-and-drag for scrolling rather than text selection was the right trade-off, since the iPhone is primarily a consumption device. The post explores why adding text selection poses genuinely difficult UI problems given the imprecision of finger input versus a mouse pointer. Gruber offers his own design ideas, including word-wise selection and a dedicated clipboard keyboard button, while acknowledging that no proposed solution yet feels obviously right.
key_points: The missing clipboard is analogous to the original Mac's missing arrow keys — constraints that forced developers to design for the new paradigm rather than falling back on old habits
Apple's decision to use tap-and-drag for scrolling instead of text selection is a worthwhile trade-off because the iPhone is primarily a consumption device, not a creation device
Touch input's imprecision creates a fundamental design conflict: the most natural gesture for both scrolling and selecting is tap-and-drag, but one gesture can only mean one thing
Apple anticipated common copy-paste use cases with direct shortcuts like 'Email Photo' and 'Share' buttons, which are actually more convenient than clipboard operations
Unlike adding arrow keys to the Mac Plus, adding text selection to iPhone poses genuinely unsolved UI design problems with no obvious solution
money_quotes: But sometimes if you can do without something, even something potentially useful, it's worth it to try. Necessity is the mother of invention; constraints lead to creative solutions.

By giving users and developers only a mouse for movement, it forced them to learn the Mac way. The lack of cut/copy/paste on the iPhone likewise forces users, along with Apple's iPhone development team, to do things the iPhone way, rather than the Mac way.
The lack of copy-and-paste on iPhone is only a frustrating irritation, not a fatal flaw. But these direct shortcuts only scale so far; copy-and-paste can be used in infinite different ways.

I primarily use my iPhone for consuming — reading, viewing, listening, looking — far more than I use it for creating. Scrolling is important for reading; selecting is important for writing. Better for the iPhone to optimize for reading.
The genius of the iPhone design is that they've already covered the common side of the 80/20 rule. But without text selection and a clipboard, the uncommon side remains out of bounds.
I've seen dozens of ideas regarding how it could be added, but still not one that jumps out for how it should.
No one has raved about how cool the Palm OS is since the '90s.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone's missing clipboard isn't a mistake but a deliberate constraint that forced Apple to design better direct-action alternatives, though the genuine UI difficulty of adding text selection to a touch device means the feature may take time to get right.

posts.summary: Gruber recaps C4[1], Jonathan Rentzsch's indie Mac developer conference in Chicago. He covers highlights from multiple sessions including Wil Shipley on marketing, Cabel Sasser's inspiring Panic/Coda talk, Daniel Jalkut on acquiring apps, and several technical presentations. The piece culminates with a critical account of Drunkenbatman's panel discussion, where a provocative claim about racial diversity in the Mac community derailed into an embarrassing, fact-free spectacle. Throughout, Gruber celebrates the indie Mac developer ethos of craft, taste, and attention to detail.
key_points: Wil Shipley's marketing philosophy centers on radical transparency and the principle that apps are nothing more than a collection of tiny details
Cabel Sasser and Panic exemplify Apple-like craft, writing an entire custom toolbar to fix a three-pixel visual discrepancy in Coda
Daniel Jalkut argues that indie developers should think of themselves as business people, and that buying and selling apps is an underutilized strategy
Drunkenbatman's racially provocative panel topic was undermined by hyperbole and a complete absence of supporting data, derailing the discussion
The conference showcased a thriving indie Mac ecosystem where small shops and one-person teams are exerting growing influence
money_quotes: This is all your app is: a collection of tiny details.
Don't announce until it can be downloaded. Don't let it be downloaded until it can be bought.
Just three measly pixels, but there was no other way to make it look just right.
The decision exemplifies what makes Panic, to my eyes, the most Apple-like indie Mac developers.
When opinions run as hot as they can regarding an issue such as racial diversity, it's essential to argue with as many facts as possible.
Much like a train, once a discussion like this falls off the rails, it doesn't come back.
Developers who are good at the former and bored by the latter might do well to sell their creations and move on to the next new app.
There's something riveting about a man driven to forge his own advanced tools.
posts.key_insight: The indie Mac developer community thrives on obsessive attention to craft and detail — but provocative discussions without factual grounding only derail the conversation.

posts.summary: Gruber reports on the results of his experiment with a full-content RSS feed supported by weekly sponsorships. Both concerns — sponsor interest and web traffic deflation — proved unfounded, so he's making the format permanent. He also added a second articles-only feed for readers who don't want frequent Linked List items. The post details his experimentation with how Linked List items link in the feed, ultimately keeping the original format where titles point away from Daring Fireball to the linked content, which he considers the better design.
key_points: The full-content sponsored RSS feed experiment succeeded: sponsorships sold briskly and web traffic remained near record highs despite giving everything away in the feed.
Gruber added a second articles-only feed to satisfy readers who wanted less frequent updates without Linked List items.
He tested changing Linked List feed links to point back to Daring Fireball permalinks, but reversed course after receiving far more complaints about the change than the original format.
Gruber argues the Linked List's purpose is to send readers elsewhere, and the feed format should reflect that design intent rather than follow conventions from other sites.
The permalink marker for Linked List items in the feed evolved from '#' to '★' to match the web site convention.
money_quotes: So, let's remove the "experimental" label and make it permanent.
My goal is to maintain a balance between simplicity and reader satisfaction.
Just one feed is as simple as it gets, but I agree there's a need for a less-frenetic articles-only feed.
Within one day I'd received far more complaints about the change than I had gotten about the original format in the course of a week.
The point of the Linked List is to send you away from Daring Fireball to read or look at something elsewhere, and the feed format should reflect that.
I suspect the reason some readers asked for the other style is that it's what they're accustomed to from other feeds. I'm convinced this is a better design, however.
posts.key_insight: A full-content RSS feed supported by sponsorships can sustain a publishing business without cannibalizing web traffic, and feed design should reflect the editorial intent of the content rather than follow generic conventions.

posts.summary: Gruber describes his own email crisis, paralleling James Duncan Davidson's declaration of 'effective email bankruptcy.' After switching from Mailsmith to Mail to use IMAP with his iPhone, he abandoned 15 years of email habits built around the assumption he should respond to most messages. Inspired by Merlin Mann's Inbox Zero philosophy, he adopted a new system where most messages are simply read and archived rather than queued for response. He reframes his old backlog not as bankruptcy but as finally doing what he should have been doing all along. He credits Gmail's archive-centric design as a paradigm shift away from hierarchical mailbox filing.
key_points: Gruber had 6,500 messages (3,500 unread) accumulated over 15 years of largely unchanged email habits since 1992
The core shift was from 'I should respond to most messages' to 'I should read and archive most messages'
Most personal email falls into a middle category that is neither very important nor utterly uninteresting, and the old system let these pile up indefinitely
Gmail deserves credit for breaking the hierarchical filing paradigm in favor of a single archive, which is simpler and more effective at high volume
He doesn't follow Inbox Zero dogmatically (weekly rather than daily) but embraces the core premise that if you're not regularly hitting zero, your system is broken
money_quotes: My old strategy was based on the belief that I ought to respond to most of the messages I receive; my new strategy is that the only thing I should do with most of the messages I receive is read them and archive them.
If you're not reducing your inboxes to zero on a very regular basis, your system is broken.

Under my previous 'system', I let them pile up in my inboxes, under the assumption that some day I'd get around to answering many of them. Under the new system, if I don't respond immediately after reading them, they go right into my archive. Out of sight, out of mind.

I don't consider what I've done to be a declaration of email bankruptcy on those old messages. Rather, in one fell swoop I've done with all those messages what I should have been doing to them all along: archive them.
I very much appreciate the way that Gmail's design completely breaks with the conventions established by all popular mail clients that came before it.

Previously, mailers were optimized for the pattern of filing messages into a hierarchical list of category-based mailboxes. Gmail strongly encourages you to simply throw everything from your inbox into one archive.
That I didn't have an email account during my freshman year of college sure makes me feel ancient.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental email problem isn't volume — it's the outdated assumption that every message deserves a response, and the fix is accepting that most messages only need to be read and archived.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects NBC Universal's response to Apple's announcement that NBC content would be leaving iTunes, focusing on NBC's claim that 'the typical iPod contains a significant amount of illegally downloaded material.' He highlights the weaselly passive voice of 'it is estimated' as a rhetorical trick borrowed from the music industry's playbook of treating customers as criminals. He speculates that NBC's anti-piracy demands likely involved either restricting iPods to DRM-only content or adding spyware to iTunes. He also demolishes NBC's pricing demands by comparing their proposed $5-per-episode iTunes pricing to the far cheaper per-episode cost of DVD box sets.
key_points: NBC's claim about iPods containing 'illegally downloaded material' uses unsourced passive voice to present speculation as fact
NBC's anti-piracy demands likely amounted to either DRM-locking iPods or adding spyware to iTunes, both non-starters for Apple
NBC's desired $5-per-episode pricing would make a full season cost $100 via iTunes versus $40-60 for a DVD box set
The whole episode follows the content industry playbook of assuming customers are criminals and treating them with contempt
money_quotes: Love that passive voice: "it is estimated". Estimated by whom? Based on what evidence?
This is straight out of the music industry's playbook: assume your customers are criminals and treat them with contempt.
Needless to say, it's no wonder Apple told them to go fuck themselves.
At $5 per episode, it would cost about $100 to watch an entire season of a typical show via iTunes.
There's a lot of jackassery to pick apart in this short statement from NBC Universal.
posts.key_insight: NBC's negotiating position combined unsourced piracy fearmongering with pricing demands that were objectively worse than existing DVD box set economics, making Apple's decision to cut them loose an obvious one.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to criticism of his calm reaction to Apple's $200 iPhone price cut just two months after launch. He challenges the notion that the price cut proved Apple was gouging early adopters, arguing that fair pricing is whatever consumers willingly pay in an honest market, not a function of production costs. He distinguishes between something being unexpected and something being unfair. He acknowledges Apple didn't anticipate the backlash, noting the story dominated news coverage far beyond anything else Apple announced that day.
key_points: A fair price is determined by what consumers willingly pay in an honest, competitive market, not by production costs or profit margins
There is an important distinction between a price cut being unexpected and being unfair — the iPhone cut was the former, not the latter
Apple clearly misjudged both the customer backlash and the media frenzy the price cut would generate
The typical early-adopter bargain with Apple is trading sketchy quality control for maximum time before price cuts or obsolescence — this time that bargain broke down unusually fast
money_quotes: A fair price is whatever people are willing to pay in an honest, competitive market, and doesn't necessarily bear any relation to the cost to produce the good or service.
A company is under no moral obligation to limit themselves to some sort of maximum "reasonable" profit in the face of consumer demand that would bear a higher price.
There's a difference, though, between unexpected and unfair.
The downside of early adoption of Apple products is sometimes-sketchy quality control; the upside is that buying early maximizes the time before it's obsoleted or the price is cut.
I don't know, I can imagine quite a bit.
News regarding the price cut — and the reaction from angered iPhone customers — far overshadowed coverage of anything else Apple announced on Wednesday.
posts.key_insight: A dramatic price cut may be unexpected and even strategically unwise, but charging what the market will bear is not unfair — the distinction between surprise and injustice matters.

posts.summary: Gruber connects Apple CFO Peter Oppenheimer's cryptic mention of a 'product transition' during a July 2007 earnings call to the subsequent iPod Touch launch and iPhone price cut. He methodically eliminates other product announcements (Shuffle, iTunes, Nano, Classic) as causes for the projected earnings decline. His central argument is that Apple is moving additional products—particularly the iPod Touch and possibly the entire new iPod lineup—to subscription-based accounting, spreading revenue recognition over 24 months. This accounting shift, rather than simply lower hardware margins, best explains the projected 30% earnings decline Oppenheimer foreshadowed.
key_points: Oppenheimer's enigmatic 'product transition' comment during Apple's Q3 2007 earnings call pointed to something beyond routine hardware updates
The iPod Touch at $299 likely has significantly lower margins than other iPods, given its multi-touch display, faster processor, and Wi-Fi cost only $100 more than the 8GB Nano
Subscription-based accounting—spreading revenue over 24 months to allow free software updates—is the most likely explanation for the projected earnings drop
The iPod Touch's sparse home screen icons strongly suggest it will receive ongoing software updates like the iPhone, necessitating the same accounting treatment
Apple's earnings guidance is notoriously conservative, so the projected decline may not materialize as severely as stated
money_quotes: Oppenheimer said, there will be a 'product transition I can't get into.'
I think what he's saying is that Apple is going to replace an older product which currently has high margins with a new product that, at least at first, will have significantly lower margins.
If you thought there were glaringly empty spots for additional icons on the iPhone home screen, take a look at the iPod Touch's.
The iPod Touch and iPhone are clearly sibling products running variants of the exact same system software; I expect future software updates to appear simultaneously for both products.
It's also possible that it was at $599 that Apple was booking its usual margin, and that at $399, it's now selling iPhones for a far-lower-than-usual margin in order to build market share.
Where by 'hate' I mean 'love'.
I say 'supposed' given that Apple's guidance is notoriously pessimistic; exactly no one will be surprised if they come in far ahead of their 65 cents per share guidance.
posts.key_insight: Apple's projected earnings decline was not primarily about hardware margins but about a deliberate shift to subscription-based accounting for the iPod Touch and potentially the entire iPod line, enabling free software updates at the cost of deferred revenue recognition.

posts.summary: Gruber revisits NYT columnist Joe Nocera, who previously criticized the iPhone's non-replaceable battery, and now finds him complaining bitterly about his buggy, crashy Palm Treo. Nocera dismisses all smartphones as inadequate, calling the iPhone only good for 'music and media' while ignoring its revolutionary web browser. Gruber argues this characterization is forced and contrary to nearly all reviews, particularly the omission of Safari's mobile browsing superiority. He draws a parallel between dismissing the iPhone as a media-only device and the old canard that Macs were only good for graphics. Gruber concludes that Nocera sounds like someone who would love an iPhone but refuses to consider it.
key_points: Nocera's Palm Treo is plagued with crashes, random reboots, and terrible web access, yet he still won't consider the iPhone
Nocera's dismissal of the iPhone as only good for 'music and media' conspicuously ignores its class-leading web browser
Describing the iPhone's phone features as 'lousy' contradicts the consensus of nearly all reviews
The pattern of dismissing iPhones as single-purpose devices mirrors the old myth that Macs were only good for graphics
money_quotes: I'll concede that the iPhone email app could be improved; it's probably the app for which I have the most feature requests.
But it seems forced — and contrary to nearly all reviews — to describe the iPhone's phone features as 'lousy'.
It's rather conspicuous that Nocera neglects to mention the iPhone's web browser, which kicks any other mobile device's ass.
There's a lot to criticize about the iPhone, but overall, it's leagues better than the Treo or Q, and better than BlackBerrys for everything other than email.
iPhones are only good for music and video is the new Macs are only good for graphics and design.
Read the whole column and it seems like Nocera would love an iPhone, but that he's not even willing to consider it. His loss.
posts.key_insight: Dismissing the iPhone as only good for media while suffering through a terrible Treo is the smartphone-era equivalent of the old 'Macs are only for graphics' myth — a bias that ignores the evidence in front of you.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on a footnote from a previous post where he flagged a grammatical error in Joe Nocera's New York Times column about smartphones. The original sentence used the singular 'its' to refer to the plural 'BlackBerrys,' and dozens of readers emailed to argue Gruber was wrong. He carefully parses the sentence to show that 'its' cannot logically refer to 'the phone' as readers claimed, since 'the phone' in context means the phone function of the device, not the device itself. He offers several rewrites that clarify the meaning and fix the grammatical mismatch, arguing the sentence was a mess that needed complete rewriting rather than a simple pronoun fix.
key_points: The singular 'its' in Nocera's sentence grammatically mismatches the plural subject 'BlackBerrys' and should be 'their'
Readers who defended the original usage misread 'the phone' as referring to the whole device, when in context it refers only to the phone function of a BlackBerry
The sentence had multiple problems beyond the pronoun mismatch, including the vague term 'Internet abilities' when email had already been praised separately
Gruber offers two progressively cleaner rewrites that eliminate the ambiguity entirely
Some readers misunderstood Gruber's criticism as an its/it's complaint rather than a singular/plural mismatch
money_quotes: As is often the case, the best part of yesterday's piece was tucked in a footnote.
The sentence as a whole is a mess, and rather than simply fixing the its/their mismatch, should have been rewritten completely.

The problem with the idea that 'its' refers to 'the phone' is that what Nocera means by 'the phone' is not 'the BlackBerry gadget as a whole', but rather 'the portion of a BlackBerry device that serves as a phone'.

If you're still tempted to insist that 'its' refers to 'the phone' in the original sentence, then what does 'the phone' refer to? The plural 'BlackBerrys' in the first independent clause? That's even worse.
My guess is that these readers only saw the 'sic' after the 'its' and didn't bother to read the footnote. And so they probably won't bother reading this footnote, either.
posts.key_insight: Sloppy pronoun reference in published writing isn't just a grammar quibble — it reveals muddled thinking about what the sentence is actually trying to say, and the fix requires rewriting for clarity, not just swapping a word.

posts.summary: Gruber catalogs the numerous problems with Apple's just-launched iTunes ringtone feature, from buggy ringtone-eligibility detection to inconsistent behavior across systems. He finds the error messages weaselly and dishonest, particularly the dialog that deflects when users try to make ringtones from non-iTunes Store tracks. He also dissects the absurd Terms of Service language, which technically prohibits using purchased ringtones as alarm sounds even though Apple's own Clock app encourages exactly that. The piece builds a case that the entire ringtone system is a user-hostile mess driven by music label restrictions and legal overcaution.
key_points: The ringtone eligibility system is broken: songs marked as non-ringtone-able can sometimes be purchased as ringtones anyway, while the eligibility icons don't display correctly across systems
iTunes's error dialogs use deliberately evasive language to avoid admitting that non-iTunes Store tracks are completely locked out of the ringtone feature
A simple file extension renaming trick or free third-party utility bypasses the entire restriction for DRM-free files, making the official system look even more absurd
The Terms of Service contain contradictory restrictions — prohibiting ringtone use outside phone calls while Apple's own Clock app actively offers ringtones as alarm sounds
Charging $1 extra to turn an already-purchased song into a ringtone, then restricting how that ringtone can be used, is preposterous
money_quotes: Look, we know it sucks that only some of the songs in the iTunes Store are available as ringtones. Trust us, we'd love to sell them all to you as ringtones. Don't blame us, blame the dopes at the music labels.
Think about how weaselly this wording is. It's like a politician who's asked a pointed question about topic A, and responds with a non sequitur talking point regarding topic B.
Perhaps whoever wrote the copy for this dialog was simply too ashamed to spell it out: iTunes will not allow you to use any non-iTunes Store audio file with its built-in Create Ringtone feature.
You've paid $1 for the song, you've paid another $1 to turn the song you already paid for into a ringtone, and you're not allowed to use the ringtone in any other context? Preposterous.
Clock actively encourages you to break this rule from the Terms of Service.
Someone needs to tell Apple Legal that it's ringtone, not ring tone.
posts.key_insight: Apple's ringtone feature is a case study in how music industry restrictions produce a user experience riddled with bugs, dishonest UI copy, and Terms of Service that contradict Apple's own software.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's new iTunes ringtone feature is a consumer-hostile racket built on the false premise that ringtones are something distinct from songs. He points out that ringtones are simply digital audio files played in a different context on the same device, and that copyright law (even per the RIAA) does not require separate payment for ringtone use. He criticizes Apple for charging 99 cents extra, limiting ringtones to iTunes Store purchases, and excluding non-Store songs — the first time Apple created a feature exclusive to Store tracks. He contends Apple should have allowed any song to be used as a ringtone for free, using it as a marketing advantage against carriers, rather than participating in an industry racket that breeds customer resentment.
key_points: Ringtones are not a distinct product — they're simply songs played in a different context on the same device, and the distinction is an artificial marketing construct from the pre-smartphone era.
Copyright law does not support charging separately for ringtone use; even the RIAA argued ringtones aren't derivative works.
Apple's ringtone feature is the first iTunes feature that only works with iTunes Store tracks, reversing Apple's tradition of treating all library songs equally.
The music labels are sabotaging their own revenue by refusing to clear all songs for ringtone sale, turning down money from willing customers.
Apple chose short-term ringtone revenue over customer goodwill, when a free ringtone policy could have been a powerful marketing differentiator against carriers.
money_quotes: You don't turn songs into ringtones; you treat them as ringtones.
The whole ringtones racket is predicated on the notion that ringtones are something different than songs. This notion is bullshit.

If you shouldn't have to pay for each device on which you play a song — computer, iPod, Apple TV — then clearly you shouldn't have to pay for each context for playing the same song on the same device.
The distinction between ringtones and songs is an artificial marketing construct.
The recording industry is going down the tubes because people are buying less and less music each year; but here, in iTunes, they're willfully turning down potential sales.
Faced with the choice between doing what's right for customers or charging them money for something they shouldn't need to pay for, Apple chose the latter.
Any business that hinges on your customers 'not knowing any better' is a bad business.
The difference with ringtones isn't legal; it's that there's money to be made.
posts.key_insight: Ringtones are not a separate product but simply songs played in a different context, and Apple's decision to charge for them represents a rare instance of the company choosing a consumer-hostile racket over doing right by its customers.

posts.summary: Gruber catalogs notable details from Apple's iPod Touch Features Guide PDF, comparing it against the iPhone. He highlights hardware differences like the relocated sleep/wake button and lack of volume buttons, software version numbering that implies a shared OS lineage, and feature gaps in both directions — the Touch lacks calendar editing and the headphone clicker, while gaining a debug console for MobileSafari and closed captioning support. The post serves as a close reading of a product manual to surface what Apple chose to include and omit.
key_points: The iPod Touch runs system version 1.1, implying the iPhone shipped with 1.0 of what would become iOS
The Touch lacks hardware volume buttons and the headphone cable clicker, making pocket use significantly less convenient than the iPhone
Several features like the double-tap-space-for-period shortcut, closed captioning, and a Safari debug console appear on the Touch but not yet on the iPhone
The inability to add calendar events on the Touch is a puzzling omission given that contact editing works fine
money_quotes: Presumably version 1.0 was the version of mobile OS X that shipped with the iPhones.
That clicker is my very favorite thing about the iPhone's music player; I think it'll be a pain to use an iPod Touch that's in your pocket.
You really can't add events in the Calendar app on the iPod Touch. Lame.
Double-tap the space bar while typing to enter a period followed by a space. That shortcut isn't available (yet?) on the iPhone.
Optional Debug Console for MobileSafari. The iPhone doesn't (yet?) have this.
posts.key_insight: A careful reading of the iPod Touch manual reveals that Apple was deliberately differentiating the Touch from the iPhone through small hardware and software omissions, even as both devices clearly shared the same underlying OS.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Apple's September 2007 iPod lineup refresh, focusing on the strategic gaps between the iPod Touch and iPhone. He argues that Apple deliberately hobbled the iPod Touch by excluding Mail, Maps, and other iPhone apps to protect iPhone's premium positioning. He notes the iPod Shuffle's stasis suggests no competitive pressure at the low end, finds the new Nano's proportions awkward despite its thinness, and observes the iPod Classic's 160GB marks a new era of carry-your-whole-library convenience. He's particularly sharp on Jobs's keynote sleight of hand in pitching Safari's inclusion to distract from Mail's exclusion, and skeptical of the Starbucks Wi-Fi deal's actual value.
key_points: Apple strategically withheld iPhone apps from the iPod Touch to maintain the iPhone as the superior device, following the principle that it's easier to add features later than remove them
Jobs used keynote misdirection to present Safari's inclusion on the iPod Touch as a bonus feature, drawing attention away from the conspicuous absence of Mail
The iPod Touch lacks the iPhone's headphone clicker, making it impractical as a pocket music player — a fundamental iPod use case
Apple's accounting treatment of iPod Touch sales (not subscription-based like iPhone) signals they may not plan free feature updates
The iTunes Wi-Fi Music Store name is a deliberate statement of independence from mobile carriers, emphasizing Apple controls the storefront
money_quotes: It's easier to lower prices than to raise them; it's easier to add features than to take existing features away.
What a browser needs is a network, but Jobs pitched it as the network needing the browser.

It would have proved disastrous if Apple had shipped the iPod Touch with more of the iPhone's features, then decided it was too good compared to the iPhone, and then tried to take some of them away in a future software update.
160 GB is more storage than many personal computers offer. Fill it up with typical MP3 or AAC audio, and it would take three months to listen to it all.
It's a big 'fuck you' to the mobile network carriers, a way of emphasizing that this store is Apple's, not theirs.
Ooh, free Wi-Fi that I can only use to buy music from you. Thanks!
Not to mention the seemingly spiteful absence of event creation in the Calendar app on the iPod Touch.
There's been a lot less bitching about the Touch not including Mail than I expected — I think that's at least partly attributable to the way Jobs presented the inclusion of Safari.
posts.key_insight: Apple deliberately crippled the iPod Touch relative to the iPhone not out of technical limitation but as strategic product positioning, betting it's safer to withhold features you can add later than to ship too much and face the backlash of taking them away.

posts.summary: Gruber provides instructions for replacing the despised Marker Felt font in the iPhone's Notes app by deleting the font files via SSH access to the jailbroken iPhone's file system. When Marker Felt is removed, the Notes app substitutes Arial, since the text views appear to be WebKit-based and handle missing fonts gracefully. He expresses a preference for Helvetica over Arial but explains why simply renaming Helvetica font files to replace Marker Felt doesn't work, since font names are embedded within the files themselves. The post includes appropriate caveats about backing up and the risks of modifying system files.
key_points: Deleting MarkerFeltThin.ttf from the iPhone's system fonts causes Notes to fall back to Arial as a substitute
iPhone text editing views are likely customized WebKit views, which gracefully handle missing fonts rather than crashing
Font names are embedded within font files themselves, not determined by filenames, so renaming Helvetica files to Marker Felt names doesn't actually change what font renders
The iPhone's arialuni.ttf is a massive 22.2 MB Unicode font, making it risky to delete as a fallback
money_quotes: Let's say you're like me, at least insofar as (a) you have an iPhone; (b) you despise the Marker Felt font used by the iPhone's Notes app; and (c) you've installed the BSD subsystem, which allows you to access the iPhone's underlying file system via SSH or SFTP.
If that's the case, I have a suggestion for how to use (c) to solve problem (b).
I'm fairly certain that Notes's text view is really a customized WebKit view — in fact, I believe that to be the case for all text editing views on mobile OS X.
Font names are embedded within the font files themselves, not just in the file names of the font files.
I suspect the readers who claim it does work are simply confusing Arial and Helvetica.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone's Marker Felt font can be removed from a jailbroken device to improve the Notes app's appearance, revealing that iPhone text views are WebKit-based and handle missing fonts through substitution rather than failure.

posts.summary: Gruber revisits the 2006 controversy over David Maynor and Johnny Ellch's claimed MacBook Wi-Fi vulnerability, which they refused to demonstrate or prove at the time. A year later, Maynor published a paper describing a related vulnerability, but Gruber argues this doesn't retroactively validate the original unproven claims. He contrasts this with Charles Miller's responsible disclosure of an iPhone Safari vulnerability in 2007, where proof was provided upfront and Apple responded constructively. Gruber's core argument is that extraordinary security claims require proof at the time they're made, and providing evidence after the fact doesn't excuse the original refusal.
key_points: Maynor's belated publication of a Wi-Fi vulnerability doesn't prove he had the exploit a year earlier when he first claimed it
The entire Maynor/Ellch controversy stemmed from a single root cause: refusing to provide proof for a serious claim
Charles Miller's iPhone vulnerability disclosure serves as the model for how responsible security research works — prove it when you claim it
Apple responded constructively to Miller's proven exploit with no backlash, demonstrating that the Mac community doesn't reject legitimate security findings
Maynor's published paper still only describes a kernel panic, not the full code injection exploit he originally claimed
money_quotes: Let's say I tell you I have in my pocket a frog that can recite the entire alphabet. You doubt it, and ask me to show you. I refuse.
A year later, I show you a frog who can recite the alphabet. That's certainly something. But it doesn't prove I had the frog in my pocket a year ago.
A serious claim must be backed by proof of some sort.
All I wanted to see was proof; it was more skepticism than criticism.
No backlash. No criticism from the Mac media. No questions regarding the veracity of their claim. Why? Because he provided proof when he made the charge. That's all there is to it.
posts.key_insight: Publishing proof of a security vulnerability a year after making the unsubstantiated claim doesn't retroactively validate the original refusal to provide evidence — serious claims require proof at the time they're made.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews Amazon's new MP3 Store and finds it to be the most credible competitor to Apple's iTunes Store yet. The store offers completely DRM-free music at 256 kbps with strong major label participation and solid Mac software support. He walks through the purchase and download experience, noting the high 'it just works' factor once the Amazon MP3 Downloader helper app is installed. While Amazon's catalog of two million songs is smaller than iTunes' six million, all of Amazon's tracks are DRM-free, potentially giving them the largest DRM-free library. Gruber argues that Amazon's combination of lower prices, no DRM, and good audio quality makes it the obvious choice over DRM-restricted iTunes purchases.
key_points: Amazon MP3 Downloader integrates seamlessly with iTunes and iPods, automatically importing purchased music with no manual steps required
Amazon offers variable pricing that undercuts iTunes, with singles at $0.89-$0.99 and albums starting at $4.99
While Amazon has fewer total songs than iTunes, all two million are DRM-free, likely giving them more DRM-free tracks than iTunes Plus
The Mac download experience is polished but the installer lacks transparency about what it installs, similar to Google Desktop's installer
Gruber sees no reason to buy DRM-restricted iTunes music when the same songs are available DRM-free and cheaper from Amazon
money_quotes: The Amazon MP3 Store looks like no previous iTunes Store rival.
There's a very high 'it just works' factor here.
They've introduced variable pricing to sell music for less, not more, than the iTunes Store.
All of Amazon MP3's songs are DRM-free.
While Amazon can't claim to offer the most songs, they might be able to claim the most DRM-free songs.
I can't see why anyone would buy DRM-restricted music from iTunes that's available from Amazon.
The Amazon MP3 Store is clearly the biggest and best rival to the iTunes Store. It's not a coincidence that they've eschewed DRM completely.
posts.key_insight: Amazon's MP3 Store succeeds as the first real iTunes rival by making a simple bet: DRM-free music at lower prices with a seamless user experience eliminates any reason to buy restricted tracks from iTunes.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier post about replacing the Marker Felt font in the iPhone Notes app, exploring progressively more sophisticated hacking approaches. After dismissing simple file renaming (which doesn't work because font names are internal metadata) and font editing software (overkill), he discovers that the Notes app's CSS rules are hard-coded in the application binary. He walks through a hex-editing hack to replace 'Marker Felt' with 'Helvetica' in the binary, while cautioning that Apple's imminent software update will likely overwrite the change and close jailbreaking methods entirely.
key_points: Font names come from internal file metadata, not filenames, so simply renaming Helvetica to Marker Felt doesn't work
The iPhone Notes app has CSS style rules hard-coded directly in its compiled binary, making it hackable with a hex editor
The hack requires maintaining exact byte length when replacing the font name, using extra spaces to pad 'Helvetica' to match 'Marker Felt'
Gruber predicts the imminent iPhone software update will close existing jailbreak methods, noting the iPod Touch is already locked down
Arial is dismissed as an inferior knock-off of Helvetica, making the default fallback font unacceptable
money_quotes: Everyone knows Arial is an inferior knock-off of Helvetica.
Font names are metadata contained within the files themselves; they don't come from the file names.
Rather than hacking the fonts, why not hack the Notes app?
Binary files are fragile; make a mistake while editing and it's likely, very likely, the application will no longer work properly.
If you have the urge to hack around on your iPhone, you might as well do so now.
If your favorite hex editor isn't Hex Fiend, there might be something wrong with you.
And if you don't know how to transfer a copy of the binary to your Mac, you shouldn't be attempting this hack in the first place.
posts.key_insight: A typographic obsession with replacing Marker Felt leads to the discovery that iPhone app styling is CSS hard-coded in binaries, hackable with a hex editor and careful byte counting.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles the common claim that the iTunes Store is merely a loss-leader for iPod sales, showing Apple earns roughly 30 cents per song on billions of annual sales — serious revenue. He argues Amazon's entry into DRM-free MP3 sales makes perfect sense given Amazon's expertise in high-volume, low-margin retail. The real story is that music labels finally realized the iPod drives iTunes sales (not vice versa), and that only by dropping their DRM requirements could they enable competition with iTunes. Universal and EMI's willingness to sell DRM-free music through Amazon is less about Amazon's technology and more about the labels' belated strategic capitulation.
key_points: Apple keeps ~30 cents per song sold, generating hundreds of millions in annual revenue — the iTunes Store is not a loss-leader
Amazon's core competency in high-volume, low-margin online retail makes it a natural fit for music downloads
The labels' insistence on DRM was the real barrier to iTunes competition — Amazon's store became possible only when Universal and EMI agreed to sell DRM-free
Universal is taking a spiteful approach by offering DRM-free music through Amazon but not through iTunes
Amazon's branding choice of 'MP3' over 'Music' cleverly signals openness, compatibility, and DRM-free downloads
money_quotes: So why is Amazon even bothering with a music download store, given that "everyone knows" the iTunes Store is a loss-leader that Apple offers just to sell more iPods? Because that's bullshit.
In short, while Apple doesn't earn much at all on the sale of a single song, they make up for it in volume.
Well, perhaps Amazon can find a book or something about how to build a successful high-volume online store.
People buy iPods because they love them. If your music doesn't play on iPods, it isn't going to sell.
The hard part for Amazon wasn't building the store. The hard part was getting two of the four major music labels to allow them to do it.
Apple's FairPlay DRM isn't (at least primarily) some sort of lock-in scheme to force people to buy iPods; FairPlay was a requirement stipulated by the labels.
Using "MP3" instead of "music" emphasizes two things: that this is about downloads as opposed to CDs, and it helps establish the openness and compatibility of the music they're selling.
posts.key_insight: The iTunes Store was never a loss-leader — it's a profitable high-volume business, and Amazon's entry was only possible because music labels finally accepted that dropping DRM was the only way to compete with Apple's ecosystem dominance.

posts.summary: Gruber catalogs undocumented new features in iPhone firmware 1.1.1 that weren't mentioned in Apple's official announcements. He highlights press-and-hold accented character input, Bonjour networking support, a JavaScript debug console in MobileSafari, and new text message notification sounds. The post serves as a crowd-sourced discovery list, inviting readers to report additional findings. He notes that some features also apply to the iPod Touch.
key_points: Press-and-hold on keyboard letters now reveals accented character variants via a pop-up menu, a significant usability improvement for multilingual typing
Bonjour/.local networking finally works on iPhone, fixing what Gruber considered a curious omission from the original firmware
A JavaScript debug console for MobileSafari signals Apple taking web development on iPhone more seriously
New text message notification sounds are praised as superior to Mac OS X's sound design
money_quotes: After years of using Bonjour (née Rendezvous), 'it just seems weird to use IP addresses again.'
This was a curious omission from iPhone 1.0.
They're great sounds — iPhone has way better sounds than Mac OS X.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iPhone 1.1.1 update quietly shipped several meaningful usability improvements — accented characters, Bonjour networking, developer tools — that went unmentioned in the official announcements.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews Microsoft's new Zune lineup announced in October 2007, comparing them unfavorably to Apple's iPod range. The new Zunes match iPod pricing and add Wi-Fi syncing, but offer nothing to compete with the flagship 160 GB Classic or iPod Touch. He notes that Microsoft's decision to announce via embargoed press briefing rather than a splashy live event signals low confidence. The post closes with a damning update: even DRM-free songs get wrapped with DRM restrictions when shared between Zunes, undermining their one potentially differentiating feature.
key_points: The new Zunes match iPod prices GB-for-GB with larger screens and Wi-Fi sync, but have no answer to the 160 GB Classic or iPod Touch
Wi-Fi syncing sounds appealing in concept but Microsoft's track record with Wi-Fi sharing on the original Zune gives reason for skepticism
Microsoft's subdued, embargoed announcement strategy undermines any attempt to compete with Apple's event-driven press coverage
Keeping Zune Windows-only hurts only Microsoft, since Apple sells iPods to both Mac and Windows users
DRM-free songs purchased or ripped still get wrapped with sharing restrictions when sent between Zunes, negating a potentially compelling differentiator
money_quotes: Wi-Fi synching is cool, in concept, but the devil is in the details — Wi-Fi song-sharing between devices is a cool idea, too, and we all know how well that feature worked out for Microsoft last year.
It's hard not to think that these are more or less exactly what Apple expected the new Zunes to be.
If you want to compete with the iPod, you've got to compete on all fronts, including press coverage.
In the past, notoriously, Microsoft has made new products Windows-only as a competitive bludgeon, but in the case of the Zune, who is hurt by keeping them Windows-only? Just Microsoft.
Apple can sell iPods to anyone using a Mac or Windows; Microsoft can sell Zunes only to Windows users.
DRM-free songs, including those you ripped yourself, will be wrapped with DRM when shared.
posts.key_insight: The new Zunes represent Microsoft playing catch-up on specs while failing to compete on the things that actually matter — ecosystem, software experience, press strategy, and platform openness.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Rob Griffiths' decision to never update his iPhone past 1.0.2 in order to keep third-party apps. Gruber argues that Apple locked down the iPhone file system primarily to prevent SIM-unlocking that would violate carrier exclusivity contracts, and that blocking third-party apps was a side effect rather than the goal. He pushes back on the claim that Apple sees no upside to third-party development, suggesting they may simply not be ready yet. He concludes that Apple has no obligation to the grassroots developer community it never invited, and that the only real pressure consumers can exert is by not buying iPhones.
key_points: The iPhone 1.1.1 update locked down file system access primarily to prevent SIM-unlocking, with third-party app removal being a consequence rather than the intent.
Apple's silence on future developer access doesn't mean they'll never open the platform — it follows their standard policy of not pre-announcing plans.
The grassroots iPhone developer community was never invited or encouraged by Apple, so claiming Apple 'screwed them over' is absurd.
The only effective way to pressure Apple into opening iPhone development is to stop buying iPhones — money talks.
There are legitimate trade-offs to opening the platform, including the link between third-party app access and SIM-unlocking vulnerabilities.
money_quotes: The third-party hacks were fun to play with, but there isn't a single one that I miss. Not one.
If you think Apple could have simply let that be without breaking the contracts they've signed guaranteeing exclusivity to their carrier partners around the world, you're nuts.
You can argue that Apple should allow it anyway, but you can't argue that that's not a trade-off.
The idea that Apple has screwed over or even somehow been rude to the grassroots iPhone developer community is absurd.
Apple never asked for nor encouraged iPhone software development, and the fact that it happened anyway put Apple under zero obligation to support it.
If 'third-party software development' is one of your requirements for a phone, buying something other than an iPhone is exactly what Apple wants you to do.
There's only one way to pressure Apple into opening up iPhone development, and it isn't by developing underground iPhone apps. It's by not buying iPhones.
Money talks, and right now, there doesn't seem to be a shortage of people willing to give Apple money in exchange for a completely closed iPhone.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iPhone lockdown was a rational business decision to honor carrier contracts, not an attack on developers, and the only leverage consumers have is their wallets.

posts.summary: Gruber criticizes Leopard's new Dock design as the one clearly bad cosmetic change among many improvements. He highlights the visual inconsistencies others have noted — conflicting perspectives and light sources — and how the design looks especially absurd when positioned on the side of the screen. He endorses Paul Kafasis's suggestion that Apple use a different, simpler Dock appearance for side positioning. As a Dock-on-the-side user himself, Gruber argues that vertical real estate is too precious for a bottom Dock, and that side users deserve a serious, non-silly design.
key_points: Leopard's new reflective 'shelf' Dock introduces visual inconsistencies with conflicting perspectives and light sources that violate Apple's own HIG
The Dock design looks especially absurd when positioned on the left or right side of the screen rather than the bottom
Kafasis's suggestion of a different Dock appearance for side positioning would let Apple keep its flashy bottom Dock for marketing while giving serious users a clean alternative
Vertical screen real estate is more valuable than horizontal on modern displays, making bottom Dock placement impractical
money_quotes: One, though, is clearly for the worse, so bad that one wonders how anyone at Apple can think it's a good idea: the Dock.
I'm a Dock-on-the-side man, myself.
Vertical screen real estate is more precious than horizontal on recent-vintage displays.
The bottom has always struck me as a silly place to keep your Dock, so, it seems fair for Apple to give the Dock-on-the-bottom a silly look-and-feel.
But the Dock-on-the-side? That's a serious Dock. And it deserves a serious, non-silly, look-and-feel.
It's a brilliant out for Apple: whoever it is who's in love with the faux-3D perspective, reflective surface, and conflicting light sources can have them.
But just move your Dock over to the side, and the goofiness goes away.
posts.key_insight: Apple should use the simple, clean Dock appearance for side positioning while keeping the flashy shelf Dock at the bottom, giving serious users an escape from gratuitous visual flair.

posts.summary: Gruber responds to Leo Laporte's criticism of Apple's iPhone 1.1.1 update, which removed third-party apps and broke unlocking hacks. He argues that Apple never sold the iPhone as a general-purpose computer, so users who hacked it were operating outside the intended use case. He challenges the characterization that Apple 'deleted' apps, explaining that iPhone updates are clean reinstalls of the entire system. While acknowledging the situation is frustrating, he rejects the framing that Apple acted nefariously, arguing that users who installed the update despite clear warnings bear responsibility for the consequences.
key_points: Apple isn't selling the iPhone as a general-purpose handheld computer, so expecting open-platform behavior is misplaced
iPhone updates are complete clean reinstalls, not targeted deletions of third-party apps — supporting hacked software would require engineering effort Apple never promised
The 1.1.1 update was voluntary and came with explicit warnings about bricking unlocked iPhones, so users who proceeded made an informed choice
Trustworthiness means doing what you say you will do, not doing whatever someone else wishes you would do
The situation is frustrating and arguably shitty, but framing it as Apple attacking its own customers is intellectually dishonest
money_quotes: The answer really is that the iPhone, as of today, isn't for you if you feel you must be able to install and modify software on it.
One of the advantages of a closed system is that it's far easier to create upgrades for.

To argue that Apple should have left third-party apps and hacks installed is to argue that Apple should have expended some amount of engineering resources to support something they neither endorsed nor encouraged in the first place.
If anything, the lesson to be drawn is that Apple is quite trustworthy — iPhone 1.1.1 is and does exactly what Apple has claimed the iPhone is and does.
To be trustworthy is to do what you say you will do; to do whatever someone else wishes you to do is to be obsequious.
Apple didn't kill or damage a single unlocked iPhone. They released a new software update which iPhone users had to agree to install.
It amounts to you killing your cow.
Your humble analogy-stretcher is himself quite enamored with the idea of allowing these clever cows to produce cheese.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iPhone 1.1.1 update wasn't an act of betrayal against hackers — it was Apple doing exactly what it always said it would do, and users who chose to install it on hacked devices bear the consequences of their own informed decision.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses the outcry from iPhone users who felt 'screwed over' when Apple's 1.1.1 update bricked jailbroken and unlocked iPhones. He argues that the fundamental misunderstanding stems from a sense of entitlement: users expect to do unsupported things and still receive support. He draws a distinction between hardware modifications, where constraints are visible and physical, and software modifications, where constraints are invisible and based on policy rather than physics. He concludes that regardless of whether Apple's decisions are rooted in marketing or engineering, the vendor sets the terms of support, and users who hack their devices must accept responsibility for the consequences.
key_points: Users who modified their iPhones felt entitled to continued support despite taking explicitly unsupported actions
Hardware constraints are visible and physical (can't), while software constraints are invisible and policy-based (won't), which is why people resist accepting them
The distinction between can't and won't doesn't change the vendor's right to define what is and isn't supported
Hacking your own device is your right, but expecting the vendor to support your hacks is not
money_quotes: What it boils down to is this: a sense of entitlement that users should be able to do unsupported things and yet still be supported.
With hardware, the constraints are visible. The limitations of what is supported and unsupported are issues of can't.
With software, though, the constraints are invisible and arbitrary. The limitations regarding what is and is not supported are issues of won't rather than can't.
That this support decision is perhaps based on nothing more than marketing rather than being rooted in the laws of physics doesn't change the fact that the decision was made.
The decision as to what is supported and what is not lies with the vendor, not the customer.
The point is that if you hack, you're on your own. You can't do unsupported things and expect to be supported for them just because you think these actions should be supported.
posts.key_insight: The invisible, policy-based nature of software constraints leads people to mistake 'won't support' for 'should support,' but choosing to hack means choosing to be on your own.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles a BusinessWeek article that portrays Universal Music chief Doug Morris's 'Total Music' subscription service as a clever challenge to iTunes. The scheme would have hardware makers or carriers pay a $5/month subscription fee per device, supposedly making music 'essentially free' for consumers. Gruber argues the cost would obviously be passed to consumers as a ~$90 surcharge on devices, making it just a pre-paid subscription service dressed up as innovation. He also catches BusinessWeek falsely implying Universal can't sell DRM-free music through iTunes, when Universal itself chose not to use iTunes Plus. The piece highlights the irony that Total Music would almost certainly require its own DRM while the industry complains about Apple's.
key_points: The $5/month subscription fee would translate to ~$90 added to device prices, not magically 'absorbed' by manufacturers
BusinessWeek falsely implies Universal can't sell DRM-free music on iTunes, when Universal voluntarily chose not to use iTunes Plus
Total Music would almost certainly require its own DRM, making the industry's complaints about Apple's DRM hypocritical
The claimed 'precedent' of Microsoft paying $1 per Zune sold bears no resemblance to a 90-fold increase to $90 per device
The article is essentially hailing Doug Morris as a genius for inventing a pre-paid subscription service
money_quotes: So hardware makers will 'absorb the cost', rather than passing the cost along to consumers. Uh-huh.
Good rule of thumb: the more an article attributes an Apple product or service to Steve Jobs personally, the more likely it's a hatchet job.
That freedom is available to buyers of iTunes songs, in the form of Apple's DRM-free iTunes Plus, which Universal Music has chosen not to sell.
The hypocrisy here is running thick.
Thank you for performing the basic arithmetic for me.
So, apparently, this $90 is going to appear out of thin air.
So, a 90-fold increase has 'precedent' in a one dollar per unit fee. Uh-huh.
So what this boils down to is BusinessWeek hailing Doug Morris as genius for coming up with the idea of a pre-paid subscription service.
