posts.key_insight: Universal Music's 'Total Music' scheme is just a pre-paid subscription service repackaged as innovation, with costs inevitably passed to consumers despite being marketed as free.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that Mac OS X Leopard will ship on October 26 and promotes pre-ordering through his Amazon affiliate links. He notes Amazon matches Apple's pricing with free shipping, and highlights Amazon's pre-order price guarantee, which already resulted in a $20 price drop within a day. The post is a straightforward affiliate pitch with Gruber's characteristic transparency about the kickback he receives.
key_points: Mac OS X Leopard confirmed to ship October 26, 2007, at $129 single or $199 family pack
Amazon matched Apple's price with free shipping and a pre-order price guarantee, quickly dropping the single-license price by $20
Gruber is transparent about earning a 7.5 percent Amazon affiliate kickback on purchases
Macs purchased after October 1 qualify for Leopard at just a $10 shipping fee
money_quotes: So the cat's out of the bag: Mac OS X 10.5, a.k.a. Leopard, will indeed ship on October 26.
I.e., you get a good price and free shipping, and I get about $10.
One day later and Amazon has already cut $20 off the price of the single-license version and $10 off the price of the family pack.
posts.key_insight: A brief, transparent affiliate pitch for Leopard pre-orders that reflects Gruber's direct relationship with his audience and his willingness to be upfront about monetization.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's announcement of a native iPhone SDK is not bad news for iPhone web apps, just as Cocoa wasn't bad news for desktop web apps. He contends that web apps and native apps don't truly compete head-to-head, and that MobileSafari-optimized web development will serve as the 'Visual Basic' of the iPhone platform — easier, more approachable, and accessible to far more developers. He envisions Apple eventually embracing network-based apps as first-class citizens, possibly through a next-generation runtime that out-classes Flash, but acknowledges that day hasn't arrived yet. He closes with a prescient (and ultimately wrong) prediction that the iPod brand will outlast the iPhone brand.
key_points: Web apps and native apps serve fundamentally different purposes and don't compete head-to-head — most good app ideas only make sense one way or the other
MobileSafari web development will be the 'Visual Basic' of the iPhone platform: easier, more approachable, and open to far more developers than native UIKit
Apple will eventually embrace network-based apps as first-class citizens, possibly through a next-generation runtime or simply through WebKit advances and Moore's Law
Jobs's WWDC claim that web apps were a 'sweet' SDK was undermined by the fact that Apple itself didn't use web technologies for any of the iPhone's built-in apps
Gruber predicts the iPod brand will outlast the iPhone brand once ubiquitous wireless IP networking makes carrier-tied phones obsolete
money_quotes: The weird thing about web apps vs. desktop apps is that they don't really compete head-to-head. Most good ideas for apps only make sense one way or the other.
I see MobileSafari-optimized web development as the Visual Basic for the iPhone as a platform. Easier, more approachable, and wide open to everyone.
Apple, someday, is going to be all over 'web apps' as first-class citizens for its computing platforms.
It was never Apple's intention for MobileSafari web apps, here and now, to be as good as native iPhone UIKit apps.
If it were so sweet, then why wasn't Apple using it for the iPhone's built-in apps?
Let the web be the web and let native apps do what native apps do best — the iPhone does both better than any competing platform.
Or maybe just give WebKit and Moore's Law a few more years, and it really will be just HTML/CSS/JavaScript.
posts.key_insight: Web apps and native apps serve complementary rather than competing roles on the iPhone, and the platform's strength lies in excelling at both rather than forcing one to replace the other.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects Steve Jobs's announcement that Apple will release an iPhone SDK in February 2008, analyzing what 'native third party applications' actually means. He argues the SDK will offer real Cocoa/UIKit development in Objective-C rather than Dashboard-style widgets, citing JavaScript performance benchmarks showing function calls are 226 times slower than compiled code. He explores Apple's likely security model, predicting a code-signing and sandboxing system similar to Nokia's approach, and speculates that app distribution will run through an iTunes Store model where Apple takes a cut of sales. The piece raises key questions about which developers will get access, whether apps will need Apple's case-by-case approval, and whether Apple will block apps like VOIP clients that threaten carrier revenue.
key_points: JavaScript performance on the iPhone is far too slow for native-quality apps, with function calls benchmarked at 226x slower than compiled Objective-C
Apple's security concerns are likely more about protecting carrier-sensitive internals like modem firmware than about external malware threats
Apple will probably require code signing tied to paid developer accounts, creating a Nokia-like trust model rather than a fully open platform
App distribution will likely flow through an iTunes Store model, giving Apple approval power and a revenue cut but potentially killing free third-party software
The 'everything runs as root' panic is overblown since even user-level exploits can access all personal data on any platform
money_quotes: Apple punditry is the Kremlinology of the tech world.
Third party developers want access to the same dog food Apple's own iPhone engineers are eating.
JavaScript on the current iPhone just isn't fast enough to provide an iPhone-caliber user experience.

I'm thinking Apple is more concerned about internal security — about having third-party apps limited to a sandbox so that user-installed code has no access to things like, say, the phone network modem's firmware.
Sure, root privileges allow an exploit to do anything, but the most important thing on your system is your personal data, and an exploit doesn't need root privileges to access that.
If you think the reason is laziness or ignorance on the part of the iPhone OS X engineers, you're an idiot.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iPhone SDK delay is less about protecting users from malware and more about building a controlled ecosystem that protects carrier relationships and Apple's business interests through code signing, sandboxing, and centralized app distribution.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's record Q4 2007 financial results, focusing on the Mac selling 2.2 million units—a 34% year-over-year increase with half of retail buyers being new Mac users. He argues there's no single explanation but rather a confluence of favorable factors: the iPod halo effect making people simply consider Macs, the switch to Intel enabling Windows compatibility, Microsoft's stagnation with Vista, Apple's retail stores, nerd switchers influencing friends and family, and surging popularity among college students. He contrasts this with the bleak 1990s when the Mac seemed headed for irrelevance, noting that the current sustained growth represents what Mac enthusiasts had hoped for over two decades.
key_points: The iPod halo effect works not through logical reasoning but by simply making people consider buying a Mac for the first time, overcoming the decades-old problem of consumers never even putting Macs on their shopping list.
The 1990s Mac exodus bottomed out around the Mac OS X era, leaving Apple with a small but fiercely loyal base—and now virtually no one is switching from Mac to Windows.
The rise of the web as the dominant platform neutralized Windows' 90-95% market share advantage, since web apps work on every computer regardless of OS.
Intel Macs with Boot Camp/Parallels/VMware made switching less scary by letting users keep Windows as a safety net, paralleling how Classic eased the OS 9 to OS X transition.
Nerds switching to Mac created an outsized influence because they recommend computers to friends and family, amplifying Apple's growth beyond the nerd demographic itself.
money_quotes: Most people buying a new computer never even considered buying a Mac. It's not that they'd never heard of Apple or didn't know the Mac existed, it's that they somehow assumed Macs were for some nebulous group of 'others', when the truth is that many of these people would be delighted with a Mac.
Do you know of anyone — anyone at all — talking today about switching from the Mac to Windows? I don't.
Windows's market share of 90 or 95 percent pales next to the market share of the web, which is effectively 100 percent.
Microsoft hasn't done anything interesting with Windows since XP.
It was a long wait for Vista and it didn't seem worth it.
Nerds are the people who recommend computers to friends and families; it seems inarguable that there are an awful lot of nerds recommending Macs today who weren't five years ago.
What we're seeing now is what Mac enthusiasts have been hoping to see for 20 years: more people deciding to buy a Mac.
posts.key_insight: The Mac's record-breaking growth in 2007 resulted not from any single factor but from a rare convergence—the iPod halo effect, Intel compatibility, web platform dominance, Microsoft stagnation, and nerd evangelism—that finally broke the two-decade pattern of consumers never even considering a Mac.

posts.summary: Gruber examines Amazon affiliate data showing that 171 out of 579 Daring Fireball readers pre-ordering Mac OS X Leopard chose the five-license family pack over the single-license version. He notes that since Apple doesn't enforce the single-license restriction in code, many buyers are paying more simply out of honesty. He contrasts Apple's trust-based approach with Microsoft's intrusive Windows activation system, expressing gratitude that Apple doesn't tie licenses to hardware identifiers. The post suggests that if DF readers are at all representative, family packs are a significant revenue success for Apple built on customer goodwill.
key_points: Of 579 Leopard pre-orders through DF affiliate links, nearly 30% chose the more expensive family pack despite no technical enforcement of the single-license restriction
Apple's decision not to enforce single-license restrictions in code creates a trust relationship with users that appears to pay off commercially
Microsoft's Windows activation system is presented as the cautionary alternative — intrusive DRM that annoys honest customers
Apple's hardware lock-in (macOS only runs on Macs) reduces piracy risk enough that aggressive license enforcement is unnecessary
money_quotes: What's interesting about this is that the single-computer license isn't enforced in code by the operating system.
Which means many readers are doing the right thing simply because they're honest.
I have no idea if this breakdown is representative of the Mac user base as a whole, but if it's even close, these family packs are a huge success for Apple.
Every time I read stories about the annoyance of dealing with Microsoft's Windows activation, I'm thankful that Apple doesn't do anything like that with Mac OS X.
But they could enforce these licenses with serial-checking code, and they could tie serial numbers to machine-specific hardware identifiers. And I'm thankful they don't.
posts.key_insight: Apple's decision to trust its customers rather than enforce licenses through intrusive activation schemes is rewarded by honest purchasing behavior and customer goodwill.

posts.summary: Gruber announces that Daring Fireball's full-content RSS feed is now free and available to all readers, having previously been restricted to paying members. He explains the feed sponsorship model, which has been successful with strong demand and measurable ROI for sponsors. He addresses concerns that offering full content via RSS might cannibalize web traffic, but reports that page views have remained essentially stable. He theorizes that most RSS subscribers use feeds as notification systems and still visit the actual website to read content.
key_points: The full-content RSS feed was opened to all readers in August 2007, removing the previous paywall requirement
Feed sponsorship has proven effective, with one sponsor tracking $1,500 in direct sales and calling it their biggest business week ever
Despite fears that full-content feeds would kill page views, traffic remained essentially flat after the switch
Gruber theorizes most RSS subscribers use feeds as notifications rather than as their primary reading interface
The non-CPM ad model (The Deck) made page view drops less critical but still worth monitoring
money_quotes: Many people who are subscribed to the RSS feed just use it as a notification system, and when they see something new has been posted, they load the web site.
I enjoy reading actual web sites.

The small segment of readers who don't visit the site anymore probably weren't visiting the site all that frequently before I offered a full feed anyway, because the whole reason they enjoy reading via feeds is that they don't like reading via regular web pages.
I was able to track about $1,500 of sales directly referred from Daring Fireball. Together with other promotions, it was the biggest week ever for my business.

Page views did drop from July to August after I switched to the full feed, but only slightly. So slightly, in fact, that it might have been more because August is generally the slowest month of the year, traffic-wise.
posts.key_insight: Offering full-content RSS feeds doesn't necessarily cannibalize web traffic because most RSS subscribers use feeds as notification systems and still prefer reading on the actual website.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews Mac OS X 10.5 Leopard on its release day, calling it a worthy update with a high ratio of improvements to regressions. He identifies Time Machine as the most significant new feature, arguing that its gimmicky UI is actually a strength because it motivates people to use backup software they'd otherwise ignore. Beyond Time Machine, he praises the accumulated small refinements — Quick Look, unified window chrome, and countless tiny UI improvements — as what truly makes Leopard better. He notes a few missteps like the translucent menu bar, but concludes that Apple's willingness to go back and polish small details is what keeps Mac OS X improving.
key_points: Time Machine's flashy UI is deliberately gimmicky, but that's a feature not a bug — it gets people to actually use backup software, which saves data that would otherwise be lost.
The argument for Time Machine's design isn't that it's more usable, but that more people will use it, and for backup software that distinction matters more than anywhere else.
Quick Look and the unified window chrome are standout smaller features that quickly become indispensable.

Apple's greatest achievement in Leopard is the accumulation of tiny, unglamorous refinements — rewritten UI copy, smarter file renaming defaults — that won't make headlines but cumulatively improve the experience.
The translucent menu bar is called out as a clear misstep that will look dated by the next release.
money_quotes: Making backup software that people can't wait to try, and which, once activated, just automatically kicks in and does its thing on a regular schedule, is like making people want to go ahead and sign up for life insurance.
The argument for Time Machine's game-like UI isn't that it's more usable, but that more people will use it.
There will be data saved that would otherwise have been lost if Time Machine instead sported a more traditional, straightforward visual appearance.
No one ever got hurt by waiting a week or two to install a new OS.
It takes about 30 seconds of playing with Quick Look to wonder how you ever lived without it.
The design of Time Machine is the single most Apple-like thing in Leopard. No way would a UI like this have come from anyone else.
This isn't just slightly better wording, it's completely better. Same exact feature, but someone at Apple took the time to rewrite the UI copy, and it's better for it.

Leopard is chock full of details like this — little things that won't be promoted on the box cover or mentioned in mainstream media reviews, but which, taken cumulatively, epitomize why Mac OS X keeps getting better with each major release.
posts.key_insight: The best measure of a platform's maturity isn't its headline features but the accumulation of tiny, unglamorous refinements that show someone cared enough to go back and get the details right.

posts.summary: Gruber lays out his recommended procedure for installing major Mac OS upgrades, centered on the principle that Murphy's Law demands thorough preparation. He advocates a four-step process: clone your boot volume to an external FireWire drive with SuperDuper, verify the backup boots correctly, physically unplug the backup drive, then run the installer. He pushes back against 'voodoo' advice to do clean installs, arguing that Apple's default upgrade path is the most tested and reliable option. The post is a practical guide wrapped in Gruber's characteristic insistence on doing things the right way.
key_points: Always make a complete bootable backup before any OS upgrade — skipping this step is foolish
Physically unplugging the backup drive during installation eliminates any risk of corruption with absolute certainty
Apple's default upgrade procedure is the most tested path and claims that clean installs are safer are 'voodoo'
Archive and Install is the right choice only for atypical users like those running developer seeds
The paranoid four-step process gives you a guaranteed fallback if anything goes wrong
money_quotes: If you don't back up daily — or at least very regularly — you're foolish. If you don't back up before upgrading your OS, you're really foolish.
The odds of an OS installation corrupting a plugged-in FireWire volume are very small. The odds of an OS installation corrupting a FireWire volume that is not plugged in are zero.

Arguments that there is something mysteriously dangerous or deficient about the default upgrade procedure — and that you should do a clean install instead, followed by tedious hours manually migrating software and data and preferences from your old installation — are voodoo.
Apple's installer engineers spend a ton of time making the default upgrade procedure as convenient as possible.
Most people, of course, skip directly to step 4. And the odds are it'll work out just fine for them. I say, why take a chance?
posts.key_insight: Proper backup discipline before an OS upgrade is non-negotiable, but the upgrade itself should trust Apple's default path rather than succumbing to superstitious clean-install rituals.

posts.summary: Gruber investigates the widespread reports of 'blue screen' failures during Leopard upgrades and traces them to a single cause: old versions of Unsanity's Application Enhancer (APE) left on users' systems. The most insidious vector is Logitech Control Center, which silently installs APE as a dependency without users' knowledge. Gruber argues that APE's installation of software into Apple's explicitly off-limits /System/Library/ hierarchy is a textbook violation of developer guidelines, and that the upgrade failures are not Apple's fault but the predictable consequence of unsupported system modifications. He notes that Apple is unfairly absorbing the bad press and support burden for a problem created entirely by third-party developers who ignored clear rules.
key_points: Every known instance of the Leopard 'blue screen' upgrade failure traces back to old versions of Unsanity's Application Enhancer (APE)
Logitech Control Center silently installed APE as a dependency, meaning most affected users had no idea APE was on their system
APE installs a bundle into /System/Library/, a directory Apple explicitly declares off-limits to third-party developers
The upgrade isn't failing — it's doing exactly what it should, moving existing software forward, including the incompatible APE
Apple bears the reputational damage and support costs for a problem entirely caused by Unsanity and Logitech violating developer guidelines
money_quotes: To reiterate, the evidence as of this writing indicates that every single instance of a 'blue screened' botched Leopard upgrade is attributable to the presence of APE.
It's utterly absurd and completely irresponsible for Logitech to base their mouse software on a completely and utterly unsupported-by-Apple system software modification.
You can't argue that users who don't even realize they have APE installed should have known to check for an updated version of APE before upgrading to Leopard.
This is pretty much the worst case scenario of what can go wrong with an unsupported system modification like APE.
The blue-screened upgrades aren't failing, they're doing exactly what they're supposed to do: moving the existing software from the old system to the new Leopard system software.
It's hard to see what more Apple could do to discourage the installation of software in /System/.

Unsanity and Logitech are responsible for these APE installations that render Leopard upgrades unbootable, but it's Apple that's getting the bad press and the 'I'm stuck at a blue screen' support calls.
posts.key_insight: When third-party developers ignore platform rules and install software into explicitly forbidden system directories, it's the platform vendor — not the rule-breakers — who pays the reputational price when things inevitably break.

posts.summary: Gruber defends Apple's decision to deprioritize Java 6 support in Leopard, arguing that shipping software requires ruthless prioritization. Leopard was already four months late, and Apple's engineers were stretched thin getting it out the door. He points out that unlike Perl, Python, and Ruby, which compile easily on any Unix-like OS, Java requires significant platform-specific engineering effort from Apple — a burden that falls on Sun's design choices. He draws a comparison to the even harder decision to drop 64-bit Carbon support, noting that Carbon underpins major Mac software while Java is largely irrelevant to the Mac platform.
key_points: Shipping software requires prioritization, and prioritizing means cutting things that are less essential
Java requires significant platform-specific engineering from Apple, unlike Perl/Python/Ruby which build out of the box on Unix — that's Sun's problem, not Apple's
The decision to drop 64-bit Carbon was far harder and more consequential than deprioritizing Java, since Carbon underpins major Mac applications
Apple wasn't withholding Java 6 out of spite — they simply allocated engineering resources to higher priorities
No important Mac software depends on Java, making it a straightforward cut
money_quotes: The only way to ship software is to prioritize, and prioritizing means dropping things that are less essential in exchange for things that are more essential.
Shipping is hard.
It's not like Apple is sitting on a top-notch Java-6-for-Mac-OS-X and withholding it out of spite. They simply decided to allocate engineering resources elsewhere.
It's not Apple's responsibility that Java isn't like that — it's Sun's.
Apple made all sorts of compromises in order to ship Leopard.
No important software for the Mac depends on Java.
What should they have done? Delayed Leopard even further? Pulled engineering resources from something that did ship with Leopard for Java?
posts.key_insight: Shipping software demands ruthless prioritization, and Apple's decision to deprioritize Java was an easy call because no important Mac software depends on it — the real hard choice was dropping 64-bit Carbon.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's dominance in technology design is partly a consequence of having no true rival that values design equally. He uses Stephen Fry's Guardian column as a springboard to observe that what appears as Apple fandom is really a hunger for great design that almost no other company satisfies. He draws an analogy to Canon and Nikon pushing each other to excellence, and to luxury car brands competing on design philosophy. He criticizes Microsoft's Zune for copying Apple's products rather than its design culture, and warns that companies dismissing Apple's success as a bubble may have it backwards — the aberration was Apple's weak 1990s, not its current dominance.
key_points: Devotion to Apple is often really devotion to great design, which almost no competitor offers
Canon and Nikon make each other better through rivalry; Apple lacks an equivalent design-focused competitor
Microsoft's Zune failed by copying Apple's products instead of copying Apple's culture and passion for original design
Nintendo is the only other tech company that shares Apple's emphasis on emotional, taste-driven design, but they don't compete directly
Apple itself would benefit from a true design rival, as competition prevents hubris and drives improvement
money_quotes: When you use a device every day, you cannot help, as a human being, but have an emotional relationship with it.
A grey box is not good enough, clunky and ugly is not good enough.
Canon's cameras are better because there's Nikon — and vice-versa.
Apple has no such rival.
If Apple is BMW, who is the Audi pushing them to be more elegant, or the Acura or Lexus pushing them to be more reliable?
Microsoft simply copied Apple's products rather than doing what they should have done — which is copy Apple's culture and passion for original design.
What if it's the other way around, and the aberration was Apple's tepid success in the 1990s?
A Nintendo mobile phone might kick some iPhone ass.
posts.key_insight: Apple's lack of a design-focused rival isn't just bad for consumers — it's bad for Apple, because great design thrives on competition from peers who share the same values.

posts.summary: Gruber examines FileMaker's new personal database app Bento, responding to Daniel Jalkut's speculation that it may have originated as an iWork project before FileMaker claimed it. Gruber offers an alternative theory: Bento may have been considered for iWork but rejected because it overlaps too much with Numbers, which already handles ad-hoc database tasks like event planning and expense tracking. He argues Bento works better as a standalone product precisely because of this overlap. Gruber highlights Bento's most distinctive design choice — it's library-based rather than document-based, meaning users never deal with files, saves, or the file system. He praises this model enthusiastically and notes technical details about Bento's SQLite-backed storage and its surprisingly robust AppleScript support.
key_points: Bento visually resembles iWork apps but may have been rejected from the suite due to significant functional overlap with Numbers
Bento's library-based model, where data lives inside the app window rather than as user-managed files, fundamentally distinguishes it from document-centric iWork apps
Numbers and Bento share overlapping templates and use cases at the basic level of creating and managing lists of records
Bento stores data in an SQLite database bundle rather than a FileMaker-style database, suggesting a clean architectural break from its parent company
Bento ships with serious AppleScript support, while Numbers doesn't even have an AppleScript dictionary
money_quotes: Much of what Numbers can do is really ad-hoc database design and storage.
That they overlap at all, at such a basic level ('I'd like to create a list of records') makes it clear that Bento works better as a standalone product than as a possible fourth iWork member.
Conceptually, Bento stands apart from all three iWork apps in a fundamental way: it is not document-based.
I'm a huge fan of this library-based (rather than document-based) concept for applications.

From the user's perspective, it boils down to creating the illusion that data lives inside the application's main window rather than living as files in the file system that the user must name, locate, remember, and manage by hand.
Opening the exported file in Microsoft Excel is the primary goal of exporting CSV, as the file format is centered around the behavior application.
posts.key_insight: Bento's library-based rather than document-based architecture represents a superior model for personal data management, freeing users from the burden of file system housekeeping.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Amazon's Kindle is destined to flop because its DRM-locked e-book format represents an unacceptable bargain for consumers. He contends that Kindle books can't be shared, printed, or read on non-Kindle hardware, making them inferior to physical books in every dimension except convenience. He draws an unfavorable comparison to the iPod, arguing that Kindle actually is the closed ecosystem that critics wrongly accused the iPod of being. He suggests Amazon should bundle digital copies with physical book purchases rather than positioning e-books as replacements.
key_points: Kindle's DRM is more restrictive than iTunes' audio DRM because there's no escape hatch — you can't burn to a DRM-free format or share with a limited circle
Kindle is what critics falsely accused the iPod of being: a device that truly locks you into a single provider for both hardware and content
Amazon should bundle Kindle e-books with paper book purchases rather than positioning them as replacements
The long-term preservation of DRM-protected e-books is entirely at Amazon's discretion, unlike physical books that outlast their owners
DRM-protected content sells best when it's ephemeral and cheap, like TV episodes, not when it's meant to be a permanent library
money_quotes: After chewing it over all day, I've concluded that Amazon's Kindle is going to flop. Or at least I hope it does.
Barring physical catastrophe, I expect that the real books I own — the ones printed on paper — will remain in good condition long after I am dead.
"Unshareable books" sounds downright oppressive to my ears.
Without Amazon's DRM-protected content, a Kindle is the world's worst handheld computer.
Kindle actually is what ignorant critics have claimed regarding the iPod: a device designed to lock you in to a single provider of both hardware and digital content.

You pay for downloadable books that can't be printed, can't be shared, and can't be displayed on any device other than Amazon's own $400 reader — and whether they're readable at all in the future is solely at Amazon's discretion. That's no way to build a library.
Change the pitch from "buy digital e-books instead of paper books" to "get a digital Kindle e-book with each paper book you buy from Amazon".
posts.key_insight: Kindle's DRM makes it the closed ecosystem critics wrongly accused the iPod of being — a device that locks you into a single provider with no escape hatch and no guarantee your purchases will remain accessible.

posts.summary: Gruber corrects his earlier speculation that the iPhone used sub-pixel anti-aliasing for text rendering, revealing through screenshot analysis that it only uses standard anti-aliasing. He explains the difference between sub-pixel and standard anti-aliasing, noting that sub-pixel rendering is a hack exploiting LCD subpixel structure. The real reason iPhone text looks so good is simply pixel density — 160 ppi versus 100-133 ppi on Macs. He references Sven-S. Porst's conclusion that sub-pixel anti-aliasing is a temporary hack that will become unnecessary as display resolutions increase. The iPhone's heavy use of Core Animation may also explain why sub-pixel rendering isn't used.
key_points: The iPhone uses only standard anti-aliasing, not sub-pixel anti-aliasing, contrary to Gruber's earlier speculation
Sub-pixel anti-aliasing is a visual trick exploiting LCD subpixel layout, using colored rather than gray pixels for smoothing
The iPhone's superior text rendering comes from pixel density (160 ppi) rather than clever anti-aliasing techniques
Mac OS X itself falls back to standard anti-aliasing in many contexts including Dashboard widgets, Leopard's menu bar, and Core Animation views
Sub-pixel anti-aliasing is likely a transitional hack that higher-resolution displays will eventually make obsolete
money_quotes: My erroneous guess that the iPhone was using sub-pixel anti-aliasing was based on the fact that on-screen text rendered on the iPhone looks so damn good.
What makes text look so good on the iPhone compared to a Mac is simply the density of the pixels on its screen.
That's apparently the point where standard anti-aliasing — simpler and easier to compute and render — is sufficient to produce good-looking on-screen type.
He concludes that sub-pixel anti-aliasing is, ultimately, a clever hack that will eventually fall by the wayside as display resolutions improve. I suspect he's right.
When you look at an iPhone screenshot rendered at full-size on a Mac, there's nothing special about the text rendering.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone's excellent text rendering comes not from clever anti-aliasing tricks but from raw pixel density, suggesting that sub-pixel anti-aliasing is a transitional hack that higher-resolution displays will render obsolete.

posts.summary: Gruber documents an undocumented feature in Leopard Mail: support for 'message:' URLs that allow linking to individual email messages from other applications. He explains the URL structure based on RFC 2822 Message-ID headers, tests which formats work best across Cocoa apps, and finds that the double-slash format is superior because Cocoa's URL parser recognizes it in text fields. He demonstrates drag-and-drop integration with apps like Yojimbo, VoodooPad, and DragThing, and provides an AppleScript to copy message URLs to the clipboard. The feature, while undocumented by Apple, represents a meaningful step toward making email messages addressable resources within the Mac ecosystem.
key_points: Leopard Mail registers as handler for 'message:' URLs using RFC 2822 Message-ID headers, but Apple hasn't documented this feature anywhere
The double-slash format (message://) is superior because Cocoa's NSTextView URL parser recognizes it for Control-click actions, while the slash-less format is not recognized
Drag-and-drop from Mail works with Yojimbo, VoodooPad, DragThing, TextEdit, and iCal, but only one message at a time
Only spam messages tend to lack valid Message-ID headers, making the feature reliable for legitimate email
Gruber provides a practical AppleScript that copies message URLs for selected messages to the clipboard
money_quotes: That Mail now supports these URLs does not seem to be documented by Apple anywhere, but it's fairly simple, and very useful. It's one of my favorite new features in Leopard Mail.
Every message-id should be universally unique, and every message should have a message-id. In my testing, the only messages I could find that didn't have Message-ID headers were spam.

What's essential to note about this is that you don't have to select the entire URL first — you can just Control-click anywhere in the text of the "message:" URL and the entire URL will be selected for you.
In short, it seems to me that Cocoa's URL guessing parser assumes that URLs contain slashes after the colon.
It may sound trivial, but it's one of the things I most missed about Mailsmith when I switched to Mail on Tiger back in June.
posts.key_insight: Leopard Mail's undocumented 'message:' URL scheme turns individual emails into addressable, linkable resources across Mac applications, and the double-slash format works best because it plays nicely with Cocoa's built-in URL recognition.

posts.summary: Gruber systematically dismantles a Fast Company cover story by Adam Penenberg that portrays Apple as a company on the brink of decline in 2008. He goes through the article's claims point by point, showing that its assertions about competitive threats from Nokia, Toshiba, SanDisk, and others are either factually wrong, misleading, or internally contradictory. Gruber highlights how the piece repeatedly attributes all Apple decisions to Steve Jobs personally, ignores Apple's existing openness (iTunes Plus, Windows compatibility, Boot Camp), and draws absurd conclusions — like arguing Apple is threatened by customers wanting devices that work together, which is exactly Apple's strategy. The piece's invocation of Einstein to predict Apple's doom via 'closed systems' receives particular ridicule.
key_points: The Fast Company piece is riddled with factual errors: Microsoft's Zune costs the same as iPods, Toshiba's top MP3 player ranks #97 on Amazon, and SanDisk's success comes at the expense of other manufacturers, not Apple
The article ignores Apple's existing openness: iTunes Plus DRM-free tracks, iPod/iPhone Windows compatibility, and Boot Camp — undermining its 'closed system' thesis
The piece contradicts itself by arguing customers want devices that work together while ignoring that this is precisely Apple's product strategy
Attributing every corporate decision to Steve Jobs personally is a reliable indicator of lazy tech journalism
The article's competitive threats (touch-screen phones from Samsung and Motorola, HP laptops with better speakers) are laughably weak evidence of Apple's impending decline
money_quotes: A good rule of thumb, by the way, is that the more a writer attributes the actions of Apple, an enormous corporation with thousands of talented employees, to Steve Jobs, who is just one man and neither an engineer nor a designer, the more likely the writer is an idiot, a hack, or both.
Empirical evidence indicates that Apple's iPod franchise is doing better than ever.
There's a lot of stupid packed into the above 13-word sentence.
Well, if Einstein predicted Apple's business is doomed, it must be so, because we can all agree Einstein was one smart dude.
Chevy already sells a sedan with a V8 engine. So does Ford.
Sure would be swell if iTunes ran on Windows, and if iPods and iPhones could work with PCs, and if Macs could dual-boot into other PC operating systems now that they're using Intel processors.
Apple products are popular because they're good, rather than popular because they're 'trendy'.
posts.key_insight: Lazy tech journalism about Apple follows a predictable formula: attribute everything to Jobs, cite impressive-sounding but irrelevant competitors, ignore facts that contradict the narrative, and dress it all up in epic language to manufacture a crisis that doesn't exist.

posts.summary: Gruber catches Reuters reporter Gina Keating citing unnamed 'media industry experts' who called Walmart's movie download service a 'game changer,' then traces the quote back to a single source: Rob Enderle. He points out that Enderle has a track record of being spectacularly wrong about technology predictions, yet major media reporters continue to treat him as a credible analyst. Gruber argues that Enderle's persistent wrongness should disqualify him as an expert source, and that reporters who rely on him are simply cutting corners. The post uses the Walmart download service's quick demise as further evidence of Enderle's unreliability.
key_points: Keating's Reuters article attributed the 'game changer' label to plural 'media industry experts' when it actually came from a single source: Rob Enderle
Enderle predicted Walmart's download service would trigger 'a long decline' for DVD sales, but the service was canceled in under a year
Gruber argues Enderle's consistent wrongness should disqualify him from being cited as an expert, yet major reporters keep quoting him
The real problem isn't Enderle himself but the lazy journalism that treats him as a credible source despite his track record
money_quotes: If being completely wrong all the time doesn't disqualify Enderle as an expert, what would?
If Enderle were just another crackpot with an obscure weblog or column, I'd just sweep him into the 'pay no mind' file.

The only way it would be worthwhile for tech reporters to continue to press Enderle for quotes would be if they were willing to describe him as 'almost always utterly wrong', thus letting readers know that the opposite of what he claims is probably the case.
Enderle's presence is a warning sign. I see a quote from him I get the message. The reporter is out of ideas and has decided to cut corners.
The reason it's worth calling him out, repeatedly, is that he continues to hold sway with major media tech reporters.
posts.key_insight: When reporters cite perpetually wrong analysts as 'experts,' the real failure isn't the analyst's incompetence but the journalist's laziness in not vetting their sources' track records.

posts.summary: Gruber recounts seeing a young boy with two artificial legs running happily at Disney World, marveling at the technology that made it possible. He notes that the prosthetics were so advanced the boy could run with only a slight limp. Gruber reflects that this technology simply didn't exist a generation ago. He uses the moment as a counterpoint to the despair that comes from focusing solely on current events, arguing that science and progress steadily make the world better. The post serves as an optimistic New Year's message.
key_points: A young boy with bilateral prosthetic legs was running freely at Disney World, nearly indistinguishable from other children
The prosthetic technology enabling this didn't exist one generation ago, illustrating the pace of progress
Focusing only on current events makes it easy to despair, but the broader arc of science bends toward improvement
The post uses a single vivid observation to make a larger philosophical point about human progress
money_quotes: If he had been wearing pants instead of shorts, you'd have simply thought he had a bit of a limp.

He was born without legs, but yet there he was, galloping across the playground on a warm, sunny December morning, every bit as happy, excited, and carefree as every little kid in the world ought to be.
His legs — sleek, lightweight, and impressively dextrous — were inspiring and beautiful.
Focus solely on current events and it's all too easy to despair at the state of the world.
But science and progress march ever forward, and the world is a better place today than it used to be.
posts.key_insight: A single moment of a child running on prosthetic legs that didn't exist a generation ago is a powerful reminder that science and progress quietly make the world better, even when current events suggest otherwise.

posts.summary: Gruber examines Bruce Tog Tognazzini's famous claim that the mouse is faster than the keyboard, based on Apple's late-80s user testing. The research found that while users perceive keyboard shortcuts as faster, stopwatch measurements show mousing is actually quicker because users experience a hidden cognitive delay recalling which keys to press. Gruber accepts the core finding but argues Tog's argument has important exceptions: two-handed editing operations and, most significantly, repetitive actions. With repetitive tasks, the recall penalty is paid only once, making keyboard shortcuts a clear winner. He concludes with a practical design guideline: commands that are likely to be invoked several times in quick succession deserve keyboard shortcuts, even if they're used infrequently overall.
key_points: Apple's user testing showed mousing is measurably faster than keyboarding, but users perceive the opposite due to cognitive amnesia during shortcut recall
Keyboard shortcuts win for repetitive actions because the recall penalty is only paid once, then amortized across multiple invocations
Two-handed input (selecting with mouse, acting with keyboard) is another genuine exception where shortcuts outperform mousing
Commands that tend to be invoked several times in short order deserve keyboard shortcuts, even if used infrequently overall
The 20-year-old research likely still holds, and mousing times may have actually improved as mouse familiarity has become universal
money_quotes: Test subjects consistently report that keyboarding is faster than mousing. The stopwatch consistently proves mousing is faster than keyboarding.
It takes two seconds to decide upon which special-function key to press. Deciding among abstract symbols is a high-level cognitive function.
The time-slice spent making the decision simply ceases to exist.

While the keyboard users have been engaged in a process so fascinating that they have experienced amnesia, the mouse users have been so disengaged that they have been able to continue thinking about the task they are trying to accomplish.
This nugget has been controversial ever since Tog first published it in 1989, mainly, I think, because it's just so hard to accept that our perception could be so wrong.
The key to understanding Tog's argument about why the mouse is faster is that there's a hidden delay where you stop to recall the actual keys to press for a shortcut.
Even if you waste two seconds remembering that the shortcut for Find Next is Command-G, you save time (versus using the mouse) if you invoke it several times in a row.

It's obvious that commands that are used frequently should get shortcuts, but so too should commands that, even if they're used infrequently, are likely to be invoked several times in short order when they are used.
posts.key_insight: Keyboard shortcuts beat the mouse for repetitive actions because the cognitive cost of recalling the shortcut is paid only once, making them a clear efficiency win whenever a command is invoked several times in quick succession.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses reader concerns about accepting advertising from MacHeist, a company he had previously criticized, and from Microsoft via The Deck ad network. He argues that advertisements are not endorsements and that editorial independence means accepting ads from companies he's criticized or whose products compete with Apple. He reaffirms that sponsorships don't influence his writing and notes he personally approves all feed sponsors. The post establishes a clear principle: the separation of advertising and editorial content works both ways.
key_points: Gruber distinguishes between endorsements and advertisements, arguing that accepting an ad from a company doesn't imply approval of their products or practices
He maintains that his critical coverage of MacHeist's developer deals from 2006 stands regardless of their sponsorship, and future coverage won't be affected
He personally approves all RSS feed sponsorships, rejecting the notion they're automated or unvetted
Microsoft ads appearing via The Deck network aren't targeted at DF specifically, but even if they were, editorial independence from advertising works both ways
money_quotes: Advertisements aren't endorsements. They're promotional messages from the sponsor, not me.
In short, an ad is an ad, and what I write is what I write.
The idea that editorial content is independent of advertising content works both ways.
There are certainly products and services for which I wouldn't accept a sponsorship, for various reasons.
If anything, though, I would hope that my reporting from last year helped the developers in this year's bundle negotiate better deals.
posts.key_insight: Editorial independence from advertising is a two-way principle: ads don't influence coverage, and critical coverage doesn't preclude accepting ads.

posts.summary: Gruber previews the upcoming 2008 Macworld Expo keynote by examining Apple's culture of secrecy and what it reveals about their announcements. He compares Apple punditry to Cold War Kremlinology, where silence and subtle signals matter more than official statements. He argues Apple engineers don't leak because they genuinely love their jobs and have no comparable alternative employer. While noting that Apple occasionally manages expectations behind the scenes when speculation runs too hot, he observes that this year's pre-Expo silence is notable but lacks the singular focus that preceded the iPhone announcement. He predicts a 'medium' keynote — interesting but not historic.
key_points: Apple's secrecy works because engineers love their jobs and have no comparable alternative employer, not because of draconian enforcement
When Apple goes silent both officially and privately in the face of intense speculation, that's the strongest signal they have something big
The massive free publicity from pre-keynote speculation can backfire if announcements don't meet inflated expectations
Apple quietly manages expectations behind the scenes when speculation outpaces what they plan to deliver
The early release of new Mac Pros the week before Macworld suggests a full keynote plate, but the lack of focused speculation points to a 'medium' event
money_quotes: One thing that makes being an Apple pundit fun is that it's akin to Cold-War era Kremlinology — to predict or analyze an opaque, secretive organization, you've got to read between the lines of the few things they do say, and you've got to know how to interpret silence.

Apple engineers really like their jobs. If you want to work for a company like Apple, on products like those that Apple makes, you've pretty much got to work for Apple, because there are no similar companies.
That's 200 engineers who knew specific details about the iPhone, not a single one of whom leaked to the press.
When, in the face of white-hot speculation, Apple goes totally silent both officially and privately, that's when they have something big.

While everyone marvels at the massive amount of 'free' publicity that Apple garners during the 'let's speculate about what they're going to announce' pre-keynote prelude, that publicity and speculation does Apple no good and even hurts them if what they're actually prepared to announce doesn't meet the level of speculation.

In a mild/medium/hot scale, where mild is a lame keynote that's mostly a 'state of the Apple Union' address and hot is a major new product along the lines of the iPhone, my gut feeling is that we're looking at a medium — spicy enough to be enjoyable, but not one for the ages.
posts.key_insight: Apple's secrecy culture succeeds not through enforcement but because its engineers have no comparable employer, and the art of reading Apple lies in interpreting their silence rather than their statements.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects the updated iTunes Store Terms of Service accompanying Apple's new movie rental feature, running a diff on the old and new versions to identify significant changes. He finds most changes unremarkable but zeroes in on two notable limitations: HD movies being restricted to Apple TV only (likely due to HDCP piracy concerns), and the 24-hour viewing window after pressing play. While he praises the overall rental offering — pricing, quality options, and Apple TV shopping UI — he argues the 24-hour window is unnecessarily stingy and out of step with the flexibility of traditional VHS and DVD rentals.
key_points: HD rental movies are restricted to Apple TV only, likely because it supports HDCP DRM output, not because of file size concerns
Movies rented on Apple TV cannot be moved to other devices, while Mac-rented movies can be transferred
The 24-hour viewing window after first pressing play is far too restrictive compared to the generous 30-day download window
The viewing window limitation has no plausible piracy justification since VHS and DVD rentals always allowed multiple viewings over several days
Despite the viewing window complaint, Gruber considers the overall rental offering strong with only this one real sour spot
money_quotes: The 30-day period is fine, but the 24-hour viewing window is far too miserly.

Everything else about these movie rentals seems terrific — the DVD and HD quality options, the prices, the shopping UI on the Apple TV — but 24 hours after you hit play the first time just doesn't seem fair.
You go to bed, and now you're sort of stuck: you've got to finish the movie the next night before 10 pm or your carriage turns back into a pumpkin.
I just don't get why it's so miserly. What skin would it be off the studios' or Apple's back if they gave you 48 or 72 hours?
Who cares if a two- or three-day window would allow you to watch the entire movie multiple times? That's something you've always been able to do with VHS and DVD rentals.
I don't think it's a deal-breaker, but this 24-hour window is the only real sour spot in the whole deal.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iTunes movie rental service gets nearly everything right, but the 24-hour viewing window is an unjustifiably stingy limitation that has no real piracy rationale and punishes normal viewing habits.

posts.summary: Gruber provides a detailed comparison of the newly announced MacBook Air against the standard MacBook and MacBook Pro. He methodically lists the Air's advantages (thinness, weight, LED display, backlit keyboard, multi-touch trackpad) and disadvantages (slower CPU, less storage, limited RAM, no optical drive, sealed battery, fewer ports). He concludes the Air is clearly designed as a secondary machine rather than a primary computer. He personally would have preferred a smaller footprint akin to the 12-inch PowerBook G4, and predicts the Air will be a hit, though perhaps not a huge one.
key_points: The MacBook Air's main appeal is size and weight (3 lbs vs 5 lbs), but it trades off significantly in performance, storage, and expandability
The Air's sealed battery will draw heavy press criticism but most users won't care — mirroring the iPhone battery controversy
The lack of FireWire, single USB port, and no optical drive firmly position the Air as a secondary machine alongside a desktop Mac
Gruber would have preferred a smaller 12-inch footprint rather than a thinner version of the same 13.3-inch form factor
The 2 GB RAM ceiling is a meaningful limitation — swapping to disk when memory is insufficient will seriously hurt performance
money_quotes: Clearly, the main appeal of the MacBook Air is its size and weight, and the main trade-off is performance and storage capacity.
The Air is almost half the weight of a 15-inch MacBook Pro (5.4 lbs.) For many people, that's all she wrote, and on this basis alone they've already pre-ordered an Air.
Swapping sucks.

I think this aspect is going to play out exactly as it has with the iPhone: (a) there's going to be a ton of criticism from the gadget and tech press decrying this as a terrible decision; and (b) the vast majority of users don't care and won't mind the sealed battery at all.
Seriously, the press is not going to shut up about this — you can't go 10 feet in the Expo media room without hearing someone complain about it.
I'd have rather seen a smaller footprint, a la the old 12-inch PowerBook G4 — something just exactly as wide as a full keyboard.
The MacBook Air is undeniably beautiful and clever, but clearly designed as a secondary machine, not a main machine.
posts.key_insight: The MacBook Air is an undeniably beautiful secondary machine, but its trade-offs in performance, storage, and expandability make it a poor choice as anyone's sole computer.

posts.summary: Gruber offers a wide-ranging roundup of observations from the 2008 Macworld Expo keynote. He notes Apple's conspicuous snubbing of Microsoft's Office 2008 demo, praises Time Capsule while questioning whether Apple killed third-party backup competition, and examines the curious $20 iPod Touch upgrade fee through the lens of Sarbanes-Oxley accounting. He covers Apple TV 2.0's content challenges, the MacBook Air's novel multi-touch trackpad gestures, and Randy Newman's provocative anti-Bush performance. Throughout, he balances genuine enthusiasm for Apple's products with pointed skepticism about some of their business decisions.
key_points: Apple deliberately downplayed Microsoft Office 2008 at the keynote, reflecting growing confidence and competitive tension with iWork
Time Capsule is a great product, but Apple may have killed third-party AirPort disk backup support to eliminate competition — a move Gruber calls 'shitty, pure and simple' if true
The $20 iPod Touch upgrade fee appears to be an opportunistic charge rather than a strict Sarbanes-Oxley necessity, especially since the update is only 9 kilobytes
Apple is taking a Microsoft-like dogged approach to Apple TV, iterating through versions despite early failures, with content licensing being the real constraint
The MacBook Air's multi-touch trackpad gestures are promising but limited by the lack of third-party app support at launch
money_quotes: I think you can really make an argument that Time Machine is the most important feature Apple has added to Mac OS X in years, maybe ever, and support for doing it over the network makes it better.
But if Apple has pulled support for hard drive/base station backups to eliminate Time Capsule competition, that's shitty, pure and simple.
I'm left with the feeling that they're charging $20 for the iPod Touch upgrade simply because they can.
Apparently the apps are already there on the 1.1.3 Touch OS, because the upgrade itself weighs in at just 9 kilobytes.
The big problem Apple faces with Apple TV isn't technical — it's content. They're constrained by what the TV networks and movie studios will allow them to do.
It's certainly hard to imagine any other major corporation in the U.S. that would invite Randy Newman on stage to perform a song like that.
There were audible groans in the keynote hall when Jobs announced that the iPod Touch update costs $20.
posts.key_insight: Even when praising Apple's products, Gruber holds the company accountable for business decisions that prioritize profit over customer trust — particularly when features are artificially restricted to sell proprietary hardware.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a special live episode of The Talk Show podcast being recorded at Macworld Expo's podcast booth. His regular co-host Dan Benjamin couldn't attend, so Cabel Sasser of Panic will join instead. Unlike the previous year's live recording, there's no audience seating, though audio will be broadcast for spectators. He deliberately gave short notice to avoid overcrowding a standing-room situation.
key_points: A live episode of The Talk Show is being recorded at Macworld Expo's podcast booth
Cabel Sasser replaces regular co-host Dan Benjamin for this special episode
Unlike the previous year's Macworld recording, there is no audience seating arrangement
Gruber intentionally avoided promoting the event in advance due to the lack of seating
money_quotes: I thought it unwise to promote this in advance.
posts.key_insight: A brief logistical announcement about a live Talk Show recording at Macworld Expo, notable mainly as an early example of Gruber's podcast taking shape as a community event.

posts.summary: Gruber explores why for-profit malware developers show virtually zero interest in targeting Mac OS X. He considers the epidemiological argument that malware spreads virally and thus only thrives on majority platforms, but finds it insufficient given that the Mac's ~5% market share should still attract some malware. He argues that Windows users' higher tolerance for annoying software — stemming from factory-installed adware — creates a slippery slope toward malware acceptance. He also acknowledges Vista's improved security as a technical factor, and poses a thought experiment about how a more evenly split OS market might affect malware distribution.
key_points: The epidemiological argument (malware needs majority platforms to spread) is partly true but doesn't fully explain the Mac's near-total absence of malware
Windows users' tolerance for factory-installed adware creates a cultural slippery slope that makes them more vulnerable to malware
Vista appears to be genuinely more secure than XP, adding a technical dimension to the discussion
A hypothetical 40/40/20 OS market split raises interesting questions about whether platform heterogeneity itself could suppress malware
money_quotes: The Mac has around five percent market share but almost no malware whatsoever.
Most new Windows machines come loaded from the factory with promotional software that most Mac users would consider adware.
It's a slippery slope to malware adware from there.
The simple truth is that Windows users are more accustomed to being annoyed by their machines.

What if the desktop OS market share distribution were something like 40/40/20 for Windows/various Linux distros/Mac OS X? Would that sort of heterogeneous split keep malware from spreading even on Windows?
posts.key_insight: The Mac's near-immunity to malware isn't just about market share — it's equally about a Windows culture where users have been trained to tolerate intrusive software as normal.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's record Q1 2008 financial results, focusing on three key areas: iPod economics, iPhone momentum, and iTunes Store growth. He argues that while iPod unit sales growth has slowed dramatically (from 50% to 5%), revenue growth remains steady at 17% because Apple is selling more expensive devices like the iPod Touch. He makes the prescient observation that the iPhone and iPod Touch constitute a single unnamed mobile software platform that is already outselling Macs, and predicts a coming gold rush for third-party app developers. He also highlights the iTunes Store's 27% revenue growth as an underappreciated sign of strength.
key_points: iPod unit sales growth slowed from 50% to 5% year-over-year, but revenue growth held steady at 17%, driven by higher-priced devices like the iPod Touch
The iPod should not be thought of as a digital Walkman destined for commodity pricing, but as 'the best pocket-sized anti-boredom device Apple can make'
iPhone and iPod Touch constitute a single unnamed mobile OS X platform that already outsells Macs in unit volume
Apple's cut of third-party app sales through the iTunes Store represents a potential gold rush for indie Mac developers
iTunes Store revenue growth of 27% is at least as impressive as the Mac's 47% revenue growth given the store's maturity
money_quotes: How do you grow revenue faster than unit sales? By selling more iPods at the high-end of the product line. Hello, iPod Touch.
It's wrong to think of the iPod simply as a digital Walkman. Better to think of the iPod as, say, 'the best pocket-sized anti-boredom device Apple can make'.
In 2001, that device was a simple hard-drive based 5 GB MP3 player. Today, it's the iPhone and iPod Touch.
Apple sold almost exactly the same number of iPhones as Macs: 2,315,000 vs. 2,319,000. That's impressive in and of itself.
I don't see how anyone could doubt that the iPhone is a smash hit at this point.
Even in a quarter with 44 percent higher year-over-year Mac unit sales, Apple sold more 'Mobile OS X' devices than Macs.
This is a potential gold rush for indie Mac developers.
27 percent growth sounds pretty damn solid for the four-year-old market-leading music store.
posts.key_insight: The iPod's slowing unit growth masked a strategic shift toward higher-value devices like the iPod Touch and iPhone, which together formed a new mobile platform already outselling Macs and poised to create an entirely new software economy.

posts.summary: Gruber pays tribute to John Moltz's Crazy Apple Rumors Site (CARS), which is going on hiatus after six years. He highlights his favorite piece of CARS media criticism about Daring Fireball itself, and tells a story about how Moltz helped him get a Macworld Expo press credential through Phil Schiller, who was apparently a huge CARS fan. Gruber notes that CARS's fake rumors were often more accurate than serious Mac rumor sites. The piece is a characteristically understated farewell to a fellow member of the Apple blogosphere.
key_points: CARS is going on hiatus after six years of satirizing Apple rumors and the Mac community
Phil Schiller was a genuine fan of CARS, and Moltz leveraged that connection to help Gruber get a Macworld press credential
CARS's fake rumors were paradoxically more accurate than many serious Mac rumor sites
Gruber's favorite CARS piece was one that critiqued Daring Fireball itself
money_quotes: I, of course, complained to friends, self-important prick that I am.
A surprising amount of stuff in CARS that sounds made up is completely true, including this one.
So Moltz told me he'd "put in a good word" for me. I have no idea what that means exactly, but next thing I knew, I had a press credential for Macworld Expo.
Moltz's fake rumors were generally more accurate that most of those from the ostensibly serious Mac rumor sites.
All of which is to say that perhaps, in some way, we're maybe going to sort of miss CARS. I guess.
posts.key_insight: The best Apple satire was so well-informed that its fake rumors were more accurate than the serious ones, and its creator had more insider access than many credentialed journalists.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Microsoft's unsolicited $44.6 billion bid for Yahoo, predicting Yahoo will accept due to the 62% premium over its stock price. He explores the fundamental engineering paradox: Yahoo runs entirely on PHP, FreeBSD, and Linux, while Microsoft has never successfully maintained non-Microsoft technology at scale. He argues Microsoft doesn't care about Yahoo's technology—only its pageviews and advertisers—and predicts they'll force users onto existing Microsoft products rather than migrate the codebase. He concludes this would effectively be the end of Yahoo as we know it.
key_points: The 62% premium over Yahoo's stock price makes it likely Yahoo will accept, since the company can't reach that valuation on its own anytime soon
Yahoo's entire technology stack (PHP, FreeBSD, Linux) is fundamentally alien to Microsoft's culture and DNA, creating an irreconcilable engineering paradox
Microsoft's interest is in pageviews and advertisers, not Yahoo's technology—they'd likely force Yahoo users onto existing Microsoft products like Hotmail
Yahoo's boutique acquisitions like Flickr and Delicious would likely be sold off as irrelevant to Microsoft's strategic goals
The cultural clash between Yahoo's open-source developers and Microsoft's .NET ecosystem would drive significant talent attrition
money_quotes: Technically, I can't see how Microsoft would migrate all of Yahoo to Windows servers and software. But culturally, it just isn't in Microsoft's DNA to accept and maintain all of these PHP/FreeBSD/Linux products.
What Microsoft sees in Yahoo isn't software but pageviews and advertisers.
How many Yahoo developers would rather quit than re-implement their products using some flavor of Dot Net?
I don't think Microsoft has ever bought — and maintained — a significant software product that wasn't written against Microsoft technology.
Rather than, say, rewriting Yahoo Mail using Windows technology, I expect them to just force Yahoo Mail users over to Windows Live Hotmail.
It's like tying the Titanic to the iceberg. It'd keep you from sinking just long enough to freeze to death.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's bid for Yahoo is driven entirely by desire for eyeballs and ad revenue, not technology—and the cultural impossibility of Microsoft maintaining a massive open-source stack means this acquisition would destroy Yahoo rather than strengthen either company.

posts.summary: Gruber observes that Twitter's reliability problems have shifted from complete outages to something more insidious: silently dropping individual tweets. He documents the issue with @replies not being delivered, corroborated by multiple users and developer Craig Hockenberry. Rather than the familiar all-or-nothing downtime, Twitter now appears up while quietly losing messages. Gruber argues this new failure mode is psychologically worse because users can't tell when their messages have been lost.
key_points: Twitter's reliability issues shifted from obvious total outages to silent, partial message loss
The @reply system specifically was dropping tweets without any notification to sender or recipient
Multiple users and developer Craig Hockenberry independently confirmed and documented the problem
A known, visible failure is preferable to an invisible, indeterminate one because users can at least adapt their behavior
Making reliability indeterminate undermines trust more than simple downtime does
money_quotes: Psychologically, I find it far better to have a big problem (the entire service being down) that everyone knows about than to have a smaller problem (occasional tweets silently disappearing into the ether) that, because of the very nature of the problem, you don't even know about.
In short, making Twitter's reliability indeterminate is worse, not better.
The old way, you at least knew instantly what the deal was. Twitter was up, or Twitter was down.
Now, though, you can't count on anything.
If it was up, you could pretty much count on your tweets working.
posts.key_insight: A system that fails visibly and completely is more trustworthy than one that appears to work while silently losing data, because indeterminate reliability is worse than known downtime.

posts.summary: Gruber performs a line-by-line satirical translation of Yahoo CEO Jerry Yang's company-wide memo following Microsoft's 2008 takeover bid. The memo, filed with the SEC, uses standard corporate PR language to reassure employees while the company faces an existential crisis. Gruber decodes each paragraph into what Yang is actually saying: the board wants to reject Microsoft but can't, the stock was undervalued, and the acquisition seems inevitable. The piece captures a pivotal moment in tech history when Yahoo was cornered by Microsoft's hostile bid with no good options.
key_points: Yang's memo is textbook corporate PR-speak designed to project confidence while the company faces a hostile takeover it cannot easily refuse
The board's position is untenable: they want to reject Microsoft's offer but risk a shareholder revolt if they do, since the stock was clearly undervalued
Yahoo's announcements of a Rhapsody partnership and Zimbra 5.0 launch are pathetically small compared to the existential threat they face
The repeated reassurances to employees reveal genuine panic about talent flight during the acquisition uncertainty
money_quotes: We very much want to say "no" but can't figure out how without triggering a shareholder revolt.
Does anyone have any wealthy relatives with 40 or 50 billion dollars to invest?
Everyone seems to agree that Microsoft would fuck Yahoo up but good.
Yahoo's stock was clearly undervalued by the market, which means those bastards in Redmond are getting a good deal and there's nothing we can do about it.
Where by "more" I mean "some".
We are completely irrelevant in digital music.
Zimbra will be the first product taken out back to be shot in the head once the Microsoft takeover goes through.
Welcome to Microsoft.
posts.key_insight: Corporate PR memos during hostile takeovers are exercises in saying the opposite of what everyone involved actually knows to be true.

posts.summary: Gruber plays devil's advocate on Microsoft's proposed $40 billion acquisition of Yahoo, arguing that while conventional wisdom says Microsoft will ruin Yahoo, there's a narrow path where it could work. He acknowledges Yahoo's board is trapped between a depressed stock price and no rival suitors who can match Microsoft's offer. The key question is whether Microsoft can resist its instinct to assimilate Yahoo and instead keep it as an independent web division. Gruber draws parallels to Microsoft's Mac Business Unit, which succeeded by operating independently, and compares Yahoo to Apple circa 1998 — talented people, good products, but terrible management. He outlines a specific strategy: install a visionary leader, preserve Yahoo's tech stack and brand, let competing products coexist, and commission ambitious new projects like having Flickr tackle video. He concludes it's unlikely but not impossible.
key_points: At $40 billion, no rival suitor can realistically compete with Microsoft's offer, leaving Yahoo's board with no escape route
Microsoft's natural instinct toward assimilation would destroy Yahoo — the 'tying legs together' analogy captures why merging two second-place companies rarely produces a winner
The Mac Business Unit provides a small-scale precedent for Microsoft successfully running an independent division with its own culture and tools
Yahoo's Linux/BSD/Apache/MySQL/PHP infrastructure should be preserved as a strength, not replaced with Microsoft technology
Yahoo's core problem mirrors Apple circa 1998: talented people and good products undermined by visionless management
money_quotes: The reason I consider it unlikely is that it would require Microsoft to be un-Microsoftian.
It's like taking the two guys who finished second and third in a 100-yard dash and tying their legs together and asking for a rematch, believing that now they'll run faster.
Yahoo is too big, and too different, to be absorbed into Microsoft.

Why Yahoo didn't do this immediately after acquiring Flickr, instead choosing to stand on the sidelines playing pocket pool while YouTube swelled into a multi-billion-dollar product, is a mystery for the ages.
That they've built their architecture around Linux, BSD, Apache, MySQL, and PHP — not a whiff of Microsoft technology — is a strength, not a weakness.
What Yahoo needs is a Steve Jobs — someone who will ruthlessly focus the company on products that are better, more popular, and more profitable.
Is that possible within the confines of Microsoft? I doubt it, but I hope so.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft acquiring Yahoo could theoretically work, but only if Microsoft does something deeply uncharacteristic: resist assimilation and let Yahoo operate as an independent web division with its own culture, technology, and leadership.

posts.summary: Gruber describes his search for a lightweight alternative to Growl for displaying script status messages on Mac OS X. After a clean Leopard installation, he found he didn't miss Growl enough to reinstall it immediately, mainly because it consumed 30 MB of memory just for occasional script notifications. He discovered that FastScripts by Daniel Jalkut offers a simple 'display message' command that provides Growl-like floating HUD notifications without the overhead. He provides AppleScript examples and suggests minor improvements like support for a secondary message string and custom icons.
key_points: Growl's system-wide self-dismissing notifications are useful but overkill for occasional script messages, consuming at least 30 MB of memory
A clean OS install revealed which third-party utilities were truly missed versus habitual — Growl wasn't high on the list
FastScripts offers a lightweight HUD-style 'display message' command with configurable position and auto-dismiss timing
The feature could be improved with support for a secondary smaller-font message and custom icons
money_quotes: When I upgraded to Leopard full-time a few months ago, I started with a clean installation and only added back third-party UI utilities one-by-one, when I actually missed them.
Using Growl just for the occasional message from a script seemed like overkill; on my machine Growl typically consumes at least 30 MB of real memory.
It ended up I didn't miss Growl much, and didn't get around to re-installing it until last week.
FastScripts's message window is a simple, dark, slightly tranclucent HUD-style floating window that accepts a single string as a parameter.
But it's a nice lightweight alternative to Growl as-is.
posts.key_insight: A clean OS install is a useful forcing function for discovering which utilities you actually need versus which you run out of habit, and lightweight alternatives often serve better than feature-heavy tools.

posts.summary: Gruber pushes back against a Gear Live report claiming Flash support is coming to the iPhone 'very, very soon.' He argues the report lacks the screenshot/video evidence that backed the site's previous 1.1.3 scoop, and that the technical challenges of running Flash on iPhone hardware are real, not fabricated. He further argues Apple has no compelling business reason to add Flash, since MobileSafari already dominates mobile browsing, developers are building for HTML/JavaScript/CSS, and the native SDK is imminent. He predicts Flash won't come to iPhone any time soon.
key_points: The Gear Live Flash report lacks the concrete evidence (screenshots, video) that gave their earlier 1.1.3 scoop credibility
Flash's CPU-intensive nature poses real technical hurdles on iPhone hardware, contrary to the claim that such issues were 'complete fabrication'
Apple has little business incentive to add Flash: MobileSafari already leads mobile browser market share, developers are building with web standards, and the native SDK is about to launch
The most popular Flash use case — YouTube videos — is already handled by a native iPhone app
Gruber bets his credibility that Flash won't arrive on iPhone any time soon
money_quotes: I'm going to take a pass on taking his word for it. I don't buy it.
Flash is CPU-intensive. I'm not saying it's impossible, but if Apple does release an iPhone update with Flash support, it'll be despite the technical hurdles, not because the hurdles don't exist.
With fewer than eight months on the market, MobileSafari — sans Flash support — already has the largest market share of all mobile web browsers.
Companies large and small are writing MobileSafari-optimized web sites and web apps, using HTML/JavaScript/CSS.
The single most popular thing people use Flash for is to watch YouTube videos, which you can already watch a subset of using the native Mobile OS X YouTube app.
In short, is the lack of Flash keeping people from buying iPhones and iPod Touches?
I've been wrong before, but no guts, no glory. I say no Flash on the iPhone any time soon.
posts.key_insight: Apple had no technical or business reason to bring Flash to the iPhone when MobileSafari was already winning the mobile web with open standards — a prediction that proved exactly right.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's refusal to add Flash to the iPhone is primarily a strategic business decision, not a technical limitation. He frames Flash as the 'wrong sort of proprietary' — technology controlled by a competitor (Adobe) that would undermine Apple's complete control over the iPhone software stack. Since the mobile web market is wide open and Flash isn't yet entrenched there, Apple has no incentive to help Adobe establish dominance in mobile the way it achieved on desktop. Gruber notes that Apple's entire mobile platform runs on software it fully controls, and adding Flash would introduce dependency, risk, and a muddled developer message for negligible sales benefit.
key_points: Apple views Flash as the 'wrong sort of proprietary' — controlled by a competitor rather than by Apple or open standards
The mobile web market is wide open without Flash entrenchment, giving Apple a strategic opportunity to push for a Flash-free mobile future
Apple controls the entire iPhone software stack top to bottom and has no incentive to cede any control to Adobe
The historical relationship between Apple/NeXT and Adobe has been fraught, including restrictive Display PostScript licensing terms
The upcoming iPhone SDK won't allow third parties to create system-level plugins, so Adobe can't port Flash independently
money_quotes: Could Apple do it? Yes, but it wouldn't be easy. But would they? I say no — even if it were easy.
