Flash, on the other hand, is (from Apple's perspective) the wrong sort of proprietary — owned and controlled by another company.
Why would Apple help Adobe establish Flash as a de facto standard for the mobile web, too?
As it stands today, Apple is dependent on no one other than itself for the software on the iPhone. Apple controls the source code to the whole thing, from top to bottom.

If there is no Flash support in MobileSafari, and the absence of Flash does not affect demand for iPhones and iPods, then there is no possible way that Flash can turn into a pain in Apple's mobile ass.
What happens if Apple decides to switch the iPhone to a different CPU architecture and Adobe says Flash won't simply recompile?
No one is going to get to diddle with MobileSafari without Apple's approval.
posts.key_insight: Apple's refusal to support Flash on iPhone was a calculated strategic move to prevent a competitor's proprietary technology from gaining a foothold in the wide-open mobile market, where Apple controlled the entire stack.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that critics predicting poor sales for the MacBook Air are wrong because they're evaluating it on specs rather than appeal. He draws an analogy to a sporty convertible: you buy it for the pleasure of owning something beautiful, not for practical superiority. He contends the Air is fast enough for most users, noting its CPU outperforms PowerBooks from just two years prior. The strongest historical parallel he offers is the iPod Mini, which the Internet consensus dismissed but which became Apple's best-selling iPod. Gruber argues that a large segment of buyers will use the Air as their only computer, and that spec-obsessed critics don't even acknowledge this group exists. Given that Apple charges a $125 premium for black MacBooks, the willingness to pay for style is already proven.
key_points: The MacBook Air inverts Apple's traditional price-performance equation: it costs more than a MacBook but performs worse, because you're paying for form factor and design
The iPod Mini is the correct historical analogy — critics dismissed it on specs, but consumers chose it for its size and appeal, and it became the best-selling iPod
The Air's CPU is several hundred times more powerful than an original Pentium, yet people still do the same tasks — 'specification lust' drives criticism, not actual needs
Whenever Internet consensus says a new Apple product is under-specced, the Internet has been wrong — the missing optical drive and ports are like the original iMac's missing floppy
The G4 Cube comparison is flawed: the Cube failed not because it was underpowered but because of case defects and overpricing relative to its market position
money_quotes: The key to understanding the appeal of the Air is that you've got to stop thinking about technical specs as the primary factors.
You buy one not for practical reasons, but because it is satisfying to own something beautiful and clever and fun.
That the Air isn't as fast as a regular MacBook does not matter because the Air — for most people and most tasks — is clearly fast enough.
Whenever 'the Internet' consensus is that a new Apple product is under-specced, the Internet has been wrong.

The initial reception of the Macbook Air proves that the current process of designing, marketing and selling computers has nothing to do with 'specification' requirements, but everything to do with specification lust.
If hard disk capacity were the only factor that mattered, we'd all be using brick-sized players from Creative Labs.
Given that Apple still charges a $125 premium for black MacBooks, it's hard to take seriously any argument that people aren't willing to pay for style.
posts.key_insight: The MacBook Air's appeal, like the iPod Mini before it, lies in the fact that for most consumers, 'good enough' specs in a beautiful package will always outsell superior specs in an ordinary one.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his 2006 essay about keyboard-based multiple item selection in Mac OS X, noting that Apple changed Cocoa's NSTableView in Leopard to use the anchored selection model he advocated for. The anchored model lets users correct selection mistakes by shrinking the selection in the opposite direction, while the unanchored model only ever grows. Because the change was made at the framework level, all Cocoa apps automatically inherited the improved behavior. However, Carbon apps like Finder and iTunes still use the inferior unanchored model. Gruber notes the inconsistency is unfortunate but considers the NSTableView change exactly what he hoped for. He closes by thanking whoever at Apple pushed for the change.
key_points: Anchored selection lets users correct mistakes with the keyboard by shrinking the selection range, while unanchored selection only grows in both directions
Apple changed NSTableView's default in Leopard from unanchored to anchored, and all Cocoa apps picked up the improvement automatically
Carbon apps like Finder and iTunes still use the old unanchored model, creating an inconsistency most users can't explain
Gruber's 2006 prediction that apps using framework defaults would get improvements 'for free' proved exactly correct
money_quotes: My argument was that anchored is better, because it allows you to correct for mistakes without switching to the mouse.
Key word there being 'was'.
That's exactly what happened — all apps that use Cocoa's NSTableView, not just Apple's, picked up the new anchored selection model for free on Leopard.
The overwhelming majority of Mac users have no idea what Cocoa and Carbon are, so the different behavior in the Finder and iTunes is, to them, seemingly arbitrary.
But if there's one single thing I hoped to see Apple do regarding this issue in Leopard, it's exactly what they did: change it in NSTableView.
I have no idea who pushed for this change at Apple, nor whether my essay was influential in the decision. But whoever you are, I thank you.
posts.key_insight: Apple's Leopard change to anchored selection in NSTableView vindicated both the usability argument and the wisdom of building on framework defaults rather than custom implementations.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects a Wall Street Journal article by Ben Charny about the iPhone's lack of Flash support, finding it riddled with factual errors, poor sourcing, and a fundamentally flawed premise. He notes the article was quietly edited after publication to remove embarrassing claims, including calling Apple's stock 'beleaguered' when it was up 45% year-over-year. Gruber argues the article completely misses the core power dynamic: Adobe needs the iPhone far more than Apple needs Flash, because no competing device can match the iPhone's mobile web browsing. He concludes that Apple has strategic reason to resist Flash, as it has the opportunity to establish QuickTime and H.264 as the de facto standards for mobile video.
key_points: The WSJ article contains multiple factual errors, including the claim that Adobe 'dropped support' for the Mac and that YouTube is the only source of H.264 video on the web
The article was stealth-edited after publication to remove the word 'beleaguered' describing Apple's stock and a claim that iPhone sales were hurt by the lack of Flash
The article's central flaw is misunderstanding the power dynamic: Adobe needs Flash on the iPhone, but no competing Flash-capable device threatens iPhone sales
Apple has a strategic incentive to block Flash and establish H.264/QuickTime as the mobile video standard
The article relies on unnamed forum posts as sourcing for a Wall Street Journal news report
money_quotes: Adobe is under pressure to get Flash on the iPhone because the iPhone is clearly the leading mobile device for web surfing, but Apple is under very little pressure to put Flash on the iPhone because there is no competing device that fits in this sentence: 'There are many people who were going to buy an iPhone, but, because it doesn't support Flash, bought a ———— instead.'
So YouTube is the only source for QuickTime or H.264 video on the entire web?
Adobe's patience appears to be wearing thin. And what happens then?
Apparently, unnamed web site forum posts are valid sources for Wall Street Journal news reports.
It's a bit rich to describe Apple's stock price as 'beleaguered' when it's still up 45 percent from a year ago.
They have the opportunity to establish QuickTime and H.264 as the de facto standards for mobile video on the web.
posts.key_insight: The WSJ's Flash-on-iPhone story fundamentally misreads the power dynamic — Adobe needs the iPhone far more than Apple needs Flash, giving Apple both the leverage and the strategic incentive to push open video standards instead.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses objections that the iPhone's lack of Flash contradicts Apple's claim that the iPhone offers 'the real Internet.' He argues that Apple's 'Watered Down' commercial makes a general advertising claim, not a specific technical promise, and that the phrase 'just the Internet' doesn't imply support for every web plugin. He notes that if the standard were total feature parity with desktop Safari, then Java and Windows Media would also be missing. He suggests Apple may be strategically framing HTML, CSS, and JavaScript as the 'real' web, positioning Flash as non-standard despite its prevalence.
key_points: Apple's 'just the Internet' iPhone claim is advertising rhetoric, not a specific technical promise of feature parity with desktop browsers
If Flash's absence contradicts the 'real Internet' claim, then so does the absence of Java and Windows Media — the argument proves too much
Apple may be deliberately framing HTML/CSS/JavaScript as the only technologies that constitute the 'real' web, marginalizing Flash as non-standard
Gruber remains open to the possibility that Flash support could arrive with the upcoming SDK release, but doubts it
money_quotes: "It's just the Internet" is not a specific technical claim. For god's sake they're using "Internet" to mean "web".
If you think "just the Internet" implies that MobileSafari should support everything Safari for Mac and Windows supports, that means more than just Flash is missing, including Java and Windows Media.

It's also possible that this sort of phrasing is an attempt to frame the issue in Apple's favor, by establishing HTML, CSS, and JavaScript as the only technologies that constitute the "regular" web — and imply that Flash, no matter how common, is non-standard.
posts.key_insight: Apple's 'just the Internet' claim is advertising shorthand that strategically equates the web with open standards like HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — implicitly recasting Flash as a non-standard add-on rather than an essential part of the web experience.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes pre-announcement rumors about Apple's upcoming iPhone SDK, drawing on iLounge reporting that Apple will use iTunes as the distribution hub and act as a gatekeeper approving all apps. He argues that while the closed distribution model will disappoint those hoping for Mac-like openness, it could be enormously lucrative for commercial developers due to iTunes' frictionless purchasing. He pushes back on claims that restricting dock connector access makes the native SDK as limited as web apps, noting massive performance advantages of native code. He predicts the SDK will feature heavy sandboxing, tiered network access, and that critics will prematurely declare it doomed.
key_points: iTunes Store distribution with Apple as gatekeeper was predictable and could be far more lucrative for developers than Mac software distribution
Claims that blocking dock connector access makes the native SDK as limited as web apps ignore the enormous performance advantages of compiled native code over JavaScript in MobileSafari
Apple's SDK approach will likely emphasize sandboxing for security and resource control, similar to how Mac OS X already abstracts hardware access
Gruber predicts tiered network access — Wi-Fi for all developers, EDGE/cellular for very few — and anticipates critics will declare the SDK doomed regardless
money_quotes: If you're hoping for an open "write whatever apps you want/install whatever apps you want" situation like the Mac, you're almost certainly going to be disappointed.

Once you've crossed the hurdle of getting Apple to list your app, all a potential user would need to do is click "Buy" in iTunes: the iTunes Store already has their billing info, and iTunes will handle downloading the software and installing it.

If what you really want to do is write iPhone software to provide an interface to a hardware peripheral, yes, a complete lack of access to the dock connector port puts the native SDK in the same boat as writing a web app: the S.S. Useless.
Clearly a big part of what Apple's been working on in advance of this SDK are ways to sandbox applications for security and control of resources.
Apple could, in theory, be on the cusp of announcing a very liberal application SDK but which still wouldn't allow for third-party hardware drivers.
The reasons for this are obvious, but I suspect won't stop The Internet from proclaiming the SDK doomed from the start.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iPhone SDK will trade openness for a controlled, sandboxed environment that — despite inevitable criticism — offers developers a far more lucrative distribution model and dramatically better performance than web apps.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews ExpanDrive, a $29 Mac OS X utility that mounts SFTP servers as Finder volumes using MacFUSE. He finds it remarkably effective for editing remote files, far superior to WebDAV or AFP in responsiveness, and praises its ability to survive network changes and sleep/wake cycles. However, he dedicates significant attention to UI shortcomings: misused keyboard shortcuts, a poorly designed preferences window, and a confirmation dialog that violates basic HIG principles. He also criticizes the undocumented installation of system-level files with no uninstaller. Despite these complaints, he concludes the product genuinely delivers on its ambitious promise.
key_points: ExpanDrive mounts SFTP servers as local Finder volumes, enabling seamless remote file editing without separate upload/download steps
Performance vastly exceeds WebDAV and AFP, with low latency, reliable reconnection across network changes, and smooth sleep/wake behavior
The UI has multiple HIG violations: Command-C used for something other than Copy, Command-D mapped to Delete without confirmation, and Yes/No buttons on a dialog that doesn't ask a question
The installer silently places undocumented MacFUSE files across /Library and /usr/local with no uninstaller provided
Despite UI and installer criticisms, Gruber concludes the core functionality is genuinely excellent
money_quotes: For many typical tasks, ExpanDrive is far more convenient and seamless than a standalone client like Interarchy or Transmit.
ExpanDrive's low latency and reliability put .Mac's WebDAV-based iDisk to shame.

As a remote network protocol, it works better in the Finder than anything I've ever used on Mac OS X — far more responsive than WebDAV or AFP, both of which, for me, have always been one-way tickets to Beachball City.
Command-C should never be used for anything other than Copy.
Action verbs almost always make for better button names than "Yes" and "No".
Ideally, the user should be able to figure out what their options are even if they only read the button names and ignore the explanatory text.
My first impression after reading ExpanDrive's promotional description last week was that it sounded too good to be true. One week later, I'm pretty sure it actually is that good.
posts.key_insight: ExpanDrive delivers genuinely impressive core functionality that makes remote SFTP editing seamless, but its UI details reveal a lack of Mac platform literacy that Gruber methodically catalogs as a lesson in why interface conventions matter.

posts.summary: Gruber offers his first impressions of Apple's iPhone SDK announcement, covering enterprise support, developer tools, the App Store model, and gaming potential. He admits he was wrong about Apple pursuing enterprise features, praises the developer tools as exceeding expectations, and analyzes the 30/70 revenue split as acceptable to developers despite being steep. He raises prescient questions about how App Store exclusivity will be technically enforced and whether EDGE network restrictions on apps like VOIP can be practically adjudicated. He predicts the App Store will trigger a gold rush driven by convenience, exposure, and volume sales.
key_points: Apple's enterprise iPhone support was unexpected — Gruber admits he was wrong to doubt Apple would pursue this market
The developer tools exceeded expectations with Interface Builder support, a desktop simulator, and the new 'Cocoa Touch' API, all Mac-only — potentially drawing Windows developers to the platform
The App Store's 30/70 split is steep but developers accept it for the convenience and massive exposure of reaching every iPhone user with one-click purchasing
The closed App Store model raises unanswered questions about technical enforcement — if developers can sideload their own apps, what stops users from doing the same?
EDGE network restrictions on bandwidth-heavy apps like VOIP seem difficult to enforce once apps are in the wild
money_quotes: There was never any question that this is a big market, the question was whether Apple wanted a part of it.
This has the potential to do for Windows developers what the iPod did for Windows users — move them to the Mac.
The unique control options — no traditional buttons but a 3D accelerometer and multi-touch screen — make the iPhone analogous to the Wii, in that it opens up new concepts in game UI design.
"70%? That's… that's… livable," which seems to sum up the consensus sentiment.
There is no option to circumvent the App Store.
What developers lose per-transaction from Apple's 30 percent take, they can more than make up for in volume. This is going to be a gold rush.
I suspect it's intended as a bozo filter to keep the process from being inundated with glorified do-little "Hello World" apps.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone SDK and App Store represent a transformative trade-off: developers surrender distribution control and a 30% cut in exchange for frictionless purchasing and exposure to every iPhone user on Earth — a deal that will spark a gold rush.

posts.summary: Gruber raises several unanswered questions following Apple's iPhone SDK announcement, focusing on the practical implications of the App Store model for developers and users. He notes that unsigned apps appear restricted to those with paid $99 developer accounts, and wonders whether developers can freely share apps among themselves. He questions whether the App Store model allows for trial periods, a standard practice on the Mac. He also speculates about app pricing, predicting most apps will settle around $9.99-14.99.
key_points: Unsigned iPhone apps appear restricted to paid developer accounts, raising questions about developer-to-developer app sharing
The App Store's pay-before-download model seemingly eliminates the 'try before you buy' demo periods common on the Mac
Gruber predicts iPhone app prices will settle around $9.99-14.99, roughly album pricing, with cheaper impulse-buy games
money_quotes: On the Mac, a "try before you buy" period is practically universal. But if you have to pay before you download from the App Store, that seems out of the question.
My guess is $9.99-14.99 — roughly the price of an album. Maybe less for simple games geared toward impulse purchases.
Does that mean that approved developers will be able to freely exchange unsigned apps with each other?
posts.key_insight: The App Store's controlled distribution model raises fundamental questions about developer freedom and consumer experience that Apple has yet to answer.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the strongest argument against Apple's iPhone SDK restriction on third-party background processes, as articulated by Hank Williams. Williams contends that background processing is essential for mobile innovation, particularly for status updates and event notifications. Gruber acknowledges the validity of this point but frames it as a deliberate trade-off Apple made to maximize battery life and system performance. He draws a parallel to earlier complaints about dock connector access, noting that while specific use cases are blocked, the SDK still enables a wide range of valuable pure-software applications. Gruber concludes that Apple's policy reflects pragmatic prioritization rather than spite or shortsightedness, and that other platforms like Android simply have different priorities.
key_points: Background processing is critical for mobile innovation around status broadcasting and event notification, and its absence is a legitimate limitation
Apple's no-background-processes policy is a deliberate trade-off favoring battery life and system performance over maximum potential functionality
The AOL AIM client demo raised unanswered questions about whether Apple would grant special exceptions to its own background processing rules
The complaint pattern mirrors earlier dock connector grievances: a real limitation for specific use cases doesn't invalidate the SDK's broader value
Competing platforms like Android may enable different innovations by making different trade-off decisions
money_quotes: Apple hasn't designed the current SDK limitations to maximize potential coolness; they've designed it to maximize battery life and performance system-wide.
There's no doubt that some very cool ideas would require background-capable iPhone apps. That's the trade-off, though.

I don't think the "no background" policy implies any spite or shortsightedness on Apple's part. It's simply the result of Apple's decision to focus first and foremost on maximizing battery life and performance.
If the app you really want to write requires it, then yes, the current SDK is disappointing. But that doesn't mean there aren't plenty of other ideas that will work just fine.
Other mobile platforms, such as Android, may well have different priorities.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iPhone background processing restriction is a principled trade-off prioritizing battery life and performance, not a failure of vision, and the SDK's limitations for specific use cases don't negate its broad value.

posts.summary: Gruber reports on confusion among developers after Apple sent an ambiguous message about their iPhone SDK beta program applications. The email left developers unsure whether it was a receipt confirmation or a rejection. Gruber notes that even top indie Mac developers received the same message, suggesting it's either a blanket form letter or Apple is being extremely selective. He contextualizes the issue by explaining that while anyone can develop for the iPhone simulator, the beta program is needed to test on actual hardware, which matters significantly for apps using OpenGL, Core Location, or the accelerometer.
key_points: Apple sent an ambiguous form letter to iPhone SDK beta applicants that could be interpreted as either acknowledgment or rejection
Even prominent indie Mac developers ('top men') received the identical message, suggesting it's not targeted rejection
The simulator has significant limitations — no OpenGL, no Core Location, no accelerometer — making device testing essential
The long-term developer program will likely be inclusive, but the beta period's exclusivity remains an open question
money_quotes: No one seems to know whether this is a 'we got your application' form letter, or a 'you've been rejected, at least for now' form letter.
Either way, the message is poorly phrased.
I have firsthand confirmation that some of the biggest names in indie Mac development — Raiders of the Lost Ark-style 'top men' — received this exact same message.

So, either it's just a form letter sent to all developers who've applied to let them know Apple is still reviewing their applications, or else Apple is being extraordinarily selective about the first wave of developers in the beta program.
The question is how exclusive the developer program will be during the beta period.
posts.key_insight: Apple's poor communication about the iPhone SDK beta program left even top-tier developers confused about their status, highlighting the company's tendency toward opacity in developer relations during the iPhone platform's critical early days.

posts.summary: Gruber defends Apple's decision to prohibit third-party iPhone apps from running in the background, one week after the iPhone SDK launch. He explains that even Apple's own apps mostly follow this constraint, with only the four dock apps (Phone, Mail, Safari, iPod) running background processes. The restriction exists because the iPhone's 128 MB of RAM, limited CPU, and battery life cannot sustain multiple background processes. Gruber argues the trade-off benefits typical users by ensuring installed apps won't degrade phone performance or battery life. He notes the iPhone's UI simplicity partly derives from having no process management interface. He predicts the restriction will eventually be relaxed as hardware improves, but not soon.
key_points: Only four built-in dock apps run in the background; even Apple's other apps quit when not frontmost, maintaining a consistent policy
The iPhone's 128 MB RAM with no swap space makes background processes a genuine resource threat to performance and battery life
The absence of process management UI is a design feature, not a limitation — it eliminates 'computer administrative debris' from the user experience
Apple chose harsh restrictions first because it's far easier to relax constraints later than to impose them after the fact
The trade-off explicitly favors typical users over technically advanced users who might want background capabilities
money_quotes: There is no concept of launching or quitting — there is only switching.
The profound simplicity of the iPhone user interface stems in part from the complete lack of interface elements for managing processes.
If you want to know what's running, the answer is simply whatever app it is that you're looking at.
This is a trade-off, one in which technically advanced users pay the price for the benefit of typical users.
What's the alternative? A "don't install or launch too much crap" policy?
It is far easier to set harsh restrictions first and relax them later than the other way around.
In short, your iPhone will not be doing much while it's in your pocket.
posts.key_insight: Apple's ban on background third-party apps is a deliberate design trade-off that preserves the iPhone's simplicity and reliability for typical users at the expense of power-user flexibility, and it's far easier to relax such restrictions later than to impose them after the fact.

posts.summary: Gruber defends Apple's decision to prohibit background apps on the iPhone, responding to Ian Betteridge's argument that OS makers shouldn't prevent poorly-written applications. He argues that the iPhone's restriction is a deliberate trade-off: users gain the guarantee that no third-party app can degrade system performance or battery life. He illustrates the problem with scenarios where typical users wouldn't know which background apps are causing battery drain or slowdowns. Gruber concludes that demanding the freedom to install potentially system-degrading software is reasonable, but the iPhone simply isn't designed for that philosophy.
key_points: A new platform can be innovative precisely by being different from established norms, including restricting background apps
The inability to screw up your system is itself a feature, not merely a limitation
Typical users lack the technical knowledge to diagnose which background apps cause battery drain or performance issues
Even well-behaved background apps can collectively degrade performance when running simultaneously
The right to shoot yourself in the foot is reasonable but belongs on platforms designed for it, like the Mac
money_quotes: What's wrong with "since now"? A new platform can't be innovative if it isn't different.
No matter what you install from the App Store, you *can't* screw up your system.
A likely reaction would simply be to regret ever having junked up their iPhone with any third-party apps at all.
What's the advice for the typical user supposed to be? "Have fun with the App Store, but don't install too much crap"?

If you truly demand the right to be able to shoot yourself in the foot with the software you install on your phone — which is a perfectly reasonable desire, and is how things work on the Mac — then the non-jailbroken iPhone isn't for you.
posts.key_insight: Restricting background apps on the iPhone is not a limitation but a deliberate design trade-off that guarantees typical users can never degrade their own device by installing third-party software.

posts.summary: Gruber investigates whether any developers have actually been admitted to Apple's iPhone SDK beta program following a mass email sent to applicants. His reporting suggests that while a small number of hand-selected developers were given early access, no one has gotten in through the official $99 sign-up process yet. He attributes this to Apple's digital certificate system not being ready and an overwhelming response of at least 10,000 applications. He also clarifies that the beta exclusivity shouldn't be confused with the eventual App Store openness, and adds a notable detail about how AOL's AIM client handles the no-background-apps policy.
key_points: No developers have been admitted to the iPhone SDK beta through the official $99 application process; only hand-selected developers have access
Apple's digital certificate infrastructure for testing apps on actual iPhones isn't fully ready yet, limiting their ability to onboard developers
Apple received at least 10,000 applications to the $99 beta program, indicating overwhelming developer interest
The long-term question isn't developer program exclusivity but App Store exclusivity
AOL's AIM client demonstrates that useful apps can work within Apple's no-background-processing constraint by staying logged in even when the app isn't running
money_quotes: There are developers who've been let in through the back door, but no one has gotten in through the front door yet.
The big question, long-term, isn't how exclusive the official developer program is going to be, but rather how exclusive the App Store is going to be.
Be wary of claims that 'An app that does X is impossible without background processing.'
I don't think Apple could let more developers in today even if they wanted to.
Two sources told me that Apple has received at least 10,000 applications to the $99 beta program.
posts.key_insight: The early iPhone SDK beta rollout revealed that Apple's developer infrastructure wasn't ready for the overwhelming demand, but the real question was never about beta access — it was about how tightly Apple would control the App Store itself.

posts.summary: Gruber identifies a quirky behavior in Apple Mail where Page Up/Down keys scroll the message content pane instead of the message list when the list has focus. He acknowledges the logic behind this design choice but criticizes it as non-standard behavior that violates how every other app works. Through Twitter, he discovered that holding the Control key makes Page Up/Down scroll the message list instead. He notes that this workaround isn't documented in Mail's own help book.
key_points: Apple Mail's Page Up/Down keys scroll the message content pane even when the message list has focus, violating standard list behavior
The design logic is understandable but prioritizes convenience over consistency with platform conventions
Twitter served as a real-time support channel where other users quickly provided the Control-key workaround
Apple's own help documentation fails to mention this keyboard shortcut or explain Mail's non-standard focus rules
money_quotes: The problem is that this is non-standard behavior. No other app works like this.
The standard behavior is that when a list has focus, Page Up/Down scroll the list.
Thanks to the miracle of Twitter, I have been informed of a workaround.
For what it's worth, Mail's help book does have a list of keyboard shortcuts, but this trick isn't listed.
posts.key_insight: Even when a design choice has obvious logic behind it, violating platform-standard behavior without documenting the deviation is a user experience failure.

posts.summary: Gruber recounts the editorial process behind choosing the headline for his takedown of Leander Kahney's Wired cover story about Apple. He cycled through 'Jackass,' 'Fucking Jackass,' and 'Irredeemable Jackass,' weighing punchiness against the risk of seeming unhinged. After toggling the headline multiple times based on reader feedback, he settled on 'irredeemable' for its dual function as an intensifier and a callback to Kahney's own language. The post is a self-aware, humorous look at how much deliberation goes into a single word choice.
key_points: The original 'jackass' headline felt insufficient for what Gruber saw as an exceptionally bad piece of journalism on the cover of a major magazine
His objection to 'fucking' wasn't about prudishness but about tone management — he worried it would make him seem unhinged rather than sharp
He chose 'irredeemable' because it worked both as an intensifier and as a pointed reference to Kahney's own rhetoric about Apple
The post is a meta-commentary on editorial craft, showing how much thought goes into seemingly trivial word choices
money_quotes: My concern over using fucking in the headline wasn't squeamishness in the face of strong profanity, but rather that it might come across as slightly unhinged.
I use "jackass" frequently, and this was an exceptional bit of jackassery: nonsensical, shrill, and on the cover of a prominent magazine.
A few readers who noticed the change emailed to say they liked "fucking" better, and, well, I aim to please, so I changed it back before calling it a night.
Tomorrow: 750 words on whether I should have used a dash rather than a colon after "jackassery" in the first paragraph above.
And, yes, I considered "irredeemable fucking jackass", but rejected it on the basis that it sounds awkward to my ear.
posts.key_insight: Even a single word choice in a headline deserves deliberation — the right intensifier should sharpen the argument, not undermine the writer's credibility.

posts.summary: Gruber eviscerates Leander Kahney's Wired cover story which frames Apple as 'irredeemably evil' by contrasting it with Google's supposedly virtuous corporate culture. He methodically dismantles Kahney's premise that Apple succeeded 'by doing everything wrong,' pointing out that the Google comparison is specious since both companies are atypical and actually share many traits Kahney claims differentiate them. Gruber highlights Apple's open source contributions like WebKit as direct counterevidence. He catches Kahney contradicting himself within seven paragraphs — first lamenting Apple's legal action against Think Secret's leaks, then citing a source claiming Apple's product secrecy generated $400 million in free publicity for the iPhone alone. Gruber concludes that the piece represents a shocking decline in Wired's editorial standards.
key_points: Kahney's premise that Apple is 'evil' because it differs from Google is absurd — most companies differ from Google, and the two companies are actually more alike than different in the ways Kahney claims they diverge
Neither Apple nor Google is dogmatically open or closed — Apple open-sources WebKit and Darwin while Google keeps Gmail and search algorithms proprietary
Kahney contradicts himself by arguing both that Apple's secrecy generates massive free publicity AND that Apple foolishly attacked Think Secret for undermining that same secrecy
The article holds up Sun Microsystems' CEO blogging as a model of corporate openness while Sun's stock had lost 97 percent of its value
Kahney conflates Google's 'Don't be evil' mantra with mundane corporate strategies like open platforms and employee perks, which have nothing to do with evil
money_quotes: A decade ago, Wired was my favorite magazine. Today, they print mind-numbing tripe like Leander Kahney's 3,500-word cover story.
Mediocre employee cafeterias are evil?
It's particularly ironic given that Apple had been in business for two decades prior to Google's existence.
The simple, obvious truth is that both Apple and Google have atypical strategies and cultures, and both companies have achieved atypical results. Imagine that.

What with Sun's stock price losing 97 percent of its value over the last seven years, it's hard to believe any company wouldn't model themselves after Sun. Layoffs are fun when the CEO blogs about them.
There's an old poker adage: Look around the table, and if you can't tell who the sucker is, it's you. One gets the feeling that if you see Leander Kahney at your table, you can stop looking.

What kind of secretive crackpot wouldn't want to speak to a writer working on a piece that labels your company 'irredeemably evil' and whose best-known work is a book that literally brands your customers as cultists?
So this is the sort of logic, research, and insight that passes for a Wired cover story today. Does anyone at Wired even read this shit before publishing it?
posts.key_insight: Kahney's premise collapses under its own weight: Apple and Google are both atypical companies with more similarities than differences, and the article's own evidence contradicts its central thesis within paragraphs.

posts.summary: Gruber addresses the controversy over Apple pushing Safari to Windows users through its Software Update utility. He argues that the real problem isn't whether Apple should offer Safari, but that the Software Update app's design fails to distinguish between updates to installed software and offers to install new software. He proposes that new software should be displayed separately with distinct visual treatment, with installation defaulting to off or requiring explicit user action. He concludes that Apple's laziness in not redesigning the update interface caused an entirely avoidable controversy.
key_points: The debate was polarized because people argued about the wrong thing — the real issue is Software Update's design, not whether Apple should offer Safari
Updates to installed software and offers for new software are fundamentally different things and should look visually distinct
New software offers should default to not installing, or require explicit user choice, with an easy option to permanently dismiss the offer
Apple likely skipped the redesign because Safari was the only new Windows app they planned to push, but laziness doesn't excuse bad design
money_quotes: The reason reactions to this controversy have been so polarized is that we've been mostly arguing about the wrong thing.
Different things should look different; the current design of Software Update doesn't allow for such a visual differentiation.
It should be clear that what's being offered is both new and optional.
Companies shouldn't abuse that to push entirely unrelated software on end users.
But laziness is no excuse.
This entire controversy, minuscule though it may be, could — and should — have been avoided if Apple had followed the design principle of making things that are different look different.
posts.key_insight: The Apple Software Update controversy was fundamentally a design failure — different things should look different, and conflating software updates with new software installations violates a basic design principle.

posts.summary: Gruber tracked down a bug in Apple Mail that removed the blank line between reply attribution and quoted text. Through community sleuthing on Twitter, he identified that the bug was introduced by the Safari 3.1 update, since Mail uses an editable WebKit control for message composition. The bug only affected users who set their message format to plain text rather than rich text. He published an AppleScript workaround that programmatically re-inserts the missing blank line when composing replies, building on his earlier anti-top-posting scripts.
key_points: A Safari 3.1 WebKit update introduced a formatting bug in Apple Mail's plain text reply quoting, removing the blank line between attribution and quoted text
The root cause was non-obvious: Mail uses an editable WebKit control for composing messages, so a browser update broke email formatting
The bug only manifested for users with Mail set to plain text format, which is why many users never noticed it
Gruber provided an AppleScript workaround that simulates keystrokes to fix the formatting, extending his earlier anti-top-posting script
money_quotes: Longtime readers are perhaps aware that I'm somewhat particular about the formatting of the email I send.
Many of you might be thinking that this is crazy — how could a new version of WebKit introduce a bug into Mail? Easy: Mail's message editing view is an editable WebKit control.
Most people aren't even affected by this bug because Apple Mail defaults to the rich text message format.
Among those who prefer to send plain text, most people will ignore the bug.
For those few who, like me, prefer plain text and cannot ignore the bug, I have a solution.
I published this AppleScript to change the format of replies to one that doesn't presume you'll be engaging in the uncouth, illiterate practice of top-posting.
posts.key_insight: Apple's architectural decision to use WebKit as Mail's editing engine means browser updates can introduce unexpected bugs in email composition — a cautionary example of shared component dependencies.

posts.summary: Gruber announces a new approach to Daring Fireball t-shirt sales, switching to periodic promotions rather than continuous orders to avoid the recurring problem of accepting orders for out-of-stock shirts. He takes personal responsibility for the fulfillment delays, absolving his partner Oddica. The post also marks the two-year anniversary of Daring Fireball as a full-time endeavor, with Gruber reflecting on how the site's revenue model has evolved from membership-driven to advertising-supplemented. He expresses pride in building a self-published, profitable website without borrowing money or relying heavily on advertising, sustained by direct reader support.
key_points: T-shirt sales are moving to periodic promotions to solve chronic out-of-stock fulfillment problems, with Gruber taking full blame for the delays
Membership revenue is no longer the majority of Daring Fireball's income but remains a significant and valued revenue stream
Gruber frames the site as a point of pride: self-published, profitable, debt-free, and built with minimal advertising
The free RSS feed going full-content reduced differentiation from member feeds, but members still get useful features like separate article and Linked List feeds
money_quotes: It is my fault, not Oddica's, that I continued to accept orders for out-of-stock shirts months in advance of the new print runs.
Membership revenue, to me, remains the purest form of support.
I'm pretty proud of what I've accomplished with Daring Fireball: a self-published, profitable web site for which I never borrowed any money, and have accepted only a modicum of advertising.
This has only been possible because of the direct support of readers like you.
My original (years ago) goal was to take this site full-time based on nothing but direct reader support; that didn't work out, but membership revenue, to me, remains the purest form of support.
posts.key_insight: Gruber's transparency about his business model — taking blame for operational mistakes and expressing genuine gratitude for reader support — reflects the direct, accountable relationship between independent publishers and their audiences that makes self-publishing viable.

posts.summary: Gruber reminds readers that the Daring Fireball t-shirt store is closing the next day, urging last-minute purchases. He highlights the Kevin Cornell-designed 'Daredevil Helmet' shirt as a particular draw. The post serves as a final call for the membership and swag promotion cycle. He closes with genuine gratitude to readers whose memberships enable him to write Daring Fireball full-time.
key_points: The DF t-shirt store is closing the following day until the next promotion cycle
The Kevin Cornell-designed 'Daredevil Helmet' shirt is highlighted as a limited opportunity
Reader memberships directly fund Gruber's ability to write DF full-time
money_quotes: Every time I say so it feels a bit corny, but it really is thanks to readers like you that I can write DF full-time.
posts.key_insight: Gruber's reader-supported membership model enables independent full-time blogging, a relationship he acknowledges with sincere if self-conscious gratitude.

posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Adobe's announcement that Photoshop CS4 will ship as 64-bit on Windows but only 32-bit on Mac OS X. He argues the immediate performance impact is modest for most users (8-12% faster), but the real story is Adobe's planned Cocoa rewrite for CS5, an unprecedented undertaking for an app of Photoshop's size. He reveals that Apple pulled 64-bit Carbon support at WWDC 2007 after promising it at WWDC 2006, blindsiding Adobe mid-development. Gruber takes a measured stance, arguing both companies made reasonable decisions given evolving circumstances, and that Adobe couldn't have been expected to anticipate Carbon's dead end when even Apple didn't know.
key_points: 64-bit Photoshop offers only 8-12% speedup for typical files, with dramatic gains only for massive multi-gigapixel datasets far beyond what most photographers produce
Apple dropped promised 64-bit Carbon support at WWDC 2007, forcing Adobe into an unprecedented Carbon-to-Cocoa port for an application of Photoshop's scale
Carbon and Cocoa are not competing frameworks — Carbon is a lower-level API set that developers use to build their own frameworks, which is why cross-platform apps gravitate toward it
Adobe found out about the 64-bit Carbon cancellation at the same time as the general public, despite actively developing against it
Both Apple and Adobe made reasonable decisions at each step; Apple's Leopard delays forced painful priority changes that cascaded to third-party developers
money_quotes: The uncomfortable truth is that the fastest way to run Photoshop CS4 on a Mac will be to run it under Windows.
Carbon and Cocoa are not competing application frameworks. Carbon is conceptually a lower-level technology than Cocoa.
If Apple had shipped Leopard with the 64-bit Carbon support promised at WWDC 2006, Photoshop CS4 would run in 64-bit mode on the Mac.
When facts change, plans change.
No one has ever ported an application the size of Photoshop from Carbon to Cocoa, so we're dealing with unknown territory.
What users want are new and improved features; a port from Carbon to Cocoa is going to require a tremendous amount of effort just to re-implement what Photoshop already does.
I think it's hard to argue that Adobe should have somehow known the end was near for Carbon when even Apple didn't know.
posts.key_insight: Apple's last-minute cancellation of 64-bit Carbon support forced Adobe into an unprecedented rewrite, but both companies made defensible decisions — the real lesson is how platform shifts cascade unpredictably through developer ecosystems.

posts.summary: Gruber returns to Safari after several weeks testing Firefox 3 betas, finding that while Firefox 3 is a dramatically improved Mac browser with genuine advantages in memory management, tab restoration, and extensions, Safari wins on the behavioral details that matter most to a Mac-native user. His complaints aren't cosmetic but deeply behavioral: Firefox fails to follow standard Mac conventions for background windows, text editing shortcuts, Services menu integration, and dictionary lookup. He acknowledges Firefox's strengths fairly, particularly its extension API and superior memory efficiency, but concludes that Safari's attention to Mac-specific interaction details makes it the better choice for experienced Mac users.
key_points: Firefox 3's shortcomings as a Mac app are behavioral, not just cosmetic — it violates Mac conventions for background window appearance, text editing shortcuts, Services menu, and system dictionary integration.
Safari's in-location-field progress indicator is a design triumph that Gruber initially dismissed as hideous in 2003, demonstrating how good design can take time to appreciate.
Firefox 3's memory management is dramatically better than Safari's, which routinely consumes 300+ MB versus Firefox's 90 MB ceiling.
Firefox's extension API is a tremendous structural advantage over Safari, which forces developers to use unsupported input manager hacks for similar functionality.
Competition in the Mac browser space is strong and getting stronger, with Firefox 3 being not just a great web browser but a pretty good Mac browser too.
money_quotes: After a few weeks in the arms of Firefox 3 betas, I've returned to Safari as my daily browser. Unsurprisingly, it's the interface that drove me back.
But cosmetic appeal is just the surface.
Design is how it works.
Click to place, double-click to select — just like any other text field.
I was wrong, and whoever designed this for Safari was right.
The truth is that page loading is the slowest and most unpleasant aspect of using a web browser.
Firefox isn't just a great web browser, but it's a pretty good Mac web browser, too.
posts.key_insight: What separates a good cross-platform browser from a good Mac browser isn't cosmetic theming but deep behavioral conformance to platform conventions — design is how it works, not how it looks.

posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his Safari 3 vs. Firefox 3 comparison by organizing his criticisms into objective platform violations and subjective preferences. He highlights that Mozilla developer Mike Beltzner responded constructively, with fixes for background window appearance and text editing shortcuts already planned. Gruber acknowledges his own error about click-to-select behavior in URL fields, correcting himself on standard Mac triple-click conventions. He also addresses the web development angle, noting that while Safari has improved its developer tools, no one wrote in to say they prefer them over Firebug. The piece demonstrates Gruber's commitment to fair criticism while maintaining his preference for Safari as a daily browser.
key_points: Gruber separates his Firefox criticisms into objective Mac platform violations (no background window styling, broken text editing shortcuts, no Services/AppleScript) versus subjective UI preferences
Mozilla's Mike Beltzner responded constructively, with fixes for background window appearance and text editing behavior planned before Firefox 3.0 ships
No one who wrote in prefers Safari's developer tools over Firefox's Firebug extension, even though Safari 3.1 added a capable Web Inspector
Gruber corrects his own mistake about URL field click behavior, acknowledging that triple-click (not double-click) is the standard Mac way to select all text
Firefox extensions like FlashBlock are genuinely useful capabilities that Safari input manager hacks can't match because they lack supported, official APIs
money_quotes: I certainly wasn't pushing for Firefox to clone Safari's UI and behavior. In fact, that would be foolish — Firefox can't be better than Safari if it isn't different.
It warms my heart that so many Mac users care about this.
I didn't bother with benchmarks because I don't care.
What FlashBlock does is very cool: it disables all Flash elements on a page until you click on them. If you're as annoyed by obnoxious animation as I am, it's just what the doctor ordered.
I know these things exist, and I've used some in the past, but they are not equivalent to Firefox extensions based on supported, official APIs.
I didn't get a single email from anyone who prefers Safari developer tools over Firebug and Firefox.
posts.key_insight: Good platform citizenship means distinguishing between objective violations of system conventions and subjective design preferences — and being willing to correct yourself when you get the distinction wrong.

posts.summary: Gruber examines Safari's two distinct tab-dragging modes: intra-window (triggered by horizontal drag) and inter-window (triggered by vertical drag). He explains that the mode is locked in based on the initial direction of mouse movement, with no way to switch without starting over. He argues this is both confusing and irritating, since the inter-window mode can do everything the intra-window mode can plus more. He concludes the intra-window mode has no reason to exist.
key_points: Safari has two tab-dragging modes determined by initial drag direction: left/right for intra-window reordering, up/down for inter-window operations
Once locked into a mode, you cannot switch without dropping the tab and starting a new drag, making accidental mode selection frustrating
The inter-window mode is a strict superset of intra-window functionality, making the existence of two modes unnecessary
This directional mode-switching pattern is nearly unique on the Mac platform, violating user expectations about how drag operations work
money_quotes: This state of affairs is irritating at best, and confusing at worst.
I can't think of any other drag operation on the Mac where you get a completely different mode based on the initial direction of the drag.
Even if you understand how it works (which is a big if), it's still easy to get locked in the wrong mode.
If you want to drag down but move the mouse even one iota left or right first, you get locked in the intra-window mode and have to start over.
I don't see why the intra-window (left-right) mode even exists.
The intra-window mode only allows one thing: reordering tabs within their current window. I can't think of a single reason why this mode exists.
posts.key_insight: Safari's dual tab-dragging modes are a design failure because the inter-window mode is a strict superset of the intra-window mode, making the direction-dependent mode switching needlessly confusing.

posts.summary: Gruber examines how Mac OS X's system-wide 'Search in Google' contextual menu command always opens Safari, even when the user has set a different default browser. He notes that while Apple generally respects the user's default browser choice, this Cocoa text system feature is a notable exception. He argues that Apple could easily construct a Google search URL and open it in the default browser, but chooses not to because routing searches through Safari's toolbar preserves Google affiliate revenue for Apple. It's a small but telling example of Apple prioritizing revenue over user preference.
key_points: Mac OS X generally respects the user's default browser choice, but the system-wide 'Search in Google' contextual menu always opens Safari
The feature hooks into Safari's Google Search field rather than simply constructing a URL for the default browser
Apple's likely motivation is preserving Google affiliate search revenue that flows through Safari
Other Safari tie-ins like iTunes bookmark syncing are more defensible since they involve file format parsing
money_quotes: A simpler way to implement the feature would be to construct a Google search URL containing the terms and open that URL in the default browser.
This would make the feature work equally well in all browsers — but would deprive Apple of a few pennies in Google affiliate lucre for each such search.
It's the only tie-in to Safari that really irks me.
posts.key_insight: Apple's 'Search in Google' contextual menu bypasses the user's default browser choice in favor of Safari, likely to preserve Google affiliate revenue — a small but telling prioritization of money over user experience.

posts.summary: Gruber investigates how much money Apple receives from Google for search referrals through Safari. Building on his earlier reporting of a $25 million annual figure from a knowledgeable source, he constructs a rough estimate by working backward from the Mozilla Foundation's public financial disclosures. By comparing Safari's browser usage share to Firefox's and applying Mozilla's known Google revenue, he arrives at estimates ranging from $13 million to $21 million. He suggests the actual number is likely higher because Apple — meaning Steve Jobs — is a better negotiator than Mozilla, and Safari users may be more demographically valuable to Google.
key_points: Mozilla Foundation's Google revenue provides a useful baseline for estimating Apple's deal, since both receive payments for search referrals
Depending on which browser usage share data you trust, Safari has between 20-33% of Firefox's share, yielding Apple revenue estimates of $13-21 million
Safari's search field is hard-wired to Google only, unlike Firefox's configurable search, which could mean Safari sends proportionally more Google referrals
Apple likely negotiates a better per-referral rate than Mozilla, partly because Safari users may be more demographically appealing to advertisers
money_quotes: That number came from, as they say, a source with knowledge of the situation.
One might also presume — as I do — that Apple has better negotiators than the Mozilla Foundation, and that Google pays Apple more per referral than they do Mozilla.
Apple may well argue that Safari users, as a whole, are demographically more appealing than Firefox users.
Where by 'better' I mean 'Steve Jobs'.
posts.key_insight: Public financial disclosures from Mozilla can be used to reverse-engineer Apple's Google search revenue, but Apple's superior negotiating leverage likely means the real number exceeds what usage share alone would suggest.

posts.summary: Gruber explains the HFS+ invisible bit on Mac OS X, prompted by helping a friend whose Desktop folder had mysteriously become invisible. He describes the two supported methods for making files invisible (Unix dot-file convention and the HFS+ invisible bit), explains how invisibility metadata works and why it isn't inherited by folder contents, and walks through command-line tools for inspecting and toggling the bit. He also recommends Bare Bones Software's Super Get Info as a GUI alternative and notes that Leopard introduced the simpler chflags command.
key_points: There are two supported ways to make files invisible on Mac: the Unix dot-file convention and the HFS+ invisible bit, each honored in different contexts
The HFS+ invisible bit is metadata that hides files from the Finder and dialogs but is not honored by the Terminal's ls command
Invisibility is not inherited by folder contents, explaining why files on the Desktop were visible even though the Desktop folder itself was hidden
Apple ships no default GUI tool to view or set the invisible bit, requiring developer tools (GetFileInfo/SetFile) or third-party utilities like Super Get Info
Leopard added the chflags hidden/nohidden command as a simpler alternative to the developer-tools-only SetFile utility
money_quotes: Files marked invisible are still readable and writable, they just don't show up in the Finder or in Open and Save dialogs.
Invisibility is not inherited by the contents of an invisible folder, which explains why my friend could see the files on his desktop, but couldn't see the "Desktop" folder itself in his home folder.
One place where the HFS+ invisible bit is not honored is the terminal — the ls command lists files and folders regardless of the state of their invisible bits.
I'm not aware of any software that ships by default with Mac OS X that lets you see or set the HFS+ invisible bit.
Invisible items pose an obvious problem, of course, in that if you can't see them, you can't drag them.
Note that toggling the invisible bit does not change the folder's modified date.
posts.key_insight: The HFS+ invisible bit is a poorly surfaced piece of Mac metadata that can cause baffling problems precisely because Apple provides no default tool to see or change it.

posts.summary: Gruber surveys every available Twitter client for the iPhone in 2008 and finds none satisfactory. The core problem is Twitter's API, which has disabled paging beyond the 20 most recent tweets for over six months, crippling all third-party clients. Twitter.com itself renders correctly on Mobile Safari but is unoptimized—tiny tap targets, broken posting UI, and sluggish JavaScript. The mobile interface (m.twitter.com) is fast but feature-poor, and Twitter's switch to user-agent sniffing from separate URLs made it worse. Third-party clients like Hahlo, iTweet, PocketTweets, and Thincloud each have strengths but all share the paging limitation and various UI shortcomings. Gruber concludes that Twitter should be the ideal iPhone web app but isn't, because no single client handles both reading and posting well.
key_points: Twitter's API has disabled paging for over six months, limiting all third-party clients to the 20 most recent tweets—a deal-breaking limitation that Twitter's own website doesn't share.
Twitter.com's UI controls are absurdly small for touch interaction: 18x18px buttons are just 16% the size of Apple's recommended 44x44px minimum, and they're rendered even smaller when scaled.
Twitter's switch from URL-based mobile/desktop separation to cookie-based user-agent sniffing removed the ability to bookmark and tab between both interfaces—a clear regression.
JavaScript-powered live character counts cause typing lag on every third-party client, revealing a fundamental tension between rich UI features and Mobile Safari's performance limitations.
No single client handles both reading and posting well, forcing users into an unsatisfying split between clients for different tasks.
money_quotes: Twitter and the iPhone seem, at a glance, a perfect match: bite-sized micro-content paired with the world's best mobile web reader. But here's the thing: there's not yet a single good iPhone Twitter client.
In terms of area, an 18 x 18 px button is just 16 percent the size of a 44 x 44 px button.
If ever there was a web app that could be — should be — better on the iPhone than on a desktop browser, Twitter is it. But it isn't.
Using one Twitter client for reading and another for posting is like getting your sandwich at Burger King and your fries from McDonald's — convenience is the whole point.
You shouldn't have to press a button to figure out what it does.
Editing is where Hahlo is a Viking.
For posting, the Twitter.com interface is a disaster on the iPhone.
It seems weird that sites like Facebook and Amazon, which do so much more than Twitter, have iPhone-optimized interfaces, but Twitter does not.
posts.key_insight: Twitter's failure to provide a good iPhone experience—through both its own unoptimized website and a crippled API that hamstrings third-party developers—represents a missed opportunity for what should be the ideal mobile web application.

posts.summary: Gruber defends his agreement with Jim Cramer's bullish take on Apple stock, addressing reader incredulity given Cramer's track record. He argues that Cramer's earlier sell recommendation was about the tech sector broadly, not Apple specifically, and that being often wrong doesn't preclude being right this time. The core argument is that Apple's dominance among young consumers is a massively underestimated factor that most analysts miss. Gruber points to Apple's remarkable laptop sales growth (61% year-over-year) as evidence of this youth-driven momentum. He contends that Apple's coolness factor among young people, unburdened by outdated Mac perceptions from the '80s and '90s, is what fundamentally separates Apple from the broader tech sector.
key_points: Cramer's January sell recommendation was about the tech sector broadly, and most stocks he warned about did decline — Apple was not the specific target
Apple's dominance among young consumers is both wide (iPods and Macs) and deep (remarkable devotion), and most analysts underestimate it because they themselves are old and uncool
Young consumers lack the negative preconceptions about Macs from the '80s and '90s because anything pre-iPod is prehistoric to them
Apple's laptop sales grew 61% year-over-year versus 37% for desktops, likely driven by student buyers
Apple's youth market strength is precisely what differentiates it from the struggling tech sector as a whole
money_quotes: Even if Cramer is often or even usually wrong about Apple, it doesn't mean he isn't right this time.
The younger one is, the more disproportionately one tends to care about what's cool.
Apple's kit is very cool, and their competitors' kit ranges from 'not cool' to 'totally lame'.
When you're old and everyone around you is doing or buying something you don't understand, you think they're assholes; when you're young, you do it/buy one, too.
Today's students don't have preconceptions (or misconceptions) about Macs dating from the '90s or '80s because anything pre-iPod is prehistoric from their perspective.
Apple's strength in this market is exactly what separates it most from the tech sector as a whole.
These analysts are old-and-not-cool.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iconic status among young consumers — who lack legacy baggage about Macs and care disproportionately about what's cool — is a massively undervalued competitive advantage that most analysts miss because they don't inhabit that market themselves.

posts.summary: Gruber shares an anecdote about the surprisingly loud fan noise in Apple's 2005 Developer Transition Kit machines — Intel PCs housed in Power Mac cases given to developers during the PowerPC-to-Intel transition. The story comes via Cabel Sasser of Panic, prompted by Craig Hockenberry's concern about noise levels in his newly quieted office. Sasser's team filmed video evidence of the DTK's fan noise to address Hockenberry's fears, which apparently confirmed his worst expectations. The piece is framed as a humorous contrast to Apple's usual hardware refinement, with a topical connection to Psystar's notoriously noisy Mac clones.
key_points: Apple's Developer Transition Kits were essentially PC hardware in Power Mac cases, not refined Apple products
Craig Hockenberry had undertaken a project to minimize office noise and feared the DTK would ruin it
The DTK's fan noise was so bad that Hockenberry's reaction contained 'unprintable' all-caps comments
The story parallels Psystar's clone machines, which were also criticized for egregious fan noise
money_quotes: I encouraged Sasser to publish this important story on his own well-regarded Cabel's Blog LOL, but, alas, due to scheduling constraints at Panic, he estimated such publication wouldn't be feasible for another few years.
He fully expected it to be the normal PC POS hardware.
We took a quick video to quell his fears.
Suffice it to say Craig's response contained all-caps comments that are unprintable here.
We waited a little while, then sent him a follow-up video with a little more… explanation.
posts.key_insight: Even Apple's own interim PC hardware during the Intel transition was the kind of loud, unrefined experience that underscores why Apple's obsessive hardware engineering matters.

posts.summary: Gruber dismantles a BusinessWeek article by Arik Hesseldahl that speculates AT&T will subsidize the next iPhone by $200, bringing it to $199. He traces the entire subsidy story back to a single report by Scott Moritz, a reporter Gruber considers unreliable given his track record of fabricated Apple stories. Gruber mocks the article's economic observations as painfully obvious and challenges its assumptions about competitive pressure from BlackBerry and Palm. He points out a critical flaw the article nearly discovers but misses: a $200 AT&T subsidy would undercut Apple's own stores and force abandonment of their innovative at-home iTunes activation model.
key_points: The entire $200 iPhone subsidy story traces back to a single report by Scott Moritz, who previously cited a trading note that didn't exist
Predictions that competitors will catch the iPhone echo the same failed predictions about iPod competitors in 2002
The article ignores that dozens of $400-800 phones already existed on Amazon, undermining the claim iPhone pricing was 'unheard of'
A carrier subsidy would fundamentally conflict with Apple selling iPhones in its own stores and its at-home iTunes activation model
Speculation about next-gen iPhone features being limited to 3G ignores Apple's history of surprising with iPod updates
money_quotes: 3G networking could well be the least of what's cool about next-generation iPhone hardware.
Sounds a lot like what people wrote about the iPod in 2002: competitors haven't caught up yet, but surely they will, because, well, just because.
In the same way that surely, eventually, Ford and GM will start making cars as well-engineered and designed as the Germans and Japanese, because, well, because.
I find this theory intriguing, that when prices go down, demand 'could' go up. With this sort of deep economic insight, it's no wonder people read BusinessWeek.
Perhaps they should give the iPhones away for free — perhaps demand would then go even higher.
The problem is this: why would Apple allow AT&T to sell iPhones for half the price of what iPhones cost in Apple's own stores?
Hesseldahl and his keen economic mind walk right past it.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone subsidy rumor collapses under scrutiny because it relies on a single unreliable source and ignores the fundamental problem that carrier subsidies would undercut Apple's own retail and activation model.

posts.summary: Gruber examines the debate over the iPhone's software keyboard versus BlackBerry's physical keyboard, prompted by a CrackBerry 'Top 10' list he finds largely unconvincing. He surveyed his Twitter followers who switched from physical-keyboard phones to the iPhone and found the consensus is that it takes about a week to learn and a month to master, but most end up satisfied. He identifies the core marketing challenge: convincing skeptical physical-keyboard users to take a leap of faith on something that feels worse in a brief store demo. Ultimately, he argues this pocket of resistance is relatively insignificant to Apple's mass-market ambitions, since most future iPhone buyers are coming from basic phones with numeric keypads. However, he notes Apple clearly covets BlackBerry's enterprise customers and acknowledges the keyboard remains RIM's strongest card with that audience.
key_points: Twitter survey of iPhone switchers from physical keyboards shows most are happy after a learning curve of about a week to a month
The iPhone keyboard's weakness is its trial experience — a few minutes in a store won't convince a skeptical BlackBerry user, so adoption requires a leap of faith
People who regularly use both keyboard types tend to like the iPhone keyboard least, suggesting full commitment is required to build the necessary muscle memory
The physical-keyboard smartphone market is tiny compared to Apple's iPod-scale ambitions — most future iPhone buyers are coming from basic numeric keypads
Apple's enterprise push with iPhone 2.0 shows it covets BlackBerry switchers, but Gruber doubts Apple will ever release a physical-keyboard iPhone
money_quotes: My own opinion is simply that the iPhone keyboard works a lot better than I expected it to.
I suspect the only way for someone accustomed to a BlackBerry-style keyboard to get used to the iPhone keyboard is to switch full-time. The necessary muscle memory is too different.
"It takes a couple of weeks to get used to it" means you've got to take it on faith.
Even if there's not a single already-using-a-smartphone user left who is going to switch to an iPhone, it wouldn't prevent the iPhone from being a mass market success.
No one can argue that typing on the iPhone doesn't beat the pants off gimmicks like T9.
The keyboard is perhaps the single biggest advantage RIM has with these customers.

If they did, it'd be one of those Steve Jobs 'a year ago he said these things were crap and now he's telling us this one's awesome just because it's from Apple' moves that drive some people — bless their hearts — spittle-flying-out-of-their-mouths crazy.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone keyboard's real obstacle isn't that it's bad — it's that its quality only becomes apparent after weeks of committed use, creating a leap-of-faith problem that brief store demos can't solve.

posts.summary: Gruber dissects NBC's claim that Microsoft agreed to build copyright filtering technology into the Zune as part of a content distribution deal. He systematically examines why every proposed technical approach — fingerprinting databases, watermarking, or DRM-only playback — would be either infeasible or commercially suicidal for a device with only 4% market share. He concludes Microsoft likely promised NBC something they never intended to deliver. An update reveals Microsoft explicitly denied any such plans, vindicating Gruber's skepticism.
key_points: NBC's stated reasons for preferring Microsoft over Apple were flexible pricing and a promise to build copyright filtering into devices
Every technically feasible approach to copyright filtering — fingerprint databases, watermarking, DRM-only playback — has fatal practical flaws
A copyright filtering system must be extremely accurate in both directions: too permissive is pointless, too strict is infuriating
Microsoft with 4% market share cannot afford to hobble the Zune with restrictions that would further suppress adoption
Microsoft likely made promises to NBC it had no intention of keeping, and a subsequent Microsoft denial confirmed this
money_quotes: But so how could this possibly work? What could Microsoft do that would satisfy these demands from NBC?
Smells a bit like magic, though, to expect a 'fingerprint' system to accurately identify an episode of a particular TV series.
It's the sort of system that needs to be extremely accurate. If the matching algorithm is too permissive and fails to identify much of the material it's supposed to catch, what's the point?

If it's too strict, and incorrectly flags non-infringing video and blocks you from watching them (or, worse, 'removes' them from your video library — and note that 'remove' is indeed the verb NBC jerkos have used when describing this dystopic feature), well, that'd be infuriating, to say the least.
It'd be one thing if Microsoft were the company with 70 percent share in the handheld media player market, but they're not. They're the company with 4 percent share.
Microsoft is craven, and they've been known to engage in boil-the-ocean sorts of software projects before, but they're not stupid.
So I think they've pulled a fast one on NBC, promising them something they have no intention to deliver.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft likely promised NBC copyright filtering technology it had no intention of building, because actually delivering it would have killed the already-marginal Zune.

posts.summary: Gruber argues that RIM and BlackBerry are in serious trouble against the iPhone by first establishing that the iPhone's computing power is roughly equivalent to a 1999-2000 Mac (like the Pismo PowerBook G3), then extrapolating via Moore's Law that handheld computing power will continue to rapidly advance. He contends that Apple's announced enterprise features for iPhone 2.0 eliminate nearly every software advantage BlackBerry held, leaving only the physical keyboard as a differentiator. Gruber dismisses RIM's security arguments as conflating carrier unlocking with actual security vulnerabilities. He frames the competitive dynamic using a Risk analogy: RIM's users are concentrated in one attackable segment, and the iPhone can do more BlackBerry things than BlackBerry can do iPhone things. He concludes that RIM lacks the engineering capability in both hardware and software to compete with where the iPhone is heading.
key_points: The iPhone's 2007 specs (400 MHz ARM, 128 MB RAM) roughly match a 2000 Pismo PowerBook G3, meaning Moore's Law could give a 2014 iPhone the power of a 2008 MacBook Pro
Web browsing speed depends more on processor power than network speed alone — even on Wi-Fi, the iPhone renders pages much slower than a Mac, making faster CPUs more impactful than 3G
Apple's iPhone 2.0 enterprise features (Exchange support, remote wipe, push email) eliminate virtually every software advantage BlackBerry held over the iPhone
RIM's entire user base is concentrated in one vulnerable market segment — email-focused enterprise users willing to pay for handsets — making them an ideal target for Apple
RIM shows no signs of having the engineering capability to compete with the iPhone's trajectory in either hardware or software
money_quotes: Think about that — in just nine years, the specs that then described Apple's top-of-the-line desktop computer now describe their phone.
Apple is the best handheld computer engineering company in the world today, hands down. They're also the best handheld computer user experience design company. And they're not sharing.
RIM has a large army, but they're all massed together in one spot on the map.
The iPhone can do more BlackBerry-ish things than the BlackBerry can do iPhone-ish things.
Unless you're Richard Stallman, you probably don't read the web through your email client.
Apple doesn't wait for someone else to knock one of their hit products off its throne or slowly run it into the ground — they do it themselves.
There are no signs that RIM has the engineering chops on either side of the ball — hardware or software — to compete with where the iPhone is now, let alone where it's going to be.
Translation: 'We'll emphasize the physical keyboard as a differentiating factor as long as it seems to work, at which point we'll try a touch-screen keyboard too.'
posts.key_insight: RIM's concentrated, email-dependent user base and lack of engineering depth make it uniquely vulnerable to the iPhone, which is rapidly closing BlackBerry's enterprise advantages while widening the gap in everything else.

posts.summary: Gruber reviews OS X Mountain Lion as a polished, incremental update rather than a blockbuster release, priced at just $19.99. He argues that Apple's strategy of annual, affordable updates has driven record adoption rates and allowed radical changes to be introduced gradually rather than all at once. The review highlights iCloud document storage as a significant philosophical shift in how users manage files, moving from filesystem-centric to app-centric organization. Gruber contrasts Apple's incremental approach with Microsoft's bet-everything strategy on Windows 8, suggesting Apple's annual cadence borrowed from iOS may be its most important innovation for the Mac platform.
key_points: Mountain Lion continues Apple's trend of lowering upgrade prices ($129 to $29 to $19.99), driving faster adoption than any previous Mac OS release
The most significant changes to the Mac over five years — App Store, sandboxing, iCloud — have been introduced incrementally across releases rather than in one blockbuster update
iCloud document storage represents a radical shift from filesystem-centric to app-centric file management, but Apple is enticing rather than forcing users to adopt it
Apple maintains two distinct systems (Mac OS X and iOS) sharing features through iCloud, while Microsoft bets everything on a single OS strategy with Windows 8
The annual incremental development schedule borrowed from iOS may be the most important thing Apple has brought back to the Mac
money_quotes: It was a wise and practical decision, marketing implications be damned, to devote over a year of engineering effort to under-the-hood OS infrastructure.
A solid technical foundation is good for everyone — users, Apple, developers alike. But it's a hard sell — like opening a restaurant that serves nutritious entrees but no desserts.
But 'faster' isn't fast enough, and the Air now feels like it's getting pretty close to the instant-on wake-from-sleep feel of an iOS device.
Some engineer spent time making copying files in the Finder a more pleasing experience.
Apple's not dragging us to the future; they're enticing us to walk there on our own.
That mindset and development schedule — 'What can we do to make this nicer by next year?' — may well be the most important thing from iOS that Apple has taken back to the Mac.
They were extravaganzas, and they were priced accordingly at $129.
posts.key_insight: Apple's annual incremental update strategy, borrowed from iOS, represents a more sustainable and user-friendly approach to OS evolution than Microsoft's blockbuster release model, allowing radical changes to be absorbed gradually.
